the place where tyrants must lament

by bluesyturtle

Summary

When the dust settles and Homelander has everything he thought he wanted, there’s still something missing. He goes to Butcher to find it.

And Butcher, well, he weren’t never one to walk away from a fight if he could come out swinging instead.

Notes

Everybody needs to send Beguile big virtual kisses on the mouf for this one because I wouldn’t have started writing it if she didn’t marathon the entire series and regale me with her live-reactions.
We’ve been having an insane amount of fun, and I thought it might be nice to share. <3

Stay sexy.

See the end of the work for more notes

Chapter 1: said Jesus to Lazarus

Chapter Notes

See the end of the chapter for notes

It isn’t a time of peace, after Soldier Boy. Homelander doesn’t know how to describe it, but it isn’t peace.

People love him, and Ryan loves him, and the sum of all the things he’s lost — all the people; Dad, Noir, Stormfront, Stillwell — has been its own kind of collateral damage to process. To live with. To overcome. Amid a time of ceasefire that isn’t a victory anymore than it is a capitulation. 

But people adore him in this age of quiet unrest, and Homelander knows that if ever there was a time to love himself, to really let himself allow for the possibility, it would surely be now. 

It would have to be, or else when? He has never been better loved in all his life.

Maybe he has never been loved in his life, period. He certainly wasn’t as a child, not in that padded room with its white halogen lights and its single window never permitting him to see the sun. Not with his head in Madelyn’s lap or with Stormfront’s hand on him in a stinking alleyway, some nothing’s skull and brainstem mulched in the well of his fist. They all just wanted to control him. They didn’t love him. Even these simpering mud people don’t love him.

Ryan, though? Ryan curled up next to him on the couch telling him stories of his dead mother; Ryan holding Homelander’s hand in his when they take flight together to watch the sunset over the whole world, one continent at a time; Ryan teaching him how to make cake mix from a box; Ryan showing him how to get high scores in his favorite video games without ever utilizing the cheat codes Homelander can pull up so easily on his phone; Ryan staying up until 2am with him to watch the old black and white movies Homelander’s been surprised to find he enjoys, but never making it through the triple features, always falling asleep with his head on Homelander’s shoulder.

He should be able to love himself if his son can love him, shouldn’t he? If he could find it in himself to love his own father, terrible though he was, at the mere revelation of his existence, of their connection, no matter how tenuous, surely — surely he should love himself by now.

All his life he’s found people to love and for whom to dedicate himself in service. His power, his body, his autonomy. He’s given himself over entirely, trying to be worthy of love.

So why is he still looking for validation from the world? Why does he still — still — feel like he isn’t enough? Why does this armistice feel, to him, more like a declaration of war than an outright call to arms would have been? Why can’t he rest? Why does this tension find him circling William Butcher’s apartment for the third time in the span of a week?

Butcher, he thinks, promised him all-out war. Further, he only half-delivered.

And Homelander hasn’t seen hide or hair or him in months. He hasn’t heard of him apprehending or killing supes; hasn’t heard a single report of someone other than him or Ryan using the red sight in an assult, publicly or privately. He’s kept his ears sharp for it, listening, and nothing.

Not a word. Not a peep.

It would give him something, he thinks, to take some of his frustrations out on Butcher. To go toe-to-toe with him again, to see where it leads. 

To see if, between the two of them, they can kickstart the real war looming out of the dark.

If there is a war looming. Homelander can feel it pulling at his skin like a blister, and he doesn’t like it. He doesn’t like feeling like there’s something happening that he can’t grip in his hands and steer, or kill, if need be. He doesn’t like that it’s happening without him, maybe even in spite of him. Vought had been that way, and now Vought answers to him. Anyone else will go the same way, up to and including Butcher.

And really, he’d have to be simple not to see that thought to its natural conclusion. To not know the truth of himself or of the things his artless flesh can be made to crave, even of a bounty he’s never before tasted. So he knows. He’s been surprised by his desires before, and the time for that has passed. The time to take is upon him. It’s his birthright, as much as anything else he deigns to covet.

Displaced air whips around him like a second cape in the half-step it takes him come down from the superspeed. It hasn’t quite settled around him when he says, apropos of nothing at all, or perhaps of everything, “I’ve been thinking about fucking you.”

“Ain’t you fucked me enough already,” Butcher grouses, without flinching or even doing him the minimal courtesy of looking up.

Homelander breaks his parade rest to stride over behind the chair his sometimes-quarry, often-irritant-nemesis has taken to slouching in. He grips the arched seat-back in his hands so that his knuckles graze Butcher’s shoulder blades, feeling capillaries and freckled skin even through two layers of leather and one sheer skein of cotton-polyester blend fabric. Still Butcher withholds his attention from the conversation at hand.

“Well, frankly, that’s debatable,” Homelander chirps back, tensing his hold on the chair so the wood creaks at the joints just as satisfyingly as bones on the cusp of breaking. “In any case, not what I meant.”

Unbothered, Butcher keeps right on reading whatever’s on the table in front of him. It would be easy enough to liquefy his skull and the offending document, if Homelander wanted, but there’d be no enjoyment in it. He’d laser a bank robber or some lowly terrorist in the back if they had the audacity to turn their back on him, but not Butcher. Not Butcher who could fight back with more than guns; who could fight back at all; whose hand wouldn’t turn to pulp if Homelander reached for him with everything he had—

Under the right conditions, anyway.

On a whim, he takes a peek through Butcher’s head to see what he’s reading, but then his eyes catch, and drag. 

“What the fuck? Did you have a stroke since the last time I saw you?”

With his cheek mashed up into his knuckles, how he’s been sitting ever since Homelander flew up outside his apartment some ten minutes ago, Butcher says, “You could say that.”

“Well, what’s the matter then? Are you too fucked up to take your medicine?” He takes a cursory look around the apartment, even sparser and cleaner than it had been during their last talk. There’s no sign of the Temp V anywhere he can see, not even with the X-ray vision. “Tell me where it is, I’ll do it for you.”

Butcher sighs and turns a page. 

Homelander physically peers over his shoulder to see what he’s reading. It’s just too depressing having to look through the abscesses in his brain.

“Why are you reading a biography about The Spice Girls?”

“Brain’s already rotted, see,” Butcher says — grunts, really. It’s a wonder he can even do that. “Little more’s not gonna hurt now.”

“All right.” 

Homelander makes a grab for the book. He takes his time, expecting to be thwarted even now. Expecting this man who turns apart from the crowd to look right at him — this man who’s managed to knock him down more than once in a fight — to defy him again. But he doesn’t. He lets Homelander take the book and toss it across the kitchen. There’s no twitch in his posture, no rise in his heart rate or in his breathing. He might as well be a stain on the wall. Pulling up a chair to sit next to Butcher tells him why.

It’s never been enough for him to know why, though, so he catches Butcher by the jaw and turns his head, dislodging him from the support of his hand. Black sludge oozes from his right nostril, reeking of blood and, more offensively, cerebrospinal fluid. His eyes, when he can raise them to Homelander’s, are all smoke and no fire.

He’s still in there, though. And maybe he doesn’t have the vigor necessary to cock his head and smirk, but that mean streak hasn’t sunken so far that Homelander can’t make out its spark.

“Did you hear what I said to you when I came in?” he asks, lowering his voice to a murmur.

“I heard ya,” Butcher rumbles back, limited some by Homelander’s hold on his face. “And I said, ain’t I had the requisite amount of fucking by now.”

“And I said, that isn’t the way I’ve been thinking about fucking you.” Homelander gives Butcher’s jaw a brisk shake. He has to be careful since the geometry of Butcher’s face would completely collapse in his fist if he let himself get too careless. It’s strange, to have to treat this man with tenderness, with some modicum of care, when he came here tonight with the express wish of putting his head through a brick wall. “I know your brain’s leaking down the back of your throat right now, but try to keep up.”

“If you wanna fuck me, mate, you’re a little late to the party. Nothing left here for you to take, break, or forcibly cram your dick inside.”

“Oh, I don’t know about that,” Homelander purrs, “but you’re getting warmer.”

“Ain’t you had your fill yet,” Butcher says slowly, and to Homelander’s ears, with great difficulty. He scoffs, exerting the slightest pressure against Homelander’s palm to let him know he’s trying to shake his head. “Who’d I think I was talkin’ to, the fuckin’ Easter Bunny? Right. You got me where you want me. Put me out to pasture then. Quick as you like. I ain’t got all night.”

“Don’t tempt me.” He releases his hold on Butcher’s jaw without bothering to divert him when he nearly smacks his head on the table. “I’m trying to talk to you about fucking you with my dick here.”

Peeling himself off the table to sit upright again, Butcher groans, “Yeah,” more of a question than anything else he’s said to now.

Homelander sighs. “Putting it in a hole.”

Butcher’s eyebrows twitch down, and then the muddiness in his eyes clears just enough for his incredulity to show through. That’s good, that’s reassuring. 

“Listen, cunt,” he starts, mulish, angry as ever, “if you pop a hole in me neck and go to town like it’s Mummy’s breast pump — cum in me windpipe or not, I’ll Lazarus my way back from the grave and ram me boot up your arse. And then we can talk holes.”

It’s tremendously annoying how hearing that doesn’t dissuade him a bit.

“While that is an appealing offer,” Homelander says, rolling his eyes, “the state I want you in isn’t actually compatible with fucking you in your neck-hole.”

“I can’t take that shite no more,” Butcher growls, lurching in his seat when the exertion sloshes the ependymal cells in his fetid brain. He presses the back of his hand to the viscous tar running from his nose, breath heaving in his chest. His eyes slam shut, and bit by bit, his rampaging heart trips and stutters and eventually plateaus at a much slower rate. “Never thought I was a good man. Never thought I deserved better. And you know what?”

His eyes creak open to meet Homelander’s. It hits him then that he’s been holding his breath.

“You, me, Soldier Boy,” Butcher rasps. “Every one of us jumped the line to stand where we stood. Every one of us cheated to have what we had. Same fucking cloth that made him made you, and it made me, too.”

Homelander sets his teeth against his lip, wonders idly how much force Butcher’s mouth could stand before tearing apart — and how much more it could stand, in his best state.

“Not yet,” Homelander murmurs, imagining the taste of salt and iron and red. “But it could.”

“Wh—?”

It’s the work of a breath, Homelander slipping out of the apartment and into the cold black night, Vought-bound. He has access codes, but that would mean waiting, and he doesn’t have to wait if he rips the doors off their hinges. The alarm hasn’t had time to register the assault before he’s left the tower and found himself circling Butcher’s meager little apartment again. He enters through the same unlocked window, and this time, he closes it delicately behind him.

He breaks the soft skin at Butcher’s inner elbow with the needle, but he goes no further. The gravity in the room rushes around him like the dithering exhalation of God.

“—at?”

Butcher stalls, feeling the shift in atmosphere, and glances down at the syringe jutting out of his arm, at the bright blue Compound V rollicking gently against the glass.

“You really want help climbing out of that early grave?” Homelander croons, brushing the pad of his thumb along the plunger like it’s a woman’s pebbling nipple. His voice softens even more, beckoning. “Well, Lazarus, ask me to roll away the stone.”

All resentment and quaint bitterness, Butcher squints at him, and says, “Think this makes you Jesus?”

“I think it makes me God, if I want it to,” Homelander answers easily.

“What do you need with me then?”

“It’s not about need. Pay attention,” he snaps. “What did I say?”

“You think I wanna fuck you, fucking brain damage or not?” 

He lists forward, clutching at his forehead in the wake of another rollicking spell of nausea Homelander can almost track with his eyes: vibrations in the duodenum zapping directly into his moldering brain, releasing all the telltale chemicals that come with vomiting. He doesn’t vomit, but he wilts in his seat, unable to pull himself back together again. His injected forearm remains glued to the table. Homelander knows that’s because he hasn’t stopped thinking about it since he laid eyes on it.

“Don’t you want to know if you can,” Homelander muses.

Butcher looks at him then, and obviously it sparks migraine pain behind his eyes to have to do it, but he doesn’t falter. “What, plug you up? Full offense, mate, but that post-nut clarity ain’t something I want on my conscience.”

“You, having a conscience. That’s adorable.”

“Had enough left to see that your cuck father weren’t half worth the effort it took to spring him.”

Humming, Homelander cedes, “That’s setting the bar pretty low, but yes, fine. Maybe you do have a soul, when it’s Ryan.”

“Maybe so do you,” Butcher relents, dropping his gaze. “That don’t change a thing about where we’ve been or what we’ve done.”

“So be and do a little more with me.” He taps his thumb against the plunger without pressing down. He wants Butcher to say yes to this. He wants Butcher to need this from him and to admit that he does. He wants Butcher to be his most glorious self again, and for Homelander to be the reason. To be the cure every bit as much as the curse. “Or, if you’d rather stay here and stew in your own fluids like a public portable restroom, you’re welcome to do that, too. By all means.”

Butcher turns his eyes to the syringe in his arm. He will have made his decision within moments of realizing what Homelander was offering him — all this time spent hemming and hawing and trying to change his mind, is just that. Half-measures expended for the sake of talking himself out of making the correct choice.

“We can fight to the death any old time,” Homelander ventures. “If you’re alive to challenge me. Compound V means nothing so trivial as brain death can kill you. It means you’ll be whole, as many times as I want you to be. That you’ll heal when I break you.”

“And you,” Butcher adds. 

A quiet, pleasant stillness washes over Homelander, warring impulses that make him feel, strangely and comfortably, aware of his skin. This is not the shape of what he wants. It’s not why he came here looking to bully this little worm into fighting him again. It’s not. He doesn’t want that. But even deteriorating before his very eyes, even wasting away inside the prison of his own body, William Butcher defies him. The cure, every bit as much as the curse. 

He makes himself scoff. None of that can be allowed to show on his face. 

“You, break me?” he says. “Don’t be ridiculous.”

Butcher tips his head back, coaxing a gush of necrosis down the back of his throat. Homelander can see it, but there’s no reaction on Butcher’s face. He just smirks, and tilts his head.

“Go ‘head then, love. Make my night.”

Homelander doesn’t allow himself a pittance of doubt. He administers the injection.

A ripple passes through the whole of Butcher’s body, torquing him at the waist like a folding chair collapsing under too much weight. His injected arm swipes involuntarily across the table, sending the salt and pepper shakers flying. Down he goes off the edge of his chair in a backwards sprawl on the tile. Homelander sinks to his knees beside him, hands waiting decorously and patiently in his lap. On the floor beside him, Butcher thrashes, the loose organic material in his brain scrambling violently in his skull. A scream tears through his throat.

He’s seen this man pared down to nerve endings and collagen fibers; he knows the Langer’s lines in his forehead, in his palms, in his fucking perineal raphe. The only part of him that was ever an unknown quantity was what that aberration, the Temp V, did to him. In the beginning, and at the end. The real stuff, the one true God-maker, can only have a stronger impact on his constitution.

But even if not, even if red sight is all he has, it was enough to stun Homelander in a fight — God, their first real fight — and it was enough to make them enough, for Dad. It can be enough again. 

Butcher’s back arches off the floor. His eyes flash red for a single breathless moment before he snaps them shut, teeth bared in another caught scream. The damage in his brain stitches itself back together, the remaining fluid positively splashing out of his ear like a ruptured water balloon. The smell is awful.

“Small wonder this isn’t a deterrent,” Homelander mutters, irritated at himself, as he always is, at his choice in indulgences.

“Ain’t no fuckin’ peach yourself,” Butcher groans, daring to let his eyes creak open. He scrubs his knuckles at the soup dripping from his ear, painting his hand in stinking putrescence. He pushes up easily onto his elbows, finally meeting Homelander’s bland gaze with his own.

Anticipation curls low in his belly like satisfaction, like the craving for a hand on his dick. Like twin beams of light burning his cheek hotter and harder than any weapon of man ever could.

Butcher cuts his eyes away. “Not here,” he says.

Homelander smiles placidly. “Your wife said that to me the day we made Ryan.”

She didn’t, actually. She didn’t say much of anything, but it gets him what he wants. The crow’s feet around Butcher’s eyes glow the red-hot of cremation furnaces. He scrunches his eyes shut and throws his arm across them for additional safety. But Homelander didn’t revitalize him so they could hold hands and play hopscotch. He sits up on his knees and wrestles that arm away from Butcher’s face to pin it by his side. It had never been his aim to restrain Butcher before. It surprises him by being more of a challenge than he expected.

“Look at me.”

“No, I’m not doin’ this with you here. You pick somewhere’s else.”

He squeezes Butcher’s wrist hard enough to grind the bones to powder if he were still a grimy human. Catching himself at it only makes him tighten his grip so the bones do twinge and creak, just like the chair had. “Where else?” he asks impatiently.

“Anywhere people ain’t.” Butcher lets out a ragged breath and opens his eyes. He wrenches his hand free to crumple the collar of Homelander’s suit. “You wanna fuck, you wanna fight, in whatever order, we don’t do it on your terms. We do it on mine.”

Homelander stares at Butcher’s fist curled against the base of his throat, ears ringing. Heat rising in his face.

“Well, ain’t that damnedest thing?” Butcher purrs, leaning in close to brush his beard against Homelander’s cheek, mouth set close against his ear. “I can see your blood moving faster.”

With his one hand still clutching Homelander’s suit, Butcher cups Homelander’s chin in his palm and pushes. Homelander hits the floor hard enough the cabinets shake and the tile cracks beneath his shoulders. The refrigerator rattles, the thin metal door unlatching and swinging open. 

Butcher crouches over him, dark eyes leering and bright as ever. 

“Pick a place,” he says. “And think about all the ways you want to take my dick when we get there.”

He holds his hand out, and Homelander passes it up to superspeed out of the apartment, out of the city, and out of the atmosphere, unable to shake the feeling that he’s started something he doesn’t completely understand. A piece of himself he’s gone his whole life trying to murder. A part he thought he had murdered.

But he’s unkillable. Maybe that side of him is unkillable, too.

 

/

 

Butcher goes into work in the morning. What else is he gonna do? He’s curious to see if anyone will notice. It’s been nearly two weeks since he last had the juice to walk into their base of operations and make like his brain wasn’t gone entirely to mush. 

The doc gave him a year optimistically, and that had really been too generous. The inside of four months had seen him wishing for death. A year would have been agony, but it’s not agony now. 

Now, it’s eating a PayDay in two bites in the lift up to their floor because the pain of chewing don’t give him aura migraines. Because swallowing food means he tastes food, rather than sputum mixed with pus and bile.

Peanuts have never tasted better.

He steps out of the lift at the appropriate floor, hears all the telltale ambient noises of an office late on a Tuesday morning; hears Frenchie speaking in that slightly disjointed way that means he’s simultaneously signing, and the punctuated silence following that means Kimiko’s either answering him or giving him that look Butcher’s sometimes seen her give in lieu of a technical response; he hears MM tapping his fingers on his desk and shuffling papers around until, pausing, he huffs a tired breath and holds out his hands to accept something — and Butcher can hear in the gentle steaming air near his hands that it’s coffee; he hears Hughie liberating another cardboard coffee cup from the drink tray to give to Frenchie, and then Kimiko; he hears Annie January pulling up a chair next to MM’s side of the room, and he hears her smile when MM, hesitating, pushes his hand through the piles on his desk so everything mixes, so it touches, just at the edges, just slightly out of place.

Butcher’s still just standing at the door to the lift, his head down, listening. He doesn’t realize he’s doing it, that he hasn’t moved, until footsteps approach.

It’s Hughie, come to toss the empty drink tray. He doesn’t notice Butcher right away, nearly circles back without looking up, but then his shoe scrapes the concrete and he turns, eyes a suspicious kind of bright when he sees that it’s Butcher.

A way no one who wasn’t family’s ever looked at him.

“Oh, hey. You’re back!” Hughie laughs a little, like he’s pleasantly surprised. Like Butcher ain’t been a constant and reliable pain in his arse ever since he walked into that little techie store and put him on this path in the first place. “When you didn’t show after a week, we all took bets on where you were.”

“Petit Hughie!” Frenchie calls from the bullpen around this kitty-corner hiding Butcher from view. “Are you talking to the garbage?” he asks, rounding the corner to see for himself. “Ah, oui, I see that you are.”

“Frenchie,” Butcher says coyly.

“Monsieur Charcutier.” He dips his head, adding, somewhat mournfully, “We thought you had died.” Pulling his wallet from his pocket, he says, even more mournfully, “Son of a bitch,” and hands Hughie a tenner. Then, turning to point at Butcher: “Even when you aren’t here, you fuck me, huh? What is your trick? I must know.”

Butcher remembers the look on Homelander’s face last night. Remembers, I’ve been thinking about fucking you, and, Don’t you want to know if you can?

“Guess I just got that X-factor, don’t I?” he muses.

Frenchie sighs and walks off, muttering. Butcher waits, and sure enough, there goes MM.

“Aww. Shit!”

Hughie, still watching him, smiles widely at Butcher’s answering smirk. “We missed you, man.”

Butcher tips his head, eyebrows raised.

“Okay, okay,” Hughie relents, cheeks pinking up to add a foundation for his guilty laugh. “I missed you. I’m glad you’re back and that you’re okay. Sorry, by the way, that I never came to check on you. It’s just, the last time I thought I was helping you—”

“’S all right,” Butcher says, soft. More than he means to, and he knows Hughie hears it, can see it written all over his face, so he clears his throat to add, “‘Sides, weren’t much to look at. Kidney stones. Fuckin’ brutal.”

Hughie winces. “Yikes. Sure you’re all right? My dad gets those, and he’s outta commission for like a month.”

“Right, and where’d you lot think I fucked off to the last two weeks then?”

“You haven’t been gone two weeks. Although, yeah. You looked pretty miserable the last time I saw you. Butcher, really, I should’ve at least called. I’m sorry about that, it’s just—”

“I’ve been a right git, Hughie. I know. You don’t gotta explain it.”

“No, that’s…” He sighs, looking down at his shoes. Beaten up Chuck Taylors, red like his always-bleeding heart, and like the tips of his ears. “Butcher…over here.”

He hover-hands Butcher’s elbow and makes like to walk him further down the hall where the loos are. It’s anyone’s guess if Annie and Kimiko can hear as far as this empty corridor. Butcher’s never talked to either of them long enough to figure out how much further their abilities go. Never thought it was worth his time to know exactly how many more ways they’d all be fucked in a second if either Hughie’s or Frenchie’s girls decided the Boys weren’t worth pissing on to put out a fire.

And now here he is. The only thing corroding away inside him is a whole PayDay. No more of that black shite has come out of his nose, ears, or other heretofore unnamed orifices since last night.

I’m trying to talk to you about fucking you with my dick here, he remembers Homelander saying.

“Look, man. I thought…I really thought — God, how stupid is this to say — I really thought that if I went and checked on you, then…then that’d be that. Like Schrödinger’s cat, right? As long as I didn’t look, as long as I didn’t open the box, then…then you had more time.”

Hughie holds his arms out and shrugs, a pinched look on his face that tightens even more into a wince.

“God, that is stupid. That’s really stupid. I’m sorry. That’s—” He sighs, frustrated, and by gradual degrees, more and more flustered at his own frustration. “That’s what I was gonna say in the first place. That…that the last time I thought I was helping you, the last time we thought we were helping, that we were doing the right thing, the only thing, you got…” Like he can’t say it, and maybe he can’t; Hughie’s always been pliable in a lot of ways, but not usually when it comes to twisting the knife, not if there ain’t a scheme in place to justify it. “I mean, Jesus, I only know how bad it was because you edged me out. So when you got sick, when you…when you didn’t come in to work, I…I thought…”

Butcher gives him a little bit of mercy. He gives it to him the only way he knows how, by cuffing Hughie on the arm, by smirking rather than smiling, and telling him a pretty lie.

“Pulled through, didn’t I?”

It catches on Hughie’s edges, on all the places where he knows now, and where Butcher’s always known, that he’s Lenny, breathed into a new body. Like an organ transplant of the spirit. Just like Lenny would’ve, Hughie starts to smile. It sits on his face nervously, not trusting the soil to bear fruit from seeds.

All in all, Butcher doesn’t know what it is that gives him away. He doesn’t know what Hughie sees in him, or what Lenny did, for that matter, that makes him care if the things Butcher says are true or absolute horse shite.

But Hughie does care, and now, as per usual, he doesn’t let it slide. 

He grabs Butcher’s wrist viper-quick, right over the healed chain of bruises from where Homelander gripped him tight. They didn’t last the night, but Butcher felt those smudges all night and into the morning. He feels them now.

“Hughie,” Butcher starts.

“Butcher,” Hughie says, daring him. All light and furious loyalty and trust, he clamps down hard enough that his blunt fingernails dig into Butcher’s skin. Staking a claim the only way he feels he can, if Butcher’s going to be a cunt about it — and, well, it is Butcher, after all; Hughie’s only playing to his audience.

“Let go, mate.”

“Make me.” Something that can only be pain ripples across his face, shines in his eyes like betrayal, and like angry tears. Like even though he’s the one holding Butcher here and forcing him to have this conversation, forcing himself not to keep his eyes closed against it. “What did you do?”

He remembers the leap of joy in his chest, engulfing the whole of his heart, at the sight of Homelander stumbling, cheek warmed and smudged by dirt and by Butcher, by his own fucking power turned against him, hurting him when not even a warhead could.

Remembers, What have you done?

Remembers, Ask me to roll away the stone.

Butcher leans in close to say, quietly, “I climbed out of the box.” He lays his free hand over the top of Hughie’s wrist, not to grab him, not to do anything, just to stake his own claim right back.

Turns out, Hughie does know him pretty well. Poor lad.

“You gotta let me go, Hughie.”

Hughie breathes in sharply through his nose. “Butcher—”

“Son,” he says, still in that same soft, soft voice. “It’s all right.”

“Just tell me what you did,” Hughie persists, through his teeth and through a wave of emotion that wreaks havoc on his nervous system.

Butcher doesn’t quite have a handle on the X-ray vision as of yet. It comes and goes, and he’s keen on keeping it that way as long as his one other confirmed power is shooting lasers anywhere he’s looking. Margin for error’s not very forgiving there, and he likes Hughie’s face in one piece. Likes all of him in one piece, preferably unhurt, though the kid’s a glutton for punishment on the best of days.

“Tell me, and we’ll fix it, okay?” Hughie says, voice low and urgent, pleading. His hand on Butcher’s wrist doesn’t budge. “All of us, we’ll figure out a way.”

“Hughie—”

“You had a near-fatal dose, so any Temp V you’re on now, when you flush it out, you’re gonna crash hard, but if we prepare for that—”

“It ain’t the temporary kind,” Butcher tells him, not unkindly. He can’t find it in himself to be anything but patient when Hughie’s giving him that look; that look Butcher can never not know means, Don’t go, Billy. Stay. Please stay.

Hughie stares at him, uncomprehending, and then his eyes widen just a smidge. His grip on Butcher’s wrist slackens to match the falling-down look in his face.

With that bit of give, Butcher eases himself out of Hughie’s hold.

“But you hate Supes,” Hughie murmurs, not getting it.

“Temp V was only ever a half-measure, and a shite one at that. I should never have got you mixed up in it. I’m sorry.”

“How did you—” Hughie shakes his head, not listening. “Where did you even…? Annie had to steal Kimiko’s dose from Vought Tower. Who do you know that could’ve…?” He blinks and shakes his head like he’s got water in his ears, the concern cooling over to give way to hard suspicion in his eyes. “Was it Mallory?”

Butcher sidesteps him to make for the elevator. He’s at the button when Hughie storms over and claps his hand over the callbox.

“Butcher’s on Compound V!” he shouts.

It just fucking figures that Hughie would be the one to Intervention him. Annie and MM walk around the corner first, Frenchie and Kimiko hanging back to peek around at them.

Crossing her arms in front of herself, Annie says, with as much judgement and disdain as can politely be expressed in a word, “Wow.”

“I second that,” MM says tightly, mirroring her stance and tone exactly. “What,” he enunciates, visibly restraining himself, “the fuck, Butcher? This is your move? We fight to take down Vought — take down the Supes — for years; you nearly destroy your body trying to compete with these assholes, trying to team up with fucking Soldier Boy, like he wasn’t every bit the piece of shit that Stormfront was; and now you want this shit — this shit you told me we could stop if I just let every good thing in my life go right down the fuckin’ drain — to be permanent? Are you fucking kidding me?”

Butcher gives him room to say more if there’s more. He gives the lot of them room to jump in and dogpile if they’re of a mind, but no one says anything.

“I’ll only correct you on one thing, mate. ‘Scuse me, Hughie.” He presses the call button for the lift and rolls his shoulders, loosening up the knots in his back. Strangest thing. He feels fine otherwise. “See, I didn’t nearly destroy me body; I did destroy it. Spent every night this last week prayin’ I’d die just so’s it’d be over, and you know who was listening, MM? Take three guesses.”

He shakes his head, too pissed off to consider the question, but it’s Annie who drops her arms at her sides, her face going white and hollowed out with fear.

“Yeah,” Butcher says. “Him.”

“Wait, wait, wait.” Hughie bars his arm across the lift when it pings, stopping Butcher from walking into it and leaving this doomed conversation behind. “Him, who?”

“Homelander,” Annie tells him, a look of agony carved out of her face. “God, of course he did! He’s completely taken over Vought. He murdered someone in broad daylight, and people cheered.” She holds her hand out to Butcher. “He killed the rest of his playthings. He couldn’t lose you, too.”

Butcher doesn’t argue with her. It’s not worth getting into semantics, and besides, fucking, fighting, and fucking and fighting are types of play. Can be, anyway, in the right hands. Or in the wrong ones.

It’s not just him who’s had to learn this lesson.

Frenchie says, “Then you are of value to him.” He peels himself away from the wall to get a better look at Butcher, to see if he can spot the differences, like he’s one of them Highlights magazines in a hospital waiting room. “This is the way he wants you. But why?”

“‘Cuz the alternative’s me dead, or braindead anyway, and then I’m of no use to anyone.”

Kimiko steps forward, putting herself in between Frenchie and Butcher. She motions to take his hand, reminding him of Mesmer and his broken arm. Whiny cunt.

He gives her his hand, and she palpitates his palm and the back of his hand with both of hers, then his wrist, and then his forearm, and the twist of his elbow. A wrinkle forms between her eyebrows. She lets go of him, and turns to Frenchie, signing something like pinching salt over a plate of eggs.

Frenchie sighs and scratches his forehead with his thumbnail. “She says you do not have bendy straws for arms. Merci, mon coeur.”

Kimiko takes her phone from her back pocket and types rapidly. She flips it around for Butcher to read.

 

Can you think of a reason homelander would want you to be harder to break? 

 

He grits his teeth and tries not to react otherwise, but it’s no use. Kimiko raises her eyebrows at him, sniffing out his bullshit. She’s expressive, always has been, and perceptive to boot, and the look she’s giving him couldn’t possibly communicate, Pussy, any more clearly than if she’d been shouting it.

Sighing, he says, “Yeah, all right, he wants to battle it out. Me and him, nothin’ held back.”

“That’s it?” Hughie says, taking his arm away from the lift. “What, he got a taste for getting his ass kicked when we…” His eyes dart over to Annie, understanding dawning completely and irreversibly. “Oh, my God, that’s it, isn’t it?”

“Hughie,” Butcher says, pinching the bridge of his nose.

“He wants to get pushed around. He wants you to push him around.” And then, sounding disappointed, “Is this just a sex thing?”

“Wouldn’t be the first time for him, would it?” Butcher mutters.

MM unfolds his arms from around his chest to plant his hands on his hips. “Butcher, did you…trade the Compound V, for sex?”

“I traded it for a clean bill of health. That’s why I did it. This gives me one last shot at him. One last time to really make it count. One of us dies, or we both do, and either way, it’ll have been worth it. All right?”

Into the fraught silence, Frenchie says, “That is a very literal and morbid interpretation of la petite mort.”

“Oh, for Christ’s sake, I ain’t fucking him!”

“You’re not?” Hughie asks, wondering aloud. He straightens out when everyone looks at him. “I mean, obviously…you’re not. That would be wrong, and gross.” Shoring up his confidence to say what he’s actually thinking, Hughie shocks them all by saying, “It’s just, what if you did?”

Chapter End Notes

Edit: 1/15/23

Some people seem to believe that I should tag Pa Campbell/Billy Butcher if I have intentions of mashing them together like barbie dolls, and so, here we are.

You read that right. Butchdaddy reigns supreme in my heart. I do what I want.

Chapter 2: bound for Nineveh

“Hughie,” MM says, calmly, almost philosophically. “Are you insane?”

“No, hear me out!” he stammers, trying to talk over the incredulity on everyone’s faces. A look passes over his face in the shocked quiet, and he waves his hand for them to follow him into the bullpen. He keeps talking, at a louder volume and in a tone like he’s making a grocery list in his head while his mouth runs on autopilot. “Since you’re a supe now, you need, like a compatible…partner.” He drops into a chair and boots up a laptop. “Someone who won’t, y’know, uh…disintegrate when — you have a moment of, uh, sexual…ardor. With your dick.”

“Mon dieu,” Frenchie gasps, with a scandalized look at Annie. “What kind of perilous sex do the two of you have?”

Annie rolls her eyes, about to snark off a response when Kimiko raises her hands, effectively cutting her off. She bumps the outside edges of her hands so her pinky fingers line up, scoops her pointer finger up, and drops her fingers in the shape of a beak into her palm. Tipping her head in Frenchie’s direction, she bumps the outside blades of her hands again and mimes taking a bite with one hand from the loop of her thumb on the other.

“She says…” Frenchie trails off, coloring.

“Don’t wanna share with the class there, Frenchie?”

Kimiko gives Butcher a look, more ‘Asshole’ than ‘Pussy’ this time. She slips her phone from her back pocket and types, for Annie’s eyes only. Butcher snorts, and somehow, Frenchie gets even redder in the face. Whatever her question had been, Annie smiles and nods.

“Of course,” she says, just as sweet as apple pie.

Resurfacing from the manifesto he’d been typing, Hughie says, nodding at the screen for Butcher to look: “To each his own, right? You gotta be happy however it makes sense for you to be happy.”

Butcher peers over his shoulder to read.

 

Homelander wants some, but I’d bet money he probably doesn’t even understand why. So give him a fight, give him all you’ve got to keep him interested, to make it something he doesn’t even know he wants. And then, when you top America’s favorite supe, we leak the video to his douchebag alt-right fanbase. World’s most powerful man is a fascist asshole with mommy issues who fucked a literal Nazi, and people forgave him, but no one will take him seriously for getting raw-dogged by some rugged guy with big hands and bass in his voice. It’s not guaranteed to work, and you’d have to fuck a terrible person, maybe multiple times, but like you said. You have a shot here to come after him.

Think you can get your dick hard for that chance?

 

“That’s fuckin’ diabolical, Hughie,” he murmurs, pleased as punch at the audacity of the proposal. Even a bit disappointed he didn’t think of it first.

“Yeah,” Hughie says, in that same multitasking voice. At the same time that he types ‘SUPER-HEARING!!!’ on muscle memory alone, he adds, “Because someone you don’t have to hold back with, that’s a turn-on for you. As a Supe.”

Only half-paying attention to the parameters of the pretend question, Butcher nods. “Sure is,” he mumbles, gripping Hughie’s shoulders in his hands — gently, he reminds himself — and giving ‘em a brisk rub. “Tight and bendy.” He gives Hughie a kitten-slap in the cheek, a little to give him a hard time but mostly because he’s happy with him. “That‘s my type.”

MM coughs, splattering an aborted swallow of coffee down his hand and wrist. Annie pats him on the back. 

Kimiko gives Frenchie a knowing look and does that same edge-to-edge hand sign like she’s holding a winning hand of poker in front of her face. Moony-eyed, Frenchie nods, and repeats it. He adds another sign to cap it off, like he’s plucking a bit of lint off the side of his hand with his thumb and one finger.

Butcher decides to leave it be. Just now, he’s got bigger fish to fry.

“Gonna need a kit if I’m doin’ this,” he mutters, already casting around for supplies and something, at least a grocery bag, to carry his necessities in.

Hearing a phrase he recognizes, that he hasn’t heard from Butcher in years, Frenchie perks up. He’s already moving, rooting around under MM’s desk, rummaging — MM, also remembering the old buzzword, nearly drops his stained cardboard coffee cup diving after him.

“Boy, I know you’re not gonna give him something of mine!”

Hughie scoots his chair back from the desk, trying to see what they’re looking for.

Frenchie throws his hands. “You keep a gym duffle under your desk! C’est parfait for a fuck-bag!”

“A what?” Annie asks, scrunching her nose up.

“An overnight bag,” MM clarifies, wrestling the duffle bag away from Frenchie when he comes up holding it like a captured flag.

Unfazed, Butcher liberates a mostly clean bin liner from one of the empty receptacles near the lift. He doesn’t like his prospects if all he’s got is the office to scavenge, but he also doesn’t know how long he has before Homelander shows up looking to spirit him away. Fridge is a good bet in any case, so he checks that first, sweeping the entire top shelf of water bottles into the plastic bag. Maybe someone’ll loan him a condom.

“Dude,” Hughie says, swapping Butcher’s repurposed trash bag for a backpack he started bringing around back when Neuman turned out to be Head-Popper.

Butcher holds the backpack open like a trick-or-treat bag, and Hughie dumps the water bottles in.

“Listen, do you, uh…I mean.” Hughie rubs the back of his neck, the emptied liner drooping from his hand like a white flag. He clears his throat. “Do you need…tips? For how to get through this?”

All he has time to do is raise his eyebrows before Frenchie whistles to catch Hughie’s attention. They both look over to see Frenchie making the universal sign for ix-nay.

“Think I got it, Hughie,” he says, charmed, a bit, that Hughie wants to help.

But Hughie must think he’s talking big just to save face because he doesn’t back down. He says, “Right, I know you’re up for it, mentally or whatever, but physically? Do you know what to expect?”

Butcher gives him a blandly sweet look. Spotting Frenchie and MM smirking behind Hughie’s back, he says, “Oy, you two. Want some of this, do ya?”

MM holds up his hands, and Frenchie pockets his.

“It is not Monsieur Charcutier’s first rodeo.”

“He’s got this, Hughie,” MM adds, nodding. “Really.”

Hughie swings his gaze back around to Butcher, consideration in his eyes. Butcher stays on his task of raiding the fridge for provisions. He’s gonna need sustenance to get his energy back once the deed’s done.

“This blowin’ your mind, son?” Butcher says, traipsing along to the loo next. “Mouthwash, toothpaste,” he mutters, counting off items as he finds them, keeping mental stock of everything. He checks another drawer but doesn’t find anything else of use. “Frenchie, you got a Z-pak I can have?”

“Do you think you will need it?” he asks, mystified, but he’s already looking through his desk when Butcher walks out of the bathroom.

Butcher catches the foil tray of tablets when Frenchie lobs his way. “Among other things.”

MM sighs gustily, opens a drawer in his desk to produce a pre-packaged toothbrush and wet wipes, and walks over to drop them in Butcher’s bag. 

“Very nice.” He gives the bag a shake, peering down into it. “That oughta do it.”

“Uh, no, that will not do it,” Annie says abruptly, stepping away to dig around in Hughie’s things. She comes up with a little tub of coconut oil and holds it up for them to see. “Unless anyone’s got a better lube alternative?”

Butcher holds the backpack open for the makeshift lube when no one speaks up. He catches sight of Frenchie and Kimiko signing again before she breaks away to go find something. She comes back with a ratty old hoodie someone left flung over the back of a chair and never reclaimed, holding it out for Butcher to take. It’s not particularly handy as a post-fucking essential, but he understands the thought behind it. Change of clothes can’t hurt, besides.

“I think I’ve got a pair of sweatpants around here somewhere,” Hughie says, having the same idea. He wanders off to look.

“Good idea,” Butcher says, squashing the hoodie down so a little more will fit on top of it. He’s gonna have to turn the bloody thing inside out to get at the more useful toiletries at the bottom, but he can deal with a bit of inconvenience as long as he’s got everything he needs.

“Man, give it here,” MM mumbles, holding his hand out to take the bag off him. He takes the bag with him to empty it and pack it from the beginning.

Butcher stands back, not wanting to get in MM’s way. Annie stands next to him.

“You’re sure you’re all right with this?” she asks in an undertone.

“Why wouldn’t I be?”

She looks at him archly. “Seriously?”

He shrugs, genuinely not seeing the problem. “Fucked people I didn’t like before. What’s the difference?”

There’s a split second of noise Butcher doesn’t have a referent for, and then, from the windows, a voice says, “How charming.”

Butcher turns his head, keeping his stance loose like he’s been reminding himself to do since he came up here. The whole of his body’s a weapon now if he ain’t careful, so he’s going out of his way to keep himself loose and relaxed. Over by the wall, standing at parade rest with his cape billowing in the breeze, is who else but Homelander. The sound Butcher heard must’ve been the window scraping open in a fraction of a second.

“That window’ll never sit right now,” Butcher admonishes.

“You’re right,” Homelander agrees, simpering in how he does it. He lays his gloved hand against the glass, soft like a caress. “Silly me.”

Groaning, the glass splinters, and then shatters.

Butcher doesn’t let himself flinch. Doesn’t let himself look away. Just holds his hand out, trusting someone’ll give him what he needs. He can tell by the shuffling of feet alone that Hughie hands the bag to MM, who gives it to Annie, who gives it to him. He swings the strap around his shoulder and advances toward the window, broken glass crunching underneath his boots. Homelander watches him, eyes sharp. 

“What is that?” he asks, eyes honing in below Butcher’s throat, roving along the slope of his chest. 

“’S nothin,” Butcher mutters, rolling his shoulders. “Got a kink, that’s all.”

“No,” Homelander says. “You don’t get kinks.” 

His razor sharp eyes zero in, probably assessing a muscle group or patch of nerves with the X-ray vision; Butcher recognizes the look. Whatever Homelander sees knit into Butcher’s meat, his eyes light up. He catches Butcher by the join of his neck and shoulder and flings him clear through the obliterated window, screams trailing him like the report of a gun.

The world rushes up around him, air whipping in his ears and at his face, blinding him. His legs windmill up over his head, and the dead drop paired with his being upside down twinges the ache in his shoulder. He tenses, thinking it’s a muscle spasm or a cramp, but then he jerks to a sudden stop in mid-air, still upside down, his jacket falling up around his ears. Straining, he bends at the waist, expecting to see Homelander, twat, holding him at the ankle to break his fall, but all he sees is open air before his center of gravity, or lack thereof, wobbles, and puts him in a spin. He white-knuckles his grip on the backpack strap, lest he drop it the remaining four stories into the empty side alley below.

“Fuckin’ hell,” he gasps, breathless, letting himself dangle so he can regain some kinda equilibrium.

Above, Homelander floats aimlessly to where Butcher’s hanging, right-side up and in complete control. He drifts down to be eye-level with Butcher, a faint smile on his pale, bloodless face. 

“Wonderful,” he murmurs, catching Butcher’s cheek in his hand. “I knew you could.”

“Makes one of us, don’t it?” Butcher grunts. It’s hard to talk with all the blood rushing to his head.

Humming, Homelander closes the distance and kisses Butcher hard on the mouth. Butcher don’t have time to react before the weightlessness dislodges and kickstarts him into falling again. Homelander catches him, guides him so his body swings around, feet-down, and this time licks into his mouth. Butcher bites him, missing Homelander’s tongue but not by much. He catches Homelander’s lip between his teeth, and swallows the low groan it earns him.

No one will take him seriously for getting raw-dogged by some rugged guy with big hands and bass in his voice, he thinks, in Hughie’s voice. Think you can get your dick hard for that chance?

He does, yeah. ‘Course, he’s operating on more than just a hunch, now.

“Pick a place, you said,” Homelander croons, inching away just far enough to be able to speak. “Up in Dresden, there’s a defunct power plant way out in the sticks. Do you know it?”

“What, Arcadia?” Butcher asks, not bothering to hide how rough his voice has gotten.

“That’s the one.” Homelander relaxes his grip, letting Butcher fall slightly until he catches himself, until he settles without slipping. “Don’t keep me waiting.”

That’s all the warning he gets before Homelander vanishes in a crash of air and motion. Butcher glances up at the broken window where his five co-conspirators are stood looking out at him. Kimiko waves. Butcher salutes back, about to give chase when he realizes he forgot to ask for one crucial item when he was prepping to go on this suicide fuck-mission.

“‘Fore I go! Any of you got a spare condom or three?”

Hughie pats his pockets, but Annie’s quicker than he is. She finds his wallet first and flings a few out the open window. They flutter down, but Butcher catches them one after the other out of the air.

“Appreciate your contribution!” he calls up, turning in the air with some difficulty, bound for the countryside. 

It’s a lot of trial and error spent hurtling through empty space, flying is. He gets flipped around the wrong way and has to find something to grab onto to get his head above his feet, and more than once, he crashes into buildings he forgets to watch out for. It still beats the commute by Greyhound, and cab fare from Syracuse to Dresden would’ve been fucking criminal, but Jesus Christ. 

All told, it takes him two hours to reach Yates County, and from there, it’s another twenty minutes to Arcadia. He feels wind chafed and he’s pretty sure he ate a bug somewhere over Scranton, but he makes it.

“Fuck me running,” he grits out, once he sees the telltale spires of the plant jutting out from the treetops. He throws his kit clear and drops down hard, cratering the grassy earth beneath him.

He looks around for Homelander, half expecting to be air-bombed from above, until he sees him standing out over the water. His back’s to Butcher, arms crossed behind him. Seems the only way he knows how to be by himself is at attention, command-ready. Butcher dusts himself off as best he can and walks to the water’s edge, hands on his hips.

“Been waiting long?” he says, not bothering to raise his voice.

But Homelander just turns his head, saying, “What’s that? I didn’t hear you.”

Must think he’s making a point with this. Drawing Butcher out to him like a puppet on strings; so they’ll both be hovering out over the water like assholes. Butcher goes.

He dunks his feet a few times trying to balance, trying not to break the surface tension of the water with his weight, even plunges in down to his knee once. Homelander looks over his shoulder at him and rolls his eyes, like Butcher’s doing it on purpose. Irritation gets him tense in the same way he’d been catching himself out of thin air, and there he goes rising out over the water, sheets of it cascading down around him once he’s clear.

Homelander turns to look out over the water again, ignoring Butcher’s approach entirely. “I was beginning to wonder if you’d taken a bus,” he says.

Butcher shrugs. “Didn’t know I could fly till you threw me out that window.”

“You’re welcome, by the way.”

This time Butcher rolls his eyes. “Are we doin’ this or not?”

“I just have one stipulation to add. A trifle, really.” Just the toes of his boots skating along the water, giving off the faintest ripples, Homelander circles Butcher as he speaks: “You said we’d do this on your terms, if we did it at all. I told you to meet me, and here you are.”

For the brief few seconds that he’s directly behind Butcher, goose pimples break out all across his flesh. As soon as Homelander’s in his sights again, they lock eyes. 

Still, he circles Butcher like a hawk circling a sparrow.

“You know what I want because I’ve told you, and I know what you want because I felt your dick move when I kissed you.” He leers at Butcher, catching his arm when he nearly crashes through the water trying to turn in time with him. “I think you’re on board for anything that happens from this point onwards. Am I right about that?”

“Ain’t like you to ask.”

Homelander smiles, at odds with the tight fist he knots at Butcher’s jacket to hoist him up so they both float some distance above the water. 

Eyes flat like those of a dead fish, he says, sweetly, “I’m asking because I don’t ever want to hear it said that I made you do this. I’m asking because I want to make sure you understand what we’re here to do.”

Butcher shoves him away, both of them circling one another way up high over the lake. “I understand,” he says gravely. “We’ll see if you do.” 

He takes his jacket off, tosses it in the direction of land, and undoes one more button on his Hawaiian shirt. With no change in his expression, Homelander’s eyes tinge red at the corners and then closer and closer toward the center. Butcher matches him.

“Let’s dance, you and I.”

The world ruptures in a wash of red light.

 

/

 

As before, the blast throws them apart. 

Homelander is faster to regain his bearings and catches Butcher while he’s still riding the impact. They crash into the bank, one rolling over the other until Homelander pins Butcher in the mud. He isn’t given long to celebrate that small triumph before Butcher bucks him off and holds him down in two inches of water.

His red sight carves a trench of steam and sizzling lake before Butcher’s hand grips his jaw, forcing his lasers directly up into the sky and then back down into the lake where it fizzles out.

Homelander relinquishes the struggle and catches Butcher’s shirt in his hands to pull him down beneath the surface with him. Having no need of breath himself, he waits for the water to settle to meet Butcher’s eyes with his, flashing him a white grin through all that shimmering green. 

Butcher lets loose a soundless yell into the water and forcefully levitates himself and Homelander out of the water. He remembers this time that he doesn’t need a foundation beneath him to keep moving and sucker punches Homelander across the face. The next hook he tries with, Homelander catches, but he cracks him with a head-butt that breaks his hold completely.

Dazed, and needing space so he won’t show it, he grasps Butcher by the scruff of the neck and tosses him clear of the lake. He takes this landing better, nearly with ease, and tucks and rolls across the grass, sprawling out on his back just like he had when the Compound V had him in its thrall. Homelander dives down after him, leading with his fist, and doesn’t miss Butcher’s head by much. He rains a series of punches down on Butcher’s face, breaking his nose and opening a cut across his eyebrow.

Flashing red teeth in a bloody smile, Butcher hawks and spits right in Homelander’s eyes. It stops him just long enough for Butcher to slam his elbow up into his temple and knock him off-balance. 

He swings himself up on top of Homelander and pummels him relentlessly, hitting like a freight train. Each blow takes a little more out of him. His heart jackhammers in his chest just like Homelander’s does. His breath come short and hard and thready with traces of his voice in each sigh, just like Homelander’s does.

There’s a red angry welt on his cheek where Homelander must’ve gotten him with the red sight. Behind him, the yellow sun blazes, carving his silhouette out of shadow like a bas-relief.

“Fuck,” Butcher says, and dips his tongue in Homelander’s mouth.

Homelander opens up to it, liking the heft and heat of him. The whole of his body goes pliant in a second, reacting without his permission.

Like he knows, like he can feel Homelander straining to have something in his mouth, Butcher trades his tongue for two big calloused fingers and presses down with his thigh. Homelander makes room for him — seeks for him, desirous of friction, of being held down, of being overpowered — and rocks up against him. 

The hand Butcher’s not face-fucking him with grasps at the grassy earth, tearing a hole out of it with just his fist. His knees bear down into the ground, too, creating an impenetrable cage out of his body.

Homelander bucks beneath him, not trying to unseat him, but thrilling all the same when the solid, sturdy body looming over him doesn’t budge. 

Then he does try it, just a little bit, just to feel Butcher’s muscle strain in defiance. He leans up so Homelander will have to look at him and nowhere else, into his searing red gaze. His leg digs in hard over Homelander’s dick, trapping him between too many layers of drenched, filthy fabric.

“You want it?” he growls. “Ask me.”

He moans around the fingers in his mouth, about to do it, about to beg for something he can’t map in words, when orgasm electrifies the base clay of his body.

Aftershocks trace their way along the slopes of his groin, like live currents arcing along a broken power line. 

The quiet feels, to him, complete.

It must to Butcher, too. He’s holding himself still, now, for a different reason than he had been. The outline of his dick against Homelander’s thigh, heavy and insistent, makes his mouth water. He nudges his leg a fraction, less than a hairsbreadth, into that weight, and Butcher meets his eyes, his hand half-hidden in Homelander’s mouth like a horse’s bit.

Quite suddenly, it is too much for him to lay beneath this man in his soiled uniform, his dick flaccid and spent between their bodies, between the barriers of their sodden clothes. Homelander shoves him off and takes flight. He uses the superspeed. There’s no other way. 

He retreats up beyond the clouds first, just to get space. Just to clear the static from his head and the buzzing in his skin. All the while he can’t stop himself from listening, from keeping his ear trained on Butcher, desperate to hear if he touches himself, cataloguing every twitch and labored breath. 

Butcher, heedless of his audience, or maybe knowing full well that Homelander’s watching, twists around to lay on his back. He palms himself through his wet jeans and moves abortively into his hand, warring with himself over whether he can do without relief or not.

Ultimately, he decides he can’t.

The ringing in Homelander’s ears dies down seeing Butcher fumble his pants open to get a hand on himself. His racing heart settles. He regrets that he can’t smell the arousal pricking through Butcher’s skin, that he can’t smell the gathering of semen in the well of his hand. 

But he can still hear the sticky squelching of spit-slicked skin working skin. He can still watch Butcher jerk himself off until he comes all over his hideous Hawaiian shirt, a bitten off cry caught between his teeth and his lower lip. A few drops of white clear his gaping collar and dapple his bare heaving throat. Homelander wishes, viciously, that he could touch down for the sole purpose of lapping it up with his tongue. 

A curse and a cure, indeed.

Homelander vaults himself across the sky back to the city, back to Vought, back to the lodging that’s always been his. It’s more a showroom than a bedroom, but it has a washroom, and everything else he needs to put himself back together again.

There’s caked mud drying in his ear and down his back. He scrubs at it with a washcloth, eyes catching on a spot in the mirror. Head turned, he leans in closer for a better look at the smudged burn seared down the hinge of his jaw. Stopping to pull off one glove, Homelander touches it with the tip of a bare finger. The sharp sting of sensation pulls a hissed noise through his clenched teeth.

He runs a hot shower and scrubs himself raw before stepping into a clean suit. His reflection in he mirror stares back at him, immaculate, but for the abrasion on his jaw. 

With his hand still bare, he presses his fingertips to the glass. He listens for the creaking of glass and metal, so faint as to be nearly inaudible, and crumples his hand, caving in the mirror, caving in the evidence of his own weakness.

Composure firmly in place, Homelander flies back out to Dresden.

Butcher’s since stripped naked to float on his back in the water. From up high, it looks a deep indigo. Forcefully held under the shallows, it had seemed a pale green.

Out of his clothes, Butcher looks slighter than he’d felt holding him down. He looks human.

Homelander begins his descent, as quiet as death, and every bit as decisive. Butcher still notices him, perhaps in the flicking of his cape, and gives him the smallest glance from the corner of his eye in acknowledgement. Homelander lets his feet touch the ground.

“I want this again.”

“What’s that?” Butcher mutters, raising a hand to his ear. “Didn’t hear ya.”

Homelander bites his tongue, feeling the red creep in at the edges of his eyes. He presses his fingers against his eyelids.

Hellbent on holding his ground, he drops his hand and steps away from the muddy bank. Butcher brought a bag with him from the city, and he goes to retrieve it, scanning it with the X-ray vision before he goes as far as opening it. He packed a good amount of water, a mismatched change of clothes, even condoms. The forethought surprises him.

He knew someone was going to get fucked today, but he didn’t think as far as supplies or preparation. Now that he’s thinking about it, though, spit and accelerated healing would’ve only gotten them so far. It hadn’t occurred to him at all that it might hurt, or that anything could.

With a water bottle in one hand, he drops the backpack and unscrews the cap to take a drink. He stops with the bottle halfway to his lips. 

Butcher walks out of the lake running a hand through his wet hair to get it out of his eyes. “Oh, cheers,” he says, taking the opened bottle from Homelander and drinking half of it one pull. “What’s that you were saying?”

Biting his cheek, Homelander doesn’t answer. For a white-hot moment, he can’t.

Tilting his head, Butcher smirks and grabs his chin in his hand. “Well, would you look at that?” he muses, turning Homelander’s head to display the welt on his jaw to match the one on his cheek. “Don’t you look pretty all marked up? Like a bird at the junior prom. D’ya know what?” He lets go of Homelander’s face but steps closer so he can speak against his cheek, into the corner of his mouth. “I can see why you’d wanna have another go.”

Homelander does what he wouldn’t allow himself the luxury of when they were rutting like animals in the grass. He takes Butcher’s dick in his hand, thinking for a moment that he’ll rip it off, that he’ll smother the coals now before the fire engulfs him again, stronger than ever. But it’s less than an urge, how the image presents itself. It’s not something he can even pretend to want.

Dark eyes twinkling, Butcher’s mustache twitches in time with his smile. “I wish you would,” he muses. “Cut out the last thing you got left, lets you be every bit as pissed off and fucking vile as you really are. Go on. I’ll be a good boy and sit still for it.”

Back teeth aching from how hard he’s clenched them together, Homelander doesn’t do it. He doesn’t let go, but he doesn’t tear Butcher’s dick from his pelvis like an errant weed. Butcher tips his chin, utterly at ease in nothing but his sun-warmed skin. Homelander can feel him through the glove, but it’s not the same. It never really is. 

“I want this,” Homelander tells him, like a secret, because it is, the only one he has left anymore. He twists his grip, savoring the gradients in Butcher’s answering grunt. “Again.”

“Yeah, all right,” Butcher says, a muscle in his burnt cheek twitching.

“Tell me,” he whispers, running his thumb over the swelling head of Butcher’s dick. “That you want it, too.”

Breathing in cautiously through his nose, like this is more perilous to bear than the threat of castration, Butcher nods stiffly. Homelander wrenches his hand hard enough to startle a cry from Butcher’s lips, pain and surprise evident there, but not just. No, not just.

“Like a little rough, don’t you.”

“You’d be hard up, too, if I took off before you could get yours.”

“I heard you take care of it,” Homelander tuts, releasing his hold on Butcher’s dick. Filing away for later the shape and feel of it in his hand, for later. “Nobody likes a whiner, William.”

He rolls his eyes, finishes his water bottle, and starts getting dressed. Homelander catalogues varying smells on each article of clothing: a hint of vanilla clinging to the joggers he pulls on first, childish strawberry fragrance on the hoodie, clean detergent smell on the clean tube socks. The globes of his ass, impressive enough on their own, stand out beneath the textured lettering there. Homelander tips his head, trying to read the writing.

Butcher catches him looking and looks, too. He sighs, apparently seeing the emblazoned ‘BUILDING MY EMPIRE’ for the first time. 

“Goddamn it, Hughie.”

Squinting, Homelander says, “No, those are Starlight’s.”

He catches Butcher looking around for something, his eyes catching on his jacket wadded up by the waterline. Homelander speeds there and back, handing it off before Butcher can react. Even then, he doesn’t react, except to take the jacket and fold it over his arm. 

“Still here then?”

“We haven’t talked logistics.”

Butcher shrugs. “Same time next week?” as if it’s that easy. As if there’s nothing else to say. Maybe there isn’t.

“Fine.”

“One more thing,” Butcher says. “Got a stipulation to add.”

“Which is what?”

“Just this. You start summat, you finish it. Fightin’ or fucking, what starts here gets handled here, up to and including experimental fondlin’. That don’t do for me what it did for you.”

“I’ve heard your request,” Homelander says through a paper-thin smile. “What will you do if I ignore it?”

“Hard to say. Liked my fingers down your throat, didn’t ya?” He palms idly at his dick through the borrowed joggers. “Maybe you’d like it if I stuffed somethin’ else down there.”

Homelander warms about the ears and down his cheeks, speechless with shock, but not just. Not just.

“Like I said,” Butcher says, unfazed. “You finish what you start, or I fuckin’ will.”

He swings the backpack over his shoulder and levitates up off the ground, already far more deft with the motion sway than he had been before. The look on his face watching Homelander on the ground is suspicious.

“How’d you know, anyway? ‘Bout the flyin’?”

“Gravity’s a force acting upon you all the time,” Homelander tells him, lifting off to match Butcher’s height. “There’s a certain tension that comes of resistance. And you don’t get kinks.”

“Huh. Good to know.”

Butcher turns, and still, as if there’s nothing else to say, he leaves. Homelander floats where he is for a solid ten minutes watching him pick a meandering path back to New York City. Once he’s faded from view enough that he can’t be seen without effort, Homelander touches down on the grass and walks up the winding path to the power plant. He steps through the door he broke when he picked this spot for their private use and pulls it shut behind him.

He takes stock of the dusty glass lighting fixtures and faulty wiring, running his gloved hand along the burnished metal wall that reminds him of a battleship’s hull. To his warped reflection, he mouths, “In the belly of the beast.”

His eyes in the dimpled copper surface are indecipherable. That’s all right with him.

Chapter 3: like lightning from heaven

Chapter Notes

See the end of the chapter for notes

Becca would’ve loved being able to fly. 

She hadn’t needed powers to be twice as smart or three times as crafty as even the trickiest of bastards Butcher’s dealt with in his time, but to have them anyway woulda made her happy.

Like junk TV or shite girl bands or draggin’ him along to do both with her made her happy.

She would’ve had fun, if she could be up here. Most things went that way in his head since he lost her this last time, lost her for real, since he’d never really let himself believe she was gone the first time. And she hadn’t been, and what a waste those eight years of his life had been. But not Becca’s. She’d been okay without him, and she would be still, if it’d been him in the ground and her up here in the sky.

Becca would’ve been happy. She would’ve found a way back to it, because of him or in spite of him, but she would’ve done it. Because she was a reckoning all her own and always had been. Because she knew she deserved better, and she weren’t never the type to wait for someone else to give it to her if she could do it herself.

It’s why he loved her and why he always will, even when he’s dust and the world keeps spinning.

Because if she were here, she’d be holding onto him for dear life, laughing at Butcher when he got bugs in his teeth. Because if she were here, she’d ask him if he was all right, and he’d tell her the truth.

Because thinking about her keeps his mind off the look in Homelander’s eyes, or the orgasms between them.

She came three times, he remembers Homelander telling him.

There’s something in there, probably, but he can’t make himself look at it yet. Can’t let himself get too close to the memory of jerking off to fucking Homelander. He can’t.

Thing is? He meant what he said to Annie. He’s fucked people he didn’t like. He’s fucked people he hated, who’ve tried to kill him or otherwise ruin his life. He’s even fucked supes, courtesy of Maeve, but none of those notches in his belt come close to this one in terms of sheer magnitude.

Homelander’s his own class and category. Homelander’s his own goddamn planet.

And Butcher don’t care. He don’t fucking care. It’s just the wind biting at his eyes that’s makin’ ‘em sting. The wind forces him to touch down on the other side of Seneca Lake. It’s not that he can’t concentrate on flying long enough to keep himself from plummeting down and crash-landing in the middle of an active campsite in a national forest. Bleedin’ wind did it.

“Oh, shit!” someone on the ground says, whipping out a cell phone. “Is he dead?”

Butcher pushes up on his elbows, notices the smashed tent underneath him. His stomach sinks, and he pushes his hands through the debris, searching for blood and finding none. Not anywhere. He sits back on his knees, so swarmed with instantaneous relief that he nearly pukes.

“Christ,” he says, looking around. “Sorry ‘bout that.”

“Where’s the fight, man?” says the kid with the phone. He’d been about to dial a number until Butcher sat up, then he switched to holding it out in front of him, recording. “You’re a superhero, right?”

“I’m really not, mate.”

He pulls himself up from out of the rubble and casts about for his kit.

“So you’re a villain?” the bird with him asks. She’s behind the camera, too. “I didn’t think we had those in America.”

“Listen to his accent, Mindy. He’s, like, Australian, or whatever.”

Butcher sighs, grabs his effects, and gets walking. He rolls his shoulders, trying to get himself back in the air, if he could just focus up. The two kids with the phone stay on him.

“Can’t you fly? What, did you just get your powers yesterday?”

He presses his fingers to his eyes and makes himself breathe. He’s heard MM talk the boys through static before. Always shrugged it off himself, but he’s listened in enough times to get a general picture of how it’s done. Flying, or at least levitating, comes easier, then.

So does shutting out the noise in his head.

When he gets back to the office, Butcher lets himself in through the same window he’d been unceremoniously tossed out of earlier. The lads haven’t had time to replace it yet, so it’s easy work. Hughie spots him and gets to his feet straight away, worried and already wringing his hands. Frenchie and the girls have gone, so it’s just MM with him trying not to fall asleep. At the commotion from Hughie, he lurches in his seat, snuffling.

“Good of you to wait up,” Butcher says lightly. “You eaten yet?”

“Are you all right?” Hughie asks, ignoring him.

“Personally,” Butcher begins, following Hughie’s example, “I’ve had a box of these power bars, and it ain’t done much for me appetite.”

“Butcher,” Hughie complains.

“Frenchie went to grab dinner,” MM says, pausing to yawn and stretch his arms overhead. “Annie and Kimiko went with him. You good?”

“Peachy,” Butcher tells him, swinging Hughie’s backpack around to fish out the factory-sealed condoms. He sprinkles them on Hughie’s desk, watching him reluctantly count them as his face turns a faint shade of green. “You’ll want those for later, I expect.”

“Oh, my God, you went in bareback?” Hughie blanches, appalled, and says, “Oh, my God. He went in bareback?”

“Jesus Christ, Hughie, is that all you think about, mate?”

MM takes one look at Butcher, top-to-bottom, and nods. He grabs his phone off the desk and talks blandly as he types: “You’re gonna have to get your shit tested, you keep fuckin’ around without protection, Butcher. Supe V.D. is real.”

He turns his phone around for Butcher to read his notepad app.

 

He on the hook?

 

“Yes, dear.” Butcher favors him with a smirk. “I’ll make a note to pick up some rubbers next time I do the shopping, shall I?”

“You do that. I still need you to be safe, man,” he says, and holds out a hand to pull Butcher in tight and quick, thumping Butcher between his shoulders. “I don’t like none of this, but I got your back. You know that, right?”

“Yeah.” He palms MM’s neck. “I know.”

A text comes through on MM’s phone. He extricates himself to check it and breaks away for the lifts. 

“They’re back,” MM says. “Frenchie got you fish and chips.”

Butcher groans. 

“You can have my burger,” Hughie tells him, smiling.

“Good man,” Butcher says, perching on the corner of Hughie’s desk as the lift pings to take MM down to the ground floor. “So, young Hugh Campbell’s got tips on how to fuck men?”

“I was mostly just gonna tell you assholes don’t self-lubricate,” he half-laughs, flushed to the tips of his ears.

“Wiser words have never been spoken.”

Hughie perches carefully on the edge of the desk next to Butcher. It’s obvious he’s taking stock, trying to see if anything’s broken, or worse. When there’s no visible damage for him to see, he gives it a rest. But only part of the way.

“Are you okay?” he finally asks. “Like, for real?”

“Why wouldn’t I be?”

“Come on, man.”

Sighing, Butcher taps the brick-outline of Hughie’s phone in his pocket, saying, “Weren’t so bad.”

Hughie narrows his eyes but hands Butcher his phone, waiting for more.

“We got us a standing appointment.” He opens the notepad app and begins typing. “Up in Dresden at a washed up power plant. Arcadia, it’s called.”

He hands the phone off to Hughie, and he reads:

 

We didnt fuck. But he wants it, and he’ll get it.

 

The line of Hughie’s mouth thins, a bolt of tension spiking in his neck. “Arcadia,” he murmurs, typing rapidly. “Funny.”

“Strange name for a power plant,” Butcher agrees aimlessly. 

“No, I meant because you go there for the sole purpose of gladiator-fucking the world’s most dangerous supe,” Hughie says blandly, turning his phone around for Butcher to see. “Not exactly peaceful or idyllic.”

 

I know this was my idea and I know you were all about it when I brought it up, but you’ll say so if that changes, right?

 

“That is ironic.” Butcher tips his head. “Never noticed.”

Hughie nods aimlessly, already typing on a new line. Butcher reads as he goes.

 

I have some ideas about how to monitor your meetup. There are drones on the market with the range we need, even some we could encase in zinc so HL wouldn’t see them. Trouble is, there’s no way to render them completely silent. We’ll have to come up with some way of camouflaging the tech audio with the airspace so it’s as unobtrusive as possible.

 

Of course Hughie’s been working the angles he has, pushing his involvement as far as it goes so Butcher won’t be alone in this plot any longer than he has to be. The proof of it warms him over. And just now, it feels good to feel warm. He palms Hughie’s shoulder and squeezes.

“Butcher,” he tries, gently this time, “are you sure this is what you want?”

“Hughie.”

“No, really. Just…are you? Because — if you are, then I’ll shut up and I won’t bug you about it anymore. But if you aren’t, Butcher, then…then we’ll find some other way to make this work.”

“No, we won’t,” Butcher tells him, giving him gentle right back. 

Hughie starts to rear up, like he does, and Butcher ropes his arm behind Hughie’s neck to steady him.

“This is the asking price. Knew it was when I agreed to it.”

“You were on death’s door, Butcher. No one thinks you did this for shits and giggles.”

“And I didn’t, right?” He nudges Hughie with the arm he’s caught him by. “I did it to have one more crack at the cunt. Now I got that chance, and I got to pay for it. That’s on me. Don’t matter if I want it.”

“And you are sure,” Hughie says carefully. “About that part.”

“I gotta be.”

Jaw clenched, Hughie nods curtly. It’s clear by his eyes he’s unhappy, and that’s lovely, that is. Don’t help him any, but it’s nice to know there’s someone in the world wants to protect him.

The lift pings, and Butcher takes his arm away. Hughie clears his throat, eyes firmly lowered, and sets about clearing a space on one of the free desks for the food. Annie and MM walk in carrying three big paper bags, Frenchie and Kimiko following suit with six drinks balanced between them.

“He persists,” Frenchie observes. “How is your dick? Still attached?”

He thinks of his open invitation for Homelander to rip his dick off and unwillingly recalls the grooves in the leather of Homelander’s glove chafing at his dick. Smirking, he says, “You wanna sample the goods, sweetheart, you gotta get in line.”

“You are insufferable when you fuck men,” Frenchie mutters, rolling his eyes. “I had nearly forgotten.”

“Not me,” MM says, dogpiling on. “I knew this would happen.”

“Oh, I’ll take those,” Hughie tells him, reaching for the fish and chips when MM lifts it out of the bag. “Thanks.”
