“Ta very much,” Butcher adds, catching the wadded up foil-wrapped burger MM tosses him. “And I take offense to that comment.”

“No, you don’t,” MM snorts. “You love all this shit.”

Like a spliced reel of film, his mind runs off on him. He sees Becca’s eyes after Homelander raped her. He sees Homelander’s eyes after Butcher held him down and got him to cream his fancy spandex onesie. 

Remembers, I want this again. Remembers, Tell me that you want it, too.

But Hughie’s watching him, so Butcher keeps his shoulders loose and his grin smarmy.

“’S only a little death, right? Cheers, love.”

Kimiko sets a drink down for him, and hers beside it, a little knit between her eyebrows. She turns to Frenchie and holds up her hands, starting with that same good-hand-of-poker sign and then sliding her right hand down the length of her forearm, still with her palm facing her. Tipping her head at Butcher, she adds another sign he half-recognizes, clamping down on the hinge of her thumb with her dominant hand.

“What’s that mean, then?” Butcher asks, setting aside his still-wrapped burger to put his hands together in an approximation of how she’d done it.

Frenchie stifles a laugh, and Kimiko presses her lips together, like she’d laugh, too, if it weren’t Butcher.

Like a schoolmarm, she holds her dominant hand out in front of her, palm facing her, and pats her chest, then her second hand out in front of her in the same position. Her second hand she holds out in front of Butcher’s chest, though she doesn’t touch him. She holds her hands out in front of her to reset, palms facing her.

“You,” he says, pointing at her dominant hand, and then at her other hand, “and me?”

She shakes her head sharply, holding out her second hand and pointing at Hughie, then MM, then Annie. They’re all quiet, he realizes, watching Kimiko just like Butcher is. She holds out her first hand and points at some middle-distant point.

“Any two people?” Annie asks.

Frenchie rolls his chair over, stars in his eyes, but he don’t say nothing. He lets Kimiko have the floor. She does well with it, too. She nods at Annie’s question and repeats the sign Butcher asked about.

“Oh,” Butcher says, seeing it now. “That’s fucking, ain’t it?”

He repeats the sign, and again Frenchie laughs, this time louder than the first. Kimiko badly conceals a wince and mimes with one hand the sign he’s made, then exaggeratedly straightens her fingers without actually finishing the sign.

“You are saying, chiens,” Frenchie says, holding up his hands, curled like Butcher’s are curled, and bumps them together. “Dogs fucking.”

“Ah, Jesus.” He goes back to his burger. “Well, pardon my fuckin’ French.”

Kimiko breaks out her phone to type and sticks her answer in his face, rather than going through Frenchie.

 

Doesn’t have to be about sex. It’s one body colliding with another.

 

“Like a car crash?” he says in between bites.

She nods, then she gives him that sign from a few minutes ago. Like taking a bite out of her thumb. Where he can watch her do it, she types, That one means pain.

He hums noncommittally, still just shoveling food down his gullet as if seeing that word, from her of all people, don’t give him any kind of pause. She looks at him searchingly, and he don’t look back.

“Anyone got onion rings?” he asks.

“In one of these…oh! Here.” Annie brings him a bag, her eyes catching on sweatpants she’s certainly seen before. “Are those mine?”

“About that.”

“Sorry,” Hughie rushes to say.

“See, I’m sorta free-ballin’ it here, and if you was gonna be wantin’ ‘em back…”

“God, no.” She holds up her hand. “Keep them. Seriously, I burned everything else Vought gave me.”

“Works for me,” Butcher says, scooping up ketchup with an onion ring.

“So we got everything we need to board up the window for tonight,” MM begins. “In the morning, we can get some repairmen up here to fix it properly.”

“Could have ‘em install a doggy door,” Hughie offers brightly.

Butcher wads a napkin up between his hands to get the grease off, then bumps the sides together in Hughie’s direction. He leaves his right hand curled and the left straight.

Soda shoots out of Kimiko’s nose.

“I get that one right?” he asks sweetly.

Coughing, Frenchie says, “That one, Monsieur Charcutier, you did.”

 

/

 

Ryan swaps Rise of the Seven out for Vought: Assemble and sits back down on the couch next to Homelander. He curls up straight away into his shoulder, hunkered down peacefully and quietly to watch the movie. 

It took getting used to. The notion that someone could touch him for want of nothing but to be close to him, to be held by him, was not one he had ever had any reason to entertain, much less have whenever he wanted it. But Ryan, maybe because he’s still young enough to want hugs from his dad, or because he’s always wanted any father figure in his life, makes it possible.

Homelander knows what his father would say. He knows what that relic of a forgotten era would think, how his lip would curl. The casual cruelty he’d spew upon his soft, disappointing progeny.

He wraps his arm around Ryan instead, asking him, “Who’s your favorite?”

Without any hesitation, Ryan says, “Black Noir’s cool.”

An ache like crying tugs hard at the back of his throat. He nods and doesn’t reply.

“Why’d you kill him, again?”

“Because he lied to me.” He scrubs hard at the corner of his eye, gaze trained upwards so no more errant tears will escape. “I trusted him, and he lied.”

“But what did he lie about?” Ryan asks, sincere and soft, genuinely wanting to know.

“Oh, it seems stupid now,” he admits, swiping again more forcefully at his eyes. “At the time it seemed so important. But now I wonder…maybe his heart was in the right place after all.”

Ryan’s quiet for a few seconds before he says, “He lied about Grandpa?”

“You don’t have to call him that.”

“Soldier Boy,” he corrects, although more for Homelander’s benefit than for his own, it sounds like. “Noir lied to you about him?”

“Yes. He kept it all a secret from me. Never told me I had a family out in the world somewhere.”

The answering silence from Ryan registers as thunderous in Homelander’s ears. He knows now that he didn’t and never would have had a family with that man. Soldier Boy’s involvement in Homelander’s conception began the same place that it ended: in a little plastic cup. 

Homelander sees now that he should never have thought it would amount to more than that.

“Noir knew what my father was, and he tried to tell me. Too late, but I should’ve listened. That was my mistake.”

“You wanted him to be wrong. I’ve been mad like that, too.”

“At Butcher?”

Ryan shrugs, but Homelander’s listening to his heart. He always is, so he hears it when it skips a beat at the mere mention of the ever-present thorn in their sides. Homelander gives Ryan’s arm a squeeze.

“Do you want to talk about it?”

“No. I dunno.”

“Okay…well, you can. If you want.”

Homelander shuts his eyes against his own awkwardness, mentally recalibrating his approach. He doesn’t know how to do this, and finding out overnight one day that he had a kid didn’t magically give him the answers. Small wonder his revelation about Homelander hadn’t given Soldier Boy the disposition for it either.

“Not that you have to, but…maybe I — should’ve talked to Noir. Instead of…” He takes a breath, only to finish lamely. “Ripping out his intestines.”

“I’ve never wanted to rip out anyone’s intestines,” Ryan says, looking up at Homelander with his eyebrows bunched down so the bridge of his nose wrinkles, just like Homelander’s does. “That sounds gross.”

“It was. I don’t recommend it.”

Ryan looks critically at Homelander’s hand on his arm and then at Homelander’s right hand relaxing on the couch arm. “When was the last time you washed those?”

“What? Oh. Ryan, please, I don’t wash my own uniforms. Housekeeping takes care of it. I have dozens of suits and capes to cycle through, to say nothing of gloves and boots. What’s that face you’re making? Hey! Don’t you stick your tongue out at me, young man.”

“Grody gloves!” Ryan heckles, in that childish way Homelander had been clearly too optimistic to think him too old for by now.

Homelander rolls his eyes and sets his teeth against one fingertip to work the glove off his right hand. Encouraged, Ryan works the glove off his other hand and tosses it to the other end of the couch. Bested in this particular battle, Homelander tosses the other one the same way. He sets his hand back on Ryan’s shoulder, savoring the heightened sensation of his bare hand against skin-warmed cotton.

“You really don’t know how to do your own laundry?” Ryan says, more teasing now than he is asking.

“Just because I choose to relegate that task to someone more suited to menial labor, does not mean that I couldn’t do it myself if I absolutely had to.”

“Yeah, but could you?”

Homelander hesitates just a fraction of a second, and Ryan laughs, all but bouncing in his seat.

“I can show you how. Mom taught me. It’s easy.”

“Are you telling me they have you picking up after yourself here?”

It’s a nice little townhouse Ashley picked out for them on the waterfront. Homelander gave Ryan pages and pages of real estate to choose from, and he wanted to be in the city. It wasn’t hard to see why, and Homelander couldn’t blame him. He’s just about had his fill of idyllic farmhouses, too, and he’s never had to live in one.

“No one makes me,” Ryan says. Then, ruminatively, “Doing things for myself reminds me of when I’d do chores with Mom.”

“Well, your mom did the best she could, I’m sure,” Homelander says, but it’s not what he thinks.

Ryan starts talking before he can say what it is he really thinks.

“I miss her.”

Sighing, Homelander leans in to press his chin against Ryan’s hair. “I know you do.”

“Don’t you miss her?”

“I didn’t really know her, and to tell you the truth, she hated me. She would’ve hated this, us being here together.”

“I don’t get it,” he says. “Why did she let you be my dad if she didn’t want you to be my dad?”

“Well, I…I don’t know, son.”

You don’t get to be a dad, he remembers her telling him, all hellfire in her eyes that no one had ever dared to point at him before. Not after what you did.

“Maybe she thought her heart was in the right place, too,” Ryan murmurs, like he’s just thinking aloud, like he’s trying to reconcile what his mother believed with what he knows. “She was a good mom.”

Homelander stows the rest of his lecture. It doesn’t seem so urgent now against everything Ryan’s telling him.

He’s been talked over and dismissed out of hand often enough to know that’s what it’ll feel like to Ryan if he starts up on a tirade about their inherent superiority over humans now. So he lets Ryan have this. He gives him his mom, and her memory, because he knows how direly he’s wished he could have that himself.

He hugs his son, and he says, “I know she was, sport.”

The movie’s been on this whole time with the volume low, and now they’ve arrived at some Hollywood-doctored fight scene, sanitized of blood, viscera, and excrement. Homelander edits the scene with his eyes as it plays out, accounting for collateral damage every couple of seconds.

“Who’s your favorite?” Ryan asks. “Don’t say yourself.”

“You drive a hard bargain,” he jokes, but he wouldn’t have picked himself anyway. “Queen Maeve. Without a doubt.”

“She was cool,” Ryan agrees. “It’s sad she died.”

“Yes, it is,” Homelander allows, feeling exactly nothing.

“But she saved everyone. That’s probably what she wanted, right? To protect people and stop the bad guy.”

Homelander doesn’t correct him about Dad. He had been the bad guy, and it had taken every single one of them to put him down. What a terrible stroke of accidental comedy; their plan to use Soldier Boy as a means of stopping Homelander, only to decide he was too much of a horrible person to be used, even as a weapon of mass destruction.

Or maybe especially as that kind of weapon.

“I’m sorry I brought him into your life,” he says, because it’s the truth. 

Even if it had inadvertently saved him from being the target of that final fight, he regrets ever dangling the prospect of a family in front of Ryan, just to have it yanked away in the worst possible way.

“I’m not. He was mean, but now I know he’s mean,” Ryan answers, as easily as if he were commenting on the costumes in the movie. “He was never going to be nice. Not like you.”

For once, rather than willfully keeping quiet, Homelander finds himself shocked to silence.

He thinks about Noir’s blood pouring out over his glove, remembering, with no shortage of despair, the surprise in that mangled, treasured face. 

Remembers that Noir could’ve stayed gone, if he’d wanted to. Remembers that Noir gave him an audience that night at all on the predication that Homelander wasn’t his father’s son. Remembers that everything in Noir’s body language communicated faith in him — hope — and he remembers shutting himself off from that plea. Remembers subjecting his only friend in the world to a bad death, a painful death, literally at his hand, to punish him for that hope.

Remembers that…

Noir hadn’t thought himself in danger until the moment Homelander opened his belly with his fist.

“Grandpa was the disappointment,” Ryan continues, heedless and undeterred by Homelander’s spiraling. “That’s why he said it, Dad. There wasn’t any room for us because the only person he cared about was himself. That’s who he was. That’s who he was always going to be. But that’s not who you are, and that’s not who I want to be either.”

Wiping at his eyes again and trying desperately to be subtle about it, Homelander asks him, “Did your mom teach you that?”

Ryan sinks back into the couch, pressing harder into Homelander’s side.

“No, Butcher did.”

Again, there goes his heartbeat skipping.

“Do you…miss him, too?”

That question receives no verbal response, but Ryan’s hammering heart is happy enough to tell him what he wants to know. He rubs Ryan’s arm, fingertips skimming his skin where his short sleeve cuts off, and then staying there, clay against clay. His hands are cold, but Ryan’s warm.

“What if I brought him here to see you? Would you like that?”

“Don’t you hate him?”

He bites his tongue against his gut response. 

“Well, your mom let me see you in the end, and you know how she felt about me. Besides,” he adds, remembering the wet pools of Noir’s eyes when the light began to leave them; and the pathetic sheen of tears in Stormfront’s, when he left her the last time. “It’s no fun wishing you had more time with someone after they’re gone. You know that just as well as I do.”

Ryan stays quiet. Sulking, Homelander thinks. He’s got more of that coming to him in adolescence, and then they’ll all be in for it. 

“He’s like us now, you know,” Homelander adds, incentivizing Ryan the only way he knows how.

“So what? He’ll still be the same.”

Homelander frowns. “No, Ryan, I mean, he’s like us. And not just from the temporary stuff. For good.”

Ryan folds his arms over his chest and sits up, leaning away from Homelander. Proof that Butcher’s on their level now doesn’t sway him, so Homelander changes tack.

“He nearly died.”

“Why? Did you fight?”

“Actually, that’s why he’s better. I fixed him.”

There’s no big dramatic reaction to hearing that. All Ryan does is press his hand to his neck, like he’s reaching for something that isn’t there. He looks worried, but not much else. “He’s okay now?” he asks reluctantly.

“Mmhmm. Tip-top. He’s even got multiple abilities.” It’s obvious hearing that diminishes Ryan’s interest, but Homelander can’t stop himself. “He’s nearly as strong as me now.”

Ryan gives him a probing sort of look, Rebecca Butcher’s eyes flaying him alive through their son’s face. He nods, seemingly resigned at whatever truth he’s uncovered, and drops his gaze to his lap. 

“What’s wrong?”

“Nothing.”

“Ryan.” Skin pricking all over at the answering silence, Homelander leans away from him. “Ryan.”

“I do miss Butcher! I miss him all the time! But this is what he does, Dad. He finds the thing you want him to be and he gives it to you, and then when he doesn’t care about tricking you anymore, he takes it away.”

Homelander blinks, surprised. He and Butcher have an arrangement; a simple business transaction. There’s nothing more between them, except Ryan.

“What is it you think I want him to be?” he asks in a small voice.

“Duh, Dad,” Ryan says, flinging his hand at the screen where Stormfront — actually Stormfront — makes her first appearance in a feature film this century. “A nemesis.”

The blood that had drained from his face creeps back steadily, leaving him feeling lightheaded and faint.

Nemesis.  

He thought Ryan had been about to say something else. 

“I don’t care. It doesn’t make any difference to me.” Ryan settles down again, sullen, but his racing heart finally slows down. “Just be careful.”

Homelander sits back against the couch, trying to get his own heart rate to follow suit, but he can’t help noticing his hands. Without the barrier of his gloves, without that extra layer preventing the rest of the world from being able to touch him, his palms have begun to sweat.

Chapter End Notes

This chapter title is from Luke 10:18 — I guess we're sticking with the bible themes. 😋😘

Chapter 4: alone in the garden

The glass globe atop one of the many metal and concrete spires flanking the power plant’s obsolete energy source shatters in a din of spraying shrapnel and noise.

Homelander, having just been thrown through it, turns in mid-air to respond with the red sight. It cuts a cauterized wound across so much debris, but it misses Butcher entirely on the first pass. He tries again, and this time, he shoots Butcher clean out of the sky.

He’s still rusty when it comes to keeping the ground more than twenty feet below. Upsetting his balance is the surest way to down him. Homelander crashes through a skylight to follow his fall only for Butcher to be lying in wait for him. He catches Homelander by his cape and whips him around into the ship’s-hull wall of residual fingerprints and indecipherable reflections. The disordered hill of Homelander’s torso leaves one more of each as he’s smeared into the copper on impact. 

Then it’s Butcher following him, Butcher crowding him into that unyielding surface, Butcher leaving behind the whorls in his hands and a distorted cast of his face right there beside Homelander’s.

The lightbulbs flicker on with a filament-hiss, and Butcher looks up briefly, cataloguing them. Or maybe he’s just surprised to see that some part of this old building still runs. Piqued at the evident break in Butcher’s focus, Homelander braces his hands on the wall and heaves off it, dislodging both of them and sending them in a backward sprawl over the warm, dusty floor.

He might’ve known this to be a mistake going in, but irritation, as usual, wins out.

Butcher establishes his four limbs around Homelander from behind, his legs crossing over Homelander’s hip, arms buckled in a seatbelt across his neck and under Homelander’s arm.

Caught, Homelander digs down with his chin into the arm barred across his throat, fumbling to break Butcher’s hold with a clumsy hand. Butcher catches that hand, too, and pulls down, trapping it under his leg, both of them crossed at the ankles. They’re stacked high enough over Homelander’s hips that all his straining to knock them loose just scrapes the interlocked mass of them in small increments across the gritty floor.

Knowing he’s got the upper hand, literally, Butcher scoops his arm up and up, tightening his chokehold until he can nudge the back of one hand up against the column of Homelander’s neck.

He strains, immobile, and flushes hot all over realizing he’s trapped. Butcher’s breath blasts his ear, warm like his body’s warm.

“You want out?”

Homelander grunts, eyes heating to match his stupid, frantic flesh. He knows what Butcher’s going to say, dreads hearing it again, remembering what happened last time, and tips his chin the barest fraction that he can, to loose the red sight through the roof, flaying rebar and sunlight alike.

“Ask m—”

The ceiling collapses, raining down rubble over their heads and effectively separating them.

Butcher tears himself out of the detritus faster than Homelander can. A cloying sweetness pulls at the edge of his awareness, fogging up more and more of his concentration and dulling his reaction time.

“What’s a matter, sweetheart?” Butcher asks, dragging his heavy boots on the floor as he prowls the room. The open floor plan means he’s got a straight shot to Homelander if he wants it, but he doesn’t take it. Instead, he lopes along the wall, putting himself as far away as he possibly can while still closing the distance. He shrugs off his jacket and tosses it. “Don’t like bein’ held down?”

Homelander tracks his approach with his eyes only. He feels stripped bare, even with his suit. The curve of Butcher’s mouth twitches, like he hears that thought as Homelander’s having it.

“Or maybe you liked it too much.”

Ire flares in him, unsticking him from the spot. He launches himself bodily at Butcher, the two of them going right through mortar and brick until they’re in the grass.

Understanding this time that he can’t leave Butcher in a position to grapple, Homelander pins him at an awkward angle with an arm twisted underneath his body. It’s almost enough, but Butcher swings back an elbow to crack Homelander across the temple and overbalances him. He doesn’t go far. Neither does Butcher. 

They lay in the grass panting and covered in brick dust, Butcher only barely drawn up on his hands and knees; Homelander spilled over onto his side.

Beneath the suit, he’s aching for relief. His gaze catches on Butcher’s, and he has the gall to look smug.

He rolls fluidly to his feet, and Homelander, albeit thick-fingered on the ground, can still be weightless in the sky. He vaults up, red filling up his vision to fall on Butcher in a cascade of violent heat. Butcher takes it on the chin. He actually, literally takes it on the chin, and then he fires back, a glancing shot meant only to interrupt Homelander’s searing gaze.

Butcher gets to him in a single bound and catches Homelander’s ankle in his hand. Hard, like cracking a whip, Butcher pulls him down and drops him in the muddy grass.

This time he doesn’t give Homelander space. This time he follows him down.

He fists one hand in Homelander’s hair, the other open and relaxed, placed low on Homelander’s belly. Again, as before, Butcher’s breath crashes in his ear, fast to match his heart. He says, “Ask me.”

Homelander’s better than this. He’s stronger than this. Even if he isn’t a piss and vinegar brawler like Butcher, he knows he can push himself harder than this. But that sweetness creeping in at him has sprouted hooks; it’s seeded and taken root.

With his eyes slipping shut on him, he moves into the hand at his scalp.

“Put your fingers in my mouth,” he whispers.

And Butcher does.

He tastes like dirt and ash and ozone, and Homelander can’t get him in deep enough.

“Want to take my cock like this?” Butcher murmurs, curling his fingers against Homelander’s tongue, thumb grazing the soft triangle of flesh beneath his chin. He pistons his hips against Homelander’s thigh, maybe seeking for some of the same relief Homelander’s chasing against his own hand. Butcher displaces him, trading the hand in Homelander’s hair for the zip on his suit. “Want me to take yours?”

Homelander moans weakly around the fingers in his mouth, and then louder when they’re ripped away to wrap around his dick.

“God!”

“Still ain’t calling you that,” Butcher grunts, reaching behind Homelander, ostensibly to touch himself.

Homelander’s approaching the edge of a cliff and that’s the only reason he laughs, high and delirious and frantic. He groans, moving into the hand at his dick and trembling all over, and says, thinly, “I could call you God.”

“You could call me Daddy if you like,” Butcher mutters, a caustic throwaway line, but it sticks in Homelander’s ears all the same.

“Daddy,” he says on a scattered breath, heart hammering at the subtle stutter in Butcher’s hand on him. Experimentally, letting himself dissolve into it like sugar in water, he tries again: “Daddy.”

“Fuck,” Butcher hisses, squeezing Homelander this side of too hard and falling against him.

Hot semen splashes the small of his back, seeping through his suit. Homelander grinds into the loose hand at his dick until Butcher trades it for the one he’d been jerking off with. The well of his fist is slick and warm and just what he needs.

“You wanna be good for Daddy,” Butcher rumbles against his cheek. “Don’t you, darlin’?” He nudges the flat of his finger along the seam of Homelander’s taint through his suit, insistent just like his hard dick had been, and muses, “Or maybe you wanna be bad, huh? I think you wanna be bad.”

Homelander’s not expecting the smack across his ass. He’d been so focused on the pressure between his cheeks that he’d already been clenched against it, wanting pressure or friction or something, and when the sharp snap of pain combines with the hand stripping his dick, he comes so hard he sees red.

Butcher shoves off him and staggers toward the water, undressing in rapid succession to wipe himself down as much as he can.

Homelander watches idly from his hole in the earth. His head’s finally clear, but he can’t string a complete thought together. It’s almost too much just to watch him, just to see the sun hitting his raw, ruddy skin, drying him of the lake as he washes in it. 

By the time Butcher decides he’s done, Homelander’s managed to sit up and comb his hair into place.

He looks blearily up at Butcher where he pauses to peer down at him, and for a moment, he imagines swooning in and lapping a second orgasm out of him with his tongue.

“Clean yourself up,” Butcher tells him, tossing his wrung out shirt so it splats in the grass ahead of Homelander. Already walking away, he adds, “Wouldn’t want people to talk.”

Homelander brushes his knuckles against his mouth, still tasting dirt. Still tasting ash and ozone.

Butcher doesn’t leave. He pulls on a clean change of clothes — a bubble gum pink atrocity that’s too tight on his chest and arms, and Starlight’s track pants — and roots around in his backpack for one more item. Homelander sits up straighter upon seeing it, a faint ringing in his ears picking up.

“What’re you doing?” he asks, his tongue still a little heavy in his mouth.

In the prim and proper tone of a schoolteacher, he shakes out the sturdy yellow blanket in his hands and says, “You ain’t too good for a shag, you ain’t too good for a nap.”

When all he can do is stare uncomprehendingly, Butcher finally looks over.

“A nap,” he repeats. “Y’know, a nap? Kip down, catch a few winks?”

Homelander rolls his eyes and snipes back at him, saying, “No, I don’t know about napping. That sounds completely pointless.”

Butcher shrugs and lays down his blanket before hunkering down to lay flat on his back. Homelander’s watching him closely, so he knows the moment Butcher falls asleep not even three deep breaths into his easy silence.

Jealousy aches in Homelander’s throat as rapaciously as hunger. He lurches into standing and whips chunks of earth and pulpy grass off the shirt at his feet. The material on his suit is conducive to wiping the mess off his back, and that’ll have to suffice for now. He can address the worst of the damage when he gets back to the city.

Arcadia is their coliseum, and he isn’t ready to leave it yet.

He glances over at Butcher’s sleeping form, as good as tracing the outlines of his dreams through his flickering eyelids and the rise and fall of his chest. Discarding the ruined shirt, he walks over to where Butcher’s laid out on his blanket.

It would be easy to mount his boot on Butcher’s skull and bear down until skin and hair give way to bone and brain and blood. 

I wish you would, he remembers Butcher telling him around an insouciant smirk. Cut out the last thing you got left, lets you be every bit as pissed off and fucking vile as you really are.

And he’d been right, Homelander knows. He’d known it before Butcher had gone so far as putting it to words. Had known it even deeper than his own skin that he could not rescind this thing he’s made with Butcher. This thing he’s made of him.

It would be the same as admitting he’d been wrong to do it in the first place, and he can’t have been wrong. Not when to be wrong about it would mean that it’s become something he can’t control or predict. 

So he doesn’t stomp Butcher out of existence. He sinks to the edge of the blanket to sit, with just his knee overlapping the soft, durable fabric, and sits there in the afternoon quiet, listening to birdsong and counting sunbeams as they break over Butcher’s skin.

It’s unintentional that he begins to time his own breathing to Butcher’s. His heartbeat, even calmed down, is a steady, constant metronome. Homelander’s listening closely enough that he can hear it when Butcher begins to wake, and he can’t be seen lingering, so he takes off into the sky, headed for Vought.

He’s got plans with Ryan tonight, but he can’t go straight to see him after gagging for Butcher’s dick for the last two hours. For Christ’s sake, he’s got fluids on him, front and back.

Back in his suite at the tower, he takes Butcher’s advice to clean himself up. 

With every soapy pass of his hands, he’s playing the afternoon back in his mind; everything said and done, on both sides. Everything not said, everything not done. He thinks of Butcher cut in darkness with the sun behind him, his dick hanging heavily between his legs, soft when he’d come out of the lake, but Homelander’s felt him when that’s not the case.

His hand drifts between his own legs, slippery and hot, even without the calluses he’s come to expect. He grips himself tighter and makes up for the discrepancy with his imagination.

Remembers Butcher saying, I think you wanna be bad.

He stuffs his own fingers in his mouth, tasting PVC and galvanized steel. Tasting chlorine.

There’s no one here with him to know he slips his fingers from his mouth and lets them stray, spit-slick and warm, where Butcher’d teased him through his suit. He’s never done this before, but he gets the gist by feel and by the pounding of his heart. His hand’s frozen on his dick, trailing suds in sluicing ribbons down his pelvis. 

The pad of his finger eases inside, and then in one fell, lush swoop, the tip of his finger sinks home. He gasps, head dropping back so the spray from the shower head blasts his throat and swallows his whispered, secret word.

“Daddy…”

He feels the muscles of his hole flutter around his finger, and then he’s shooting his load all over the shower tile. His head feels cottony like before, but it’s easier to shake it off now that he’s alone. 

The smashed mirror outside shows him a broken face with a dozen eyes all watching him, the same God-giving blue as the Compound V that made him. In part, anyway.

Later that night, after Ryan’s beaten him at all his favorite video games, and after he’s climbed into bed with Homelander following a terrible nightmare, Homelander lays back in his king bed thinking about the sun on Butcher’s bare skin and about his breath in Homelander’s ear.

He thinks of him fast asleep on his yellow blanket in the grass, wound tight and wide awake.

All night, he doesn’t sleep.

 

/

 

The call from Mallory comes in while Butcher’s out grabbing a snow cone. He gestures vaguely at the syrups as he’s picking up and says, “Gimme that blue shite there, yeah? Good lad. This’s Butcher.”

“There’s been a development,” Mallory says, in lieu of a greeting.

“Well, hello to you, too, love. There you are, son,” he says, handing over a crisp bill to cover the shaved ice and a little extra. To Mallory, as he’s turning, he adds, “Want me to get the boys?”

“It’s need-to-know.”

Taking a bite of ice and tangy sweet syrup, he asks, “And do they need to know?”

“Not as yet. We’ll read them in at a later date, once we get confirmation that the intel we have is good and actionable.”

“All right,” Butcher muses. “I’ll wait for your call then.”

“This is the call,” Mallory counters, stopping him in his tracks. “I’m sending coordinates to your phone.”

“Is it him?”

“You mean mine, or yours?” she asks dryly.

A blue droplet runs down the side of his thumb. He holds his hand out away from himself, ears ringing faintly.

“What do you mean, yours?”

“As I said,” she continues, stony and unmoved. “There’s been a development. Check your phone. Make sure you aren’t followed.”

Mallory hangs up on him, and Butcher’s phone screen goes dark, then lights up with a text. It’s a series of numbers denoting a location. He’ll have to pull up the GPS on his phone to find it, but it should be easy enough flying out to wherever Mallory wants him.

He slurps the runny, tacky syrup off the edge of his hand and walks a ways further until he can finish his snow cone. It’s a nice day out. Clear blue skies, just a little bit of chill in the air. 

The coordinates, when he pulls them up on his phone, point to Redding.

For a while, he just walks, enjoying the day and the feeling of sunlight on his face. He could walk all day and only just cross over into Connecticut, but now he could probably run and be there in an hour. To say nothing of how short a trip it would be if he leapt into the sky and made a contest out of it.

But he’s not in the mood for flying. Doesn’t feel like souring the snow cone with splattered bugs, especially since he hasn’t figured out how to avoid them yet. And anyway, the smells are better down on the ground. They’re worse, when it’s sewage and smog and pit stains he’s smelling, but they’re better, when it’s dough rising from the bagel shop on 20th Street or dark chocolate shavings shaken out over fresh gelato. Everything’s boosted up to eleven, and like this, when it’s just simple things and no one has to get hurt for it, he can almost see the use in the thing that let him have this luxury of a good day without brain damage.

But that’s just it, as far as he’s concerned. Someone will have to get hurt for it. So many already have been hurt for it, or worse, and no amount of bread, chocolate, or artificially blue anything changes that.

He gets as far as Chelsea before he decides it’s time to go to the sky.

Mallory’s place is a bunker in rural countryside. He circles it a few times before touching down and walking the rest of the way. Her security detail pats him down at the pneumatic gate and lets him back to see their fearless leader. Mallory, when he spots her, is standing alone with her back to him in front of shatterproof glass. He climbs up the winding side ramp to stand beside her, to see what she’s seeing, and sure enough.

“Fuck’s sake, Grace.”

“Oh, spare me the theatrics,” she mutters without so much as sparing him a glance. “He’s my responsibility. That’s as far as this goes.”

“Just because Frau Stormfront went down and never got up again, don’t mean the same’s gonna happen with Soldier Boy. You keep him on ice, he can come back just like he did the last time.”

“Whose fault was that?” she asks dryly, finally giving him an arch sideways glance.

Butcher averts his eyes, trying to couch it belatedly in an eye roll. He says, “We needed a weapon. He was good for it.”

“Some weapons should never have been made in the first place.”

“I don’t disagree. But here we are, and twats like him and Homelander have full run of the place.”

“Not anymore, they don’t.” Mallory turns to face him, arms folded tightly in front of herself and a grim expression on her face. “Not with us as their wardens.”

“I ain’t anyone’s keeper.”

She gives him one of her coldest, most assessing looks. Not judging him, no. She’s too far beyond her own acknowledgement of a fundamental truth to waste any time or effort with opinions, and certainly not where they concern him. There’s no bias with Mallory when it’s business. There’s just facts.

Butcher heaves a sigh, knowing he’s done for. “Said you had a development,” he prompts. “Let’s hear it then.”

She reaches into her pocket to palm something, then shows him a flask. It’s some kind of chrome material, no bigger than a travel size bottle of Listerine. Pausing, maybe hesitating, she just studies it in her hand for a moment before tossing it to him. He catches it, bemused.

“Drink it.”

“Why? You lace it with poison?” Butcher asks idly, already unscrewing the cap.

“Depends entirely on the rat.”

Butcher’s got the mouth of the bottle near to his nose so he can smell it, but as soon as the pressure’s released from the cap, a spurt of evaporated gas escapes and gets him right in the face. Thick rubbing alcohol fumes steal the breath right out of him, and dry heaving, he drops to his knees. The remaining liquid in the flask spills between his splayed hands, over his knuckles, and dries the instant it’s out of its container. He breathes that in, too, and promptly seizes up.

Mallory hums. “I thought so,” she says, and starts playing a recording on her phone for him to hear. 

It isn’t until he hears his own voice on her crystal clear phone speakers that he realizes it must be that video from the campsite. Cunts from the park whose tent he smashed must’ve posted it. Bad move on his part not to have gotten rid of the footage before they could do anything with it. Maybe he wouldn’t be crumpled up on the floor at Mallory’s feet feeling like he can’t catch his breath.

“I suppose I can guess why you didn’t come to me,” she says, peeling her foot away from him when he paws weakly at her shoe, “but all the same, I’d like to know just what the hell you were thinking aligning yourself with him.”

“I didn’t,” he wheezes. “You got it wrong, Grace.”

“There was a break-in at Vought; damage the likes of which only a supe could have inflicted. And do you know how I knew which one gave it to you?” She takes a knee next to his face where he’s drooling into the concrete. “Because you didn’t correct me on the phone. And because there’s no other supe out there, active or alive, that gives half as much a shit about you as Homelander does.”

Butcher grits his teeth, tries to get his hands under him, and can’t. He feels like he’s got cooked spaghetti for limbs.

“You’re gonna feel like shit for the next four to five hours,” she warns, again, more a statement of fact than an outright threat. “Or you can feel a little bit better right now. If you tell me what’s going on.”

His mind races, deepening the nausea turning in his gut. He squeezes his eyes shut against the migraine building behind his eyes. It hasn’t been that long since violently ill was his baseline, and he’s been sicker than this, even recently, but it’s a different type of sickness. He feels like his cellular structure’s being unraveled and turned inside out.

“Our boy’s got an itch,” he hisses, writhing in the wake of a muscle spasm that creeps slowly and painfully up his spine. “He thought I could help him scratch it.”

“And was he right?”

“I don’t know, Grace.”

“Billy,” she says, extracting a matte black canister from her opposite pocket. “Don’t go soft on me now.”

“He just don’t wanna be alone, all right? That’s all I can tell you. Grace, please, just—”

She pulls the pin on the canister, sending a cloud of white billowing out around him and making room in his lungs for oxygen. He gulps it in, hyper-aware of his body and the change in it, even as the pain leaves him. His shoulders feel heavy where he can tell, now, after having the mechanism of flight explained to him, that he’s no longer resisting gravity. He tenses his hand against the ground, cataloguing with interest the fragile pink lines etched into his knuckles from scraping them on the concrete.

“You found an antidote,” he murmurs, impressed in spite of himself.

“Not technically.” Mallory holds her hand out to him and helps him get back onto his feet. She steadies him with her hand on his elbow when he wobbles on unsteady knees. “Replicating Soldier Boy’s ability has been like trying to bottle lightning. Even with an abundance of organic matter to sample and test, this is the best we’ve managed to manufacture. This variant has a short half-life. Even undiluted, it doesn’t last the day.”

“And you knew that before you let me get roofied,” Butcher says, leading.

“Don’t be so maudlin. Of course I knew. Although I’m surprised you of all people don’t want a permanent cure for your present condition.”

“Believe me, I do. It’s just that now’s not the best time for me to be on the perishable end of things.”

“Because of Homelander’s itch. You said.”

Figuring they’re probably out of range here, as he knows he wasn’t followed and he’s got no reason for anyone to suspect him to be in rural Connecticut, he shrugs and matches her speaking volume.

“He sprung that V on account of me bein’ half-dead two weeks ago. The Temp V, you remember? Weren’t kind to me in the long run. Thought I was done for. Cue the Caped Cunt.”

“And his itch?”

Butcher stares at her, and raises his eyebrows in answer. Mallory’s mouth twists in disgust.

“You didn’t.”

“Sure, I did.”

“Why, Butcher?”

“Cuz my only other weapon’s a popsicle!” Butcher says, gesturing beyond the glass at Soldier Boy’s slack, sleeping face. “And I can’t be in the fight if I’m dead!”

“That’s not what I’m asking you.” Her face now, and her eyes, too, are studies in pain — for him. “I could have helped you. I could have gotten you treatment at least. Why didn’t you come to me?”

He holds up his hand for her to stop. He’s not interested in hypotheticals, even if he does believe her. What’s done is done. Tracing his decisions to now in reverse, he can’t always say he understood what he was getting himself into, or what all he’d find in the box he was helping Homelander to open, but he’s in it now. They all are, for better or worse.

“I didn’t need you to help me,” he tells her, not unkindly, but honestly. Gently. “I was good with goin’ out that way. Felt like I had it coming.”

“Butcher…”

“For a long time I have,” he continues, unbothered. “I got a bad death waitin’ for me at the end of all this, Grace. But I’ll tell you this. If I can take him with me, one way or another, I’ll count it as a win in my book.”

She shakes her head, maybe about to say more, when her eyes fall on the canister in her hand. Raising her eyes back to his, the remorse has shifted into absolutes. Facts and figures and corollaries. Butcher’s following her eyes, so he can follow her next line of inquiry. They’ve worked together long enough that he doesn’t need it spelled out.

“Oh, that could be very interesting,” he murmurs, already imagining it.

Homelander’s got a string of obvious tells that fall into two categories: things he likes and wants more of, and things he can’t figure out or put into words. The latter category’s the headier of the two for him, go figure. 

It’s always the loud-mouthed, hateful types that wanna get wrecked the worst.

“You said you wanna take him down. In what way?”

“With a raunchy sex tape and my dick in his arse,” he says, shrugging.

Mallory narrows her eyes. “You plan on taking him down by besmirching his reputation?”

“Think of his demographic, love.”

He watches her go over the analytics in her head, and yeah, there she goes coming around to his side of things. She pockets the matte black canister and folds her arms in front of herself, turning to face the glass again.

“I’ll have one of my men supply you with the anti-V serum.”

“And the feel-good stuff?” he asks, pointing with his chin at the matte black canister in her pocket.

Mallory gives him a smooth look, one finely manicured eyebrow arched on her face.

With his hands pissily planted on his hips, he says, “Well, that was unkind of you.”

“I couldn’t be sure of your intentions,” she says, in what amounts, for her, as a casual apology. “When our facial recognition software picked you up on that cell phone video, I had my team splice the formula. The compound I give to you won’t be isolated. Unless you’d like for it to be?”

“Not as yet. Not really conducive to fucking, is it?”

“I couldn’t say,” she replies, deadpan as anything. “That’s all I have for you today, Billy.”

“Hopefully not all,” he says brightly but with his distinct oiliness running down the side. “Maybe you could spare a car?”

Butcher marches his happy arse out of the bunker with a six-pack of anti-V serum disguised as seltzer water and a Kia Sorrento key fob. It’s about a two-hour drive back to the city, and then he’ll have several hours more to wait out his lack of superpowers. He weighs the pros and cons of going back to the office. Homelander knows to find him there, but he knows to find Butcher at home, too, and out of everything else, there’s one thing he’s absolutely gotta do while he’s de-powered.

He stops by his flat to grab Terror, over the moon and besotted like a lovesick fool at Terror knocking him down and slobbering all over him. They wrestle for a bit, and then, once his boy’s cooled off, they hit up the nearest dog park. Butcher throws a tennis ball until his arm’s sore, and he runs as far as he can as fast as he can, only for Terror to lap him every time.

Whether Terror can tell anything’s different or not, he’s having a great time. He jumps up on Butcher, not his usual fare except when he knows he can get away with it, and knocks him down just as he had in the flat.

It’s wonderful.

When he’s fatigued, and reeking of dog drool, he walks back with Terror to the office. While he’s waiting for the lift to take him up to his floor, he hears someone calling out.

“Hold the elevator, please!” Hugh Campbell jogs into lift, red-faced and panting hard. He doesn’t notice it’s Butcher shut in with him until he straightens out and does a quick double-take. “Mr. Butcher, hi! I didn’t see you there. Oh! Look at this little guy. Hello!”

Terror gives him his fiercest boof in reply.

“Big little-guy,” Hugh corrects, chuckling to himself and already holding out his hand. “It’s Terror, isn’t it? Hi, Terror.”

Apple didn’t fall far from the tree with him and Hughie. Kinda darling, that.

“Give the nice man a kiss, sweetheart.”

Terror obediently laves Hugh’s hand in dog kisses. Like it’s the highlight of his day, Hugh Campbell just laughs gleefully and gives Terror the ear scratches he’s especially fond of.

“Good boy,” he murmurs, crouching to ruffle Terror’s ears until his leg gets to kicking.

It’s a funny thing, the places tenderness can grow out of. Like a flower or a weed or a thick-trunked tree with aerial roots. That’s on Butcher, probably. He’s starved for kindness on a good day, he can admit it, but this mess with Homelander’s been rough on him. Not physically, at least. He’s lucky his dick’s been cooperative the last few times, or Homelander maybe might have ripped it off.

Hugh looks up from patting Terror on the head, and his smile falters a little at whatever he sees on Butcher’s face. Voice soft, he says, “Mr. Butcher?”

“Billy,” he corrects.

“Billy. Are you all right?”

“Never better.” He forcefully loosens his shoulders and lets his eyes do the rest of the work he’d usually put on his smirk. “Could show ya later, if you’ve got the night free.”

Hugh stands properly, obviously bewildered at Butcher’s suggestion until his cheeks pink up on him. He stammers, not saying much of anything one way or the other. The lift pings and the doors slide open. Terror wanders out along the kitty-corner into the bullpen to kick up excitement everywhere he goes, leaving Hugh and Butcher alone in the quiet lift.

Butcher nudges Hugh’s chin with his thumb. “Somethin’ to think about,” he purrs, and sees himself out the same way Terror went.

“Hey, big guy,” he hears MM saying from around the corner. “Where’s your daddy, huh?”

His boot catches the floor, and he nearly falls tripping over his own feet. Hugh appears beside him, steadying him with a hand on the small of his back. Fuck, Butcher’s desperate for intimacy of the uncomplicated variety. It must still be showing on his face because Hugh lets him go with the softest touch known to man, a look of worry set firmly on his face.

“Do you need help getting into a chair, Billy?”

“No, mate, I’m good. I’m—” 

He pauses, seeing MM walk around the corner to investigate the hushed conversation they’re having. MM looks between them, neutral, unassuming.

“What’s on the menu tonight, Mr. Campbell?” he asks, giving them both an out.

“Ah, Hughie wanted Indian food, and Annie knows a place. They’re treating. Great kids.”

“Yeah, they turned out all right,” MM says, palming Hugh’s arm and gently steering him in the direction of the bullpen without having it look like that’s what he’s doing. “Indian food, huh? Not that spot in Midtown, up on Madison Avenue?”

“That’s the one! Is it any good?”

Butcher gives himself another handful of seconds to get his head right before he follows them in. The scene he walks in on gets those roots of tenderness digging into him even deeper. Annie and Kimiko are rubbing Terror’s belly while Frenchie and Hughie look on, suitably enamored. Butcher gets it. Dogs bring that out in people, and he’s no exception.

Hell, he’d been gooey in the lift over Hughie’s dad not two minutes ago.

In the mix of everyone spoiling Terror with affection, Butcher slinks off to a desk in the corner and reads through the backlog of their encrypted notes from the day. He finds more of the same. Hughie’s drones can’t get close enough while remaining undetected, and the lighting fixtures at the power plant they rigged with bugs don’t have clear enough audio to be of any use.

He’s still sifting through everything when Hugh Campbell sits on the edge of the desk he’s slouched at.

“Billy, listen.”

Butcher looks up at him, letting his eyes do the same devious shit they’d done in the lift, and thrilling a little when it has the same effect. This time smirking comes easily. “Yeah, dove?”

“Oh.” Definitely flustered now, he looks down and away, drawing both their eyes, pretty unwillingly, to Hughie’s across the room — Hughie who’s giving Butcher his bitchiest dirty look, which is saying something — before he sighs and looks back at Butcher. “Hugh thinks the world of you, Billy.”

He snorts.

“No, really. He does. It wouldn’t be fair of us to complicate that.”

Because he’s been thinking nonstop about how twisted-up and sideways all this shit with Homelander has him, he knows straight away what Hugh means. He nods, unable to make it the dismissive brushoff he knows it oughta be.

Hugh covers Butcher’s hand with his own, a small smile on his face when Butcher can meet his eyes again. He finds himself smiling back.

His hand on Butcher’s, like everything else, feels different. Feels more in the opposite way that everything else felt amplified with the V. It’s lovely, remembering what it is to be vulnerable, to be flesh and bone in the truest sense. Without all that fake shite getting in the way, emboldening all the things that don’t really matter and turning down everything that does.

“Have a nice night, Hugh.”

“Thanks, Billy. You, too.”

He goes, and as Annie’s walking with him to the lifts, Hughie circles back to Butcher’s desk, incensed, and speaks in an urgent undertone.

“Butcher, I swear to God, do not fuck my dad.”

“Listen, son,” he drawls, precious and unrepentant. “Your dad and I, what we got, it’s special.”

“Butcher—”

“Hughie, let’s go!” Annie shouts from around the corner.

“Coming!” He points at Butcher right in his face. “Fuck you, Butcher.”

“Oh, so you can, but not your dad?”

“Hughie!” his dad calls.

Butcher folds his hands behind his head and lounges, enjoying Hughie’s futile rage. He points at Butcher one more time and turns to go, sidestepping Terror with a muttered, “Good boy,” before he disappears around the corner for the lifts.

“Truly, your debauchery knows no bounds,” Frenchie muses. “I am almost impressed.”

“Only almost? What, am I losin’ my touch?”

Kimiko rolls her eyes at him, but any words she might’ve had don’t outweigh the belly rub she’s giving Terror. His tongue lolls out the side of his mouth. Handsome lad.

“Y’know, Butcher, as much as Hughie genuinely, inexplicably loves the hell out of you, he might actually strangle you in your sleep if you fuck his dad.”

“All right, keeps your panties on,” Butcher says, hamming it up to play the part. He sweeps up out of his chair, doesn’t account for the wheels or for his weight — and gravity’s weight — that he’s not quite adjusted to just yet, and fumbles with his feet twice in the same hour. He don’t know how to be clumsy, and he goes down awkwardly, catching his elbow on the corner of his desk. “Fuck. Shite. Piss,” he grits out, holding his dinged elbow and rocking a little from side to side. “Bugger.”

Terror comes to lick at his face, and Butcher, still holding his elbow, lets the rest of his body relax. He knows what’s coming. Knows there’s no use getting up yet.

And like clockwork, MM, Frenchie, and Kimiko step into view in a crescent around his head.

“What the fuck,” MM says.

Kimiko drops her one hand in the shape of beak into her opposite palm. Frenchie nods, answering faintly with his hands in tandem with spoken words.

“It would seem so,” he murmurs.

“Guess I got summat to tell you lot, don’t I?” he grunts, realizing too late that Terror’s been pawing at him because he wants to use Butcher’s chest as a bed, same way he did as a puppy. “Oh, no, darlin’, don’t—” He wheezes, dropping his head back. “Terror.”

He pants happily in Butcher’s face, his open drooling mouth in the shape of a smile.

“Little help,” he rasps.

MM just hums, but it’s Frenchie who answers.

“Not just yet, I don’t think.”

Kimiko presses her hands to her chest and makes the sign for dog.

“Yeah, I noticed there’s a dog smothering me,” Butcher snaps, or tries to, as much as he can when he can’t draw a full breath.

“She says, let him comfort you,” Frenchie translates. “And frankly, Monsieur Charcutier, I agree.”

“Same,” MM says. “You’re all wound up, Butcher. I know you think we can’t tell, but it’s plain as day. We’ll come grab your ass in a minute, all right? Just try to relax.”

“Ah, you…cunts,” he mutters, but they’ve already gone. “Man’s best friend, my arse.”

Terror whines and drops his chin over Butcher’s heart, thawing him out in more than just the one way. Knowing when he’s beaten, Butcher sighs and sets his hand on Terror’s head.

“Good boy,” he says.

And as best he can, he tries to do what MM said. He tries to relax, to really relax, even though his ribs ache and Terror’s claws are biting at him through his shirt and the ground is digging into the back of his head painfully. For all that, he wouldn’t want to be anywhere else. Or anyone else.

He ruffles Terror’s ear in his other hand and shuts his eyes.

Chapter 5: Jonah

Chapter Notes

Had a sick kitty this week and didn't manage to get this out quite as soon as I wanted. My girl's all good now. Just very sleepy and wiggly and cuddly.

See the end of the chapter for more notes

By the time the lads deem Butcher fit for being scraped off the floor, Hughie’s on his way back to the office. They find out he’s en route at all because Hughie calls and tells them his dad’s not coming along. Butcher don’t feel any kind of way about it except tickled that it gets under Hughie’s skin as much as it does, so he doubles down.

“Oy, Hughie,” he gripes at him, even in the midst of Frenchie and MM peeling him off the concrete.

Kimiko shakes her head at the cluster of them. She picks up Butcher’s phone and switches it over to speaker so everyone can hear Hughie bitch at him.

“Barring the fact that the guy you’re currently boning famously doesn’t share, and barring the fact that I know this is something you’d do purely for the sake of holding it over my head, my dad is not some straight guy you’re gonna flip.”

“Why put your papa in a box, Petit Hughie?” Frenchie grits out, heaving Butcher over onto MM.

“Yeah. Not that it’s any of our business,” MM adds delicately, “but he didn’t look uninterested.”

Hughie squawks a few choice stammered words before settling on a crisp: “Fuck all of you.”

Butcher snorts as he’s lowered onto the beat-up couch Frenchie and Kimiko brought in about a month back. He whistles for Terror to come to him, and he’s quick about it, parking himself right between Butcher’s drooping, lopsided knees.

“Listen, Hughie,” he says, glancing around at the lads, Kimiko included, to see them all watching him with thinly veiled interest. He sighs. “There’s been a development.”

“Are we still talking about you fucking my dad? Because I don’t wanna hear it.”

“Me fucking your dad’s always on the table,” he says idly, scratching Terror behind the ears.

Frenchie, MM, and Hughie groan discordantly, and Kimiko, fed up with his antics, takes the phone off speaker and gives it to MM.

“Hughie, how do you feel about meeting us out for a drink?”

Without the V at the forefront of his perception, he can’t tell what Hughie says in response. It’s almost nice to have the quiet, especially when it’s not just on his own terms. All he has to be aware of is Terror’s big brown eyes locked on his, his warm ears flopping between Butcher’s hands where he’s rubbing him down.

MM picks a place around the corner from the office and disconnects the call. He walks over and tucks Butcher’s phone back in his breast pocket.

“Nice choice,” he notes idly.

“Dog-friendly,” MM counters, shrugging. “Think you can make the trip, or do I gotta carry your ass?”

“I got it,” Butcher says, hauling himself up onto his feet. “Let’s go, darlin’.”

Terror trots off in the direction off the lift doors, but he gets distracted by Frenchie and Kimiko slowing him down for pets. That’s all right. 

“Fuck,” Butcher mutters, pressing his fingers at his eyes when they spot up on him.

MM palms his elbow, steadying him. He says, “You sure you’re all right? What happened to you, anyway?”

“Mallory happened. Tell ya the rest once we get to where we’re goin’.”

The four of them, plus Terror, load up in the lift and ride down. Kimiko dusts her hands and signs to Frenchie. Butcher recognizes the sign for dog, but the signs following blur together: one like a beak plucking at birdseed, another like swinging a lasso, and one that looks to him like the start of rock-paper-scissors.

Frenchie signs back without answering vocally, and Butcher narrows his eyes suspiciously.

“You two better not be scheming to take my dog.”

Jolting, Frenchie says, “That would be foolish and also pointless. There is no one alive who adores you half as well as Terreur-Dodu.”

Kimiko locks eyes with Butcher and pointedly brushes her fingers against her temple.

“Ah, mon coeur.” He smiles and signs, as he says, “Reason and loyalty often have little to do with one another.”

“Don’t need reason if you got love,” MM says, getting there before Butcher can say something meaner and altogether more ruthless. Holding his hand out to knuckle at Terror’s wrinkled head, he adds, “Huh, big boy. You don’t need to know anything about your daddy except that he’s good to you.”

It catches and sticks in Butcher’s brain like a Greek chorus. Like a lightning flash of the unstoppable end to this thing he’s found himself caught up in with Homelander.

The lift opens, and Butcher walks out into the cool night, counting the few stars he can make out through the light pollution. It makes him wonder what the view looks like at Arcadia this time of night. The sky out over Dresden must be like a colony of fireflies right about now, never mind how it’d look on the V. He’ll have to remind himself to stick around and watch sometime, once Mallory’s serum wears off.

Two blocks later, walking up on the Daedalus, they get another call from Hughie.

“Look up,” Hughie says, in lieu of hello.

Butcher tips his head back, eyes catching on Hughie’s waving arm. Annie’s sat beside him with a pint already in hand.

They climb up to the rooftop and pick their way to the table Hughie and Annie have cornered. The noise level’s enough to mask their conversation in the murmur of voices, and the view gives Butcher some semblance of control in spite of currently not having an ounce of it to speak of. He won’t be able to do much of anything if Homelander decides to swing by, but at least he might be able to spot him upon arrival.

“So what’s this new development you didn’t wanna talk about on the phone?” Hughie asks once their server’s left with their drink orders.

Butcher looks around at his table-mates, wonders what the most concise explanation would be, and holds his hand out, palm-up, to Annie. She looks between his face and his hand, obviously confused, until she sets her glass down to take his hand in hers. That wrinkle between her eyebrows clears, turning his wrist and gripping it gently and experimentally with both hands.

“This is very fun for me, just so you know,” Hughie says.

“He’s human, Hughie,” she says in a low voice, giving Butcher his hand back. “How?”

“Mallory’s keeping an old friend of ours on life support. You know the one. Great beard. Explodes when he’s upset?”

“You gotta be fucking kidding me,” MM moans, putting his face in his hands.

Butcher tosses a companionable arm around his shoulders, squeezing at MM’s bicep in a way he doesn’t have to think about. The fact that he doesn’t have to modulate his strength to make sure he’s not hurting him, is a comfort he didn’t even know to anticipate.

“Oh, shit,” Hughie says, watching Butcher’s hand on MM’s arm. “His ability. She found a way to distill it?”

“Temporarily,” Butcher tells him. “For now.”

“Did she give you the compound?” Frenchie asks, leaning in with interest.

“Half a dozen doses. More than enough for my purposes.”

“Which are?” Annie wonders aloud.

They make room on the table for their drinks when they’re brought out. When their server leaves, they close formation again, tight knit and hushed amid the cozy din of the rooftop bar.

“Still need a sex tape, don’t we?” he muses, tucking into his pint of Lager.

Frenchie hums. “That is especially dastardly of you.”

“You ain’t seen him,” Butcher says, licking foam off his lip. “He’s the begging kind as-is. Once he’s had a taste of what I got hit with? Game over.”

“Can’t hurt that you’re the type to make someone beg,” Hughie quips.

“What’s that supposed to mean?”

“Butcher,” MM says. “Come on.”

“No, no — what’s that supposed to mean, Hughie, huh?”

He spreads his hands, tipping his head when Terror hops up to plant his front paws on his thigh. Hughie ruffles his ears, and Annie pats her hands down Terror’s sides.

“Don’t change the subject,” Butcher chides, throwing his voice over the edge of the table.

Terror glances at him sideways in that almost coy, saucy way that only a dog can, and then he budges his head up under Hughie’s hands.

“I’m just saying,” Hughie continues, giving Terror the pets he wants while redirecting the rest of his attention back to the conversation at hand. “Homelander likes to be held down, but you like holding him down.”

“Yeah, and?” Butcher shrugs more loosely than he feels. “Gotta find something about it to like.”

Annie dusts her hands under the table. She’s got a look on her face like she’s trying to read something in Butcher’s face. 

He isn’t given long to puzzle out what she’s looking for before she comes right out and tells him.

“Are you sure you’re okay?”

The resounding quiet at their table makes the surrounding noise feel much louder all of a sudden. Butcher drags his glass around in small circles to make a widening ring of condensation in the paneled wood. He feels a clawed foot standing on him and looks under the table. Terror rests his chin on Butcher’s thigh, and Butcher drops a hand to his neck, fingers inching under his collar to scratch.

To his left, he hears the clacking of Kimiko’s phone keyboard as she types. She slides it in front of him, and with his head still angled down to look at Terror instead of at them, it’s right in his line of sight for easy reading.

 

Sometimes a thing being easy to do doesn’t make it any easier to live with. Everyone at this table knows that.

 

“Not me,” Butcher mumbles, sliding her phone back to her. “I got myself into this because I can live with things the rest of you can’t.”

“You mean like when you broke Ryan’s heart?” Annie asks.

“Or when you drugged me,” MM says bitterly.

“Or when you served me up to Little Nina on a platter just to get your way,” Frenchie piles on, smiling faintly, though it don’t touch his eyes.

“Or when you left Lenny the last time,” Hughie adds, nearly in a whisper, but Butcher hears him.

He bumps the table trying to get to his feet, but MM catches his shoulder. It’s Kimiko who slams him back down into his seat. Under the table, Terror barks only once and settles when Butcher does. Kimiko shoves her phone in front of him again.

 

Or when you put me a in a dress and sent me to kill a bad man.

 

She frowns at him with her whole face, but he can tell it’s not for the sake of judgement alone. It’s a demand for a response, as palpable a language as the system of signs she and Frenchie use to communicate. Taking up her phone to type one-handed so she won’t have to release her hold on his wrist, this time she holds her words directly in front of his face.

 

Talk to us.

 

“What do you want me to say?” He looks around at them, truly, genuinely curious. “All I’ve done, or asked you to do, I did it because it was necessary. I’m doin’ this because it’s necessary. It don’t matter if it hurts,” he says, feeling his hands shake and his heart race. “That’s the price, don’t you get it? If it don’t stop with us, it’ll land on someone else, someone who can’t do what we do. Someone like…”

Like Robin, he thinks. Like MM’s dad or Kimiko’s brother or Annie’s supe friend what died all alone on a rooftop not unlike this one.

Like Becca, or yeah, like Lenny.

He presses the back of his hand to his mouth, smells faint apple and sulphur notes from the Lager. Terror whines under the table, and Butcher reaches for him.

“There, darlin’. Daddy’s fine.”

His nose burns and the corners of his eyes sting, but he’s not talking about it as long as no one calls him on it. And for a long pause, nobody does. Then Frenchie surprises him by being the first to try, tentative about it though he is.

“All things being equal, Monsieur Charcutier,” he begins, gently, as if it means something to him that what he says don’t bring Butcher undue pain. “And speaking only for myself, of course,” he continues, cautiously, like Butcher might flip the table if he speaks out of turn, “it does nothing for me to see you treat yourself with such cruelty. We are none of us machines. Not even you.”

“In spite of your best efforts,” MM adds, palming the back of Butcher’s burning neck. “You a hard motherfucker, but you ain’t that hard.”

Kimiko taps on the table beside her phone, still displaying the same three words: Talk to us.

It shouldn’t matter that they mean it. It shouldn’t matter that his assignment should be the easiest out of them all to get through without whinging.

He can handle it. He’s gotta, moreover.

Across the table from him, Hughie leans in with his hands folded in front of him. He’s quiet until Butcher acknowledges him, until their eyes lock, a bona fide confidence man if his big heart would only let him be. With unthinkable kindness in his eyes and in his voice, the kind that don’t gotta be forced because there’s infinite reserves of it safely stored away, Hughie says, “You’re inside a whale, dude. Let us help you out of it.”

Butcher presses his lips together, the bars of his control straining. Their server comes and refills their drinks. Butcher downs his so it’ll get topped off, too. Once they’re left alone again, he runs his finger along the frothy rim of his glass.

The floor’s his, and he knows it.

“Back when Becca first told me Homelander raped her,” he says, back to drawing sweat rings in the table with his glass, “there was this thing she said, stuck with me. She said…” He presses his fingers to his mouth, remembering that she used to do that, too, when she was worried or upset. Peeling his hand away, he laughs bitterly. “She said it happened because she trusted him. Because she let herself walk into a room with him alone. Like it was her skirt or her smile or her Louboutin’s that made him do it. 

“Like it had anything to do with her that some twat with lasers in his eyes and hands like a fucking bulldozer—” He holds his own hands up in front of him, remembering the fleeting power of his own two fists. Useless now, and then, in keeping his wife safe from harm. “—couldn’t be fucked to know the difference between a woman wanting him, and being afraid to death at the thought of hurting his pride.”

He rubs at his cheek just beneath his eye, disgusted at himself. At his own powerlessness, as much as at small, pathetic men who think themselves untouchable.

“Every time it’s me and him and no one else, I remember that look on her face and her thinkin’ it was her fault.” He shakes his head, knuckling at his eye again, before lowering his gaze to the table. He can’t make himself look up, not when the heat pricking at his eyes persists. “And then I think about this arrangement we’ve got — how that is my fault — and I want to tell her she was wrong, but it’s too late. It’s always too late for her.”

Butcher presses his palms to his eyes, shaking his head. Feeling himself shake as he tries and fails to keep his shit together. MM squeezes his shoulder, and on his left, Kimiko presses her hand to his arm.

“Every time I’m alone with that cunt and he gets to leave, I feel like I’m mocking her. Like I’m part of what happened to her. God…”

He hears a chair scrape, and then he feels arms around him from behind and a chin pressing over the crown of his head. Hughie’s strawberry shampoo fills his nose, and then his voice hits his ears over the pounding of Butcher’s heart, difficult to hear at first, but stubborn in the way Hughie always is, when it comes to this kinda thing.

“You’re not, Butcher. Okay? You’re not.”

MM wraps an arm around him, too, and Kimiko. Her chin presses in where her hand had been. Frenchie leans across the way and covers the back of Butcher’s hand with his own. Butcher can’t see him, but he recognizes him by his calluses.

They do it for Becca, he thinks. Because they loved her, too. Even if only for a little while.

He lets his hands drop from his face, moving one to Hughie’s wrist to hold onto him and the other to follow Frenchie’s down to the table. Becca’s not here, Butcher’s all they’ve got. Maybe he’s a shit and maybe they hate him most of the time, but they can’t now. Loving her made him human; it’s still one of the only things left that has that effect, and here they are, lads all of ‘em, doing all they can to further that legacy.

With his hands down, he can see Annie on the other side of the table wiping at her eye with the tips of her fingers. It’s good to know sometimes that it’s not just him that can’t always be made of stone. He lets himself forget, truthfully. Works at it.

“Any one of ya starts singing Kumbaya, you’re gonna get it,” he threatens the table at large, albeit weakly.

“Yeah, yeah,” MM mutters. “Hardass.”

Hughie sighs almost directly into his ear, saying, “Jesus, Christ, Butcher. Next time just say you need a hug.”

“But it’s so much easier to hit on your dad.”

MM barks a laugh to Frenchie’s chortle. When Hughie flicks his ear, Annie laughs, too.

“Oy, you, I’m fragile!”

Kimiko shakes her head, drawing his attention away from giving Hughie shit. Once she has it, she pulls back enough to knock on his arm with her knuckles. She shakes her head again. 

Across the table, Frenchie draws in a slow breath. 

He signs something to Kimiko that looks only a little at odds with what he says, which is, “Truly, Petit Hughie. Be gentle with him.”

He rubs his thumb along Hughie’s wrist, feeling the exact moment he can no longer make out that strong, steady pulse against the pad of his finger. He deliberately softens his hold on Hughie and on Frenchie, catches Kimiko’s eye long enough to see that she notices, and sinks into Hughie’s hold more overtly.

“Right, love,” he muses, tipping his head back so Hughie’s jaw brushes against his hair. “I’m only little.”

“You’re a pain in my ass is what you are,” Hughie grouses, but he doesn’t let go.

Butcher tucks his chin, pleased at the compliment. Their server swings by, hesitates before asking if they need anything, and smiles nervously when Butcher, puffy-eyed and red-nosed, catches her eye. 

He smiles and says, “Think we got everything we need, dear.”

She lingers a moment, but he sees her smile firm up on her face before she goes. He feels about the same. 

“You know what I think you need to do the next time you’re in Arcadia,” Hughie says, in that certain voice he always gets that tells Butcher he’s back on his scheming bullshit. 

Butcher knows it when he hears it, being that it’s his favorite look on him.

“What’s that, pet?”

Hughie pulls back enough for Butcher to see his smirk, says, “Show Homelander what it feels like to be one of us, to be breakable,” and gives Butcher a kitten-slap of his own on the cheek. “And then fucking break him.”

“Hughie, my son,” Butcher croons. “You read my mind.”

“Sometimes I don’t know which one of you frightens me more,” Frenchie says despairingly.

“All’s fair in love and war, right?” Annie says, reaching for a menu from the napkin tray. “I want mozz sticks. Is anyone else hungry?”

“We literally just came back from dinner,” Hughie says.

“You ate half my tikka masala.”

“Okay, fair.”

Butcher reaches for a menu, too. Now that the anti-V serum’s worn off, his appetite’s picking at him something fierce. He skims through it quickly and says, “I’ll see your mozzarella sticks and raise you some chicken wings.”

“Their potato wedges look pretty good,” MM says, grabbing Butcher’s menu to show Frenchie.

Kimiko scoots over to Hughie’s empty chair and pats hers for him to sit. She pulls her chair over to Annie’s to scan her menu, and Butcher, once Hughie does sit next to him, loops his arm around Hughie’s neck.

He smears a quick kiss at Hughie’s temple and tells him, “Thanks.”

“We got you, man.” Hughie leans into him, equal parts mischief and conviction brightening up his eyes. “And we’re gonna get him, Butcher. I promise, okay? We’re gonna fucking get him.”

“I know, Hughie. I know.”

Because whatever else comes of this? Whatever else happens, they will. As God is his witness, they will.

 

/

 

It’s early for him to be at Arcadia, but he doesn’t have anywhere else to be. He’s not hurting for tv spots or sponsorships, what with his entire brand these days consisting of ad hoc public appearances. No scripts necessary. No calendars. No contracts. No fucking around.

His numbers are astronomical. People love that he doesn’t give a shit. That he’s no one’s puppet. They love that seeing him at a rally means blood in the street. It makes them feel like warriors.

There’s still no war to speak of, and more and more, he feels like its absence is his doing.

He’s the mascot and the conscription call. The weapon and the army. He doesn’t need a battalion of mud people killing each other off in his name, but it would be the considerate thing, for their part. The least they could do, to spare him the effort of parsing the chaff from those with potential. The ones like Butcher, who could be interesting if properly motivated — and suitably medicated.

It’s where his mind wanders as he stands in front of the broken mirror in his bathroom, pretending he isn’t counting the minutes before their scheduled window. He locks eyes with himself, with one of his reflections, and hears the faintest whisper at the back of his mind.

 

I killed the crying boy in you for a reason, buddy.

 

He shuts his eyes, and the voice, the other him, croons, nearly laughing.

 

What’s the big idea with the mirror, huh? You know I’m closer to you than that. You know I’m always closer to you than that.

 

“I don’t need you anymore.”

The resulting silence sounds to him like the roar of an airplane engine. But what does he care, when he can tear planes out of the sky with his fist? With less than that. With a glance. When he has done it.

“You know what I think?” he murmurs, letting his eyes drift open and scan all over the jagged mosaic of his fractured reflection. “I think you’re the crying boy.”

 

Well, like I said. I’m closer than you think I am. Aren’t I?

 

“Arguing with a fucking voice in my head,” Homelander mutters, turning to go. To leave this behind him. He did it once and it worked. It should work again.

 

You’ll do anything just to be picked once. You’ll sell yourself again and again, and every time you’ll be disappointed. You really don’t see it? Open your eyes sometime.

 

Heart racing defensively, he rounds the corner to the next whole mirror and points his gloved fist at it. That’ll do it.

“How’s this for open,” he seethes, and looses the red sight.

He waits, the battering ram in his chest slamming and crashing in time with his breath. The smell of vaporized plastic and melted metals burns in his nose. Faintly, so far away as to almost sound like a shout from a few rooms away, he hears it. One last cloying rebuke. Distant, but at the same time, inside his skin. Fused with his DNA. Deeper than his mind.

 

Don’t forget I told you so.

 

It’s early for him to be at Arcadia, but he can’t sit here another minute alone with himself. And it won’t matter, besides, if he’s waiting all day for Butcher to show up. Arcadia is his. Homelander found it. He brought it out of disrepair, for his own use. For their use.

So they have an agreement whereupon they meet here at a certain time on a certain day of every week. That doesn’t mean Homelander can’t show up any other time he chooses. He has dominion here; he reigns supreme, here the same as anywhere. ‘Early’ and ‘late’ don’t apply to him. Rules can’t confine him.

But when he touches down at Arcadia, no sooner than his boots have hit the ground, he floats back up to peer over the roof, frowning.

Lounging with his black jacket spread out underneath him, Butcher looks over at him.

“Oy, what’re you doing?”

“What am I doing?” Homelander echoes, inane, but he can’t help it. “What are you doing?”

Butcher shrugs, the gaping collar of his hideous Hawaiian shirt shifting to reveal the hollow where his throat meets his chest. He says, “Nice day out. Figured I’d enjoy it.”

Homelander narrows his eyes.

“Why am I not surprised,” Butcher drawls, “that the twat who don’t nap, don’t get no fuckin’ enjoyment outta life?”

“I enjoy life plenty,” Homelander snaps.

With an infuriating smirk on his face, Butcher nods and lets that be his reply. He tips his head back so the sweating plastic bottle in his hand pours a glug of sparkling water down his working throat. Exhaling and rubbing moisture away from his lips, Butcher idly looks over at Homelander again. The smirk on his face becomes more of a leer, warming Homelander’s face as effectively as a gust of steam.

“When you’re finished wasting my time and yours, come and find me.”

“Yes, dear.”

Homelander drops down to the grass, brushing his knuckles against his neck and underneath his collar. He still feels hot. Conspicuous. All that skin his eyes had devoured, his mouth had wanted to taste, too, and they haven’t even fought yet.

Irritated at himself, he walks up the dirt path to the plant and lets himself in through the door.

As he’s wont to do, he gravitates toward the dimpled metal wall with his hand out to feel it. All he gets through the glove is warmth and a suggestion of ridges.
