His eyes, as usual, are little more than dark pools standing out against the pallid canvas of his face. He looks like an afterimage. Like a memory half-forgotten.

“My eyes are open,” he whispers.

There’s no answer to that, but where he thought he’d be satisfied, he only feels hollow. He takes his hand away from the wall and then his gaze, which falls naturally on Butcher’s bag. Scanning through its contents shows him more of what he always brings with him: the tightly rolled yellow blanket, clean but mismatched clothes, condoms. He’s also got another bottle of sparkling water, untouched, unopened.

He tips his head back to see Butcher sprawled out on the roof, now laid back completely and twirling the empty bottle between two fingers. His eyes are shut against the yellow sun. Homelander tears his eyes away, throat dry, and crosses to Butcher’s bag.

The bottle’s cool even through his glove, and wet where it’s been sweating against a folded up towel. He sighs, saying, “In for a penny,” and cracks the cap off.

That feeling of steam hitting his face rolls over him again, stalling the breath in his lungs. Disorientation like leaning, like shattered equilibrium, pulls him back from between his shoulders, and overcorrecting, he lurches forward, slamming hard onto his knees. He crushes the bottle in his hand, fountaining the remaining contents all over him.

Sharply, at the front of his senses, he’s aware of stinging in his knees where they grind and pull against the dusty ground. He plants his hands out in front of him, bowing his back, and breathes in slowly, flexing his fingers.

Inside the creaking, overly warm leather of his gloves, his hands are clammy. He buckles down to his elbows and clumsily works one glove off his hand. The sun streaking in through the window dances on his soft pink fingertips, illogically cool in the cup of his palm. Except — no, that isn’t the sun. It’s the air.

“What…”

There’s a scrape of boots on the roof, and then Butcher steps down through the skylight Homelander made by throwing him through it. He lands hard on his feet rather than catching himself, and he walks over to where Homelander’s doubled over into himself. Black boots circle around in front of Homelander’s face. A hand grips his hair and forces him up onto his burning knees, drawing him so tautly that he nearly overbalances following the natural curve in his spine.

His scalp, likes his knees, jolts alive, pain receptors kicking into overdrive. Homelander blinks, not even breathing. Not understanding at all what’s happened.

Butcher hums low in his throat, and Homelander, beginning to go numb with shock, tries to fly, to superspeed, to use the red sight, to use his X-ray vision, anything, but can’t. It isn’t there. When he widens the scope of his hearing, all he gets is this room with Butcher’s hand in his hair. The only heart he can hear is his own, hammering in his ears.

“What’s a matter?” Butcher purrs. “Cat got your tongue?”

Homelander opens his mouth to speak, to curse, and what he does instead, most humiliatingly of all, is suck in a gasping, needful breath. As it was on his bare hand, the air in his lungs is cool and restorative. He lets it go, astonished, and breathes in again to repair the ache stitched behind his ribs.

“Did you come by any of it honestly, or was it all the V? D’you even know?”

Butcher tightens his grip in Homelander’s hair until tears well up in his eyes in time with a noise from his lips.

It isn’t of pain.

“Well,” Butcher says, smiling like a knife. He puts his face near to Homelander’s, dark eyes glimmering like the end of the world. Like a warhead set to detonate over a slumbering city. Like a nuclear apocalypse. “Like a little rough, don’t ya?”

He could do anything. 

Snap Homelander’s neck, crush his skull in his hands, flay him alive. Pinch his nose and wait for hypoxia to set in.

Butcher’s fist tenses against his scalp, and Homelander, fumbling, clasps his hand to Butcher’s. It’s a reflex more than anything else. He doesn’t believe he can stop him. In fact, he knows he can’t. Even if he can’t explain why, he knows there’s no use trying. Butcher drags Homelander into him, and Homelander goes, as soft and pliable as pulled taffy.

“Tell me to stop,” Butcher growls. “Ask me not to hurt you.”

“Don’t hurt me,” Homelander hears himself saying, all of it melded into one long, smeared, rolling utterance from his lips. There’s too much saliva in his mouth. His head feels like cotton and clouds. “Stop…”

Butcher’s hip is hot on Homelander’s cheek. His mouth falls open, and he turns so his forehead can warm that spot instead. The hand in his hair relaxes.

Face flaming, Homelander presses his open mouth over Butcher’s fly. He hears Butcher breathe in sharply, and that’s all the warning he gets before he’s opening his pants. It’s all the command he needs. Not that he could stop himself now anyway.

Homelander’s never.

He’s never.

But Butcher gives him his dick to swallow, and all he knows — all that matters to him in all the world — is that heady slide of flesh on his tongue, gagging and choking him until he can’t anymore. Until there’s no room, he thinks, until Butcher keeps giving it to him, and he claws at Butcher’s thighs, at his ass, begging with his body, with that wail inside of him that can never be sated. His lips, stretched wide, kiss Butcher’s pelvis in benediction.

He scrapes his eyes open, wet eyelashes tangling with the tears spilling out of him, blurring his already limited sight. Butcher watches him, holds him there for as long as Homelander can match his stare, and finally pulls him off so he can breathe.

Homelander gasps raggedly for air. Butcher gives no quarter, but he doesn’t need to. He keeps the head of his dick in the well of Homelander’s mouth. Homelander tongues the slit in the head, tasting the heat and musk of him, the gathering of fluid there. The next pass of Butcher’s dick down his throat chokes him as much as the first, but now that he’s braced for it, his eyes drift closed for a different reason.

Butcher changes his grip to hold Homelander in place with both hands. Homelander changes his grip, too, surprised to feel skin when his hand slips up under Butcher’s shirt, palming the beveled channel of his spine. His body’s like a furnace, and the planes of his flesh are softer than Homelander expected.

He wants to feel all of him like this. Wants to be caught and overwhelmed by all of Butcher’s body, by his hands, lips, and teeth. His eyes. He wants to be turned inside out, in the way only this man can give him.

A long trail of spit and semen and moans escape Homelander’s seeking mouth as Butcher pulls away. He pulls at his dick until ribbons of white break over Homelander’s face, catching in his eyebrows and eyelashes, down the mound of one cheek and on the edge of his lip. He would’ve liked to catch it all in his mouth. He would’ve liked to try and spit it out, only for Butcher to grab him hard by the chin and make him swallow it.

Homelander wilts shakily once Butcher releases him. He’s still coiled tight like a spring, skin thrumming in a way he’s never known it to do.

“Liked that, didn’t you.”

“Yes,” Homelander rasps.

Homelander tips his head back to meet Butcher’s eyes, enigmatic at the best of times; now, perfectly unknowable. Unmapped territory. A continent unexplored. Unclaimed.

Using his one remaining glove to wipe his face clean — for a relative value of clean — Homelander strips that one off, too, and sits back on his knees, groaning. He tosses the discarded glove and runs his hand through his hair. A lock of it flops over his forehead, the rest laying flat and smooth beneath his hand, gone damp with sweat.

Butcher catches his wrist and bends it back behind him, wrenching a bolt of pain all the way up Homelander’s arm into his shoulder. To do this puts his face near to Homelander’s, and he can’t help it. He’s so hard he’s nearly woozy with it, and the nearness of William Butcher, the incinerating heat of him, the smell of him, swims in his senses, swallowing him whole. He licks his lips, to speak, he thinks, to protest or to ask for more or to plead for relief, when Butcher’s gaze drops from his to his mouth. Still twisting Homelander’s arm behind his back, Butcher seals their open mouths in a sweltering kiss.

Good lord.

Is that what this feels like?

He’s nearly disappeared down that dark, warm chasm of sensation when Butcher shoves him flat on his back. Homelander hits the ground so hard his teeth rattle in his head and a tinny whine picks up in his ears. He blinks, distantly aware of the lower half of his suit, his pants, being yanked off of him. Or one leg of them, anyway. Butcher pulls that boot off Homelander’s foot and tosses it blankly over his shoulder before rolling Homelander around onto his stomach.

It could be — 

This, now —

Could be his last act on earth. Surely, it could. He doesn’t know otherwise. He doesn’t know his abilities are coming back or that Butcher will let him survive this encounter, much less enjoy it, and all he feels, in spite mounting uncertainty, in spite of danger he can’t possibly combat in his current state, completely and implacably, is unbound. Released. 

Freed.

Butcher twists a thick finger into him, the way eased with spit and with something else, he thinks. Something that smells sweetly and inoffensively of coconut. 

Homelander writhes against the gritty, dusty floor, and his body, this unbreakable vessel and fortress of unfettered power, comes apart around him like a buckling dam. The first time he comes, he doesn’t know it’s happening until it arcs through him like lightning.

Is that what this feels like? Is it always?

Dimly, he’s aware of the ground pulling out from under him, and hands mounting and positioning him, his knees up by Butcher’s ears, the ground a long way below and behind him. Still, all the while, Butcher’s fingers — more of them, now — catching on the rim of his ass and sinking back in, like they’re meant to fit there when no one else ever has. Butcher sucks the tip of Homelander’s dick into his mouth, and then more of it. It’s easy for him, Homelander notes with hazy, half-hearted interest. 

He wants to know if Butcher would swallow for him, or if he’d spit, but before he can get there, Butcher’s swinging Homelander down from his shoulders and slamming him up against the wall. With a good four inches between the tips of his toes and the ground, Homelander has to hold onto him. 

At his core, beneath the veneers and the smokescreens, he thinks he’d do that anyway.

He thinks they’re here at all because he’s been doing that. Because he hasn’t let himself see it for what it is. Him, holding on. Him, forcing Butcher to match him. To be exactly this.

 

Don’t forget I told you so.

 

The knee-jerk impulse to defend himself, to object, sees his mouth open when the head of Butcher’s dick finally breaches him. Homelander’s head drops back into the wall, the same dimpled metal wall he’s touched so many times without ever really feeling it. He flings one hand back, the one not gripping Butcher’s shoulder tight through his awful Hawaiian shirt, to brace his hand there and feel that smooth, imperfect metal against his slipping, straining palm. A thin, animal sound, high, nearly singing if he had the voice for it, leaves his quivering lips.

It’s all the chance he gets to calibrate himself to this new reality, where his body feels like this, closer than he’s ever known it and yet, somehow, shut out in a way that’s hard for him, even now, to quantify. This is it: the last reprieve he gets before Butcher starts to fuck him for real.

He grips Homelander’s hips in his hands, hurting him, almost definitely covering him in bruises, and hitches him up higher on the wall. Homelander’s nearly bent in half, his one leg slung up over Butcher’s shoulder and the other, the one trailing the deflated bottom half of his suit, dangles weakly to one side. His cheek slips against the metal wall, slippery with sweat, and he nearly goes spilling onto the ground.

Butcher steps away from the wall, one hand splayed against the small of Homelander’s back so he won’t fall. With just that hand propping Homelander against him, and with no effort at all, he bounces Homelander on his dick. Rolling, languid bounces. Idle. Like he isn’t flushed a delicate, sumptuous pink all over or covered in a sheen of sweat. 

These are nothing to the fat, plentiful beads of sweat leaping off Homelander’s burning skin, but he isn’t indifferent. No matter how he pretends.

With his lips brushing Homelander’s, he whispers, “Tell Daddy.”

Homelander sinks his fingers into Butcher’s dark hair, soaked through and matted with sweat. God, the smell of him. The water of him — his liquid eyes and his endless mouth and his clever hands electrified by blood and muscle and impulses and his unknowable mind, almost lost forever — touches the deepest clay at Homelander’s foundation.

Touches, and deliquesces.

Until Homelander is all water, too.

He scratches his nails down Butcher’s scalp, wondering aimlessly whether it registers for him. Whether it ever will again.

“Daddy,” he breathes, the barest sliver of his voice breaking.

“Yeah?” Butcher grunts, dragging Homelander onto his dick in such a way they both cry out, mouths dragging and only just missing. “Yeah, what?”

“Kiss me.”

Again, that uncoiling abyss of smooth, flexible darkness closes around him. Butcher flicks his tongue at the roof of Homelander’s mouth, and Homelander, coming in under him, grazes the slick underside of Butcher’s tongue with his own. The hand at his dick, at this point, is superfluous.

But he comes, and he comes hard.

Butcher’s still grinding Homelander down onto his dick when he comes down, oversensitive, face twisting up in pain at the culmination of all that he feels.

Calmly, like he isn’t even close, Butcher says, “You’re gonna give me one more.”

“No, I can’t,” Homelander whines, clutching weakly at the burning body he’s tied to. “I can’t, I can’t…”

“I think you can.”

Homelander gulps at the air, straining, until Butcher’s lowering him down onto the floor. Again, he turns him over onto his belly so his cheek scrapes the ground. Butcher pulls him back by his pelvis so his knees, still painful, take root beneath him. The slide back inside is exquisite as much as it is agony. Hot tears stream down his face, mixing and mingling with sweat, all of it stinging his eyes.

His hands curl up into fists, and then, Butcher’s hands covering the backs of his, they slide up and up, high over his head. Homelander realizes, belatedly, that he’s been whimpering, an unbroken litany of desperation and need spilling out of his mouth.

“Tell me to stop,” Butcher murmurs, voice all gravel and smoke against the shell of Homelander’s ear. “Ask me not to hurt you.”

Homelander’s eyes creak open to peer over his shoulder at Butcher. Clarity finds him through the haze of sex and abrasions and anguished tears. 

Mouthing the words more than he speaks them, he says, “Hurt me, Daddy.”

A short, percussive breath scrapes his ear. Butcher keeps Homelander’s wrists pinned with one hand and cages the back of Homelander’s neck with the other. A killer’s hold, if he likes. The hand on his neck squeezes, igniting pain and gratification and a feeling of such weightlessness he’s only ever felt previously in flight.

And then—

For a few seconds, his vision occludes with dark spots, and it’s only once they clear that he realizes it had all been black before that.

“Oh, good,” Butcher says, from somewhere well above him. He nudges Homelander’s arm with his heel. “Still alive.”

Homelander twists arduously onto his side, the whole of his body throbbing painfully. Butcher stands over him, as tightly put together as he ever is. He crouches so Homelander can see him without straining his neck.

Butcher digs in his jacket pocket and liberates a small water bottle.

“This one ain’t been tampered with,” he says, tossing it within Homelander’s reach.

Wrung out beyond the point of exhaustion, beyond the point of even wanting to try to sit up and drink yet, Homelander says, “What did you do?”

“There it is. Post-nut clarity. Gets ya every time.”

“What,” Homelander repeats, “did you do?”

Butcher runs his hand through Homelander’s ruined hair. Not affectionately, but that doesn’t take away from how it feels.

“Nothin’ you didn’t want me to do.”

Homelander licks his lips and doesn’t answer.

“Uh huh.” Butcher leans down and smears a kiss on Homelander’s slack, unsuspecting mouth. “You sit pretty and think about it.”

“What’s there to fucking think about?”

Butcher laughs, stands tall, and turns to leave through the same skylight he’d used to come in. Left alone, Homelander seethes, choking on dust and his own bitter frustration. When he can tolerate twisting around to lay flat on his back, all his shallow anger goes out of him like so much air from a punctured tire.

The bottle did it, that’s obvious enough. Whatever was in it, whatever that chemical was that he breathed in or that absorbed into his skin, did this to him. And Butcher knew to bait him with it and to keep his distance until it wouldn’t affect him, too.

Would he have done that if he didn’t know firsthand how it worked? Where did he even get it?

To what end had he been given leave to use it? For this?

Just for this?

He looks over at the dimpled, reflective wall, still close enough to touch, and holds his hand against it. The metal is warm from the sun. Warm like Butcher’s body pressed against him. Warm like the weight of Butcher’s stare upon him. Warm like a request masquerading as a command from his lips. Warm like his own two hands holding Homelander as securely as a stationary mount would a spinning globe.

In the back of his mind, like loneliness, like laughter in a dark, empty room, he hears it.

 

Don’t forget I told you so.

 

“My eyes are open,” he whispers to himself, to this room full of sun that’s his, whether he’s his or not. “They’re open.”

Chapter End Notes

Everybody leave a 💓 in the comments for Emma, who slept on my legs for an entire hour while I typed this.

Help me, I am turn to jello.

Chapter 6: the flood

Chapter Notes

In case I have to say this: don't take pictures or videos of someone without their consent, and definitely never share them. For the love of God.

For a long time, Homelander lays where Butcher left him. It’s out of astonishment as much as it is out of utility that he does this.

He doesn’t feel different. Doesn’t feel smaller or weaker. Doesn’t feel his own fragility. All he feels is sore, wrung out. Immobilized by the sum of his many exhaustions and tender contusions. There are muscles on his body that he’s never felt before, screaming out in stark agony.

But for all that, he’s alive.

How fraught and wonderful to think, that he could’ve been otherwise — that it hadn’t been any work of his imagination that he was in danger at all, but that he truly was. That it wasn’t in his power to do anything but to surrender. And even with Butcher’s fist in his hair and his teeth at Homelander’s throat, poised to kill as much as to kiss, Homelander had given himself over. Jubilantly, and unconditionally.

The thought warms him, beckons for his body to stir and rouse again. But he can’t. God, but he can’t. He barely has the energy to sit up, and when he tries…

“Ah—”

He shifts, carefully and painfully, onto his hip where his very bones grind and ache. Shuffling abortively onto his hands and knees is hardly any better. His one bare leg has scrapes all down the kneecap and shin, a single mottled bruise covering his thigh in the shape of a cluster of grapes. It’s as he’s taking stock of his injuries that he notices it.

The smallest finger on his right hand sits crookedly atop the trunk of his hand.

Faintly, and from a long ways off, he becomes aware of his ears ringing. As far as collateral damage goes, he’s done worse, but when did it happen? He can’t pick a moment out among all the many moments that put him here, on stinging knees within his own temple. Torn down to tatters.

After a labor of scrabbling, cautious hands and gritted teeth, he maneuvers onto his less hurt side. With more of his weight on that leg, he can raise his hands to his face for inspection. Just grazing that misshapen knuckle sends sparks down the back of his hand.

He steadies himself with a breath and tries to stand. When that attempt fails, he lets out a ragged sigh and sinks into himself, mind racing. No longer comfortably blank. 

This isn’t permanent, he tells himself. It can’t be.

You sit pretty, Butcher had said, and think about it.

It’ll wear off, whatever it is. Butcher wouldn’t give him this the way that he had just for it to be the end, and he wouldn’t have left the way that he did, gloating, if he wasn’t counting on retaliation. Homelander knows that much about him. He just needs to weather this until it passes, and then he’ll be strong again.

He tries to stand again and nearly makes it, until his knees buckle. Slamming his fist on the ground does nothing but exacerbate his scrapes on that hand.

On the third try, he braces himself against the wall until the strength in his legs reconstitutes. His pants hang off his one leg and the top half of his suit is ruched up nearly to his shoulders. He pushes his pants the remainder of the way down his leg until they pool around his one remaining boot. The top half he unzips in its entirety so he can shrug out of it. Once he’s out of the last of his clothes, boots, cape, and all, he wilts against the metal wall, bracing his hands behind him for balance.

Peering down at his bent pinky splayed along that dimpled copper surface, he tries one more time to remember how the fracture happened. Maybe when Butcher had him folded in half against this very wall, or maybe when he threw him down onto the ground to take him from behind.

He sighs fretfully, palming his abdomen, trying to get his unruly skin under control. He can’t again. Not so soon after Butcher took him for everything he was worth, and then some.

Slow heat warms him about the groin. He closes his eyes and presses his head back into the wall. A spot on his scalp aches at the pressure, right about where Butcher had first wrenched him back and held him tight.

“You’re pathetic,” he chastises himself. “Even when Butcher was human, he didn’t get like this.”

There’s no way he can know that, of course. Maybe Butcher did get like this, once. For his wife. Maybe it wasn’t weakness, then. Maybe it was something else.

Maybe this could be something else.

But that’s only foolishness. He gave Butcher the Compound V so they could fight — only they didn’t fight today, did they? Parts of it certainly felt like combat, but that had all been an overture to sex. Butcher hadn’t hurt him half as much as he could have. Even before the Compound V, Homelander’s positive he’s wrought more damage than breaking the smallest finger on a person’s hand.

When Homelander can stand unaided by the wall, he puts one foot in front of the other, in front of the other. As much as it costs him just to get to the door, opening it is twice the effort. Has it always been so heavy?

Outside, the early afternoon sunlight breaks in a million rays of radiant heat. It warms his delicate skin, gone sickly cold with chilled sweat. His feet take him to the water.

The lake closes around his ankle like a bear trap, so much colder than he remembers.

He wades out into it anyway. He’s sticky with sweat and semen — his, Butcher’s — and the raw, red sites on his knee and his palms, even the pinprick of blood welled up on his lip, cry out for the cool restoration the lake offers. To be weightless in the water reminds him of flying, but not of his own flight. Rather, it reminds him of Butcher in flight; Butcher’s reliance upon an arbitrary axis sending him into an accidental spin.

Homelander had known why Butcher was hunching into himself and rolling his shoulder. He’d known, but until that bottle sent him crashing to his knees, he hadn’t understood.

He thinks he does now. 

When he gets back on relatively dry land, shivering and cold, he lingers for a while in the sun naked as the day he was born. Perhaps he wasn’t conceived in the ordinary way, but he was born, after all. 

Born, and left alone in his skin, knowing the taste of broken, forbidden flesh. Only it’s his flesh that’s broken now; his blood in Butcher’s mouth like the sweetest wine.

This isn’t the end. It can’t be.

He presses his hand over his heart to feel it beating, to feel the chill waning as his core temp comes up. Floating in the lake gave him back some of his vigor, but not all. Just enough to see him back inside the power plant, back where Butcher left the water bottle for him to drink.

“In for a pound,” he says on a sigh, and resentfully cracks it open, expecting another trick.

But there is none. The water is life-giving on his tongue, revitalizing. He drinks it too fast and spills a little down the side of his mouth.

Lowering the bottle from his lips, his eyes catch again on Butcher’s bag.

He has to stop himself from trying to see into it like he’s always been able to and walks over to it instead. Plunging his hand inside reveals another unopened water bottle at the bottom, much to his unfettered and embarrassing relief. The towel he disturbed uprooting the water bottle peeks out through the open flap. Homelander grabs that, too, and drinks slowly from this second water bottle. Small sips rather than the big mouthfuls of before.

The towel when he rubs it over his head is warm, even in spite of a few damp patches. He remembers how the other bottle, the decoy, the trap, had been sweating against it. Enticingly cold in his hand, even through his glove.

Inside the bag, Butcher’s untouched change of clothes sits unobtrusively. Doing nothing at all to draw Homelander’s eye, but luring him in all the same. He plucks out the joggers, the ones he recognizes as Starlight’s from some brand deal back when. A backward glance at his suit reinforces what he already knows and doesn’t want to accept. The mere thought of putting it back on now, all that crisp, unyielding fabric pressing in closely to his aches and bruises, makes him feel — tired. 

So he shakes out Starlight’s joggers, black with white text across the back, and he pulls them on. They’re soft and loose enough to billow rather than cling. 

Gratitude chokes his throat. He hates himself for it.

He examines the shirt next, mouth scrunching up in distaste at the design. Rolling his eyes and sighing for good measure, he sticks his arms through the holes and pulls it on over his head. Like before, he picks up saccharine notes of strawberry beneath the clean detergent smell. What he hadn’t noticed before, though, and what he notices now, is the hint of leather and woodsmoke.

Butcher, he thinks distantly. 

As before, heat churns low in his gut. Sense memory replays for him what it had been like — what Butcher had been like. How he’d reduced Homelander to a series of impulses. How he could’ve had anything he wanted if he just decided to take it.

That’s power. Not the taking of things; possession is petty and impermanent, as Homelander has come to know well. But the exhumation of another person’s will? The coaxing toward enthrallment and liberation, the manifestation of these things as supplication?

Homelander knows it for what it is because he’d done it with his fanbase. He’d shown them an unapologetic representation of what they resented ever having to hide in themselves, and he had given them full reign to share in his glory. Familiarity with the mechanism doesn’t prevent its efficacy, though, and he’s impressed, in spite of himself, that Butcher found his way to it so quickly. And in fact, he can’t begrudge Butcher that power. Not truthfully. Not when it only serves to prove that he’d been right.

Butcher is a match for him. He’s exactly what Homelander knew he could be, and more.

More than Maeve or Stilwell or even Stormfront. More than Becca Butcher, for all that she’d given him something no one else could. He’d tried in different ways to be worthy of them. Had grasped at prestige and power and perfection in an effort to deserve them. And still, he hadn’t been what they wanted. Still, he could never bend them the way they could bend him.

It makes him wonder if Butcher would bend. If he ever has, for anyone.

Arming himself with a few scavenged power bars and another bottle of water, Homelander walks back out onto the grass. 

This time he ducks under the blasted wall so he won’t have to contend with the door. Lowering his head sends floating currents of migraine pain behind his eyes, but it subsides as soon as he straightens out.

He spreads out on the grass in a patch of sunlight and closes his eyes. The grass is itchy against his skin, but he couldn’t make himself touch the blanket in Butcher’s bag. Not even to test whether it was as soft as the clothes he’s deigned to wear. And besides, it’s a boon enough to his painful muscles to lay back and release the tension in his body. 

Sleep doesn’t come to him easily, but he does feel himself drift in the space between thoughts. In his drifting, a dream finds him, and in his dreaming, he finds her. 

Becca Butcher. 

She’s dead or dying, laid out at the base of the tree where she’d breathe her last breath.

Homelander’s knelt beside her, not quite where Butcher had been. Not close enough to hold her face in his hands and kiss her cooling forehead. He looks around, wondering where Ryan is, or Stormfront, or Butcher. But it’s only the two of them, and when he turns to study her drawn face, she’s looking at him.

There’s no hatred in her face. She only looks calm, and strangest of all, like she’s been waiting for him to get here.

She doesn’t speak. She just watches him, blue eyes pale in the gray light.

“He chose you,” he hears himself saying. “Why?”

Becca Butcher doesn’t respond. Not even to mock him for his curiosity or else spit in his face.

“What was it about you?”

Certainly, she was beautiful. So many women are, in some capacity, but only a rare few could be said to have her fight. Her backbone. Was that why? Was that the reason?

She closes her eyes, and Homelander seizes her shoulders, shaking her. Trying to keep her here.

“What made him love you?”

“I didn’t need him,” she says, not from her body, but from behind him.

He turns, baffled in the next instant when he feels her vanish out from under his hands. She stands tall and proud, the way she had at that holiday party when she first caught his eye, unhurt and unblemished. The color in her cheeks matches the warmth entering the sky at her back. Like all the vitality in the daylight depended upon her alone.

“I wasn’t waiting for him,” she continues. “I wasn’t waiting for anyone. But you don’t know anything about that, do you?”

“What don’t I know about?” he asks, looking up at her from his place on his knees.

Becca Butcher cups his cheek in her hand, all tenderness she never had for him in life, and she says, “Being alone.”

“I’ve been alone my whole life.”

“No,” she muses, sliding her hand back to fist it in his hair exactly the way Butcher had done. Her lip curls cruelly at one end, exactly like Butcher’s. “You’ve been lonely.” She yanks hard on his hair, pulling him tight like piano wire, just like Butcher. Against his cheek, she growls, “Tell Daddy.”

He wakes to a pink sky and uneasiness behind his sternum. He presses his hand there to feel his quickened heartbeat, and beneath that, way down below deeper than he can explain, he can feel pinpricks of sensation returning to him. Coming back.

Breathing in deeply, closing his eyes, and feels around in that abstract darkness, that complex well of vacuous openness at the core of him.

Behind his eyes, the barest, gentlest heat blooms. Not quite at full power, but burgeoning.

It isn’t the end. Not even close.

 

/

 

They’ve gone through the footage from Butcher’s button cam four times before anyone says anything. Butcher polishes off the last of his Big Gulp and bins it.

It’s Frenchie who clears his throat and says, “You couldn’t get a clearer shot of him taking your dick?”

“With the camera up here and my dick down here?” Butcher says, pointing out the physical discrepancy. “You weren’t gonna see too much of it anyway, bein’ that it was inside him at the time.”

Hughie, with his face pressed right up against the screen, says, “There’s a good shot in this frame.” He sits back and slows the playback down so they can follow where he points with his finger. “See?”

Butcher claps his hands together. “That’s that then. We got the cunt.”

Hughie, the lads, and Annie hum noncommittally. Even Kimiko, when he looks at her, winces.

“What?” Butcher asks.

“From the usable angles you’ve got, it just sorta looks like a halfhearted deepfake.”

“English, Hughie,” MM says.

“He’s saying it looks like you found someone that looked a little bit like Homelander and then got creative with Photoshop,” Annie explains. “Even with the uniform falling off him, he could be anyone you picked up at Vought-Con.”

“I was this close to him,” Butcher says, holding his hand up directly in front of him.

Hughie shrugs, telling him, “That’s kind of the problem, dude.”

“Fuck do you mean?”

“The button cam is either too close for his face to be visible,” Frenchie says, over the sound of Kimiko typing rapidly on her phone beside him, “or he is facing away from you.”

“Just had to put him facedown in the dirt, didn’t you?” MM murmurs.

“He weren’t fucking complaining.”

“Ehhh, he was a bit, but we can work with that,” Hughie says.

“Goddamn it,” Butcher mutters, pinching the bridge of his nose. “Didn’t I say we’d only get one shot at doin’ it this way?”

“The pictures are serviceable,” Frenchie observes, motioning for Hughie to pull those up in place of the looping sex video. “Artistic, even. I predict these will be purchased and traded over the internet like baseball cards in the days to come.”

Finally finished typing, Kimiko holds her phone out for him to read:

 

It isn’t the face or the body that makes a person recognizable. How we look, even how we talk and act, can be copied. It’s the hidden things that can’t be faked.

You were close here, but you looked away too soon.

 

“What, I shoulda stayed behind to see if he’s a big spoon or a little ‘un?”

“Not a bad idea,” MM says, hijacking Hughie’s cursor to pick through the photos more deliberately. “Think about how much he might say while his guard’s down.”

“Not merely that — pardonnez-moi.” 

Frenchie selects a picture Butcher’d snapped of Homelander when he hadn’t been sure if he’d choked the cunt to death or not. The look on his face was more peaceful than Homelander deserved. Small wonder it’d been Butcher that made him feel it.

“It is entirely likely you have an opportunity here to render him even more helpless than this.” Pointing at the screen, Frenchie adds, “With a minimum expenditure of force on your part.”

Hughie looks over his shoulder at Frenchie, eyebrows settling blankly on his face. “Oh,” he says.

“Yes, I’m afraid,” Frenchie says.

“I ain’t winin’ and dinin’ that twat.”

“But you wouldn’t need to,” Hughie rushes to explain. “What did you say before, Butcher, he’s the type to beg? So sweeten the pot. If this is how he gets when you’re rough? What’s he gonna do if you take it the other way? Try a little tenderness?”

“As much as I hate to say you’re right — excuse me, Frenchie.” Annie toggles the display over to pull up the video and skips ahead to a point near the end. “Just here, what did his face look like? Do you remember?”

With the camera pointed steadily at Homelander’s torso, there’s no way for them to know unless Butcher tells them, how Homelander’s pupils had blown wide and dark and hazy. How his mouth and the tension in his neck had gone loose and pliant, drunk at a word from Butcher’s lips. Rapturous at his own loss of power. Of control.

Tell Daddy.

“How should I know,” he mutters, turning away from the screens at long last to put some distance between himself and the camera showing his perspective. “I’m not hearin’ a plan for how we fix the matter of the camera. Nothin’ else we’ve got works. This was s’posed to be the answer.”

Immediately in a peacemaking mindset, Hughie says, “The obvious thing would be to scrap the button cam.”

“Could you use your cell phone?” Annie proposes. “He didn’t know I was filming him that time at Vought. He’d hear a camera or a drone because it’s not supposed to be there, but everyone has a phone in their pocket all the time.”

“Not in Arcadia. It’ll look conspicuous anywhere I try to put it.”

“Maybe we don’t scrap the button cam just yet,” Frenchie says, scrounging around for a legal pad and a pen to write with. “I can’t believe I didn’t think to do these calculations before you left. MM, would you engage the button cam, s’il vous plaît? Merci.”

“What’re you doin’, Frenchie?” MM asks, holding the button cam aloft and testing the position against the corresponding display on the screen.

“Solving for field of view,” he mutters, scribbling numbers all across the page. 

“Give it here,” Butcher says, motioning to take the button cam. He pins it crudely over his fly and waves his hand in front of his crotch. “This more what you had in mind, Frenchie?”

“Casse-toi, méchant.”

“You know what,” Annie says, looking from Butcher to the screen and then, of all places, to Hughie. “I bet we could workshop the blocking.”

MM’s quicker on the uptake than Butcher and Hughie combined. He says, “Good idea.”

Frowning, Hughie tries to object. “Sorry—”

But Annie’s faster than he is. She says, “You be Homelander.”

“Sorry?” Butcher muses, incredulous. “You want us to act it out like barbie dolls?”

“It’s this or advanced trigonometry,” MM warns pragmatically.

“I don’t need instructions on how to fuck someone,” Butcher argues, or tries to.

MM gestures at the footage they can’t use, and at the button cam poking out near the button on Butcher’s jeans. He says, “Clearly you do.”

“Uh, what’re we workshopping here?” Hughie asks, raising his hand nervously. “I don’t need to be naked for this, do I?”

“We’ve all seen you in your birthday suit, dude,” MM says.

“Yeah, well, I was under the impression none of you were happy about it,” Hughie grumbles, letting Annie nudge and prod him to stand shoulder-to-shoulder with Butcher. “Hey,” he says in an undertone just for Butcher.

“All right,” Butcher answers reflexively.

“Okay, let’s try it with the camera closer to your collar?” Annie says, waving her hand to show where she means.

It’s MM who sets about unclipping it from Butcher’s fly.

“Remember Vegas?” Butcher croons, grinning.

Interest sparking to match the light in his eyes and in his matching grin, Hughie says, “Oh, my God, Vegas? What happened in Vegas?”

MM rolls his eyes and repositions the button cam, poking Butcher with it in the process.

“I second that question, actually?” Annie says, helping Hughie to climb Butcher like a tree.

Kimiko gets in on it, signing something halfway to frantic — nearly that sign for fucking, but stalled just shy of meeting in the middle. She looks at Frenchie, trying to clue him into the conversation, but he’s still got his head bent over the chaos of maths on the yellow paper in front of him. MM isn’t paying attention and doesn’t see that Frenchie’s occupied, so he points at him without looking over and says:

“Frenchie, don’t you fucking dare.”

“It is only a hypotenuse,” Frenchie mumbles distractedly, finally looking up. “Oh, merde. But what is this?”

“Staging,” Annie answers dismissively, waving her hand. “What happened in Vegas?”

“Frenchie,” MM blurts.

“I did not say anything!”

Butcher chuckles and switches Hughie over to one hand, fiddling with the camera on his collar and checking the the screen as he does. Kimiko signs something to him with her whole body, pleading by the looks of it, and Annie piles on, over MM’s insistence that they drop it.

“You are literally the most chaotic person I know,” Hughie muses.

“Don’t gotta sound so pleased about it, do ya, Hughie,” Butcher teases back, tweaking the camera and then his collar when it won’t lay flat.

“Kinda think I’m the last person with any business judging you on that.” He unclasps the button cam and mounts it on Butcher’s shoulder instead of his collar, angling it up at their faces and cross-referencing it against the display on the screen. Sighing at the camera’s limitations, he says, “Stationary mount might be our best bet here.”

“You don’t say,” Butcher murmurs, propping him up a little higher.

Hughie gets a little pink-cheeked at that and twists away in the slightest capacity that he’s got room to do it. Butcher smirks, and Hughie gives him a tetchy little glare.

“Don’t you start,” he threatens.

“Start what,” Butcher says, but his smile’s too wide to be anything but carnivorous. 

“Annie,” Hughie complains, “save me.”

A camera shutter clicks, and when they look over, it’s Annie what’s snapping pictures of them, a sly little grin on her face. Her phone pings while Hughie’s flailing for her to delete it, and the look on her face goes from amused to bewildered.

“Hughie, is that…your shirt?”

She turns the phone around, and there, trending on Instagram, is a series of photos of Homelander in the sky. His suit’s wadded up under his arm, and he’s wearing Annie’s joggers. The shirt he’s got on is blue with Billy Joel’s face plastered across his chest. He looks smaller than usual out of the cape. His bare feet are the color of milk against all that open sky.

“Guess it wore off,” Butcher says idly, letting Hughie down when his struggling ain’t the funny kind anymore.

“Think he’ll come here?”

“No, why would he?” 

Butcher looks around at their setup. They’re not at the office but at a squat little storage unit downtown, more unfindable to the average passerby. Frenchie hadn’t liked the idea of keeping sensitive footage where they worked, and where people like Hughie’s dad might be stopping by on any regular day ending in -y, so they’d improvised. It ain’t anyone’s picture of ideal, but it’s private, off-book, and it’s got just enough room for them to stretch out and work.

“You don’t think he’ll be a little upset that you drugged him?” MM asks blandly.

Frenchie kills the feeds and the audio, glancing around at the ceiling in unveiled paranoia.

“We lined this with zinc when we set up, didn’t we?” Hughie says, pounding his fist experimentally on the flimsy wall.

“Certainly we did,” Frenchie confirms. “But who knows what a man recently repossessed of laser vision will do if properly motivated? He may strike at random, purely to remind himself that he can.”

Kimiko shakes her head, holding her hands up for Frenchie to read. But it’s Butcher she’s looking at; she hooks her finger up from the mound of her palm like a bird taking wing, and bounces her knuckles against that selfsame foundation before drawing her thumb down the hinge of her jaw. Frenchie frowns, looking from her to Butcher like he’s seeing him for the first time. Butcher looks expectantly between them.

“Well? Let’s hear it.”

“She says, uh…”

Rolling her eyes, Kimiko takes out her phone to type.

 

He’s wearing your clothes. It isn’t his powers he’s having trouble holding onto.

 

A lick of heat, small but insistent, unfurls low in his belly. He motions for Annie to give him her phone and scans through the trending photos. His eyes aren’t processing the information. He just don’t know yet how to play it — like it’s what he planned, or like he’s as surprised by this development as the rest of them are.

“Still having second thoughts about going in with a softer touch?” Hughie asks faintly, looking over Butcher’s shoulder to peer down at the pictures. He sighs, fussy in how he does it. “He looks like an asshole. He looks like such an asshole, he makes Billy Joel look like an asshole.”

“Oy, Hughie,” Butcher murmurs. “Don’t you ever besmirch Billy Joel.”

With slack, soft sweetness on his face, Hughie looks at him, as vulnerable as he’s ever looked. Butcher hooks his arm around Hughie’s neck and knuckles his ear.

Another camera shutter draws Hughie’s attention, but Butcher stays on him.

“See that?” he says, tugging at Hughie’s earlobe. “I can be sweet.”

“You can be a complete menace,” Hughie retorts, but his smile’s on the watery side. “And I know you can be sweet.” He pulls back just enough to point at a blurry photo of Homelander wearing his t-shirt. “But he doesn’t know that. So show him, dude.”

Butcher sighs, letting him go. “Got any ideas about how?”

“As it happens,” Frenchie says, hiding his phone away when Butcher turns to look at him. He clears his throat. “Yes. But I doubt if you will like it.”

His face must go to show just how right Frenchie is because this time it’s Hughie feeling bold enough to wind his arm around Butcher’s neck. He smiles brightly at Butcher’s flat look, undaunted, now as ever, by Butcher’s moods.

“How’s your pillow talk, man?”

“Ah, Jesus.”

“That bad, huh,” MM says.

“Oh, so you do remember Vegas.”

MM groans, and it must be clear as day that he needs a break before they start in on him because Annie and Kimiko descend on MM and Frenchie instead. It’s just Hughie that sticks with him.

“It’s gonna be all right, Butcher.”

“You think so, do ya?”

“Yeah, I mean…you were married. I know you’ve got moves.” He hesitates, grimacing. “Moves you maybe don’t wanna use…in this scenario. Yeah…”

“It ain’t that.”

“No?”

“Nah, she weren’t never one to charmed, Becca. Not like you’d think.”

They played and joked and gave each other shit, him and Becca, but they weren’t romantic in the way people generally thought of romance. He loved her, and everything he wanted for them and their life, he wanted it for the sake of both of ‘em being happy.

Hughie watches his face, something just like glee crossing his face at the confluence of all the things Butcher’s remembering about Becca, and what loving her was like.

“Oh, so that’s how it was. You didn’t charm her, she charmed you.” Hughie laughs, saying, “Okay, tough guy. We’ll sort you out. Uh, I mean…sometime, we’ll sort you out. You’ve probably had enough of us picking all your shit apart for one night.” He braces his hands on Butcher’s shoulders and spins him around to face the group where they’re a few choice words from squeezing the truth out of Frenchie. “Uh, guys? Are we done here?”

“Sure are,” MM says, powering all their equipment down in one swift motion and scooping Butcher up to march him toward the one exit. In an undertone, so the noise of the rolling door drowns him out as he opens it, he hisses, “It was one time, Butcher.”

“What are you two whispering about?” Annie calls after them, audible laughter in her voice.

“A whole lotta nothing!” MM shouts back, still just marching Butcher in the direction of the subway so they can head home.

The sky overhead is dark gray with clouds and pollution. He thinks about Arcadia again. Wondering one more time what the sky looks like out there after the sun’s gone down. Wondering, for the first time, if he’ll have that many more chances to see it. Just once, before it’s all over.

However it ends.

Chapter 7: mercy seat

Chapter Notes

It's my birthday (the 13th)! 🎉
Tell me I'm cute and brush my hair. 💓

See the end of the chapter for more notes

Butcher’s halfway through the playlist Kimiko sent to his phone when the black SUV pulls up onto the curb just ahead of where he’s walking. 

He tugs an earbud free, music trailing out through the loose speaker. 

Such a thing of wonder in this crowd
I’m a stranger in this town
You’re free with me

She had the right idea, probably, but rather than get him in the mindset to be gentle, he’s in the mood for a good, old-fashioned brawl. As if in answer, a crewcut in a suit steps out from the passenger seat and leans back with his arm draped behind him over the top of the door. He’s got a polished and professional look about him, from his matte black shades to his shiny Oxfords.

“Mr. William Butcher,” he says. “We’re your ride, sir.”

“That so,” he demurs, beginning to smirk. “On whose dime?”

“Vought International.”

Butcher feels the edges of his mouth pull down. He’s been waiting on Mallory to jump him since reading him in on her little black site, but he hadn’t been expecting Vought. Seems foolish in hindsight. He’s alive at all right now on account of stolen property. Did he really think they’d never figure it out? 

Mallory had it in two guesses, and she weren’t motivated by profit margins in the same way a multimillion dollar company would be.

Crewcut cocks his head like a dog hearing a whistle, torquing Butcher’s suspicion.

Doing the mental equivalent of shouting in his own head, he thinks, What if I’d rather smash you to pieces in front of all these nice people than go anywhere with you lot?

Crewcut’s mouth twitches incrementally into a smile, sewing a dimple into his cheek on that side. He ducks his chin, peering at Butcher over the lenses on his glasses, and muses, “Ms. Barrett told me you were good.”

Sighing, Butcher pulls his other earbud out and winds the cord around his knuckles. He says, “Somehow I doubt that.”

“She sent me instead of the usual guys,” Crewcut replies gamely, drawing Butcher’s eye to the straight white veneers in his mouth when he laughs, light and breezy. “That’s a compliment. Believe me.”

Butcher takes a step toward him, and Crewcut, matching him, peels himself out of his casual lean against the door.

“To answer your question,” he says, idly pocketing his hands. “In the event that you decide you don’t want to come quietly, I suspect we’d have an incident on our hands — lots of unwanted attention. Press, law enforcement,” he continues in that same clipped, pristinely polite voice. Tipping his head at the busy sidewalk and the small number of rubberneckers they’ve already accrued, he adds, “Not to mention witnesses. The collateral damage at this hour would be catastrophic. But it’s up to you, sir.”

When Butcher don’t have a response to that, Crewcut shrugs expansively, hands still hidden away in his pockets. 

“I know,” he says. “You hate supes, especially the ones that get in your head.”

“Last two of ya that tried didn’t have the chance to do it twice,” Butcher warns. 

“You’re not lying about that,” Crewcut observes coolly. For all his imperturbable calm, a wrinkle knits its way between his eyebrows, embellishing otherwise clear, spotless skin. “Why talk to me at all then? Why not settle it your way and be done with it?”

Walking up to the car, Butcher drawls, “Like you said. I fuckin’ hate supes.”

“Another truth.” Crewcut opens the rear passenger side door and motions for Butcher to climb in. Inching his shades down the bridge of his nose so Butcher can see the sharp blue of his eyes, he adds, “How humanitarian of you.”

Butcher wastes no time getting the music going in his ears again. He’s in a metal box with a mindreader for the next however-long, and he can’t have his thoughts running away from him.

It’s one of MM’s playlists he tabs over, honing every ounce of his concentration on the lyrics.

Blast me, but they didn’t finish
Didn’t diminish my powers
So now I’m back
To be a motherfuckin’ menace

Eventually, the more that the city passes him by from the other side of tinted windows, his mind does begin to wander. With the music still filling his head, he opens a text to MM. He doesn’t even know what he’s gonna say until he’s twenty words into the text. 

MM’s gonna kill him for bringing this shit up twice in as many weeks, but the clearance level on their biblical weekend in Vegas is lower than — other things he can’t be heard thinking about.

 

You ever think abt Treasures in the Industrial Corridor? That job we did. The political one. We followd that justice twat cross the city all night til he wound up in them VIP backrms covered in a mntn of coke w that muzzled up wolf in his bed. We lost Frenchie a couple clubs back nd he never believed us abt the wolf. Not even w the pictures. It was on account of him the rest of it even happened. Remember? We were wrapping the job up nd he found that shite little joint called Fox n Wolf r whatever the fuck.

 

He doesn’t have the luxury of waiting for a response and keeps typing, in spite of the checkmarks showing that MM’s seen his text.

 

God bless horny suburban housewives on the Vegas strip r I woulda never stuck my tongue down your throat. What was the one birds name that suggested it? Susan?

 

He conjures up an image of the single mom in question: bottle blonde, light plastic surgery, and legs for days. He’d been trying to make it with her when she got the idea that he should lay one on MM. Did something for her seein’ two guys together — and that might could’ve gotten him in with her, but then he had kissed MM, hadn’t he? And MM had kissed back. 

She hadn’t much interested him once that happened.

Sharon, MM texts back. Her name was Sharon.

 

Right yeah, Sharon. Think she still got them pictures on her phone? I bet I hve her number lyin around someplace. She could send em to me. At least the one of you w your hand up my shirt. I liked that one.

 

You do this shit on fucking purpose, Butcher, MM texts back. Everyone’s looking at me now like they know.

 

Not my fault you got tells a mile wide, is it?

 

The SUV pulls to a stop, whisper soft and deliberate up against another curb. 

Crewcut opens the door for him, an obvious smattering of pink across the bridge of his nose. Apparently MM ain’t the only one with tells. Butcher smirks and pulls his earbud, the music trailing while he takes his time feeling around for the button to silence it.

For real
I keeps it ill
Like a piece of blue steel
Pointed at your temple
With the intent to kill

“See anything you like?” Butcher croons.

“Most people think about sex to keep their mind off real things,” Crewcut says. Then he tips his head. “Usually there’s an element of shame involved. Or at least a…notion of privacy.”

Butcher gives him a look, coyly amused.

“I’ve never been made so fast before,” Crewcut admits. “You caught me with my pants down a little here.”

“Not yet I haven’t,” Butcher pops back, oozing sleaze just for the hell of it.

That wins him another laugh, sincere-sounding, warm. Butcher’s surprised to find he don’t mind him. If they were anywhere else and the circumstances were just a tiny bit different, and if Butcher didn’t know all the chatter in his head were bein’ broadcast directly into Crewcut’s, they might have something fun here.

Crewcut turns to walk with Butcher toward the Vought building. He says, “Could still.”

“Nah. No offense.”

“Oh, what,” Crewcut says, playful-like, but obviously fishing. “You don’t fuck supes?” 

He’s been balls deep in two of the Seven now, and while he ain’t remotely lookin’ to up that number, he can’t do nothin’ to bring it down either. It hits him, belatedly, that he shouldn’t be thinking about that in such clear terms just now, but Crewcut don’t wait for an answer in words.

“Or is it more that you do fuck supes, but you’re more of a one-at-a-time kinda operator?”

“Don’t know what ya mean.”

“Don’t start lying to me now, Mr. Butcher,” he teases, still like he’s playing, or like he’s flirting, Butcher can’t get a clear read on him. “It’s not that complicated, is it? A vial goes missing in a matter of seconds, and not too long after, there you are on a low quality cell phone video taking to the sky like it’s what you were made to do.”

“Sounds pretty fuckin’ complicated to me,” Butcher mutters.

He does his absolute damndest then not to think of that first time in his kitchen, slamming Homelander down onto the linoleum, feeling stronger than he ever has. Even on the Temp V.

Crewcut glances sideways at him, that cunning lilt tugging at the edge of his mouth again. They walk under the awning of the building, out of the sun, and he slides his shades off his nose and tucks them into the breast pocket on his suit jacket. He looks more like a regular person outside of direct sunlight. Still good-looking, maybe even more so.

“Thank you.” He gets the door for Butcher and motions for him to pass through first before walking shoulder to shoulder with him in the direction of the lifts. “That’s why you’re here.”

“What is?”

“To explain what makes it so complicated,” he answers glibly, meeting Butcher’s eyes. “And why you’re doing everything in your power to keep from thinking about one person in particular, to the point of blue-balling your poor friend via text message.”

He presses the button for the lift, and it comes straight away. They board together, watching the numbers climb and climb without speaking. Butcher can’t help it. Without the music or MM’s texts to distract him, his mind wanders to the forbidden thing. Or one of them, anyway. And that being the case, he chooses the lesser of two evils. It don’t escape him, the irony of that bein’ Homelander.

Homelander trapped underneath him in the muddy grass at Arcadia; Homelander’s tongue curled around Butcher’s fingers in his mouth; Homelander half out of his suit, slighter in his bare skin than he ever looks with the gauntlets and epaulets and muscular padding, gilt ornamentation meant to bulk up the image of him as perfection in a cape; Homelander’s eyes, hazy and far away, but right-fucking-there, locked onto Butcher’s eyes, nowhere else; Homelander crying out for him in desperation, in frantic need the likes of which he’s never known before; Homelander asking so sweetly to be kissed.

For Daddy to take care of him.

Crewcut breathes in slowly, deeply, and loosens his tie. The lift climbs and climbs.

“I’m Forester, by the way.”

“Billy,” Butcher replies.

“Good to meet you, Billy. Maybe when this is all over, if Ms. Barrett lets you leave, we can get outta here. I’ll buy you a drink for your trouble.”

Butcher sees through layers and layers shiny chrome to what’s waiting for them at their designated floor. In time with the inoffensive pinging sound from the lift, he says, “Wouldn’t count on that if I were you, son.”

The doors slide open. In a cleanly pressed uniform with his hands held placidly behind his back, Homelander greets them with a bloodless smile.

His eyes rove impassively across Crewcut’s features before coming to roost on Butcher, blanking out all the noise in his head. He thought he had another few days before he’d see Homelander again; thought they’d be in Arcadia when it happened and that he’d be braced for being near him again. But here he is, and Butcher don’t know what to feel at the sight of him.

Like it’s him that can read minds, Homelander’s mouth quirks in a smile, effusive, the kind he’d break out for a photoshoot. “Butcher,” he gushes. “And Forester, was it?”

Hesitating, Crewcut says, “Yes, sir.”

“Oh, we can dispel with all that, I think.” He claps Crewcut on the arm, chummy-like, but hard enough to dislodge him from his stance. “I can take it from here, bud.”

“I…respectfully, s—”

“Homelander,” he enunciates, when Crewcut colors at his own misstep and doesn’t recover.

“Respectfully, Homelander, I’m — supposed to report to Ms. Barrett. With Mr. Butcher in tow.”

“Are you,” Homelander muses, smile widening. He palms Crewcut’s arm and walks him down the corridor, looking back at Butcher to make sure he follows. “Well, all right. Best not keep Miss Ashley waiting. Lead the way, Forester.”

Butcher gets out his phone, ignores the wall of texts from MM to text back:

 

Urgent. Monitor me. Maybe category red.

 

He toggles the recording feature on his phone that streams directly to the cloud Hughie set up for what he’s called ‘The Butchlander Spank Bank’. They won’t have much to look at from inside his pocket, but as long as they get the audio, that’s all he needs. He doesn’t know what’s coming once him, Crewcut, and Homelander are on the other side of a closed door, but it can’t be good.

Homelander bursts in through the door to Ashley’s office with Crewcut’s bicep firmly gripped in his fist. Ashley startles to standing, and across the desk from her, A-Train and Deep jump to their feet, too. All of them are on high-alert.

Butcher trails into the room last, staying close to the wall.

Trying to get her wits back about her, Ashley straightens out her blazer and says, “Insight, are you all right?”

“Insight,” Homelander echoes. “That’s funny, he told me his name was Forester.”

He gives Crewcut a shove so he stumbles in the direction of Ashley’s desk. A-Train steadies him with a hand on his arm, him being the closer of the two.

“All right, man?”

“Yes, sir. I’m sorry, ma’am, I—”

“Oh, I see what’s going on here. Ashley,” Homelander says, clasping his hands together like he’s talking to a child or a puppy. “That was very clever of you, sending a supe to catch a supe.”

A-Train and Deep exchange dubious looks, before glancing around Homelander at Butcher.

“What the fuck did you just say?” A-Train says, nearly starting to laugh.

Homelander’s flatly unamused expression paired with Butcher’s straight stare must be enough of answer for him because he doesn’t ask again. Bored of their onlookers, Homelander returns his attention to Ashley alone.

“I don’t think we need the peanut gallery here for this conversation, do you, Ashley?”

Her mouth presses into a thin line. She says, “Deep, A-Train, Insight. Give us the room.”

“Uh-uh,” Homelander says, pointing out Crewcut. “He stays.”

All the blood drains from Crewcut’s face, but he keeps his spine straight and he doesn’t complain.

“Homelander, man,” Deep starts, tentative, but bodily putting himself in between Homelander and Crewcut. “We’re all that’s left of the Seven. Let us…help with…” He gestures vaguely at Butcher. “Whatever this is.”

“Do I look like I need your help,” Homelander asks tonelessly. Less a question, and more a joke without a punchline. “Are the words fucking pathetic pussy currently flashing in buzzing neon on my forehead? Well? Are they, Deep?”

Deep opens and closes his mouth, his big guileless eyes searching for contingencies and not finding any. Simple twat like him, it’s hard to imagine him ever getting the better of Annie.

Butcher’d be lying outright if he said he didn’t get some enjoyment outta seeing Homelander take a step forward to Deep and A-Train’s cautious steps back. They weren’t doin’ that when it was Robin half a fuckin’ step off the curb, or when it was Annie on her knees with makeup running down her cheeks, all mixed in with tears. No, they were the ones stepping forward then.

From across the room, Crewcut — Insight — locks eyes with Butcher. 

“If I need help breaking into a swimming pool or a public aquarium, I’ll text you,” Homelander says. “How about that?”

Deep flinches and starts for the door with his head hanging low. Butcher stays right where he is. He hasn’t moved a muscle since he planted himself against the back wall near the door.

“You don’t have to do this,” A-Train’s telling Crewcut. “You can come with us. We’ll walk outta here together.”

“As I recall, you don’t have much say in that department.” Homelander flashes a hundred watt grin, the likes of which Butcher’s seen on TV and on the covers of a dozen different magazines in the supermarket checkout. “Your brother is still bedridden with a spinal cord injury he obtained on your watch, isn’t he?”

“Man—”

“Yes?”

A-Train hesitates, then he makes the bigger decision to drop his hands. It looks like it costs him. He looks from Crewcut to Ashley, their matching long faces and glassy eyes.

“Nothing,” he says tightly.

“Great. Now fuck off.”

Butcher waits for them to go. A-Train’s eyes catch on his as he’s crossing to the door.

He says, “You have no idea what he’s capable of.”

“Don’t I,” Butcher mutters.

“No,” Deep says quietly and mournfully from the door, still with his head down. “You don’t.”

The two of them leave without much more in the way of fanfare, and Homelander claps.

“Well, now that it’s just the adults,” Homelander announces, rubbing his hands together. “Ashley, what was it you wanted to talk to us about?”

Holding her ground, Ashley says, “I know you stole the Compound V, and I know you gave it to him. I just want to know why.”

“Hypothetically, and not that I’m admitting to anything, of course, but if he’d been, I don’t know, terminally ill at the time, it would’ve been just the thing to spruce him up. Add some much-needed market value to a highly distressed property. Oh, don’t frown, Butcher. Your brain was leaking out of your ears. Remember that smell? Pus and rot festering in your ear canal, dripping down the back of your throat always. You weren’t anyone’s idea of a prize.”

At that, Crewcut looks pale and like he might be sick.

“What’s wrong with him, why does he look like that?”

“I read minds, sir,” Crewcut answers. He must figure it’ll out eventually. Maybe he thinks he can minimize his punishment if he gets out ahead of it. “But I swear, I was only supposed to read Mr. Butcher. That was the plan. I’d never — I’d never try to interrogate you, sir.”

Homelander turns to face him. Never a good sign.

“He don’t need to be here,” Butcher says, stepping away from the wall. “He got what he needed from me on the lift.”

“Oh, really,” Homelander muses. “You mean when he offered to buy you a drink?”

“Are you two fucking?” Ashley blurts out reflexively.

Rolling his eyes, Homelander says, “What, are you still here?”

“I am CEO of this company,” Ashley asserts, rounding the table, more in her element, it seems, with variables at play that she recognizes: sex, dominance, the inherent power struggle therein. “I called for this meeting because there was a threat to our product. Our branding. And if you attach yourself to that threat, if that threat is you, then we will take action to protect Vought from the danger you pose to it. Do you understand?”

Scowling as he says it, Homelander tells her, “I am your branding, Ashley. Or did you need reminding?”

Unwillingly, and with an impulse behind it that he can’t rightly explain or justify, Butcher thinks of the last time they were in Arcadia. Of the bruises he left all over Homelander’s uncovered, unprotected skin. The rouged scrapes and friction burns down his one knee and across the mound of his thumb. He thinks of how fragile Vought really is, if Homelander is its face and symbol.

Crewcut looks up at him sharply. He really is all made of tells, just like anyone else.

“You lost them,” he says shakily, unbelieving of the words even as they’re coming out of his mouth.

There’s a tenuous, awful silence, and then Homelander, shoulders squared, grabs Crewcut by the chin to stare right into his eyes. Butcher knows even without a running commentary that Homelander’s giving him the whole show from beginning to end. Letting him see every last damning thing he’d been tasked with getting out of Butcher. Everything — or nearly everything — he’d tried and failed to hide from him on the way up here. Homelander lifts Crewcut by his chin so his feet dangle, toes scraping the the floor and never catching. 

“What did I lose,” he whispers.

“Your powers,” Crewcut grits out.

Staggered, Ashley lets out a shocked, bewildered breath.

Butcher feels rather than sees Homelander’s eyes go blisteringly hot, gearing up to use the lasers. His hand twitches to interfere, but he doesn’t let himself follow the instinct to action. Homelander looks at him anyway.

With his eyes gone red like the steaming, ripped out innards of a bird still in its death throes, Homelander murmurs, tonelessly, “Ask me stop. Beg me not to hurt him.”

A sideways, slipping feeling comes over Butcher. He can’t move.

“I said,” Homelander intones, only to raise his voice a few decibels. “Beg.”

Again, that Dutch-angle, slanted-screen feeling rises in him. Like this is a test with only two outcomes: pass or fail. Butcher looks from him to Crewcut. 

Forester, he’d said his name was.

Smoothly, Butcher returns his gaze to Homelander’s and says, “Hurt him. What do I care?”

The loose set to Homelander’s mouth grows edges, serrated along every gleaming tooth like a knife seeking for a vein. He lowers his arm just enough for Crewcut to balance on just his tiptoes. 

“So he means nothing to you?”

“Less than,” he answers blandly, making himself smile through his rising gorge like he’s seen Hughie do a hundred times over. “Ain’t no one in the world makes me feel like you do.”

“Mmm. Every bit as pissed off,” Homelander purrs, “and fucking vile as you really are?”

“Just so.”

“Just so,” Homelander echoes calmly, the corners of his eyes crinkling. 

He turns and sends twin red beams right into Crewcut’s once-blue eyes, burning them right out of their sockets. Hot blood sprays Butcher’s face, and a smell like drilling a tooth, fills the air. Ashley screams.

Homelander drops Crewcut, alive still, twisting on the floor in agony, and fists his other hand in Butcher’s collar. He doesn’t even know they’re moving until his back’s hitting a wall. 

The room’s changed around them, dropped in darkness, and there’s Homelander, crowding him.

“Devouring you all the time with his eyes like I couldn’t see,” he’s saying, teeth set against the hammering pulse in Butcher’s neck. “Blushing like a girl imagining you like this, with him. With anyone.”

There’s a sheen of blood on Homelander’s cheek from nosing at the stain on Butcher’s face.

He opens Butcher’s pants and palms him, chafing him with the glove, gripping him too tightly, too soon. Hurting. But not just. Not just. 

Butcher tries to form a coherent thought and can’t. Tries to tell himself crazy and fucked don’t do it for him, because it don’t, but that smell like shorn bone clings to him like static preceding lightning. Like a hurricane coming to heel at his feet. He fills out in Homelander’s hand, the metallic taste of pennies and salt lingering in his mouth. Without a word, Homelander twists his hand on Butcher’s dick and drops to his knees. 

He bumps the phone in Butcher’s pocket, and Butcher’s eyes slam shut, remembering the audio he’s recording. A second later, with Homelander’s mouth on him, he can’t open his eyes for a different reason altogether. He drops a hand into Homelander’s hair.

When he does manage to scrape his eyes open, Homelander’s looking right at him. He pulls nearly all the way off before gripping Butcher’s hips in his hands and swallowing him down as far as he can, which isn’t saying much. But he holds Butcher’s gaze with his, tightening his grip and loosening his jaw until Butcher understands what he wants. He scoops his hand around to the back of Homelander’s head to hold him still, and then he pulls him down on his dick.

Gasping, Homelander moves one hand to the wall, crafting a bar out of his arm to pin Butcher in place.

He tightens his fist against Homelander’s scalp, feeling himself right on the edge, and pins Homelander right back. Homelander don’t fight him. He swallows it all. They’re frozen like that for a suspended moment in time.

Butcher’s dick goes soft in Homelander’s mouth, and still he don’t pull off. 

His head’s a mess. He can’t string a complete thought together that ain’t just fixating on the smell burnt into his nose. 

Crewcut’s seared orbital ridge gave off the same stench as the inside of Stilwell’s liquefied skull.

Butcher’s still trying to get his breath back when Homelander raises his head and breaks the silence.

“Take off your clothes,” he rasps, “and wait for me on the bed.”

Butcher reaches for his collar to pull his shirt off over his head. Appeased by that, Homelander sits back on his knees and gives him room to pass. In the time it takes him to round the corner and walk to the big four-poster bed on the other side of the huge room, Butcher gets his phone free and mounts it innocuously against his wallet and the ring of keys in his pocket on the bedside table. 

It’s the widest view of the room he can get, the clearest shot. If Hughie can’t find a way to make it work, he doesn’t see them getting a better chance.

He drops trou, in addition to everything else, and climbs up onto the bed. And then he waits.

 

/

 

He should’ve killed Insight is what he should’ve done. But he’d felt a possessive thrill in showing him exactly what he’d never have and in taking his namesake from him.

Maybe he couldn’t sap Insight’s powers the way Butcher’s concoction had done to him, but he has plenty of power to take everything else he wants. Just like he has every power to take this, now, with Butcher.

Unfolding to his full height and undoing the catch at the collar on his suit, he begins to undress as he rounds the corner. He shucks his clothes where Butcher let his own fall, seeing out of the corner of his eye the sweet, lissome shape Butcher cuts against the bedspread.

His legs are stretched out in front of him. One of his hands is laid out, palm up, on the pillow beside his head. The other is at his mouth.

When Homelander looks directly at him, only Butcher’s eyes move, flicking up to his.

He matches his gaze for a few seconds before his eyes drift down and down, scanning the planes of Homelander’s body for the first time in their entirety. Homelander looks at him, too. It’s a first for Homelander, too. He’s seen Butcher naked, but not like this. Not such that he could let himself feast with his eyes, every bit as bold and unapologetic as he once thought himself to be.

How misguided he’d been, thinking he knew himself and what he wanted. When he could barely make himself say in words what it was about Butcher that pleased him, or what it is, even now, that lures him down deeper into this velvet darkness shared between them.

He speeds onto the bed, astride the bridge of Butcher’s hips. Again, only Butcher’s eyes move, following him. Homelander leans in slowly, telegraphing his moments, as he takes Butcher’s wrists in his hands and gently pins them both. He bends down the rest of the way, nosing at the jet of blood drying on Butcher’s skin. 

The taste on his tongue is like rust. Like the phantom feeling of Becca Butcher’s sneer against his cheek.

Against the crow’s feet near Butcher’s eye, he lavishes him with a kiss. Butcher’s eyelashes flutter against the corner of his mouth. Feather soft.

Butcher drops his head back on the pillow so his nose grazes Homelander’s chin. He’s hungry enough for Butcher’s kiss that he brushes their mouths together, lips skimming and breath mingling. Homelander wants it, but he makes himself wait, heart pounding in his ears and in his throat until Butcher cants their mouths together in a soft, burning kiss. It feels like it had in Arcadia, when he’d been without his powers.

The mental encumbrance of fetters that he carries all the time, dissipates. 

Why he should feel more untethered the less he feels able to stop all of this whenever he wants, he can’t reconcile. No chain on mortal design could hold him, but Butcher can.

He had, in Arcadia. 

And how here he lays, allowing Homelander to restrain with the barest pressure of his hands manacling his wrists. Opening his mouth for Homelander’s tongue with such ease, like he feels unable to stop any of this from happening either. Homelander draws his hands away from Butcher’s. He keeps them exactly where Homelander left them.

There’s a noticeable flush in his cheeks and down the supple column of his throat, but his eyes, when they slip open, are far away.

“Say something,” Homelander whispers.

Rolling his shoulders, Butcher only answers, “Like what?”

Homelander seizes his chin and kisses him, chasing that dead drop and only feeling worlds more lost when he finds it. Unmoored, all that warmth and essence of animation passes through him like smoke from an altar. He sinks a hand in Butcher’s hair and makes a fist against his scalp, desperate for a piece of him that he can keep.

Butcher palms his cheek, and Homelander realizes with a shock that his face is wet. A moan from his lips breaks against Butcher’s teeth. He’s on the cusp of begging for more when Butcher’s other hand smooths up his side.

He melts into that touch completely, sick with relief and even more so when Butcher peppers him with kisses; first his lips and then his jaw and then the tender join beneath his ear, unraveling him as handily as if there’d been a seam there waiting to be pulled. Waiting to spill all of him, whatever remains. His voice like smoldering coals follows a puff of warm breath in Homelander’s ear.

“Thought you made a scene back there ‘cuz you wanted to be in charge,” Butcher murmurs. “But that weren’t the shape of why you did it. Was it, darlin’?”

It’s the work of one rollicking motion, Butcher rolling them over. It puts him looming, the obelisk of his body parting and grounding Homelander’s. He felt like he was made of water the last time they fucked. Now he feels earthen. Solid. Whole.

“You want this, sweetheart? Want me?”

Butcher drinks down Homelander’s involuntary whimper. He guides one of Homelander’s legs by the knee up to hook around his hip. 

His fingers tap at Homelander’s lip until he can slide them back over his tongue. Homelander spreads wide open for him in every way that his body will allow, and then, when those fingers seek where he wants them most, the push comes easily, without an ounce of resistance. One gives way sweetly to two, stoking fire in his belly, in his throat, in his balls.

With a hum at Homelander’s hairline, Butcher says, “Want me to take you apart? Like last time?”

“Yes,” he answers on a thready moan, tossing his head back against the pillow. “Yes, Daddy, please.”

Butcher slithers down the length of his body. His head dips lower than Homelander expects, setting him alight in an entirely different way. Incinerating him. All the while, he works Homelander with his fingers, stretching and searing and breaking the world open around him.

It’s by accident that he begins to float off the bed. Butcher hangs off him for a second before following him up, so much more fluid in his handling of flight than he was in the beginning.

Seeing him this way, beautifully nude and in full command of his faculties, sparks mischief in Homelander to rival his need. Bolstered by Butcher’s participation, comforted by having gotten at least a taste of what he wants from him, he rebels.

He swivels back and away to dislodge Butcher’s mouth and fingers, and then he shoves him, hard, several feet to the floor. The impact rends a tear in the carpet beneath Butcher’s shoulder.

For a moment, the silence is deafening.

But then Butcher sits up on his elbows, raising a hand to wipe at his mouth. Around a smirk, he says, “There he is.”

He barrels into Homelander like a freight train, sending them both crashing into the wall. Homelander’s elbow goes through the window, sending a spray of pebbled glass everywhere.

They scrape and scrabble and bite, wrenching and clawing at each other until the fighting inevitably gives way to fucking. Butcher clasps Homelander’s hip with one big hand and pulls him back onto his dick, his other hand braced against the ceiling. Homelander rocks back into him, ravenous for more of however much he can get. Down to the last drop. Like last time.

Butcher’s grip on him slips, and as always, his fraying focus spins his balance in flight just a little off kilter. Homelander seizes him by the meat of his arm and flings him a full arc, slamming him bodily into the adjacent wall.

Seeking for a handhold, Butcher punches a hole through the ceiling and grasps the galvanized steel pipe funneling water through the upper floor. A waterfall opens over their heads.
