Homelander hits the ground from the force of that first jettisoned blast. Butcher touches down at his feet, breathing hard amid the deluge. A color like ichorous amber burns in his eyes.

“C’mon then, love.”

God, but the sight of him.

Homelander meets his refulgent, molten gaze in a swell of vibrant light. The cataclysmic crash obliterates the chandelier overhead, exploding light filaments and tinkling crystal alike. The remaining glass in the window shatters.

They loose another volley of lasers at each other, leaving scorch marks in the walls and burning a  crater through the half wall separating the living quarters from the foyer. 

Butcher tosses Homelander through it and breaks the bed when Homelander vaults back to return fire. The mattress seesaws, half of it flipping sideways into the wall and the other dipping where the pulverized boxspring sits crookedly.

He tackles Butcher onto the floor, climbing on top of him when Butcher pulls at him rather than pushing him away. He spits in his hand, and anticipating his next course of action, Homelander speeds to the bedside table, rips the drawer out, and extracts the pump bottle within.

Upon slamming the drawer shut, Butcher’s meagre belongings rattle on the table; his phone skitters to lay flat. 

Butcher’s still spitting into his palm when Homelander reaches for him. He’s hot and heavy in his hand, and the line of his throat when his head thunks the carpet begs to be tasted. 

Homelander obliges. He swoops down to leave marks all down the side of Butcher’s neck and his Adam’s apple, guiding his dick where they both want it and sinking down, down. When Butcher bottoms out, Homelander licks the groan right out of his mouth.

They’ve never fucked and kissed for so long without interruption. Not even the last time in Arcadia, when Homelander found the words to ask for it. He barely has the words for it now, outside of knowing he wants to stay in this moment forever.

Hands squeeze at his hips, hard enough they would’ve bruised him, once. Butcher urges him to move, and it’s a dead sprint to the end from there. Homelander only breaks their kiss so he can watch Butcher’s face pull tight and pained when he orgasms. In the work of a moment, there’s so much semen, wet and unbelievably hot, inside him.

Homelander touches himself, close enough that it doesn’t take him long. He’s in the air when he finishes.

Sated and pleasantly dazed, he leans back into a lazy recline and floats aimlessly in a ring around Butcher. He lays there with an arm thrown over his face for ten, maybe fifteen seconds, before he lets it drop.

Finally, his eyes open, and he locks eyes with Homelander, still making a loose circuit around Butcher’s spot on the floor. He rolls his eyes and floats opposite Homelander, the two of them lounging in midair, a clear expanse of open air between them; below, charred carpet, broken glass, loose rubble, and burbling water.

“Thought you’d be mad, after.”

Homelander glances over at him, distantly curious. Not understanding. “About what?” he asks.

“You know. Last time.”

“What was it you said to me,” Homelander murmurs. “It wasn’t anything I didn’t want you to do?”

Butcher tips his head, saying, “Even still.”

“I did wonder where you got it, but you aren’t exactly spoiled for options these days. Your little Frenchman must have engineered the compound. Or your friend, Grace Mallory, I suppose. Do you have more of it?”

“Why?”

Homelander shrugs loosely, thinking of Insight’s eyeballs melted down to soup. He hums, saying, “Some of our ilk could stand to be without the gifts they were given.”

“From your lips,” Butcher replies, folding his hands behind his head.

Watching Butcher’s body in profile, he asks, languidly, “What happened to the other two?” 

“Other two what?”

“Out by the car, you told Insight the last two supes that looked into your head didn’t have a chance to do it again. What happened to them?”

“I killed the one,” he answers, faster on the uptake this time. “Your dad got the other.”

Homelander feels himself flinch. 

“Don’t gotta be jealous,” Butcher muses. “It weren’t like that with Soldier Boy.”

Heat sparks at the backs of Homelander’s eyes, and he forces them shut. It drives pain like cutting into his heart to think there might be no one left alive in the world who knows what his father was like, more than Butcher. And he can’t pretend to be ignorant of the reason. 

A warm hand curls around his ankle, cooling the flash of his ire. He opens his eyes to meet Butcher’s gaze, to see his arm hooked beneath the heels of Homelander’s feet so he can cup his ankle. His other arm stays folded up behind his head. The thumb he flicks back and forth over the knob of Homelander’s ankle turns gentle, harmless warmth in his stomach.

“He would’ve loved to have you as his son,” Homelander mutters. “You’re everything he thought I should be.”

“And what’s that then?”

“Self-possessed. Exacting. Indomitable.”

“It ain’t like you think it is,” Butcher says, his line of sight shifting to the middle distance.

“What isn’t?”

Butcher’s expression sharpens. He meets Homelander’s eyes again and says, somberly, “Bein’ what they want ya to be.”

“But you don’t care about that,” Homelander says, nearly asking.

“Not most times,” Butcher allows. He gets a look on his face, roguish and sly. His hand slips half an inch higher to squeeze Homelander’s calf. “Maybe you were right. Maybe I do got a soul.”

“I suppose that’s why you rushed to young Forester’s aid back there,” Homelander croons.

“Please. I done worse than that, even before the V.” Butcher stretches out again. He’s wondrous like this, all that warm skin on display. “Besides,” he adds, in a voice like he’s reading items off a menu. “If I did what you said, if I begged you to let him go, would you’ve done it? Or would you’ve kept going till there weren’t nothin’ of him left just to teach me a lesson?”

“What lesson would that be?” Homelander asks, throat gone tight and hot.

“All this is ‘cuz of you,” Butcher answers. “And only you get to have it back.”

The stinging heat in Homelander’s eyes this time isn’t the red sight. It’s anguished tears he can’t explain away as anything but exactly what they are. He wills Butcher not to look at him, not to catch him weeping twice in the same day, but as befits his nature, Butcher defies him. He looks, and he keeps looking. 

His hand when it comes away from Homelander’s ankle cuts a hole like loss behind Homelander’s sternum. He doesn’t know what to do with himself by this point, with Butcher still just watching him.

Usually he’s gone by now, or Homelander is. Usually that’s enough for him to escape the pit of after and this feeling that no one will ever really take him as he is. But here it is, eating him alive, and Butcher—

He crosses over behind Homelander and anchors an arm around his middle.

Madelyn used to drape herself around him, but not like this. She encouraged him to be small when she held him.

Butcher only holds him.

Through rising panic, Homelander says, “What are you doing?”

“What’d I say,” Butcher mutters. His lips grazing the back of Homelander’s neck are warm, soft. “Start summat, finish it.”

Homelander makes himself breathe, in and out, in and out, until his racing heart slows down. He covers Butcher’s hand with his, the tension in his neck and shoulders leaving him the longer he doesn’t leave.

In the course of roving idle fingertips along the backs of Homelander’s knuckles, Butcher feels the spring of his shame. He hooks his chin over Homelander’s shoulder to peer down the planes of Homelander’s body to his hand, his fingers fanned out over Butcher’s for inspection.

“What happened here?”

No words come to mind. How can he say he doesn’t know? How can he say that when he tried to pop it back into place after the V replenished him, that it didn’t heal right? How can he say he’s glad for it? He can’t.

He can’t.

“Oh, when I…” Butcher trails off, still just holding Homelander’s hand in his. “Huh.”

Feeling the heat rise in his face, he looks over his shoulder, as vulnerable as he’s ever felt. Even including the last time at Arcadia.

This close, the hazel of Butcher’s eyes reminds Homelander of the first touch of fall. He feels himself turning, opening in the way his body’s learned to do by rote when it’s Butcher; when he can unfurl all that he is without diminishing himself.

He sees Butcher’s gaze dip to his lips, and then his own eyes are halfway to closed when the door to his suite slams open, accompanied by a harried heartbeat he knows well.

“What the fuck?” Ashley says, muttering to herself. She rounds the corner and immediately spins around when she sees them tangled up in each other some feet above the ground. “What the fuck!?”

Butcher runs his knuckles along Homelander’s misshapen pinky and brushes that deferred kiss over his shoulder.

“What do you want, Ashley,” Homelander asks tonelessly.

“The — plumbers, uh. We got word of a…” She gestures vaguely at the torrent coming through the ceiling. “Leak, so. They’re outside. The — plumbers.” She risks a peek over her shoulder at them and whips back around when she sees they haven’t moved. “I need you to put a pin in this until we get the issue resolved. Please, for the love of God.”

Homelander sighs. Butcher angles a look in his direction before easing his hold and dropping down and holding out a hand to guide Homelander down, too.

He doesn’t need it, but he takes it, greedy for the attention. For the care.

Ashley keeps her back turned until she hears drawers rustling, but when she turns again, they’re still naked and rummaging haphazardly through Homelander’s wardrobe.

“I ain’t wearin’ that,” Butcher says of Homelander’s super suit.

“It’s the only thing that’s dry.”

“Oh, for God’s sake,” Ashley mutters. She takes off her blazer and wads it up before throwing it forcefully at Butcher’s chest. She’s got mascara smeared at the corners of her eyes and eyelashes stuck together from crying. “Put on the pants and get the fuck out of my building.”

Butcher shrugs and pulls on the pants, then Ashley’s blazer. He looks around for his phone, hesitating when he sees the broken screen. They must’ve been a bit more overzealous with the red sight than he realized.

Practically vibrating with agitation, Ashley shouts, “Please get the fuck out of my building.”

“All right, darlin’. Keep your hair on.”

Homelander laughs, startling himself. Startling Butcher and Ashley, too. Butcher walks over to the window, and Homelander trails after him.

“Arcadia?” he asks.

“Yeah,” Butcher says easily. “I’ll see ya there.”

He takes Homelander’s face in his hands and gives him the kiss he’d been wanting when Ashley walked in on them, deep and warm and curling like paper exposed to fire.

There’s a smirk on his face when he pulls away, and a wink he favors Ashley with, before he takes to the sky and disappears.

“I don’t know what the fuck you think you’re doing with him,” Ashley spits, voice wavering with emotion, “but I am not spinning this for you when it comes out.”

“When what does?” he asks indifferently, scratching at the semen drying on his belly.

“Your boyfriend, Homelander.” She points out the window. “Your boyfriend? The guy you’re fucking. The scruffy Brit with the big uncut dick who just left here covered in laser burns. Ringing any bells?”

He plucks a piece of broken glass out of his hair.

“I’m not following you.”

“Oh, my God,” Ashley gasps, pressing her hands to her forehead. “Oh, my God, I’m going crazy. Billy Butcher, Homelander. You’re fucking him.”

“You’re referring to the person you had your men kidnap off 42nd Street?” he asks flatly.

Ashley drops her hands, red splotches filling her face. Her eyes are huge and terrified.

“It’s funny,” he continues, “Vought surveillance footage won’t incriminate me if your mysterious break-in comes to light. But it will be very interesting to see how it plays out, your closed-door meeting with a civilian who, until a month ago, had no powers to speak of. Now, suddenly he does, and you’ve very publicly made moves to establish — or maintain — contact with him. You even had those imbeciles, Deep and A-Train, waiting to welcome him into the fold. How will that look?”

Ashley abortively touches her wig and then crosses her arms tightly over her chest. She takes a quick heaving breath, looking on the verge of tears.

“Don’t ever threaten me again, Ashley.”

She presses her lips into a thin white line. The dried tear tracks on her cheeks stand out in starker definition with all the blood drained from her face.

“And not in front of him,” he adds, just for good measure.

“No, I won’t.”

“Good.”

“But you need to be careful,” she says in an undertone. “Before he passed out, Insight said something.”

“What’s that?”

She swallows and wets her lips, hesitating for a few long seconds before she says, “He didn’t know. There was…he said Butcher hid something behind you. So that he wouldn’t see it. But it was there, Homelander, and you need to be careful.”

“Because of something nobody saw?” he asks, beginning to smile.

“We know better than most that just because something can’t be seen, doesn’t mean it isn’t there.” She shakes her head and reaches for her phone again. “I need the plumbers in here.”

He finds a suit to change into, just to tide him over until he can get to the townhouse and clean up properly. “When you bring the cleaning crew in here next,” he says, “tell them to take the mirrors.”

“All of them?”

“What do you think, Ashley?”

Floating out through the same window Butcher took, he breaks the sound barrier traversing the clear blue sky.

The last thing on his mind is reflections or hidden things. All he’s thinking about as he leaves the atmosphere to drift in the cold, soundless void, is how Butcher’s hand had looked holding his. How his lips on Homelander’s shoulder had felt, of all things, like protection.

He cradles his imperfect hand with the other and allows his eyes to drift closed.

Chapter End Notes

Tunes:
Everybody Here Wants You by Jeff Buckley
Ambitionz Az a Ridah by 2Pac
How High by Redman & Method Man

Chapter 8: hyssop, myrrh, &amp; spikenard

Chapter Notes

See the end of the chapter for notes

Butcher flies straight to the storage unit, after Vought. He needs a shower, and he needs to touch base with the lads, but he ain’t ready for that yet. He opens up the unit and slams the rolling door shut behind him.

The whole of the unit bein’ lined in zinc gives him some peace, but only just. He leans his back against the rolling door and waits, trying to get his breath now that the day’s beginning to catch up with him. That’s what he blames it on, that it takes him a second to notice the flashing red light on one of the mounted cameras. Butcher’s staring right at it when something in the unit with him starts to vibrate.

He goes rooting through the little tower of drawers shoved over into the corner until he finds it. There’s a burner phone hooked up to a power bank. 

Glancing up again at the red light on the camera, he flips the phone open. He holds it to his ear. “Frenchie,” he says. “That you?”

“Vraiment,” Frenchie replies. “More importantly, it is you! We thought you had died.”

“What, again?”

“You went off comms,” Frenchie reminds him. “In the course of a kidnapping.”

“Only technically,” Butcher pops back dismissively. He allows for a ruminative pause, and then he asks, “Didja get it?”

The weighty silence that comes in answer tells him everything he needs to know. He sighs, long and slow, and drops down into the beanbag chair someone brought into the unit. Frenchie gives him a moment to get his wits in order, and then he starts in on him again, more sympathetically than Butcher deserves outta him.

“You may access what we have from the laptop in the unit,” Frenchie advises. “Everything from the time you positioned the phone up until the device was destroyed. But perhaps you leave it for some other night, non?”

Butcher tips his head back, the beanbag chair squishing around the crown of his head. He asks, “Have you seen it?” 

Frenchie clears his throat.

“Guess I did say Category Red, didn’t I,” he mutters.

“Mais oui, you did.”

Another silence rises up between them. In it, Butcher can hear Frenchie switching the phone over to speaker, and he can hear, too, those punctuated slicing gestures cut the air — him and Kimiko signing. Butcher thinks back to her advice to him on how to catch Homelander on film in such a way as for it to be indisputable. Deeper than skin and mannerisms.

“Well, how’d I do then, Frenchie?”

“Your performance seemed to be a satisfactory one,” Frenchie sputters, intonation rising like he don’t understand what Butcher’s really asking him.

“No, I meant, the other thing.” He crosses his ankle over his knee. “Sweetening the pot.”

Sighing, Frenchie says, “I believe your performance in that category was satisfactory as well.”

“Great.”

“Ah — will you be ready to regroup tonight, or will tomorrow be more preferable?”

Butcher knuckles at his eyes. He’s tired, he needs to wash the rubble and spunk off him, and he don’t understand half of what happened at Vought with Homelander. If he tries to talk it out with the lads tonight, he don’t see himself being anything but a cunt about it. He’s hungry, too. Starving, actually. The stink of Crewcut’s burning meat had put him off his appetite for a good hour, but it’s creeping up on him again.

“Tomorrow,” Frenchie confirms, though Butcher hasn’t said anything. “I will inform the others.”

“Good lad.” He lowers the phone to his knee, hesitates, and raises it back to his ear. “And Frenchie?”

“Monsieur Charcutier?”

He glances over at the flashing red light. “Thanks, for lookin’ out.”

The ensuing quiet is suitably shocked. While Butcher’s still just watching that light blink and blink and blink, it drops into darkness like the last eye of Argus put out for good.

“We are a team,” Frenchie reminds him, his voice gone soft. Near reverent. “Spice Girls, remember?”

“Yeah, I remember.”

“See that you keep remembering it,” he says, building back some of his steel, some of his backbone. In that same kinda gentle way as before, how Frenchie always is if he just has the chance to be. “You let yourself forget once and I had not forgiven you, when this happened. When your life was so transformed by the shit in your veins, and by the man who put it there. But your life was almost not transformed, and you were almost not saved. And I had almost not forgiven you. Do you understand?

“But maybe it is nothing to you,” Frenchie continues without waiting for an answer. “Maybe compliance is more to you than the regard of your friends. Maybe that has always been so.”

It was, once. And as for the present moment, he’s never believed more that the ends justify the means. He wouldn’t be knee-deep in the shit he’s in with Homelander if he didn’t. It has to be worth it. It has to be for something. But don’t the rest of it gotta mean something, too? Don’t the people he’s got left, have to be some kinda consolation for the ones he’s lost? Don’t he carry Lenny and Becca like he does because his life only ever mattered on account of them? 

And don’t life matter now, at least in some part, because of Frenchie and the others? Because they stick with Butcher even when he’s being a right bastard and he don’t deserve it. Because they are a team, and what they’re doin’ takes all of ‘em and not just him.

“You got it right, Frenchie.”

“Which part?” Frenchie asks, sounding justifiably baffled to get it in as many words from Butcher.

“I almost didn’t get saved,” he says, running his knuckle absentmindedly down the tessellated material on his borrowed trousers. The texture reminds him of a dead snake. “And you almost didn’t forgive me.”

Something in the quiet between him and Frenchie feels, for a moment, forlorn.

“Were you counting on it?”

Butcher rolls his shoulders, squashing the beanbag chair beneath him. He doesn’t have an answer he can vocalize, but it sounds about right. 

“Kimiko is saying, ‘You cannot change the memory that endures after you have gone, nor can you control the suffering of those that survive you.’” Frenchie pauses, his voice noticeably thicker when he speaks again. “She says, ‘All anyone can do is live, and trust that when all is said and done, those left behind will remember how to live, too.’”

He doesn’t know that he ever did, after Becca. But he likes the thought.

And if anyone knows what they’re talking about when it comes to this kinda thing, Frenchie and Kimiko are good candidates. All the lads are by now. Loss connects them just as much as spite. Among other things.

Rather than say any of that, Butcher mutters, “That’s nice, but I still ain’t convinced this don’t end the way it would’ve without the V.”

Even through the phone, he can hear Frenchie shaking his head and Kimiko throwing her hands.

“You are more indestructible than you have ever been,” Frenchie says. “And Petit Hughie’s plan is working, besides.”

“Fat lot of good that does us when we can’t see it through to Phase Two.”

“Do not despair. We have taken on greater feats than this and come out triumphant. We will come out triumphant again.”

Butcher knuckles at his eyes again. In this small space, all the smells clinging to him are amplified tenfold. He gets chlorine and metal sharp on the nose, scorched skin and blood beneath them, and jizz and lube throughout. A crude bouquet that don’t got any business stirring heat beneath his skin. He clears his throat.

“Am I gonna need a passcode to open what we got today?”

“Same as the others, yes.”

“All right,” he says on a sigh. “I’ll lock up after.”

“Bonne nuit, Butcher.”

“Yeah. Night.”

He hangs up the phone and sprawls where he’s sitting for a ten-count before making himself get up. Ashley’s blazer’s tight across his shoulders and he’s been sweating through it since he put it on, just about. The higher temperature in the storage unit ain’t helping either, so he shrugs it off and lets it pool on the beanbag chair.

At the laptop station, he gets everything up and running and pulls the file for today. He gets on a pair of headphones, settles in, and starts the video.

It’s unreal, watching the playback. He can see himself almost as if in slow motion walking the phone to the bedside table with blood on his cheek and disaster in his eyes. Homelander’s got a look on his face, too, when he approaches the bed. Some cross between wonder and resignation. Or maybe that’s just what happiness looks like on a mad tyrant with only one point of challenge left to him in all the world.

The rest of their fight-fucking plays out as he remembers it. Butcher only listens with half an ear, anticipating the exact moment the video cuts out. They blew the windows and the chandelier with the fucking laser vision. He never even thought of what it’d do to his phone, or the video.

C’mon then, love.

He yanks the headphones off his head and drops them onto the flimsy desk in front of him.

“Fucking brilliant,” he mutters, burying his face in his hands with a heavy sigh that wracks his whole body. “Goddamn it.”

For a white-hot moment he considers trashing the place, he’s so pissed at himself. All he had to do was get them the footage. He didn’t need to crank the dial till it broke, taking the feed with it. But it had felt—

Well.

He kills the power on the laptop station and whips Ashley’s jacket off the beanbag chair to pull it on over bare skin, cold sweat, and goosebumps. The rolling door rattles shut behind him, and then all he gets in every direction is distant traffic and low tides rollicking in a harbor to the east of him. He closes his eyes against it and lifts off the ground, just floating up and up, directionless, despite knowing he needs to get home and see to Terror, as well as the mess he’s made of himself.

Something stops him short of going in that direction, though. A voice down on the street.

“Oh, shit.”

Butcher looks down in time to see Hughie recognize him and bend double, hands braced on his knees.

“Jesus fucking Christ,” Hughie mutters under his breath. “For a second, I thought you were him.”

“That don’t bode well,” Butcher mutters back, himself going unheard. He drops back down onto his bare feet about a meter away from Hughie and walks the rest of the way to him. “What’re you doin’ out here so late? Frenchie send you?”

“What? No, why? Did he know you were here?” Hughie swings the backpack he’s got on around to his front and digs around in it. He procures a neatly folded shirt and tosses it at Butcher. “You didn’t go to your apartment or to the office, and I thought, maybe if you came here, like the workaholic son of a bitch you are, maybe you’d need another fuck-bag.”

Butcher shakes Ashley’s blazer off. It’d been tight on him through the shoulders and a few inches too short to cover his whole torso, leaving him chilly and over-warm simultaneously. 

Hughie’s shirt, on the other hand, fits like a glove. Butcher smooths his hand down the front.

“No Billy Joel tonight?”

“Considering my other one’s still in the possession of America’s Fuckboy? No,” Hughie simpers. “No Billy Joel tonight. Hey, are you hungry? I got protein bars, I got half a vegetable tray, some fruit…”

“Lemme see.”

Butcher peers down into Hughie’s bag and finds an apple; Hughie gets the baby carrots, and they turn toward the street without saying another word, both of them walking in step with each other toward the subway.

Around a crisp crunch, Hughie tells him, “You did good today.”

“Nah,” Butcher says, and idly scans the skies so Hughie will see him and remember to be careful how he talks. “I blew it.”

“I don’t think so,” Hughie answers, just as airily, though he looks up at the sky, too.

“Oh, yeah? How’s that?”

Hughie shrugs. “Do you know what a candid photo is?”

“Like when I take your picture and you don’t know it.”

“Yeah. The whole idea is, y’know, it’s supposed to be pure. No redos, no fuckin’…staging,” he adds with a shy laugh. “In theory, with the right picture, you could get someone’s whole nature in a single frame. Like bottling lightning.”

Butcher watches Hughie in profile in the half-dark. This whole thing was his brainchild. His singular stroke of genius. Trust him to see the deepest lore on his own plan.

“Before, Kimiko said you looked away too soon.” Hughie retrieves his phone from pocket and taps away, navigating screens until he finds what he wants. When he gets it, he slides a sideways glance Butcher’s way and hands him the phone. He says, “Today you didn’t.”

Right there on the screen is him and Homelander, together in their nakedness for the first time.

Hughie’s enhanced the picture here so the look on Homelander’s face comes out clearer for just what it is, and Butcher can see that he hadn’t taken his guess far enough.

Because the look on Homelander’s face? 

It’s surrender.

“Well, I’ll be a nun’s sweet perky tits,” Butcher murmurs.

Hughie tries to hold back his laugh at that but fails, shaking his head. He takes his phone back from Butcher and closes everything out before dropping it back in his pocket. “You just gotta stay cool for a little while longer, dude,” he promises, as earnestly as he does anything. “We’re right on the fucking pussy-trigger here. I can feel it.”

Butcher can feel it, too. He hums and takes a bite of his apple.

“Oh, hey,” Hughie adds, looking over with eyes gone soft in that Bambi kinda way of his. “About that Insight guy—”

“It were for the best,” Butcher says through a mouthful of red skin and white flesh. “With a little more time, he’d’a gotten everything he needed outta me.”

“You mean, like…?”

“A certain twat on ice, yeah.”

“Aaaand you’re still thinking in circles like he can hear you,” Hughie translates, half-smiling. With a wry glance up at the clear night sky, he adds, “Guess we’ve had a lotta practice with that lately.”

Butcher takes another block to eat his apple, and then Hughie starts in on him again.

“What’s next in your bag of tricks, do you think?”

“How do you mean?” Butcher asks, chucking the apple core in a bin as they pass it up. “Like with him?”

“Yeah.” Hughie shrugs. “You’ve exhausted the entire Daddy spectrum by now.”

Butcher beckons for Hughie to give him the backpack and roots around for something else to fill his stomach. He wants something more substantial than fruit, but he’s jonesing for sweets just now.

“I dunno,” he says. “Could take a page outta your playbook.”

Laughing a little, Hughie says, “How’s that?”

“Play it by ear. ‘Yes, and?’ Ain’t that the rule?”

“Yeah, if you’re a basic bitch. When was the last time you let somebody else take the lead and write the scene for you?”

For a second Butcher just lets the question hang between them, then he looks over at Hughie. Not any special kinda way, just till he gets it. When he does, his eyes go all Bambi again, and he smiles, soft and sweet how Butcher hopes he don’t ever lose.

“Sap,” Butcher teases, walking into Hughie so their shoulders bump. 

Hughie rolls his eyes, but his smile don’t budge. Though it does take on a conspiratorial lilt, like what he says next is a secret for just the two of them.

“Y’know, you’re different ever since Soldier Boy. Or maybe it happened when MindStorm got the jump on you, I don’t know. We’ve had a lot of close calls lately — you more than anyone — and I’m…” He bumps Butcher’s shoulder back. “I’m glad I got to know this side of you, too, man.”

Butcher don’t know about all that. He don’t know if he’s the person Hughie thinks he is, or if he ever was, even before he lost Becca, but it’s easy comfort to believe that he could be. He winds his arm around Hughie’s neck as they walk and takes a bite outta the peach in his hand. The juice runs down his wrist, sticky sweet and warm like the night around them.

 

/

 

“After Stormfront, I never thought I’d find anyone that was worthy of me. She was everything I wanted. Everything Maeve could never give me. Oh, she tried. I realize that, and I know I didn’t make it easy on her. The children we might’ve had… but in the end, she wasn’t enough. In the end, neither of them were. Not really. You understand what I’m telling you, don’t you?” 

He dabs a white square of cotton gauze at the scabbing apple of Insight’s cheek, just beneath the bandages covering what used to be his eyes. His chapped lips wobble, but his only answer is to nod.

“No one’s ever known me for everything that I am and stayed anyway.”

The iodine stink in Insight’s room is pungent, to go with the mild laundry detergent clinging to the linens everywhere they haven’t been stained a ruddy, mucous pink. Homelander trades the yellowing square in his hand for a crisp, sterile one and dips it in a little tub of vaseline.

“I’m curious, though,” he murmurs, smearing the ointment across Insight’s bottom lip. “When he thought about me, is that what you saw? Or did I imagine it?”

Habit forces Insight to roll his lips together. He shakes his head against the silence.

“I don’t know,” he whispers. “Please, I don’t know.”

Homelander sighs and screws the lid back on the vaseline. He tosses it over his shoulder so it bounces on the bedspread. Insight squirms helplessly against his crunchy hospital gown, deepening the faintly acrid smell of morphine clouding at the juncture of his elbow. His breath catches, one sharp inhale away from hyperventilating by the sound of it.

“I’ll tell you — anything you wanna know, sir, I swear. I just…I can’t…I can’t—”

“You can’t what?”

Insight shakes his head, beginning to cry, or to blubber at least, since his lacrimal glands are in no condition currently to bear him tears. Trying to reign himself in, with little success, he says, “I can’t tell you if you imagined it. When he thought about you, it was — it wasn’t like what you thought about him. It was something else.”

“How did he think about me?” Homelander asks silkily, letting his eyes warm over just for fun.

Goosebumps break out all over Insight’s bare arms. He makes a noise like a whimper, and Homelander settles him with a gloved hand on his cheek, beneath the waxy burns. He hushes him until he quiets, and then he tries again, softening his voice.

“Tell me.”

“It was…” He swallows hard, gathering his thoughts, trying to anticipate the leanings in Homelander’s temper. “Like he — couldn’t stop.”

His voice breaks over the last word, and Homelander’s mind clears. The only reason he trusts that it’s the truth is that he didn’t expect it. He didn’t know it was precisely what he wanted to hear, and so it follows that Insight couldn’t have known either. Bypassing the bandages to palm a handful of Insight’s short hair, Homelander runs his fingers idly through it.

“And what about you?”

“He wasn’t interested,” Insight hurries to say.

“Oh, no?”

“You were listening, you — you heard him. He said no.”

Homelander squeezes a fistful of that dark hair, asking, “Did he mean it?”

Insight hesitates. His mouth opens and closes, half-forming the shape of half a dozen words before settling on, “What?”

“Did he,” Homelander whispers, “mean it?”

“I — yes, sir, he…” Another compulsive swallow rocks his Adam’s apple. “He didn’t like having me in his head. Nobody does, really, but with him…it was like he wanted to hurt me, but he didn’t. He wanted to, and he didn’t want to. I don’t know.”

“Mesmer and Mindstorm weren’t so lucky.” Homelander resumes his gentle petting of Insight’s limp, greasy hair. “I wonder what makes you so different.”

“Mindstorm?” Insight asks distantly. Even through the bandages obscuring half of his face, he looks confused, the point of distraction. “The other one was — Mindstorm? Like, Payback Mindstorm? As in…”

Insight trails off then, and Homelander can’t guess why. Until he can.

“Yes,” he says quietly. “He knew my father.”

“Knew him?” Insight’s mouth flaps open and shut again, dizzy and senseless with the opioids pumping through his system. “Wait, you mean — Billy did?”

Unwillingly, he remembers how Butcher had looked that day at Dad’s shoulder, radiant and obscene with the Temp V lighting him up from within. Homelander hadn’t known what to do. He’d never considered it. He’s given Compound V to known terrorists, and somehow, somehow, William Butcher was the unseen, unplanned danger: Butcher, and Soldier Boy.

“It was him,” Insight mumbles, and he doesn’t even flounder when Homelander looks at him.

“Who,” Homelander asks lazily, taking up a pair of scissors.

Insight seems to become aware of him again, suddenly and viciously, and he reaches blindly for Homelander’s hands, cutting his own fingers on the metal blades.

“What’s the matter?” he chirps.

“Don’t! Please—”

“It’s all right,” Homelander chides. “I’m trained in combat medicine. Well, the basics anyway. You’re in capable hands.”

Insight doesn’t tell him he knew that, but he also doesn’t point out that said training was part of a publicity stunt from Homelander’s first days in The Seven. The campaign was mostly kissing babies and patching up soldiers. All told, he picked it up quickly. He liked learning the medical jargon, and it been an illuminating experience for him, being able to put a name to things he could always see any old time he liked. The same year he’d been ordained as a minister, he learned about the use of tourniquets in field amputations and the increasing magnitude in devastation as the caliber size of munitions increased. 

Ain’t that America.

He angles one steel shearing blade beneath the coil of bandages around Insight’s head, saying, “These ones are all bloody, bud. They gotta go.”

Insight’s shiny bottom lip trembles, but he gives up the fight. He puts his hands down.

Homelander snips straight up in a line, wrinkling his nose at the stale waft of salty blood and noxious cell death. It reminds him of Butcher that first night in his kitchen, when he’d been dying.

Insight isn’t dying, though. Maybe he looks the part, but he’ll live.

Thermal necrosis hadn’t set in for him right away, but the dense connective tissue of his eyes has since warped like melted plastic. The silver coins of his ruined corneas stare sightlessly out of his face, one of them looking almost normal in comparison to the more malformed of the two. 

His eyelids have been completely burned. Homelander can see retinal detachment on the more obviously damaged eye and wonders idly if or when the surgeons will perform a tarsorrhaphy.

“When,” Insight mumbles, feeling around on his bed, perhaps searching for the roll of gauze. “Not if.”

Homelander holds it up and out of the way.

“Why wait?”

Haltingly, Insight says, “I thought one of them might be salvageable if I gave it some time, but…” His meek attempt at a smile, against the salmon pink of his eyelids and his opaque, misshapen corneas, registers more as another ghastly wound than an expression of mirth. “I guess it looks pretty bad, doesn’t it?”

Homelander feels his eyebrows pulling down on his forehead. He hadn’t thought of that. 

But accessing someone else’s thoughts would be almost like seeing, wouldn’t it? 

Calm for the first time in Homelander’s presence, liquid with it, Insight rolls his shoulders. He looks remarkably like Butcher when he does that, but maybe that’s the morphine. Maybe the other thing’s the morphine, too.

“Sir, if I can say so,” he says, sighing threadily and patting the bed blindly for something that’s eluding him. “You’re very candid with your thinking just now. Maybe…if you show me something you don’t actually want me to see…maybe you can make it quick?”

Homelander follows Insight’s hand to the morphine trigger. He lets him up the dose. It hadn’t been very high to begin with, though his pain must be considerable.

“I’m not going to kill you,” Homelander tells him, hearing the petulance in his own voice.

Insight doesn’t answer except to continue upping his morphine. Homelander snatches it away from him.

“Stop that.”

“What do you want,” he whines, voice dropping off near the end.

“You told Ashley he was hiding something behind me,” Homelander says, grabbing his chin when his head lolls forward onto his chest. 

His one swollen eyeball, unnaturally oblate, jiggles when Homelander shakes him.

“Him, it was him,” Insight murmurs tiredly.

“Who?”

A strand of drool hits Homelander’s glove, snapping when he pulls his hand away. He pushes Insight to lean back against his pillows and sees his mouth trying to shape a word. Whatever it is, he doesn’t find it. What he does find, with one seeking hand, is Homelander’s heart. Another casualty of the morphine’s influence, perhaps, but it isn’t long before his hand drops limply to the bed. A second later, he begins to snore. Homelander wipes his wet glove off on the bedsheet.

“Fuck,” he says, standing and going to the door.

There’s a team of medics lined up outside the door when he walks out. Not a one of them will look him in the eye. That’s fine. He doesn’t need them.

As he’s walking down the corridor, there’s only one pair of eyes that does meet his, and it feels like an accident more than anything else. Other Ashley looks up at him mid-stride and then freezes in place. He keeps walking until they’re stopped a few paces away from each other.

“Can I help you with something?”

She jumps and steps away, visibly startled. 

“Ah! No, Homelander, I’m sorry. I just didn’t expect to see you in this part of the Tower today. Does Ashley know you’re here?”

“She’s not my keeper, Other Ashley.”

Stammering, she says, “Oh, no, no, no. I, um, I didn’t think that. Actually,” she adds, brightening. “I do have good news for you! Since you’re here!”

“Is my room ready?”

“Not yet! The structural damage to the ceiling was pretty extensive, not to mention the support beams and the flooring, but we do have rooms available that you can have in the meantime. Maybe you’d like to take Stormfront’s, or maybe Queen Maeve’s? I know you were close with both of them.”

For a moment, for a wild, spinning, uncatchable moment, he’s overwhelmed with the thought that he was never truly close with either of them. They both wanted him to be something other than what he is. 

Stormfront wanted him to be a monument. Maeve wanted him to be human.

Nothing more than ghosts now, either of them, and here he stands. Still wondering why he wasn’t enough.

Hugging the opposite wall, that same team of medics from before wheel Insight down the corridor in the direction of the operating room. He flaps his hand out in Homelander’s direction as they pass, forcing the doctors to stop.

“Yes, Insight?” he asks, pasting on a thin smile for their onlookers.

Not because he cares if he looks friendly, but because he knows he doesn’t. Not now.

Insight’s still waving his hand around. Homelander catches it by the wrist, feeling the sluggish crawl of his pulse.

“What?”

With clean bandages bound tightly around his eyes, his hazy smile is wide and arresting, full of straight, white teeth Homelander would love to shatter with his fist. He’s almost handsome, like this. Even with his eyes mutilated beyond repair, even with Homelander being able to see the full extent of his injuries, there’s something sly and devilish about him, charming. Even without his eyes or his beauty or his wits about him.

Beginning to feel frantic the longer Insight lays there without speaking to him, Homelander violently squeezes his wrist.

“What, Forester?”

He licks his lips, pink tongue slipping out over shiny full lips, and he says, “Tell Daddy.”

Homelander yanks his hand away, ears burning.

“It’s the drugs,” a faceless medic rushes to say, and amazingly, unbelievably, places herself in between Insight and Homelander. “He doesn’t know what he’s saying.”

“Just get him out of my sight,” he says tightly.

She nods, and the whole procession jolts into motion. Over the rattling wheels on his gurney and the stampede of footsteps, Insight’s voice, surprisingly smooth and lovely, rings out. Clear, like a bell reigning in the dawn.

Don’t you see, don’t you see?
You’re just the torch to put the flame to all our guilt and shame
And I’ll rise like an ember in your name

His voice trails off as they pass through a set of doors into a different wing.

“It’s so sad,” Other Ashley says. Her mournful tone is somewhat at odds with the fact that she’s typing rapidly on her phone. “He saved all those kids from that theater that caught fire, but the burns he sustained completely mangled his face. Ashley said he’ll probably never see again. He’s so brave.”

“Oh, yes.”

“So about the rooms—”

“I don’t want them,” he says woodenly, turning to leave.

“Oh, but—”

He walks away, directionless, not knowing where he’s going until he finds himself at his room. The door opens to him, left unlocked while various teams require access to it. A cursory glance tells him the mirrors have been taken away, as per his request. A plastic sheet has been pulled taut around the broken open segment of ceiling. He fists a handful of it and tears it away, revealing the natural skylight he and Butcher rent with their bodies.

Foolishly, inanely, he thinks of Insight’s parting words to him, the familiar refrain that visits him even in dreams. Insight didn’t lift that memory from Homelander, and that leaves only one alternative.

He strips off one glove, presses his bare hand against the column of his throat, and lets his head tip gently back, eyes set on the golden sky without seeing it.

Chapter End Notes

S P I K E N A R D 🌺

Chapter 9: altar

Butcher’s walking Terror out into the lift when who else but Annie January steps in after him. He looks around the side of her, expecting Hughie or MM, and not seeing anybody else. The lift doors slink shut, sealing them up together. Turning, he catches her eye and presses the button for the ground floor.

“Annie,” he prompts.

“Butcher,” she answers, matching his tone perfectly.

She was sweeter than this once, or maybe she still is and she’s just figured out how to not need it. Leastways, not all the time.

“I wanted to talk to you before you left for Arcadia,” she says, uncrossing her arms and holding one hand out to Terror when he goes nosing for pets. “That is today, isn’t it?”

“Yeah, it is.”

Annie nods, and the doors open. Terror leads the way, and Butcher follows, Annie traipsing out after them. They make it a whole city block without speaking.

“If this is about the condoms,” he offers.

“I don’t want to talk about what you put on your dick.”

“Right, so if this is about the condoms—”

“What, do you mean you aren’t using them?”

“Thought you said you didn’t wanna talk about ‘em,” he answers idly, letting a little bounce into his step.

“Oh, my God,” she groans. “No, I’m not here to give you a safe sex talk. I’ve just been wanting to say something to you, and there’s never been any time. It’s about Homelander.”

“You do know he’s been on my dick,” Butcher quips, purely to be a cheeky shit.

Annie doesn’t go for the easy bait. She gives him a look, all defiance, and stands her ground, saying, “I had to be his love interest for five minutes, and I thought it was gonna kill me. When I kissed him, I felt like I had a bomb strapped to my chest.”

He looks at her then, remembering the press junket she’s talking about. She’d looked, even to him, with her big hair and her fixed smile and her cat eyes, like a young Jane Fonda. Fearless, glamorous, and ready at a word to raise hell. He knew she couldn’t have liked going along with the bit, or having to sell it — and herself — but she’d never had any reason to tell him so. Butcher’d never had much occasion to ask, either.

“Sorry,” he says stupidly, but he can’t see what else to give her.

She waves her hand, and they stop at a street corner, waiting for the walk signal. Terror parks it right in between them, a warm weight against Butcher’s leg.

“I tell you this at all because maybe you’ve felt that way lately? With him?”

Butcher dodges her meaningful look in favor of stepping off the curb when the light changes. The crush of people around them, the noise of the city, is as good as a zinc forcefield netted around them. Does as much for his mood, in any case.

Annie catches up to him on the next stretch of sidewalk. She lets him be till they reach the dog park Terror likes. Billy unclips his leash and sits on an open bench, leaving room for Annie to sit next to him. When he tosses her the tennis ball from his jacket pocket, she catches it gamely and throws it for Terror to chase. She don’t even whinge about it being covered in dog drool when he brings it right back to her.

What can he say? He don’t take what she’s tellin’ him lightly, and he don’t wanna lie just to get her off his scent. But the alternative might fuckin’ be worse.

“He is what he is,” he makes himself say as evenly as he can. “But I already told ya—”

“You’ve fucked people you didn’t like before,” she finishes. “I remember.”

He presses his lips together and distracts himself with Terror when he trots over, rubbing at his ears and down his back, patting down his sides. Handsome lad. He’s gonna need a bath after today, all this grass and dirt and city muck bogging him down.

“It just seems like maybe this time was harder on you,” Annie says, looking at him with curiosity but without accusation. “A guy did get his eyes burned out of his head right in front of you.”

“There you go. Fuckin’ traumatic experience. Done. Easy as that.”

“Sure, but as I understand it,” she says, delicately. “It’s nothing you haven’t seen before.”

And fuck, well. She’s right, and not merely as concerns Stilwell. He had been the one to turn Gunpowder’s skull into juice, after all, and had done nearly the same thing to Mesmer once upon a time. He tips his head, conceding the point.

“You got me there,” he tells her.

Terror pivots then and drops his chin onto Annie’s knee. Butcher dusts his hands off, still contemplating further deflections when she redirects.

“When I got to know him a little better,” she begins, voice soft and low but unwavering. “Even at his most horrible, at his most violent, there was a part of me that believed, deep down, if he had the chance, if he had a reason, someday, he could be good. Someday, he could see himself for what he really is, and that’d be enough to make him grow up.”

Something about the kindness in her eyes when she looks at him, tells him she’s winding up for a punchline he ain’t gonna like. But as he can’t predict it, he’s at a loss to stop her before she gets there.

“But all along, that said more about me than it did about him. Do you know what I mean?”

And, oh. Oh, no. He don’t like that a bit.

“No,” he says, recoiling a little, like that’ll do anything to put distance between him and what she’s implying. “Fuck no.”

Annie doesn’t say anything. Not even to tell him he has the right idea or the wrong one. And it’s that that tells him what her play is. He can’t even be annoyed at her for coming at him with an angle; for giving him just enough to jump to his own conclusion and give the game away. 

She’d nearly had him, too.

Butcher leans back so his shoulders hit the bench back. He says, “Who taught you that one, huh? Was it Hughie?”

She shrugs and wipes her hands off on her jeans when Terror trundles off to inspect a patch of dandelions in the grass.

“Actually, that one’s courtesy of my dad.”

He feels the knit between his eyebrows, and she takes it for a question.

“My mom can be a lot. It makes sense. She wanted a lot; for me, from me. And ever since I can remember, it’s always been: you smile, you think about the camera, you remember your steps. You remember that people are watching you. That you can’t step out of line. But my dad wasn’t like that.”

She don’t say anything for a while, and Butcher lets the quiet lie. He watches Terror kick at his ear, jowls flapping with every thump of his claws.

A sleek little pit bull strides right up to Terror and sniffs him. They go back and forth.

“He used to do this thing, before he left. He’d just…say something true, innocuous. And Mom would ignore him, or she’d get passive aggressive. But she’d be polite about it, always. She’d smooth over it so she wouldn’t have to be honest about how she felt, or how we did. But it worked like clockwork every time. He never really called her out for anything, but she just had to defend herself so badly, that she’d come right out and do it for him.”

 Butcher considers the obvious flaw in that logic.

“It’s good,” he allows. “But only if I mind what you think of me.”

“God forbid,” she counters sarcastically. 

“God forbid.” He folds his arms in front of himself as the pit bull presses its chest low to the grass, smiling in that way dogs do, in a way Terror matches. Grumbling, he adds, “Or I’d have to tell ya I don’t feel fucking sorry for the cunt. Be a right pillock if I did that.”

He glances at her from the corner of his eye, just in time to see her sneaky little smile. She reminds him of Hughie when she does that. Bugger.

“For what it’s worth, I understand the impulse.”

“I really doubt that.”

“No, I do.” She sighs, twisting her hands in her lap. “Even hearing myself say that sounds so fucked up, but…it’s the truth. I mean, Jesus, the moment I realized that what I could give her, what she could brag about because of me, meant more to my mom than I ever did? That one moment, I wouldn’t wish on anyone. And that, that’s been his whole life. Hasn’t it?”

Butcher thinks about his own shit of a father, proud of him the one time that he was because Butcher’d ruined his fucking life. Because he fell in line and did what was expected of him.

Out on the grass, Terror chases the pit bull in a circle until one of them slows or the other advances. They crash in a heap, rolling and panting and playing. The pit bull springs up first from the pile, and Terror pounces him. There they go rolling again until one of them yips.

“Oy! Terror!”

He comes bounding over, bringing the pit bull with him, looking sheepish. Annie holds out her hand for the new dog, and he says hello in the language of dogs.

“Thank you for cleaning my hand,” Annie says earnestly, rubbing the lad’s ears and reading his tags. “Charlie.”

Good-looking fella. Floppy ears, red nose, white and brown coloring. Serious eyes. Very polite, all told. Someone a ways off whistles, and the dog’s ears lay flat. He turns tail and takes off, fifty pounds of muscle and a jangling collar ringing out after him.

Terror looks up at Butcher, visibly chuffed to have made a friend, and Butcher knuckles at his ear.

“Have fun, did ya?”

Boof, comes his reply.

Butcher pets his wrinkled head.

“What do you care if I’m havin’ a bad time of things anyway?” he asks, still looking at Terror and his sweet, guileless face. “What’s it to ya?”

“We have to do whatever we have to do,” Annie says, deliberately, so every word carries as much weight as it can. “We have to take this as far as it goes, and I know you will. You’ll do it because you have to, no matter what it costs you. Because it already cost you everything.”

She looks away suddenly, eyes shining for the last second that he can see them clearly. Her voice when she speaks again is rough.

“The people we’ve lost because of him died for nothing, but it only stays that way if we let it.”

It’s a call to war, if she wanted it to be. Hell, he’d follow her into battle on that line alone. On the strength of her resolve and nothing else. He wonders if she even knows that about herself, that she can turn tides just by making up her mind to do it. And not because of the V, no. Because she’s got the same thing Becca did, that makes her wanna go out and get the things she wants rather than wait for them to be given to her.

“You know, when Homelander was done with him,” she continues, in a low, low voice, surprising him. “Alex… he didn’t even look like a person anymore.”

He’d heard from Hughie. 

Heard about the collapsed cherry pie of Supersonic’s face and his leg bent around the wrong way, white bone jutting out of red flesh. His uniform about the only recognizable thing left of him.

It gets Butcher thinking about Homelander’s stupid fucking pinky finger. How he’d felt when he held it his hand and looked down at it, knowing it was him that made it that way. Him that left a wrinkle what couldn’t be ironed out and smoothed to perfection. Him that couldn’t be flushed out and reset. Him that couldn’t be erased. Him that was more stubborn than fucking brain lesions, because he outlasted them and because he left marks they couldn’t.

Because Homelander could take a man and make him look like meat, but big fucking deal. Anyone could do that. To take a god, though — or a twat what thinks he’s a god — and make him into a man? To make him glad for the opportunity?

That had felt better than good. Better than really good coke or really good fucking or really good food. It had felt righteous. He’d been proud of it. Possessive. 

Of Homelander.

And wasn’t that just a mindfuck on top of a mindfuck.

Terror huffs and curls up by Butcher’s feet, pulling him out of that dangerous pit he’d fallen into.

“I know you know how important this is,” Annie tells him, swiping at her cheek so quickly he can’t even catch the sight of tears before they’re dashed away. “You don’t need me to tell you that, but you’re important, too. And you shouldn’t have to almost die to hear that.”

She lets that hang in the air between them, and as ready as he’d been to dismiss her out of hand before, he finds it don’t come as easily to him this time around. Not if she’s gonna shoot straight with him and leave out all the bullshit. And if nothing else, he respects her enough by now to acknowledge the effort. She’s earned it.

Not to mention, to her credit, that she’s right about a lot of it. This last time at Vought changed something. Touched a part of him he thought he’d been managing to keep separate. The danger had been real in a way he wasn’t used to, but so had other parts of it.

He’d been pleased, and he wasn’t supposed to let himself feel that for real. He wasn’t supposed to fucking like it.

“D’you wanna know what it’s like for me,” he murmurs, “bein’ with him?”

She turns to look at him, surprised. Her eyes are wide and all-seeing, just the barest shimmer of moisture under her eyelashes. He huffs a wry laugh, running his finger along the line of his bottom lip.

“Feels like I’m the bomb strapped to him. Same thing, though, innit? One wrong move and we’re both dead. Don’t make no fuckin’ difference.”

“It makes a difference,” she says softly. 

“How’s that?”

“Because he’s trapped, and you’re not.”

That stops him short just looking at her. He hadn’t said that thinking she’d have anything for him, save disgust. He sure as hell didn’t think she’d have an answer. He looks away, trying to play it off as indifference.

“Be honest. Hughie put you up to this, didn’t he?”

“Actually,” she says, letting a little bit of a lilt into her voice, like a sliver of light breaking in a windowless room. “It was MM’s idea.”

The level of innocence and levity in her voice tips him off.

“And in exchange?”

“Oh, I made him tell me about Vegas.”

Nodding, he says, “Good on you.”

“Yeah, I thought you’d like that.”

“Did he tell you the whole thing or just the Made-for-TV stuff?” he asks, leaning into it on purpose, because that’s what life rafts are for.

Annie brings her knee up on the bench to face him, a look like trouble on her face that reminds him so much of Hughie that a spike of tenderness hits him right in the chest. She says, “I suspect what he told me was heavily censored.”

He hums and brings his knee up on the bench, too, bringing his hands up so he can talk with them. Terror glances up at them but doesn’t move. He’ll be ready to bully them off the bench and back to the office soon.

“Started out as a game of chicken, right. See, sometimes,” Butcher says, with an instructional note entering into his voice, “a bird’ll boss you around just to see if she can. Just to see what you’ll do about it. And sometimes, every once in a while, it’s to her detriment. Like with me and MM, as it happens. Now, mind you, we’d been up three days on a job when this happened. He weren’t at his best; maybe I weren’t either, but this dame, Susan—”

“Sharon,” Annie corrects.

“—this dame, Sharon, she’s there with her friends, every one of ‘em as fit and horny as she is—”

Annie wrinkles her nose, and he waves her off. She’s seen his dick; a story ain’t gonna be the thing that ruins her.

“And she’s sayin’, to me…” He pitches his voice, effecting a vaguely Middle America accent without quite nailing it. “‘Billy, you know what would be so hot—’”

“No,” Annie sputters, laughing. She’s got a laugh for pubs, Annie has. The kind you’d hear from the door and know it was her. “That was why? She wanted to watch?”

“Oh, they all did,” he says, blandly, like this ain’t news. “This was prime diddling material, Annie.”

She covers a snort with her hand. “Okay, but you guys hooked up, right? That’s why he gets…?”

“Tetchy?”

“He does! He gets tetchy!”

“Yeah.” He tips his head. “More so than usual.”

Annie watches him, her mind working. He can see the follow-up questions behind her eyes: so then, why don’t they again? Why leave it at that?

“Eh. We don’t all luck out like you and young Hughie, do we? It’s complicated.”

“Me and Hughie are plenty complicated,” she says, rolling her eyes but in a way that don’t feel like it’s directed at him. “For different reasons. But I get it. It takes work. You have to want it, even in the shit.”

“True enough.”

The other thing is, they weren’t really ever not thinking of the women they married. And maybe that oughta be different now, circumstances being what they are, but it ain’t. He don’t say that, though. He don’t need it out there in his words that Becca was it for him. People like him get one shot, if they’re lucky, to be happy the way he was with her. That chance ain’t coming to him twice.

He glances down at Terror who’s pretending not to watch them. 

“Ready to go, son?”

He sits up straight away, and Butcher bends down to clip his leash, purely so he won’t catch shit for going without. Terror leads the way, Annie and Butcher trailing behind him. He checks his watch, expecting it to be earlier than it is. They killed more time than he thought, though. Maybe he won’t be able to beat Homelander to Arcadia after all.

“I can take him,” Annie says. “Hughie bought a bunch of toys the other day. We can watch him until you get back.”

He hesitates.

“You trust me with Vegas but not with your dog?” she asks, so dryly he nearly laughs.

“Vegas don’t need special kibble to keep its weight in check.”

“We got the special food. You think we have toys but no food? This is Hughie we’re talking about. Plus, his dad’s coming over later, and he loves Terror. We’ll be all right. You just take care of what you have to do.”

Butcher sighs, beaten out by reason of all things, and crouches to give Terror a kiss right between his eyes. “Be good for the nice lady, darlin’,” he murmurs, holding Terror’s eyes for a moment, as if to drive home that he’s serious. He stands up, holds Annie’s eyes with the same air of gravity, and says, “Thanks.”

“Don’t mention it,” she says, with a smile that ain’t for Hollywood or for tabloids or for stray cell phone videos. It’s just for now, for this moment.

He never thought he’d see the day, but he has to admit. Sometimes it’s nice to be proven wrong.

She’s a few steps away from him when he calls after her.

“Annie,” he says.

Turning mid-step to look at him, she raises her eyebrows in question.

I was wrong about you, he thinks. I’m a dickhead and you outclassed me, and I knew for a long time but I couldn’t say it.

“What?” she asks, facing him head-on now, Terror sitting patiently beside her.

I’m sorry, he thinks.

He pockets his hands, and he goes so long without saying anything that the expectant look on her face changes incrementally to one of suspicion. He rocks back on his heels, saying, “Weren’t you worried?”

“About what?”

“Homelander,” he says, casting his eyes skyward. “Listening in.”

Rolling her eyes, she says, “Everything I said about him, he knows. I’m pretty sure knowing was part of what made tormenting me more fun for him.”

Butcher nods. He thinks he knew that about Homelander, but he didn’t know Annie did.

She stays watching him. “Were you? Worried, I mean.”

“Nah.” He shrugs. “Not about him.”

Annie opens her mouth, but no words come out. She presses her lips together, thinking for a good long moment. “Butcher,” she says, and then nothing else.

“Yeah,” he says back, looking away. He’s caught himself doing that a lot with her today. “Listen, I’m — I was…” He nods, like that’ll do the work his mouth can’t. “Yeah.”

Nodding, too, Annie gets a look like she’s worried he might be having a stroke.

“Are you okay?”

He sighs and starts to go, halfway to lifting off. “Yeah, fine. Just — fine.”

But when a few more seconds tick by, and he hasn’t gone anywhere, she’s still standing there looking at him like she’s seeing him for the first time. He makes himself swallow down whatever reservations he has left about her. Because, really, he ain’t got the right to think less of her. He never did, but especially not when the choices she’s made matter more than a decision somebody else picked out for her her whole lifetime ago.

“You’re all right, Annie,” he croaks, catching her eyes. “All right?”

She stares at him without saying a word, and she keeps not saying a word until he’s in the air flying away. He shouldn’t be able to hear her at all when she says, soft as snow:

“Maybe you’re not so bad yourself.”

Hell, maybe. Maybe.

He flies up to Dresden and then over to Arcadia. It’s not quite as early as he meant for it to be, but Homelander ain’t around yet. It gives him time to pick the bugs out of his teeth, which is always nice.

There must be a secret for not choking on bugs, right? The fuck.

He walks to the water’s edge and stands looking out at all that blue, picking idly at his teeth. At the very fringes of his awareness, he can sense Hughie’s drone camera circling high above the power plant. It’s as close to soundless as they possibly could’ve made it, and when he tries to catch a glimpse of it, it looks like a mirror for the clouds. They’ll have to fuck where they bulldozed a gap in the roof to be seen, but he feels pretty capable of making that happen.

It’s as he’s swiping around on his phone checking for last minute updates that two texts come in. The first from Kimiko is a picture message of Hughie patting Terror’s chest down. The second from Hughie just says, I love you man.

Butcher smiles, thumb moving over the screen to reply when he hears the familiar whipping of Homelander’s cape. He looks up over his shoulder and freezes, blood going cold. Already firmly holding his gaze, Homelander smiles and touches down right in front of him. Tucked under his arm, unsmiling, like none of this were his idea — and why the fuck would it be — is Ryan.

 

/

 

Homelander’s had a lot of practice by now listening to the barometer of William Butcher’s heartbeat. He’s never heard this song before, though; he’s never managed to surprise Butcher quite like this. It would be wonderful to know what he’s thinking right now, but maybe that’s just residual jealousy from the ordeal with Insight speaking. 

Jovially, like he isn’t aware of the tension in the air, Homelander explains what he’s done. As much for Ryan’s benefit as for Butcher’s.

“He asked where I’ve been disappearing to. I thought, if I just showed him, that would take the mystery out of it, and it’d be a surprise for both of you.” He squeezes Ryan’s shoulder, nudging him forward. “Well, don’t just stare at each other. Ryan, say hello.”

Ryan hardly moves, he’s gone so stiff. “Hi, Butcher,” he mumbles.

“Ryan,” he grits out in response.

Grinning, Homelander claps Ryan on the back and makes like to give them some privacy, but Butcher takes a step towards him. Just one, but his intent is clear in his demeanor, and Homelander draws short on reflex alone.

Before Butcher can say a word and collect on Homelander’s payment to him — his regard, given without a second thought — Ryan steps forward. 

And then it’s Butcher freezing in place to listen. Yielding.

He doesn’t say a word. Neither of them do.

Homelander rubs Ryan’s arms to encourage him, to support him in whatever it is he decides to say to Butcher today, and then he turns and floats out over the lake. It’s an illusion of privacy more than anything. Even if he wanted to tune them out, this close, he’d have a hard time of it without some other type of audio stimulus to distract him. So, he listens, and he does it knowing that they can’t possibly expect anything else.

“I’ll take you back if you want,” Butcher says to start, his voice quiet and subdued.

“I can get back by myself,” Ryan pops back, and crosses his arms in front of his narrow chest. “I don’t need you.”

Homelander feels the breath that punches out of Butcher, restrained though it is. His heart’s a little more telling than his respiration; there it goes again in his chest like a runaway horse.

“That’s right, you don’t,” Butcher replies, still just as softly as before. “I know that.”

“You made sure of it,” Ryan snaps, turning away from him to face the power plant. “That’s why you did it, isn’t it?”

Faltering, Butcher says, “Don’t know what ya mean.”

Ryan fumes, positively mutinous in his silence. Homelander looks over his shoulder to better view the train wreck, but neither of them turn and acknowledge him. Butcher’s eyes are lowered; Ryan’s facing away from the both of them, breathing gone all erratic in his lungs.

“I been awful to you.” Butcher shakes his head, eyes still on the ground. “If your mum knew…”

“But she doesn’t. She doesn’t know anything anymore because I killed her. Right? That’s what you said.”

Homelander waits patiently for Butcher’s answer. He’s heard all this before, so it doesn’t surprise him to hear the hatred in Ryan’s voice. It doesn’t sound to him like Butcher’s surprised either, though he’s certainly not indifferent. Homelander wonders, then, why he’s trying to hard to appear unfazed. To look neutral and carved out of stone while Ryan chips away at him with perfectly justified anger.

“That weren’t fair of me,” Butcher says, turning away. “You deserved better. You and your mum. Better than this, better than me.”

Eyes blue like a cloudless day, and red at the edges only from tears, not from the red sight, Ryan whirls on him. Voice breaking in that childlike way, he says, “You don’t get to decide what I deserve!” And having said that, having seen that Butcher held fast and listened, he only gains inertia falling off that ledge, shouting, “You don’t get to make me think you care about me and then take it away! And for what?” His voice breaks again, tears surfacing. “Because you’re selfish? Because it’s easier for you to leave than it is to stay?”

Butcher flinches. Whatever nerve that hit, he hadn’t been braced for it.

Ryan scrubs the back of his hand across his eyes, his mouth squashed in a wobbling line on his crumpled face. The crash of his racing heart telegraphs more to be said, and Homelander’s in the same boat as Butcher. He doesn’t know what Ryan will say or do next.

Sniffling against a runny nose, he says, “I think you wanted that stuff to kill you.”

At that, Butcher recoils so senselessly from the accusation that he turns unwittingly towards Homelander. The look on his face in that first breath is strangely young, but then he realizes Homelander’s watching him and tears his gaze away. That moment is all it takes, though. The damage is done.

As a knee-jerk revelation, it makes no sense to Homelander, until he considers it.

Butcher all those weeks ago in his apartment had been resigned to his fate, conquered by illness and desolation. He hadn’t fought back a bit until Homelander roused him to it, and even then, it seemed more like he’d been humoring Homelander than clinging to life.

And if he’d known all along, or at least part of the time, that the Temp V was killing him, then the last time he took it—

Well, he would’ve been signing his death warrant. And even still, he gave it up to save Ryan.

“I did, you’re right,” Butcher says, devoid of flourishes or theatrics of any kind. “I wanted it to kill me.”

Ryan’s heart skips in his chest. His eyes fill and spill with more tears, like he hadn’t anticipated to get this reaction. Like he didn’t really want to get it, so much as he wanted to get something, anything.

“You wanted to die?” Ryan whispers.

“I wanted to do what needed to be done,” Butcher tells him calmly and deliberately, albeit hoarsely. “And I’d’a died for that chance. Come down to it, I think I did. I think, when your dad—” he says, looking sharply at Homelander, “stuck that needle in me arm, I weren’t even here no more.” With his resolve cracking at the edges, he returns his attention to Ryan, telling him, “I did just like you said, Ryan. I left. ‘Cuz it was easy, and it hurt less havin’ to go out that way, knowin’ you’d want me gone.”

Ryan stares at him, furious and bitter and incredulous, but something else, too. Dismayed. 

Breathing shallowly, like he’s run a long way, or like he might begin to hyperventilate, he says, “But I didn’t want you gone.” Shaking, he whispers, “I didn’t, I missed you. I missed you and I thought you hated me, and I was so mad at you. You were my only friend.” Hiccuping and crying again, he adds, gasping, “You were the only one I could talk to about Mom.”

Even as closely as he’s watching them, Homelander doesn’t notice which one of them moves first. He only knows that one moment they’re standing rigidly and miserably, and the next they’re locked in a tight embrace. Both of them are weeping. Ryan, and Butcher.

“I’m so fucking sorry,” Butcher’s saying in a breathless litany. “I’m sorry. I’m so sorry.”

“Don’t try to die again,” Ryan moans in between sobs. “If Mom knew you did that, she’d really never forgive you. And…neither would I.”

Butcher sets his hand against Ryan’s hair and doesn’t reply.

“I do, though. I forgive you,” Ryan murmurs, pulling away to look up at Butcher and his matching tear-stained cheeks. “I just…have one question.”

“What’s that?” Butcher asks, scrubbing at his face with his sleeve.

Ryan copies him, getting the worst of the mess off his face before he says, “What’s up here that you and my dad come out to do?”

Homelander’s ears burn, but he forces himself to stay rooted to the spot. It’s been one of his more clarified goals with Ryan to let him express himself, to allow him the dignity of his own words as much as his own voice. Unfortunately, now, that means answering power as to the nature of Arcadia, and their relationship, is fully in Butcher’s court.

And because Butcher is a fiend and a bastard, he tells Ryan, a child, with complete and unflappable sincerity, “This here’s where I fuck your dad.”

“Fuck,” Homelander mutters.

He waits for the worst to come, waits for Ryan to be disgusted with him. Waits for the world as he knows it to end.

But Ryan only squints at Butcher, and then at Homelander, and says, “Oh.”

That’s it. Oh.

As if it’s no big deal. As if there’s no reason it should be.

Ears roaring and heart pounding, Homelander floats back across the water to the grass. Stiffly, he keeps his hands bolted behind his back and says, or rather, announces, “What do you think, bud, are you ready to go home?”

“Um.”

For a long moment, none of them move. Ryan still has his arms around Butcher, and Butcher still has his hand in Ryan’s hair. He drops it to Ryan’s shoulder, loosening the clasp of their embrace so Ryan can disentangle from him if he wants, but Ryan only twists around to face Homelander without stepping away from Butcher.

“It is okay if I stay for a while?”

Homelander bites his cheek to keep his distress under wraps. He had dimly let himself hope for some conclusion approaching reconciliation, or at least catharsis for Ryan, and so he can only be glad for this outcome. It’s the best thing for Ryan. It will make him happy.

But, oh, Butcher is going to get it once they’re alone.

“How ‘bout it, Billy?” Homelander chirps. “Can he stay?”

“Fine with me,” he simpers, then, softer, for Ryan, “if it’s what you want.”

The next two hours are spent idly chaperoning Ryan and Butcher’s visitation. They’re gentle with each other; Butcher had never not been gentle with Ryan, but now Ryan is, too. 

With the vitriol out and the air cleared, the feeling between them is lighter. They don’t even say all that much to each other, but it’s enough, even for Homelander, to feel the shift in the mood between them. The sun’s setting and Ryan and Butcher have been sitting in the grass in easy, warm silence for fifteen minutes when Ryan finally looks at Homelander.

“Okay,” he says. “I’m ready.”

He hugs Butcher goodbye, a gesture that goes on longer than either of them seem to have anticipated. They’re all golden in the sun. Looking at them stirs fondness in Homelander’s chest, loosening the knot of unease behind his sternum.

Butcher avoids looking at Homelander once he takes Ryan under his arm to get him home.

It takes a fraction of the time it usually takes Butcher, flying Ryan back to the townhouse in the city. Homelander touches down on the front stoop and walks Ryan inside.

“That went better than you thought it would,” he says brightly, staunchly avoiding the elephant in the room.

“Yeah.”

“Got it all off your chest.”

“Uh huh.”

“And — Butcher, too,” he adds, effervescent.

“Yup.”

Ryan kicks off his shoes and sits on the couch with his feet tucked up underneath him. He has a mead journal open in front of him on the coffee table, pencil in hand. Homelander tracks the scratching of graphite against paper, watches the shapes come together to form little doodles in the margins.

“You…”

In the middle of shaking out a pack of colored pencils, Ryan looks up at him, only half paying attention. He hums a question.

“Well, y-you don’t…think less of your dad, knowing he’s — I’ve — got…weaknesses?”

The sky blue pencil twirling between Ryan’s fingers keeps twirling without interruption. He shrugs, saying, “I don’t care if you like my dad. My mom did.” Without seeming to hear what he’s said, he starts filling in his drawing with the colored pencil. “Are you going back?”

“Wha…” Homelander blinks. “W-why would I?”

“It looked pretty when we left,” Ryan says, shrugging again. “You can still catch the sunset, and Butcher—” He hesitates, studying the line he’s just etched into the page. “He’s probably still there.”

“It really doesn’t bother you?” 

Homelander hears the pathetic crack in his voice and immediately wishes he could recall the words, but he can’t. Ryan, when finally looks at him, doesn’t appear to be upset or suddenly, irretrievably hateful. He also doesn’t appear to be confused.

“It doesn’t bother me. Does it bother you?”

“No,” Homelander answers, too quickly for it to sound even remotely believable. “No, of course not.”

Ryan studies him for a long moment, then he drops it in favor of returning to his notebook.

“Cool,” he says. “You should go back then.”

Homelander doesn’t move, still trying to adjust to this reality where someone he loves discovers a hidden piece of him, and continues to love him anyway. He wrings his hands behind his back, conflicted over how to react.

“I’ll be fine,” Ryan says, glancing up at him. “I’ll order a pizza.”

Like it’s that easy. Like the heavens haven’t moved and caused the world to shift slightly out of focus. 

He’s still in his head when Ryan surprises him with a hug.

They don’t say anything for a long time, but Homelander’s overwhelmed with a multitude of things he wants to say. Things he feels helpless to quantify in words, but that he needs to explain. 

“It just happened,” he whispers.

“It’s okay, Dad. Really. It’s not a big deal.”

Homelander hugs him back, dithering but trying to pretend he isn’t. All he can think of, much to his own deepening sense of dread, is Soldier Boy. He would never let this stand, if he knew. For Butcher, somehow, Homelander feels like he’d make an exception; like Butcher’s proclivity for throwing Homelander through reinforced steel and sticking his dick wherever he wants after could be boiled down to dominance and pure male aggression. 

He knows that’s how Dad would see it because Homelander had thought that’s what he himself wanted, and that was precisely why he’d felt compelled to pursue it. That was why he found himself in Butcher’s kitchen that night, saying the words, I’ve been thinking about fucking you.

Because it took Butcher grabbing Homelander by the chin and slamming him into cheap linoleum for him to realize that was precisely where he wanted to be. 

Dad wouldn’t see power in that. He’d just see weakness. A crying boy in need of a firm hand. 

One more disappointment. 

 

Don’t forget I told you so. 

 

“I’ll be back late,” Homelander says, palming the back of Ryan’s neck. “Don’t wait up for me.”

“Kay. Pepperoni?”

“Sure.”

At it just so happens, Ryan’s right on both counts. Homelander’s there in time to see the last rays of sun go out over the horizon, and when he touches down on the rooftop of the power plant, he can feel Butcher’s heartbeat within.

He drops in through the hole in the ceiling and looks around. Butcher’s coat and his phone are on the ground near his bag. The screen on his phone is smashed in.

“You’re going through these pretty quick lately,” he mutters.

Behind him, Butcher drops down from the rafters where he’d been lounging. He stomps over, his heart rate climbing into a battle haze. Hearing it, feeling it crescendo into him like a building, terrible wave, sets Homelander ablaze.
