He half turns once Butcher’s close enough to grab him, like a dance partner anticipating a twirl, and Butcher does grab him. He fists both hands in Homelander’s suit, knuckles scraping against his chest and holding him. Homelander stares at him, skin thrumming, heart pounding in his throat, and waits for a blow that never comes.

Butcher’s hand claws up his shoulder, around to the scruff of his neck, and reels Homelander in close. Close enough that Homelander can feel his breath in his mouth and see the flecks of hazel in his eyes. Homelander’s still waiting, still listening with his whole body, when Butcher surges against him, binding him with a kiss. Homelander kisses him back on reflex alone, hands settling at Butcher’s waist, to steady him more than anything. In the next moment, Butcher crashes bodily into him, arms wrapped tightly around Homelander’s neck.

He’s shaking, and his heart is like thunder. It’s surreal.

Homelander peels himself away, in spite of the single wounded noise that falls from Butcher’s lips in protest. In spite of the fight that sparks in him when he thinks Homelander’s trying to get away.

Gentling him, barring an arm behind Butcher’s back, he whispers, “Let me.”

Amazingly, Butcher does. He lets him.

Homelander floats them back up to the rafters where Butcher had been hidden away like a cat waiting to die — and isn’t that an apt description, knowing what he knows now — and sprawls out on his back with Butcher pulled over on top of him. Stripping off his gloves, he keeps one hand between Butcher’s shoulders and the other in his hair. Butcher, for his own part, hasn’t relaxed that fist he made against Homelander’s heart.

He doesn’t speak, and so neither does Homelander. It’s funny, he thought he’d have a tirade ready for when he got back, but his mind has been comfortably blank since he took to the sky outside the townhouse. He didn’t think he’d come back to find this. In fact, he felt pretty certain Butcher would have left as soon as Homelander took Ryan home.

But he stayed. He stayed here in solitude and made a nest out of shadows.

And then, Homelander understands it all perfectly: Butcher’s vendetta against the world, his decision to use the Temp V at all, his alliance with Dad, his moment of cruelty towards Ryan. Even that carrion smile on his face that night in his apartment, when his life changed.

“You wanted it to be you,” he murmurs. “You wanted to be the sacrifice.”

“Why not,” Butcher says, muffled with how his face is turned into Homelander’s chest. But the closed fist of his hand does steadily loosen. “’S always gotta be someone.”

Homelander covers Butcher’s hand over his heart the exact second his fingers unfurl.

“There was never a way out for you, was there?”

Butcher raises his head to meet Homelander’s eyes. He looks the way he did standing with Ryan by the lake. He looks young. Younger than Homelander’s ever known him to be. Homelander realizes, when Butcher just keeps looking at him without answering, that he’s holding his breath. It occurs to him that this isn’t the first time he’s caught himself hanging on Butcher’s every word, or more often than not, his taciturnity.

It’s because of this habit of his, this tendency he has to tune into Butcher with all of his fine-tuned senses, that he feels the slight twist in his wrist as his hand begins to turn beneath Homelander’s. Turning and turning, until he can grip Homelander’s hand back.

“I never could see a way out,” he rumbles, “before you.”

Slow, insistent heat blooms beneath Homelander’s eyes and down his neck, across his chest. He feels his heartbeat begin to quicken, a little at a time.

“You don’t even know what you did, do you? You never thought about what you were givin’ me when you jabbed that needle in me arm. Or what that’d be like for me, bringin’ Ryan here like you did. Givin’ me that chance to fix summat I shoulda had to die to ever get clear of. That weren’t nothin’ but a game to you.”

“That’s not true.”

“Oh, no?”

Homelander sighs through his nose. “It’s not all true. I didn’t think about you when I brought Ryan here because I was thinking about Ryan.” He sighs again, clutching Butcher’s one hand and waving the other around for emphasis when he talks. “He asks about you. Most of the time I don’t think he even knows he’s doing it, and I’ve — I’ve been cast aside and neglected. I’ve been — lonely. I don’t want that for him. That isn’t a game to me. That is my son.”

I don’t care if you like my dad, Ryan had said. My dad.

“He loves you.” Homelander’s eyes sting with tears. Of course, they do. He can’t think of being loved by anyone, least of all by Ryan, without feeling like his heart’s going to tear itself in two. “Do you understand? Do you — can you fathom what a gift that is? To be loved in the way that kid loves you.”

“I understand.” Swallowing tightly, he adds, “I had it once.”

“I’m telling you you never lost it,” Homelander intones, gripping Butcher by the hair. Not hard, merely to hold him. To feel how close he is, and to feel the fact of his obeisance, as good as genuflection in this, their church. “Maybe you lost everything else. So did I.” He shakes his head, at a loss. In disbelief over what he feels himself wanting to say, and wondering if this is it, if he’s finally lost his mind. “I could…never see a way out either. I didn’t even know I was trapped, sitting idly by while the leash passed from one hand to another, to another, nobody ever wanting to keep me.”

Butcher stares at him, all of him so tightly focuses on Homelander that he feels almost drunk at the attention. The feel of him breathing, of his chest moving against Homelander’s, is so like sharing a body. Like returning to a single composite flesh he has always been searching for. Like coming home.

“William—” He searches Butcher’s eyes, decision made before he can even consider the ramifications. “Billy.”

Roughly, he asks, “Yeah, what?”

“You freed me.” Homelander nods shakily, warm, so warm. “You did.”

And then, the unthinkable. Butcher presses Homelander’s hand up by his head to brace against, interlacing their fingers, and leverages himself up, his other hand draped whisper-soft against the base of Homelander’s throat. He bends his head as if to drink from a plentiful fountain and presses his mouth, lips soft and sweet, to Homelander’s. 

He opens up beneath it, hot and pliable and liquid, as a wellspring should be. Sweet, crushing darkness descends, stealing the breath right from his lungs. Butcher makes a noise against his teeth, and Homelander, fluid, grinds up into him.

All between them then is friction and heat and broken gasps.

It’s Butcher that reaches down and opens first Homelander’s pants, and then his own. His calloused hand is dry, warm. Wonderful.

“Wanna try something,” Butcher murmurs, giving Homelander a squeeze before levering off him.

Protesting the loss of that hand around him, he reaches for him. “No, don’t—”

Butcher watches him, eyes inscrutable. Always so impossible to know, even with the cyphers he’s revealed over the course of this long afternoon.

“You’ll like it,” he says.

“Do you promise?” Homelander asks, halfway to mulish, entirely for the chance to pout.

Butcher takes hold of his chin, how he had that first night when all this started, but his hand is soft, knowing his strength. Eyes glittering, he smirks and says, “Would I lie to you?”

Homelander drops his head back to laugh. Why wouldn’t he? It’s funny.

He understands pretty quickly what Butcher’s up to. It's in the way he orients himself along the vertical axis of Homelander’s body, and then in the way he uses his mouth to kiss and lick and suck. Groaning, Homelander follows his lead, mouthing at Butcher’s dick, heavy and full between his legs.

Always more innately graceful at this act than Homelander, Butcher swallows him down with ease while Homelander strains and chokes. It’s exquisite, to feel himself bottom out in the back of Butcher’s throat while Butcher holds his head steady and fucks his face. It’s like nothing else, having Butcher hard and sumptuous in his mouth while Butcher’s mouth shatters a low, glorious moan around Homelander’s dick. His orgasm is tremendous. He spills and spills, and Homelander swallows it all.

Gasping, with Butcher’s spent and softening dick on his cheek, Butcher pulls off him for only a moment, just long enough to suck his fingers into his mouth. He circles Homelander’s hole with them and then sinks inside in the same moment that his mouth sinks down Homelander’s dick.

His mind whites out in a cascade of perfect incinerating pleasure, like Moses must have felt when God spoke to him and filled him with radiant, divine light.

When he can think again, all he can hear is their breathing and their heartbeats. Their clothes are rumpled and pushed aside, where their grasping hands and rollicking bodies left them. Butcher’s under his arm, his cheek pressed against Homelander’s collar bone. He smells like Homelander, and he looks — completely at peace, for the first time since the night of the Vought Christmas party so many years ago.

“I wish you’d ask me for something,” Homelander slurs, most of his brain still offline in the wake of their fucking. “Anything. The nation, the world, the star systems. I’d get them for you. I’d find a way. But only if you asked.” He brushes his lips against Butcher’s hairline. “Would you?”

The silence, frenetic and weighted, lasts only a few seconds. 

“No,” Butcher says thickly.

At hearing that — though he knew it would be the answer — his heart fills right to the brim, and spills. Like a cup overflowing endlessly, tirelessly.

“No,” Homelander echoes, pressing a kiss to Butcher’s temple and then to his cheekbone. “No,” he says again, laughing, even as his eyes sting and his heart races and his throat aches. “You wouldn’t.”

Butcher kisses him, and Homelander, tasting salt, touches Butcher’s cheek, feeling for the dried tracks of his tears. He finds new tears. They slick Homelander’s fingertips and his palm.

“It’s all right,” he says into Butcher’s mouth. “It’s all right.”

But for a long time after, Butcher buries his face in Homelander’s throat, and his tears continue to flow. Like a cup overflowing. 

Endlessly, tirelessly.

Chapter 10: spear of destiny

They fuck all night at Arcadia. They fuck in the rafters with Butcher’s teeth set against the pulse in Homelander’s neck; they fuck on the floor with Homelander holding Butcher down while he rides his dick; they fuck on the roof underneath the stars — Butcher gets distracted looking up at them — and they fuck out on the grass with only the moon to illuminate their bodies. 

In between orgasms, when his heart’s still going erratically in his ears and he can hear Butcher’s hammering to match, he finds Butcher’s hand with his own.

“What are you thinking about?”

“Tryin’ not to.”

“Then by definition, you’re thinking of something,” Homelander persists. “What is it?”

Butcher groans, tired and sulky, and says, “You really ain’t never gonna be satisfied.”

“You didn’t just realize that,” he points out.

Tugging his hand out of Homelander’s to rub at his forehead, Butcher shakes his head and leaves that as his answer. Homelander recaptures that hand to hold it captive once more.

“I’m just asking. You don’t have to get so pissy about it.”

A languid silence stretches between them. Arcadia is quiet during the day, but at night the crickets and frogs bring the place to life. Butcher shrugs his shoulders against the ground as if trying to get comfortable.

“That old Nazi cunt,” he says, seemingly out of nowhere. “She really did it for you?”

Homelander’s still stupid from his last three orgasms and doesn’t catch onto who he means. He blinks against the dark, remembers Stormfront, and rolls his eyes.

“She was the only match for me in all the world,” he mutters.

“Was she.”

“And Maeve before her,” he adds, following the threads in what he assumes must inform Butcher’s train of thought. “Understanding didn’t come until much later.”

“Understandin’ of what?”

“That they weren’t what I needed.” He lets his eyes linger on Butcher in the moonlight, on his sweat-slick skin glimmering silver in the dark. “When it came right down to it, I know I wasn’t what they needed either, much less what they wanted. I never have been exactly what anyone wanted me to be.”

Butcher breathes and doesn’t answer. His chest rises and falls, rises and falls.

“Sometimes I wonder what it is you’d like me to be,” he continues, laying an idle, thoughtless trap.

“Already said, didn’t I?”

“Yes,” Homelander concedes, smiling. “There’s nothing you’d ask for, but isn’t there anything you want from me all the same?”

“For you to be quiet?” Butcher tries.

The smile on Homelander’s face expands, like a flower opening to the sun. Infuriating man. Dreadful and intoxicating, often simultaneously.

“Billy,” he says, to no reply. “That thing you do sometimes.”

He doesn’t ask what thing.

“When you put your mouth on me,” Homelander clarifies.

Eloquent as ever, Butcher asks, “Like when I blow you, or when I eat you out?”

Owing to most of his blood having yet to circle back to his head, Homelander can’t feel it if his face warms any at the question. He just says, “The latter.”

With a meditative hum, Butcher tells him, “Sit on my face if that’s what you want, but I’m gonna need a minute if you had somethin’ more strenuous in mind.”

“No. I meant, do you like it?”

Mouth twisting, Butcher laughs and says, “Sure.” He rubs the back of his hand across his forehead, scooping away dark, sweat-drenched hair as he does. With a kind of flatness entering into his tone, he adds, “Wouldn’t do it otherwise, would I?”

Homelander twists around, combing his fingers through the first dustings of Butcher’s pubic hair. With his other hand still lined up with Butcher’s, he interlaces their fingers and gives him a squeeze. That gets Butcher’s attention, barely. He opens his eyes, at least. Gratified, pleased under the heady weight of Butcher’s hazy stare, Homelander leans in to brush his lips against the edge of Butcher’s mouth.

“But do you like getting eaten out?” he presses.

“Oh.” He hitches his knee up to make way for Homelander’s wandering fingers, his heart only stuttering the tiniest fraction at the first deliberate brush. “Guess I do, but it’s been a while.”

“Had you been with a lot of men before me?” 

“Why?” Butcher sighs, tipping his head back for the trail of kisses Homelander leaves down the curve of his jaw, his throat, his collar bone. “Want me to give you their names so you can track ‘em down and burn their eyes outta their heads?”

“Only if you’re feeling generous,” Homelander says, looking up with his mouth soft and open over Butcher’s heart.

“Well, I ain’t,” he grouses, but his hand in Homelander’s hair is tender.

“Hmm, you know…” He mouths down the beautifully sculpted muscles of Butcher’s abdomen, down his belly to his groin, and lower, slipping one of Butcher’s knees over his shoulder. “I think I’d like to be, now.” 

Unable to resist the lure that is Butcher’s dick, when it’s right there, he closes his lips around just the tip and sucks hard. He means to pull off then, but he bobs his head, liking the familiar motion, the heat and smell and taste of Butcher on his tongue. 

It’s only because Butcher makes a bitten off noise at the back of his throat that he remembers he had a specific goal in mind when he put himself down near Butcher’s lap like this. When he does manage to take his mouth away, he’s struck as if by lightning at the tightly wound bow of Butcher’s body beneath his, at Butcher’s inkblot eyes pinning him in place like a rabbit in a snare. 

Some piece of Butcher has always been untouchable, even in the face of his own reckoning. Even in the face of death. Even with tears on his face and his hand gripping Homelander’s, it has always been Homelander holding onto him, pulling him back, using him to drop anchor.

How can that be, he wonders. What is his secret?

Homelander runs his lips down the length of Butcher’s dick and lower, past his balls to his taint, savoring the meal of his shiver.

“What do you think, Billy?” he asks. “Want me to be generous?”

Thinking an answer won’t be coming, he chooses not to wait for one and sets his mouth where he wants it. Knowing it’s what Butcher wants, too, he experiments with tension and pressure, with licking and sucking and humming his pleasure so it reverberates in Butcher’s bones and shocks up the length of his nervous system. 

Incrementally, the tension in Butcher’s body loosens and uncoils, and drunk at what that means, Homelander’s hands and his mouth only get greedier. He braces his palms against Butcher’s ass — only to touch at first but then — to make room for his tongue, to spear Butcher on it as deeply and wonderfully as he can. 

Butcher curses convulsively, momentarily frantic and resisting, until he just… melts.

The proud column of his neck drops back against the grass, his hands seeking and grasping and pulling blindly, desperately. His body, normally a pillar of strength and resolution, collapses, writhing, beneath the assault of Homelander’s mouth.

He might’ve guessed Butcher would like it, knowing firsthand how it feels to be touched this way. To be devoured. But Butcher more than likes it. There is relief in his abandon, evident in the arcing of his spine and the tossing of his head. Homelander seeks to give Butcher as much of it as he can take, and more. He seeks to shower him in every last thing he can’t, or won’t, ask to be given.

It sets all of him alight to know they could topple the axis of the world if they wanted. That Butcher could have Homelander — Homelander, to say nothing of the world — on his knees, indefinitely.

If he just wanted it.

Homelander plunges deep inside with one finger, unthinking of anything at all except how loose and slippery Butcher’s become with only the ministrations of Homelander’s mouth to help him along. Butcher’s body seizes with familiar tension, his dick curving up toward his belly, hot and hard and begging to be tasted. Homelander can only oblige him, skin thrumming at the low groan it scrapes out of Butcher. 

How good it feels good to know his body; to anticipate the rushing of his blood and divine meaning from his twitching muscles; to map out all that wondrous flesh with his hands and his eyes and his mouth. To smear broken moans and gasps into Butcher’s skin and be met with more of the same music.

There’s so much in the way of sweat and saliva and semen between them. It should be disgusting, the squelching of his fingers making room in Butcher’s body to hold him.

“Do you want me,” he asks on a thready exhale. Barely able to string together a coherent question with the frenzy in his blood. His hands, when they aren’t on Butcher, shake and lead him astray. “Tell me you want me. Say my name.”

“Fucking…” Butcher drops his head back, straining beneath the two fingers Homelander drives into him. “Homelander, fuck.”

He stills, heart in his throat as much as in his dick. “No,” he says, soft.

Butcher spasms and squirms, tendons standing out on his neck and muscles tensing into sharp relief all down the planes of his powerful body. The choked off sound in the back of his throat is one of pain, but he doesn’t reach for Homelander’s hand or ask him to stop.

“John,” Homelander tells him.

“What?” Butcher gasps.

“My name,” he murmurs, taking his fingers away. “Call me John.”

He ruts home, clarity finding him and taking root, as it always seems to when he’s fucking someone and it feels right. Butcher scratches down his back, across his shoulders, blunt nails raking across burning flesh. 

“My name,” Homelander grits out. 

“John,” Butcher cries out raggedly.

“Tell me you want me,” he pleads, already stuttering in his pace, slipping in his control and in his composure. “Say it.”

Butcher clasps a loose hand against the back of Homelander’s head, beard brushing Homelander’s cheek like gravel to match his voice when he says, “Want you.”

Shuddering, he spends and spends until the imaginary strings holding him taut disintegrate. He lays there, spilled across Butcher’s body and heaving for breath, the universe reorienting around the slowing drum of Butcher’s heartbeat under his cheek. When he remembers himself and reaches for Butcher, he bats his hand away, and Homelander, divested of sense or discernment, relaxes against him. 

“Guess I should be flattered,” Butcher grunts, pushing Homelander off him.

Rather than be cross, Homelander sits up on his elbows to watch him walk stiffly into the lake. He twists around to rest his cheek in his hand, tracking the emergence of horripilation all over Butcher’s skin as he steps into the frigid water. His proud heartbeat jolts and races.

Homelander listens to his hissed, “Fuck!” and smiles, hopelessly fond.

“You’re perfect,” he whispers.

Butcher cocks his head and looks over his shoulder at him. “Piss off,” he says.

“I mean it,” Homelander insists, laying down on his stomach and resting his chin on his hands.

Ignoring him to wade out deeper into the water, Butcher drops himself, head and shoulders, into the lake. He springs up a full ten seconds later wiping a hand down his face and then back up over the wet mop of his hair.

“You’re everything I want, y’know. More than everything.”

Still ignoring him, Butcher sets about scrubbing his skin raw. Pulling himself up from the grass and floating over, Homelander sets his eyes on the water and brings it to a simmer with the heat of his gaze. Butcher looks up at him, not quick enough to dodge Homelander’s tackle. The water is delightfully hot when he bombs into it. Butcher doesn’t fight him, but he does twist around in his hold to be pressed against him sideways rather than back to front.

“Been waitin’ on me to ask what you want, have you? Let’s hear it then. What do ya want?”

“Currently?” Homelander brushes a wet lock of hair away from Butcher’s forehead. “This. You. To be a dad. For you to do it with me.”

Butcher’s eyebrows pull down. “I ain’t Ryan’s dad.”

Homelander gives him a look, not judgmental, but leveling. He says, “He told me how you stepped up, after his mom died. I know what it meant to him, and for all that you pretend, I know you know it, too. Today demonstrated that well enough.”

“He’s a good kid.”

“And you’re what, a good man?” Homelander muses, loosening the coil of his arms to bring Butcher around to face him. “A doting husband?”

“You fucking be careful.”

“I’m simply making the observation that you love him,” he points out, to his mind agreeably. Reasonably. “You don’t have to put on airs with me, Billy. Not when I know what you gave up that day to — save our son. Not when I was the one who saw what that choice did to you. What it left you with.”

“And you care, why? ‘Cuz you’re a good man? ‘Cuz you’ve ever fuckin’ loved anyone that much?”

“I loved Maeve,” he says, feeling defensiveness rise up in him. “I loved Madelynn, I loved Stormfront—”

“They’re dead!” Butcher shouts.

“But you’re not!”

Butcher stops, breath hitching. He stares at Homelander, a storm happening on his face, in his eyes. Abruptly hearing what he’s said, Homelander lets go of him, unsure and unbelieving of what he’s just narrowly avoided admitting to.

I loved them and they’re dead, but you’re not, and I —

“You’re not,” Homelander repeats, voice gone brittle with revelation. “They were weak. They were beneath me.”

“Like Becca was beneath you?” Butcher asks, softly, like quiet that precedes a violent backhand. “Like she was weak?”

Tell Daddy, he remembers the phantom of Becca Butcher saying to him in a dream. He’d been pared down without his powers then, as good as human, and though it had been a fiction of his mind that she was there at all, didn’t he dream that she stood over him? Didn’t he hear Butcher’s words in her mouth like she was an extension of him, or a beginning?

Didn’t she do what all the other women in his life couldn’t? Didn’t she survive him? Didn’t she give him the one thing he loves most in the world? 

And didn’t Butcher cherish her, despite years and circumstances, despite doubt. Despite death.

A man like him, to love like that. Even now.

“No,” Homelander murmurs.

The cobra-stillness of Butcher’s body sharpens infinitesimally. Against the brightening red horizon, the lines of his face stand out in stark relief. “What the fuck do you mean, no?”

“She wasn’t weak.” 

He thinks back to that night she confronted him, when he could’ve killed her for her sharp tongue, just as easily as Stormfront could have. He hadn’t known then that it wasn’t Ryan that made her bold. Had foolishly thought her defiance stemmed from thinking she was untouchable being the mother of his child, but that hadn’t been the case. She just wasn’t afraid of him anymore, which only meant that she had been, once.

Nothing he’d gotten from her had been earned, or won, and wasn’t that precisely what Butcher said to him the day his powers were taken from him?

Did you come by any of it honestly, or was it all the V?

Blinking, he says, “That’s what you meant.”

“Fuck are you talkin’ about?”

“I’ve been weak my whole life. I thought I killed that part of me, but I had my hands around the wrong piece of myself. Didn’t I?”

The red dawn fractures into hues of yellow, green, and pale blue, like sea glass crushed underfoot. A ray of golden sunlight strikes Butcher’s face like a benediction, or like scales falling from his eyes.

“I can’t predict you at all, Billy,” Homelander murmurs. “I can’t control you and I can’t cut you out of me. What does that make you? Who are you to me?”

Butcher’s eyes are vibrant amber against the rising sun. He doesn’t answer.

“Do you even know?”

“Thought I was here to give you a war,” Butcher says tightly. “Scorched earth. Weren’t that the idea?”

“I don’t need a war.”

He snaps his mouth shut, ears ringing and face burning at the simple admission. Ever since Dad, there’s been something nagging at the back of his mind telling him things aren’t over. That voice is quieter lately — not gone, but close — and standing here in the cooling water with Butcher while the sun comes up, he can see a different way.

“I don’t need a war,” he persists, reaching for Butcher’s hands through the brightening water. “I don’t, if you don’t.”

Half a smile, disbelieving, flickers across Butcher’s face in the sun. “You wanna play house.”

“It’s what they do,” Homelander says, flinging his arm out of the water in the direction of Dresden, and the bigger cities beyond it, and all the little mud people inhabiting them. “What makes it real for them and impossible for us?”

“You’d get bored and make supe terrorists. Oh, wait, that’s right. You already did that.”

“You’re really gonna come after me for one measly international incident when it was you that brought Dad back?”

Butcher rolls his eyes. “I did that to get rid of you.”

“Is that what you were doing back there, getting rid of me?” Homelander asks preciously. “Well, hey. I could put you on my dick again and see if it takes on the second try.”

“Another twenty seconds of my life I won’t get back? Fucking tragic.”

“It’s a compliment!” Homelander calls after Butcher as he tromps out of the water and onto the grassy bank. He floats out after him, does a few quick circuits around Arcadia to dry off, and digs his suit out of the rafters where he shucked it. His gloves aren’t anywhere he can see, so he deigns to go without and regroups with Butcher. “It’s a compliment,” he murmurs, wrapping his arms around him from behind. “See what you do to me?”

He’s got joggers on and a towel ruffling his hair, but no shirt. It makes Homelander regret getting dressed so quickly, but at least his hands are bare. He presses a feather soft kiss to the back of Butcher’s neck and then another behind his ear.

“Daddy,” he murmurs.

“What?”

Smiling against Butcher’s skin, smelling the lake in his wet hair, he says, “If you’re done trying to pick a fight…” He waits for Butcher’s snort, and suitably rewarded, adds, “I want to show you something.”

Butcher sighs, pulls on his boots and a t-shirt reading Footloose across the chest. He grabs his jacket, shrugs that on, and holds his arms out. “Well?”

Homelander takes him by the wrist and tows him over to the hole in the ceiling. “Hold onto me.”

Quicker than a 747 taking off, Homelander speeds them up and out, far enough away from Dresden that the sun has yet to peek out over the hillside when they get there. He makes sure he’s not about to accidentally snap Butcher’s neck and stops them in place. Butcher shudders, nearly plummeting to the earth when Homelander lets go of him. He fists his hand in Homelander’s collar where it meets his cape and steadily regains his bearings. 

Butcher’s hand’s still just clenched at Homelander’s collar when he realizes where they are and what they’re doing. The deep purple sky glows a carnelian red with the first blush of sunrise, ochre seeping in around jewel toned greens and blues.

From up here, the whole of the world feels quiet. Even to Homelander. He covers Butcher’s slack fist with his hand.

“Pretty impressive, right?”

“It’s nice,” Butcher says, shrugging.

“Always so laconic.”

Taking his hand back, Butcher turns away from the orange canvas of the sky to look at the distant brightening city at their backs. His shoulder brushes Homelander’s, comfort in a grazed touch. The sun lights up the coppery highlights in his hair and casts his face in silky darkness.

“Just tell me one time what’s on your mind,” Homelander coaxes. “I know you weren’t really wondering about my past exploits, earlier.”

“That how you talk about people you loved?”

“Every single one of them only ever saw me as a tool or a weapon.” He rolls his eyes, at himself more than at his string of failed relationships. “At a time in my life when I was glad for the excuse to show off how useful I could be.”

Butcher pockets his hands, a bit of a breeze catching in his hair when he looks pointedly over his shoulder at the view he’s missing. 

When his eyes catch next on Homelander’s, he flushes from sternum to hairline.

“Well…” He fidgets. “Old habits.”

He refuses to quail under Butcher’s straight stare, but there’s something so expectant in the weight of it that he can’t help feeling beckoned into reaching for Butcher’s cheek. The impulse to kiss him has been with him ever since the last time they kissed, which by his estimate was far too long ago. He doesn’t give in fully to his craving; there are other things he wants more.

But he does press his thumb to Butcher’s bottom lip, just to tide himself over before he does let himself take what he wants.

“Are you really not going to tell me what you’re thinking?”

“You wanna know that badly? Why?” He smiles suddenly, sharp as switchblades, right beneath Homelander’s thumb. “Oh, I get it now. Still jealous of that Insight twat, are ya?”

“What?” Homelander tears his hand away. “No.”

Rather than set his teeth on edge, the low rumble of Butcher’s laugh warms him through. From embarrassment, yes, but from something softer, too. Something that makes him feel like he’s in on the joke rather than the butt of it. He feels himself smiling back, even laughing.

“I went and saw him, after. Do you know what he said to me?”

Butcher’s smile stalls on his face. “No, what?”

“He said you couldn’t stop thinking about me,” Homelander says, only pretending to preen. “Once you ran out of smokescreen, that is. I guess that’s why I’m curious. What did he see when you thought about me, Billy?”

“Greatest Hits.” He shrugs. “Just a supercut of us fucking.”

Homelander turns it over in his head, remembering what else Insight had said. He huffs a laugh through his nose and squints up at the sun, now refulgent and well above the horizon. Without giving Butcher a warning, he takes him by the shoulder and speeds them into the next time zone. The sudden plunge into darkness startles Butcher, and he clings.

Adorable.

“He said you couldn’t stop,” Homelander echoes, understanding. “You couldn’t, because I was just the next iteration for your smokescreen. What was it, Billy?”

“Doesn’t matter,” he grinds out against Homelander’s neck. “You went and fuckin’ told him.”

Frowning, Homelander helps to steady him. “What do you mean?”

“Christ, it’s cold up here,” Butcher huffs, pulling his jacket closed over his front. “The anti-V serum, remember that? Serious fucking R & D that’s got the goods to get rid of all them pesky supes what don’t pass muster with you, and you went and gave ‘em a courtesy call.”

“Oh.” He opens and closes his mouth. “Ah, that — well, yes, okay. That actually makes sense.”

“And for your information, darlin’,” Butcher says, loping in close so his breath’s warm against Homelander’s chin. “I went through two other diversions to keep him off you.”

“Hmm.” Homelander parts Butcher’s jacket with his hands so he can grip him by the hips. “What were they?”

“Rap music,” he lists, so officiously that Homelander nearly laughs. “And, uh, Vegas coke binge from a few years back. Plenty of stock footage there to choose from. You ever hear of Hunter Thompson?”

“You better take care of me, Lord,” Homelander hums, “if you don’t, you’re gonna have me on your hands.”

Butcher tilts his head. “The caped twat reads.”

“Yes, I contain multitudes,” he drawls, edging his fingertips beneath the hem on Butcher’s shirt to chart that smooth, supple skin with his hands. He holds Butcher close, their bodies pressed together as closely as they can be with clothes in between them. “As I know you do.”

“That your way of asking one more time,” Butcher muses. “All right.”

He catches Homelander’s chin between his thumb and one curled knuckle, just as golden light is breaking across the sky, and kisses him.

“Don’t think you can get out of telling me,” Homelander says against that soft mouth he knows so well.

“I am tellin’ you,” he growls. “Git.”

The next kiss he gives Homelander is harsher, testier, but it softens as it deepens, and the feel of it is as crushingly wonderful as ever. Homelander presses in lightly with his fingertips, feeling Butcher’s ribs and the organs contained within them: his spleen, his liver, his pancreas, his lungs, and his heart.

Butcher runs his fingers up the back of Homelander’s head and licks into his mouth.

“I’m the one who’s never gonna be satisfied?” Homelander teases, when he can breathe again, and even then, it’s a near thing.

“You get that that last time didn’t do a thing for me,” Butcher grouses.

“It was a compliment!”

“Fuck your compliments.” He catches Homelander’s chin in his hand again, equal parts intensity and control. “Said you wanted to be generous. Ain’t I shown you how?” He licks the swell of Homelander’s upper lip where the shiny skin becomes a vulnerable pink. “Ain’t I been so good to you?”

“Yes, Daddy,” Homelander gasps, pulling Butcher harder against him, feeling the outline of him through his clothes, lightheaded at the nearness of him. “Yes.”

“Want Daddy to remind you,” he rumbles, shifting his hips, “how good he can be?”

Homelander makes a sound like keening into Butcher’s teeth, and then he’s speeding them across the morning sky again in search of an arena that will hold them. A second Arcadia, perhaps, if it could be called that. Someplace that’s only theirs, and only for this — for fucking and feeling and loving with complete abandon. What do the mud people call it? A honeymoon? 

Well, sure. Why not?

 

/

 

Butcher’s plan to get them back to Arcadia backfires somewhat. They wind up on a beach, reset to nightfall over an amorphous continent that sees them tumbling in the sand. 

He hasn’t got anything with him. No phone, no money, no kit, no longevity in this scheme, whatever it is. Don’t stop Homelander peeling all his clothes right back off him, heedless of whether they got the supplies for this or not, which they don’t. Spit and fingers, hey.

The sun’s high in the sky and blazing at full power by the time they stop, sand and spunk in just about every hole between them. In nearly every direction ahead of them, it’s all blue — sea and sky — and at their backs, crisp jagged mountains cutting them off from the nearest civilization. Even scanning through them, all Butcher gets is rocks.

No cell towers out here, much less cameras.

“What’s the dismount here, John?” Butcher mumbles, scrubbing the back of his wrist over his forehead to dislodge a patch of drying sand. When his question goes unacknowledged, he adds, “‘Cuz we’ve been fucking for summat like six hours now, right? Continuously. My dick’s chafed, I ain’t got the foggiest where we are, I got a sunburn on my arse, and I am fuckin’ famished.”

“I’ll take you to breakfast,” Homelander slurs without looking up from under his arm. “Vought owns a resort in Santorini not far from here. They’ll give me anything I want.”

Well, shit.

“Greece,” Butcher says, staggering to his feet. “This is Greece?”

“Part of it.” Homelander drops his arm off the side of his face. He’s a burnished pink all over from the sun. “A small part. Wanna see more?”

Butcher flaps his arms. “I can’t exactly leave.”

“It’s a straight shot across the Atlantic,” Homelander tells him vaguely, angling his chin what may or may not be due west. “Go if you want.”

Tempting, that. Sure. Pick his way back to the States on an empty stomach with jizz in his hair, and might be his reaches solid ground before exhaustion drops him in the ocean. Yeah, good idea. Grand.

Or he could play nice. Go along to get along. Really lean into it and stretch this little vacation for all the mileage it’s worth. For his trouble.

“Fine,” he grits out.

Homelander hums, twisting around to sun his back. He folds his arms beneath his cheek, and as Butcher’s looking at him, he falls asleep.

To hell with it. He’s been stranded worse places. 

He goes for a swim in the water. It’s warmer than at Arcadia, barring Homelander’s trick with the heat vision, and it is beautiful, there’s no fuckin’ about on that front. He still doesn’t get what they’re doing here, but he’s starting to get an idea. Doesn’t know how he feels about that idea, but he’ll cross that bridge when he comes to it. For now, he occupies himself floating on his back in glittering blue water.

Half an hour after he dozed off, Homelander swims out next to him. For fuckin’ once, he don’t say anything. Just swims next to Butcher with wet hair in his eyes. He looks like a man. Any man. 

When Butcher’s starting to prune, he trudges out of the water to sit on the beach, letting the sun dry the sea off his body. Homelander lingers in the water a while more. It’s almost nice. Almost relaxing, being near him like this without the stakes being quite so high. He feels a little like he can properly breathe, actually fills his lungs up in a way he hasn’t in years. Can’t remember the last time he went on holiday; can’t remember the last time his mind went quiet and he didn’t have to fight to make it that way. 

Maybe it is nice, being here. Maybe it’s more than nice.

Movement in all that blue draws his eye to Homelander where he’s walking out of the sea. For a second, backlit by the white sun and the glow of whiter sand, he looks like the god he claims to be. Fuck if Butcher’s gonna tell him that, though. He’d never hear the end of it.

They sit side by side on the beach, the surf nipping at their toes and water droplets leaping off the ends of their hair. It’s colder and colder on Butcher’s skin the more that his skin warms. Always suspicious of this particular brand of tranquility that’s settling over him, Butcher’s mind starts to work. Starts to make him wonder why he’s being given this and what it might signify, and sure enough. That’s when it hits him.

There’s nothing you’d ask for, Homelander had said, back at Arcadia, but isn’t there anything you want from me all the same?

A smile tugs at the corner of Butcher’s mouth, remembering his answer.

For you to be quiet?

He tips his chin to point his face to the sky, trying desperately not to laugh and give himself away. It’s imperative right then that he don’t let his eyes stray. If he does, if he has to look at the maddening, insufferable twat next to him just now, he might bite him on the fucking mouth and never stop.

Wouldn’t that make for a sticky situation.

And in more ways than one, considering the morning they’ve had.

His stomach growls, saving him from having to look at Homelander or at himself, and Homelander, hearing it, tilts his head in his direction.

“Time to go?”

Butcher feels a twinge behind his sternum. He leans back on his hands. “For breakfast?”

Homelander turns his head to look at him properly. His mouth twitches into the smile Butcher’s been fighting on his own face. “Sure,” he says. “What are you in the mood for?”

So many things, as it happens. More and more every second. But he can’t say that.

“Don’t know what’s good around here, do I?” He shrugs loosely, hearing in the spaces between words that he’s talking about more than food. “Surprise me.”

Happy enough to do any old thing he feels inspired to do from one moment to the next, Homelander takes Butcher’s cheek in his hand and leans over to kiss him. Butcher does bite him then, and his theory that he wouldn’t be able to stop once he started, turns out to have been a pretty good fucking guess.

Days pass in this fashion.

They’re photographed in Santorini, or Homelander is, but that’s the only time it happens. As soon as the swarm lets up, Butcher drags Homelander into a gift shop to kit him out in the full touristy ensemble. He looks completely ridiculous. It’s fantastic.

There’s a phone in their suite at the resort. Butcher’s late getting to it, but there’s only one number he has memorized and he’s been reluctant to use it. Gets to be that he can’t put it off much longer, though, and there’s no sign of them leaving Greece anytime soon. He draws himself up onto the king bed where the landline is and dials the number. The line rings twice before connecting.

“Hullo!”

He winces. He can’t help it. “Mum, it’s me. It’s Billy.”

“Darling,” she says, or rather chides. “Where’ve you gone? I had a call from Marvin — dear heart —  he’s been so worried.”

“That how he put it?” Butcher asks, eyes catching on Homelander’s where he’s sprawled out in an armchair on the other side of the room, one knee over the arm and his head tipped back. “He said he was worried?”

“You boys.” Mum tuts. “No, he didn’t say he was worried. But I could tell, couldn’t I? Billy, what’s the matter? Are you all right?”

Homelander sweeps out of the armchair, trailing a plush bathrobe tied at the waist but barely.

“I’m all right, Mum.”

She sighs, fretful and fussy but letting it lie. “As you like, lamb.”

The intense look on Homelander’s face shivers. He stops with his hand on the bedpost at the foot of the canopy bed, covering his mouth with his other hand. But Butcher sees him snickering.

He rolls his eyes, saying, “Mum, could you let the lads know you heard from me? I ain’t got my phone.”

“Of course. But while I have you, darling, when are you coming to visit? I haven’t seen you since the last time when you were comin’ down with that terrible flu.”

Homelander sits in front of him on the bed, pinning Butcher in place with an enigmatic kind of look. Blue like the ocean at midday, his eyes. Must be the deep tan he’s acquired making them stand out like that. Butcher’s never noticed them so much before now.

“I’ll come see ya when I’m back in town, Mum. I promise.”

“So you have gone!” she crows, just a touch cheeky at catching him in a lie. She always could do it better than anyone, save Becca; and now, Hughie. “Somewhere warm, sweetheart? Tell me you’ve gone someplace warm.”

“It’s warm,” Butcher says, taking his knee out from under Homelander’s hand. “Getting lots of sun.”

“Oh, lovely, Billy, that’s lovely.”

The bathrobe slips off Homelander’s shoulder. He scoots forward on the bed, nearly in Butcher’s lap, clean-smelling like the complimentary soap from the bathroom. Citrus and sage.

“Mum, I gotta go.”

“Okay, darling, be safe. I love you.”

He hesitates, knowing this is his chance to tell her something real, something she’ll be able to take back to the boys. An old trick of Dad’s, this. A classic.

“Smooches,” he says, and for Dad, that always meant, Can’t talk now. Not alone.

Homelander eases the phone out of his hand and hangs it up for him. Butcher leans back against the headboard, hands coming up around Homelander’s hips beneath the bottom hem of the robe. It’s the same one Butcher’s wearing, except Butcher tied his properly — for all that’s worth with Homelander plucking it apart.

“Is your dick still chafed?” Homelander muses, reaching for Butcher without waiting for an answer.

“What, would that stop you?” Butcher mutters, thunking his head against oak.

“No,” Homelander says, smiling against Butcher’s mouth. “You feel so good in my hand. Remember when you dared me to rip it off?”

“Thought you might, too.”

“No, even then, you’d conquered me.”

There’s a pump bottle in the nightstand they’ve half emptied just this week. Homelander empties a little more into his hand and tosses the bottle over his shoulder. He’s still loose from an hour ago when they fucked in the shower, and Butcher can feel it, how relaxed he is. How open. The long column of his throat’s hot beneath Butcher’s mouth. He covers all that skin in bites and open-mouthed kisses until he’s mostly just gasping against his neck.

“Did you know, ah, that… that you’d won? As early as that first time?”

“What’d I win?” Butcher groans, hazy, not thinking a bit.

Homelander kisses him, lips soft and absorbing the frantic sounds spilling out of Butcher’s mouth. He wraps his arms around Butcher’s neck, bracing himself so he can fuck himself harder and faster on Butcher’s dick.

“God,” Butcher grits out, thoughtlessly, senselessly. “Oh, fuck me, God.”

A high, frail laugh answers him, and then there’s wet and hot spattering his throat and his chest. Butcher doesn’t wait for Homelander to snap out of his afterglow. He shoves him down onto the bed, mind set on jerking off, but Homelander moves with him, twisting around onto his elbows and knees, chin pressed to his shoulder to catch Butcher’s eyes with his.

“Fuck me. Make me scream.”

Butcher feels drugged. He feels insane. His dick’s so hard he could cut glass with it, and Homelander’s sweet. He’s so fucking sweet.

“Oh, you beauty. You fucking—” He clutches after Homelander’s hips, moves him where he’ll feel him best, and gives it to him. Gives it to him till he don’t know where he ends and Homelander begins. “You fucking treasure, you.”

He curls into the bedspread, yellow hair flopped into his face and sticking to his forehead. Butcher shoves the bathrobe up to his shoulders so he can watch the muscles in Homelander’s back twitch. He struggles out of it, and Butcher, sweating up a storm, fights against his own robe.

“Wanna give me space stations? Consulates? Wanna give me armies and skyscrapers and all the people in them?”

 “Yes!” Homelander’s hand shoots down to tug at his dick. Every subsequent cry from his lips gains in honeyed euphoria. “God, yes! Yes! Yes! Yes, God!”

“Wanna give me the mountains,” Butcher grunts, canting his hips so Homelander can’t miss his dick at his prostate, even when he falls flat on his face and ruts into the mattress. “Wanna give me the sky?”

“Yes, Billy,” he whispers, voice breaking, face twisted up in pleasure. In overstimulation.

Butcher bends down to sink his teeth into the scruff of his neck, fucking Homelander all the while, pounding him through blissed out tears and tinkling moans. He loosens his bite into a sweltering kiss, gentle-like, tender.

“Want me to ask you?” he purrs. “Huh, darlin’?”

Homelander shivers. He keeps face buried in the bedspread. 

Butcher noses at his hair, along the drenched ends of it tapering off at the nape of his neck. He grinds deeper into him, inexorably patient, waiting out Homelander’s full-body shudder and the whine in the back of his throat. “John,” he simpers, mouthing at his neck. “Do ya?”

“N-no. No.”

“And will I?” Butcher sets back on his heels, piling Homelander back against him so their bodies stack like kindling. “Will I, John?”

“Mm-mm.” He shakes his head, at first a negation and then an expression of desperation. Maybe both in the same moment. “Daddy… Daddy, please — ah! Daddy!”

He does get Homelander to scream, but every inch of him Butcher tears down, something comes loose in him to match. Brick by brick. A fortress coming undone. Homelander shifts onto his shoulder, dislodging Butcher like a house built on shifting tectonic plates. Like a toppling tower crumbling into the sea. Foundations uprooted and reduced to rubble.

“You freed me, too,” he breathes into Homelander’s shoulder. “You did.”

Homelander reaches blindly for his hand, and Butcher, though he knows better, reaches for him back. He traces the bent pinky knuckle he’s been feeling for since they crash landed on the beach however many days ago.

“What should we do next?” Homelander murmurs, the smile audible in his voice. “What do you want to do?”

So many things, as it happens. More and more every second. And more, and more.

And fucking more.

“Just this,” he says tightly, holding onto the last bit of himself by the skin of his teeth, it feels like, gripping on with all his strength. Except all he feels in his hands is Homelander. All that fits in his arms is Homelander. All he can think or see or fucking taste is him. “Just now. All right?”

“Believe me,” Homelander croons, twisting around so he can bite Butcher on the mouth, burning and consuming and altering. “I’m amenable to more of this.”

His hands seek, and his eyes, and his mouth, and Butcher’s wrung out, completely tapped, nothing else to give, but he surges to meet that touch all the same. All he wants is more, and to want anything now — to just want to feel good, when he’s spent so long wishing he could feel nothing at all — is such a shock. Such an atrocity. 

Living hasn’t felt like this in so goddamn long. Dying’s seemed so much better by comparison, but it doesn’t now. And he has to wonder if this is what Homelander meant. If this is what he took it to mean, saying Butcher conquered him. Did he mean that life had verve to it because of Butcher; did he mean that death seemed far away with him near?

Is that what it is? This feeling in him, voracious and cavernous and pulling like a vacuum. Like a blackhole.

More.

The days pass, and pass.

Chapter 11: shepherds

Chapter Notes

See the end of the chapter for notes

It’s been seventy-two hours of radio silence when Hughie decides he’s had enough.

He rips everything they have — the damning and the inconsequential alike — from the Butchlander Spank Bank and copies it to a separate account. The massive data migration must alert the others because Frenchie texts Hughie a prompt and very succinct: What are you doing petit hughie?

A second later his phone’s ringing.

“MM,” he says, shouting to be heard over the screeching train pulling into the tunnel. “Listen—”

“Hughie, just take a second, all right. Whatever you’re thinkin’ about doing, let’s all sit down and talk first. Okay?”

In the screaming din of ancient brake lines straining against tons and tons of metal and bodies, Hughie can trick himself into thinking he’s waiting, rather than hesitating. But once the noise is replaced with the smaller sounds of people breathing and conversing and moving through their busy lives, he can hear it more for what it is. 

Uncertainty.

Hughie stands there, fuming, percolating, trying to figure out what the hell he thought his play was going to be. An anonymous leak to the Daily Mail? An email from a throwaway account to the New York Times? Talk about Pandora’s fucking box.

“Hughie,” MM says again, in that low, horse whisperer voice he’s so good at doing. “Take a breath.”

And fuck, Hughie hadn’t even noticed. He sucks a sparse inhale through his teeth. Lets it go shudderingly the same way, back molars clenched tight and aching. The automatic passenger doors slide shut with a pleasant chime and, catching on one business douche’s ass cheeks, spring open again.

Hughie, nearly panicked to tears and standing all alone on the platform like a different kind of douche altogether, starts to laugh. Huge peals of laughter just ringing out of him, gone silly with relief. Dancing right on the knife’s edge of stupidity.

MM sighs on the other line. The business douche turns and flips Hughie off through the little window. Hughie happily returns the favor, smiling wide as he does.

“Ah, jeez,” he says, sighing himself as he watches the train leave the station. He rubs just beneath his eye where a tiny bit of moisture snuck past him in the middle of laughing his ass off. “MM. He’s out there, man. Homelander’s out there, and he’s got Butcher with him, wherever he is.”

“I know, Hughie. But you going off on your own is the wrong play. We’re already down one; we don’t need you dropping off on us, too.”

“Okay, okay,” Hughie concedes, turning to head for the stairs. He doesn’t get two steps when he pulls up short, seeing who’s waiting for him at the bottom landing. “Oh.”

Kimiko smiles and waves. She’s too sweet, really.

“I think I’m offended?” he says, beginning to walk again. “But I also kinda feel a little bit better, too.”

“Good. I’m texting you an address. Frenchie and Annie, too. Shoulda called this meeting yesterday when your ass was climbin’ the walls for leads.”

“I’m worried about him,” Hughie mutters, falling into step with Kimiko as they climb the steps together. “Aren’t you?”

“Based on the last bit of footage we got?” MM quips.

Hughie winces, not bothering to try and argue. It had been different, the last few hours at Arcadia. Even if the video quality was for shit in terms of visibility, they’d had plenty of decent audio to parse through. Plenty of really fucking confusing audio, to put it lightly. To put it — well, how else? Thickly? Jesus Christ — in the most objective possible terms, what they had from Butcher’s last time with Homelander…

Shit was incriminating. In a way none of the other fuck tapes to now really have been. These most recent recordings did what candid photos should; captured the thing Kimiko said makes a person who they are, inimitable, beyond the picture perfect exterior. For Homelander, definitely, but also — for Butcher.

“Yeah,” Hughie finally says. “Yeah, I know.”

A burst of cool morning air hits him right in the face. Kimiko walks out ahead of him onto the sidewalk and turns to wait while Hughie idles on the landing. He has the strangest feeling turning in his stomach, this tightly blended and awful maelstrom of dread and anxiety with a healthy amount of doubt sprinkled in. 

That feeling’s why he ended up here; why he’s in a train station with Kimiko at 6 in the morning, and why Frenchie’s been compound-texting him since Hughie moved the files at the asscrack of dawn rolling out of bed sloppy and undressed to jump on the first train, and why Annie’s sleeping soundly in their bed back home, and why MM’s sitting patiently on the phone with him, and why Butcher’s just gone somewhere but pulling at this sick feeling in Hughie’s gut like a goddamn lifeline.

Hughie can’t leave well enough alone, ever. Not when it’s someone he cares about. Not when feeling helpless has always been the impetus for him to get up and fucking do something.

Kimiko steps over to where he is, calm and surefooted. He waits for her to get her phone out and type something, but the trajectory of her hand’s all wrong for that. And then, when she places it gently on his arm, when that’s the way she chooses to communicate to him instead, he feels himself actually beginning to thaw and relax.

She breathes in deeply, letting her shoulders rise with it so he’ll see her doing it and follow her example. He does, and he feels better. The smile on his face is tentative, but it’s real. She nods, and he nods back.

“Okay. Okay… I’m going home. Meeting later?”

“Yeah, I’ll buy you breakfast. Try to get some sleep if you can.”

“Thanks, MM.”

“Mmhmm.”

Hughie pockets his phone, tips his head back, and tries to let all his nerves out in one heavy sigh. It works, a little. Enough to get his feet moving, in any case. Kimiko falls right back into step beside him. They make it a whole block before he has to ask.

“How did you know I was gonna do something stupid?”

She gives him an arch look that’s so loud a laugh startles out of him. Not a goofy, desperate laugh like before, but smaller. Warmer. It’s nice to be seen and understood. To be taken exactly as he is, flaws and fuckups and all. It’s nice to walk shoulder to shoulder with a friend knowing that they’ve got each other’s backs, knowing that even if everything’s gone to hell in a hand basket, they can try to make it right together.

“Okay, fair point. But how’d you know I was gonna be at this train station?”

Again, she gives him a look like, You idiot, and gets out her phone. He watches over her shoulder, thinking she’s going to start typing, but instead she thumbs open snapchat. It’s as she’s tabbing over to the map that he realizes how much of a stupid bastard he is.

“Oh, my God,” he mumbles, horrified at his bitmoji skating over the GPS as they move. “Wait, but when did I… Oh, when I went to tail Neumann. That’s right, I enabled location services just in case we both got murdered and taken apart in a nightmare alley. Wow, that’s really ironic,” he says, shaking his head and switching back to Ghost Mode, with a few exceptions.

He adds back Annie’s and Kimiko’s permissions to view his locations and turns the screen to show her. She smiles, still conveying, You idiot, but softer now.

“Sorry it’s so early.” He drops his phone in his pocket and then his hands go the same way. It’s cold out this morning. Fall’s gotta be right around the corner. “It just made sense at the time since I couldn’t sleep anyway. But if I thought someone was coming to get me I would’ve at least waited for the sun to come up.”

Kimiko shrugs. She makes a quick sign Hughie recognizes as Frenchie’s name.

“What, he was up already?”

Nodding, she adds another sign. Her knuckles pressed to her chin and then fanning her fingers out, almost like a chef’s kiss.

Hughie squints, considering his options. He’s taken back to his very first time encountering higher math operations, trying to figure out whether letters were stand-ins for quantities unknown like a,b, and x, or whether they represented entire theories and concepts like i  and e. Hughie used to count on his fingers during math tests; not because he couldn’t do it in his head, but because it made it that much easier to solidify the abstracts.

He presses his knuckles to his chin and mimics the sign, thinking, mulling it over, picturing Frenchie in a cramped little kitchen, his soft, deliberate footsteps in a quiet space he keeps for just himself and Kimiko and for anyone they welcome in to share it with. Early morning darkness, up before the sun, and a gentle presence in the hand near the face, fanning out.

Like warmth blooming, or like steam. Or waking up. Maybe all three?

“Coffee?” he guesses, brightening. “He was making coffee?”

Kimiko flashes a wide smile, encouraging. Hughie’s never known what all went into Frenchie’s education when it came to speaking with Kimiko as easily as he does. He wonders if the acquisition of every sign went like this with them back to the beginning; if they built a system of communication the same way a home is built, one brick at a time, and why not? Hughie’s learned all of five words from Kimiko, and every one of them has earned some magnitude of this joy in her, unfiltered and unrestrained. 

Someone like Frenchie, actually patient, and focused to boot? Makes sense he’d go and learn an entire language to give that joy a place to live. And to let Kimiko know she never had to be alone if she didn’t want to be. That she could always be found, and understood. 

Not entirely unlike sharing a location on Snapchat. Letting someone in close, letting them learn how to lift meaning out of hand signs or facial expressions or words on a screen. Giving them permission to save your ass from time to time. Out of a cage or a hospital bed or a subway tunnel, or anywhere.

“How do you say, breakfast?”

Kimiko holds her hands to her face like she’s going to take a bite out of a burger, then hooks her knuckles up by her cheeks. Hughie copies her, contextualizing it against what he knows of ASL to better commit it to memory: near the mouth to mean eating, the higher placement to mean time of day. He asks to see if he’s right.

“Food, in the morning?”

She nods, and they work their way through a dozen other signs on the walk back to his apartment. It’s maybe ten minutes, not far at all. Hughie’s cycling back through the signs for sleepy and cranky when Kimiko takes her phone out of her pocket and starts typing.

“Sleepy,” he murmurs, brushing the back of his hand down his cheek on the side opposite from the hand that he’s signing with. “Cranky,” he says, planting his hand in a similar position along his jaw on that side, knuckles curled up like claws. He takes the phone from Kimiko when she hands it to him. “Must be like talking to a toddler,” he jokes, lifting the phone to his face.

But Kimiko stops him with her hand over his wrist, stopping his feet, too. She shakes her head once sternly, and then she signs like she’s catching a bug out the air. Language acquisition, he thinks. One word at a time. 

Brick by brick.

“I’m learning?” he asks.

Kimiko tips her head and sets off walking at a brisk pace. He jogs to catch up to her, beginning to read on the curb just outside his building.

We only get one chance to take him out, Hughie. It has to be permanent. We had a chance to stop Stormfront in that field. Maybe if we had, Becca would be alive now. Who knows who we can save by getting Homelander in time. But only if we do it right. It isn’t enough to react. Do you understand?

The pit of his throat aches like a swallowed scream. It hurts to remember that day. He hardly knew Becca at all, but it’d been impossible not to feel fond of her just on principle. She’d been one of them. One of their own. And the only time it counted for anything, they’d failed her.

“Yeah,” he rasps, giving Kimiko her phone back. “I understand.”

Kimiko watches him, holding her hand over her heart and making a sign not that dissimilar from the one for coffee. He’d thought it meant warmth at first, or waking up.

“That could mean a lot of things,” he says, voice still a little rough owing to how high his heart’s sitting in his body remembering Becca Butcher.

So Kimiko does the sign in reverse, closing her hand into a fist over his heart and then opening it again, deliberately. Demonstrating a point, reading the definition of the word for him out of the dictionary of her mind.

“Don’t… shut down? I don’t know.”

But she nods, and then she’s typing again rapidly.

Feel it. All of it. But don’t let it push you in the wrong direction. And listen to MM. Try to get some sleep.

“Got it,” he says, and conveniently, fights a yawn. “Actually, yeah, that. That sounds like a good idea.”

Kimiko holds up her arms and hugs him, warm and close so that sleepiness uncoils in him like a dream half escaped. Like relief, sliding him right back out onto that precarious ledge overlooking stupidity.

“I’m not worried he’s hurt, you know?” he whispers tightly, superstitiously, half-afraid that saying it out loud will make it realer somehow. “Butcher, I mean.”

Her chin taps his shoulder in a nod. Of course she doesn’t need it explained. It was her observation that Butcher had a habit of looking away too soon. It makes sense she’d be primed to understand why more than any of them. She shrugs, her shoulders bumping his.

“But that’s so fucked up,” he adds, still afraid of even admitting it. Of giving it that power.

She pulls away, stepping out of his bubble, and signs, Breakfast.

Right, he thinks. The meeting. The meeting and breakfast and everyone else and not rushing headlong into a very bad idea without backup. Right.

“Right,” he says. “Yeah. Okay, I’ll see you later, Kimiko. Thanks.”

He turns and walks to the entrance doors at her little salute, and when he turns to see if she’s left, he sees her still just standing there. That’s fair. It’s been an unpredictable, borderline feverish morning for him already; he barely trusts himself to do what he’s said he would do.

But he pushes on and doesn’t let his feet stop until he’s inside the apartment he shares with Annie. It’s quiet and comfortably dark and just a little bit cooler than he likes. Annie runs hotter than he does on a regular day, and she sleeps hotter. Something about the V, he’s pretty sure. Everything felt better when he was on it; sharper, and yet farther away, too. He kicks off his shoes, shrugs out of his jacket, and picks his way carefully and quietly to their room.

Annie’s curled up on her side of the bed, courteous and downright chivalrous even in her sleep. If it was him, he’d be starfished across the center and hogging the blanket.

She makes a sound when he climbs in behind her, halfway to plaintive but lodged firmly in the world of dreaming. Still she manages to twist around in his arms to press her face into his neck, and the chill clinging to him doesn’t wake her either. They pass a few slow breaths, Hughie setting his heartbeat to hers, when the pattern of her breathing changes subtly and she stretches against him.

“Hughie?” she murmurs, burrowing into him closer. “Did you go?”

“Only for a bit,” he tells her, rubbing circles between her shoulder blades. 

“Butcher’s gonna be okay,” she says on a sigh, a continuance of last night’s talk. “Homelander’s in love with him, he’s not gonna do anything to hurt him.”

“Yet.” Hughie presses his chin into her hair. “But that’s not what I’m worried about.”

“What’re you worried about?” Annie asks, already slipping back into a doze.

“I’ll tell you later. Go back to sleep.”

Hughie’s never been the type to be able to do much of anything in the way of maintenance when he’s nervous about something. Eating and sleeping are usually the first casualties to take the hit, but there’s a plan in place now, no matter how tenuous. They’re meeting up for breakfast; they’re going to figure out next steps; it’s going to work out—

“Hughie,” Annie grumbles, squeezing him on that hairsbreadth side of too tight that she always forgets to soften this early in the morning. “I can hear you thinking.”

“Sorry, sorry. I’ll think quieter.”

She snorts, the circle of her arms around him loosening. 

He doesn’t know if he manages to think less like he’s shouting in his head, but he does manage to drift off at some point. It’s only the smell of coffee brewing that wakes him up a few hours later, full sunlight hitting him square in the face as soon as he peels his eyes open. He zombie walks into the kitchen to see Annie still in her oversized t-shirt and his basketball shorts from last night, a steaming cup of coffee held in her two hands.

“Morning, runaway.”

“Hey, I came back,” he protests weakly, somewhat losing the adamant edge he’d intended when a yawn cracks his jaw. “Jesus, what time is it?”

“Little after nine.”

“Oh, shit.” He pours himself a cup and drinks gratefully, eyes slipping shut. “Fuck, I love when you add the cinnamon. Mmm.” He switches his mug to his other hand and signs while he says, “Thanks, for the coffee.”

Annie hums, fighting a smile but not doing much to hold it at bay. That little dimple he loves appears next to her mouth, and she says, “How did she find you anyway?”

“You know, it’s… it’s just one of those, uh, things that we’ll… never know. Like, a mystery. Basically.”

“So you didn’t leave your snapchat location on and make it possible for literally anyone to find you at the press of a button?” Annie asks innocently, sipping her coffee.

“Annie, that would be such an amateur move,” he says, already nodding. “And that… is exactly what happened. Okay,” he adds, laughing when Annie does. “Yeah, that was stupid. I was in such a rush to go and make something happen I guess I left my brain on the pillow when I went.”

“At least you came back for it,” she says, making coffee spurt from his nose. As if she hasn’t noticed, which only makes the whole thing funnier to him in the same way asscheeks guy in the subway was, she adds, “Wanna talk about why?”

Hughie dabs under his nose with a folded up paper towel. “This whole thing was my idea, Annie.”

“Yeah.” She shrugs. “But Butcher said he wanted to be the one to handle it. And as much as I will deny it if you ever repeat this, I think he was right when he said it had to be him. This never would’ve worked with anyone else.”

“But what if it’s working too well?” Hughie asks, the latent panic starting to go in him again. Probably the coffee isn’t helping. “Annie, what if our honeypot became a… like a Venus flytrap instead?”

“Hughie,” Annie soothes, setting her coffee down and taking his out of his hands, too. “I’m gonna say this, and don’t take it the wrong way, okay?”

“Uhh, okay?”

She covers his hands with hers and holds his eyes with her own. Giving him roots to keep him upright and slow him down. In an even voice, she says, “So what if it did?”

He flinches, a little, but he doesn’t answer otherwise. He doesn’t know how.

“I’m not saying that because I don’t care. I’m saying it because you and I both know exactly who Butcher is. Do you think, even if this did become real for him — or at least, realer than it was supposed to be — that he wouldn’t still go through with it? Even if it was gonna break his heart, or maybe even especially if it did.”

Hughie takes his hands out of hers and begins to pace. “Yeah, I know he will. Jesus Christ.”

“What are you trying to get out ahead of, Hughie?”

“I just!” He stops, hands on his head like that’ll help to keep his thoughts in order. “This was supposed to give him the power back. I didn’t want it to hurt him. I thought maybe if I moved forward on the leak, then wherever he is, whatever he’s doing, he’d…. he’d snap out of it and remember this was all a con from the start.”

He catches himself looking at Annie then, and realizes his mistake. Realizes his hypocrisy and his stupidity. Because he never could’ve stopped himself from loving Annie once he started. Not when the truth came out and not when she hated him and not when he’d had to work to earn her trust back. It’s gotta be some kind of cosmic joke, that it had been Butcher then. Telling Hughie to think with his head and not his dick, never getting it that all along Hughie had been thinking with his heart.

Annie catches him by the shoulders, just to look at him, it seems like. Her expression moves in incremental twitches from bewilderment to understanding to concern. She rubs his arms and says, “You can’t protect him from this, Hughie. Not if it’s like you think it is. And he wouldn’t want you to try.”

He ducks his head and wraps his arms around her. “I’m such an asshole, Annie.”

“You can be,” she says, rubbing his back when he laughs weakly. “I think you’re in pretty good company, though.”

“You’re tough, but you’re not an asshole.”

“Thank you.” Leaning away from him, she ruffles her hand in his hair. “Why don’t you have a shower and get dressed? MM wants us all to meet for breakfast.”

“Yeah. Yeah, good idea.”

He goes through the motions. Washes up and arms himself in his cleanest pair of jeans and his favorite shirt. While he’s waiting for Annie so they can leave, he checks his phone. He’s got the promised text from MM with a location uptown and several texts from Frenchie upbraiding him in exasperated French.

Hughie texts him back, My bad, Frenchie.

Yes your bad, Frenchie replies. Kimiko is no babysitter. Lock down your shit, ma petit.

Hughie opens his front-facing camera and snaps a selfie of him signing, Cranky, and sends it to Frenchie with the accompanying text: Somebody woke up on the wrong side of the bed.

He gets an eye roll emoji back in response, and then Annie’s coming out of the bedroom with her purse and her shoes on. Hughie grabs his keys and walks out with her, zipping up his hoodie as they head for the elevator.

“Are you all right?” Annie asks him when the doors slide shut.

“I don’t know.” He lets his shoulders drop from around his ears. “I have a knot in my stomach.”

“It’s not your fault, Hughie.”

“Really? Because it feels like it’s the Temp V all over again.”

“I still can’t believe you feel responsible for how that turned out.” Annie sighs shortly, obviously frustrated but tempering it for his sake. “You’ve always just been trying to take care of him. It’s why you came up with this plan at all, isn’t it? To give him a reason to look at it as something other than a suicide mission?”

Hughie fidgets with his hands, hating just for right now that she knows him so well.

“I know you want him to be okay. I do, too.” The doors slide open, and Annie walks out, pulling Hughie along by the hand. “But he’s the only one who can get himself back home this time, and we have to trust him to want to do it.”

“You’re right. I know you’re right.”

“Yeah?” Annie favors him with a smile, and then a kiss. “Then trust me. Trust us. We’re in this together.”

He nods and walks with her the three blocks to the restaurant MM picked out. It’s a hole in the wall, so close in essence to a speakeasy that he’s dimly surprised nobody asks them for a password. The stoop-shouldered woman at the front just asks who they’re looking for and shows them behind a curtain where MM, Frenchie, and Kimiko are holding down the only table with not piled high with overturned chairs.

MM springs to his feet and catches the woman’s elbow as they’re coming down the few steps leading into the otherwise closed dining area. Hughie gets Annie’s chair for her and then seats himself.

“Thank you, Ma’am,” MM intones, gamely letting himself be patted and leaned on.

“Oh, ma’am. Don’t you start. Your friends gonna want coffee, Marvin?”

“Yes, please, Nona.”

“I’ll send Kitty down with menus.”

“I really appreciate that, and it’s generous of you to let us set up here.”

“So polite,” she tuts, letting him escort her back up the stairs. Each one of her steps is cautious and gingerly taken. “You tell your dear mother to come by when she has a night free. I run a poker game Wednesdays around back. Clean. Stupid men with too much money in their pockets.”

Hughie covers his snort with his hand, but Frenchie and Annie aren’t so quick to catch theirs.

“Oh, yes,” Nona adds, turning at the top of the stairs to wag a cautionary finger at them. “Such men are useless otherwise.” Just for MM, she says again, “You tell Lucy. She’s welcome here anytime she likes. We will have a wonderful time. Stupid men, handsome men.”

“I’ll tell her you were askin’ about her, Nona. Thank you.”

“Such a good boy,” she remarks, almost despairingly, and pats his cheek before drawing the curtain closed behind him.

“She’s fun,” Hughie says. “Sure she can’t join us?”

MM points at his face and pulls up a chair right next to him. “Don’t go making jokes like you’re not in trouble.”

“I’m in trouble? What’d I do?”

Everyone at the table has a reaction to that, and Hughie throws his hands.

“I came back!”

“Barely, and I heard you thinkin’ against it.” MM catches Hughie’s shoulder in a big gentle hand. “That was soft, Hughie. I don’t hardly expect that kinda shit from you anymore. What would Butcher say?”

“Call him a cunt and put him in a sensuous headlock?” Frenchie proposes nonchalantly from across the table.

“I’m not putting him in a headlock,” MM says flatly.

“Want me to do it?” Annie asks, raising her hand.

“Annie!” Hughie whines. “Okay — guys, I know I screwed up. Or, I nearly screwed up, whatever, that’s all semantics. I was wrong. I shouldn’t have taken off like that, and I’m sorry, okay? But you can’t act like you don’t know why I did it. Butcher’s been gone with zero contact for three, coming up on four days now. Aren’t any of you the least bit concerned?”

“I am concerned, at a disturbing and upsetting frequency of late, with the condition of Butcher’s dick as it pertains to the rest of his body,” Frenchie laments, burying this answer in the lip of a fragile white teacup. “But if all of him is still in one piece—” He jolts at Kimiko’s elbow in his side. “—as I posit that it must be, given our mutual acquaintance feels an obvious affection for him and the functionality of his genitals, it is my belief that he is quite all right, wherever he is.”

Kimiko catches Frenchie’s eye and tells him something so like that reversed hand-sign she showed Hughie that he really feels like he should be able to guess at its meaning. She tips her head in Hughie’s direction and signs another series of words, a whole phrase or sentence, that he can’t track.

“She says, Butcher will find a way to use what has happened, whether he has become compromised or no. Frankly, I agree.”

“And what if he doesn’t?” 

Hughie stiffens, stopping himself from saying more when a girl in her teens steps around the curtain to bring out several menus stacked together. She passes them around, holds out a pen and notepad to take any further drink orders, and skips back out again, smacking her gum the whole way.

“You were saying, Hughie?” MM asks, already reaching for a menu and scanning through it.

“What if he comes back and he’s changed his mind? What if he wants to call it all off?”

“Impossible,” Frenchie says. “Oh, beignets! Are they any good?”

“Everything here’s good,” MM says, raising his eyebrows at Frenchie over the top of his menu.

“Ah bon? We shall see.”

“You guys are being really dismissive about this,” Hughie points out, momentarily distracted when Annie points out the bananas foster. “Ooh… hey.”

“What do you wanna do, Hughie?” MM asks him, in his levelest, work with me voice. “What’re you chasing?”

“Leverage.”

“We have hours of footage,” Annie reminds him. “Most of it’s usable.” 

“No, I’m talking real leverage,” he insists, catching Kimiko’s straight stare. He leans in and says, “This is bigger than us, right?” At her controlled nod, he adds, “We can’t afford to have come this far and then have nothing to show for it. What if he deletes the files? What if he tells Homelander what we’ve been doing?”

“Do you really think—”

“That he’d gamble away our only bargaining chip to double the payout?” Hughie says, stopping MM in his tracks. “Especially if he thought he was doing it to prove a point?”

The shocked, tense silence at the table spurs him on.

“He’s in deep shit. This is exactly the kinda thing he’d decide to do all on his own just to take back control of the situation.”

“So you tried to take it back first,” MM hazards a guess. “That what your little pilgrimage this morning was about?”

“I don’t know, dude.” He drops his hands, finding them no help in getting his point across. “Before, he could come back to us after. He could reset and get his head right. Now he’s just in the wind, and we don’t know what he’s thinking. Or feeling. Or what he’s gonna do about any of it.”

“Petit Hughie,” Frenchie says, in an almost academic tone. “Are you suggesting — that is to say, do you suspect that our Monsieur Charcutier has…” He holds up his hand like he’s pinched a quantity of salt between his fingers. “Comment dites-vous?”

“Caught feelings,” MM fills in.

Frenchie looks vaguely peaky at that and says, “Ah…”

“I think he’s saying it might be a little bit worse than that,” Annie warns.
