“Mais non.” He rattles of a few choice expletives in French, visibly reigns himself in, and tries again, smiling with obvious strain. “Do you mean to tell me you believe our friend has… incomprehensibly, and against all sense, fallen in love with our greatest enemy?”

Shrugging, Hughie says, “I think it’s a distinct possibility.”

“Connerie,” Frenchie spits. “This is absurd and not to be borne.”

Kimiko catches his gesticulating hand in hers, anchoring him. She gives him a look, so tender and open that it seems completely at odds with the discussion at hand, but whatever she intends by it, Frenchie seems to understand. He deflates at once, all the air gone out of him and knocked clean out of his chest.

“Fuck,” he whispers. “Well, fine. And in that case, what can we do? Send them a hundred roses stained in the blood of a hundred doves to celebrate their cursed union?”

Kitty comes through the curtained alcove with a drink tray. She passes them out, and with uncontrived adolescent boredom, she says, “Why don’t you guys just go to his boss with whatever you got?”

Freezing, and seeming to share a brain cell, they all look up at her stupidly.

She shrugs, saying, “The Seven’re just professional athletes-turned-movie stars. Just because they have superpowers don’t mean they sign their own checks. Not even the Incredible Fascist.” Again she whips out her pen and notepad, the disinterest in her face and voice never once wavering. “You ready to order?”

There’s a shocked moment of wordless horror before MM clears his throat, picks up his menu, and says, crisply, “Could I have the coush-coush, please, Kitty?”

“Make it with bacon grease,” she tells him.

“Ain’t no other way.”

She nods companionably, warming finally, if only the slightest bit. She goes around the table taking orders, and as soon as that’s done, she goes the same way she came, popping her gum. Not a care in the world.

After a heavy, telling silence, Kimiko holds out her hands in a gesture he doesn’t think is a sign of hers but more of a universal signal. Translating as, roughly, Is she wrong?

Frenchie looks dubious, Annie thoughtful, and MM finally beginning to appear worried.

“I say we do it,” Hughie announces to the table at large. “Fuck it.”

“Do what?” Frenchie asks, appalled. “Hand-deliver our crude and ill-gotten materials right into the viper’s nest?”

“Yeah. I’ll do it. You know I was going to anyway.”

MM shakes his head, but he doesn’t argue. Annie folds her arms in front of her, still just thinking in that loud way she often teases Hughie about. Kimiko signs across the table. Gesturing between him and herself as if a line connects them. Hughie’s no interpreter just yet, but he can intuit her meaning from that one based on context.

“They’ll think something’s up if I don’t go alone.”

“Bullshit, Hughie,” Annie says.

“I second that,” MM piles on. “You’re not going in there by yourself.”

“Okay, then I won’t. I won’t, I’ll have… an escort.”

Everybody asks him in one voice, “Who?”

“Um. Well…”

 

/

 

“Remind me why I’m doing this?”

Hughie rides up the glass and steel elevator with Insight’s hand nestled in the crook of his elbow. He doesn’t strictly need it, but Hughie can guess how disoriented he must be having lost his eyes so recently. Wouldn’t know it to take one look at the guy, as well as he wears it. Only the slightest bit of scarring shows from behind the dark lenses of his glasses, and there’s a noticeable spring in his step. It’s there, somehow, even when he’s standing still.

“Because you’ve got more reason than most right now to want to see this asshole answer for the things he’s done. Am I wrong about that?”

“You are not,” Insight answers around a smile that could cut glass. “And I admit, I’m intrigued.”

“But?”

Insight hums noncommittally and strides neatly out of the elevator as soon as the doors slide open. He swings Hughie around when he starts toward the wrong direction. “You can just say you need me to win Miss Barrett over. We both know she’d toss you out on your ass otherwise. If not worse.”

“Worse is what happened to you,” Hughie pops back, and then feels like a jerk. “I meant—”

“That’s all right,” Insight answers idly. And then in a more muted undertone, “Did you get it on camera, what he did to my face?”

“Only the audio.”

“‘Urgent, monitor me, maybe category red,’” he recites blandly, and then heaves a sigh. “I might’ve known. Guess I was distracted.”

Fumbling now, and feeling guilty, Hughie tells him, “If there’s something else you want out of this—”

“What’s he like?”

“…Wh- Huh?”

“Billy Butcher,” Insight says, shrugging insouciantly. “Likes, dislikes — does that daddy shit really do anything for him? You know, what’s he into?”

“Oh, my God,” Hughie mutters, “ugh, I’m in hell.” His phone rings in his pocket, and he takes it out, seeing his dad’s name on the screen. Distantly, he notices Insight letting go of his arm. “Ugh, I’m in hell,” he groans, answering the call on the fourth ring because he’s a masochist apparently. “Dad, hey, can I call you back? I’m a little bit—”

“Really, really quick — I’m sorry, I just — I can’t remember what you said. Is it two scoops for the little guy, or—” There’s a bark in the background. “—for the Big Little Guy, or just one?”

“Uh, it’s — I didn’t write it down?”

“I read over it twice, you say how much of the can but not how much of the kibble. It’s an important distinction, Hugh. We’re struggling here.”

He winces, remembering, as if there’s a gun to his head, Butcher’s stupid comments and the totally offensive look he’d been giving his dad that day at the office. 

Stopped just in front of him with his head tilted in Hughie’s direction, Insight catches a sputtered laugh in the cup of his hand. Hughie feels himself blushing to the tips of his ears. Great. That’s great.

“Just… try one for now and see if he wants more after.”

“Well, of course he’s gonna want more, he’s a good healthy boy with a big appetite!” Dad says, veering off dangerously close to baby talk.

Having seen the inexplicable reality of Terror shutting his eyes halfway when Dad scratches his ears and babbles nonsensical encouragements to him, Hughie can’t find it in himself to be impatient like he normally might. Even standing here creating a rift in foot traffic, forcing people to break around him like a stone dropped in a river, he just feels fond.

“Dad,” he laughs, and schools himself back to neutrality. It only halfway works. “Dad, go for the one scoop. If he wants more, I’m sure he’ll let you know.”

“Yes, sir, we can do that, can’t we, Terror? Good boy. Hey, Hugh?”

“Yeah?”

“Billy’s all right, isn’t he? I know you… you boys gets up to a lot. I hate to think I’d never even know it if things started to get bad, like before. Would you maybe… well, now, I-I know you can’t tell me everything, and I’m not asking for that, but… you aren’t in some kinda trouble again, are you, Son?”

Hughie sighs. He’s not about to tell his dad they’re handing over nonconsensual sex tapes to a multimillion dollar company with the intention of collaring the world’s most volatile, brattiest supe. Just like he can’t tell him they’re doing it at all because of Butcher. 

Because he might be in love with the most dangerous man in the whole fucking world. 

Insight tips his head back and lets out a low, forlorn whistle. He turns so Hughie can see himself reflected in his shades and taps his watch.

“No one’s in trouble, Dad. Butcher’s — we’re working on it. But everything’s okay, I promise.”

“Well, as long as you’re sure…”

“I am. I gotta go, okay? But I’ll call you as soon as I finish up a few things.”

“All right. I love you, Hughie.”

“Love you, too.” He pockets his phone and starts walking. “Don’t even.”

“I didn’t say a word.” And he doesn’t until they arrive at an information desk where he struts up ahead of Hughie to lean his elbow on the counter. “Miss Yvette,” he purrs, to the girl who’d already been gawking at him from further off. “Would you be a dear and see if Miss Barrett has the time to see me and my friend here?”

She starts and stammers, dropping the phone when she misdials it a few times. When she does get the right sequence and manages to ring through to the correct extension, she says, “A-Ashley, um, there’s, you have, i-it’s…”

Insight reaches mildly over the desk and gestures for the phone. “It’s all right, darling, give it here. Thank you kindly. Miss Barrett, that you?” 

There’s a pause, and then a smile breaks across his face. Handsome guy. Fuck.

“Sure, clean bill of health for me. Doctor said so.” Another pause. “You know. Eyes’ll never be what they were, but I’ve seen sunsets since the surgery. It’s not so bad. Hmm? Yeah, do you know a Hughie Campbell? That’s the one. I appreciate that, Miss Barrett. Thank you very much, Ma’am. You can have that back, Miss Yvette.” He turns to Hughie. “We’re in.”

“That was so much easier than I thought it was gonna be,” Hughie says faintly. He lets Insight guide him away from the information desk. “You’ve really seen sunsets? Can you see me?”

Insight tilts his head, then turns to Yvette who’s still watching them with unveiled interest. “Sure,” he says, like he’s reading from a grocery list. “That’s a good color on you, kid. The green really makes your eyes pop.”

“Whoa.” He smiles on reflex. “Fuck, that’s cool.”

“Look alive. You’re about to get reamed.”

“What?”

“You,” a commanding voice thunders from down the hall. “In my office, you son of a bitch. Insight,” Ashley Barrett says, immediately softening so that her voice is no louder or sharper than the pristine clacking of her heels on the marble floors. “I thought we put you on mandatory medical leave. What are you doing here? Are you all right?” 

She turns on Hughie as soon as she’s within striking distance, as if he forced Insight here at gunpoint rather than just showing up at his address with a smile and a dream. Hughie’s surprised he doesn’t get decked or spontaneously burst into flames under the heat of her glare.

Insight catches his arm before he can flee. “Miss Barrett,” he soothes. “This one here’s my guest. Let’s be civil with him, can we?”

“Fine. But you’re on thin fucking ice, Campbell.”

He can just hear the boys giggling their asses off from the unmarked van two blocks away. They’ve got comms closed from their end, barring extenuating circumstances, but they can hear everything he hears, and he’s sure they’re loving it. Ashley Barrett talks shit to Hughie for ten full minutes before she runs out of steam. Through it all, he gleans two important pieces of information: that she doesn’t know where Homelander’s fucked off to, and that she hopes he stays gone, him and Butcher both.

“Could I say something?” Hughie tries, when she stops to aggressively down an entire stainless steel water bottle while maintaining ferocious eye contact with him. “With the proviso that you don’t bludgeon me to death with your water bottle.”

She glares daggers at him and keeps on chugging.

“Hear him out, Miss Barrett. I think he’ll surprise you.”

Her water bottle chimes like a gong when it hits her desk. She drops heavily into her chair and steeples her hands, taking several deep, measured breaths before trying to speak. When she does, her voice comes out even, just the tiniest warble to it.

“I’m listening.”

Like he’s introducing a precious antique at auction, he says, “I have illicit videos of Homelander getting fucked, hard, by Billy Butcher. Gagging for and on his dick, bouncing on his dick, begging to take it deeper. I have video of him eating ass, getting eaten out, swallowing dick, fingers, tongue; you name it. It’s like a porn factory, and it’s all right here.”

He holds up his phone.

Ashley’s gone deathly white. He can see a vein twitching in her temple. The tension ebbs and bleeds out of her like air from a balloon, and she drops her head in her hands.

“Oh, I fucking knew this was going to happen,” she moans. “What the fuck do you want?”

“To give it to you.” Hughie shrugs. “I don’t know where they are either, but when they come back, if they come back, we want you to have the kill switch. Who could start a media frenzy out of thin air better than you?” He airdrops it to her iPhone, everything they have, and closes out of the master archive. “You can ruin him with this, Ashley. Strongest man in the world’s a nobody overnight if his profit margin tanks. Like it will, when his fanbase gets a taste of what we have. It’s your ace in the hole. Your airplane video.”

Ashley stares at him, obviously still considering murdering him with her water bottle, but then she sits up in her chair and opens a file at random. Courtesy of the timestamp curated at Frenchie’s discretion, it auto-starts in the middle of a woman’s horrified scream — Ashley’s scream. She glances at Insight, blue eyes round and glassy and hurt. He’s gone very still next to Hughie, listening to his own weak groans of agony.

There’s a whir of movement, a loud, heavy thump, and at last, a pained grunt out of Butcher.

And then, Homelander: “Devouring you all the time with his eyes like I couldn’t see. Blushing like a girl imagining you like this, with him. With anyone.”

Sound of a zipper and muffled breathing. Ashley stops the playback.

“Jesus,” she mutters, staring emptily at her phone.

She’ll do it, Insight murmurs, loud enough to be overheard by Ashley, but Hughie looks at her and doesn’t see her register that anything’s been said. On one condition, he adds, except this time, Hughie’s looking right at him, and his lips don’t move. “Soldier Boy.”

This time Ashley does look up, and Hughie does, too.

“What the fuck, were you talking in my head just now?”

Insight waves his hand, dismissive. That silky, borderline oily thing that would remind Hughie of Butcher if he was more of a scumbag about it takes a backseat. He’s all business when he says, “I like this plan. It intrigues me, but you aren’t taking it far enough. Why do you think that is, Mr. Campbell?”

“What are you talking about?”

“Miss Barrett,” Insight croons, favoring her with a smile that’s warm but barbed. “What did I say to you, just there,” pointing at a space on the floor just behind where they’re seated across from her, “when the vitreous fluid in my eyes was still boiling and spilling out of my head?”

She bites her lip, swallows hard enough Hughie can hear it. Her answer comes in a thready whisper. “You said you couldn’t see it. That it was hiding.”

“Him,” he says smoothly, calmly, mellowing out the edges in his smile. “It was him. It was Soldier Boy.”

“He’s alive? What the fuck!?”

“He’s in a medically induced coma,” Hughie clarifies. “He’s not part of this.”

“Bullshit, he’s not!” Ashley bursts out of her chair and paces. “No, you are out of your mind if you think I would release this while he’s at full power. You don’t know what he’s been like. I don’t care if you have him on tape getting fucked in a Sailor Moon outfit, you are the one who’s fucked as long as no one can stop him. And this? This… library of smut, is only going to piss him off.”

Insight parks his elbow on the arm of his chair and then his chin in his hand, face turned coyly in Hughie’s direction.

Fucking asshole, he shouts in his head.

Why, yes, Mr. Campbell. You didn’t think you’d cornered the market, did you?

He jerks to his feet, too. If Ashley can pace, he can, too. Fuck it.

“We already tried to turn him against Homelander,” Hughie says to the two of them. “It didn’t work, and we were all nearly fucking mincemeat at the end of it. Maeve died.”

Insight hums, sussing out the lie, though he keeps his mouth firmly shut at the icy look Ashley nails him with. “Right,” he says, docile as a lamb. “Very beautiful, very tragic.”

“Fucking mind readers,” Ashley mutters, crossing her arms in front of her chest and stopping at an angle from Hughie so that she doesn’t have to look right at either of them. “You took his powers from him once. Could you do it again?”

“I…”

Static wails in his ear, and he recoils, wincing.

“Yes,” Frenchie gushes into his earpiece. “Tell her we will.”

“And how,” Insight amends, standing, too.

“Did you get that, Frenchie?”

“Putain. Did I or did I not say to you this would be a disaster? You tempt fate just like him, you know. Monsieur Charcutier.”

“Thanks.” Hughie presses a button his phone screen. “You’re on speaker.”

“Oui. Allo. The compound, I have reverse engineered it. All we need is for our troublemakers to come home. Does this satisfy?”

“How do you know it works?” Ashley asks, suspicious.

“We have tested it,” Frenchie assures her. “A superhero factory you may well be, but there are more in the world than can be bought and sold under the Vought emblem.”

Hughie remembers Kimiko hugging him outside his apartment. He wonders if she’d been on it then. Had that been why she used Snapchat to track him down? He tries to remember what hugging her had felt like, and if he’d even know the difference between her pulling her strength and her just going for it with the help of the anti-V.

“Fine. Fine,” Ashley says, huffing a sigh. “If you can de-power him, we’ll handle the leak. But let me be perfectly clear about one thing: this will never circle back to me. We never had this conversation. My name will not appear anywhere in the press.”

“Just like a C.E.O.,” Frenchie murmurs, to Hughie’s fumbling to get him off speaker.

“Great, so that settles it. I’ll just be going.”

Wait.

And Hughie, still unused to hearing somebody else’s voice in his head, stops dead in his tracks. More out of surprise than anything else. He glances over his shoulder at Insight, but he’s not watching Hughie.

He’s looking at Ashley, and by the looks of it, having a separate private conversation with her.

“What’s going on, Hughie?” MM says in his ear. “Are you clear?”

“Not yet,” he mumbles. “Something’s happening.”

The staring contest lasts all of ten seconds, but it feels like a gunfight. As still as they are watching each other for any sudden moves, Hughie’s braced for a hail of bullets any minute, except that’s not what happens. Instead, Ashley lets loose a heavy sigh and pulls a keyboard out from under her desk.

Hughie sags a little. He’s so on edge he thought it was gonna be a fucking shotgun.

Sidling up next to him, Insight snorts.

He says, “Less a shotgun and more… a warhead, Miss Barrett? What do you think?”

“I think the next time I put you on medical leave, you should fucking stay on medical leave,” she says, voice tight with irritation. “There. Are you happy?”

Hughie’s phone pings, but when he checks it, all he sees is a new file in the master archive they created just for this meeting. “What’s this?”

“Something Stilwell buried,” she mutters. “Now get the fuck out of my office. Both of you.”

“Ma’am,” Insight croons.

“Suck my dick, Forester.”

He just grins and slots his hand into the crook of Hughie’s elbow. “Take us away, kid.”

Hughie’s legs are wobbly the whole trek back to the elevator. He feels like he might puke, but he also feels like his face might get stuck from how hard he’s grinning. “I can’t believe that worked,” he mumbles. “Dude.”

“Call the elevator, Mr. Campbell.”

He presses the button, starting to laugh a little bit. Delirious. “Dude, that? Back there? That’s what Butcher’s into.”

“Really?”

“Yes, really. Fuckin’ yeah, really. Goddamn! That was amazing! I mean, fuck you, a little bit for blowing our cover, but the rest of it? Whatever you did at the end there to get her to play ball? Fuck yeah.”

“You might hold your judgement until you see what it is Miss Barrett sent you.”

“Why? What was it?”

Insight sighs and nudges him into the elevator when the doors open.

“It’s Becca, Hughie,” MM says, in such a deadened, quietly angry voice that Hughie doesn’t need the clarification. But MM gives it: “It’s Becca, on the other side of that locked door. In the conference room where it happened.”

Hughie feels his heart leap into his throat. There's noise like blood rushing in his ears, making everything outside of that sensation seem strange and out of focus.

“Sorry for spilling the beans on your secret weapon, as it were, but you’d’ve lost her otherwise. Ashley Barrett’s a hard case. She had no intention of giving you anything for your trouble today, but hell, from where I was standing, it seemed only fair.”

The doors slide open just as Hughie’s realizing they’ve gone up instead of down.

“This is your stop, Mr. Campbell.”

“Huh… wha—” He catches the door before it can close. “What floor is this?”

“Homelander’s, incidentally. He’s not here, but I think you’ll like to check his room all the same. Something there you might find real interesting.”

“What about you?”

Insight feels around for the buttons, and realizing he can’t see them without Hughie to look through, Hughie looks at the numbers instead of at him. With the tiniest bit of tension around the corner of his mouth, Insight thumbs the button for the ground floor.

“I’ll make my own way back. You’ve got business up here, even if you don’t quite know it yet.”

Hughie jumps out of the elevator to miss the closing doors, but then, reconsidering, catches the door again. “I’m sorry about what happened to you, man.”

“I’m not,” Insight intones, flashing a smile like razor wire. He slides his sunglasses off his face, revealing the worst of his scars where crows’ feet would naturally sit. Where his eyes should be, there are just two caverns revealing hollow eye sockets. “If it’s the price of admission to watch the bastard fry, I’ll take it.”

The doors begin inching shut again, and Hughie, standing there like he is with his mouth open, inadvertently stops them from closing one more time.

“Still think I’m a handsome man, Mr. Campbell?” He hums a laugh, sliding his glasses back on his nose. Miracle he still has it, but then, Homelander’s lasers are eye-sized. “I didn’t think so. It’s down the hall that way, what you’re looking for.”

Hughie steps out of the path of the sliding doors, and this time they slink shut all the way. He watches the numbers tick down and down and down.

“Do we think this is a trap?” Frenchie asks.

“Hard to say. Seems like he’s on our side for now, at least.”

Sighing, Hughie says, “If I die, delete my internet history,” and makes his way down the hall as instructed.

There are two doors to choose from, but only one emanates sound from the other side. Hughie braces himself and knocks, not knowing at all what to expect. The faint TV din cuts out, dropping everything into a severe, lonely silence.

And then the door swings open, and after a stalled moment of confusion, Hughie smiles.

“Ryan?”

“Hi, Hughie.”

“Hi! What, uh, what’re you doing here?”

“Well…” He leans on the doorknob while he thinks. “I was at our townhouse waiting for Dad to come home, but then yesterday he called Ashley and told her to bring me here.”

Hughie blinks, carefully aware of every muscle in his face and how he can’t let himself look like any part of this news makes him as furious as it does. He's read somewhere — probably at that adoption agency come to think of it — that kids sometimes take their cues from adults about how to react to uncomfortable or scary situations. So Hughie nods, trying to emanate concern and care and not homicidal rage on a child’s behalf.

There’s no need to upset him if he’s not already upset. He repeats that in his head so that it becomes a mantra.

“Hughie? Huuuughie, earth to Hughie?”

“Yeah, mmhmm?”

He’s making a face at Hughie like he just grew a second head, but he’s closer to smiling than he is to frowning, so Hughie will charitably call that a win. For now.

“I said, what are you doing here? Were you looking for my dad?”

“Uh, sort of? I was… trying to find Butcher… because they, um.”

“Like each other?” Ryan says, innocently factual and without any concept of how gracious and sweet he is.

“Right, because they like each other.”

“They’re not here. They’re in Santa-rini.”

Hughie blinks again. “Is that what your dad told you?”

“He just said they're on vacation,” Ryan says, turning to walk into the room and leaving the door wide open behind him. “Look, I’ll show you.”

Tentative, Hughie follows him in and shuts the door behind him. The room doesn’t look like it was blown apart by two guys violently, and gleefully, taking each other apart anymore, but Hughie recognizes it from the footage Butcher got. It’s strange to see it looking brand new, almost hollowed out from the renovations.

When Hughie catches up to Ryan, he finds him digging through a backpack to produce a smart phone. He opens Twitter and passes the device off to Hughie for inspection. It’s a series of photos condensed into a single post.

He stares at it for a long time without thinking much of anything.

And then he reads, “Hobolander?”

“Open the last one,” Ryan tells him, pointing. “Look on the very left.”

Hughie opens the picture. It’s Homelander in a floppy t-shirt with “I HEART SANTORINI” except the heart is in the shape of Greece. He’s waving with a pasted-on smile for the camera, and in the far left of the photo, nearly cropped out but caught just enough for his hair and part of his face to have been preserved by the camera flash, is Butcher.

“Greece,” Hughie says on a sigh. “They’re in Greece.”

“They went on a fucking honeymoon?” MM hisses.

“Language, you brute,” Frenchie hisses back, just as the comms abruptly cut out.

“Was that Frenchie and MM?” Ryan asks, taking his phone back when Hughie hands it to him. He drops it in his backpack, rather than pocketing it. “Do you guys still hang out with Annie and Kimiko?”

“Yeah. Frenchie and Kimiko live together. So do Annie and me.”

“That’s nice. I wish my dad lived at home.”

It takes a herculean feat of restraint not to let any of what that makes him feel migrate onto his face. He’s pretty sure he gives himself a fucking hernia.

At least Ryan seems okay, but Hughie knows this situation is fucked, and he can’t leave it like this. He won’t.

A stark reminder of the woman he failed is burning a hole in his pocket even now. He’s not gonna add insult to injury and fail her son on top of everything else.

He makes his voice as gentle and sincere as it gets and says, “I’m sorry about that. But hey, what do you think about, uh, coming out to lunch with me? We can see if everyone else wants to meet up later, too.”

“Burgers?” Ryan asks hopefully.

“Sure, I like burgers.”

“Okay.” Smiling, Ryan pulls his backpack on over his shoulder and stands at attention, already smartly dressed and in his shoes. “Let’s go.”

Hughie smiles back. He just smiles back, because that’s all Ryan needs to see him feeling right now, and together they head out to the hallway and back to the elevator. Hughie wonders distantly if Insight made it out okay, if he’s still in the building or if he caught a cab home by now. It’s also nagging at him, that Insight knew to point Hughie in Ryan’s direction.

Was Ryan thinking about how lonely he was feeling and Insight picked it up from the elevator, or was Ashley thinking about what to do with Homelander’s kid while he’s fucking off in Greece? There’s no way to know.

“Have you talked to Aunt Grace in a while?” Ryan asks when they’re riding down the elevator and it’s only the mild tinkling music to keep them company. “Dad says I don’t have to call her that, but I still like to. Do you know if she thinks about me?”

“I’m sure she does,” Hughie tells him, fighting the urge to take a knee and come off like a patronizing adult. “Everybody’s crazy about you.”

“But have you talked to her?”

“I haven’t, but Butcher has.”

“And she’s okay?”

“Yeah, Ryan, she’s doing all right.”

“Okay, good.” He nods and steps off the elevator as soon as the doors open. When Hughie catches up to him and they’re walking shoulder-to-shoulder, he adds, “I can’t ask Dad about her, or about Mom. He doesn’t try to be mean, but I can tell he didn’t like them.”

Hughie stops walking, his eyes stinging, but by the grace of God or some other higher, unspeakably benevolent power, they don’t water. He makes himself breathe.

“Hughie? Do you not really want burgers?”

“No, I…” He clears his throat and unsticks his feet. “I do! I’m starving. I’m so ready for lunch.”

Ryan studies him for a second, and Hughie doesn’t know. He doesn’t remember Becca well enough to know if they’re her eyes or Homelander’s, but they’re clear and all-seeing in that way kids all seem to have in common, whether they’re human or supe.

Whatever he thinks of Hughie’s halting responses, his face softens almost imperceptibly, and he says, “Sometimes, talking about Mom makes me sad, too. But I still like to anyway. Is that weird?”

“It’s not. That means — it means you love her and you wanna keep her with you. That’s not a bad thing.”

Accepting that as the truth, Ryan turns and starts walking again. Hughie follows him, helpless to do anything else. It’s smooth sailing once they’re outside, right until they hit their first intersection. Crisis rages in Hughie — is Ryan too old to hold his hand? But does it matter if he is? They’re surrounded by crowds and rushing cars and noise on all sides. It’s the right thing to do, to hold his hand, isn’t it?

He’s still in the process of indecisively unfurling his fingers when Ryan reaches up and clasps their hands together. The light changes, and on they walk. They reach the opposite sidewalk, Hughie’s hand held loose enough that a kid without super strength could pull away if he wanted, but Ryan doesn’t. He just keeps holding Hughie’s hand.

“So, uh, do you know when your dad’s coming back?”

Ryan shakes his head. “I don’t think they even know. I guess they’re having fun being together. Like they do in Arcadia.”

Hughie chokes on his own spit. Ryan stops walking without letting go of his hand.

“You’re probably right,” he finally manages to say.

“I think it’s nice. Maybe Butcher will come to live with us, and then Dad would be home more. Don’t tell him — Butcher, I mean, but I guess don’t tell Dad either — it’s just… I always sort of wished I could stay with him, before. With Butcher. But I think… we didn’t know each other very well back then, and he was still really sad about Mom. I think he’s still really sad about Mom.”

“Yeah, he is,” Hughie says, softly. “That’s not your fault, though, Ryan. You know that, right? You know none of this is your fault?”

“I know. Butcher said.”

“Wh… said what?”

“That he wasn’t really mad at me. He just… you know. He thought he wanted to be dead. I don’t think he does anymore, but I don’t know. Sometimes he lies because telling the truth hurts too much, or makes things dangerous. I don’t really understand why he’s like that. Do you?”

Hughie tries to remember how to breathe, much less speak.

“Your dad — I mean.” He shakes his head. “Butcher, I mean, he… he doesn’t always want to be understood.”

Ryan nods, more sagely than a kid should be able to, and says, “I used to think he was like that just to get things, except, then he was sad when it worked. Is it always so confusing being a grownup?”

“Sometimes.” 

Hughie tries to catch Ryan’s eye, but Ryan’s more focused on where they’re going. Apparently he’s got a favorite place in mind. Good thing, too, because Hughie hasn’t been leading them anywhere specific. Ryan tugs at his hand and leads them over to another intersection where they’re forced to stop for traffic.

“People like Butcher and your dad,” Hughie begins carefully, watching the red Don’t Walk sign flash and flash and flash in time with the accompanying ticking sound. “It’s hard for them to have regular relationships. That doesn’t mean you have to be the same way.”

“It does when it’s them,” Ryan counters, not like he’s challenging Hughie, but like he’s trying to understand. “How do you do it?”

“Uh.” 

Hughie steps off the curb when the light changes, and Ryan yanks him back up onto the sidewalk out of the way of a zooming cab. 

The people next to them on the sidewalk shout after the driver, but Hughie’s too dazed to join in.

“Thanks,” he says faintly, flexing his wrist subtly to feel out the definite sore spot where the joint torqued awkwardly. It’s extreme for discomfort but shy enough of pain that he can ignore it for now. “Nice lookin’ out.”

“We’re nearly there. Sorry, I should’ve asked if you wanted to fly.”

Barking a laugh, Hughie says, “God, please don’t apologize for that.”

Ryan steps off the curb and tows Hughie along. “So?”

“How do I keep being friends with your dad? I guess by being there. By looking out for him, like you just did for me.”

“Do you ever fight?” Ryan asks, steering him into a cramped little burger joint that has Hughie’s mouth watering as soon as they walk in the door. “Does he get on your nerves? I bet he does.”

“You have no idea.”

Smiling a little more easily now, like he’s actually having fun now, Ryan nods at the menu over the counter. “What do you want?”

“Why don’t you go crazy and get whatever you're in the mood for? It’s my treat.”

“That’s okay.” Ryan swings his backpack around to his front and goes rooting around in the front pouch for a little card case with cartoon superheroes on it. He edges out a shiny black credit card and shows it to Hughie like it’s a very fragile, mostly uninteresting dead bug. “Ashley gave me this to use.”

“Right. Uh—”

Before he can talk Ryan out of it, he dashes over to the register and starts their order. Hughie gets his phone out and fires off a quick text to Annie, saying, Remember that time I almost adopted a kid? How do you feel about a trial run?

While she’s typing, he catches sight of smudged bruises creeping in around his wrist.

He pulls his sleeve down to hide it. Ryan comes running over with a little plastic number card pinned to a metal stand. His little grin is so contagious, Hughie smiles back automatically.

“Where do you wanna sit?”

“How about by these open windows?”

“Cool. Do you like milkshakes? I got you a milkshake.”

“Are you in my head?” Hughie tells him, serious as a heart attack. “I love milkshakes.”

For days now, Hughie’s had a sick feeling stuck to his ribs over this mess with Butcher and Homelander. Sitting across from Ryan waiting for their food to come out, he doesn’t have that nauseous uneasiness clinging to him. He just feels comforted, and glad for the chance to give some of that comfort back to Ryan. However else this day has been completely not what he expected, this makes everything else worth it.

And he does mean everything else.

It’s going to be worth it. It’s going to make all their lives happier, and safer, and more like what life should be. At least he hopes so.

Under his sleeve, his wrist twinges. The ache there builds like a migraine, but he pays it no mind.

Chapter End Notes

Thank you guys so, so much for your patience on this update. I desperately needed a break. Happy reading!

Chapter 12: palm tree grove

Chapter Notes

Shout out to the culty megachurch I attended all through college without realizing it was a culty megachurch for that one sermon Pastor Dustin gave about "climbing the palm tree." It's been 10 years and I have written so much gay porn. Here's some more.

 

*Content warning for canon references to violence and suicide.*

Butcher scrambles at Homelander with frantic hands, a whole lexicon behind the tide of his grasping and seeking. This pattern says he’s nearly satisfied; searching, rather than catching. Which means Homelander’s nearly where he wants him to be. He hitches Butcher’s leg up higher around his waist, warmly amused and expectant at Butcher’s increasingly fevered fumbling.

Homelander seats his palm beneath the small of Butcher’s back to elevate him while he tests a different angle. Butcher’s limbs coil like anticipation, and so do his hands, in Homelander’s sweaty hair and down one bicep.

“There?” Homelander breathes, as softly as freshly fallen snow.

Butcher’s mouth drops open, halfway approaching a word, when Homelander swivels his hips. All he gets out then is a guttural groan. 

Bending then, carefully, so as not to lose form, Homelander licks into Butcher’s mouth. “There,” he croons, smiling.

“Fuck’re you waitin’ for then?” Butcher grouses through clenched teeth, trembling, and braced for impact. For the moment where they collide and merge and throw sparks by the wreckage of their fucking. “Do it.”

“I thought you were going to teach me about being generous,” Homelander muses.

“It ain’t fucking generous if you got off twice already,” he snaps, digging into Homelander’s back with his heels. “You fuckin’ tease.

With a thoughtful hum, he pulls Butcher down on his dick by just the strength of his one arm. He liked that little flourish when he was without powers and it was Butcher doing the fucking.

“Oh, you,” Butcher grunts, tossing his head back against the thousand-count bedspread. “Cunt.”

That is more what Homelander wants — Butcher writhing in broken pleasure so close to agony he can’t control his hands or his mouth. Maybe he needs an orgasm under his belt before he’ll let himself get to that point, but Homelander doesn’t let that possibility distract him from the task at hand. He grinds into Butcher with patient twists of his hips, keeping himself close enough to kiss and lick and suck at Butcher’s mouth when he’s not getting bitten for his trouble.

“Is that any way to speak to a lover,” Homelander chides, his tongue grazing Butcher’s teeth on the word lover.

Butcher gnashes his teeth. Even bloodies Homelander’s lip on the second try.

“Lesson one about generosity,” Butcher quips, a smear of blood ringing his bottom lip and staining two of his teeth. Like he’s been brawling, and not at all like he’s spent the last hour split open on Homelander’s dick. “Think about someone other than yourself.”

“Doesn’t sound like me.”

“No, it don’t,” Butcher says, laughing sharply.

“Then again, it doesn’t sound like you either,” Homelander murmurs, testing out a rougher, harder shove of his hips, and thrilling all over at the instantaneous glazing over of Butcher’s eyes. “But here you are,” he continues, hauling Butcher into him in time with his movements, “more a master of my flesh than I could ever have claimed to be.”

Butcher reaches for him, hands clasping senselessly against meaty part of Homelander they light upon — his shoulder, the upper trunk near his armpit, lower and gripping every one of his ribs until his palm comes home to roost at the jut of Homelander’s hip. His eyes are swallowed up whole by hungry pupils, like the darkest night choking out all the light left in the universe. Homelander fucks into him again and again, as hard as Butcher wants, filling the chambers of veins at his throat, high in his cheeks, and through his temples with blood. 

Red-faced and clawing at Homelander, he leaves gouges where his nails rake along Homelander’s equally flushed skin. Butcher gasps, the spaces between his breaths shortening, quickening, until he strains and spills with a shout. If Homelander hadn’t had two himself by now he would’ve followed right after him. He loves to watch Butcher come apart.

As it is, Homelander just keeps fucking him. He fucks Butcher through orgasm, and when he’s languishing in the afterglow, when Homelander knows he’s two seconds from pushing him off, he slows down. Not quite stopping, but resuming his tender, investigative plundering. 

He was right, as it happens. With the relief of one orgasm unstacking all Butcher’s silly reservations about control and inhibition, he’s like putty in Homelander’s hands.

How he wants to worship him. 

He laps at the sweat gathering in the notch at the base of Butcher’s throat, loving the taste and smell of him. The immense presence of him. That he’s always had, whether he could pull airplanes out of the sky or only dream of it.

Butcher shivers hard, raking his nails down Homelander’s back at the next turn of his hips. Too much, too soon. Homelander softens his approach, flirts with Butcher’s sensitivity rather than decimating it, as he’s wanted since he coaxed Butcher into taking him this way again. Sweat drops off the end of his nose, breaking against Butcher’s burning cheek.

Homelander brushes that scattered bead of sweat with his thumb and ends up cupping Butcher’s cheek in his hand, breathing his breath. 

The next time Butcher shudders, Homelander can taste it on his tongue. Butcher winds an arm around Homelander’s neck, keeping him right where he is, so every part of his unraveling carries just such flavor and texture right in the well of Homelander’s mouth.

“Do you want me,” Homelander says on a sigh.

Butcher sighs back, “Yeah.”

“Want me to take you apart?” he asks.

“Yeah,” Butcher says again, nodding almost deliriously.

Homelander smears a kiss into Butcher’s mouth, his days-old stubble scratching and mingling with Butcher’s beard. He says, “Want me to make you weak?”

Butcher knocks his temple against Homelander’s tossing his head. He goes long enough without answering that Homelander ruts into him, messy and needful and nearly forgetting himself, but it works. The answering moan from Butcher is high, splintered with pleasure.

“Billy,” he whispers, dulcet and filled to the brim with it, like pollen spilling out of a flower. Like Butcher’s a bee he means to delight and overwhelm. He slips from Butcher’s temple down to his cheek to his jaw and then along the curve of his neck. But it isn’t the only way he feels himself slipping. “Do you want me to make you weak?”

“Yeah.”

He can’t quantify what happens then. It’s like when Butcher kisses him, but amplified by his fastest speed in flight. It’s that, and then Butcher’s fisting a handful of Homelander’s hair to turn his face so he can get at his mouth, instantly transforming it into something fathomless and interminable. 

Because then, he might as well be hurtling to Earth at terminal velocity. Him and Butcher both.

They’ve fucked so many times. Here, the beaches of Agriomandra, Arcadia, even at Vought Tower. A few times they’ve come close to approaching what this is — where it’s transported him, and Butcher, too. He couldn’t have explained it or even recognized his capacity to feel it, never having experienced its like before, but now? Now he can tell.

Now when he comes inside Butcher, he knows the nakedness surrounding him is deeper than skin and tangibility. Now he knows himself to be completely vulnerable. Fully known.

Butcher’s fraught with nearly spent ecstasy, and Homelander, well-versed by now in what turns rapture in him like a key, gives Butcher his mouth. To taste, to breathe, and whereby to find obliteration.

They could probably fuck for hours more yet, but Homelander’s sated for now. And he can see that Butcher’s at his limit. It’s in the rapid rise and fall of his chest, and in the tight seal of his eyelashes brushing his cheeks. He’s so, so beautiful, and Homelander wants to tell him. Wants to shower him with more and more promises Butcher will never collect on. Wants to give Butcher his power and his strength and above all else, above every corporeal thing he has to offer, his freedom.

It was nothing to him but loneliness before this thing with Butcher began. It was contracts and transactions and bargaining chips. It was never about partnerships or about love, no matter how much of his heart, soul, and dreams he sold to make it so. He’d given up on the idea. Given up on the hope that it could ever work for him. That anyone would ever really deserve him, or want him, if they did.

He rests his head on Butcher’s chest, both of them sticky with sweat and effort. A thousand beginnings, all of them more effusive than the last, form and die on his lips. 

Paltry things, words. He wonders if that’s why Butcher has so little patience for them.

And he wonders, too, if the silence corroding all of the things Homelander wants to say now, has afflicted Butcher in the past. If he’s felt this same kind of powerlessness when it comes to naming his truths.

“You see the irony, don’t you,” Butcher rumbles, shocking Homelander out of his aimless, sedate drifting.

“In what?”

“Any part of this bein’ about weakness.”

“Weakness is just an inversion of power,” Homelander muses, skimming his fingers through the cooled semen pooled on Butcher’s stomach. “Its undistorted reflection cast in a bright mirror.”

“Where’s that from then?”

“Hmm?” He realizes what Butcher’s asking him and rolls his eyes. “I have been known to have original thoughts from time to time.”

“Sure you have,” Butcher says, his smoky voice curling sensuously around a smirk.

Homelander flattens it with a kiss, or he tries to. What actually ends up happening is the corners of his own mouth turn up and up until he can’t hold the shape of the kiss any longer. When that happens, Butcher bites him. Lacking in ferocity, but burning him all the same and stoking heat low in his belly.

“That why you smashed all your mirrors? Felt like somebody else was lookin’ back?”

Blinking his eyes open, Homelander meets Butcher’s gaze. Steadfast and uncompromising. Not unlike the other Homelander looking out at him from his own reflection.

In his own voice, but unrecognizable, he hears, You know I’m closer to you than that.

He shakes it off, saying, “I didn’t understand myself,” mistrustful of the veracity in his words even as he’s speaking them. “But then, I was never meant to.”

“Why’s that, do you think?”

“What is it you’re so fond of calling me,” Homelander teases. “The caped cunt?” He laughs darkly. “Vought never wanted heroes, or they wouldn’t have made us to be parted out and mass-produced. That’s why I got rid of the mirrors.”

Butcher pushes his fingers into the whiskers on Homelander’s cheek. It’s nearly a beard, though it can’t quite compete just yet with Butcher’s. “Didn’t like what you saw.”

“You do love to cut right through the bullshit, don’t you, Billy?”

“Think you don’t?” Butcher murmurs, still bunching his fingers in Homelander’s scruff. He pitches his voice so it’s softer, clearer, but incongruously sharpened by the accent he affects: Homelander’s accent. “Weakness is a bright mirror of power undistorted.”

Mouth gaping stupidly, Homelander shakes himself and says, “I don’t sound like that!”

But Butcher doubles down, and admittedly, bizarrely, it isn’t the worst impersonation he’s heard: “Do you want me?” He paws at Homelander, nearly grinning, like the bright mirror he’s playing at mocking. “Want me to make you weak?”

Homelander brings as much bass into his voice as he can to sling Butcher’s own accent back at him: “Oh, yeah, you beauty, you treasure, you fucking delight—”

“All right, Van Dyke,” Butcher says with a snort, rolling them over to sit across Homelander’s hips. 

Running his hands up Butcher’s sides, idly feeling the grooves between his ribs with his fingertips, Homelander murmurs, “You’re a better reflection than he was.”

“What d’you mean?”

“My bright mirror. Or, maybe I was the mirror, and that’s why he tried to destroy me.”

A look crosses Butcher’s face that Homelander hasn’t seen on him since that morning they spent in the sky. Apprehension. 

In a low voice, he asks, “Who’re you fuckin’ talkin’ about, John?”

He realizes then that Butcher’s staring at him. Not in that heated, wonderful way Homelander’s grown accustomed to these past few weeks, but in the manner of someone listening with his whole body. Homelander sorts through the last few admissions he’s made, trying to see an opening for him to back peddle but not finding one. Although, when he searches himself, he’s alarmed to find he doesn’t especially want a way out of this conversation.

They’ve been in darkness, him and Butcher. They’ve been in free fall. They can be in the light, can’t they? 

Isn’t that what Homelander tried to tell him when they brought in the dawn together?

“The part of me that could survive without breaking,” he whispers, knowing it for the confession that it is. “That knew what it would take and what I’d have to give up—” I killed the crying boy in you for a reason, buddy, he remembers his other self telling him, and the memory, vivid and brutal, makes his eyes sting. “Who could do the impossible because nothing ever hurt him. Not like it hurt me.”

He drops off into silence then, wondering where the hemispheres in himself diverge. If they do, or ever did.

Butcher cups Homelander’s cheek in his hand. Straight out of the past, he echoes Homelander’s words from long ago, saying, “You, havin’ a conscience,” softly, ruminatively. And then, like he maybe doesn’t trust Homelander to remember, he bends down to kiss him.

But Homelander’s never forgotten Butcher. Maybe he didn’t always recognize him, but he never relinquished him from memory. Not really.

“And a soul, too,” Homelander hums. “Imagine that.”

Pinching his chin between his thumb and one knuckle, Butcher says, “Adorable,” laying it on thick because it’s part of a memory they share, no matter how grim. 

Because they have an inside joke.

“I wonder who your bright mirror is,” Homelander thinks out loud. “Maybe your young friend. Starlight’s little boyfriend.”

“Yeah, he wishes,” Butcher sighs, leaning down to lay shoulder-to-shoulder with Homelander. “Be a better person, me. If he were.”

“You wouldn’t have spent the last few days teaching me the lay of your body if you were a better person.” Homelander twists onto his side to wind an arm around Butcher’s back, pulling him flush against his chest. Their hearts overlapping through the barriers of skin and blood and bone. “But you wouldn’t have let me give you the V either. Do you think you’d be dead by now, if you hadn’t?”

“Maybe braindead.”

“How tiresome,” he says, thumbing Butcher’s ear and remembering the sludge that once drained out of it. “What a waste that would’ve been. A waste of your hands, your eyes—” he kisses the corner of Butcher’s eye, feeling the brush of his eyelashes against his lips. “Your smart mouth.”

Butcher laughs, loud and sharp and surprised. He’s lovely when he laughs.

“Why don’t we stay here,” Homelander tells him, hardly posing it as a question, but he knows before Butcher answers that he won’t hear of it.

Except then, tellingly, he doesn’t say no.

“In a tourist trap, why’d we do that? You wanna keep living outta bathrobes and eating seventy-dollar filet mignon every night?”

It doesn’t sound like the hardship Butcher maybe intended for it to be, but Homelander can hear it in anyway that he was never going to be satisfied with gloss and luxury for long. Of course he wouldn’t. Homelander just had to go and fall in love with a practical man, didn’t he? Couldn’t be a glutton for extravagance in the way Homelander knows how to nurture. 

No, he had to be single-minded and focused and borderline ascetic. Of course.

“We could buy a little house by the water, away from the noise and the commerce. I can be on the phone with Ashley in a minute. She’ll give us anything we want and call it a severance package if it means she never has to see me or you ever again.”

“You leave Vought,” Butcher says, leaning away to look at Homelander. “No, I don’t think you will.”

Homelander matches Butcher’s excoriating gaze. He doesn’t feel stripped raw beneath it, as he might’ve once. Rather, he feels a rush of clarity. He turns and reaches behind himself for the phone to dial the front desk.

“What’re you doing?”

“Hi, hello, could you please patch me through to Vought International? Yes, the headquarters office. Thank you.”

The tinkling hold music trills out across the traveling connection.

Butcher sits up. “John,” he says, dark eyebrows pulling down on his serious, perplexed face. He looks so somber and severe and mistrustful that Homelander’s fondly reminded of where they started.

He leans over to kiss that look off him, pleased with himself even as the line connects.

“Vought International,” a soft, feminine voice answers. “This is Yvette. How may I direct your call?”

“Hi Yvette, would you be a doll and transfer me to Ashley’s direct line?”

“Ms. Barrett’s in a meeting. May I take a message?”

“You can tell her Homelander called—” He evades a grab from Butcher to take the phone out of his hand. “Are you listening, Yvette? Write this down—”

“John,” Butcher snaps, finally wrestling the phone away from him.

“I quit!” Homelander shouts, dwarfed by the ruckus of the phone smashing right through the receiver. He clicks his tongue and says, mildly, “They’re going to add that to the bill, you know.”

“And you’re gonna settle that tab, are ya? You’re gonna pay fuckin’ property taxes on a cliffside mansion in Greece next year when you ain’t never had a real job? That’s your plan? Fucking off to live some kinda normal life like the rest of it never happened?”

“You, me, and Ryan,” Homelander amends. 

Butcher blinks, and then his face seems to drain entirely of blood, even beneath the golden tan Greece has bestowed upon him.

“Where’s Ryan?”

“I had Ashley send a car for him.”

“When?”

“Two weeks ago? He’s fine, Billy. He’s practically a man already.”

“He’s ten years old, you twat!”

Bemused, Homelander watches Butcher rush around the room getting dressed, telling him, “What are you hurrying for? I know you don’t really want to leave. Oh, sit down. There’s no need for this whole production.”

Butcher ignores him in favor of pulling on a pair of jeans he hasn’t needed in days.

“I don’t think you’re actually worried about him,” Homelander tells him, surveying the muscle groups in Butcher’s back as they twitch with coiling tension. 

“How do ya figure that?” he asks without turning.

“Because if you were, you’d just ask me if he was okay. I could be there and back in a moment. But that’s only if peace of mind is what you actually wanted.”

“Peace of mind. Peace of mind. You think that’s what I fucking care about? How ‘bout, we took off and left him; how ‘bout, he’s had enough of people he trusts lettin’ him down without us adding to the list again. How ‘bout he’s a fucking child and not—”

“—a dog with very particular dietary needs?” Homelander muses, anticipating the color that floods Butcher’s chest, neck, and face when he turns around. “Your little entourage will have made arrangements to provide for him, Billy. Just as I made arrangements with Vought for our son.” He gives Butcher a steady, probing look. “Will you sit down?”

Butcher tosses the shirt he was about to pull on over his head and does sit at the foot of the bed. It’s irritating to see him even partially dressed; he’s far less likely to allow himself to be coaxed back into bed, but Homelander does have one more avenue he can try. It won’t prolong their stay, but it should keep them in the room another few hours if he’s lucky. He thinks that’ll sustain him for now. At least, until he can bring Butcher around to his suggestion from earlier.

They’ve had a few weeks in paradise. Why not have it all the time?

Why not have each other all the time?

Homelander crawls over next to Butcher to hook his chin over his shoulder. He rubs a gentle hand over Butcher’s stomach, evidence of their leisurely, delicious morning in flaking off beneath his seeking fingers.

“You’re not really going to go out like this, are you?” he asks dryly, teasingly.

Butcher scowls at him, obviously decided on his course of action: that they need to leave, that they’ve got to return to their responsibilities. It’s in the stiffness of his shoulders and in the implacable distance on his face. Homelander would’ve felt wrong-footed to see it once, or dismally servile, but not now. 

Now, he can read that look — that uncertainty, however well-hidden it is — on Butcher’s face.

He wonders if Butcher can read his.

“I don’t need a war,” he says, carefully, wary of repeating himself, but knowing that it’s for a purpose. “I don’t need airports christened in my name or action figures modeled after my likeness or film franchises or fucking brand deals. I don’t need Homelander.” He slips off the edge of the bed to kneel in front of Butcher and cradle skull in his hands, one hand one either side, fingers disappearing into his hair. “I don’t, if you don’t.”

Butcher catches his wrists, then covers Homelander’s hands with his own.

“I ain’t gonna ask you to do that.”

He tenses his grip in Butcher’s hair, nearly pulling. Nearly protesting that this isn’t a test; isn’t another one of those things he asks for because he wants to be denied.

But then he realizes that he can’t. 

I don’t, if you don’t.

Because that was him asking, wasn’t it? Even lacking the usual flourishes, it had all the distinguishing features of an ultimatum. Read back like a palindrome, it was an ultimatum.

If you don’t ask me to throw it all away, then don’t think I will.

“How do I keep you?” he asks, trying to pull the helplessness in his voice. He only half-succeeds. “How do I hold onto you?”

“You are holdin’ onto me,” Butcher says, extracting Homelander’s hands from his hair.

Their hands overlap between them, sunlight streaming in through the open windows. It doesn’t escape Homelander at all that he could almost be asking Butcher for his hand, if he just had a ring on him. It doesn’t escape him either that the only reason he didn’t prepare for that outcome is that he wants more than Butcher’s hand. More than his eyes and his smart mouth and his strength and his power, he wants Butcher’s freedom. Wants it to be tied together with his.

Going back to the V in Butcher’s cramped little kitchen, the stink of fetid brain rot souring the air, he thinks that was always what he wanted. 

For Butcher to need him in order to be complete.

“And you don’t really wanna keep me.”

Homelander closes his eyes against the inordinate tenderness, surprising and terrifying, in Butcher’s voice. He tucks his chin against Butcher’s thigh and turns into the hand scratching through his stubble. “I do,” he whispers. “I do.”

For a moment, Butcher just scrunches and un-scrunches his fingers against Homelander’s cheek. “Could you really know that quickly? If he’s all right.”

Sitting back on his heels and turning his ear toward the Atlantic, he listens. There’s a lot of noise to filter through until he can find Ryan’s voice. Like he knows his dad’s keeping an ear out for him, he simplifies the task by letting loose a bright, happy laugh. Surrounding sounds of mirth swallow up Ryan’s, many other people laughing with him. Their voices come to Homelander easily, even if he’s unfamiliar with the different cadences of their laughter. 

He shuts them out, whittling his concentration down to just this room. To just Butcher with his knees framing Homelander on both sides. “He’s with your friends,” he says.

“The boys?”

“Do you have other friends?” Homelander asks blandly, offset by a wicked smile.

“Got a guy I fuck sometimes,” Butcher retorts, matching the wickedness of Homelander’s smile in his tone, but keeping his expression relatively innocent. Even as he hauls Homelander up onto the bed with him. “Out of his mind. Says he wants to keep me.”

“Ludicrous,” Homelander says into Butcher’s mouth, already wrestling his jeans off him. “Insane.”

“Tried to tell him.”

“He didn’t listen?” — reaching down and closing a hand around him — “I wonder why.”

“Thinking with his dick,” Butcher groans.

My heart, Homelander thinks desperately, wantonly. Thinking with my heart. My whole heart. Whatever’s there in my chest that exists for loving people, it loves you. I love you. I love you.

“Billy, I…”

“You what?” — twisting his hand so Homelander curls into him. “Huh, John? You what?”

“I’m… oh, I’m… weak, and I… want you, and I…”

They lay there shivering, panting into each other’s mouths in a lush, soporific haze. Butcher combs a hand through Homelander’s hair, the motion carrying his palm to the back of Homelander’s neck where it comes to rest. Their heartbeats merge into a single pounding drum. Playing the same music amid their silence.

Until Butcher says, quietly, “Yeah. Me, too.”

 

/

 

Homelander stops them over a dwelling he don’t recognize from an aerial view. A cursory glance with the x-ray vision tells him it’s Hughie and Annie’s place. 

What’s more, between them, the lads, and Ryan, they’ve got a full house. Just as advertised.

“See?” Homelander hums, holding Butcher closer than he needs to, being that they can both hover without it. “He’s fine.”

Butcher leads him down onto the rooftop proper, not liking the visibility out in the open air. He don’t need the boys to see him like this. Not before he figures out how he’s gonna play it. Homelander goes easily, pliable in that cheeky way of his, how he gets when he’s humoring Butcher and wants him to know it.

Solidity beneath Butcher’s feet makes Homelander bolder with his hands. He reels Butcher firmly into his chest, familiar after so many days of just the same. Comfortable.

It’s a trick of biology what makes his muscles unwind. Chemistry, nothing else.

Still, he does make an effort to lock up. They’re not in an unknown city any longer, hiding behind anonymity and room service. They’re on the roof of Hughie’s building, and everyone underneath it knows them for exactly who they are. And more to the point, Butcher knows. 

Who he is, and John, too.

He doesn’t quite manage to extract himself from the arms enveloping him. Maybe he’s being held too tightly, or maybe he just ain’t trying so hard to get away anymore.

“This was fun,” Homelander muses, the declaration obscured by Butcher’s mouth.

“Uh huh.”

“And I…” His laugh, low and scraped from his chest, gets lost in Butcher’s mouth just like his words. “I meant everything I said, Billy.”

“What’d you say?” Butcher mumbles. “When?”

Homelander laughs again, sonorous, and catches Butcher’s chin to steady him for a real kiss. Butcher catches him back, spinning them down and down into supple, languid darkness with room enough for two.

Because what kinda bomb vest would he be otherwise?

“Come home with me,” Homelander whispers, his mouth canted softly and sweetly against Butcher’s. “With us. It’s where you belong.”

“I can’t.”

“Why not?” he asks, forgetting to be patient how he always does when he’s got Butcher in his arms like this and still, can’t quite seem to get a good grip on him. It’s one of those tells he has, a signifier of that category of need he can’t explain, much less get rid of. “Give me one good reason.”

It wasn’t a life, what they had out in Greece. Butcher knows that. He’s done life with someone before; with Becca, and he never forgot that most of it amounted to routine and mundane, everyday bullshit and honesty. No matter how plain; no matter how uncomfortable.

They weren’t, neither of them, batting a thousand here, and Butcher don’t know where to put his feelings about it. Just like Homelander can’t make sense of Butcher or give him up, Butcher—

Well, it don’t matter. He’ll go back to the job, back to knowing which side of the green he’s meant to be playing, and he’ll finish it. And it won’t require an answer out of him. It won’t demand that he justify Santorini or Agriomandra or the last time in Arcadia. He won’t have to think about it. He won’t have to put his feelings anywhere.

He’ll shut them out and remember his default unhappiness, and he won’t let himself think about how that means he was happy, when this was real. 

Or when it felt like it could be, anyway.

“You can’t, can you,” Homelander murmurs, not disappointed. Revelatory. “You want to come with me.”

“That don’t mean that I can.”

“What’s stopping you? What could ever stop you?”

Butcher steps back unsteadily. His first mistake.

His second mistake is looking down at the loose gravel beneath his feet. 

Annie’s friend was killed on a rooftop, pulled apart and mashed inside his suit like an insect; and Kimiko’s brother, gone the same way; and the girl from the evening news smiling out of a pristine college graduation photo.

She’d reminded Butcher of Becca when he saw her, something about her eyes. The earnest guilelessness of them. The hope. What was her name? Kacey? Chrissy? Ellie?

No, he thinks, toeing at the pebbles. It was Chelsea.

“What’d you say to that kid?” he asks, idly. “The one that fell to her death from that building uptown. Chelsea something. Horowitz.”

Homelander blinks, not following for a long moment, but then it clicks. Scales coming away from his eyes and sharpening the profile he cuts against the blue midday sky. Making him look at once familiar and unknown. In a muted, colorless voice, he says, “The jumper.”

“Did she?” Butcher asks. “Jump.”

Beginning to smile, beginning to look away, and caught between the two, Homelander flashes a little-boy grin at Butcher. He shakes his head. “That’s not what we’re talking about.”

“Did she,” he persists, calmly, “jump?”

Even with the Grecian sun putting some color in him, it’s blatantly obvious how he pales at the question. Something about his eyes, how the color seems to go out of them, too, so they look more silver than blue, gives him away. For a long apoplectic moment, he doesn’t answer, but then, the urgency dims just like a light going out. Without it, he looks empty. Like a paper man.

“Did she jump,” he echoes hollowly. “Chelsea, Chelsea, Chelsea.” His smile is a dead thing on his face, in sharp contrast with his clear bouncing voice. “Did she jump. As opposed to what, Billy?”

He takes a step forward, and so does Butcher.

“As opposed to bein’ thrown.”

“So what if she was. I didn’t put her on that ledge.”

“I ain’t asking if you did. I’m asking—”

“If she jumped.” Homelander nods, still with that faraway, disjointed moue on his mouth. Nearer than his smile had been, but cut off from the rest of him. “Chelsea Horowitz. Pretty girl. Confused. She wanted to get down. To go home. She cried and cried, and she called out to God to save her. To intervene. To stop me. But God didn’t stop me,” he says, taking another step forward. “God didn’t intervene. Didn’t save her. I’m curious, Billy. Are you hoping God will separate us?”

“Thought that was you,” Butcher tells him glibly. “It is God what decides who lives and who dies, innit?”

Another insipid smile, though this one touches the very corners of his eyes. “I see now.”

“Do ya?”

“Mmm. You don’t care about the dead girl.” He pivots slightly on his heel and paces around Butcher in a slow, methodical circle. Shoulders relaxed, head on a swivel to cast his mercury gaze on the clouds overhead. “You said as much. How silly of me.”

Butcher waits, following Homelander with his eyes until he’s behind him, touch replacing sight. Homelander’s hand pressed flat to the small of Butcher’s back. Familiar, too. Comfortable.

Biology. Chemistry. Muscles unwinding.

“You want to know what I said to her?”

“Was my question.”

Homelander hums. “I asked her why I had to give and give and give, just to be torn down and made into a fucking gimmick. Just for these same people that clamored for my destruction to claim me, as if they have any right to my strength.”

Closing his eyes, Butcher says, “Your leash, you mean.”

The hand lifts away from his back, leaving a void so much bigger than Homelander’s palm and the fan of his fingers. 

He’s fucked, Butcher is. He’s so fucked.

How’d he let it happen? What the fuck? What the fuck?

‘Cuz no. Fuck no, he weren’t actually trying to understand. He was trying to butt heads; trying to pick open scabs and force out the bile if it meant he might have a chance at getting the hell out of this tarpit he willingly waded into.

It weren’t supposed to go any higher than his knees, but here he is dropped into the fucking deep end not knowing which way’ll take him up to breathable air.

“Look at me.”

Butcher waits for a hand at his jaw. Waits for anger. For force.

“Billy,” Homelander whispers, small, boyish — who gave him the fucking right to sound like that and mean it? “Look at me.”

He shakes his head, eyes slammed shut. Homelander does catch his face in his hands then, but he’s gentle about it. Greece didn’t acclimate Butcher to the softness of those hands, as often as he’s felt them on his body.

“Look at me, Billy.”

Sick with hatred at his own fucking weakness, Butcher opens his eyes. The week’s worth of beard on Homelander’s face draws his attention, and he sinks his fingers into it, not thinking at all about the direction his thoughts take on. Not even when it goes spilling out of his mouth. “Couldn’t’ve just been a man, could ya?”

“Could you bear it then?”

His gaze darts up to Homelander’s. “What?”

“Knowing you want me,” he says, brushing Butcher’s lips with his. “Even though I’ll never be innocent.”

And Butcher—

Well, he—

Fuck—

Fuck.

“Even though you’ll never be,” Homelander adds, sealing the question with a kiss that culls.

Butcher ain’t proud of himself for what it does to him, that kiss. Even holding on for dear life, he disappears inside it, and nearly for good.

But then, he hears, “Dad?”

Homelander breaks the kiss. Butcher jerks away from him, the spell broken with Ryan standing on the edge of the roof looking at them. He’s got a smile on his face, and he don’t let the circumstances of how he’d just caught them together put him off running over. Homelander scoops him up into a hug before swinging him around to Butcher. 

Butcher gets his shit together just in time to catch him. “What’re you doin’ up here, huh?” he says, setting a hand in Ryan’s hair and firmly ignoring his pounding heart.

“I thought I heard you guys talking. I tried not to listen, but then you kept talking.”

Snorting, Homelander palms the space between Ryan’s shoulder blades. He shoots Butcher a sly look over Ryan’s head, saying, “I was just trying to convince Butcher here to come stay with us for a while.”

Butcher has enough time to narrow his eyes at Homelander, but then Ryan’s looking up at him.

“Butcher, do you want to? We can play Mario Kart, and I can show you this new comic I started reading, and Terror can come, too!”

Homelander sets his hands on Ryan’s shoulders, a serene smile on his stupid handsome face.

“I gotta see to some things, now we’re back,” he tells Ryan apologetically.

Ryan’s obviously disappointed, but he don’t push him on it. He just nods, dials back his excitement, and says, “Okay.”

Ruffling his hair, Butcher adds, “Some other time, all right? I promise.”

Without a second’s hesitation, Ryan holds out his pinky. Butcher hooks it with his own, even managing to smile. He can’t help it. He missed Ryan something fierce.

“Well, kiddo, are you ready to go home?”

“Can I say bye to everyone? I told them I was coming right back.”

Homelander catches Butcher’s eye, devious but with something else beneath it that makes Butcher’s skin feel tight and hot. “Sure, son,” Homelander says, buoyantly, like he’s just had an idea he can’t wait to inflict on someone. “You don’t mind, do you, Billy?”

Butcher has a feeling he’s about to mind very fucking much, but what can he do? He feels like a tit about the last few weeks as it is; he don’t wanna make it any worse on Ryan than he already has.

“’S all right with me if it’s what you want,” he tells Ryan, and promptly gets frogmarched toward the ledge with Ryan’s hand in his.

He walks right over the side, and Butcher goes with him, resigned to his fate.

Chapter 13: linguam bisulcam

Chapter Notes

See the end of the chapter for notes

When Ryan takes Butcher in through the window to Starlight’s apartment, its inhabitants erupt in busy, flustered chatter. It’s mostly of the jovial sort, though they’re obviously more surprised than anything else. 

They get a little more surprised when Homelander floats down to the window. Unlike what he’s grown accustomed to, they don’t seem to recognize him. It takes him longer than it should to realize it’s because he doesn’t look like himself. Ryan’s finishing up his rounds throwing hugs on the last few of Butcher’s cohorts when it seems to sink in for them that it’s definitely Homelander and not anyone else. 

“Ah, non,” he hears the Frenchman say under his breath. “Ils croqué la pomme.”

Helping himself in through the window, Homelander says, “Une délicieuse pomme,” and though his accent is far more ungainly than the Frenchman’s, at least it’s passable. With a warm, lingering look at Butcher, he adds, “Très douce.”

“Look at you,” Butcher muses. “Mr. Man of the world.”

The small Frenchman, Serge Whoever, mumbles a few more expletives to himself. Some of them Homelander half-remembers from a Quebecois lingerie model he met and bedded in Montreal, Spring of ‘97. She’d been equally impressed by his limited vocabulary, but as good as that year was for him in the press, it probably wouldn’t have mattered one way or another. At twenty-two the world had been his oyster, even more than it is now.

Ryan crosses back to Homelander’s side and takes his hand. Then, pivoting back to Butcher, he says, “Are you sure you don’t wanna come with us?”

He asks quietly, knowing nothing of artificial brightness or prevarication. 

Homelander’s tempted to pick up the slack and lay it on thick, but he knows better by now. Butcher will spit and cuss if he piles on, but when it’s Ryan, he’ll at least consider his request. Predictably, Butcher hesitates. He even looks at Homelander, as if waiting for him to make the choice easier on him, but Homelander doesn’t. All he does is set his hand in Ryan’s hair, and wait.

It’s so interesting then, watching Butcher flounder for an excuse that he won’t hate himself later for using on Ryan. More interesting still to catalogue the expressions of conflict adorning the faces in their audience. They love Ryan, that much is clear. But how do they reconcile that love when they have nothing but hatred for Homelander and everything he represents, and when Ryan is inextricably a part of him?

“I can’t,” Butcher says, cupping the back of Ryan’s head so that his fingertips graze the edge of Homelander’s hand.

It feels accidental, until it doesn’t.

“Not just yet,” he says again, catching Homelander’s eyes. “All right?”

“Okay,” Ryan answers easily, and steps forward to wrap Butcher in another hug. Longer than the one from the roof because, according to Ryan, goodbyes take longer to set in than hellos do. Muffled by Butcher’s shirt, he asks, “But when you can, will you?”

Butcher hugs Ryan to him, one hand spanning his narrow back and the other curled protectively in his hair. “Yeah, I will.”

Ryan pulls away with a bright smile for everyone, but especially for Butcher. It seems to have a seismic effect on him, breaking down walls in a moment that Homelander on his own barely managed to erode over the course of weeks in relative isolation. But he understands perfectly. As far as Achilles’ heels go, there are worse weaknesses to have.

“Bye!” Ryan calls out to the apartment at large, already towing Homelander back to the window by his wrist.

Butcher follows behind, lingering at the window sill, most likely to make sure they go. Even knowing better than to take it as one, Homelander can’t help reading it as an invitation. He pauses at the threshold to peer back at Butcher and catch his eyes — Butcher, who’d already been staring after him. 

Homelander wants to bite him on the mouth. Wants to hold him tight and never let him go. 

He wonders if Butcher realizes these same impulses Homelander’s feeling are showing in his own face like a looking glass. Or like a bright mirror.

Glancing past Butcher to the apartment beyond, to the five sets of eyes watching them and doing nothing at all to pretend otherwise, Homelander cuts a smile and palms Butcher’s cheek. It is an invitation, as much as it is a warning. The invitation is for Butcher, and the warning is for his little friends.

He’s not yours anymore, Homelander thinks, watching the challenge glitter in Butcher’s eyes to match the smile twitching at his lips. He’s mine. 

“I’ll see you soon, Billy.”

“Yeah.” He steps away from the window with a wave for Ryan. “See you.”

“Bye, Butcher! Come on, Dad.”

They take off into the sky headed for the townhouse, Ryan leading the way at about half the clip Homelander usually employs when using the superspeed. It makes it easier to keep his attention back at the apartment with Butcher since he doesn’t have to navigate their path back home.

“Fuckin’ hell, Hughie, what happened here? Old masturbation injury acting up?”

“Oh, uh, I stepped in front of a cab by mistake—”

“I leave for two weeks, and you’re walkin’ into traffic?”

“Three and a half weeks,” mumbles Hughie Campbell.

“You’re really gonna come in here and act like you didn’t drop off the face of the earth to go be this asshole’s kept boy,” Marvin Milk hisses. “Like we haven’t seen pictures of you dressing him up like a fucking tourist?”

“Well, we were tourists,” Butcher simpers effusively with mock innocence.

“No, Butcher,” Hughie Campbell says. “You’ve gone fucking native, man.”

“Did all of you know he spoke French?” says the Frenchman distantly, but despondently, still on this thread, apparently, and sounding like he needs to sit down. “What the fuck.”

“Have you thought about what happens when the other shoe drops,” Starlight asks, in that diplomatic tone of voice Homelander’s heard so often in soundbites. “Because it’s going to, Butcher. It is never true or right or real with him. You know that.” She pauses, and then, “Don’t you?”

“Dad?”

Homelander starts, realizing where he is. They’re hovering over the townhouse, and Ryan’s tugging on his wrist for them to go inside.

“I’m right behind you, buddy. Give me just a minute.”

Ryan frowns, watching him. “Are you gonna leave again?”

“No, I…” 

From Butcher, “What makes you think I—”

“What’re you doing?” Ryan asks, talking over Butcher without having any concept of doing so.

“Trying to listen,” he says, and then lets his focus snap like a rubber band losing tension, so he’s just here with Ryan. He softens his voice and sets his hands on Ryan’s shoulders, gently, gently. “You know Butcher and I have a complicated… history.”

“Yeah.” Ryan shrugs. “You guys used to hate each other.”

“Well, and his friends—”

“Aren’t they awesome?” he gushes, that boyish grin filling up his whole face. “I got to hang out with them almost the whole time you were gone. Aunt Grace even bought everyone breakfast one morning. I was so happy to see her, and she was happy to see me, too! Isn’t that cool, Dad? I really thought she would’ve forgotten about me.”

Homelander sets his teeth against his cheek and doesn’t answer right away. He finds that he can’t. There aren’t any words.

Whatever his face looks like, Ryan’s cheer dims, and he says, more neutrally, “I’m sorry I didn’t stay at Vought. It was just really quiet, and it didn’t take me that long to watch all your movies. I even watched some of them twice, but… then Hughie came by.” Here, he brightens again. “I guess Ashley told him where to find me.”

“That was nice of her,” Homelander says, trying to match him, trying to paste on the wan smile he breaks out for morning TV.

“Anyway, what’re you listening for?” Ryan asks, steamrolling right over any mention of Ashley.

Homelander will have to evaluate that later. For now, all he says is, “Butcher’s friends. They don’t think very highly of me, but most of them would never say so if they thought I could hear them.”

“Isn’t that eavesdropping?” With a glance back the way they’d just come, he adds, “But Dad, that’s rude!”

“So is talking about someone behind their back.”

The look on Ryan’s face then is all Becca Butcher. He says, “Maybe if you didn’t spy on them, they wouldn’t have to wait for you to leave just to talk about you.”

“Now you’re being ridiculous.”

“I’m not! You came in, but you didn’t even say hello or thank you or sorry.”

“What would I have had to apologize for?”

Ryan folds his arms tightly over his chest, and even though he seems incapable of putting it in words, Homelander can infer the direction of his thoughts.

Clasping his hands behind his back, he says, “If you were made to feel like a burden—”
