“No, it wasn’t like that. But you left, Dad, and they had to take care of me, and even if they wanted to do it, that was a lot to make them responsible for! You didn’t tell anyone where you went or how long you’d be gone, and they didn’t have to stay with me. They could’ve…”

“What, Ryan?”

He pockets his hands and lowers himself down to the roof. “Nothing. Never mind.”

“Hey, hey, hey.” Homelander follows after and catches Ryan by the tops of his arms. “Don’t you turn your back on me in the middle of a conversation.”

“Well, I don’t want to have a conversation with you,” Ryan pops back. “But that’s okay,” he says, in such an uncanny approximation of Homelander’s faux-bright, fit-to-print voice that it sends something like a chill down his back. “There’s a whole other conversation happening over there that you can be part of.”

And then he turns and stomps over to the roof access door, uses his key to let himself in, and shuts the door tightly behind him. Homelander stares at the metal door in disbelief and makes himself breathe through the sound of the locking mechanism sliding home.

He’ll have to deal with that before it snowballs into a bigger fight, but it’s probably in his best interest to give Ryan some cooldown time before he tries.

Which, fortuitously, gives him a window of opportunity to do just what Ryan said.

He opens up his hearing again, expecting very little given how much time has elapsed since he stopped listening. When he pinpoints the residence he wants, he encounters just as many heartbeats as before. Everyone’s tucked in just where he left them, and they’re still just scolding Butcher for his choices.

“It must be a very good dick,” says the Frenchman belligerently. “To make you forget yourself this way.”

“You knew I’ve been fucking him for weeks. Suddenly it’s a problem?”

“It’s not just that, Butcher,” Hughie Campbell says. “You bailed, and we didn’t hear from you. Did you just… I mean, did you do anything else the whole time you were out there? Or was it all about the sex?”

“Sometimes we got room service.”

“You don’t fuck with shame, is that it? You don’t got time for it? Fine. What’s that leave you then? You gonna stand there and tell us you’re proud of yourself?” Marvin Milk says, clearly throwing down a challenge. “Or does that sound like bullshit to you? Because to me, it sounds like bullshit.”

“I’ll tell you what it sounds like. Sounds like my fuckin’ business. Speakin’ of, where’s my dog?”

“My dad has him.”

“Great. Cheers. Good to be back.”

“Butcher, wait!”

And he does wait. Homelander can hear Butcher hesitate with his hand on the window frame, turning to look back at Hughie and staying rooted in place when Hughie crosses over to him.

“There’s something else.”

Homelander strains his ears against the silence. All he hears in it is Hughie Campbell’s heart beginning to race. He wonders if Butcher can hear it, but then, surely he must. It was one of the very first things he noticed about Homelander after the V transformed him, albeit in a slightly different configuration. It strikes him, though, that Butcher may not be motivated in the same way to observe the finer details of young Hughie Campbell’s body.

It’s a heady thought, but he ignores it. For now.

“You were gone,” Hughie says, and trails off.

“I came back.”

“Did you?”

“Hughie,” Butcher sighs. 

“You’ve been dick-notized, man!” 

“Fuck is that.”

“Dick-notized: hypnotized by dick,” Hughie tells him, taking great care to enunciate. “As in, when you spend three weeks fucking and getting fucked by a guy you claim to hate.”

“What, you don’t want me to fuck him no more? Is that it?”

The resounding quiet from Hughie, and from everyone else in the room with him, is oppressive. Butcher doesn’t notice it, or if he does, he gives a hell of an impressive performance to the contrary.

“If you got summat to say to me, you go ’head and say it.”

“Because you are in the right place to hear him,” the Frenchman says sarcastically, to some kind of movement swishing the air beside him. “You have been away too long, Butcher. You cannot see yourself anymore.”

Starlight steps in beside Hughie and softens the riot of his heartbeat with her presence alone. “Just let him go, Hughie,” she murmurs.

“It’s not us letting go,” Marvin Milk says, marching over so his sturdy heartbeat closes ranks with Starlight’s, fencing in Hughie’s fluttery rabbit heart like the high walls of a castle. “Go on, Butcher. Do what you do. Disappear again.”

“Fucking drama queens,” Butcher grumbles, “the lot of ya.”

Homelander’s tempted to follow Butcher’s exit, but he’s curious about the unspoken report from young Hughie. To his great irritation, though, they don’t speak of it. They go on and on in hushed tones about Butcher’s wellbeing. To hear them tell it, you’d think Butcher would have to be clinically insane to want to have anything to do with Homelander—

But then, Homelander’s reflection does converse with him on occasion. Maybe he and Butcher go together for a reason.

There’s nothing else coming from Butcher’s little coterie that’s of any use to Homelander, so he pulls his focus back to himself and bends his ear in Ryan’s direction next. From the sound of his feet and the banging around of soft plastics against metallics, he’s doing laundry.

“Oh, fuck me,” he mutters, pinching the bridge of his nose.

He steadies himself and floats down to the fire escape. The door leading out to the metal landing is unlatched, so he lets himself in.

“Do you want me to wash that for you?” Ryan asks, spinning the plastic bin in front of him.

“What?” Homelander looks down at Butcher’s clothes on his body. He doesn’t quite feel ready to relinquish them just yet. “Oh.”

“I washed your other stuff. I found some shirts Hughie said were his, and I gave those back, but he traded me for them.”

“Traded them for what?”

Ryan loops back around toward the bedrooms, and Homelander trails after him. Folded at the foot of his own bed is a small stack of shirts: a button-down decked out in neon palm trees, a plain black mock neck jumper, a navy polo shirt patterned with pink flamingos, and another button-down made in what looks to be a Japanese design. Homelander runs his fingertips along the gold threading, following the curling swish of a tiger’s tail across the buttons, and lower, to trace the silver threads denoting ocean waves.

He sits on the edge of the bed with the shirt open over his lap. Ryan sits next to him.

“Did you hear anything you didn’t like?”

“I don’t know yet.”

Huffing a short sigh, Ryan says, “I was gonna say they could’ve called Ashley.”

Homelander looks at him, confused at the non sequitur.

“If they didn’t wanna take care of me,” Ryan adds, shrugging. He’s gone back to that single-note voice from before. “They could’ve called Ashley. But they didn’t. You did.”

“Ryan—”

“You were happier out there when it was just you and Butcher.”

“Ryan,” Homelander soothes, reaching for him. “That’s not true.”

He relents, even allowing the arm Homelander loops around his shoulders. “Yes, it is. Before you had to be my dad you could always just do whatever you wanted it. And now things are different, but you’re still the same.”

Homelander swallows hard. He hadn’t considered this outcome when he flew Butcher out to Greece. He hadn’t considered that doing so would result in nearly a month-long sabbatical, or that that would affect Ryan so negatively. His childhood, or lack thereof, had been decorated with lab technicians and glorified chaperones. They’d sufficed, in lieu of a proper caregiver, and what he wouldn’t’ve given to have had a parent, just one, that loved him like he loves Ryan. 

“I made sure you were provided for,” Homelander tells him, wheedling a little bit, but he can’t help it.

Ryan stares at him, blue eyes big and round with emotion he’s been trying not to feel. Tentatively, he says, “When I lived with Mom, everything was always the same. Every day was like the one that came before.”

“Sounds boring.”

“I liked knowing what to expect,” Ryan answers, softly. His voice breaks a little. “I liked knowing I could always go to her if I needed her. I never had to be scared of her leaving and not coming back.”

“You thought I wouldn’t come back?”

Ryan scrubs at his eyes, a few tears escaping.

 

Weak, just like his dad.

 

He takes his hand away and stands unevenly, Butcher’s shirt falling to the floor at his feet. That voice wasn’t the smoother, richer manifestation of his own voice. It was Dad’s.

Startled, Ryan looks up at him. A second later, he looks ashamed. It turns a screw of agony in Homelander’s chest. He drops to his knees and catches Ryan’s face in his hands, tempering his tone as much as he can because he can’t control what’s happening on his face.

“I’m sorry,” Ryan whispers, crying openly now like a dam’s fallen apart inside him.

Homelander shakes his head and closes him in a hug, his own eyes stinging. 

“No, no, I’m sorry. I’m sorry, okay, buddy? I messed up, and I’m sorry. Of course I’d come back for you. I’m like a bad penny that way. You can’t get rid of me.”

“Do you promise?”

“I promise.”

Clinging to Homelander’s neck, Ryan just nods. More tears break apart on Homelander’s shoulder.

“I’m still learning how to do this, pal. I never got to grow up like you did. I never got to be a kid at all. I can’t imagine what any of this feels like for you. I’m flying blind here, but I’m trying.”

“You don’t ever wish you didn’t have to be my dad?”

“I never wish I wasn’t your dad.”

It just doesn’t equate to knowing how to be one, and that’s another thing he never could’ve imagined before it became his reality; that fatherhood would be more an acquired skill than an innate talent. Ryan began in Homelander. They have a connection born out of biology and love. Parenting should be a natural process. Doing right by his son should be a no-brainer, but he feels himself stumbling at every possible obstacle. It’s not something he’s used to; not when everything else in his life has always been as easy for him as breathing.

He pulls away to wipe Ryan’s tears away. “Hey, what do you say we go out just the two of us? Anywhere you want.”

Ryan looks at him for a second. He puts his hand on Homelander’s cheek, not unlike the way Butcher’d continually done out in Greece. “You’re gonna go out like this?”

With an unkempt beard on his face and in clothes that are slightly too big on him, he means.

“Would you be terribly embarrassed if I said yes,” he muses in a brighter voice than feels true at this very moment. He doesn’t want to put the suit back on yet. Doesn’t want to be aware of a flicking cape at his back or feel people’s eyes on his face so soon after such a golden time in the sun. It had been liberating in a way he’s unused to feeling, being outside of his second skin and the glitzy falsehoods built into it — the padded-on musculature hiding the slimmer planes of his body, the deep blues and reds washing him out in a violent semblance of this country’s flag.

“I wouldn’t be embarrassed,” Ryan says, scrunching his fingers against Homelander’s stubbled cheek. “But maybe you should have a shower first.”

“Are you saying I stink?” Homelander asks with mock injury.

Ryan leans in to sniff at his hair. “Like the ocean.”

He ruffles a hand behind his ear to see if that’s true, and it is. His leisurely flight across the Atlantic with Butcher must’ve done it. Normally, the superspeed takes care of that, but he hadn’t flown them back at full speed and some of that frigid sea air must’ve gotten into his hair.

“Take this one,” Ryan says, passing off the button-up covered in palm trees. “I like the colors.”

Homelander had been partial to the tiger print himself, but he goes with Ryan’s selection and does so again when he asks that they catch a ballgame downtown. He even cedes to Ryan’s request that they walk. 

It’s a nice day out, so he doesn’t mind. There’s just one little hiccup.

“Is there a parade in town?” Ryan asks, leaning up on his tiptoes to try and see over the crowds. “Can you tell?”

Homelander doesn’t bother looking. The conversations reverberating back to him, even without a concentrated effort to pick out the words, tell him who these people are. 

“It’s not a parade, Ryan.”

“But can you see what it is?”

“It’s a rally. You’ve been to one before. Remember?”

“Oh.” He loses interest then, and then he drags his feet, pulling on Homelander’s hand. “Won’t they recognize you—?”

Just then, a number of people shoulder past them where they’re stopped on the sidewalk. Homelander catches one of the guy’s eyes who jostled him, braced for discovery, but there’s no recognition in the flaccid, white face looking back at him.

“Get out of the way, man! We’re utilizing our right to free speech and assembly!”

That Homelander’s eyes don’t spark and singe is some kind of miracle, but it helps that Ryan’s holding onto him. He tugs Homelander on to keep walking, and their route takes them right through the heart of the gathering. 

Boorish trash, the lot of them. If he still put any kind of stock into the love these people claim to have for him, it might hurt him to walk among them like this and not be known as exactly who he really is. Such as things are, though, it only reinforces something he began to think out in Greece, on his knees before Butcher like a supplicant in prayer. 

He’s only ever wanted to be loved, but these people have made a mockery of it. Their eyes are empty, so greedy for even a shred of power to latch onto that they can’t see what’s right in front of them. It only gets worse the deeper into the pit they go.

“Are you sure you don’t want to fly?”

“Uh huh.” Ryan looks up at him, unflustered, unfazed. “They like you, don’t they?”

“Do they?” Homelander grits out, getting shoved for the third time in as many minutes. “I couldn’t tell.”

“I guess they think they do.”

Hearing footsteps, he turns to look over his shoulder, and then Ryan’s hand is jolted out of his. He turns to stone, holding back the red sight with every ounce of self-restraint that he has, knowing a show of that kind of force will only upset Ryan after the morning they’ve passed. 

“Whoa, whoa, whoa!” someone’s shouting, elbowing their way through the chain of people who knocked Ryan down. “There’s a kid, you guys! Back up!”

“Fuck you!”

“Real nice,” the skinny, pale man says, already sinking into a crouch to help Ryan up. “Hey, are you okay? Where’re your parents, huh?”

“My dad’s right there.”

Tense with impatience, Homelander shoves the heavyset guy in front of him to the side. He does this with the greatest of ease, no more care or strength needed than if he were batting away a fly.

“Take it easy! Jesus!”

“Ryan.” He holds out his hand. “Let’s go.”

Ryan trades the mousy man’s hand for Homelander’s. There isn’t a scratch on him, of course, but he’s beginning to look overwhelmed at the mass of people all around them. He never took to the noise and pageantry involved in these types of events. That was Homelander’s mistake. He should’ve taken them down two blocks to avoid these insects.

The mousy man cocks his head at Homelander, eyes lingering on his face. 

“Hey, aren’t you…?”

Homelander wills him to shut the fuck up, and wonder of wonders, he does. The surprise in his eyes gives way to adulation, and somehow that’s worse.

“Dad,” Ryan says.

“What is it, Son?”

He might actually pulp the putrid men standing around gawking at him if Ryan suggests he thank the one that helped him up, but Ryan, whether because he senses this or because he isn’t feeling particularly generous, only says:

“We’re gonna miss the game.”

Homelander turns and gets them walking again. He doesn’t let himself stop until they’re clear of the throngs of mud people, stood outside an idyllic community garden.

“Are you okay?”

“I’m fine. I’m not the one who was almost trampled.”

Ryan squeezes his hand. “Yeah, but you’re shaking.”

“I’m angry!”

“Why? They didn’t hurt me. It was an accident.”

“Animals,” Homelander mutters. “Little more than beasts.”

“Sometimes people show you who they are,” Ryan says, shrugging. “You just have to believe them when they do.”

His mind scrapes to a halt, trying to process that or at the very least figure out where to begin. His fans were tame once, as stupid as they are now, but insipid and serviceably polite. It’s because of him that they’ve made savages of themselves. He was always a gateway for them to confirm their own biases, and his temerity has only emboldened them.

“I wonder what you believe about me.”

“Mostly that you’re sad a lot. Even when you seem mad or like you want to hit someone, I think it’s just that you don’t want to cry.”

There’s no way he knows of to speak about what he’d felt in that crowd of imbeciles. It doesn’t make sense. He has more power in his little finger than all of them combined, they’d been congregated for the purpose of celebrating him, and yet, he’d felt so helpless. Not merely because he couldn’t control them without using his powers and causing a scene, but because the experience of them, the excessive waste and vitriol of them, repulsed him. Because he didn’t like what looking out into them revealed about himself.

“We don’t have to go to the game, Dad.”

“We’re going,” he says, rubbing at his eye and turning to continue his stride down the block toward the stadium. “We’re going right now.”

Except they don’t. Ryan plants his feet and keeps them rooted to the spot. Homelander doesn’t get the chance to ask what Ryan thinks he’s doing. There’s no time. Ryan gets out ahead of his questions and hugs him. It shouldn’t solve anything. It should only serve to remind Homelander how pathetic he is that his son, so small and still so young, could wrap his arms around him and make him feel totally contained in that embrace.

Given how little danger Homelander’s known in his life, he should be an old hand at this feeling blooming in him like smoke out of a great fire. But it’s new. It’s always so new, this feeling that, finally, he’s safe.

It’s only then that it occurs to him maybe that’s what he took from Ryan when he absconded with Butcher to Greece, and then the tears come full force.

“I’m sorry, Ryan.”

“It’s okay, Dad.”

He cradles the back of Ryan’s head with his hand. “I wasn’t happier without you. I was just better at not noticing it.”

Ryan looks up at him. “Are you happy now?”

“Right, right now?” Homelander asks, making himself laugh.

“Right, right now,” Ryan tells him.

Taking a knee right there amid fragrant basil and blooming dogwood, having his tears wiped away by the only true family he has left in the world, Homelander says, “More than I knew I could be.”

And it’s the truth.

 

/

 

Butcher wishes the boys hadn’t gone so far outta the way to pull their punches about Greece. He’s sure that’s not how they’re looking at it, and really, that only worsens the spark of irritation flaring under his skin. 

They’ve had unspoken rules for how to talk about this shit with Homelander going back to the beginning; ways to mask what they’re saying so it gives them a believable cover, but he knows a lecture when he sees one. He didn’t much appreciate having to stand there and take it just so he could focus on the real conversation they were having in between the lines.

You’re really gonna come in here and act like you didn’t drop off the face of the earth to go be this asshole’s kept boy, from MM as he typed hurriedly on his phone. Like we haven’t seen pictures of you dressing him up like a fucking tourist?

Turning the screen to Butcher, to show: [Vought has the tapes. Will release when H is depowered.]

Well, we were tourists, from Butcher with a languid shrug.

You bailed, from Hughie, followed by more keyboard clacking. Did you do anything else the whole time you were out there? Or was it all about the sex?

Turning his phone to reveal: [You don’t have to do this anymore.]

And from Butcher, with forced indifference, Sometimes we got room service.

It didn’t surprise him, the reception he’d come back to. Pissed him off something fierce, but it didn’t surprise him.

From MM, You gonna stand there and tell us you’re proud of yourself? Or does that sound like bullshit to you? Because to me, it sounds like bullshit.

On the notepad in his phone, [This is the next phase. Everything’s ready, we just need you to initiate it.] 

I’ll tell you what it sounds like, Butcher’d said, snatching MM’s phone out of his hand to type. Sounds like my fuckin’ business.

[It was you lot said I wasn’t in this deep enough. Don’t fuckin talk to me about shame.]

Great. Cheers. Good to be back.

So the cat’s outta the bag. That’s it. They’re square. Or they will be, once certain provisions are met. He doesn’t know how to feel about it. 

There’s relief, definitely, but irritation vastly overshadows it. This operation weren’t never his to lead, but at least when it started, he was in control of the parts that affected him. He set the pace and laid out the countermeasures; his body was the piece on the board opposite Homelander’s, and he could move forward or hold fast. It was up to him, as much as it could be. 

But now it’s out of his hands, and for what? Because he just did what they asked him to? Because he had to go a little off-script to get what they needed? He can’t even take all this anger he’s feeling with him to Arcadia. There wouldn’t be any point in. Homelander’s not there.

He flies out to Hugh’s instead, thinking at least seeing Terror will make him feel better. Calmer.

A resounding bark at the door answers him, and just like a shot of opium, the tension running up Butcher’s spine loosens like a screw. He knew it would.

A chain comes unbound behind the door, and then there’s Hugh Campbell pulling it open.

“You’re back! Look who’s here, Terror.”

“Hello, Son,” Butcher croons, sinking into a crouch to ruffle at Terror’s ears and jowls. “Didja miss me?”

Terror squares up with his front paws on Butcher’s thigh to lick his face. Butcher pats down his sides, every bit as besotted with his boy as he was when Terror fit in his hand. What a life they’ve had, the two of them. Butcher’s missed a lot of it, wasted so many of their years together chasing shadows and striking at empty space. He regrets that, but at the same time, he can’t see any way it could’ve been different.

“How was he, Hugh?”

“Perfect. Very well-behaved.”

“Yeah?” Butcher tips his head back to look all the way up at Hugh.

He’s leaned up against the doorjamb in a jumper the color of oatmeal, and he’s only got eyes for Terror. The soft smile on his face flickers once he catches Butcher staring at him. It’s only then that he starts to fidget.

“I’ve got some toys Hughie left.” Already beating a hasty retreat inside to rummage around collecting things off the floor, Hugh calls over his shoulder, “This’ll only take a minute! Please come in! I won’t keep you, I know you must be anxious to get home.”

“Do I look anxious?” Billy muses, stepping over the threshold and pulling the door shut behind him.

Hugh laughs, flustered, and misses several times trying to shove a fabric giraffe into a plastic shopping bag. “Not that I’ve ever seen, and that’s including the time we were fugitives.”

As soon as he’s sure Butcher ain’t leaving again, Terror takes it upon himself to investigate the toy situation. He noses at Hugh’s leg until he surrenders the giraffe. Butcher thinks it must be a favorite of his, but then Terror drops it and stares at Hugh until he sighs, reaches down into the bag, and reveals a rope toy made to look like a sloth. Terror parks it right on Hugh’s foot and watches the toy.

“Oh, fine. You win.”

Terror catches the toy as it’s tossed to him, and Butcher catches a laugh with his hand. Hugh shrugs, pink in the face and smiling bashfully.

“Uh, how ‘bout some tea?”

“Love some,” Butcher says, and sits on the couch.

It’s been a minute since his shoulders felt tight like this. Used to be his normal, but coming home after a long holiday, he’s having a harder time shaking it off than he usually does. Hugh returns from the kitchen with a thumb hooked over his shoulder.

“Kettle’s on. How was your trip, Billy?”

“Restful.” He throws an arm along the back of the couch and drops his head back to meet Hugh’s eyes. “Divisive.”

“Oh,” Hugh says, nodding, sitting down, and then standing up again. “Uh.” He sits down. “Divisive, really? In what way? If you don’t mind my asking.”

Butcher shrugs grandly. “Strange bedfellows.”

“Ah.” Hugh palms the back of his neck. “You mean, you and Homelander?”

“Hughie tell you about that?” Butcher muses, beginning to smirk.

“Ryan let it slip at the dog park one day. He’s a great kid. Really thinks the world of you.”

Butcher looks away under the pretense of watching Terror gnaw on the sloth’s rope limbs. Hugh nods, though Butcher ain’t said nothing, and clasps his hands together. It changes the look of him somehow, so that the earnest expression he’s got bleeds into the rest of the way he’s holding himself. However flighty he might’ve seemed a minute ago, now he only looks grounded, stable. When he manages to catch Butcher’s eyes again, he holds him in his gaze easily. 

Makes sense. Where’d Butcher think Hughie got his occasional self-possession from? The side what didn’t stick around? Nah, composure like that comes of putting down roots. It comes of hard work and waiting, and perseverance. 

And where else did Butcher think Hughie got his ability to parse through bullshit, no matter if he’d always thought Hughie’s knack for it was specific to him and not a genetic quirk?

“Don’t suppose that was all he told you?”

“He said a little about his mom. Seemed like he needed someone to talk to.” He hops up at the whistling from the kitchen and continues over his shoulder, “I remember when Hughie was his age. He had something to say about everything. Usually it seemed like enough just to be there, but I wonder now if I was letting him down in some way.”

“How d’you mean?”

“Well, you know.” There’s some clanging of utensils and ceramics, a cupboard opening and shutting. Hugh walks back out with two mugs, little carded strings dangling over the sides. He hands one to Butcher. “Oh, I used a bit of honey. Is that all right?”

Butcher breathes in the steam billowing out around his face. Chamomile.

“It’s good, thanks.”

“Ah, what was I saying?”

“You were tellin’ me about Hughie’s gab phase, and you were sittin’ next to me,” Butcher says, taking hold of a boxy throw pillow and tossing it to the other end of the couch.

Mention of Hughie must clear out the last of his nerves because he don’t faff about. He just sits.

“Oh, yeah. Hughie just, y’know, he had all these ideas. He was so sure of himself. I remember hoping he’d find a way to hold onto that for as long as he could. Now that we’re both older and on the other side of it, I don’t know if he knew that was always my dream for him.”

Thinking only of his bastard father, Butcher says, “Would’ve only got him so far, the things you wanted for him.”

“Hmm?”

“He had to choose what kinda man to be. I know about deadbeat dads, Hugh. If anyone let him down, it weren’t you.”

“I appreciate that.” Hugh smiles, small, embarrassed, and takes a sip of his tea. “And for what it’s worth, regardless of whatever else is going on here that you can’t tell me about, I know you’ve done your best. With Ryan. It can’t have been easy for you.”

“I ain’t his dad,” Butcher tells him, gently.

“Maybe not,” Hugh allows, tipping his head. “But you love him, don’t you?”

“Yeah. Yeah, I do.”

Hugh’s smiles softens somehow, and his eyes go tender-like when he adds, “I’m sorry, by the way. About Becca.”

Butcher looks down at his tea and fiddles with the string. Terror comes over and butts his head up against Butcher’s knee, and Butcher, grateful for the belated excuse to have something to do with his hands, sets about ruffling at Terror’s ears.

“Thanks.” He knuckles at his eye. “Christ, was there anything Ryan didn’t tell you?”

“There was one thing he didn’t seem so sure about,” Hugh says, setting his tea on the coffee table to scratch at a spot on Terror’s ribs.

Like clockwork, Terror’s leg starts to kick at empty air.

“What’s that?”

“He told me he couldn’t understand why his parents didn’t get along, or why they didn’t live together.” Hugh shakes his head. “Just, he mentioned that since you like his dad so much, he thought his mom… must not’ve had a good reason for feeling the way she did about him.”

It’s like a gun’s gone off right next to his ear. He can’t breathe for how fully made of noise he is.

“I’m sorry, I shouldn’t have told you that.”

He waves his hand, but he still can’t speak. Should’ve seen this coming, shouldn’t he? Or had he really thought none of this would ever have to touch Ryan? And more to the point, did he think there’d never come a day when Ryan would learn exactly what kind of man his father really was?

All Butcher’s done is make that day, when it comes, so much more impossible to explain. He already didn’t think he could tell Ryan the truth about Becca’s rape, but now? He couldn’t have made it any more fucking confusing for Ryan if he’d made a conscious effort.

Swallowing down his queasiness, he says, “I didn’t mean for none of this to happen, Hugh.”

Hugh nods, sympathetic and without a bit of judgment. “What do you think you’ll do about it?”

“What I have to.”

Another nod, and his eyes are like the grave just now, Butcher thinks; serious, seeing past the conundrum and out to what Butcher’s handling of it will convey about his character when all’s said and done. He knows a watershed moment when he sees one. The time to choose’ll be on him soon, and when it is, there ain’t no other way this can go down.

And yet.

“What’s in a man what don’t have a soul, d’you think?”

“I don’t understand.”

“Like,” Butcher holds up his hands, “if you believe in souls, or if you believe we all got summat inside what makes us people, and you have a man without it, without that thing in him that makes him a person, what’s he got in place of that? If his whole life’s about fillin’ in that hole, but nothin’ else fits there that’ll do the job a soul does, what d’you think’s in a man like that?”

“Drive?” Hugh guesses, looking at Butcher in a way that serves as its own answer.

“I don’t mean me.”

Hugh clasps his hands together, and then he says, earnestly, “Drive?”

Butcher snorts. Yeah, he walked into that one. 

“Why do you ask, Billy?”

He shrugs. “He said that to me once, Homelander did. That maybe I had one after all, and I said maybe we both did. Not ‘cuz I believed it or ‘cuz I give a fuck about my soul or anyone else’s; I don’t. But right then, in that moment, it felt like the truth, and I could see we was really just two sides of the same coin. That we always had been.”

He doesn’t add that it was the looming proximity of his own demise, slow and debasing and excruciating, that provided the necessary clarity behind that particular revelation.

“Billy, are you sure… hi, sweetheart,” Hugh croons, scratching Terror’s ears when he trades Butcher’s knee out for Hugh’s thigh. “Are you sure you have to go through with it? Whatever ‘it’ is?”

“Yeah, I am.”

Butcher ruffles at Terror’s ear, letting his fingers brush against Hugh’s in the process. Not to spook him or rile him up for a laugh, but just for that taste of closeness that don’t come with quite so many strings attached. Hugh, maybe because he’s a dad, or maybe because he’s always been a gentle man, even before Hughie, covers Butcher’s hand with his own and squeezes.

“Will you be okay?” he asks quietly.

“Yeah.”

Hugh studies him. “And are you?”

He aims for brusque but falls short at painfully raw and naked, as he says, “Not hardly.”

Wild how much Hugh looks just like his son when he’s wearing his broken heart on his face. He palms Butcher’s shoulder and then the back of his neck, and if it was Homelander, Butcher can’t help thinking, this would be when he’d go in for the kiss, either of them; Butcher to distract Homelander or to keep either one of them from talking, or Homelander to catch Butcher in a moment and preserve him inside it like an ant trapped in amber.

But Hugh doesn’t try to kiss Butcher. Of course he doesn’t; Butcher has the distinct feeling Hugh’s the type to ask permission when it’s the first time, and isn’t that funny? Isn’t it strange to think on how preciously and undeniably wonderful it would be to lay one on Hugh when he’s too polite to even consider it? 

Hugh, though. He don’t seize on Butcher’s vulnerability, or try to twist it so he can use it for his own gain. All he does, is pull him in for a hug. He’s warm from the tea and from his jumper, and he smells like apples and cardamom.

“It’ll all work out.”

Butcher even believes him, or nearly. It’s enough, in any case, to unravel the rest of the tension between his shoulders. He gives Hugh a cautious squeeze, but he can tell by his short, startled inhale that it’s too tight anyway.

“Sorry.”

But Hugh just laughs and gives Billy a pat on the back. “Annie crushes me all the time. I’m used to it.”

Leaning back, Butcher gives Hugh’s shoulder — not a squeeze — a nudge. He lets his smile slip just the tiniest smidgeon into a leer to say, “Y’know, I weren’t joking before.”

He doesn’t have time to clarify that statement at all before Hugh’s cheeks fill with color and his eyes get a little rounder. It’d been funny the first time, but he don’t feel like laughing now. All he feels, now, is warm.

“Aren’t you with—?”

“It’s complicated,” Butcher says, brushing his lips against Hugh’s cheek. “It’s really fucking complicated.”

“It shouldn’t be. Oh, I — I-I’m not criticizing you, but… well, it shouldn’t be. I wouldn’t want it to be, and you’ve… oh, Billy, you’ve got a lot on your plate right now.”

“Is that the only reason?” he says, pinching Hugh’s chin between his fingers.

“W-well,” Hugh gasps, “I don’t really think I could keep up with you.”

“I don’t mind doin’ most of the work. ‘Sides, I think you’d be generous in other ways.”

Hugh flushes full-on tomato red at that, but he’s remarkably composed other than that. “Sure, but…”

Butcher smiles at that, tickled at the serene confidence of the answer paired with the undercurrent of humility powering it up. He turns his head but catches himself just shy of making contact, conscious of the fact that while Hugh ain’t said no, he hasn’t said yes either, and really, he should’ve backed off by now. It’s just nice, is all. 

In spite of the clusterfuck that is his life currently, and in spite of the thrashing Hughie’ll give him if he catches wind of Butcher putting the moves on his dad again, it don’t feel a bit complicated. Not like everything with Homelander does.

Hugh touches his cheek, but not to pull him in. 

“You have an impossible decision to make,” he whispers, edging away so that they aren’t mingling breath anymore. “I can see how much it’s weighing on you. Sometime, when that’s not the case, and if you still want to, we can try then. Okay?”

That pulse of steady, calm warmth behind Butcher’s ribs lingers, not the least bit discouraged.

“All right. Raincheck.”

“Raincheck,” Hugh echoes with a smile.

I’m gonna fuck Hughie’s dad, Butcher thinks, and then, deviously, I’m gonna let him fuck me.

But then he passes the rest of the afternoon without a single thought for how to get Hugh to fall into bed with him, and that’s easy, too. Surprisingly so.

They have another cup of tea, take Terror for a walk around the block, and catch the last twenty minutes of a college theatre production of The Tempest over banh mi in the park. The last time Butcher was anywhere near Shakespeare, he was still in school. He hadn’t cared much for it then, but it’s something else seeing Hugh’s eyes light up at one of the set pieces near the end. It’s a brilliant piece of staging, utilizing the horizon and time of day to make the scene look as much like magic as it possibly can, even before the fireworks kick in.

It’s contagious, Hugh’s joy.

“That’s great,” he says, grinning without much, or perhaps any, thought for how it transforms his whole face. He’s not a bad-lookin’ man, Hugh, but he’s handsomer when he smiles like that. Makes him look so much more alive.

“Like a bit of pyrotechnics, do ya?”

“Hmm? Oh. No, I meant — okay, yes, a little. Who doesn’t like fireworks?” Hugh grins, finally comfortable enough not to be self-conscious about how much fun he’s having. “But no, I meant, the story. The wizard let him go in the end. I think that’s great.”

It gets Butcher thinking about all the ways freedom can be given away or taken back, and he wonders, for the first time, if there’s any place for that kind of thinking up against what he’s trying to do with Homelander. He wonders if there’s anywhere to put it, or a way to act on it to make it a reality.

Maybe. Maybe.

He drops Hugh off at home, thinks about hitting on him but restrains himself, and walks Terror back to the flat, a bag of chew toys slung around his wrist. Butcher upends the bag near Terror’s dog bed, fights with his clothes, and drops into bed. Exhaustion sinks into him fast from there, and almost the second his head hits the pillow, he’s asleep. 

A few hours later, tapping at the window snatches him clean out of his REM cycle. Terror raises his head in the direction of the noise, but he don’t kick up any more fuss than that.

“Terror,” Butcher mumbles, muzzy and slow from dreaming. “Attack.”

Terror looks at him and doesn’t get up from his bed.

“Bugger.” Groaning, Butcher tosses back the blanket and gets out of bed. “See if you get a treat with breakfast.”

He already knows what he’s gonna find at the window. He figures Terror does, too, or else he’d at least be growling. As it is, he’s been smelling this visitor all over Butcher since he came home, so he ain’t got cause to worry, but then, Butcher figures neither does he. The last time this visitor came to his window, he didn’t wait to be invited in.

Butcher unlatches the window and opens it a crack to see who else but Homelander draped against the building six floors up from the ground. He’s wearing one of Butcher’s shirts buttoned up to his throat like a twat.

“Where’d you get that,” he says, in lieu of hello or what the fuck or I was dreaming about you.

“Apparently, I had some of young Hughie’s things in my closet at home,” Homelander muses, tossing something at Butcher that he’s forced to fumble and catch.

It’s a baseball.

“What’s this?”

“Fly-ball,” Homelander explains, pretty officiously for someone who sounds like he’s never gone to a ballgame for fun in his life. “I caught it.”

“Congratulations.”

Homelander starts to smile, and Butcher rolls his eyes, pulling the window fully open.

“Take it you didn’t really come here just to brag,” he says, turning his back to the window in time to see Terror trundle around the end of the bed to peer at Homelander. “Terror, attack,” he says again, pointing.

Terror sneezes.

“Good boy,” Homelander says over Butcher’s shoulder.

Obviously bored with them, Terror trots out to the kitchen. Old age has made him keen on knowing where he can get the most uninterrupted sleep, and Butcher loves that for him. 

Ever since the Temp V fucked Butcher up and he had to spend some nights sleeping upright at the table just to keep from drowning in his own pus, Terror’s used to being in the kitchen if he can’t be in the bedroom. He even has a second dog bed under the table just for the rare occasion that he needs it, spoiled lad.

“Some attack dog you have there, Billy.”

“Always was too sweet,” Butcher says, in the midst of getting pushed back onto the bed. The baseball falls out of his hand and bounces on the floor.

“Well, you’re plenty vicious on your own.”

“Pot, kettle.”

With a disinterested hum, Homelander presses Butcher flat and licks into his mouth. He don’t have any excuse for how that lights him up from the inside out. 

Oh, chemicals. Fucking biology. Proximity and familiarity. But that don’t cover it.

It’s disloyalty is what it is — disloyalty against the boys, against Becca, even against Ryan, when he lets himself really think about it. ‘Cuz it’s like MM said back at Hughie’s: Butcher leaves. He turns tail and gets out, to save himself or to save someone else the trouble of following him down into hell, but he leaves. That’s him in a nutshell.

Making that choice has always been such a dire thing for him, and easy, in that context. Death’s been hounding him around every corner since he was a boy thinking of putting his father in the ground, but it don’t feel quite so near these days as it used to. And in that light? His usual system of wrecking his life in the most explosive way possible don’t seem half as palatable as it usually does.

He don’t know what to do with it. The last person he wanted to live for was Becca, and if she could see him now, laying down with the man who blew their life to pieces, what would she say? Could she even stand to look at Butcher if she knew what was in his heart right now?

Could he stand to look at her?

“What’s wrong?” Homelander pulls back to balance on his hands where they’re braced on either side of Butcher’s head. “Bored of me already?”

Butcher tips Homelander off and over to lean over top of him. He says, “I was asleep.”

“And then I came and woke you up,” he hums, cradling Butcher’s cheek in his hand. Then in that ruminative way that he does when he’s saying things that sound like they’ve come out of books Butcher’s never read, he adds, “You didn’t plan for that, did you?”

“Who knew you’d have it in ya,” Butcher mutters, trying to pass this comment off as unimportant, as not even interesting enough to faze him, but then—

Eyes twinkling and mouth twitching at one end in a triumphant smile, Homelander just says, “Who knew.”

Butcher deflates, dropping his head in the hollow of Homelander’s throat. He doesn’t know when or how that part happened. Was it at Arcadia that they were learning to read each other like this? Or did it happen on that beach in Agriomandra? Or was it even earlier than all of this?

“We fucked every day for three weeks,” Butcher says.

“Well, you can’t have too much of a good thing.”

“John, I’m knackered.”

“Then sleep. I’ll be here.”

“What about Ryan?”

“He’s out for the night.” Homelander hesitates, or maybe he’s just getting comfortable, rearranging himself around Butcher’s body, in Butcher’s clothes, in Butcher’s bed. In his fucking life. “The noise and the crowds tuckered him out.”

“Good,” Butcher mutters on a long, slow exhale.

Homelander presses his chin against the crown of Butcher’s head. The constant beating of his heart lulls Butcher back to sleep.

As if from very far away, he thinks he hears Homelander say, “I want this. I want—”

Butcher doesn’t hear the rest. He spirals down into a dream about a spirit cut out of a tree and bound to a man by magic; about bare feet gunning it through wet grass headed straight for the setting sun; about flying up into a darkening sky full of stars and dissolving in the crisp, clear night air like so much gunpowder and smoke.

Chapter End Notes

I just want to take a second to formally/informally announce here that I intend for Tyrants to be my last contribution writing for fandoms. It's been a fun ten years, but it's time to put up the mantle. You should know going forward that I don't give any single solitary kind of fuck. We are going off the rails full throttle to finish this.

Buckle up or get off the ride. You've been warned.

For anyone that's still here, let's have some fun one more time and make it count. I love you.

Chapter 14: out of the belly of Sheol

Chapter Notes

Trigger warning for Becca's rape. Nothing graphic, but it is talked about. Please be cautious if you think mentions of it here will be in any way harmful for you. <3

See the end of the chapter for more notes

Homelander stares for a long time at the portrait of himself outside Madelyn’s office. He used to peer through it to look in at her, but it’s been years now since such a thing was possible. 

Now there’s only him, an empty office, and a lonely photograph, white-gold ring lights caught in his eyes like manacles for the soul. Did he always look so much like a doll in the full Vought regalia with his hair combed back? He raises an ungloved hand to his own hair, in need of a cut, and then to his cheek, in need of a shave. 

Why he bothered to come here before he’d seen to these basic needs, he can’t rightly say except that he likes the departure from form. It’s comfortable in a way he used to sneer at, the ability to melt into the scenery and go unnoticed. No one looks to him when he’s in plainclothes half a size too big for him. No one seeks miracles, or atrocities, of his pale, slender hands.

He’s a ghost, nearly. As close to normal as he’s ever been in all his life.

It doesn’t last. Someone comes whistling around the corner, and Homelander’s annoyed before he even sees him properly. True to his congenial and ultimately useless nature, Deep spots him standing around and comes to investigate before he’s assessed the situation.

“Sir, this is a restricted area. I’m gonna have to ask you to move it along.”

Homelander gives him a pained, reluctant glance out the corner of his eye, and much faster than expected, Deep’s eyes go wide with recognition. His boots squeak on the floor as his pace grinds to a halt.

“Oh…h-hey, ah.” He blinks, owlish. “Homelander?”

He turns to face his portrait again, saying nothing, trusting that even a simpleton like Deep will see that he wants to be left alone. Contrary to what he believes about Deep’s self-preservation skills, Deep carefully sidles in next to him. Seems he does at the very least understand how much danger he’s putting himself in by not walking away while he has an out. Or perhaps, given that he’s never willingly walked away in the past, it isn’t the presence of danger he’s detecting but something else he perceives in the yawning quiet between them. Unbidden, a smirk twitches at Homelander’s lips. Trust Deep to see an ally in him — an equal.

“Liking the new look. It’s very, uh, modern. For you.”

Homelander doesn’t reply.

“I saw that you were trending. We all thought it was a PR thing or a merger with some new brand in Greece, but then A-Train pointed out your…friend? I thought for sure Ashley’s head was gonna spin when she saw.” He laughs meekly, obviously nervous, though that doesn’t prevent him from trying to dig around for more. In a sly undertone, he asks, “Does anyone else know you’re stateside?”

He makes no move to answer, and Deep nods, gleaning the correct assumption.

“Guess this means you’re back.” He waffles in the blistering silence and bounces anxiously on his heels. “There any reason you, uh, haven’t told anyone?”

“Should I have texted?” Homelander says flatly, aware of how intently Deep’s looking at him.

“Be kind of a magic trick, what with your whole thing, about—” Deep fidgets briefly before forcefully pocketing his hands. “How it…ruins the line of your suit,” he finishes weakly.

Homelander looks at him sharply. “What?”

Floundering beneath his full attention, clearly not having anticipated it, Deep stammers out, “Yeah, it — you said that, once. We were at a thing.”

“A thing.”

“Yeah, a Vought thing,” Deep clarifies, desperation in his voice and in his big, round eyes. “With — it was a crossover event. What was it, Armani? There was a runway, models, a mini red carpet.” He relaxes then, probably seeing Homelander search his memory. “There was this reporter, I swear she was trying to pick you up. She asked if you were on any dating apps.”

It strikes him then what Deep’s talking about. The reporter in question was bouncy and pretty enough, in a plasticine kind of way he tended to like more in his twenties. He and Maeve had just split. Her absence at his side had been palpable, more vibrant to him than her actual presence usually registered. 

“I remember.”

“Guess that was just something you said,” Deep says, with a self-deprecating laugh.

“Marketing came up with it.”

“Oh.” Deep colors, a ruddy red rising toward his hairline. He bounces on his heels. “Y-yeah, Marketing writes all my one-liners, too.”

Silence settles between them, fraught and loud, before Deep breaks it.

“So what’s the real reason?” When his question only deepens the chasm between them, he adds, “For why you’ve never had a phone, I mean.”

He never had anyone to call, before.

“I could be anywhere in a moment,” he answers instead, deflecting away from the thing he doesn’t care for Deep to see. “I could hear anyone in a moment.”

Seemingly unfazed by this, Deep says, “Yeah. That’s not really why you text, though.”

Homelander gives him a look, and he can feel it in the way Deep doesn’t quail at all that it lacks his wonted ferocity.

“Look,” Deep says, taking his phone out of his pocket and opening a conversation with someone called Daryl. He taps out a concise ‘yo dude wyd’ and hits send. After a few seconds, the typing… alert dances across the bottom of the chat. “I don’t have to stop what I’m doing or anything. It’s just casual.”

A crystalline chime sounds. They both look down to read the text.

 

Daryl
You can’t see the cuttlefish again, man. They look at me weird now.

 

“You’re right, I’m having a blast.”

Deep flushes, but he gives Homelander a considering look that brightens with revelation. “You could text Ryan! Ashley gave him a phone when he was staying in your room,” he says, trailing off while he thumbs through his contacts. 

He selects Other Ashley’s number, opens the front-facing camera, and snaps a picture of them that drops automatically into the chat. Deep hits send, and this time, the text goes unread longer than before, his peace sign and Homelander’s flat stare looking out at them hollowly. Deep hits send, and this time, the text goes unread longer than before. 

“Well, maybe he’s watching TV or something.”

Homelander scrolls up in the chat and skims through a one-sided and uninspired attempt at smalltalk from Deep’s side. He finds a selfie near the beginning of the conversation, mild for Deep, but still.

Following his train of thought, Deep says, “They did a factory reset. So, uh. Yeah.”

The phone chimes again.

 

Other Ashley
Dad,?? Did you get a puone?

 

Homelander stares at the screen, the tension behind his sternum unraveling like wrinkles in a fabric exposed to steam. He’s still holding it away from himself, fingers deliberately tucked away from the touch screen.

“You can write him back, dude.”

“On your phone,” Homelander echoes, a little inanely, admittedly, but he can’t follow Deep’s train of thought.

“I don’t care if you hang onto it. Just gets me into trouble anyway.” Deep shrugs, hunching into himself slightly with newfound bashfulness as the phone continues to chime in Homelander’s hand. “I saw him for a minute when Ashley brought him in. He’s a nice kid. It was kinda cool. I mean…seeing what coulda been, y’know?”

Sparing a few more seconds to pare Deep down with an excoriating stare, he tears his eyes away briefly to type a brisk reply for Ryan. Only long enough to give him a clear answer before pocketing the phone.

“What do you get out of it?”

“Huh? What do you mean?”

The phone chimes twice in Homelander’s pocket. He ignores it to stay on Deep.

“What,” he echoes, “do you get,” softly, menacingly, “out of it.”

“Well…” Deep wets his lips. “Okay. There is one thing. See, the new look, the new, um…thing that you’ve got?”

When this draws no reaction from him, Deep visibly trembles. He tilts his head, beseeching with his eyes not to be made to give voice to the self-evident, but damning, thing they’re both thinking. Homelander gives no quarter. He hasn’t felt his temper rise once throughout this entire exchange. It would assuage his restlessness to have a reason.

“C’mon, man, you know what I’m gonna say.”

“Do I?”

Caught out and frantic not to be, it explodes out of him. He says, “You and Billy Butcher, dude.” Looking terrified out of his mind, Deep rushes to add, “You like him, don’t you?”

Homelander takes a step toward him, and though he quails, Deep holds his ground.

“Are you going somewhere with this?” he asks, keeping his tone honeyed and slow — all the better to tempt Deep toward a punishable misstep.

This gambit is one Deep knows well, though, and he reacts accordingly. Like an animal in a predator’s sights, he goes still all over, the only mobile parts of him being his jumping Adam’s apple and his eyes. He gives Homelander a conspicuous once-over, searching out something Homelander’s not convinced of his ability to find.

Or, he isn’t. Until Deep goes and names it.

“It’s just, I never thought I’d see you of all people go native.”

A flinch spikes through him, and seeing it, Deep falters, inching away from him. Homelander clenches his jaw tightly, enamel creaking in his molars.

It had pleased him some to hear Butcher’s friends accuse of him of this very thing; delighted him to think he was coaxing Butcher across a dividing line demarcating his side from Homelander’s. But to hear Deep say it, too, he hasn’t been pulling true. Neither has Butcher, if they’ve met somewhere in the middle — if neither of them has come out unscathed from this wild experiment of theirs. It’s unacceptable, but even so, dizzying to think about. He can’t quantify all of what it makes him feel, nor does he care to try when Deep’s still watching him so closely.

“It’s just that…I mean, you’d only do that if…if it was better with him, right?” He swallows again. “Hey, I’ve always done for you, haven’t I? Whatever you need. For — sometimes for bad stuff, and things I didn’t wanna do, but I did ‘em, man. And all that…fucking chess you’ve been playing for as long as I’ve known you, it only ever made you miserable, dude.”

The phone in his pocket chimes. He bites his cheek and opens the string of texts from Ryan to see assorted cat pictures and looping gifs of confetti cannons. 

The last text from him is a bitmoji crafted in Ryan’s likeness, waving out at them from the screen. Deep presses and holds a spot on the screen until a prompt populates. He sends back a heart that attaches itself to Ryan’s avatar.

“I know we’re not friends, Homelander,” Deep says, holding his hands out. “I know I did all that terrible shit for you because I’m just someone you handle, but it goes both ways.”

“Oh, you think you handle me, do you?”

Deep squares up, stands a mite taller. “You’re standing here talking to me, aren’t you?”

The corner of Homelander’s mouth twitches, this time closer to a smile than a smirk. Maybe he’s still riding the wave of boosted serotonin from the last time he saw Butcher and that’s why he hasn’t already walked away from this. Has to be. It couldn’t possibly be that Deep knows him, even if they have passed years and secrets in this tower.

“If we were just regular people,” Deep says, and then, amending, “if you were just a regular person, I’d tell you I’m glad.” He looks uncomfortable then, shifting his weight between his feet. “I’d say it’s about time you got to have something outside of…” His eyes skitter away from Homelander’s and catch on the portrait. “This.”

“And since that isn’t the case, what will you say instead?”

Hurt registers in Deep’s sad fishbowl eyes. He pockets his hands, saying, quietly, “I’ve always done for you, man. For better or worse.”

Homelander tips his chin thoughtfully. The phone in his hand chimes. He glances down at the screen display, expecting to see Other Ashley’s name displayed. An unsaved number winks up at him, the accompanying text reading: This a private line, John?

He opens the text, heartbeat already beginning to quicken.

“For better or worse you say,” he murmurs, thumb hovering over the keyboard.

“I’m your wingman, dude,” Deep says, brightening some. Going with a word that’s altogether kinder than any Homelander would choose to describe him. “Between you and me?” He angles his chin toward the phone, keeping his eyes averted from the screen. “Having a guaranteed place to put my dick solved like, ninety percent of my problems. When I was married, I mean.”

Private enough, he says via text. Outwardly, he says, “The other ten percent was you wanted to fuck fish.”

“She wouldn’t even try to accommodate our needs—” Deep deflates. “You don’t care.”

“I don’t care.”

Private nd you aint demanded a dick pic yet? Butcher replies. Dnt believe it.

“I’m just saying,” Deep pushes, maybe genuinely not hearing that Homelander’s half-forgotten him already. 

He leans in so their shoulders nearly brush, on the verge of companionable. Deep came to the Seven too late for them to ever have a true friendship, but there was a time Homelander pretended in earnest. It was no burden to him then to play the part. Why would it have been? There was nothing for him outside of the charade, for so long.

Another text comes through.

 

Unknown
 Show me a good time tonight.

 

“Keeping you happy’s in my best interest. Besides, this is way less violent than the other stuff you’ve wanted my help with.”

Homelander considers the text winking up at him, an idea sparking. He looks at Deep, and some of what’s struck him as far as inspiration must flash in his eyes because Deep stiffens and takes a half-step away from him. The satisfaction it brings can’t come close to anger, but it appeases him. 

“Well,” he murmurs, beginning to grin even as he responds to Butcher. “Aren’t you just full of good ideas today.”

Pick you up at your place or at work?

And Butcher: Surprise me.

Laughing, Homelander says, “Well, you heard the man. Orders are orders.”

Throat clicking around a swallow, Deep jolts under the hand Homelander drops at his shoulder. Together the walk down the corridor, set on a course to redraw that dividing line separating all that makes him from all that makes Butcher. It’ll be easily done. They’ve always been more alike than either of them have ever cared to admit. But tonight? Tonight, they’re going to celebrate. 

Tonight, he thinks, warm all over and shimmering with it, is the night he gets what he wants. The night Butcher does, too.

 

/

 

Butcher drops his phone in his lap, the stench of scorched wires and melted plastic filling his nose. The singed concrete and humming metal lingers like a piece of music hanging in the air. His eyes ache like the beginning of a migraine, and then, as a call comes in from Hughie, the pounding in his head starts. He lets it ring out a couple times, then picks it up.

“I’m sorry, Butcher,” Hughie says without preamble.

He can’t speak yet. He don’t know how. The afterimages seared into his eyes blot out everything else.

“We didn’t want you to have to see it, but you’d’ve been pissed if we kept it from you.”

The segment of back wall between the ceiling of the storage unit and the hole Butcher rent in it with his eyes creaks, groans, and collapses in on itself. Butcher drops his head back against the beanbag chair, the only survivor left standing in the wake of his wrath. He sinks his hand into his pocket, unearthing the round, cherry red capsule Frenchie presented him with this morning over at the office. Butcher’s familiar enough with his handiwork by now that he recognizes the cochineal extract Frenchie’s partial to when he’s working with colors.

MM comes onto the line. Must be Hughie switched over to speaker.

“Hey, man. You all right?”

He just watched the rape of his wife by the guy he’s been fucking. Saying he ain’t all right just now is a bit of a goddamn understatement. 

“Did you see it?”

“No,” Hughie says.

MM: “Fuck no.”

And Frenchie: “Absolument pas.”

Butcher presses his fingers against his eyes, keeping lasers, tears, and a monster of a migraine at bay. He manages not to raze the rest of the storage facility to the ground, with molten radiation or with salt, but he can’t stop the migraine creeping up on him. The coating for the anti-V capsule in his hand smears his palm a bloody red when he can finally unclench his fist. Beneath his head, the polystyrene beans shift and rustle.

Billy, he’d heard Becca say on the video, after the first time. A plea and an apology rolled into one, spiking goosebumps all down his arms and the back of his neck. 

And Homelander, who’d heard her, too, had only said, There’s only me here. He can’t hear you.

Except Butcher had heard her. Death hadn’t been enough to stop his name on her lips from reaching him one last time. It hadn’t been enough to stop the immediate calm in him when he got Ryan’s text either. Loving Becca, now and always, gave him clarity. Gave him direction. Resolve.

It meant he could copy the contact info Ryan forwarded him and open a new text. It meant he could make a date with the devil just as easily as he’d made the deal that started all this. For Becca.

He rubs a finger into the red blot staining his skin.

“Monsieur Charcutier,” Frenchie tries. “What can we do?”

Knuckling again at his eye, Butcher tries to cram all of what he feels into the tight little box it usually fits inside of. He tells himself the water in his eyes is grit or dust from the pulverized concrete, or a burnt eyelash, but he knows better. So do the boys.

MM says, “Butcher—”

“She called my name.”

Hughie this time: “Becca did? You mean, when…?”

“Yeah,” he grits out, thumbing the little capsule into its case and pocketing it for later. “When.”

“Shit,” MM mutters, like the thought of what Butcher’s telling him hurts him — and yeah, he knows the feeling. “God fucking damn it, man.”

The anger’s good. It’s familiar, clarifying. He’d deny ever needing a reminder about what it was he’s been trying to do here, but the kick in the dick helps all the same.

“What’re you gonna do?” Hughie asks.

Pulling up an unread message on his phone, Butcher murmurs, “Got a date tonight.”

 

Unsaved
Careful what you wish for, Billy.

 

“Attention à the compound if you mean to activate it,” Frenchie warns. “Remember that it is a gas at room temperature. Very volatile. If you are not clear when the capsule breaks, it will affect you as before.”

He’ll take those consequences if it comes to that. He only needs the V in his system to fend off attacks; he doesn’t need it to kill a supe. Never did, Butcher. And right about now, he’s not so worried about whether he makes it out of this. 

It’s too bad, that. He was just getting used to this whole wanting to live thing. Now all he wants is a fight or a hard fuck. Anything to take him out of this moment, out of this den of darkness and smoke that he built for himself. He pushes up off the beanbag chair, restless, and flies up through the gutted hole in the ceiling.

“By the by, we’re gonna be needing a new unit.”

“Is the beanbag chair okay?” Hughie asks, frankly, with way too much concern in his voice.

Butcher rolls his eyes. “She’s fine. Come get her if you want her.”

Frenchie: “Where are you going?”

He doesn’t know. He’d left it open for Homelander to come and find him whenever, and that means, until he does, nowhere’s safe. Arcadia’s a good candidate, and it’s been a minute since he popped round, but the idea of going now gets under his skin for a reason he doesn’t like. That leaves him all outta answers, hesitation giving way to silence as telling as it is complete.

“Butcher,” Hughie tries, when it’s clear no answer’s coming. “Give us something here. How do we help you?”

He swallows down a scream and the string of curses wanting to trail after it like bile. Bad enough he can feel Hughie’s kind eyes and sympathy from here; he don’t need to hear it in his voice on top of that. It makes him antsy for unkindness, in any kinda way he can get his hands on it.

“Make like I’m a gun and point me at someone,” he rasps.

Maybe Hughie’d ordinarily be alarmed at the request, but straight away, Butcher hears the clacking of the phone’s keyboard. 

“Say less, man,” Hughie replies, and he sounds like he’s smiling.

Butcher feels his eyebrows draw down and checks his phone when the screen flashes against his cheek. There’s a text waiting for him: a street address, a series of numbers, and a weblink. He opens the last of these and stares. A snarl of something like fury that isn’t fury tangles in his chest. 

“Hughie, what the fuck is this?”

A paragon of innocence, Hughie says, “Insight used to be an underwear model, apparently.”

“That’s photoshopped, right?” MM asks, a little ways away from the phone.

Over the rapid onslaught of keystrokes, Frenchie says, “Non, that bulge is — qu’est-ce que c’est — au natural.”

Butcher sighs, already touching down to the sidewalk. 

He’d like to know what Hughie’s trick is for always pulling the cleverest solution to any problem straight from the hip. Butcher’s already steadier in his skin than he was five minutes ago, and even though his eyes still sting from more than just the wind, he can roll the tension out of his shoulders long enough to say, “I’m still gonna fuck your dad.”

Hughie squawks. The last thing Butcher hears as he’s hanging up is Frenchie and MM cackling in the background.

On the train out to Manhattan, Butcher swaps out Insight’s nudie pictures for his supe stats. Blue eyes peer out of a picture perfect headshot. Great-looking guy, or he used to be. Butcher hasn’t seen him since Homelander burnt his eyes out of his head. He’s thought, in passing, that maybe the V would’ve healed it up for him, but the way it looked — the way it smelled — Butcher hadn’t entertained the idea for long before dismissing it.

When he’s riding the escalator up out of the subway terminal at the necessary stop, he starts a litany in his head, broadcasting as loudly as he can.

 

You there, Forester? I’m thinking Hughie mighta called ahead since he’s got a vested interest here. He’s hoping your dick’s gonna keep me off his dad’s, see.

 

He steps out into the sunshine and hears a voice behind him.

“Is he right?”

Anticipation shimmers down his spine. He turns to see Insight half-sprawled against the safety railing, elbows perched behind him. His hair’s grown out since last time; not like the crewcut, long enough to fist and pull. Insight smirks a little from behind his sunglasses — a heavier, industrial-looking pair that covers more of where his eyes should be. Puckered scar tissue peeks around the edges, but only just.

Oh, not hardly, Butcher thinks, swaggering a little closer, letting his anger filter back up to the surface — clarified in the fifteen minutes it took the train to ferry him here, and honed like a blade. He’s pointed at Insight, but that’s just logistics; the real cut won’t land till later. Butcher’s counting on it. 

Fucking on its own ain’t a big enough vessel to hold the depths of his rage, but fucking as a knife in someone else’s ribs? Well.

Let me see if I understand, Insight thinks back. His voice is crisp and neat, the equivalent of typewriter print, in Butcher’s mind. You want him — meaning Homelander — to smell me on you the next time you see him?

Tonight, Butcher amends.

He’s still half-reclining against the railing, so when Butcher takes that last step to catch his jaw in hand, he’s standing over him. Eyes or not, Insight cants his head back in Butcher’s grip.

How ‘bout it, Forester? Wanna start a fight?

Y’know, Billy, Insight croons, switching fluidly from speaking in Butcher’s head to speaking beneath his hand: “I’ve been waiting for someone to ask.”

His mouth when Butcher kisses him tastes like mint. Pleasant enough, if Butcher didn’t find himself searching for notes of clover honey and chamomile; if he didn’t want him to smell like apples and clove rather than some sandalwood Tom Ford horse shite. Insight smirks, laughing through his nose. He doesn’t pull away, just speaks directly into Butcher’s head again.

Armed and dangerous Billy Butcher, gagging for some suburban dad with a soft midsection.

“That gonna be a problem?” Butcher asks, not bothering to deny it.

“Not for me.” Insight pushes off the safety railing and tips his head for Butcher to walk with him. Idly, hands in his pockets to match the easy slope of his shoulders and the relaxed lilt of his half-smile, he says, “I like to know what my partner wants.”

The significance of that comment doesn’t hit Butcher at the time. He just falls into step with Insight heading in the direction of Gramercy. 

Casual, like he’s asking about the weather, Insight says, “What is it about him anyway?”

Butcher ain’t the type to sing anyone’s praises, and especially not to third parties, but when the answer comes to him in just a series of disconnected memories, there’s no picking and choosing. It’s just automatic, the things that want to be remembered: Hugh’s softness, his respect for others, the quiet dignity of him that he don’t mind if people can see or not. How he ain’t out to prove or take anything. How the life he lives is a sturdy house built out of responsibility and fairness and truth, in a world of ramshackle cheapness shellacked in gold paint and glitter. How he’s sweet just to be sweet, without any thought of what it can get him.

There’s something else in Hugh that Butcher’s always been drawn to. Becca had it, and he had fallen in love with her for it. Had wanted to spend the rest of his life with her because she was a complete dork at heart. Because she made him laugh straight from his gut. Because she loved to have fun and never took herself too seriously.

He’s seen it in Hughie often enough, and now Butcher can recognize it as an artifact of his dad’s.

Insight keeps quiet, ostensibly listening in. Butcher can’t think what good it does him, except maybe to give him further fuel to mortify Hughie in case their paths cross again. It’d be worth it, if that was the only reason.

Their destination turns out to be a brick-and-steel walkup in Gramercy. Butcher don’t take any of it in. He’s still distracted thinking about Hugh in that oatmeal jumper of his when Insight shows him into an apartment on a shared floor. Butcher hauls Insight in for a rough kiss as soon as the door’s shut behind them. He bosses him over to the nearest wall and backs himself into it, setting Insight up to cage him in if he wants, to set the bar for how rowdy he wants things to get.

But Insight’s mouth gentles against his, and he don’t go for the obvious bait. Butcher’s about to shove him, or bite him, when he catches a hint of apple pie and tea in his nose. He frowns, pulls away, and—

Warm eyes peer back at him, lined at the edges with good cheer, photorealistic right down to the brown fleck in the right eye, the oatmeal jumper bringing it out all the more. His smiling mouth is a little red from kissing, a little shiny with Butcher’s spit.

Goddamn telepaths.

“Fuck do you think you’re doing?”

“What did you say to Hughie,” Insight, little shit, says, curling the syllables of the name just like Hugh does. He ruins the illusion a second later with a wry uptick to his mouth that doesn’t resemble the man himself at all, much like the words he says next. “‘I’m still gonna fuck your dad?’”

“You ain’t him,” Butcher growls, too pissed to be embarrassed — as he should be — at how protective it sounds coming out of him.

Smiling still, and it’s an Insight look, controlled hallucinations notwithstanding, Insight leans in close to kiss a high spot on Butcher’s cheek. That don’t feel a bit like something he’d ever do. Neither do his hands at Butcher’s waist, delicate and unassuming, holding Butcher like he’s precious. He nips at the spot behind Butcher’s ear and then grazes a kiss against the pulse in his neck. One of his hands slips beneath Butcher’s shirt to palm his ribs.

“You don’t wish he was here?” Insight murmurs, latching onto the join of Butcher’s neck and shoulder with gentle teeth. His hand on Butcher’s side smooths around to the small of his back, hot, hot, and then he’s adding another point of contact with his knee between Butcher’s legs. He opens his mouth against Butcher’s neck.

Butcher’s head knocks into the wall at his back with the effort of smothering a gasp. Insight holds him tightly, their bodies pressed together. 

“Stop that,” he mutters, mostly meaning it, maybe. Or partly. Wanting to mean it.

“I just want to give you what you want, Billy,” Hugh’s voice croons, self-satisfied in a way Butcher’s never heard but that feels possible, under the right circumstances. Probably feeling the jump against his leg, Insight smiles, teeth brushing and then catching against Butcher’s burning skin. “You could cut glass right now.”

If Butcher concentrates, he can feel the hard edges beneath the soft dream Insight’s giving him of Hugh. It takes him out of this, gives him a way to escape being fooled by smoke and mirrors and a pretty convincing bit of playacting. Barring all of that, he could just as easily force Insight out of his head and leave. Walk away, as if that could even remotely equate to never having gotten his dick hard from this in the first place. But Insight’s right. 

He can’t remember the last time he was this desperate from a kiss and a little bit of heavy petting. His hands open and close in the front of Insight’s jacket. 

His lips skate up to Butcher’s ear, Hugh’s voice and cadence spilling into him like sunlight.

“All I want is to take care of you.” He rocks forward, grinding his own hard dick into Butcher’s hip. “Doesn’t that sound nice?”

Resolve turning to jelly right there with his knees, Butcher feels himself nodding. 

“Want me to show you how much I want you?”

Making up his mind in the same moment that he reaches down to palm Insight’s dick through his clothes, Butcher tells him, “He wouldn’t say that.”

“My mistake,” he groans, more in his own voice, until he rights himself, even as he pushes into Butcher’s hand. He licks his lips and, with an almost bashful smile, ducks his head. “You’re pretty good at this, Billy.”

“Give ya an ‘A’ for effort,” Butcher mutters, dragging him down the hallway toward what he hopes is a bedroom.

Insight takes over and leads him through the correct door. He maintains the look of genial, lovely Hugh Campbell right down to his dad paunch. Butcher can’t remember if it’s true to life or if Insight’s exaggerating. He doesn’t care one way or another and gets his hand on it, liking what it makes him feel. Like he’s been trusted with someone who’s nothing like him, someone soft and sweet and good. Someone who’d run from him if they knew any better.

“Oh, Billy,” Insight says mournfully, light-fingered hands ridding Butcher of his clothes one item at a time. He presses a series of tender, doting kisses from Butcher’s chin, down his throat, across his collar bone, and over his heart. “Why would I run?”

I could never deserve this, sparks in his mind, reflexive and disjointed, unstoppable.

“You don’t really believe that, do you?” he asks, sealing his lips around a nipple and sucking.

Butcher fists Insight’s hair in his hand. It’s longer than Hugh’s, but he knows it’s there and good for grabbing.

“What do you want,” Insight says, needful, more a moan than a question.

“Fuck me.”

Like he’d do it, he thinks, dizzy with how much he wants this. Like he would. Like he’s gonna.

Insight’s happy enough to give him what he wants, exactly how he wants it down to the slightest adjustment of his fingers stretching Butcher open. He’s out of his mind and trying not to shoot his load too soon when Insight swallows him down with a gag reflex he doesn’t think Hugh actually has. To add insult to injury, he looks up at Butcher, the tips of his ears bright red to match that face Butcher’s spent more time than he realizes thinking about in this context, and that’s all she wrote.

When he sits up to strip his hand over his dick, fuck, but that’s pretty. He wonders if that’s how Hugh does it when he’s touching himself. He’d probably blush the same way, and the muscles in his arm would jump the same way.

It’s backwards, but it isn’t. If Hugh’s thought of this — and Butcher don’t think he’s full of himself for thinking maybe he has, after their close call last time — he probably saw their roles reversed. Probably saw himself laid out and spent and out of sorts, while Butcher looked down at him, composed but far from unaffected. What had he said? Butcher casts him memory back, recalling it: ‘I don’t think I could keep up with you.’

“Something tells me he’d figure it out,” Insight muses, glancing up at Butcher over the condom he’s unwrapping. “No one really knows how much you want to be handled with care, do they?”

“Not anymore.”

Insight’s hands still. “I know you believe that, but I don’t actually think it’s true.”

Butcher’s loathe to think of any of the people Insight’s no doubt referencing just now, not while Insight’s got his dick in his hand with every intention of putting it in him. With his head clearing up after his orgasm, he likes the charade less than he had when there was less blood routed to his brain. He doesn’t even have to say so before Insight’s putting his hands near his face, and then it really is him Butcher’s seeing, glasses and all.

“Had you taken them off?” Butcher asks lazily, drawing himself up onto his knees and then into Insight’s lap.

“Only for a minute.”

“Want ‘em off now?”

Insight hesitates. “You won’t.”

“You ain’t uglier than some people I’ve fucked,” Butcher says, thinking about the tape again for the first time since he stuck his tongue down Insight’s throat outside the train station.

Insight must know, about the tape. About the other tapes.

One more time, Insight hesitates. A muscle in his jaw twitches. He starts to say something, maybe, but Butcher distracts him reaching for his glasses. Insight doesn’t even try to stop him. He just lets Butcher lift them off his face. Smooth skin gives way to cauterized cartilage and keloid scars and a cavernous void where his eyes used to sit.

“Jesus Christ.”

“I told you,” Insight mutters, starting to take the glasses back.

“Just make me think it’s you from before if you’re that sensitive about it,” Butcher offers, still loose-limbed and pliable. 

Stopping short of sliding the glasses back onto his nose, Insight says, “If I am?”

“I fucked a girl with a glass eye before.” Butcher lifts one shoulder in a shrug. “It popped out of her head when she came. Thought it broke my nose, fucking thing.”

Insight presses his hand to his mouth, trying not to laugh, but for what? Butcher sets about lining up Insight’s dick so he can sit on it, and that stops him again from trying to hide behind his glasses. Butcher gets a long look at him while he’s preoccupied, at the ridges and whorls in his skin, at the places where his flesh ends. After a minute, once he’s bottomed out, the lines of Insight’s face begin to slip-slide like a Magic Eye puzzle. Before Butcher can fully track the progression of changing features, he’s looking down into technicolor blues.

Butcher stares at him for a second, lets himself buy into the idea that Insight’s looking back at him, and fucks him just how he wants. He don’t have Insight’s knack for knowing what’s in his head, but he gives it his best shot and Insight seems to like it. He’s holding his own dick flat to his stomach, not quite roused for a second round, when Insight shouts and spends into the condom. Insight sprawls out on the bed, and Butcher leans back beside him.

Not bad.

“You liked it better when I had a gut,” Insight says, complaining even as he’s trying to catch his breath.

“Don’t tell Hughie. He’ll shit his pants.” When the only response to that is a snort, he adds, “You know he sent me pictures of you in your tighty whities?”

Insight claps his hands over his face and groans. “I should tell him I fucked you as his dad. You do a guy a favor, and he exposes your embarrassing past.”

“What favor?”

Slipping his glasses back on, Insight tells him, “She almost didn’t give him the footage.”
