Butcher sits up on his elbows. Insight tips his head gamely in an approximation of Butcher’s direction, not really seeing him. Maybe he’s going off what he can hear.

“I was there. I thought Hughie might’ve told you. It was his idea.”

“What was his idea?”

“For Vought to have their airplane video. He was just gonna hand it over, no questions asked. But I was there,” he repeats, taking extra care to enunciate. “I heard them thinking. Prisoner’s dilemma, but they were looking right at each other, both of them thinking the other was going to flinch. Both of them thinking you’d never play ball if there was any way you could still pick him instead.”

“And you thought, what, you’d give me a nudge? Make sure I’d keep my head on right?”

“Well, did it work?” Insight asks smoothly.

“I should fucking kill you,” Butcher mutters.

“Why, because I made an executive decision that she might belong with you more than she ever did locked behind a military-strength firewall?” He sits up when Butcher does. “They buried her. You don’t feel better knowing the truth of what happened is in your hands now? I didn’t secure that footage to torment you. I did it because it was my understanding you were in this at all to raise hell.” He taps the corner of his opaque glasses. “To bring punishment.”

Butcher rolls his eyes, angry at how much sense there is to his argument. He masks it by climbing off the bed and passing up his discarded clothes to disappear into the bathroom.

By the time he’s done in the shower, Insight’s folded up some of his things at the foot of the bed. Butcher reaches for the topmost piece, sees that it’s Insight’s shirt, and sets it aside for the next one — his — and pulls it on. Insight steps off the bed and goes to take his own turn in the shower.

“What’s a better way to tell Hughie how much you loved taking his dad’s dick, singing telegram or as many bouquets of flowers as it takes to compose the declaration one word at a time?”

“Second option.” Butcher steps into his underwear and then his jeans. “He likes sunflowers.”

“Noted. Well, this was fun.”

“Fuck off,” Butcher says without heat.

Insight smiles and walks into the bathroom holding his hand out in front of him. Butcher finishes dressing, get on his shoes and jacket, and lets himself out of the apartment. The whole way to Arcadia, he keeps expecting to get mobbed by Homelander, but he never shows. Whatever his plan for the night is, it must be taking up a lot of his attention.

Arcadia’s all crisp air and blue water and light for miles in every direction when Butcher touches down. Beautiful, like it’s always been. 

He wonders now, when he ain’t never been curious before, what possessed Homelander to pick this place over anywhere else. Time and men have forgotten it. Butcher looks forward to the day that’ll be true for him, too, but as it is, he looks at the smashed in rooftop and the broken parapets and sees memory. In the whorled metal wall, he feels the weight of Homelander’s body draped against him, the fall of limbs and the lay of muscle leaping beneath skin.

It takes no doing at all to think of Homelander remembering Becca that way. Fucking Insight and his warped view of justice matching Butcher’s. His eyes sting with twin impulses; to spill, to burn. And that’s just it, really. He wants to spill and burn — bodies and blood and whole fucking continents. That’s what he wants, but he’ll settle for Homelander.

As the sun’s setting over Arcadia, streaking the sky a glowing orange, who else should materialize just as the edges of the horizon begin to bleed indigo, but the man himself.

Butcher stands on the bank looking up, a tremendous feeling of déjà vu nearly bowling him over, and smiles.

 

/

 

They’re three states over by the time Homelander realizes what the smell sticking in his nose is. He stops abruptly, forgetting — or maybe he only tells himself he forgets — to let go of Butcher’s arm when momentum snaps him in two directions. To Butcher’s credit, he only cries out a little when his shoulder wrenches clean out of its socket. He even catches himself as soon as Homelander lets go of him, without falling a bit.

“Who?”

“Why would I tell you?” Butcher grouses, palming his arm and shoving it back into place. No other sound passes his lips except a thready, wet inhale, and then, “Forget you don’t actually own me?”

Butcher’s mouth keeps moving, but Homelander doesn’t hear the accompanying language beneath it.

A dull roar swells at the back of his skull, picking up in force and magnitude until the tremor crowds the edges of his vision, until he can taste it on the back of his tongue, until the breath in his lungs becomes like an acid tearing him apart. Until there’s nothing left in him but that noise, building into a terrible, decimating crescendo. Until it overtakes him, until his mind goes blank with it, whited out and noxious.

A bland smile crosses his face.

“Well, one of us forgot,” he chirps. “Luckily for you, Billy, I’ve got just the thing.”

Whatever reaction he wanted to provoke, Butcher looks positively mutinous to be on the receiving end of munificence instead. Bright and dark, Homelander thinks pleasantly.

“You’re trying so hard to be anything but mine,” he purrs, grasping Butcher’s dislocated arm in his hand. On the other side of too firm and definitely hurting him, if Butcher’s clenched jaw is any judge. Another mouth kissed him there — another man, he thinks — and that won’t do a bit. “When all along, you and I were never going to be anywhere but here.”

Butcher jerks back, slipping a little, but shoving away in spite of it. Homelander can feel the radiating pain in his arm, written into his recoil.

“Here with you’s just where I wanted to fucking be.”

He reaches into his pocket, and then, every muscle in his face relaxing, he turns that pocket inside out. They both look down at it. Homelander doesn’t know what he expected to find in there, but it has him at a great loss to find it gone.

“Bollocks,” he mutters.

Homelander claps him on the shoulder and drops them down just where they are, two state lines ahead of schedule for his purposes, but that’s all right. He’s rougher than he needs to be on the landing, and braced for it, Butcher catches himself without any assistance from Homelander. That’s a very good thing for him. Homelander isn’t feeling especially keen on charity at the moment.

“Fuck are we?”

“Kentucky?” Homelander murmurs carelessly, dragging Butcher by the scruff of his neck and slamming him into the nearest upright surface. Turns out to be a telephone pole.

Wood groans and shudders, shifting out of its foundation. The wires mounted above uncoil with elastic buoyancy on one side, and on the other, stretch taut with tension, just like the line of Billy’s body when Homelander kisses him.

Biting him, hissing against Butcher’s red, red mouth, Homelander says, “Tell me his name.”

“Why?” The word from Butcher's lips holds more pain in it than when his shoulder popped out of place. He leverages that same shoulder against the telephone pole to shove Homelander off him, his hands hot like branding irons. Telephone wires spring and snap free from the transformer overhead, raining down sparks all around them. A refulgent cascade of white illuminates the snarl on Butcher’s much beloved face. “There’s only me here,” Butcher growls. “He can’t hear you.”

Understanding filters in one breath a time. The taste of mint turns to ash in his mouth. 

Shit, he thinks. 

Fuck.

And then, sweetly, softly, so near he can almost feel her lips at his ear, Becca Butcher’s ghost whispers with such supple, hateful viciousness, Tell Daddy.

Chapter End Notes

Boy, howdy. I am alive! Yeah, lemme tell you what happened to me in December: I got sick, I had to move, and then it was Christmas! @_@ But I live!!! Apart from a submission I'm going to be working on these next few weeks, I don't foresee any huge delays like this last one. Sorry for that. But yeah, I was not having a good time. I'm all settled and better now, bogged down by neither mucus nor bruises. What a gift. <3

*singing off-key to the tune of Untitled by D'Angelo*
Girl, it's only Hugh~~ have it your way~
and if you want, you can decide~~
and if you'll have me, I can provide~~~
everything~ that you desire~~~ mmmmm~

PLAYLIST:
https://open.spotify.com/playlist/3oJKf5tC1OoAH8KTHKsO49?si=s-vzozkeS2KIn2afBG0q_Q

Chapter 15: Gomorrah at sunrise

Chapter Notes

Chapter warning for violence, could be considered dv. Use your discretion.

Becca Butcher is the first thing he sees once the dust clears.

Four layers of concrete and pulverized wood shingles flutter down all around him, searing spots on his face stinging from the red sight, white-hot and twinging beneath his ear and at the corner of his mouth.

Standing over him, wearing the same outfit he peeled off of her that stupid, meaningless, monumental day that he changed all their lives forever, Becca speaks through and around the fog in his mind with the greatest of ease.

You belong to everyone, she murmurs. But no one’s ever belonged to you. Don’t you wonder why that is?

“Billy,” he says, with Butcher’s hands fisting the loose collar on his own shirt clothing Homelander’s body.

Even now, or perhaps especially now, to feel his hands there stokes frantic heat in the well of Homelander’s belly. He loves Butcher’s strength, his savagery, his ruthlessness; loves to be its roosting place when so much of how they’ve come to worship each other began as violence.

He’s thrown through another two concrete walls, and Homelander entertains the passing thought that he hasn’t felt this from Butcher before.

Oh, he’s been on the receiving end of Butcher’s hatred and his rage, but there’s something else clinging to his anger now that Homelander doesn’t recognize. He can’t name it, and he can’t find its counterpart in himself. This bothers him more than the single rivulet of blood trickling out of his hairline, warming a blazing path down his cheek. Hot like tears, but not tears.

To be surprised right now feels somehow superfluous given the circumstances. Part of him had been hoping for a fight from the moment he heard the pop of Butcher’s shoulder dislocating in his hand, but he’d thought, just this once, that he’d done everything right. That it hadn’t been his fault they wound up here again, that they always would.

Did you think he’d ever love you, Becca says, smearing her thumb through the line of his blood, after what you did?

Well, that’s the thing.

Butcher goes for a punch, and Homelander catches his fist in his bare hand, finally putting a kink in the smooth unfurling of Butcher’s momentum. Their eyes meet, and for a single drawn out second, Homelander recognizes that oft-indefinable current lighting Butcher up like an atomic bomb.

Refulgent light washes out every last trace of Butcher’s fury, obscuring the evidence, simple, common, and impossible, of his broken heart. 

“Billy—”

He sidesteps the first blast and slaps his hand across Butcher’s eyes when he spins around for a second. Normally, Butcher would have some hindbrain reflex at the ready to get out from under Homelander’s grip, but he’s all brute force tonight. While that undoubtedly makes him easier to catch and flatten, it’s not in Butcher to stay down.

It usually isn’t, but tonight he fights like a man possessed by equal parts betrayal and desolation. Homelander wonders at their origins; is exceedingly keen on knowing just who it is that Butcher blames most for rendering him so out of control.

“Billy.”

Homelander takes his hand away, and Butcher promptly smashes him with his forehead. It dazes him enough for Butcher to shove him off. The blows raining down on him then could be like any they’ve traded at Arcadia, but this time is not like the others.

It’s not even like their first fight at Herogasm; it’s ragged in its desperation, like a crank has turned deep inside Butcher and snapped off a few clicks beyond the true apex. It doesn’t hurt. 

There’s blood on his face and in his hair, but the accompanying pain doesn’t register. No, he’s too preoccupied with the revelation he’s had to feel anything but jubilant. 

He tries to tell Butcher this — tries to interrupt the automatic flow of his movements so he can get a word in — when clarity dawns. It is in this moment, becoming aware of his body like a coiled snake not yet given to striking, that he curls his fingers into a fist and, with the aid of the superspeed, swings.

This time when he goes down, though he tries, Butcher doesn’t get up. 

Homelander’s aware in a distant way of the tingling in his knuckles. It’s the first of the tremulous aches he can tangibly feel in his skin.

“I feel the same way,” he says, sitting up. He shifts onto his hands and knees to loom over Butcher and look him in his dark eyes. “Being with you has changed me in ways I never could have anticipated. You’ve made me want to be gentle.” He cups Butcher’s cheek in his hand, spying the fracture in his wince and in the splinter marring his zygomatic bone. “Even when I know, and you know, that’s not really who I am.”

“Thanks for the reminder,” Butcher grits out, his right eye watering when the simple effort of speaking jars his injury.

“You knew who I was when you agreed to this. You knew how Ryan got here.” He presses his thumb to Butcher’s bottom lip in lieu of kissing him. “It’s no trick of my creation that you went and fell in love with me.”

Butcher turns his head. “Is that what you think’s happenin’ here?”

“Oh, you love your wife, and you’re angry,” Homelander allows. “But you always loved your wife, and you always have been angry. Whatever you think you know—” He bars his arm across Butcher’s chest when he starts to struggle. “Whatever you want to believe it’s changed about this, you weren’t actually surprised, were you?”

“No.”

“Then, correct me if I’m wrong, but…” He flashes a smile meant to be disarming, keeping his voice light and personable. “Your reaction seems disproportionate to the revelation itself, unless what you realized wasn’t that I’ve done exactly what you’ve known I did all along, but that you’d started to let yourself overlook it. And Billy,” he adds, his smile softening to a moue. “I can only think of one reason you of all people would turn a blind eye to the truth. That’s what happened, isn’t it? That’s why you’re hurt now, when you weren’t before.”

Butcher doesn’t answer. The look on his face is so hateful, so vicious, Homelander knows he’s on the right track.

“You can say it if it’s true, Billy. Or if it’s not true, you can say that, too. What you’re not going to do is let some other man touch you as if that’s an answer.”

“It weren’t no answer,” Butcher says, baring his teeth so the blood between them shows. He starts to smile, though the action alone gets his eye back to watering. “I make you wanna be gentle, right? That’s what you said? Well, why don’t you try and guess what it is you bring out in me.”

Homelander eases off the arm bar he’d had pinned to Butcher’s chest, thinking. He hasn’t yet placed it when Butcher forces the heel of his hand up into Homelander’s nose. The crunch of cartilage and squelching blood unseats him just as much as the sharp sting of pain behind his eyes. But they’re even now, break for break. Butcher twists around onto his elbows before bracing on his palms and lurching to his feet. He sways, steadies, and turns just his head to look behind him.

From his seat on the ground, holding his bloody nose, Homelander cranes his neck to look up at him. Butcher’s swollen cheek has already begun to puff up significantly.

He’d had this same furious, undeniably forlorn look on his face when they were up in the sky a little while ago. He’d looked this way after their time abroad. He’d looked this way in Greece, as early as that first morning they spent on the beach.

You’re trying so hard to be anything but mine.

Homelander floats up rather than stumble on weakened knees. “You want this! You want it just as much as I do. You know you do.”

A hand only Homelander can see snakes around Butcher’s waist. The ghost of Becca Butcher rests her cheek against Butcher’s arm and stares at him, implacable.

“You’re going to throw me away, when I’m right here, for her? She’s dead, Billy!”

It must mean something, how hearing this gets no reaction from him. They’ve come close to having this conversation so many times, and each one of those instances was like a hair trigger to Butcher’s rage. There’s none of that now. He just looks tired, and more than any show of wrath could have, it sends cold ratcheting through Homelander’s blood.

“Yeah. My girl’s dead. She is.” He turns, and while the swelling in his cheek looks better straight-on, the discoloration looks worse. “And ain’t nothing I do to you can ever bring her back. I know that.”

“Then why are you being this way?”

Butcher laughs, and while it must pain him, he doesn’t let it show. “Ask me how I knew what you said to her.”

“I assume our mutual friend told you, the one you currently stink of?”

“There was a camera, John,” he seethes, pointing at Homelander’s face. “I watched the fucking video.”

He shoves Homelander, and Homelander shoves back, both of them slipping and sliding in the fray. With a scrape of their feet on concrete, they lift off the ground and wrestle midair, Butcher cussing and spitting at him as they wend up and up toward a skylight like moths searching for the moon.

“She was afraid of you!” Butcher’s saying, shaking Homelander by the collar. “She was afraid, and she didn’t want you, and you fucking laughed!”

He can remember how she’d said Butcher’s name then. Not in the manner of a slip-up, but deliberate. She was calling him, even when there had been no way for him to hear her, and somehow, all these years later, he had.

Like a bill coming due, like her ghost really was here with them in some way, calling out to be avenged and remembered.

“What do you want me to say? That she was just another conquest? She wasn’t.”

Butcher falters, only for a second, but Homelander seizes the opening to throw a punch of his own.

“You were a conquest, but her? I didn’t even see her,” he says, swinging his fist and slamming Butcher against the nearest wall when he starts to fall. He hits him again. “She wasn’t special just because you loved her.”

A steady line of dark blood runs down Butcher’s nose. He says, “Nah, you got it wrong.”

Homelander’s fist sings like it’s a separate organ, singular from his hand. He says, “Which part?”

“She didn’t need me to be special.” Blood and spittle spray on the operative word special. “It was me, needed her. Never the other way round. But you don’t know anything about that, do ya, John?”

A great tremor begins to take hold of him at the root. His ears ring, and he remembers the Becca Butcher he dreamed, how it was only there that he understood her for the first time. Shaking, he says, “What don’t I know about?”

“Normal.” Another laugh cuts through him then, loud and percussive and wet. It sounds like it hurts. “Fucking normal life, John. Doin’ life with someone. It ain’t pulling stars outta the sky or ripping up mountains, it’s loving someone, even when it’s fucking borin’. Even when they lose themselves because someone like you comes along and smashes everything to bloody pieces—”

The weapon of his fist strains and arcs toward its target. Butcher flashes a horrible grin at him through split skin and a chipped tooth, so like the man Homelander was happy to hate. Homelander never cared to earn that man’s forgiveness, much less his acceptance or his love.

He’s come too far and done too much to apologize, and damn Butcher for making him think that he should start now. He hits him again and again, and in between blows, he sees Butcher’s eyes glaze. Alert one moment and starless the next.

Tell me to stop, Homelander thinks. Ask me not to hurt you. 

But Butcher doesn’t. Even if he wanted to, he doesn’t get the chance. It’s less painful to make that decision for him than to offer mercy and be refused.

His head bounces off the exposed brick, and Homelander lets go of him, lets Butcher spin end over end, down and down, until he hits the ground. Like the retort of a gun, the sound of impact echos in a rising wave until the sound finally dies out.

In the remaining quiet, Homelander’s frayed breathing is all that remains. He fans his fingers out in front of him, observing the splits in the skin, the blood shining between his knuckles.

Illuminated by blurred moonlight filtered in through the skylight, they don’t look like his own hands. 

The roar of blood and breath in his ears dims a little at a time. He becomes aware of Butcher’s thrumming pulse down below. Homelander descends, gingerly, as if now is the time to be careful. As if it matters now whether he breaks something that can’t be put to rights again. As if there’s any way to make this anything other than exactly what it is and what it was always going to be — the two of them punishing each other.

His feet touch the ground, and then he’s lowering himself further to sit, to pull Butcher’s head into his lap. Even now, even with his face and his hands dappled in this man’s blood, he wishes this could be what he has tried so many times to promise. Even now, he can see a way back to it.

“You have such a power over me, Billy,” he whispers.

He doesn’t answer.

Homelander runs tacky fingers through Butcher’s hair. His scalp’s sticky with cold sweat and congealing blood. “I love you,” he says, barely breathing the precious words. He scrubs the back of his wrist across his eyes. “Why did you make me do this?” 

Gripping Butcher by the shoulders and shaking him, he cries, “Why do you all do this to me?”

When there’s still no response from Butcher, Homelander scans his injuries with an ear on his breathing. It isn’t good, but it’s survivable. For a reason he can’t readily define, this irritates him.

At least if he felt like he’d gotten some point across, he might feel better, but as it stands, Butcher fucked someone else — a freak with no eyes — and somehow, it’s Homelander’s fault. Somehow it’s his fault he’s too powerful; somehow Butcher thinks he owes the world, and not the other way around.

Ugly impulses war inside him. When Stilwell made him feel this way, he got rid of her. And Stormfront—

Well. Then, it had been him making her feel this ugliness. He knows that now.

To hear Butcher tell it, if he’d loved either of them the way he thought he did, he wouldn’t have discarded them as soon as it was convenient. He wouldn’t have let them think he wanted them gone. He wouldn’t have let himself be bored at the idea of, what?

Of letting them be imperfect? Letting them lie to him? 

Letting them need to be cared for?

He scoops his arms beneath Butcher’s shoulders and the nook of his knees, and lifts him up. The complete lack of resistance in his body stitches a different kind of unease in Homelander. It doesn’t feel right. Even sleeping, he never felt like this. There’s always been something solid about Butcher, but now, he’s fragile like he’d been under assault from lesions in his brain.

Homelander carries him out of the broken building, disappointment an albatross behind his sternum.

This wasn’t the outing he’d had planned for them. They were supposed to terrorize some low-level supes a few states over and call the bloodbath foreplay. He’d had his hair done, for fuck’s sake. He’d let Deep put beard oil on his face.

Now, instead of fucking Butcher to the tune of a happy massacre, he’s flying him to the nearest hospital.

This being a dilapidated small town in Kentucky, he’s flying for a while. Butcher comes to some ten miles later, nearly over Bardstown. Or, he nearly comes to.

When he tries to talk, only a fitful grunt comes out. However much he remembers about what happened tonight, if he remembers anything, he tucks his bloody face against Homelander’s neck and promptly goes slack again. In spite of everything, in spite of himself, Homelander thaws beneath the sweltering heat of Butcher’s forehead.

It’s as he’s feeling that telltale craving to be a soft, yielding lee against the world that he feels something in Butcher’s heartbeat shift on a downward slope.

“No,” Homelander says. “Don’t you dare, Billy.”

He speeds the rest of the way to Flaget Memorial, carrying Butcher across the threshold like a blushing bride. No one recognizes him, but they take Butcher back to be triaged anyway. The nurses shove a clipboard into his hands with countless blank forms he can’t fill. 

Ten minutes later when they come to check on him, he’s still just clutching the pen awkwardly like a knife.

“Sir?” A tired-looking woman older than him, though not by much, motions to take the clipboard back from him. “Do you know of any next of kin we can call?”

He blinks, tries to change the set of his face and his shoulders, and says, in as commanding a voice as he can, “I’d like to do that myself, Ma’am. If that’s all right.”

Now she blinks at him, like she’s trying to put a name to his face. “Say, haven’t I seen you on the TV?” She points at him, squinting. “You talk to fish.”

Homelander nearly breaks a tooth grinding his teeth together.

“No, wait! You’re the other one!”

“Yes, Ma’am.”

“Huh. Guess you saved our patient. Do you types usually stick around for this part?”

“He’s a friend.”

“Oh. That’s all right then, I s’pose.”

He bites his cheek. “Has he stabilized?”

“Head trauma’s pretty bad. Caused a cardiac event. We’ll keep him here to monitor. Have you filed a police report?”

“The matter’s been resolved,” he says tightly, and then, with a grandiose look all about the crowded lobby and the many sick and injured still waiting to be admitted, he adds, “But if you’d like to call the proper authorities, by all means.”

“You’re basically police,” she says, waving her hand.

He bullies his way back to Butcher’s room and sits in the little chair by the sealed shut window. It’s a dingy little room. The close air and the iodine reek reminds him of Stormfront. The crisp white bedsheets remind him of her, and Butcher’s face—

Homelander gets up to pace rather than complete that thought.

It’s not his fault, or if it is, he only folded under Butcher’s expectations. He can’t be blamed for doing just what he was bid to do. Can he?

His eyes stray to the plastic telephone mounted on the wall. 

He’d told Deep he never had any use for the things before, and while true, he can see the utility in them now. A phone call would get him out of this room. It would get someone else inside it in his place. But who could he call? Not Ashley, she wouldn’t give a shit about his unseemly lover laid up in a hospital in Bumfuck, Kentucky. He crosses over to the phone and starts punching in a series of numbers he’s seen Butcher dial once before.

The line rings. It goes on for such a long time he nearly hangs up, but then, there’s the sound of clattering and a frail voice comes through.

“Yes, hullo? Who’s there?”

He clears his throat to ask, “Am I speaking with the mother of William Butcher?” rather pointlessly. He’d heard her voice on the phone that slow, honeyed morning in Santorini. He knows it’s her.

There’s a pause wherein he nearly repeats himself, but then she says, “You shit. It’s you, isn’t it?”

Plastic creaks in his palm. He shoots Butcher a sideways look, annoyed all over again. Affronted at the gall this family has to hate him for something that’s got nothing to do with them. As if he can never be anything more than shit on their shoe after what happened that day with Becca Butcher.

Like she can hear him thinking discourteous thoughts, Butcher’s mother sniffs on the other line. She still sounds every bit the old lady when she speaks, but there’s something in her tone that makes him stand up straighter, a backbone and a bite to match it. It’s impossible not to heed it, no matter how it rankles. Even if he can hear the faintest of wavers in her voice.

“Is my son alive?”

“He’s alive,” he croons, suddenly vibrantly aware of how much he’d like to hurt this woman for having the nerve to speak to him the way she has. “How long he stays that way depends on you. How fast can a little human run?”

He has more lined up on the tip of his tongue to say when she cuts him off.

“Just tell me where he is, cunt.”

His mouth flaps and gapes. Well, she’s Butcher’s mother all right.

He gives her the name of the hospital, hangs up the phone, and goes to stand by Butcher’s bedside.

“Now would be the time to wake up and call me a cunt,” he says, starting to touch Butcher’s hair and stopping himself. “Your mother certainly didn’t waste any time.”

He stands in the near-silence tracking the tenth-of-a-second delay between Butcher’s heartbeat and what’s on the monitor. Counting out the beats where they synchronize softens the static in his head, helps him begin to rationalize what happened tonight. They had a — disagreement. A tiff. A quarrel. And Butcher—

Well, Butcher started it. Didn’t he start it? Didn’t he orchestrate all of this so that Homelander would react? So that he would finish it? That was his stipulation, and Homelander followed it.

Could he have stopped it some other way? Oh, maybe. Maybe.

“You couldn’t just say it, could you?” He covers Butcher’s hand tentatively in his, then takes it away. “No, you had to blow it all apart. Throw it in my face like it didn’t mean anything, but I know you, Billy, and doesn’t that—”

He catches Butcher’s hand in his again, deliberate, like it had been trying to evade him before, and stops short when the last two broken fingers on Butcher’s hand catch his eyes.

Holding his one imperfect hand over the top of Butcher’s, he whispers, “Doesn’t it turn your whole world upside down.”

Butcher’s heart marches onward, slow, methodical, tenuous. Saying something to Homelander that he isn’t fit to hear just now, or perhaps ever. Telling him what he’s desperately trying not to know. That he did this, and it was wrong. That he’ll never be able to take it back. 

That it was different from a fight—

“Wake up,” he says, going to his knees right there on the cold, gritty floor. He folds his hands over Butcher’s and presses his forehead against his cut open knuckles, a litany of, “Wake up, wake up,” falling from his lips.

Anger had twisted in him like a knife and made him cold, but as the ice recedes, shame creeps in and brings him to a boil. Tears spring to his eyes, his hands prick all over where they’re covered in myriad cuts and bruises, and worst of all, shame unspools deep in his belly. Shame like he’s never known or even imagined.

It’s too much for him to be here. It’s too much for him to sit and wait in this oppressive quiet. The silence hangs over him like a condemnation, terrible and unshakable.

Homelander pulls himself up, sniveling, pathetic, weak, and presses a shuddering kiss to Butcher’s hairline where a break in the gauze allows for contact. His lips follow a path down to Butcher’s unhurt cheek, catching a fleck of gravel the doctors missed, and lower, to kiss his slack, sleeping mouth; saline on the nose, rust on the tongue, and devastation on the eyes. 

Different from a fight, he thinks, and then shuts the thought out entirely. It’ll all look better in the morning. Won’t it?

 

/

 

Butcher wakes to the inoffensive metronome of a heart rate monitor, and a hand curled up in his. 

He ain’t awake enough to think yet, so when he cracks open the one eye that’ll cooperate with the command, there’s nothing going on in his head at all. The most he’s aware of is the few faint sensory details that come through: sunlight, a scratchy blanket strapping him into the bed, a few springs that creak when he tries to move, and then—

“Mmmff,” he says, pretty goddamn articulately.

“Butcher,” MM cautions from somewhere in the cramped little room. “Don’t try to move.”

Hughie extracts his hand from Butcher’s, and as he’s pushing to sit up, their eyes lock; both of his, to Butcher’s one. Hughie’s got a sheen of drool on his cheek and frazzled bed hair, but these are nothing to the look on his face. Sweetheart that he is, he’s got salt stains creased in the corners of his eyes and along the hinge of his jaw.

Butcher wants to take the piss so badly, but the pain in his ribs is making him fucking dizzy. There’s more heat radiating from different sectors of his face, cool twists of an opiate haze chasing out the waves of hurt as they rise in him and crest. He tries to talk again, with shit results.

“Your jaw’s dislocated,” Hughie tells him, a flat nothingness to his voice Butcher notices but can’t interpret much less acknowledge.

His only recourse is to prod the end of Hughie’s nose with his finger.

“Here, turn towards me,” MM says, patient-like and attentive, holding out a bendy-straw-adorned plastic cup for drinking.

It hurts like hell, but he manages to drink a little. MM takes the cup away after a few sips and thoughtlessly goes to sop up the dribble that escapes the corner of Butcher’s mouth. Butcher waves him off, staring at his splinted fingers when his eyes catch on the sight of them, and then at the electric trails they leave in the air. That’s oxy, right? He don’t see shit on tramadol, and codeine makes him nauseous. 

“Listen, Butcher.” MM folds his arms in front of his chest. “Homelander called your mom.”

He starts to sit up, but Hughie settles him back against the springy mattress with no effort at all. His hands are firm but gentle, to match the look on his face, and he smells good. He always smells good.

“Take it easy. You’ve—”

Hughie stops mid-sentence, and then his hand’s cradling the back of Butcher’s head. Oh, yeah, Butcher might’ve leaned into him when he wandered too close.

“You’re in bad shape, Butcher,” Hughie says, and he sounds so sad it moves through Butcher straight from Hughie’s chest to his. “Just try to relax, okay? Can you lay back down? There you go.”

His pillow’s not nice like Hughie’s collar bone was nice, but it matters less and less as exhaustion creeps up on him. There’s a single tone blaring in his ear and rattling in his back teeth.

What were they talking about?

Like he’s picked up mind reading since the last time they talked, MM says, “Your mom wanted to come with us to get you, but we didn’t know what shape we were gonna find you in. She didn’t fight us on it, so maybe she was thinking the same thing.”

Dazed, and unraveling the longer that he stays awake, he raises his hand to his neck, fingertips tapping his throat. It’s a struggle just to do that, but it’s fucking important. 

Shaking his head, Hughie asks, “Is your throat hurting? Do you want some more water?”

St. Christopher, he thinks. Pillock.

But Hughie doesn’t get it. Butcher favors him with an eye roll and plants the heel of his left hand against his hip, bumps the few undamaged knuckles of his other hand off his palm in a swooping motion. Just like he’s seen Kimiko doing.

“Huh? Oh! Uhhhh… Supe! Supe, uh—”

Butcher pins MM down with his one working eye, and he gets it in one.

“Ryan,” he guesses.

Butcher drops his hands, relieved.

“He’s with Mallory. Frenchie and Kimiko stayed back with them.”

Fuckin’ A, he thinks. Spice girls get it done.

He makes a circling motion with his uninjured hand and gives in halfway to drowsiness. With his eyes shut, it’s harder to keep track of the room, but he figures a workaround while they’re discussing what his impromptu hand sign could mean. MM catches on to what he wants quicker than Hughie and catches his hand.

Hughie’s still hypothesizing what Butcher’s trying to tell them: “—almost a thirteen hour drive out here, but MM made it in eleven. Fucking Compound V, Butcher. You already look a lot better than yesterday—”

“Hughie,” MM says, soft-like, and on account of Butcher holding his hand, he can feel MM gesturing with it.

“Oh.”

He scoops Butcher’s hand off the bedspread. Good lad.

Butcher hums, half a question. He’d still like to know where Annie is, and who’s looking after Terror while he’d laid up here. He hopes they left him with Hugh. His toy cache is better than Hughie and Annie’s. It’d give him an excuse to drop by when this is over with and they can get him home.

Giving his question one more shot, he squeezes Hughie’s hand to get his attention and wiggles his fingers.

“Did you wanna try signing again? I really only know how to talk about food at this point, or dog-related insults. Oh! Terror? Are you asking where Terror is? We brought him with us in the van. He was here last night. Annie and my dad have him now.”

It takes him a second to understand what Hughie’s telling him. He cracks an eye open.

“Oh, fuck off, I know what you’re thinking,” Hughie mumbles without heat.

Butcher’s not — thinking, that is. He really hasn’t since he woke up, outside of fleeting impressions and a running litany of ow, fuck, does that hurt, too? But at the weary look on Hughie’s face, he starts to remember why he’d be cagey about having his dad within goosing range, particularly when Butcher’s being handsy as it is.

He can’t quite smile, but a muscle in his cheek bunches slightly, crinkling the corners of his one working eye.

“Easy, killer,” MM says, patting Butcher’s hand like he’s an old geezer pinching bottoms and terrorizing the staff. “You’re in no condition to be startin’ shit.”

Why’d you bring his dad then, Butcher thinks, and wants to laugh. 

He surprises himself by giving into the impulse. It’s hell on his ribs, but once he starts, it’s hard to stop. 

“Oh, my God,” Hughie moans. “Do you think if I smother him now, the nurses would suspect foul play?”

“Be nice. It was your idea to bring him along.”

“It was not my idea. He invited himself!”

After a pause that feels interminable to Butcher, MM says, “Well, on the bright side? It’s not likely anyone’s getting dicked down on this trip.”

Butcher nods off in the middle of Hughie’s answering groan. He wakes up in sips a few times after that.

One time he thinks he hears Annie’s voice in the room, but he don’t get eyes on her in time to confirm before he’s asleep again. The first time he properly dreams, he’s at a dog park with Becca.

Terror’s little enough not to want to walk, and Butcher ends up carrying him against his ribs like a soft, squirmy football.

“I’m sorry, which one of us spoils him?” Becca asks around a grin.

“You,” Butcher tells her, unrepentant. “Hundred percent you.”

“Uh huh. Sure.” She drops her hand to Terror’s wrinkly dome. Gentle and sweet, like he remembers. She leans her head on his shoulder, and she says, “He loves you.”

“Good to him, aren’t I?” He gives Terror a wee bounce so he’ll look up. “Aren’t I good to you, son?”

“Mm, he’s gonna be sweet like his daddy.”

“Nah, he’s a killer. Ain’t ya, sweetheart? Just like Mummy.”

He wakes up with her laugh in his ears for the first time in a long time. As it’s leaving him to make room for all the pain currently taking up space in his body, he starts to feel like he’s just been hit in the face with a shovel.

His one eye still won’t play ball, but he has some success working his mouth open. Not a lot, but enough to get out the words, “Ah, Christ.”

There’s a jangling to his left, the tags on Terror’s collar as he perks up to investigate. Next to him, folded up in a chair by the window with a book in his lap, is Hugh. He stands.

“Billy,” he says.

“Hey there, darlin’,” he says to Terror, holding his hand over the edge of the bed. Then, to Hugh, “Have a nice drive?”

“Oh, sure.” He huffs a laugh, bashful right out the gate. “Pretty sure Hugh tried to ditch me at a gas station in West Virginia, though.”

“Cheeky. I’d’a gone with Maryland.”

“Really?” Hugh asks, smiling.

“Sure. Here’s what we do on the drive back, all right? Seafood restaurant,” he gives a little flourish with his busted hand so he can keep patting Terror on the head. “They’re out crackin’ crab, and you, me, and the lil ‘un here abscond with the getaway car. Easy.”

“And then we loop back for them?”

Butcher rolls his eyes, as much as he can when half his face is swollen. He gives Terror a break and waves Hugh over. “C’mere, you.”

Hugh sits on the very edge of the cot, using both hands to scratch Terror behind the ears. 

Butcher’ll allow it. For now.

He’s rolling flirtations around on his tongue to get him to come closer when Hugh sighs.

“I’m sorry, Billy.”

“For what?”

He raises his head, showing the color high in his ears and in his forehead. His eyes, like Hughie’s eyes, are sad. He moves a piece of hair away from Butcher’s eye that won’t open, touch so precise and featherlight Butcher can’t even feel him there. Fucking oxycodone.

“Hugh,” he says, feeling along Hugh’s forearm to grab the meaty heel of his hand and pull it down over his chest. “What’re you sorry for?”

“I-I thought…”

Butcher crosses his other arm over his front, locking Hugh in with him so they’ve neither of them got anywhere to go. “What’d you think?”

“That maybe you’d, um, like for me to be here.”

“I look like I don’t?” Butcher asks, idly rolling his head on the pillow.

“Well, that’s just it. You’re—oh.” He gives a fretful little sigh and scooches over closer like Butcher’s been wanting. It’s less pressure on his neck that way, but he weren’t banking on Butcher getting greedy and snaking an arm around his waist. Squirming, but laughing while he does it, he says, “Billy, have some mercy.”

“Who, me?”

Hugh snorts, and while he still sounds like he’s in high spirits, his eyes get brighter. Filling up with tears, like.

“Steady on.” Butcher lets go of him straight away, conscious right in that moment of how easy it’d be to forget his strength. “Did I hurt you?”

“No, no, I’m okay.” 

It’s what he says, but there he goes, one hand over both his eyes. Something’s hurting him.

“When Hughie told me you were hurt and they didn’t know how bad, I didn’t let myself think about what that would look like. I just wanted to be here for you through it, if you’d let me.” He takes his hand away, smearing shiny tears on the apples of his cheeks. “Maybe that was naive of me, to think I could see you like this and still be strong for you, even just as a friend.”

“Sweetheart, I am drugged right up to the gills right now,” Butcher slurs, “but if I didn’t want you here, you’d be gone in a minute. Know how you can trust that’s true?”

Hugh swipes at his eye one more time with his fingertips. “How?”

“Cuz me, I got a knack for pushing people away.”

He’d be doing it right this minute except it feels much too nice laying here looking up at someone he wants to be looking up at.

Watching him, bedrock even for the water standing out on his face that’s looking more and more like iron the longer this answer sits with him, Hugh says, “Is that the reason this happened?”

Butcher tries a shrug, and then a smirk. His lip won’t curl, exactly, but he gets it to quirk.

“Started a fight and lost,” he mutters. “Forgot to keep my hands up.”

“Doesn’t sound like you.”

Butcher heaves a sigh, all of him loose and limbered down. “Which?”

“I don’t think you lost.”

So Hughie did get his gift for reading people from his dad. Interesting, that. If it weren’t for the hospital bed he feels all but strapped into and the drugs helping to weigh him down like stones — what? What does he think he’d do then? Push Hugh away? He ought to; him and the boys.

But no, he’d pushed Homelander, nothing at all on his mind but for the magic pill in his pocket that’d knock them both out of the sky. Two dickheads, one stone.

When that hadn’t taken, he couldn’t think of a reason not to sit and take his punishment. It had just been pure dumb luck that he knew it’d hurt Homelander, too. Little more than a convenient coincidence.

“Billy,” Hugh murmurs, “is this what you wanted?”

Butcher turns into the hand at his cheek, until he realizes it’s there to catch the steady stream of tears coming out of him. Goddamn oxy.

“Told you it was complicated, Hugh, didn’t I?”

Terror whines from the bedside, and Hugh leans away. Before Butcher can get his shit together enough to call him back, there’s Terror budging up under his arm. It brings his dream back to him, of Terror small and round and baby soft, Becca’s hand on his head. Whatever holdups Hugh’s got, it don’t stop Terror giving Butcher the kisses he’s been wanting all this time.

Hugh’s phone rings just as Terror’s getting good and cozy nestled in Butcher’s shoulder. He holds it to his ear and says, “Hello, MM? Yes, he’s awake. Sure, one sec. How do I…?”

Butcher misses the button himself a few times, but between the two of them they switch the audio over to speakerphone. Hugh pulls the chair from the window closer to the bed and holds the phone flat in his palm so they can both hear it.

“MM.”

After a brief pause Butcher chooses to interpret as meaning ‘I liked it better when you couldn’t speak’, he says, “Hey. How’re you feeling?”

“Like shit warmed over.”

“I bet. Listen, what I’m about to say to you right now, you’re not gonna like it. But I gotta tell you something, all right?”

“Tell me fuckin’ what?”

There’s some scuffling then while the phone changes hands.

“Butcher?”

Cold spikes through him. Hugh meets his eyes over the phone in his hand.

“Ryan,” he says.

In an instant, Butcher’s aware of every bruise and break on his body: his banged up ribs, the itching cuts in his hair, the fire in his recently dislocated shoulder, the mangled fingers on his right hand, his eye that’s swollen shut, his jaw that’s tender as hell after all this fucking chitchat, and to top it all off, his one eye ain’t stopped leaking since Hugh first caught him crying a minute ago.

“Aunt Grace mentioned that she had to talk to you about something, and I was thinking maybe Dad has something he wants to talk to you about, too, except that he said he hasn’t seen you, so it got me wondering if maybe you got in a fight. I know it’s not my business if you did, especially since I think you guys like it when you fight, but I don’t wanna not see you just because Dad isn’t. Because — well, it’s not like we’re not fighting. Are we?”

“No,” he answers, too goddamn quickly, not letting himself think about it at all. Fucking oxycodone.

“Okay! Your mom’s nice. She brought scones for the plane ride. I’d never been on a real plane before — Dad and I usually just fly-fly — but it was fun. I thought Frenchie was gonna puke, though.”

Butcher’s pretty sure he might puke. Jesus fucking Christ.

“I heard MM telling Aunt Grace on the phone that you got hurt really bad. Were you trying to rescue someone?”

Maybe he thought he was at the time, but who’s he fooling if he thinks anything he does now can save Becca? Even if he does manage to someday bring Homelander to justice for what Butcher saw him do on that tape from Vought, it’s like Butcher said. Nothing brings her back. Nothing makes it so they can have their time together back. Nothing takes away what was done to her.

And yet.

“Uh, Ryan? Butcher’s on some powerful medication right now, so he’s having some trouble staying awake. How about you wait to see how he’s feeling when you get here, okay?”

“Oh, yeah! I can do that. Thanks for taking care of him, Mr. Campbell.”

“Sure thing, kiddo,” Hugh says, like he manages crises equal to or bigger than this in his spare time. He hangs up the phone, pockets it, and very calmly asks Butcher, “Are you gonna tell him what happened?”

“Guess I should,” he says, hedging when he should be telling.

Hugh ruffles Terror’s neck where he’s got rolls. Butcher seizes on the opportunity to tangle their fingers — because it’s Hugh, yeah, but also because he just wants to be touching someone.

“Is there a reason you wouldn’t?”

“Now which one of us ain’t got any mercy, huh, Hugh?”

“Still you,” he muses, flashing a tired smile that speaks of ten-plus hours in a van and many more shut up in this room with Butcher waiting for him to do something. Like an apology, he adds, “But you’re right, I’m pushing, and you’re in no condition to push back.”

“Sure I am,” Butcher says, all careless, weightless bravado. “Ask me anything.”

Laughing, his cheeks and his forehead turning a pretty pink, probably without any notion of what it means to Butcher, he says, “No, Billy.”

“Lemme ask you summat then. If it was you here and Hughie was comin’ round to ask you what happened, could you tell him the one person he’s got left in the world went twelve rounds on your face and then dropped you a couple hundred feet onto some concrete?”

“I can think of a few reasons I wouldn’t want to,” Hugh admits, looking only sadder for the absolute lack of judgment in his expression. For the abundance of compassion. “And I understand why Becca didn’t.” He covers Butcher’s hand with his. “But ask yourself, would you be making that choice to protect Ryan, or Homelander?”

Butcher doesn’t have an answer for that. He thinks he does, but when he searches his mind there’s nothing there. He’s just tired beyond words.

Like an inadvertent answer to a prayer, a knock comes at the open door.

Annie pokes her head in, says, “I told you they’d be fine,” and pulls Hughie in after her by the wrist.

Hughie, dear heart, clocks his dad holding Butcher’s hand and narrows his eyes. Butcher must really be a sorry sight to see, though, because he don’t say anything overtly bitchy, except, “MM went to meet Mallory and the others at the airport. He said you’d have about thirty minutes from when he called to fake a coma, so…” He looks at his watch. “I mean, I’m happy to help if you need an assist.”

“Cheers, Hughie.”

Never let it be said Butcher can’t take a hint. He pats Terror down his side to give Hugh an excuse to make his escape and he pops right up, darling and red in the face. His chair being the only one in the room, he offers it up to Annie.

“Hey, Butcher,” she says softly, distracting him from the hushed conversation the Hugh’s are having by the door. “Are you okay?”

“Sure. Right as rain.”

“Yeah, you look it.”

“Saucy,” he muses. “Well, all right. Give it to me straight, doc. Am I gonna lose the eye?”

She leans in to get a better look at it. The lighting’s for shit, so she does her bit with the halogens overhead, flaring up supernovas in her eyes. Reminds him of the showdown against Soldier Boy when she turned out to be their ace in the hole.

He thought for a long time they’d never be anything but enemies, but he can’t see through the tangles in his logic like he usually can. All he can see is her face lit up like Christmas.

“What’re you doin’ with us fuckups anyway, huh, Annie?”

Her eyes jump from his swollen shut eye to his good, working one. She don’t look surprised, bless her, but she does look — how to say it? — careful. He can’t see through that snag either. Doesn’t know why she’d give him that. 

Except, he does know, and he knows because she told him.

Overcome by the immensity of this knowledge, choking with it, he says, “You’re a good girl, Annie.”

And from the doorway, Hughie: “Dude, you can’t say that to my girlfriend.”

For her own part, though, Annie just looks warm. Not charmed or won over or, God forbid, pitying, but more like who she always has been and Butcher never allowed himself to believe.

“To answer your question, once the swelling goes down, you should be back to normal no problem.”

“Cor. Hughie, come over here and be sweet to me.”

Hughie makes a big show of sighing like his suffering has been interminable since Butcher came into his life, but he does cross that meager distance. His dad trails behind him and stands watch at the foot of the narrow bed.

“Just so you know, I hate you,” Hughie mumbles, holding out his hand so Butcher won’t ding his splinted fingers when he reaches out to capture it. He does something with Butcher’s hair to get it off his forehead and asks, softly, “Do you need anything?”

“Nope.”

He falls asleep just like that, one hand tucked up under Terror’s belly and the other folded up in Hughie’s hand.

It ain’t no coma, but it’ll do.

Chapter 16: many-colored

Chapter Notes

Chapter warnings for canonical child abuse and violence. Also depictions of injury.

Ryan’s never been to Kentucky before. He was excited about seeing someplace new, but now that they’re on the road, he’s starting to see that it’s not that different from home. Or from New York. It’s a little bit different from New York. Smaller, quieter. Slower.

He likes these differences. He tells Frenchie and Kimiko as much, but he can tell by their smiles that they think this is a little-kid thing to say.

“Have you guys ever been here before?”

Kimiko shakes her head.

“Not me,” Frenchie murmurs. Pitching his voice up, he turns his head toward the front of the car and says, “MM, have you been down this way before?”

MM laughs sharply. “Nope.”

“Et vous, Madame Charcutier?”

She twists around in the front passenger seat. The look she gives him is so dry Ryan can’t help but laugh. Kimiko’s lips turn up at one side, too.

“Ah,” Frenchie says. “We are all of us Kentucky virgins.”

“Serge,” Aunt Grace chides, but Butcher’s mom snorts.

Frenchie leans forward in his seat and sets his hand on her armrest. She covers it with her own and stays facing the front. In the backseat behind Frenchie, Kimiko leans forward, too, with her temple against his seat. He can tell they’re worried about Butcher and trying not to show it. Ryan wasn’t, at first, but it’s starting to sink in that maybe he should be.

It’s okay, though. Even if it is bad and Butcher’s really hurt, everyone’ll be there with him. Ryan knows pretty well by now that Butcher’s not as untouchable as he likes people to think he is. If anything, it’s the other way around and he feels things way more than he lets on. Plus, Ryan can remember times with Mom, and then with Aunt Grace, that he’s been sick and needed help to get better. It always made things easier just to know he wasn’t alone. 

So if it is bad — and if it is hard to see Butcher hurt — then they can help it to be less bad for him. Less scary.

Ryan looks out the window, eye drawn to the crowds he can see gathering on the far side of the highway. A nauseous, sideways feeling like dread sweeps over him, and he reads the signs: REGULATE SUPES, I’M NOT COLLATERAL DAMAGE, and REAL HEROES DON’T WEAR CAPES.

Relief, cool and sudden, replaces the dread. He’d thought they were his dad’s friends. His fans.

There’s a tap on his shoulder, and then Kimiko’s smiling at him. She shows him the notepad app on her phone: Are you doing okay?

“I’m okay. Are you?”

Nodding, she points out the window and types, flipping her phone around for him again.

None of that is about you.

He wonders if she believes some of it might be about her or Annie, and all at once, he wishes she wouldn’t. 

“I don’t mind if they think that stuff. Dad says…”

Frenchie turns in his seat, eyes soft, but something serious in his face. “What does your papa say?”

“He calls them—” You, he thinks but can’t say. He calls you— “Mud people.”

The quiet in the car then rings in Ryan’s ears. Even trying to make it sound better, he can hear that he’s said something ugly without realizing it.

“Aryan fucking Barbie doll,” Mrs. Butcher says under her breath, and then with tears in her voice, “Shithead white Supremacist ra—”

“Connie,” MM says, taking just one hand off the wheel to touch her arm.

Aunt Grace looks back at Ryan in the backseat. There’s a look on her face like she wants to apologize, but her eyes are wet, and the same sound like crying is in her voice when she talks to him.

“Ryan, do you remember what your mother used to say about fairness?”

“Um, to treat everyone the same. To be kind. Because…everyone’s been sad before, and you never know when anyone was nice to them last.”

“That’s right,” Aunt Grace tells him.

Ryan sneaks a look past her to the front seat. He can’t see anything of Butcher’s mom but her tightly crossed arms. She reminds him of Butcher when she holds herself like that, tightly guarded and closed off, like barbed wire.

“Is it a really bad thing to call people?” he asks in a quiet voice.

“It is a storied and violent slur rooted in anti-semitism,” Frenchie tells him. With that same soft, serious set to his face and his voice, he adds, “Anti-semitism, do you know what this is?”

Shame creeping up the back of his throat, Ryan nods. 

He never thought that word really came from anywhere. Dad always just throws it around like he made it up. Like it’s never meant anything but what he wants it to mean, and then Ryan went and repeated it, like it was something worth claiming. Like Dad’s been trying to get him to do all along.

“Talk like that’s coded,” MM adds from the front seat, checking his mirrors and then flicking the turn signal before cautiously changing lanes. His voice, like Frenchie’s voice, is calm and patient. “Goes back to this idea some people have that being born a certain way makes you less than human. Guess your dad liked the shorthand of it.”

Kimiko shows him her phone again. The kindness on her face tells him the same thing she typed: That’s not about you either.

But it is about him. It’s exactly about him and the kind of world Dad wants for and because of them, and that’s not the way it should be. No matter how much he wants them to be together and to be happy, he has to come to terms somehow with…

With how many of Dad’s ideas have to do with hurting people.

“I’m sorry,” Ryan says into silence that pricks right under his skin.

“You didn’t know, mon chou,” Frenchie says. “But now you do, hmm?”

Ryan blinks, and then, still warm in the face but brightening for a different reason, for a feeling he can’t name right away that opens up to breathe in his chest, he nods. 

“Yeah! Yeah, now I know.”

Aunt Grace smiles at him, but her eyes are still shiny. Ryan’s heart feels like it lurches up into his throat looking at her. It’s like he misses her, even though she’s close enough for him to reach out and touch. He does reach out and touch her, just to cover her hand with his.

“Aunt Grace, after we see Butcher, can we go and get something to eat?” He has a second before she replies to feel embarrassed that maybe it’s rude to ask her this in front of everyone. He rushes to add, “And — everyone else can come, too.”

She brings her other hand over his, sandwiching his hand with both of hers. “We’ll go anywhere you want.”

He thinks maybe the feeling behind his ribs is forgiveness. It’s how he knows he’s fixed a mistake, or righted a wrong. Clear and weightless, like relief.

To his right, Kimiko’s phone buzzes twice. She checks it and signs something like a twist near her chin to Frenchie. She knocks the back of her hand into the flat of her wrist on the other hand. Frenchie blows a long sigh. When he sees Ryan watching them, he explains.

“Petit Hughie is in need of rescuing—”

Kimiko signs something else. Frenchie raises his eyebrows, all of him looking like a laugh from his twinkling eyes to his smiling mouth.

“This is what I said!”

Kimiko rolls her eyes, sees Ryan, and waves it off, but the two of them continue to sign rapidly. Frenchie starts to translate, until his eyes also drift to Ryan. His face gets twice as expressive when he signs.

They’re so weird.

MM brings the car off the highway and takes a couple turns Ryan can’t predict until they’re happening. Gradually the streets get more and more congested, and then the hospital comes into view. While he’s waiting to make a left turn, MM looks in the rear view mirror.

“It’s gonna take me a minute to find parking. Anyone wants to get out, I’ll drop you at the roundabout up here.”

“Thank you, Marvin,” Aunt Grace says. “Connie, would you like to go up alone first?”

“No, I would not, Gracie.”

Aunt Grace nods. She’s obviously tired from the flight and from the short ride in the car, but she holds herself the same way Ryan’s always seen. “Then would you care for some company in that case?”

“I would, dear. Ta very much.”

MM pulls up to the visitor drop-off. Frenchie’s door faces the curb, and he opens it as the car’s still slowing down.

“Ey, man! Hands and feet inside the vehicle until we come to a full stop!”

“My hands and feet are inside!” Frenchie objects, raising his hands near his face, palms out. “And look, we have stopped.”

He doesn’t argue, but MM rolls his eyes at Frenchie in the rear view mirror.

Frenchie jumps out, then Aunt Grace, and Kimiko waves for Ryan to go ahead of her. She follows him out onto the sidewalk, then turns to sign at MM through the open passenger’s side window. She taps her chest with her hand in the shape of a cage and points in a sweeping motion at the parking lot. 

“Nah, I’m good. You guys go ahead.”

She nods and without looking, she catches Frenchie’s hand in her own before turning to look up at the building. Frenchie catches Ryan’s eye and flashes a shy smile. He starts to walk toward the hospital with Kimiko.

Ryan hangs back, looking between Aunt Grace and Mrs. Butcher. They’re having some kind of conversation with their eyes.

Aunt Grace notices Ryan watching them and says, “Connie, you don’t have to go up right away.”

Mrs. Butcher nods, statue-still for a long moment before she shudders and presses her hand to her eyes. His own eyes stinging, Ryan starts to step toward her, starts to try and hug her or hold her hand or something. A hand drops to his shoulder. He looks up at Frenchie.

“Why don’t you come up with us, huh?”

Ryan looks to Aunt Grace, but she’s walking Mrs. Butcher over to a bench. She sits next to her and covers her with an arm around her shoulder.

“But…”

Kimiko positions her hands like she’s hugging herself, a sign she clarifies a second later by pointing out to the parking lot.

“MM will come to meet them,” Frenchie explains.

“Okay.” He takes Kimiko’s hand before he even realizes she’s offering it, and the three of them walk up to the main entrance together. “Is she gonna be okay?”

“Perhaps in time.”

That building feeling in Ryan’s stomach reaches an endpoint. Worry, huge and unshakeable, rises in his chest like motion sickness. Heat flashes in the tips of his ears and in his fingertips. He feels more than ashamed suddenly that he didn’t take this as seriously as everyone else did. Even on the phone with Butcher, he thought—

He just thought it had to be part of the work they do. He didn’t think—

But that was so stupid. He’s so stupid.

“Ça ne vas pas?” Frenchie guides Ryan away from the doors and behind a support beam, away from foot traffic and prying eyes alike. “Ryan, what is it?”

“Sorry.” He tries to dash the tears from his eyes before they fall, but there’s too many of them. They’re hot on his face, just like his shame. He shouldn’t cry. It doesn’t fix anything. All it does is show that he’s still just the baby they all think he is. “I know I shouldn’t…”

“What, cry?” Frenchie asks, with his face so open and earnest it just makes Ryan cry harder. “Oh, ma puce. Come here.”

Ryan sinks into the offered hug, grateful.

“Do you think Butcher’s maman should not cry if she is fearful, or if it wounds her to think of her child suffering?” He asks in such a gentle voice, he could almost be reading from one of Mom’s books he used to ask for before bed, back when he could still ask her. “Do you think we should not cry for feeling helpless at the sight of her pain? When all we can do is to feel it with her?”

Pulling away, Ryan’s heart gives a little skip at the tears standing out on Frenchie’s face. The glistening tracks on his cheek overlap with the curve of his smile. He presses his hand to Ryan’s shoulder.

“You are okay, you know? We are right here. Me and Kimiko and MM, and your Tata Grace. And Annie and Hughie are upstairs even now.”

Kimiko, knelt next to Frenchie, nods and signs rapidly.

“Oui, and everyone loves you.” He ruffles Ryan’s hair. His hand is cool against his burning scalp. It feels nice. Reminds him of Mom’s hugs, and Butcher’s, too. Cool like relief, like a strength that’s greater than his own, enough to lift the weight off his shoulders. “It’s okay, huh?” Frenchie says, soft. “It’s okay.”

Ryan looks at Kimiko, and she nods. Her eyebrows pull down, and she moves her hands, forceful, raised up to her shoulders, knuckles bunched. She presses her palms together, and then holds her hand over his chest without making contact. She nods.

He nods back, feeling like he understands even if he can’t pick out the words like Frenchie can.

She brings her arms up to catch him when he lurches into her. Frenchie eases out of the way and rubs his hand in a circle between Ryan’s shoulders. 

Muffled by Kimiko’s shirt, Ryan says, “He’s hurt really bad, isn’t he?” 

He feels Kimiko turn her head to look at Frenchie. They have a silent conversation with their eyes, too. Frenchie clears his throat.

“Yes, we think so. Ryan, if you don’t want to go up there, no one will make you.”

“I want to see him,” Ryan says, drawing some of their strength into himself. He scrubs his sleeve across his eyes, less to make them disappear and more to acknowledge them this time. “He makes bad choices when he thinks he’s alone. I don’t want him to feel that way anymore.”

Sighing, Frenchie squeezes Ryan’s arm. “Shall we wait for the others to come in?”

Ryan turns his head to look outside. He has to peer through the concrete to do it from this angle, but the picture’s the same. MM’s joined Aunt Grace and Mrs. Butcher on the bench. Aunt Grace is telling a story about Butcher in a low, steady voice. Mrs. Butcher has MM’s arm tucked up against her side, both her hands gripping him tightly.

“Let’s go up now,” Ryan says, rubbing at his eyes to get them to refocus. 

“Yeah?”

“Yeah.” He raises his chin. “Yeah!”

Frenchie and Kimiko look at each other, the same small smile passing between them. Nodding, Frenchie pushes on his knees to stand up and holds his hand out for Ryan to take. Maybe this is a little-kid thing, too, but Ryan’s not bothered by that. There are places that feel right for holding onto the people closest to you: standing on the sidewalk, crossing the street, walking to the information desk of a hospital late in the afternoon.

On the elevator ride up to the second floor, Ryan’s heart tries to climb up and up with them. He does his best not to let it get out of control. Kimiko on the plane ride here showed Frenchie how to breathe when he was scared and it helped, so he gives that a try.

He’s still counting out his breaths when the doors ping and slide open. They step out together and look both ways. Down one end of the hallway, Annie spots them and waves.

Ryan doesn’t think. He takes off running.

“Whoa,” she gasps, catching Ryan when he hugs her. “Hi,” she laughs, hugging back. After a beat, she presses her hand to Ryan’s back. “Are you okay?”

“Yeah. Are you?”

“Mmhmm.”

“How’s Butcher?”

She takes a breath and holds it. Frenchie and Kimiko walk up behind them.

Pulling back slightly, she says, “You wanna go in and see him?”

“Is he awake?” Frenchie asks.

“You just missed him,” Mr. Campbell says, stepping out of the room with Terror’s leash loosely held in his hand, and Terror hunkering down to sit on his foot as soon as he stops walking.

“Terror!”

He licks Ryan’s hand.

“Hi, Mr. Campbell.”

“Hi, Ryan.” He smiles, looking the same kind of rumpled and tired as Aunt Grace. “I was just gonna take Terror for a walk. Want us to wait up for you?”

“Um.” He bounces on his heels. From here, without the see-through vision, he can’t see anything but the foot of Butcher’s bed. He’s afraid to cheat and look. “That’s okay. Thanks, though, Mr. Campbell.”

“Sure. C’mon, big-little guy.”

Annie holds her hand out for Ryan to take, and together, with Frenchie and Kimiko following in behind them, they walk into Butcher’s room. Hughie’s sitting on the edge of his bed, and he turns when they come in, careful in how he does it. Probably because Butcher’s asleep and he doesn’t want to wake him.

Where he’s sitting, Ryan can’t see any part of Butcher except his arm on Hughie’s other side. There’s a deep bruise peeking out from under the sleeve on his drooping hospital gown, painting the inside of his elbow a deep, shiny purple.

“Hey, guys,” Hughie says, starting to carefully untangle his hand from Butcher’s. “Come sit over here, Ryan.”

And then he stands, clearing the view. 

Ryan’s stomach flips. Behind him, someone sucks in a gasp. 

“Mon Dieu.”

Butcher looks like Ryan when he fell off the roof, except times a hundred.

Ryan’s glad then that he got most of his tears out of the way in the lobby. If he hadn’t, he thinks he’d start crying all over again, and that won’t do for Butcher if he wakes up and has to worry about Ryan. It won’t do for Mrs. Butcher either if Ryan makes it harder for her to be strong. They’re all gonna need all the help they can get being strong right now.

“You can hold his hand,” Hughie says, leaning back against the wall. “He’s actually a hardcore cuddler in his current state.”

“Of course,” Frenchie muses.

Even though his stomach’s in knots, Ryan laughs. He laughs, and he holds Butcher’s hand.

It strikes Ryan while he’s looking at Butcher, trying to see all the things that are hurting him, that he’s not wearing his St. Christopher medallion. He feels guilty wondering where it is. Butcher gave it to him to wear once, and Ryan had thrown it back in his face.

Yeah, for a reason. Because Butcher was being a jerk, but he’s apologized since then, and Ryan can’t help but wish for that warm weight over his heart again.

“Did he tell you guys how it happened?”

When the silence stretches around him in the way it’s been doing all day, Ryan looks up. Annie’s looking at Hughie, and Frenchie’s looking at Kimiko. None of them are looking at him.

“Did he?” he presses.

“Uh,” Hughie says, scratching his head. “Ryan, the thing is…”

“What?”

Annie steps up, a hand on Hughie’s elbow. “It was your dad that called Butcher’s mom.”

“Okay.”

“Ryan,” she says.

He frowns, not understanding. He turns back to Butcher, to study his face, barely even recognizable like this, with his cheek puffed up painfully beneath his equally swollen eye. The skin there is the same color as the bruise falling down his elbow, under his eyebrows, from his jaw and down one side of his neck, across the center of his palm. He’s covered in them.

Like when I fell off the roof, Ryan thinks. Like when…

Like—

Ryan squeezes Butcher’s hand, feeling — small. Helpless.

“He hasn’t talked about it,” Hughie says, when Ryan can’t get any words to come out. “We still don’t really know. For sure.”

But it’s all Ryan can see when he looks at Butcher. It’s —

From the moment he saw him, it was all Ryan could see. All he could think. He looks like me when I fell off the roof.

Except he didn’t fall off the roof.

Dad pushed him.

It was before. It was when he didn’t know better. It was Homelander, but then so is this. And what does that mean? What is he supposed to be feeling right now?

Times a hundred.

A hundred.

There’s a knock at the door, and Ryan snatches his hand out of Butcher’s, startled at the sound. He turns and sees MM with his arm around Mrs. Butcher, Aunt Grace lingering in the doorway behind them. Ryan jerks to his feet, wobbling, until Hughie catches him. Something in his flinch snaps Ryan out of his daze, and he realizes as Hughie’s walking him toward the door to make room for Mrs. Butcher that it’s his wrist that bothers him sometimes.

Ryan stares at his hand in Hughie’s, casting his memory back to that day on the curb. He didn’t grab him that hard, did he?

Is that why he sometimes wears the brace?

“Ryan,” Aunt Grace calls, motioning for him. “Do you need to sit down?”

He shakes his head, eyes trained near his feet. “Actually…can I go look for Terror and Mr. Campbell?”

“By yourself?” Frenchie asks, because he’s kind and he wants to protect everyone, and they’re all living in Mom’s world, where he’s just a kid to protect and be kind to. But how long have any of them actually believed that? Frenchie’s one of a few to follow Ryan out into the hall, saying, “Maybe one of us should go with you, non?”

“Who could hurt me?” he hears himself ask, in a voice he doesn’t recognize. Thick with tears but shaking for a completely different reason. He bites his cheek hard, and the pain changes the shape of his tears, too. “Or is that not why you’re worried?”

“Ryan,” Hughie says, sounding like he’s two seconds away from telling Ryan to apologize.

And he should. He’s being mean and he knows it, but he’s angry. He’s angry and made of wasps, and he’s only ever felt this way once before. 

All he’d done then was rip Butcher’s medallion off his neck, and even that, he regrets.

“You know what,” MM cuts in, dropping a hand on Hughie’s shoulder. His voice and the way he’s holding himself are soft like snow falling. “He’s got this, Hughie. Let him do his own thing. You come find us when you’re ready, Ryan, all right?”

It’s as good as permission. He walks off without answering, face hot, eyes hotter. He gets as far as the elevators before he has to scrunch them shut. Luckily it’s a little while before it stops at his floor, and when it does, he steps in without looking. It won’t be safe for him to open his eyes until he’s away from them, and that thought just makes the buzzing, thrashing thing in his chest so much worse. It’s not a big hospital. He can’t get very far just riding the elevator, not like he’d done his first day at Vought taking the elevator all the way up and down, and up and down.

Two floors and roof access isn’t enough distance to put between him and Butcher’s room. The length of the state of Kentucky wouldn’t be enough. Not right now. Everything’s too amplified.

“Kinda caught me off guard there, Annie,” he hears Hughie say, as clearly as if he’d been in the elevator with Ryan, the worry lines on his forehead as clear as if Ryan was seeing him in the infinity mirrored walls closing him in, and not through his eyelids.

“Did you want Butcher’s mom to say something first?”

Hughie flops his arms out by his sides. “She didn’t the whole way here!”

“She hadn’t seen his face yet!”

“Annie! We — Annie, we don’t even know if Butcher wanted him to find out like this.”

“He asked, Hughie. He deserves to know the truth.”

“Should we tell him the rest then?”

The answering silence rings in Ryan’s ears. He opens his eyes.

“You know that’s not what I’m saying.”

“No, but you’re right. He has a right to know. Doesn’t he?”

“Does anyone wanna know what I think? Before one of you says something that lands you in the doghouse, again?” MM says, to a pause that pricks under Ryan’s skin not so much as the other one. “That kid is carrying a whole lotta sad big-talkin’ as mad right now feeling like it’s his responsibility to be his dad’s guilty conscience. The sooner he figures out that’s not actually his job and none of this is on him, the sooner he can start realizing he doesn’t have to shoulder the blame for any of this. Which is what we need to be coming together to make sure he knows right now.”

The doors ping and slide open on the ground floor. Ryan gets out to make room for the nurse rolling in a cart of medical supplies. He shoves his hands in his pockets and walks outside.

Sunsets in Kentucky look more like sunsets at home with Mom than they do in New York. He’s started to get used to the skyline replacing any kind of horizon, but he hasn’t gotten used to the light pollution, or the smog, or the smoke. Maybe it’s just because Bardstown is smaller than New York. Dresden has clear skies like this, too.

He hears a bark and looks up, heart lifting. He hadn’t really been looking for Terror and Mr. Campbell so much as he’d just needed an excuse to get away, but he’s glad to see them. Even if Mr. Campbell’s happy smile and wave make him feel even worse for being so awful a minute ago.

“Hey, you.” Mr. Campbell falters a little, once he’s close enough to see the tear stains on Ryan’s face. His smile goes softer at the edges, but warmer. “What do you say to a walk around the block?”

“Okay.”

“Do you want to hold Terror’s leash?”

Ryan takes it when it’s offered, and they set off. They have to clear a few crosswalks before they get to the nearest residential neighborhood.

It’s only once they’re away from the cars and the noise that Mr. Campbell speaks again.

“Hospitals freak me out, too.”

“I’ve never really been to one before.”

“Oh, no? Well, that doesn’t surprise me, hale and hearty kid like you.”

Terror stops to smell a bunch of weeds growing up through the cracks in the concrete. The blue flowers at the ends of some of the stalks catch Ryan’s eye. He bends down to pick them, twirling them one way and then the other between his thumb and his fingers.

“Why’d you come, if you don’t like hospitals?”

“Ah.” Mr. Campbell sinks his hands into his pockets. “Sometimes you just have to be there for the people you care about, even when it’s uncomfortable.”

They walk behind Terror, flowers forgotten. Ryan keeps a tight grip on the bunch in his hand.

“I think so, too.”

Mr. Campbell glances his way. “That’s good. He’ll be glad to see you.”

“What if he’s not?”

“Why do you think he wouldn’t be?”

“Um…” He brushes the blue round petals with his thumb. “Because Dad hurt him.”

“Oh.”

“Did you know?”

The look on Mr. Campbell’s face then is so sad, but his heart doesn’t go any slower or faster in his chest. Ryan’s reassured by that. He doesn’t mean to listen, but his ears are picking up everything in higher definition than they usually do, and he’s noticed that a heartbeat says a lot about a person.

Take Dad’s. His is always all over the place, even when his face looks cut from marble. Butcher’s pretty much the exact opposite, usually so steady and controlled, except for when he does get upset. And then, it’s like every part of what he feels shows through in some shape or form. His face is like its own character, though, a different kind of mask from Dad’s.

“Yeah, kiddo, I knew.”

“Did he tell you about it?”

“Ah, yeah. Yeah, he did.”

They have to stop again so Terror can smell a fire hydrant. It’s way more interesting to him than the flowers were.

“So what did he say?”

“Ryan,” Mr. Campbell says on a sigh. “Don’t you think maybe you should ask him?”

“Butcher?” Ryan meets Mr. Campbell’s eyes then. He doesn’t know what his face is doing, apart from wobbling in a few places, his lip, for starters. “Or my dad?”

Terror loops around the fire hydrant and backtracks to Ryan. He licks his hand, and Ryan scrubs the back of the other one across his eyes.

“What do you think your dad would have to say about it if you did ask him?” he asks, gently.

Everyone’s been so delicate with him today. Like they’re worried they’ll hurt his feelings if they get mad, but they should be mad at him. They should be mad. They should look at the things Dad’s capable of and see Ryan in the blueprint. Ryan does.

He already—

He killed Mom. He hurt Hughie. He would’ve hurt Butcher that day at Aunt Grace’s house, if not for the Temp V. He’s the same as Dad, and Dad did this.

If Ryan asked him about it, he’d just do that thing he did on TV when a hundred different people asked him about Stormfront. His eyes would go blank and he’d lie, but it’d be even worse than a lie because he wouldn’t believe the words coming out of his mouth anymore than Ryan would.

“Hey, Ryan?”

“What?”

“Come sit with me for a second, okay?”

Mr. Campbell sits right on the curb, and Terror nearly trips in his rush to mob him. Ryan sits next to him, and Terror splits the difference, sitting on Mr. Campbell’s foot and setting his paw on Ryan’s knee. Ryan scratches him behind the ears, grateful he can look at Terror and not at the kindness on Mr. Campbell’s face.
