“You know, we all have to choose what kind of people we’re going to be.” He looks up at the sky for a minute, and then like he’s picking the words out one at a time, like Ryan had picked the flowers from the sidewalk, he adds, “The things your dad wants for you, just like the things your mom wanted for you, they only have as much weight as you let them have.

“At the end of the day, it’s up to you. It’s not something that’s decided in a day. It’s something you have to work on constantly because it’s a combination of decisions, and every time you make a choice, you’re building the man you’re gonna be.” He smiles, something shy in it. “Actually, Butcher’s the one who made me realize that.”

“Really?”

“Uh huh. We were talking about you, and about Hughie.”

Ryan rubs the top of Terror’s head, feeling warm in his face at being lumped in with Hughie in that context. Like he could ever be anything approaching a son to Butcher, or claim him for a dad the way Hughie gets to do with Mr. Campbell.

“You can’t pick your parents, Ryan. No one can, but you can pick the pieces of them you take with you.”

He nods, mind drifting back to Soldier Boy. A cold chill runs right through him remembering the look in his eyes. Faraway like Dad’s, but something else in them that he’s never seen in Dad’s eyes. Almost compassion, almost sympathy, almost love. He hadn’t known what it was then, but he can name it now.

Pity. Regret. 

Disappointment.

Ryan doesn’t want those pieces. He doesn’t want the cold anger or the wrath or that bottomless thing he sees in Dad sometimes. That thing that never really lets him be happy, even when he swears he is.

“Butcher won’t be mad at me when he wakes up?” he asks, his voice coming out even smaller than he feels.

“No, he won’t. Hey, look at me. He won’t.”

They’re not sitting close enough for Ryan to lean against him, and he’d feel silly now scooting closer. And besides that, he doesn’t really feel like he deserves it. He was such a brat to Hughie and Frenchie when he left the hospital. Maybe Dad didn’t teach him better than that, but Mom did.

“Do you think it’s really possible to be just the good parts of a person?”

“I think so. Hughie’s definitely only the best parts of me, and he’s a lot of things I’m not, too. He’s brave, for one thing. Braver than I’ve ever been in my whole life.”

“But…” Ryan wipes his hands off on his jeans. “But you said before. You do things you don’t want to do so the people you care about can be happier, and Hughie…he does that, too. Like…” When Mr. Campbell doesn’t interrupt him, he just comes right out and says, “Like when I hurt his wrist pulling him out of the street, and he didn’t tell me because…”

“Because he didn’t want to hurt you?”

Ryan’s lip wobbles, and he nods.

“Ryan, I don’t think…I don’t think that’s something anyone inherits. I think, whether your dad was your dad or not, you’d have the same ability to hurt people that anyone has. Not more, not less. You’re not dangerous just because you’re someone’s son.”

“But what if I’m dangerous for other reasons?”

“That’s something you have to reconcile with yourself, kiddo. No one but you can make you someone you’re proud of.” He hesitates. “Do you feel proud right now?”

“No,” Ryan says, miserable and little else. 

He palms at his face, and Mr. Campbell does what he’d been too embarrassed and too guilty to do. He moves over and wraps an arm around Ryan’s shoulder. Ryan leans into him, wishing— wishing he could talk to Dad like this. Wishing Dad had room inside him for ideas like this, without everything having to hurt so much.

“What can we do to fix that, do you think?” Mr. Campbell asks, chin moving against Ryan’s hair.

Ryan thinks about it. He thinks about everything everyone’s been trying to do for him all day, and he tries to reframe it. Tries to see it from their point of view. Not walking on eggshells, not — not hiding the truth from him, but just what Mr. Campbell said. Showing up for him. Bearing it, even when it’s uncomfortable. Even if he lashes out. Because, maybe…

Maybe they know he’ll make the right decision in the end. Maybe they trust him enough to let it be his choice. Because he can still make up his mind to be who they think he is. 

Because he can still be the best parts of Mom, and even the best parts of Dad. 

“I got upset, and I…took it out on everyone else.” Like Dad, he thinks but doesn’t say. “I want to apologize.”

“I think that’s a good idea.”

“Can we go now?”

“Following your lead, buddy.”

They walk back together in the direction of the hospital, Mr. Campbell taking Terror’s leash back when Ryan breaks away to run for the visitor drop-off. He scrapes to a stop in front of Frenchie, Kimiko, and MM, looking from them to Hughie and Annie where they’re sat on a bench a few feet away.

“I’m sorry,” he says, still flushed from running. “I got — mad. It wasn’t your fault, I just. I just…”

“We kinda dropped a bomb on you, little man,” MM says. “I’d be more worried about you if you weren’t overwhelmed.”

“It is no great crime to feel,” Frenchie adds. “But to act is where we must exercise caution.”

“I know.” Ryan nods. “I…I should’ve talked to you guys. Instead of running away.”

Kimiko signs, bracing one cupped hand against her opposite palm and drawing it away in a brisk line. From there she traces a circle parallel with the ground. Frenchie opens his mouth to translate, but MM gets there first.

“So long as you know you can talk to us.”

“Eh, yes. What he said.”

Annie stands, and Hughie catches her arm. He stands, too.

“Butcher’s awake,” Hughie tells Ryan. To Annie, he says, “Why don’t you take him up before visiting hours are over?”

“You guys can’t come, too?” Ryan asks, already moving to stand with Annie.

“Not me. I said I’d check you guys in at some hotel Mallory picked in the next town over. Dad, are you coming with? I can run you by the airport on the way up.”

“Gosh, is it that time already?” Mr. Campbell jogs over to where they are, checking his watch. “Oh, yeah, I guess it is. You gonna be okay, kiddo?”

“Yeah.” Ryan reaches up to hold Annie’s hand. “You’re leaving already?”

“PTO is a killer,” he says in apology. “Do me a favor and run the big-little guy back up to his dad for me, will you?”

Ryan takes the leash and a hug each from Mr. Campbell and Hughie. As he’s watching them walk off together toward the parking lot, he thinks he can see the pieces Hughie took from his dad. He wonders if they’re the same pieces Mr. Campbell sees, or if some of them would surprise him.

“You don’t wanna come back up with us?”

“Nah, that’s all you, kid. Between you and me, I’m pretty sure he’s sick of my mug by now.”

“This I know is untrue,” Frenchie says, lofty in how he lets the words roll out. “It is a very good face, MM.”

“Yeah, yeah,” he mumbles, to baffling secretive smiles from Kimiko, Frenchie, and Annie. “Go on up and give him a hug. He’s been asking for you.”

He goes back inside with Annie. At the elevators, she turns to him.

“Ryan, I owe you an apology.”

“Huh? Why?”

“We talked about telling you, but it wasn’t supposed to happen like that. I hurt you because I wasn’t thinking, and I’m really sorry about that.”

“It’s okay,” he says, and it’s an easy thing to mean. “I do that, too, sometimes. But, Annie? I’m glad you told me.” He fidgets with Terror’s leash. “Everyone’s tried really hard to keep the bad things far away from me. Mom — she didn’t even tell me who Dad was. She didn’t tell me about her life before, or why it was always just me and her. I know now she just didn’t want me to be sad for what she had to give up because of me, or to feel like it was my fault, but…I think that only made me believe it wasn’t anyone’s fault, and that’s not true, is it?”

She gives his hand a squeeze, and the doors ping open. Terror walks out ahead of them, and the follow him onto the second floor.

“He tries to be so different now, I almost forgot…how much he scared me at first. Dad, I mean.”

Annie stops walking, and Ryan stops a few steps after she does, still joined to her at the hand.

“I’m sorry, Ryan.”

“I’m not! Or — I am for earlier. I was being a jerk. But you shouldn’t feel bad. You didn’t do anything wrong, but if you feel like you did, then it’s okay because I forgive you. Do you forgive me?”

For a second her eyes look shiny, and then she blinks a few times to clear them up.

“I forgive you,” she says.

“Okay.” He gives her a quick hug and then tugs on her hand. “Come on.”

Back in Butcher’s room, the red sky fills the room with amber light. Aunt Grace is standing by the window looking out, and Mrs. Butcher’s in a chair by the bed. Butcher has to crane his neck to see around his mom to the doorway, but when he spots Ryan, something like a smile flickers beneath the swelling and the bruises and the split in his lip.

“There he is. Mum.”

“As you like, darlin’.” 

She pats his hand and starts to rise out of her chair. Annie slips Ryan’s handhold to help her up. Mrs. Butcher clings to her arm, and on her way out into the hall, she pauses to cup Ryan’s cheek in her hand.

“You’re so much like her. Just the spitting image.”

Aunt Grace drops her hand to Ryan’s shoulder as she’s walking out behind Annie and Mrs. Butcher. When it’s just him and Butcher and Terror left in the room, he approaches the bed.

“Hey, Butcher.”

“Hey, monkey.” His words are mushing together a little, but that’s probably the medicine Mr. Campbell mentioned on the phone. Butcher drops his hand off the side of the bed to ruffle Terror’s ears. “Where’d you run off to, huh?”

“Just around the block with Mr. Campbell.”

Butcher hums, and then, like he’s trying to enunciate, except the syllables just squish into each other more, he says, “Mr. Campbell.”

“He said you guys talked about me.”

“Which time,” Butcher mumbles, opening his one eye again. 

Ryan passes up the chair to sit on the edge of Butcher’s bed, like before. “There was more than one time?” he asks.

“Sure. Feel like I’m always talkin’ about you. Light of my life, ain’t ya. Only good things left, you and Terror. Isn’t that right, sweetheart?”

Terror gives a little huff in answer.

“Everyone came and visited you today,” Ryan says, giving Terror head pats since Butcher can’t.

“Hmm. Not everyone, eh?”

Ryan hadn’t let himself think about it. He didn’t tell Dad he was coming out here to see Butcher. Doesn’t think he’s actually even noticed him missing yet. When he strains his hearing hard enough, he can hear the fragments of the same conversation he’s been having with Ashley all day. He’s been doing his best not to pick out any words.

He listened in on everyone’s conversation earlier, but it was an accident. No matter how mad he is at Dad right now, he doesn’t want to know anything from him that’s a secret if Dad’s not choosing to share it with him.

“Heard you got wind of what happened,” Butcher says, giving Ryan’s hand a weak little squeeze. “With me ’n your dad.”

“Sort of. Actually, I think I knew as soon as I saw you.”

“Oh, yeah?” Butcher fumbles trying to cover Ryan’s hand with his. He lets Ryan clasp their hands together and shuts his one eye. “Smart lad.”

“Not really. It’s just that sometimes people tell you who they are, and you have to listen.”

Butcher smiles in that lopsided, hazy way. “Becca used to say that.”

Ryan smiles back, feeling the rush of tears in the back of his throat like a cloud of gasoline. “Yeah, she did.” 

He feels in his pocket for the flowers, a little squashed but pretty, and presses them into Butcher’s opposite hand. His fingers flex, thumb pressing against the wilted petals to try and feel what it is. He laughs, soft and low, the sound living right in his chest.

Eyelashes fluttering, Butcher says, “What’s this for?”

“I don’t have anything else I can give you, and…you don’t have your St. Christopher medallion.”

“Don’t I?”

“No, you’re not wearing it.”

“Bollocks.” He blinks, but that doesn’t last long at all before his eyelid falls shut again, heavy like sleeping. “What, really? I don’t?”

“Mm-mm. Maybe the nurses have it. I can ask.”

On a deep exhale, Butcher says, “Grand.”

“Butcher?” 

There’s a long pause spent listening to Butcher’s breathing, and the beeping heart monitor. Butcher squeezes his hand.

“Hmmm?”

“Were you scared?”

“Nah, but — ’m sorry. I shoulda held it together.”

“What do you mean?”

Butcher’s most of the way asleep, all of him gone slack. But still he says, “I coulda been…family to you, maybe…”

And then he’s all the way asleep, Ryan’s sidewalk flowers drooping in his fist.

“But you are,” Ryan whispers, tears spilling over. “You are.”

It settles in Ryan like a promise. 

Chapter 17: lion’s den

Chapter Notes

Content warning: some non-con sexual assault elements in this chapter.

After soundly screaming at him and bullying him back into his suit for the first time in over a month, Ashley books him an appearance in Times Square for a live concert at dusk alongside Deep and A-Train. Apparently he’s been out of the public eye long enough that he needs to show his face and let people know he hasn’t gone anywhere. 

Obviously he went somewhere. The deep tan and half beard give him away even at a glance, but the way she’d pitched it, people had their own theories about his trip to Greece, ranging from a solo excursion to a romantic getaway. No one had connected it to Butcher yet, but there was no reason they should. Even if anyone had gone through the surveillance footage at the Vought resort in Santorini, it wasn’t like they’d done anything indecent within sight of cameras. Let people have their little theories. It’s not like they can prove anything.

This event with Deep and A-Train is softball stuff. Standard meet-and-greet PR bullshit, the likes of which he hasn’t had to trifle with in months. It grates.

At least in Times Square they’re working with natural daylight. No glaring floodlights angled right at the chalky makeup pancaked on his face. Deep’s stylist, the same one who’d trimmed and tidied Homelander’s hair and beard, does what he can, but there’s no arguing against the polls. People liked the look of him better before he went and fell asleep naked on a Mediterranean beach. 

And he’s under no illusions what they’d think if they knew the rest of what he’d done naked on that sandy coast.

He crosses paths with Deep and A-Train as they’re walking out onto the platform where the cameras are set up to broadcast to the jumbotron. Deep raises his eyebrows and visibly holds his tongue, but A-Train outright laughs.

“Oh, shit,” he says, pressing his hand to a toned, flattened stomach. “Man, what, they got you in white face now?”

“Dude,” Deep warns, pulling A-Train back.

Homelander can smell the minibar on his breath. Not enough to keep him drunk, but enough to loosen up his mouth and make him stupid. Homelander flashes a wide, sedate smile and lopes ever nearer, hands tucked benevolently behind his back.

“Well, at least when people look at me,” he purrs, “what they see is what they get.”

“Fuck is that supposed to mean?”

“It means I never had to ‘rediscover my roots’ to be relevant to a modern audience.”

“That’s funny,” A-Train says, half a sigh and half a smiled exhortation. “Cuz you know who you look like with that shit on your face?”

“A-Train, don’t.”

“Fucking Soldier Boy.”

Homelander freezes, and if it weren’t for the fact that he can hear the blood pumping through his body, powering up his organs, he’d think the insult had turned him to stone. Like a spell or a hex, or something out of a fairytale.

Before he can find an answer within himself, the curtain walling them off from the cameras slinks to the side, and A-Train’s walking away, hands up high like he’s just won a race in his heyday. Deep pivots and takes a backward step out into the crisp daylight. His face is a mask of calm panic, eyes huge and terrified.

“He’s drunk, he doesn’t mean it.”

And then he whirls around, agile on his heels like one well-versed in walking on eggshells, and meets the crowd with a flourish and an unmic’d laugh.

Raking a gloved hand down his cheek, each individual whisker scratches leather. His hand comes away flecked in pasty, sticky white. Offstage, a voice he doesn’t recognize announces him, and calling up that void deep within him, that emptiness that responds most to attention, Homelander forces a razor sharp grin and walks out last. He waves preciously for the cameras, favoring the few that he knows will capture him at his best angles. The cheers from the audience are riotous. He’d almost forgotten how this many of them could make such a racket.

Live music rockets out of the speakers perched like gargoyles off the elevated stages. It spares them the effort of any serious playacting, but it does mean they have to look glamorous and happy, like they like each other.

Homelander winds an arm each around Deep and A-Train, his elbows twin nooses perfectly fitted for them both. Cameras flash, preserving their counterfeit smiles like pressed flowers.

He angles his face toward A-Train without looking away from that glittering tinsel stage.

“If I killed you right here, in front of all these people, they’d rush forward. But not to help.” Now he does turn his eyes to meet A-Train’s, both of them still grinning, though their eyes have gone hard. “Did you know in France, at the helm of the guillotine, onlookers would dip their handkerchiefs in the blood of the executed while it still ran warm from their bodies?”

Tightening his hold around A-Train’s neck as if to cuff him on the ear with his arm, he looks out at the crowds, knocking his temple against A-Train’s. Affectionately, in the matter of a brother, maybe. But Homelander wouldn’t know.

Although A-Train would.

“People are the same everywhere,” he says tonelessly, through a bloodless grin.

“Cuz you’d know?” A-Train shrugs him off, fluid enough in how he does it that it looks rehearsed. Nearly a step in a dance. “Thought you were above all that. Oh, but I get it.” His smile tightens at the edges, becomes more like an expression that he’d really make. Fierce, like he used to be. Hungry in that way he’d had, before he could let himself get comfortable. “It’s different now, and you’re different, because you found someone. Right?”

Homelander feels his smile dim to match the starless chasm behind his eyes. Seeing it, A-Train brightens. Everything about him seems momentarily thrown into the past, rough hewn but playing the sharp edges to his advantage so that he seems, simultaneously, young, and jaded.

His voice is a deadened thing when he speaks. 

“It’s an easy fix.” The fire in his eyes burns and burns, and finally his smile slips, curdles into a sneer. “You remember, don’t you?”

“A-Train, just…bring it down, man, okay?” Deep pleads, doing his best imitation at Melpomene to A-Train’s derisive Thalia, Tragedy and Comedy. “You’re on top. You worked your ass off to get it back.” He sweeps his arm out to indicate the throngs of people dancing in the streets. “Don’t lose it over this.”

“He’s right, you know,” Homelander offers placidly. It’s easy to be calm in the face of such seething rage. He clasps the tops of A-Train’s arms in his hands. “This is as good as it gets for you. You wouldn’t want to go and ruin it. Not now that you went through so much trouble to cut out all that dead weight.”

“You’re a fucking pussy!”

The music covers this declaration, and just now, the cameras are following the performers onstage. Homelander notices these things idly, in the nanoseconds he speeds up to give himself time not to react. 

Circumstances being what they are, A-Train being who he is, he can speed, too. He can see Homelander making himself count to ten.

“Yeah, that’s what I thought,” A-Train says, smirking. “Pussy.”

And then he’s walking away. Again.

Homelander can’t get to him without making a scene, without hauling him back over the railing before he can jump down into the street to make his way toward the stage. People flock around him, loving him for his proximity and his energy and his fame. Deep lingers, white knuckling the railing that separates him from a similar escape.

“Spare me your fucking mother hen complex,” Homelander mutters, levitating up and over the safety rail himself. He has no ear for this music, but he can fake a smile easier amongst people unversed in every one of his tells.

Or he thinks so.

From some fifty feet away, he hears, “If it isn’t the caped cunt.”

He looks up, through the gyrating skeletons and loose confetti alike, to find a pair of heavy, matte sunglasses peering out at him. Homelander crosses to him, summarily summoned.

“Sorry,” he murmurs, no louder than he’d spoken his initial invitation. “Was it just Billy who was allowed to call you that?”

“Say his name again.”

Those perfect white teeth gleam in a smile, but he doesn’t take the bait.

“It must really stick in your craw, that you couldn’t give him what he wanted.”

Homelander slows to a stop directly in front of him and places his hand over the xiphoid process just beneath his ribcage. He doesn’t apply pressure. Not yet.

“Be very careful what you say next.”

“Hey, I couldn’t either,” Insight muses, smiling. Puckish. He covers Homelander’s hand with his, adding, “Damnedest thing about being a mindreader. You always know exactly what the other person wants.”

“What’s your point?” Homelander’s torn between bearing down and yanking his hand away when Insight’s face — changes.

He blinks. The man in front of him, covering Homelander’s hand with his own, smiles.

“Turns out,” Insight — surely Insight — says, in that aimless, devil may care drawl of his. “Billy boy likes daddies even more than you do!” He flashes a grin, not his own picture perfect Hollywood smile but emanating the same radiance somehow. The same confidence. “That is, he likes real daddies.”

Now Homelander does tear his hand away from Insight’s. He’s halfway to using the red sight when it dawns on him. Where he’s seen this face before.

Insight sinks his hands into deep pockets, bouncing buoyantly on his heels and knowing the exact moment Homelander’s connected the dots. He huffs a generous sort of laugh, his voice changing to match the face he wears — the face Homelander sees — and it slots into place. No longer a thing he can pretend to be ignorant of, anymore than he can pretend he doesn’t recognize young Hughie Campbell’s dowdy, plain-faced father.

“Hey, that’s what I said. But, the heart wants…”

“You’re—” He makes a mistake. He takes a step back. “No. You’re lying.”

“I couldn’t make this up if I tried. What would be the point? You already know I fucked him. Why is it a stretch,” he asks, starting to circle Homelander in a lazy, careless stroll. “To think it maybe might’ve gotten his dick a little bit hard, to picture a familiar face instead of, well. You know what you did to mine.”

Homelander can’t help his curiosity. Fatal, intolerable.

“Just his face?”

That face contorts playfully into a moue. “Well. No.”

“What else?”

Smiling, and having the gall to look bashful on this pass, Insight says, “You did smell me on him. In him. He really keeps it tight for you, doesn’t he?” He evokes a reedier affectation of that simulated voice. “Billy, oh! I just want to give you what you want, Billy!” A laugh then, acrid. Crude. “He ate that shit right up. It was kind of sweet.”

A moment later, the voice Homelander hears next is Butcher’s, saying, “Fuck me like he would. Like he’s gonna.”

Holding his hand near his face, the hills and valleys of his face shift back into place. Those thick dark glasses settle once more over the bridge of his nose. Insight flashes a grin, bone white and relentless.

“Suppose I could just be telling stories. You’d never know, right?”

And then, as if it’s just that easy, because it always has been easy for people to walk away from Homelander, he turns on his heel and disappears into the crowd. Homelander burns in his eyelids, in his fingertips, in the well of his gut. He burns all over, that face standing out in his mind.

No, he thinks. There’s no way. Not in a million years.

Butcher’s affinity for humans — well, he lived most of his life as human, didn’t he? — could be more or less forgiven. But that one? Even taking Insight’s disfiguration into consideration, he couldn’t have picked a more offensive body to attach his fantasies to. And it had been more than that, hadn’t it?

You always know exactly what the other person wants.

“What the fuck. What the fuck?”

He pricks his ears without realizing he’s doing it, singling out one voice in the whole sprawling, bustling city, until he finds it. Humming a tune under his breath over the tinny rush of tap water.

Homelander follows that music, so much louder in his ears than the concert at his back, and lets himself take to the air. The cold rush of fresh air on his face invigorates and spurs him on. He can hear the soft billow of steam fogging steel, and the pass of slippery hands under a steady stream of water. Beneath those sounds of pressure and heat, he can make out the shape of words.

“…until the stars fall from the sky…” There’s the muffled slide of socked feet on tile, a back and forth shuffle to match the idle scrubbing of sponge on food residue. “Yours until the rivers all run dry. In other words…”

A taste like soured milk lingers on Homelander’s tongue getting eyes on the man in question. Oblivious to his audience, he sways in place. As if slow dancing with the ornamented plate in his hands.

Once more with feeling, Homelander says, “What the fuck.”

The cold anger he’d felt wither at the pitiful sight before him flares up, renewed. 

This man?  

Really? 

He’s inside the dwelling in a moment, and then that muttered singing reaches his ears at a closer frequency. His voice.

Oh, Billy. Oh.

“Baby, I’m yours,” he murmurs under his breath, running the water over a collection of spoons and catching the water in the bowl of one so it sprays him in the belly. “Ah! Sheesh. Not again.”

Homelander touches down at the mouth of the kitchen, one shoulder leaned against the wall, silently watching the show. Hugh Campbell shuts off the water, leaving bubbles all down the handle before he realizes he’s covered in dish soap and running the tap again. With his hands washed clean, he searches around for a dish towel to dry his hands and looks twice upon realizing, at last, that he isn’t alone. The dish towel flaps loosely in his hands, but he doesn’t drop it. An artist’s rendering of a robust lobster peers up at Homelander, nearly the same red as his quarry.

“Mr. Homelander,” he breathes, rooted in place, hardly moving at all. A field mouse, sighted by an owl.

He flashes a smile the likes of which he’d save for daytime TV, saying, “Pardon my intrusion.”

“No, I…that’s all right.”

Homelander lets the silence rise up between them, an insurmountable gulf. He continues to smile all the while, betraying none of his bitter hatred and resentment.

“W-what are you…uh…?”

“What am I doing here? Well, I was rather hoping you could tell me that,” Homelander purrs, “Hugh.”

“I don’t know what you mean.”

Homelander peels his shoulder off the doorless jamb and lopes nearer. To his credit, Hugh doesn’t move. Neither to run, nor to avert his frightened gaze. At least he knows well enough to be afraid.

“You know, your son has this same nervous tell that you do.” He brushes his fingertips over that pear-shaped organ, smooth leather against textured cotton. “When he lies, his little heart skips.”

He smooths his hand to lay flat against that soft, delicate chest cavity and taps the paddle of his hand in a gentle parody of a rapidly climbing heart rate. Hugh Campbell swallows hard, his Adam’s apple dropping and lifting with the nervous motion. Humming thoughtfully, Homelander swipes his hand idly in the direction of a clavicle, testing the give of flesh and bone. 

How like a bird he feels. Delicate, flimsy. As likely to flutter away as to break.

“At first I didn’t see it,” he murmurs, trailing his hand down, from the hollow beneath that collar bone, to the fleshy mound of his pectoral. Thoughtlessly, he gives that soft swell of excess skin a squeeze, trapping Hugh against the counter with his body when he recoils. Spurned on by his struggle, he kneads. “But maybe he likes this about you.” He presses his nose to that flushed, brilliantly red hairline. Scents the sweet, untarnished warmth of him. “Your softness.”

His heart thunders to match the blood rushing in Homelander’s ears. Panicked now and obviously near tears, Hugh says, “Who are you talking about?”

“Guess.”

His hand resumes its downward trajectory, scaling a leaner torso than he anticipated. Soft, certainly, but not gone completely to fat. He finds more of the same behind Hugh’s fly. Inert meat, dressed smartly to the left. He gets a look at it just for good measure, before he cups it in his hand. Like a bird. As likely to sing at his feet as to burst.

“I…” Hugh gropes at Homelander’s wrist, desperate. “Billy—”

“There you go.” Homelander catches his chin in his hand, tender as a lover. “You can call out to him, you know,” he whispers, observing the brown fleck in Hugh’s right eye. “She did.”

He applies pressure to the offending weight in his hand, mind racing. Thinking of what it would look like engorged with blood, or running over with it. Thinking back to a time when he might have done this to Butcher. When he might have ripped his dick off. The trouble it would’ve saved him. 

“Stop! Stop, please, I don’t know what you’re talking about. Please,” he gasps, holding onto Homelander now. It’s all he can do. “Please! Please stop. Please, just…”

Had he looked like this to Billy, that day in Arcadia? Blubbering and weak and so wonderfully pliable? He remembers arcing into the chaos, for all that he’d felt fit to shatter beneath the violence of Butcher’s hands, his body, and his clever, wonderful mouth.

Homelander eases his grip, feeling the blood immediately rush down to the constricted organ.

“It’s not like that,” Hugh whimpers, ragged, his voice stripped raw. “He doesn’t see me that way.”

And for all that his heart crashes in his chest and the tears stand out on his steaming face, Homelander can detect no falsity in the claim. It is not the hopeless fumbling of a dead man reaching out for the nearest lie that will save him, but the confident seizure of a ripcord, steady and sure. Reliable.

“I have it on good authority he does.”

“What?” Hugh takes a great, trembling breath to fill his lungs. His exhale is a watery sob. He has to take another inhale before he can speak again, but Homelander allows it. A bead of sweat rolls down the side of his face and catches in his eyelashes, mingling and coalescing with tears. “No, he…he talks, that’s — it’s just funny, that’s — that’s all it is, is blowing off steam.”

Dryly, Homelander asks him, “Is that why he fucked a mind reader wearing your face?”

Hugh’s head snaps up, eyes wide and round and red-rimmed with crying. His face can’t get any redder, but it’s in his expression. Astonishment warring with diffidence. “He what?” he asks, on a feathery breath.

“Fucked a mind reader,” Homelander repeats, “wearing your face.”

“Uh…well, I…” Hugh blinks. “I didn’t know about that.”

“No, I suppose you didn’t.” He applies the barest pressure with the heel of his palm, fingertips curling against that plump, pitiful protuberance in Hugh Campbell’s pants. “Appeals to you, though, doesn’t it? His strength. Godlike, unstoppable.”

“…what?” It must be instinctual, how he grasps after Homelander’s hand. As if he could actually stop him. As if anyone could. “Don’t, please. Don’t!”

“Don’t what?”

“Don’t—” Hugh pauses then, shuddering. Revelation flickers across his face. “Oh…oh, he knew you’d do this.”

“What did Billy know,” Homelander presses, toying with the zip on his fly. Toying with the idea that this can go as far as he wants it to. “About what I’d do.”

“No, not him.” He takes another breath to fill his lungs, and raises his chin. “Ryan.”

Homelander meets his eyes, floored at the smile touching the corners of those eyes. Small and tired and sad, but infallible. He takes a step back, burned. Not understanding the audacity of it, or how anything could be so true. So uncomplicated.

Hugh Campbell catches himself against the counter, but he doesn’t buckle. Doesn’t fall. Neither does his smile.

“He knew you’d never choose be to someone he could be proud of.”

“Excuse me?”

“He’s such a good kid, Ryan.” Softly, like he’s thinking aloud more than he is deliberately speaking his mind, he continues, “And she must have been so wonderful.”

“I don’t know who you think you’re talking to—”

“But I do. You’ve — oh, you’ve made it impossible for me not to know exactly who you are. I read all about you in a little hospital room in Bardstown, and so did Ryan. There was such a look on his face, when he thought turning into you was this…inevitability. This curse. It’s a look I know.” He hunches his shoulders, companionable about the eyes, as if to say he gets it. “I’m sorry you haven’t learned yet how special he is. You aren’t—” He swallows, licks his lips. “Mr. Homelander, you never have as much time as you think you do, to become someone he’s happy to recognize in himself.”

Cold unravels down the nape of his neck. Bardstown, he’d said. 

“You’ve been to see him then.”

Gone back to holding himself as still as possible, Hugh confirms Homelander’s assumption with the barest tuck of his chin. Pressure crowds the back of Homelander’s throat. It crawls and seeks, cloying, suffocating.

“And my son.”

Rather than answer overtly, Hugh drops his gaze. If Homelander had the ability to listen in on his thoughts, perhaps he’d know how a little about how that went over with Ryan. But he thinks he can guess. And what’s more, he doesn’t need Hugh to tell him. Not when he can just listen for Ryan. 

Finding him is easily done. He’s always making some sort of noise, Ryan. Always talking animatedly, or humming, or on the move in some capacity. He’s talking now, with some sense of urgency to his voice.

“It’s my dad’s, he asked me to get it back for him. See?” The break in his words gives way to rustling, the sound of a zipper against flimsy polyester. “Ryan Butcher. I’m his son.”

Homelander blinks, returning to this kitchen with this man, keenly aware of his world falling apart around him. Remembering all too clearly how it felt the last time, that smell of blood and bone beneath an indifferent blue sky. That same stench of carnage had been in his nose the first time he ever got eyes on Ryan. It seemed, by now, that it would never leave them, not for long. 

He rubs his mouth with his fingertips, unwilling to reckon with the tremor overtaking his hands. And he doesn’t have to if he follows the lessons of his boyhood. What he learned back in that colorless void of a room with only a blanket to pacify him. He fills his hands, so as not to notice his inability to fill the rest of himself up. No matter what he does. It just so happens that the nearest thing at hand is Hugh Campbell.

Homelander is on him in a moment pinning him to the wall by his throat. Like an insect, indistinguishable from the rest. 

How can it be, that while he came here to punish this man, to humiliate him, to make it clear to him that none of what he holds dear is safely his — of the two of them, somehow, Homelander is the one who feels unaccountably small.

Throat leaping beneath Homelander’s implacable hand, Hugh squeaks out, “Y-you can s-still be… good.”

Shame crowds the back of Homelander’s throat. Shame. 

He can’t explain it anymore than he can trace it back to a source, and the thought of trying churns something so dangerously near to fear in him that he shies away from it, even as his hand bears down. Flashing his teeth in a snarl that tastes too much like tears, heat flares behind his eyes, and he says, “No, I can’t.”

Not now, not after everything. It’s too late.

“It’s not,” Hugh gasps, pressing his hand over Homelander’s on his throat without grasping anymore. “It’s not— too late. You can— still—”

“No! I can’t!”

A wash of red light rushes out of him. Plaster and brick and shattered glass chase him out of the window into a fluid, vibrant blue evening. He rockets up into the sky, up and up and up, until the atmosphere thins around him. There’s a scrap of fabric the color of cream clenched in his fist.

The rushing in his ears quiets, his heartbeat slowing from a storm to a canter. He blinks away tears, searching out that meddling voice within him that will banish them now as it had before. But he’s gone, nothing left of him anymore but echoes of warnings Homelander never had the patience to heed. He finds he can be sorry about that loss, out of all the others. But then, of course he can.

Did Baby lose his temper?

Homelander turns, and there she is, pouting, though her raised eyebrows betray her true feeling. 

Stormfront.

“You’re dead.”

So’s Baby Mama, she muses, her dark eyes sparkling in the cold night. She frowns exaggeratedly. So’s dear ol’ Dad. She counts on her fingers. So’s Mommy, so’s Mirrorlander—

“Mirrorlander?”

Looks and sounds just like you? Except he’s not a pussy. She pretends to wince, her teeth every bit as sharp as he remembers. Which you are, apparently. What happened to you? We had this world by the taint, and now, here you are, back to letting them walk all over you. Second-class supes, humans. She rolls her eyes. I can’t believe I let you laser my tits.

Oh, but he misses her. Warts and all.

She tips her head at him, amusement lifting the supple curve of her mouth. Darling, she says. That’s not the tune you were singing when I gave you your balls back. Is it?

He turns away from her, but she’s everywhere he tries to escape her. Inexorable. 

What’s the problem, bud? You had the answer right in front of you before you went and got sidelined by a penis. She rolls her lips together, rosy pink giving way to bloodless white. What, like you forgot? Come on. You know you didn’t.

And she’s right. He didn’t.

Billy Butcher’s Modern Cure for the Problem of Supes, she says with a flourish. Her perfect grin turns wolfish. You want back what you lost, don’t you?

He stares at her, powerless to do anything but. And this is what she’s offering him, isn’t it? The power to make anyone powerless. The power to truly, undeniably, irrevocably be God.

This is what you want.

Except…

“No,” he says. “I…no.”

It’s impossible to track the gradients of change in her smile that make it coy and conspiratorial one moment, and cruel the next. But he feels it, even if he can’t explain it. And what’s more, he recognizes its comportment from his own face. An expression he’s made countless times before.

She is his dark mirror, he realizes. Now, just as much as then. His disappointment is a living thing at the back of his voice, suffocating him.

A dimple appears in the corner of Stormfront’s mouth.

What’re you gonna do, John? Run to your sweetheart and beg him to take you back? She shrugs grandly, mischief playing about her eyes like tendrils of lightning. Like you didn’t beat the shit out of him and leave him for his mother to find? Like you didn’t brutalize Mr. Middle America back there? She scrunches her nose, a wrinkle forming over the narrow cartilage. See, the bitch of it is? You don’t need to be forgiven.

“What else is there if—” He pulls back on his voice, belatedly remembering that he doesn’t need to yell to be heard by a voice in his head. Much less speaking at all when he can’t stop his voice from breaking. “If I can’t—”

Love who I love, he thinks. 

If I can’t keep him, what else is there?

It would be pointless, then, for either of them to say what he knows now to be the truth. That he abandoned her, and vice versa, well before their time truly ran out. 

And if it was never more than mere obsession between them, then what he has with Billy—

What you have with Billy, what? She bares her teeth in a gleaming smile, the twin to his own, and plunges her hand into the pocket of her suit. As if searching for loose change, she turns it out, her dark eyes never once straying from his. Feels like falling?

The clear moonlight illuminates her white face. A soft breeze scatters her laughter amid the starless night, rending an emptiness just behind his sternum. 

Because of her, certainly, although not just. He can’t put his finger on it.

Bereft of Stormfront’s voice to distract him, the aftermath of his chaos pricks up in his ears. He hears caterwauling ambulances and fire trucks approaching the scene he left, and many harried footsteps running from the wreckage. He hears, beneath all of that, and much farther off, Ryan talking excitedly to someone.

Ryan in Kentucky, Homelander remembers with a leaden, creeping kind of despair. How did he manage to sneak away? Homelander’s been preoccupied with Vought the last week or so, sure, but he’d seen Ryan in the intervening days, hadn’t he? Surely he had, but…

He knew you’d do this, he hears again, and recoils, clenching his hand into a fist. 

A torn scrap of fabric thinly protests where it’s trapped between his fingers and his palm, pliable, worn cotton against stiff, uncreased leather.

You can still be good.

Homelander carefully tunes his hearing away from the destruction behind him and carries himself out toward Bardstown, toward the only living family he has left in the world. Toward Ryan, and Butcher. The cool night all around him dries the even colder sheen of tears all down both his cheeks.

He touches down on the roof of the hospital almost as soon as he’s made up his mind what’s to be done, and waits, easing his thumb one way over the scrap in his hand, and then the other. He listens to Butcher’s heart monitor, steady and ineffable with sleep. Even over the wailing sirens of emergency responder vehicles, and of people crowding that busy, frantic street so far behind him, Butcher’s heartbeat rings out so much clearer.

He can’t believe Butcher’s still here. Homelander felt better almost the instant the temporary serum wore off, and Butcher was much the same back when he was first injected with the Compound V. It seems so mundane, his little hospital bed and the IV stand immediately adjacent pumping him full of drugs.

Even wilting in his papery gown and with an ugly yellow blanket pulled up to his chin, he still stops the breath in Homelander’s lungs. He’s so beautiful and he contains so much, and Homelander loves him. He loves him. How wonderful it had been when they could pretend it was something else. When they could call it a game that they liked to fight as much as they liked to fuck.

But there’s no game anymore, and Homelander’s done pretending.

Finally the elevator climbs higher than the standard two floors to bring its passengers up to the roof. The doors chime open, and out walks Ryan, flanked on both sides by Butcher’s cohorts: Marvin Milk, Serge the Frenchman, the Female, Starlight, and of course, young Hughie. They’re clearly on high alert but standing back, giving Ryan some modicum of privacy. Clearly they aren’t forcing him to be here. If anything, they’re here to protect him. 

What a thought that is. It burrows down deeply into Homelander’s sternum and sinks barbed roots into his heart. He thought they were past this fear. He thought he had become a safe place by now.

“Dad,” Ryan says, Butcher’s necklace catching golden in the orange overhead lights. Like bottled sunshine. He frowns a little harder, scanning Homelander’s face from a careful distance. “What’s wrong?”

“Nothing. I’m here to bring you home.”

“I can’t go,” he says, sounding and looking, for all intents and purposes, genuinely sorry. “I have to be here for Butcher.” He bunches his hands up into fists, more an accumulation of nervous energy than a manifestation of anger. “One of us has to be.”

Blood singing shrilly in his ears and behind his eyes, Homelander says, “What did you say?”

“I said I have to be here.” Ryan’s lip trembles only once, and then he raises his chin, feet firmly planted beneath himself, as if it means more to him to stand on his own than to rely on his powers. “I’m not leaving him alone again.”

“You are if I say you are.”

Young Hughie takes a step forward, to be promptly and cleverly blocked by Starlight. Homelander glances indifferently in their direction, feeling oblivion pull and tear at him. It rises up in him, bigger than he is, and stronger, made of steel in a way even that other side of him, Mirrorlander, never was. It cuts him away from himself, cuts him out of the world, out of feeling. Rips the bottom out of his perception, so that all there is, is a single tone in his ears.

“Did you have something to add?”

Hughie Campbell clenches his teeth so hard it’s a wonder they don’t break. Homelander remembers that color high in his cheeks. The boy has the same tendency to flush as his father.

“Ryan,” he begins, only for his son to cut him off.

“I’m staying! Dad, I…I couldn’t leave him. Not even if I wanted to, but I don’t want to, and…” He raises his hands to his eyes, crying just like Homelander was crying before he came here. “And I don’t understand! How could you do that to him! How could you just go? Didn’t it mean anything to you to see him like that? Didn’t it make you sad?”

Homelander can’t answer, lest he break. And if he breaks now, he’ll never be whole again.

Ryan’s face crumples at his silence, but he doesn’t look surprised. Nor does he look ashamed of the salt stains standing out on his face. He only dashes the tears from his eyes once, ostensibly so he won’t be blinded by them.

“I love you, Dad, but I love Butcher, too,” he sobs, surging forward to clasp Homelander in a hug that drops into him like the plunging warmth of a hot drink. Muffled by Homelander’s suit, Ryan continues, voice thick and ragged  with tears, “And right now he needs me, but you don’t, and that means I can’t go with you. Okay? I’m sorry if that hurts you. But you hurt me, too, when you hurt him.”

If I can’t keep him, he thinks, looking into his son’s streaming blue eyes. Becca Butcher’s eyes, filled to spilling with misery Homelander created. For both of them. What else is there?

Homelander presses his hand to Ryan’s hair, thinking of his own father. Thinking how much it mattered to him, then and now, to be better. Thinking how that was the whole point— to be more than the sum of the efforts that came together to create him. More than Vought, more than Soldier Boy, more than a fucking petri dish.

“Ryan,” he says, gripping Ryan’s shoulder and dropping down to one knee before him. “Ryan, it’s not what you think, buddy—”

“You fought. And then he stopped, but you didn’t.”

Hesitating, Homelander feels himself blinking rapidly in lieu of responding with words. It was a mistake to give ground, to let himself thaw beneath the first touch of kindness. Ryan gives him a look then that Homelander would misunderstand if he could, but he can’t. Not now that he knows Ryan looks at him and sees a broken future. A curse.

Like maybe he associates that smell of hot blood and shorn bone with his dad as much as Homelander has come to associate it with him.

“Ryan, it was real! And when he couldn’t pretend it wasn’t anymore, it scared him!”

In a small, confused voice so near to hysterics, Ryan asks, “That’s why you dropped him out of the sky?”

Homelander stops with his mouth hanging open, remembering Billy with his pocket turned out over dark rural backwoods, and then Stormfront with her pocket turned inside out, so many city lights beneath her, glittering like her eyes. Like the lightning at her fingertips.

Here with you’s just where I wanted to fucking be.

Billy Butcher’s Modern Cure for the Problem of Supes. What else could he have thought to find there that would’ve made the slightest bit of a difference? 

But oh, if that was true—

If he’d meant to kill them both that night—

“Dad?”

“It was real,” he whispers. “No, no, it was real.”

“I know.” Ryan’s throat clicks around a hard swallow. “Butcher told me.”

“Told you what?”

“Ryan,” Starlight says in a soft voice, reaching carefully for Ryan’s hand.

“No!” Homelander points a red-tinged glare in her direction, and she backs away, shielding Hughie with her own body. “What did he say to you?”

For a moment, Ryan stares at him, dark circles under his eyes and a hollowness to them that Homelander recognizes the same way he recognized Stormfront’s hateful smile.

“He said we could’ve been a family,” Ryan whispers, pulling away.

At first Homelander won’t let go of him, but unbelievably, Hughie ducks Starlight’s protection to take Ryan’s hand in his own. The Female is quicker than Starlight to react, and she closes rank on Hughie’s other side, making a shield out of her body to match the one Starlight had made.

With the authority of an indestructible man, rather than a highly breakable one, Hughie Campbell says, “If he wants to be done, he’s done,” and steps back, with Ryan in tow.

All day, people have been walking away from him. All day, people have been telling him to his face just what he’s raged against his whole life — that he’s inadequate, that he’s weak, that he’s not what they hoped he could be. Not Madelynn’s media darling, not Edgar’s profit margin, not Noir’s friend, not Stormfront’s tyrant, not his father’s son or his son’s father.

Not Billy’s family.

He’s just a man on his knees, somehow, with nothing to show for his strength but a torn piece of sweater still caught in his fist. There are many lives represented by this piece of fabric. Right now, all he sees is his own.

“Now I see what your father meant,” he says in a deadened voice. 

Hughie stops, turning to look at him. The Female takes Ryan’s hand in her own when Hughie lets him go.

“What did you say?”

Homelander unfolds to his full height, painfully aware of the swishing of his cape behind him. It makes him feel larger than he is, like he takes up the correct amount of space. Words clipping along at a brisk, impersonal tempo, he says, “You know, I’ve known that you’re a consistent pain in my ass. I’ve known that you’re basically a tiny dog in a human’s body,” he adds, sandwiching his hands for emphasis, “but I couldn’t have known before tonight that it didn’t actually start with you. Did it?”

He turns his hand over, the flattened piece of fabric laying in his palm like a crushed mouse.

Young Hughie walks closer, gaze fixed intently on the bit of material twitching gently in the breeze. His eyes when he raises them to Homelander’s face are wide and glassy, unseeing.

“What the fuck did you do,” he whispers.

Aware of the tumult at his back, Homelander whispers, “What do you think I did?”

He could sidestep the fist at his jaw, if only for Hughie’s sake, but he doesn’t, and he feels every bone in Hughie’s hand break, even if Hughie doesn’t. He gets in two solid hits before his friends, Marvin and Serge, cart him off, screaming and struggling and weeping.

“What the fuck did you do? What the fuck did you do? I’ll kill you!”

He turns his sights next on Ryan, hiding now behind The Female, and behind Starlight, too, when she steps in front of them.

“Homelander,” she says, tears in her voice as much as in her eyes. “That’s enough.”

But why should that be up to her? Backing down and letting himself be controlled only ever earned him mockery and contempt. This is his world, and Stormfront was right. He doesn’t need to be forgiven. It’s them. They’re the ones. They should be on their knees. They should have to know what it feels like, to have nothing. To be nothing. To feel nothing.

He feels nothing.

“Ryan,” he says.

Starlight holds her hand out, and the orange overhead lights strain and flicker, sparking first through her eyes and then in the palm of her hand. Beneath the whining of electricity, young Hughie cries and cries.

“Don’t make me do this,” Starlight pleads.

The red sight warms his vision, hotter than tears and brighter than the sun, but pain, sharp and vibrant, stops him. He blinks, pressing his fingers to the twin scorch marks eating through the pectoral padding on his suit.

“Ryan?”

“Stop fighting!” His eyes are still magma red and steaming where his tears can’t coexist alongside such tremendous heat. He’s holding Starlight tightly about the waist, his cheek smashed in closely against her ribs. Shaking all over and sounding more like a child than ever, Ryan wails, “Please! Just stop!”

“Ryan—”

“Why does it always have to be like this with you? Why does someone always have to get hurt?” He lets go of Starlight and steps forward, eyes gone back to blue. “If you were really as strong as you think you are then you wouldn’t have to be so mean to everyone! And Mom wouldn’t have been sad because of you!” Ryan surges forward to pound his little fists against Homelander’s chest, his shoulders, any part of him he can hit. “She wouldn’t have even been there the day I killed her if you hadn’t taken me! You’re the same as you’ve always been. You don’t care who you hurt or how scary you look or how hard it is to keep defending you, and I can’t do it anymore, Dad! I can’t! I hate you! I hate you!”

Ryan’s eyes flash red, and this time, without a second’s hesitation, the red sight pours out of him, clean across Homelander’s face. As effective as any slap.

“Go away! I want you to go away!”

Without a thought for anything but cutting his tirade short, Homelander uses the super speed to clap his hand over Ryan’s eyes, shutting them. Maybe he uses more force than strictly necessary, but it stops Ryan from lasering him again. He still swings wildly with his hands, hardly even in the appropriate shape for hitting. But how would he know?

Either from losing his footing or from Homelander applying too much pressure, or perhaps from both, Ryan buckles and falls roughly onto his seat on the rooftop. Looking more dazed than anything, he holds his hands to his face, showing the gravel embedded in his palms. Hughie steps forward then, almost involuntarily, by the looks of it. He almost doesn’t notice Homelander’s eyes on him, taken up with Ryan as he is.

“I’ve had just about enough out of you,” Homelander snaps, turning to him, letting loose the red sight targeted straight at Hughie’s tear-stained face, and then two things happen in rapid succession.

The first is that Ryan shoves him, hard, and the second is that the roof access door bangs open.

Homelander meets Butcher’s eyes as he’s catching himself. God, but he’s everything. If Homelander is a broken future, then Butcher is possibility. He’s hope. A dream of what could’ve been. 

“Hughie?” Starlight says, holding her hand out to touch his shoulder.

Butcher’s gaze snaps away from Homelander’s then and lands on Hughie. His face is intact, lucky boy, but a great jet of blood the color of midnight pumps out of the wound in his neck. He flaps a mangled, deflated hand near the gushing mess of it, visibly paling even in the orange light. Blood runs all down the length of his forearm.

“Oh, God,” he squeaks. “I’m…I’m okay? I-I think…I’m…”

His legs fall out from under him, but Butcher and the others rush forward to catch him, to keep him, to protect him. 

There’s no place here for Homelander. There never was, and so he leaves. Chaos heralds him in one direction, and chaos screams at his back in another. The smell of blood and bone burns in his nose.

Chapter 18: fear &amp; wonder

Chapter Notes

Tw: wounds, pain meds, and suicidal thoughts/ideation

Everyone’s talking all at once when Hughie hits the loose gravel rooftop. Everyone’s moving and reaching for him, trying to figure out how bad it is. Whether they’ve got seconds or minutes.

“Hughie, move your hand,” Annie’s saying, tears in her voice, though her hands are steady. “Let me see, okay? Hughie?”

But Hughie ain’t listening. He keeps his palm clamped tight over his neck, lips moving all the while, his voice a breaking, pleading thing: “My dad. Annie, my dad— you have to— call my dad—”

“We’ll…” Annie swipes the back of her wrist across her eyes. “Hughie…”

“We’re gonna call your dad, son,” Butcher snaps, trying his luck at peeling Hughie’s fingers back without hurting him, “but you gotta let us help, all right? Move your fucking hand!”

Right back to weeping, Hughie lets go of the seal he’s got pressed over his neck.

“Il ne saigne pas,” Frenchie mutters, holding the flashlight on his phone out for better visibility. “No more blood that I can see. Did it cauterize?”

MM passes his hand over Hughie’s eyes so he won’t be blinded by the fucking light, saying, “If it did, we can’t trust that shit to stay plugged up. No sudden moves, Hughie, okay? You’re gonna be fine.”

“No, I’m not gonna be fucking fine!” Hughie shouts hoarsely. “Somebody call my fucking dad!”

“Jesus, Mary, and fucking Joseph, Hughie, do I gotta fucking sit on you to make you quit? I’ll call your fucking dad. Frenchie, anyone” he says, motioning for a phone to be given to him, but no one moves. No one fucking breathes. Butcher looks around. “What?”

No one’ll look at him, ‘cept Kimiko. She meets his eyes, and—

Just then, Hughie lets out a sob that shakes through the whole of him, a full-body-wracking ordeal that digs right under Butcher’s skin. An outcome he can’t begin to comprehend and one he won’t accept under any circumstances.

“Someone give me a fucking phone.”

“Butcher,” Annie says, soft.

“Oh, my God,” Hughie moans, starting to move again, starting to fuss, and then, right there as they’re all watching, a jet of blood shoots out of his neck. “Oh, no.”

“Fuckin’ nursery school,” Butcher curses, pouncing on Hughie proper, open-back hospital gown and all. “Annie, Kimiko— one of you— go down and get help. Frenchie— light.” He gropes around with his fingers, trying to stop up the bleeding. “MM!”

“Fuck, uh— fuck, try—” He moves Butcher’s hand up half an inch. “Press in, man. Just— firm.”

“Where are we on getting a fucking doctor up here, lads?” Butcher yells, taking it as an answer when he sees Kimiko and Ryan both run and leap off the ledge. “Hughie? You still with us?”

“My dad…”

“Your dad’s all right,” Butcher tells him, leaving no room for anything else. He maintains pressure just where MM told him, and when he tries, he can see where the pinched off tissue wrenched and came open, where it’s still leaking out around his fingers. He nods at MM. “Get his head.”

“What’re you doing?” MM asks, but there he goes, supporting Hughie’s head.

“I can grab it better, but—” 

He moves his hand and loses another gout of blood out of Hughie’s artery, just for a second. The next time he situates his fingers, the seal is as airtight as he can get it. Hughie tenses up, but again, it’s only for a second before he goes lax.

“You’re okay, Hughie,” Annie murmurs, brushing the tears off his cheek.

“Yeah,” he whispers, shaking some, but remembering in increments how to breathe.

Butcher seizes this opportunity while they’re staring at each other to catch Frenchie’s eyes, Frenchie who’s still got his phone out. He picks up on what Butcher’s asking of him and doesn’t waste any time looking sad. He dials a number and walks off, phone pressed tightly to his ear.

“Butcher, I better not ever hear you say,” Hughie mumbles, “that you fingered me on top of a hospital in Kentucky.”

With his attention split between the ringing on Frenchie’s phone and Hughie beneath him, it takes him a second to hear what Hughie’s said. When it hits him, though, he laughs. 

“I got my full arse out here to give you the all-star treatment, and that’s how you’re gonna be?”

He glances over at Frenchie. Sees him disconnect and redial, the stony expression on his face never budging a bit. He doesn’t turn to meet Butcher’s eyes, just listens to the phone ring.

“I think my hand’s broken,” Hughie says on a sigh.

Butcher won’t risk dislodging himself to look, but he sees Annie turn to assess the damage. The fall in her face tells him exactly how bad it is. MM’s watching her face, too, and he comes to the same conclusion.

“You’ve got a whole-ass hospital underneath you,” MM says. “You’re gonna be fine.”

Frenchie hangs up and tries again.

Movement near the ledge draws his eyes, and he looks up, spotting Kimiko first and then Ryan, flying them both up with his arms about Kimiko’s waist. They touch down on the roof together and come to check on Hughie.

“The doctors are coming,” Ryan says. “I, um. I blew up a lamp.”

“Holy shit,” Hughie mutters, his laugh tapering into a wet cough.

“They didn’t believe me when I told them what happened,” he rushes to explain. Then, looking from Hughie to Butcher, he adds, “Is he gonna be okay?”

“Yeah, monkey.”

“You did good, Ryan,” MM tells him, pressing an open hand to Ryan’s shoulder.

Butcher watches Kimiko sign to Frenchie as she crosses to him. He takes the phone away from his ear, redialing with his thumb, and signing with his free hand. He doesn’t say the words out loud, but this time he does look up to meet Butcher’s eyes. The stoic veneer to his face slips the tiniest bit to go with the shine in his eyes. 

Stung, Butcher looks away, but then he’s forced to look at Hughie instead. He’s already looking right at Butcher, finally gone calm.

“I wish I…” He swallows with difficulty. “…knew what my dad said to him.”

“You’re gonna ask him when we get outta here,” Butcher says, ignoring the trilling of a line he can hear ringing through and never connecting. “All right, Hughie?”

“Butcher…” His eyes fill up with tears again, but his face stays relaxed. “I don’t— what is that?” He tries to look around Butcher and down at himself without moving too much. “Is that…? Annie—?”

She digs his phone out of his pocket, Christie Lee by Billy Joel rocketing clear and loud across the rooftop until Annie picks up the call, dropping them back into silence. All of them strain their ears to listen.

“Hello?” she says, and then, lighting up, “Hugh!”

Hughie’s eyes slip shut, more tears squeezing out of him when Annie switches the call over to speakerphone. His mouth squashes into a wobbly line, trying not to make any noise.

“Hughie, are you there? Can you hear me, Son?”

“I hear you, I’m here.”

The lift doors ping open, and a full team of doctors runs out onto the roof, armed with a gurney and various other odds and ends. MM takes the phone from Annie so she can stick with Hughie.

“Dad, stay on the phone, okay? Dad?”

“I’m here, Hughie. I’m right here.”

“Please get off of the patient,” one of the doctor says, approaching.

“Not gonna happen. I’m pluggin’ up a massive bleed here, mate.”

“We can’t transport you this way.”

One of the farther off floodlights bursts, the rest of them straining. Butcher looks around and up, seeing first Annie’s fist all made of warm crackling light, and then her eyes, gone the same way. 

“How about you try?” she says.

“You heard the lady.”

Between the combined manual efforts of four doctors, Annie, and MM, they get Hughie on the gurney with Butcher still sitting pretty over top of him. His arse is cool in the breeze, but Hughie’s bleed is under control. The blood stays in his neck, and his dad’s tinny voice follows them into the lift on the gurney next to Hughie’s head. It’s a better turnout than Butcher’d really let himself hope to get for a minute there.

“Hugh, what’ s happening over there?”

“Uh, you know,” Hughie says vaguely, his voice coming out thready but strong given the circumstances. “Near death experience. Nothing new.”

“I take your meaning. Was it him?”

A muscle in Hughie’s cheek twitches. “Yeah, it was.”

“Are you okay?”

“Yeah,” he says, a lone tear escaping. “Are you?”

“I’m okay.”

Hughie nods slightly. The doors ping open and they’re off to urgent care. Annie and Ryan are in the lobby ready to chase after them, but they get no farther than the OR doors. When the noise settles and they’re waiting on another doctor to oversee, everyone else fusses around getting prepped for surgery. Butcher stays on Hughie. Can’t go anywhere else, nor would he.

“Will you stay on the phone with my dad after they kick you out?”

“Yeah, I will.”

“Thank you.” He sighs, eyes slipping shut. “When you come for a king, you better not miss. Who said that?”

“Emerson,” Butcher tells him.

Eyes still shut, Hughie’s eyebrows bunch down.

Taking great pains to come off as officiously as possible, Butcher adds, “Though you are paraphrasing, love.”

“I don’t understand. Who’s the king in this scenario?”

“Well,” Hughie ruminates, eyes drifting back open. “I’m not the one who missed.”

Butcher finds there’s a smirk pulling at the edge of his mouth. He fucking loves it when Hughie gets on his unstoppable beat. It’s contagious, it is. “Hugh, darlin’? How’d you ever raise such a menace, huh?”

“By raising a kid,” Hugh says, bless him.

Something bigger than fondness twists behind Butcher’s breastbone. Before he can open his mouth and act on it, the doors bang open and the doctor everyone’s waiting for finally makes an appearance. It’s a game of musical chairs then, trading Butcher’s fingers for a clamp and medical tongs.

“You take care of him,” Butcher shouts, letting himself get ushered out of the room and into the hall. He’s covered in red. He hadn’t noticed till now. Raising the phone to his ear, he leans back against the wall and asks, “What the hell happened, Hugh?”

“I was going to ask you that.”

“Hughie caught a laser in the neck. He’s in surgery. Are you all right? Sounded like— for a second there, at least—”

“I know. Billy, I… I think he wanted it that way.”

Dread pits deep in his stomach. He can taste bile on the back of his tongue. “What did he do to you?”

The resounding silence hits him like a condemnation, but he don’t break it. He asked a question; he’ll wait to hear the answer.

“Well, he tore a hole in my favorite sweater, to say nothing of my phone and— oh, the apartment. But he didn’t hurt me.”

Butcher relaxes, but— “Not the oatmeal jumper?” he says.

Hugh laughs, startled. “That’s the one.”

“Damn it.”

“Billy,” Hugh says, laughing again in that small way. Soft, but with something else in it, strained-like. “Thank you. For keeping Hugh safe for me. I can’t think of how to ever repay you for that.”

“I can think of one way,” he says, on high-alert still and listening, listening so hard he thinks he can catch the sound of Hugh’s heart running amok through the phone. 

“What’s that?”

“Tell me why your ticker’s pounding so hard right now.”

“You can hear that?”

Butcher closes his eyes, concentrating. He can hear everything when he puts his mind to it. Can hear the drip-drip of anesthesia running down Hughie’s IV into his veins, can hear Ryan showing Kimiko a game on his phone back the way they’d come, he can even hear John, fucking git, crying and slow-tugging way up high in the atmosphere, little tufts of ice forming like snowflakes in the ends of his hair, in his eyelashes, and at the tip of his dick. Butcher swings back around to Hugh, hearing tears and a heartbeat and—

“Somethin’ else, too. It’s like… a trickle.” He opens his eyes. “You’re not bleeding?”

“N-no, but…” Hugh sighs. “I don’t want you to worry about me.”

“You said he didn’t hurt you,” Butcher says, dialing back his voice. “Was that a lie?”

“It’s just… it’s bruises. I’ve— I’m a little…”

“Where?”

There’s quiet again, and this time Butcher recognizes the shame in it. He bites cheek so hard he tastes blood, but he makes himself wait without interrupting. He has to; he remembers well enough that he gave Becca this choice once. There’s no taking back the fact that she’d needed someone and it wasn’t him. He ain’t making that mistake twice in one lifetime.

“It’s nothing. I’m okay,” Hugh says, the crack in his voice betraying him. 

“Send me a picture then if you can’t talk about it.”

The pause then is a little more fraught, and Butcher don’t know what to do with it. It makes him feel the same way he’d felt as a lad stood in front of his dad, powerless and all of two inches tall.

“Hugh, whatever it is, he did it on account of me. I’m sorry, I’ll make it right. Some way I will.”

“I appreciate that. I really do, Billy, it’s just— I-I can’t send you a picture. N-not of everything. Some of it, sure, if you want me to, I will, but…” Hugh sighs, blustery, and then there’s a sound of shuffling and the unmistakable pull of a zipper. A pained little grunt accompanies the slide of skin on skin. “This isn’t exactly the way I wanted you to… see the rest of me.”

Butcher stops, distantly aware of the heat rushing to his face. The swoop in his stomach wars with the knot of anger tearing at his throat. “He touched you fucking where?”

He must make too much noise then because one of the doctors comes out to shoo him out into the lobby. Butcher shoulders the phone and pushes through the OR doors. Annie mobs him straight away.

“How is he?”

It takes Butcher a second, but once he gets himself seeing through the correct number of walls, he can track where in the surgery the doctors are at.

“They put him under to get the bleed sorted. ’s just gonna be a matter of stitching him up.”

“Thank God.”

“You get that, Hugh?”

“I got it.”

“Good cuz I ain’t done with you.” To Annie, and then to the lads and Ryan, when they jog up to him next, “I gotta take this. You lot wait for him to come out, yeah?”

Annie hesitates. Says, “Um,” and unzips her hoodie to lasso him with it. She ties it around his waist like a makeshift fig leaf for his bare arse. She’s a real one, that Annie January.

“Ta, darlin’.” 

“Is that Mr. Campbell on the phone still?” Ryan asks, budging up under Annie’s arm to look up at Butcher with his sad puppy eyes. “Can I talk to him?”

Butcher looks at him, moving Hughie’s phone over to his other side so he can drop his cleaner hand in Ryan’s hair. “Whaddya say, Hugh? You feel up for that?”

“I’d love to talk to Ryan.”

“All right then. I’ll allow it, but I still ain’t done with you.”

“I understand,” Hugh says, with an audible smile in his voice.

Butcher hands the phone off to Ryan. He catches Annie’s hand in his before he walks off with it, chattering away. 

“Perhaps we see what we can do about cleaning you up, Monsieur Charcutier.”

Holding his bloodied hands out in front of him, he can kinda see Frenchie’s point. Kimiko gives him a vigorous pat on the shoulder that still makes his teeth rattle and heads for the squat little chairs Annie and Ryan are sat in. 

“C’mon, Butcher. Won’t take but a minute.”

Butcher pulls funny looks all the way to the bathroom, then he pulls a funny look on himself when he catches sight of his reflection. He’d been feeling better since he got back the use of his one eye, but he’d forgotten what a sorry mess he looked around the fucking eye. Bruises everywhere. Does Hugh look like this? Does he look like this everywhere?

MM sighs, running the tap. “You know what you look like, right?”

“If you say a fucking domestic, I will rip this sink right outta the wall.”

“Sure,” Frenchie says, grandly. “Then they can bill us for infrastructure as well. Good plan.”

“Hands, tough guy.”

Butcher rolls his eyes and sets about washing his hands. The blood what’s dried takes more vigorous scrubbing to come out. “He fucking put his hands on him,” he says over the rush of water in the sink. “Hughie’s dad.”

“Fuck,” MM mutters.

“Is he very injured?”

“He says no.” Butcher scrubs at his neck for blood spatter and looks no higher than the stains as he’s washing them out. “Just bruised.”

MM sets his hand on Butcher’s arm. He doesn’t say out loud how bad just bruised can look. Doesn’t need to, does he? Not when it’s stamped all over Butcher like a fucking watermark. 

If he wanted to, MM could say a whole host of things to Butcher now about how this is all his fault. About how he’s always out courting trouble of the catastrophic sort, and this time the consequences caught up to him. About how someone else had to pay for his hubris — again — with the flesh off their own body. Just says what kinda man MM is that he don’t, even if they both know Butcher would, were they on the back foot. MM, though. 

He just keeps his hand gentle on Butcher’s arm and says, “What’s our next move?”

And Butcher, well. He don’t rightly know.

He can’t make himself say as much, though. Not with the weight of everything so near and fresh in his mind, like he’s the one who’s been cut into with a look and terrorized tonight. 

How can he stand here and say that’s what it feels like? He don’t got the right, not when it’s because of him it happened at all. Not when it’s Hughie’s blood he’s washing off his hands. Not when the most recent bruises  to bloom in the shape of John’s hands are flowering under Hugh’s skin.

“I have a suggestion,” Frenchie says.

Butcher switches off the taps. All the better for them to hear in his voice those things he can’t stand to say. 

“Let’s hear it.”

Frenchie tears off a section of paper towel. He braces Butcher by the elbow with one hand and pats him dry with the other, methodical, eyes lowered to the task he’s undertaken. “How did Petit Hughie put it to you?” he asks softly, in a voice like servitude that Butcher’s heard before but never much in his direction. More than obedience, it is. Near to reverence. He raises his eyes to Butcher’s. “Try a little tenderness?”

“Go back to that fucking psychopath,” Butcher mutters, snatching his hand back. “Licking my fucking wounds?”

And cuz he ain’t completely immune to talking shit, MM mumbles, “Think the point is he’d probably do it for you.” 

Butcher whirls on him. MM doesn’t flinch.

Calm as anything, he says, “You’re pissed off. I would be, too. We coulda lost Hughie tonight, and he might’ve died thinking his dad was dead. You think that don’t rattle me?” MM’s eyes catch the cold lights of restroom, shiny for just a second in time. “I don’t wanna ask you to do this, but it’s you, Butcher. You do what we can’t. That’s true now more than it’s ever been.”

“À la fin, Butcher,” Frenchie says gravely. “We finish it.”

Butcher turns away from them to rip out some more paper towels. He gives himself a cursory pass down each arm, across the back of his neck, and down his cheek. The complaint at the back of his throat stirs and stirs, wanting to fill the space with noise. He was supposed to be out. He wasn’t supposed to have to go back, but who’s it help for him to whinge and drag his heels now? 

MM’s right, and Frenchie, too. The path forward’s easy enough to see just as long as he mans up and does what’s required of him. 

So why’s it burning up in him like a scream?

Why’s it hurt him so much that a supe did what supes do? Cunts like Homelander blow through entire lives in a night, and that’s the whole point. That’s why Butcher’s been on this path to rid the world of the lot of them ever since Becca up and vanished from his life, and nothing’s changed since then. It’s still worth it. He just thought it’d be enough to buy that victory with his life. He thought it would’ve ended there, dropped down into Kentucky pay dirt, no telling where the bits stopped being him and started being John. It was supposed to break his body. Not Hughie’s, not his dad’s.

“To be kept by you is still a matter of importance to him,” Frenchie says, gently, gently. “Or else the mere sight of you tonight would not have been enough to stay his hand. But ultimately, Monsieur Charcutier, if you are done, then you are done, and we will find another way.”

“There ain’t no other way,” Butcher tells him, yanking the door open.

Ryan comes bounding over once he’s re-emerged, a bright, easy smile on his face. “Hughie’s out of surgery. They’re gonna move him to your room as soon as they’re done making his hands better. Do you wanna go see him?”

“I might step out for some air.”

“Can I come? Oh, I don’t have the phone. I gave it to Annie in case Hughie’s awake when they finish.”

“That’s good.” Butcher drops his hand to Ryan’s shoulder and starts walking with him toward the main entrance. “Did you see him?”

“For a second. They dressed him up like you except he had a big square stuck to his neck.”

They find a bench out front to occupy, Ryan cozying in close to Butcher’s side like there ain’t room for him to stretch out. Butcher gets it, though. He ain’t exactly leaning away either. ‘Sides, in just his drafty gown, it’s cold out. Even with the help of Annie’s hoodie.

“How’re you feeling, after all that?”

Ryan looks up at him and then off to the side, thinking, thinking. He does that for a while.

“Do you remember how I said that when I saw you, it was like I already knew what happened? I guess I feel like that, but when it happened to you I was scared. I’m not anymore, though. Now I’m just mad. I’m mad that he does this and nothing changes. I’m mad… that he scared Hughie and Mr. Campbell, and Mom, and that— that people just have to be scared of him if he feels like being scary. That’s not right. It’s not fair, it’s— it’s crappy.”

Butcher snorts, and Ryan darts a shy, testing glance at him from the corner of his eye, tips of ears gone tomato red. Butcher passes a hand through Ryan’s hair, ruffling it. 

“Well, look at the little spitfire.”

When it’s clear he ain’t in trouble for his bad language, Ryan relaxes slightly. Or his shoulders do. His face stays serious. “What are you gonna do?”

“Y’know,” he sighs, feeling every ache and break in his body. “I don’t know.”

Back to staring at him, Ryan fiddles with the St. Christopher medallion at his neck. He swallows and looks away, saying, “I heard you telling Mr. Campbell you’d make it right.”

“You were listenin’ to that?”

“I didn’t mean to,” Ryan rushes to say. “I’m sorry. It’s just, lately when I get upset, it’s like…”

“Everything gets real loud?”

“Yeah!” His face lights up, though he still looks embarrassed. “I caught him listening to you guys after you came home from Greece. It’s not a way I wanna be like him. Mr. Campbell was telling me how that’s something I get to choose. He said he learned that from you.”

Strange to remember the day Ryan’s talking about and think anyone mighta gleaned wisdom from him, state he was in. Wasn’t like he even had his heart in it till Hugh did the Campbell thing and made so Butcher couldn’t pretend at indifference. Had to be a superpower to be able to do that after everything, didn’t it? To be genuine in the face of frauds and sweet talk ‘em into quitting their masks? And to be able to do it from a distance, too.

“Do you like him?”

Butcher looks at Ryan, at his open curiosity. Then he looks away, trying to shrug the question off. “Sure I do. Don’t you?”

“Yeah, but.” Ryan shrugs back at him. “It just seems like maybe you like him more than that.”

A plane passes overhead, flying low enough that it’ll probably be touching down in Lexington to land. Butcher thinks about John’s promises to him, all the great many things he wanted to rip out of the sky to lay at Butcher’s feet. Up to and including the people he loved.

“Mr. Campbell’s nice, and he likes you, too.”

Butcher keeps his gaze forward. “What’s not to like?” he mutters.

“Butcher,” Ryan says, catching Butcher’s hand for emphasis. Maybe he needs the help feeling like Butcher won’t leave him again. “Sometimes a lot, but that’s on purpose and you know it. You saved Hughie up there. You saved him, and— and when he was crying and everyone was scared, you weren’t. You couldn’t be. You were just there for him, even though you didn’t have any answers either.” He pauses, a palpably grownup gravitas rolling off him in waves, until he adds, solemnly, “Even though your butt was out the whole time.”

A laugh startles out of Butcher. “Well, you got all the answers. What would you do next?”

“First we take Hughie home,” Ryan says without missing a beat. “And then, we go home.”

“You mean back to your dad?”
