“No. Home for me— I know Aunt Grace did everything she could— but home for me is still back with Mom. I thought, maybe if you feel the same way, and if you always felt like that when she was gone, then… we could figure out together where home’s supposed to be now.”

Floored, Butcher stays looking straight ahead. Becca hadn’t trusted Butcher with Ryan when he was just a cluster of cells, and the only thing to change her mind had been her dying. 

He hasn’t done right by his girl in a long time. It’s hard to know if this, what Ryan’s saying, will be a nudge in the right direction or in the wrong one. He wants it, is the thing. But it weren’t never his to have.

“You said you wanted to make it right,” Ryan prompts.

“I do wanna make it right.”

“So then let’s all go home. When Hughie’s better, we’ll drive back, and… whatever has to happen to make it safe for us, and for everyone, it happens. We make it happen.”

He’s just like Becca. 

In the way he won’t ever stand to be counted out, and in the way he’ll find the path to his own happiness on the other side of toil. In the way he won’t ever stop trying to get there, or let the journey take away that light in him, that joy. That will to keep going.

Butcher coaxes Ryan to him with an arm around his neck. Ryan readily crashes into him, flinching away a second later when he remembers Butcher’s bruises. Butcher holds him right there, though. Not minding the creeping sting of pain.

“You’re all right, monkey. I got you.”

Once he believes what Butcher’s telling him, Ryan relaxes against him. He holds out the hand not wrapped behind Butcher’s back, no flowers this time, and holds Butcher’s hand in his.

“I got you, too, Butcher.”

They sit out in the cold, clear night until MM comes to get them. Butcher ain’t technically been released from care yet, and visiting hours are nearly up. Hughie’s been asking for them besides, so they go. Sure enough, he’s wide awake and bitching up a storm as soon as they get to his room. He’s also attempting, badly, to get dressed.

“Butcher,” he crows, pointing at Butcher’s face as soon as he walks in. “Tell them I don’t need to be here!”

Turning to address the room, Butcher preciously announces, “He ‘don’t need to be here.’”

“Your air quotes are rude and offensive. Somebody just— help me with my pants, for God’s sake.”

“Hughie,” Annie scolds him, “you have three working fingers.”

Kimiko pinches at her neck.

“And a perforated artery in your neck,” Frenchie translates.

“They tied it off!”

“Sit your ass down, Hughie,” MM says, sweetly, for all that he ain’t asking.

Hughie tries one last time with his jeans, then sighs gustily, “Levi Strauss, you fucked me.”

Butcher walks over and tips him onto the bed where he and Annie split the work of making him decent. Once Hughie’s laid out, much of the fight goes right out of him. The last of the wild skittishness from the drugs lingers in his eyes.

Still in the mood for proselytizing, Hughie says, “Ugh.”

“We’ll see how you’re doing in the morning, all right?” Butcher says, pushing a hand through Hughie’s hair, feeling cold sweat against the fever heat of his scalp. “But you gotta sleep first.”

“You’ll be here?”

“’s still my room, innit?”

Hughie looks around, frenetic and then sheepish. “Oh, yeah, it is.”

“You had a big night, mon petit.”

Kimiko signs, bringing her hands around in a circle and then dovetailing one away from the other. Frenchie nods, signing in tandem with his spoken words.

“She says you have earned your rest.”

“And you have, Hughie,” Annie intones, cupping Hughie’s cheek. “Everyone’s okay, and we need you to be okay, too. So sleep.”

Hughie relents to stay covered up by the bedding, though his eyes won’t stay shut. Even after everyone’s shuffled out and it’s just him and Butcher in their rickety little beds some fifteen minutes later, his eyes bounce between points on the ceiling. Butcher leaves his shit mattress to sit on the corner of Hughie’s. He don’t say anything for a few long minutes. Just waits for Hughie to get bored of counting water stains in the tile.

“Why did he go to my dad, Butcher?”

“I’ve thought about that.”

“Yeah, so have I.”

“Hughie,” Butcher says, gentling his voice to catch Hughie’s attention out of the downward spiral the drugs are draining him into. “Shut your eyes for me, all right?” When Hughie lurches halfway into sitting up, Butcher presses him back down with one hand. “I ain’t tabling the conversation, I’m just saying. Shut your eyes.”

Hughie gives him a look, eyes red-rimmed, pupils lost and feral and spreading like ink. But he does, he shuts his eyes. Smoothing his hand across them so they’ll stay that way, Butcher continues as if there’s been no interruption.

“Musta been Insight what told him. Nobody else knows that’d talk.”

“Fucking mind readers,” Hughie mumbles, brow furrowing. “Nobody else knows what?”

“‘Bout our tumble in the sack,” Butcher says, though his mind keeps working from there.

Because surely if Insight had talked, he would’ve said it all. Not like him to do anything by half, and Butcher could kick himself, really. Insight narced on them to Ashley Barrett about Soldier Boy first chance he got. That was the whole point of him going back to the first time she put him on Butcher’s detail, and Butcher let himself get played like a fucking fiddle.

“Okay, so—” Hughie squirms, digging his shoulders against the mattress, trying fruitlessly to get comfortable. “Insight, and by extension, Homelander, got wind of your grody locker room talk. Still seems like an overreaction.”

“We are talking about the same twat?”

Hughie sighs, letting the muscles in his neck relax. Hopefully that means he’s winding down again. “I feel like this is my fault.”

“You always think everything’s your fault.”

“No, but…” Hughie twists out from under Butcher’s hand, softer now than before. When Butcher lets him go, he looks up at him through half-lidded, hazy eyes. “You said to point you at someone, and I pointed you at him. I should’ve known it’d go like this.” Eyes welling up and spilling over, he says, “Butcher, what he did to you, and now my dad…”

“Hughie.”

“I’m sorry.”

“Hughie,” he croons, catching him behind the head so he’ll look at Butcher. “That ain’t on you.”

“We had a fucking plan, man,” he moans, plaintive and inconsolable against the tide of grief, only amplified by the drugs. “What’re we gonna do now?”

“First things first, you’re gonna sleep this ugly shit off,” Butcher tells him, gripping a loose fistful of Hughie’s hair to make sure he’s listening. “And then,” he adds, summoning up Ryan’s game plan, “me and the lads’re gonna take you home to your dad. He’ll make you a whole plate of them little microwave things you like so much.”

“Pizza rolls,” Hughie says, the apples of his cheeks all smeared with tears.

Butcher tweaks Hughie’s nose. “Fucking pizza rolls.”

Smearing at his eyes with the back of his wrist, Hughie mutters, “He puts them in the oven.”

“Yeah, I’ll bet he does,” Butcher replies, moving Hughie’s hand out of the way to get at his tears for him. “Got you back to him before, didn’t I? I’ll do it again, all right?”

Hughie sniffles, completing the look of him as the last puppy in the cardboard box. God, but Lenny could look like this, too, and it pulled at these same heartstrings Butcher’d never quite managed to rip out of himself.

“How’d you know?” Hughie mumbles, eyes beginning to droop. “That he’d be okay?”

He didn’t know. He didn’t know any of this would be okay or that they’d have the smallest chance in hell at saving it. All he had known was that he couldn’t leave it.

“Same way I knew Becca would be, before.”

“I thought you didn’t know that,” Hughie mutters, letting his eyes close this time without any interference from Butcher.

“Guess I didn’t.”

“You take that habeas corpus shit pretty fucking literally.”

Butcher covers Hughie’s face with his hand to pie him off to his dreams, and sure enough, Hughie’s only response is to flash a sleepy grin, his eyes glued shut chasing rest. 

As quietly as he can, Butcher gets up to go, watching Hughie the whole time for a last ditch effort to escape, but he’s out harder than a toddler all spun out and grateful for the pillow under his head. That’s all well and good. 

If only Butcher felt the same way. He backtracks to his own bed and feels for the book tucked under his pillow. It’s Hugh’s, he knows. He’s been trying to find a minute to crack the spine on it, but between his hands and the pain meds, he hasn’t had the best luck. Not wanting to undo his efforts to get Hughie sleeping, he hunts down his phone to use that for light rather than the overheads. There’s an unread text waiting for him. He almost ignores it.

 

Hugh Daddy
I got a new phone! Worked it out with my provider so I could keep my old number. Good thing because I couldn’t send you this from the payphone I was using before.

 

Butcher opens the file and pinches to zoom in on the wine-colored imprint the shape and size of a hand spanning Hugh’s throat. His creeping suspicion about what exactly Insight said to John curdles with dreaded certainty. It was Butcher’s punishment, but Hugh caught the brunt of it. He opens a new reply, glancing surreptitiously over his shoulder at Hughie, slumbering away.

It shouldn’t have happened, he types, and then lingers with his thumbs over the screen. He deletes that and starts over. I’m so fucking sorry. 

He deletes that, too, and types something else, sending it before he can think better of it. Hugh’s reply comes back quickly. 

 

Me
Hughie just fell asleep

Hugh Daddy
Thank you for staying with him, Billy.

Me
Don’t have much choice bein busted up myself

Hugh Daddy
I don’t believe that. It’s because you’re a good man. You’ve saved him before, even when it was the hard thing to do. I’m grateful for that.

 

“I got a hard thing for you,” Butcher mutters under his breath, easing back in his bed to lay flat, as quietly as he can so he won’t wake Hughie. He’d call Hugh if he could, but at this hour he’d just get sent back to his room if the staff caught him wandering the halls. Especially after the chaos of the last few hours.

 

Me
What can I say, you Campbells are a good influence

 

He takes a picture of Hugh’s book open on his lap and sends it. Hugh, bless him, responds just like Butcher hoped he would.

 

Hugh Daddy
There it is! My book club gave me so much grief when I picked it. They were all really happy it turned up missing.

 

They go back and forth like that, text for text, whiling the night hours away. Butcher reads in between replies, sending Hugh pictures of the passages that stand out to him for one reason or another. Hugh’s replies slow down around three in the morning, but right around then, Butcher’s battery’s dwindling down into the red. 

He starts to send a goodnight text, thinks better of it on account of not being a fucking preteen on the phone with his boyfriend, and snaps a picture instead. Not a selfie, the light ain’t good enough for that, but a shot of the full moon hanging outside the window. Hugh meets him exactly in the middle, sending a picture out the window of his temporary digs. One of his gym rat friends from book club was good enough to put him up for the night apparently. There’s a leafy green plant in the window.

It gives Butcher some peace to know Hugh’s cared for, even being as far away as he is. Butcher ain’t been impatient to get home of late, but he’s jonesing now. With his phone at 4% he types: Ryan says he wants to come live with me.

He waits for a while, stirring about the chest region, and hits send.

It’s a few minutes waiting for a reply, if there’s even gonna be one, then his phone lights up. The banner display shows him the preview, and his stomach sinks reading it. He tabs over to that conversation and the doomed thread leading up to this fucking hospital room.

 

Unsaved
When are you coming home

 

Butcher tabs over to that conversation, and it’s as he’s idling over how to answer that he gets that message from Hugh he’s been waiting on.

Well, he loves you.

Knuckling at his eye, Butcher turns his phone over and presses his face into the pillow. He wonders what home’s supposed to mean to John, when he hasn’t been providing that for Ryan. Does he mean Arcadia or his fucking suite up in Vought Tower? Maybe he means the townhouse. Butcher still ain’t been by to see it yet.

He flips his phone back around and types: Why? Got another lesson you wanna teach me?

 

Unsaved
There’s no lesson. I miss you. I want to give you what you want. Even now I want to give you what you want, and I have.

Me
hows this what i wanted john

Unsaved
You wanted me to be a monster.

Unsaved
I am a monster. I’m yours.

Unsaved
And Billy?

Unsaved
You’re all mine.

 

Not even knowing how to respond to that, Butcher stares blankly at the screen. Before he can think of what to say, his phone makes the decision for him and powers down. He tosses it toward the foot of the bed and curls up onto his side, Hugh’s book braced against his chest like a shield or a raft.

Just now, he could use either.

 

/

 

At their peril, the hospital deigns to keep Hughie under lock and key for a total of four days. When he’s ready to be packed off for home, Butcher gets the honor of wheeling him out to the parking lot with an ice pack pillowed in his lap.

“This is demeaning,” he says, complainer.

“What is?” Butcher pops back, propping one foot one the back bar of the wheelchair to ride it like a scooter. He screeches to loping a halt right at the curb, taking care in how he does it to blow Hughie’s hair back. “Your chariot.”

“I hate you,” Hughie mutters, and then, seeing the aforementioned ‘chariot,’ he adds, “What the fuck is that?”

“La grand dame is your ride home, Petit Hughie,” Frenchie says, whipping the side door open. He thumps his hand on the metal siding. “Behold, Sa Majesté.”

“What happened to the minivan?”

“Had a problem with the alternator,” MM explains, hopping out from behind to wheel to walk around the front of the bus to help Hughie out of the wheelchair. “Started to break down on our way taking Connie and Mallory up to the airport. Avis wasn’t gonna have anything for us in the meantime, so we found this bad boy on Craigslist.”

They get Hughie loaded up into the bus sharing a bench seat with Ryan, and Butcher, after circling back to return the wheelchair, opens up the rear hatch to climb in over the last row of seats. Terror stands up against the seat back in front of him to get his ears scratched, and then they’re off. Hour One of a twelve-hour drive. 

Butcher alternates between staring out the window and reading from Hugh’s book. He’s nearing the 50-page mark, and it’s starting to clip along now that he’s gotten used to it.

“What’re you reading, Butcher?” Ryan asks, peering over the top of his seat at Butcher.

“‘The Greatest Love Story Ever Told,’” he reads directly from the cover.

Hughie squints, looking over. His face goes blank. “Is that my dad’s?”

“What’s it about?”

“Odd couples,” Butcher muses, maintaining cheeky eye contact with Hughie.

He’s kept Hughie off the scent of what-all Insight said to John, but it won’t be long before he puts it together. Only thing to keep him from doing it while the hole in his neck’s quite so tender is to carry on like nothing’s changed. Maybe double down. Business as usual.

“God, you’re insufferable.”

Butcher cheerfully goes back to reading, but now that he’s thinking on what happened to Hugh, his eyes skim the same paragraph a couple times without retaining it. He gives up the pretense right around Lexington and sets the book aside to pillow his arms over the back of the bench seat in front of him. 

“MM,” he calls. “What’s our ETA look like?”

“Depends. If we drive all day, we’ll probably get in around ten.”

“No road blocks,” Annie announces from the passenger seat. She’s careful not to touch the GPS. Clearly she’s in the front seat for a reason. “We should make good time.”

“God,” Hughie mumbles, sinking against his seat. “All I want is to sleep in my own bed tonight.”

Terror climbs up on the seat to lick Butcher’s face, and because he can, Butcher scoops him up easily with one arm under his chest to bring him over to the back with him.Taking after his dad, he hams it up and sprawls out, legs flopping to make room for a hand at his belly. Sweet lad.

MM takes the 68 east. They get rain on and off till they get to Charleston, and then it’s a constant downpour. For all that the bus looks like a shit heap, the windshield wipers do at least work.

“Do you guys wanna watch a movie?” Ryan asks, retrieving a tablet from his backpack. “I downloaded some at the hospital.”

“What did you have in mind, mon loulou?” Frenchie asks, twisting around in the bench seat ahead of Ryan to hear his options.

Ryan reads off a list of titles. When he gets to An American in Paris, Kimiko sits up next to Frenchie and motions excitedly with her hands and with her eyebrows. She looks like a kid on Christmas morning, touching Frenchie’s arm like she wants him to know just how jazzed she is about Gene Kelly. Between the three of them, and by the silent entreaty of everyone else to be left outta the festivities, they break out a headphone splitter to share the audio three ways. Ryan climbs up and over Frenchie and Kimiko’s seat to sit in between them, and then they’re off, ready to spend the next two hours with Gershwin.

With his row cleared out, Hughie stretches out. He’s all elbows, trying to keep his hands in his lap. “Why’re you even driving with us?” he asks, leaning his cheek against the seat.

It’s a fair question. Butcher could probably make the trip on his own in half the time if he wanted, but he’s got his reasons. “Don’t feel like choking on bugs for the next six hours. Sides,” he adds, rocking Terror like a baby. “My dog’s here.”

Plus his ribs are sore. His mobility’s better than it has been, but even with the V helping him along, he gets tired quickly and he’s still mottled all over in bruises. His strength don’t suffer any for it, though. Terror’s proof enough of that, napping happily away in the crook of his arm. 

And not that he’s given it much thought, but Butcher ain’t been out flying since the night he got all fucked up. 

It’s not that he can’t. He’s just been in the hospital. There hasn’t been any need. It wouldn’t stand out in his mind at all if John hadn’t texted him. If John hadn’t been texting him, he should say. After the first exchange, Butcher left him on for a while, but now he’s been letting the texts pile up without even opening them.

He doesn’t wanna get home ahead of the lads and have to make the call on what to do next without them. The last time he did that he tried to kill himself. Or, John. 

Tried to kill John.

“Guess I don’t blame you,” Hughie mumbles.

“For what?”

Nodding at Terror, he says, “I’d wanna stay behind with him, too.”

Like he knows he’s being complimented, Terror pays them back by letting one rip. Not a bad one, thank fuck, but enough that Hughie snorts.

“You know he was gassy on the way down, too? Drove MM insane.”

Butcher laughs, too. It just feels nice. “Always did like a drive, didn’t you, darlin’?” He bends down to leave a kiss between Terror’s eyes, spiting his ribs but hardly regretting it a bit. “Surprised me you brought him down.”

“My dad,” Hughie says, sighing. “We went to drop Terror off with him, and I guess I had a weird look on my face. He figured out something was up.”

Nodding sagely, Butcher muses, “Did you really try to ditch him at a gas station?”

“He told you about that?” he asks, eyes narrowing. “Also, no, I didn’t, I was joking. Mostly.” He gives Butcher a suspicious side glance. “What do you guys even talk about? You don’t have anything in common.”

Butcher gives him a look.

“If you say you talk about me in the same breath that you’re hitting on him every chance you get, I’m gonna re-break my hand on your face.”

“It ain’t like that, Hughie.”

“What is it like?” 

The look he gets then is more focused, like he’s really trying to understand, and Butcher shouldn’t encourage this. He should be pushing Hughie away from the truth to keep him as far away from revelation as he can, but some selfish part of him wants to clear things up. Wants to claim one of the last few things he has left that only makes him happy and nothing else. He’s still just trying to work himself up toward misdirection when Hughie’s eyes catch on his indecision, and drag.

Eyebrows pulling down, Hughie says, “Wait, do you actually… are you fucking serious?”

“Listen.”

“No, you—” Hughie struggles against the bench seat, trying to sit up and turn without jostling his hands or his neck. “You’re fucking with me. You’ve been fucking with me.”

“Hughie.”

“Oh, my God.” Mortification sharpens into dread right there on his face. “What did—” He reigns himself in, shooting a paranoid glance over his shoulder at the bench seat in front of him. When there’s no immediate interruption in their daily scheduled programming, he whips back around on Butcher and says, in a stage whisper, “What—exactly—did Insight say to Homelander?”

“I don’t know what he said,” Butcher says, more blithely than he feels. And then, because he really can’t stomach the devastation in Hughie’s eyes, he clarifies: “Your dad wouldn’t tell me.”

Disappointed, but without heat, Hughie says, “You are so full of shit.”

Butcher is, but that’s about all he can admit to presently. He shrugs and leans back in his seat, budging Terror up so his nose notches against Butcher’s shoulder like a gunstock.

“He’s lucky my hands are fucked up,” Hughie mutters.

“Didn’t stop you on that second punch.”

“That’s the beauty of damage boost.” He bounces his eyebrows at Butcher, clearly anticipating that he won’t know what he’s talking about. “Invincibility frames, dude.”

“You made that up.”

“Do I or do I not have two broken hands?”

Butcher tips his head, conceding the point. He hadn’t been up to the roof in time to see Hughie whaling on the world’s most dangerous man, but the story was consistent across everyone who had been. Hughie swung, and he didn’t miss. John— Homelander— hadn’t let him, more accurately. That superspeed of his is more the killer than any of the rest of his tools, as Butcher can well attest to by now. It’s given him a newfound respect for the Hughie of yore what stood up to A-Train. Even in spite of having seen firsthand how explosive that ability could be, he didn’t let it push him off course. Neither had Hugh, come to it. Butcher’d heard all about it.

Point is, Homelander coulda sidestepped the blows. He’d done it to put Butcher in his place the night they came to blows, so he definitely could’ve kept Hughie from making contact, but he chose not to. He chose to let Hughie smash his hands apart on his face just like he chose to keep hitting Butcher even after he blacked out. 

Wasn’t like he felt any of what come after, at least. Maybe Hughie had the right of it after all. Maybe sometimes the pain was good for something.

“I never apologized to you.”

Hughie snaps his head up. “Never apologized to me for what?”

“For the hole in your neck.” Butcher flutters his fingers for good measure. “Weren’t your fault. It was mine.”

“No,” Hughie says, slowly. “It’s Homelander’s. I don’t care if you did fuck a skeevy underwear model dick-bag or if you’ve got a weird crush on my dad. If Homelander’s so torn up about it, he can destroy a pint of Ben & Jerry’s like the rest of us. He doesn’t get to do this.”

“What’s to be done about it, d’you think?”

Hughie meets his eyes. Maybe it’s dawning on him now that Butcher didn’t really have a plan before, if he’s asking to hear one now. With his expression gone soft and open, he says, “Well, what do you wanna do?”

Kill him, he thinks. 

John and Forester both, for all the trouble they caused. Except that’s not it. Not really. Killing them would be easy, there and gone in a flash.

What he really wants is for them to languish, like they’ve been languishing. He wants them to know how it feels, to be so lost and so beaten down, that giving up feels like a viable option. That’s what he wants, and who’s he kidding if he thinks Hughie fucking Campbell won’t have an exact means and method of making that a reality?

“When we were up on that roof and you were dyin’, and maybe your dad was dead already,” Butcher says, “or when Becca was laid up against that tree — when she was goin’, and I didn’t know, but she did… there was a moment there, both times, in between hope and the other thing. What I want is for that moment to be the last good thing that ever happens to either one of ‘em.” He nudges Terror’s ear, twitching in his sleep. “Can we work with that?”

For all that he’s bedrock in that moment, there’s a flicker still of that relentless tenacity Butcher’s come to look for in Hughie. That thing in him that means he’s never gonna not stir the pot if he don’t like the smell it gives off. Not even if he’s gotta do it with two busted hands and a hole in his neck.

Invincibility frames. Who’d’a thought.

“I’ve got just the thing.”

And wouldn’t you know? Warmth uncoils from behind Butcher’s sternum. Warmth like safety and like coming home. Like faith well-invested. He smiles, and Hughie smiles back.

“Hit me with it, Son.”

“Okay,” Hughie says, holding up his hands to talk with him, like the pain can’t touch him. Like his broken bones are petty, smalltime bullshit, and not a direct consequence of the bravest, stupidest thing anyone ever did for love. “So it goes like this.”

Chapter 19: tabernacle

Chapter Notes

I realize we’re not quite done here yet, but Beguile finished her COLOSSUS of a fic For Forever that you should absolutely read, and her parting notes had me feeling some type of way, so! Here is where I declare, full in heart and ecstatic of spirit, that the place where tyrants must lament is a RIDONKULOUS love letter and receipt of purchase for one (1) goofcart for myself and for Beguile.

What is a goofcart, I hear you ask? It’s a vehicle, not unlike a box, to hold things like love and support and other such big feelings inside of it. Big B, until I have a bigger vessel, or indeed, a book, to carry all those things for you, I hope this will suffice. I’m pretty proud of everything we’ve managed to cram into it, and still, well. It’s not quite full, is it? 3>3> 3>3>

Go and read For Forever and God Help the Mister REDUX and everything else my beautiful friend has made. You’re welcome in advance. :3

Hugh’s waiting for them outside a glittering high-rise at ten sharp, stood alone on the sidewalk all bundled up in a turtleneck and pressed slacks. Put together and neat and catching rain in his hair like he didn’t nearly have his whole world blown apart a few days previous. Hughie’s scrambling to get out of the van before they’ve reached the building proper, but all he can do is wait for someone to get the door for him. 

Butcher clocks MM’s eyes in the rearview mirror before reaching across Hughie’s seat to crank the handle, and there he goes, spilling out right into his dad’s arms.

“Oh, there you are,” he’s saying, “there you are, Hughie.”

“Dad—”

He doesn’t say anything else for a hot minute. Neither does Hugh. They just hold onto each other in the cool, misting rain, streetlights and familiar smog setting the scene for their homecoming. Butcher folds his hands over top of the seat in front of him and drops his chin there, waiting. He couldn’t say for what, but then Hugh looks right at him over Hughie’s shoulder, alive. Whole.

Like the world’s most homicidal supe didn’t wring him out like a dish towel and smash a crater into the side of his home, because of Butcher.

Hugh sweeps his gaze along, from Butcher and Terror, to Ryan, Kimiko, and Frenchie, then to MM and Annie in the front. He cracks a watery smile and says, “Boy, am I glad to see you guys.”

“We’re glad to see you, too, Mr. Campbell,” MM says, polite as can be, like he ain’t been white knuckling the wheel these last twelve hours.

Annie hops down from the passenger side, ever the trendsetter, and everyone else shuffles out, following her lead. MM’s first, then Ryan, Kimiko, and Frenchie. Butcher lets himself out through the back, and when the drop down is too high for Terror to make the jump, Butcher tucks him up under his arm, like he had not too long ago in that dream of Becca. Less agreeable now than he’d been in the dream, Terror squirms under Butcher’s arm, probably just as antsy to be moving again as the rest of them are. Butcher lowers him down to the curb, and they’re of a mind, apparently, because he runs straight to Hugh to smell and harass him.

“Hey, big-little guy.”

Butcher makes a show then of stretching out and popping his joints so he won’t do something stupid like walk up to Hugh and crush him in a hug of his own. He might not have worried, though, seeing as Hugh does it for him. No fuss, no muss. He smells like baking in winter, sweet apples and warm spices, and he could’ve been dead now because of Butcher.

“Hi, Billy.”

“Hey, Hugh.”

As before, Butcher catches Hughie’s eyes over his dad’s shoulder, and counter to the murderous glare Butcher’s expecting, Hughie just looks beat. Butcher can’t blame him.

“You must be exhausted from the trip,” Hugh says, pulling away to address the group but not so far that he dislodges Butcher’s arm from around him. “I’d love it if you came up.”

Frenchie tips his head back to peer up at the shining planes of the building. He whistles. “Whose palms did you have to grease to stay in such a place,” he muses, glancing around almost wistfully at the clear, well-lit streets. “In such a…neighborhood?”

MM elbows him. Butcher has a look around for himself. Just looks like a regular street to him.

“My friends from the gym heard what happened. Actually, it’s around the corner from here. That’s been super convenient.” He laughs good-naturedly. “They’re a great bunch of guys. Very kind.”

“We don’t wanna put you out, Mr. Campbell,” MM says, ever the gentleman.

“I think you can call me Hugh by now, MM.” He steps away from Butcher to press his hand to MM’s arm, companionable, sweet. It’s as he’s gravitating back to Hughie to loop an arm around his shoulders that the barest sign of a limp makes itself known in his gait. Just like Hughie does when he’s hurt, he tucks in and bears it. “You’re welcome, all of you.”

“I cannot deny such generosity,” Frenchie says, managing to sound gracious. “Think of the view, mon coeur!” he adds, when Kimiko signs a cutting motion at him.

“I wanna see, too!” Ryan grabs Butcher’s hand, already heading toward the building. “Let’s go!”

Hugh lets them up to a floor that’s all windows looking out over the city. For all his talk about the view, it’s Kimiko that carts Frenchie over to see. Ryan trots after them, equally charmed, or maybe just loving them that much more after their Gene Kelly marathon on the ride up. Butcher heads that way, too, wanting to leave Hughie alone with his dad and Annie, as much as he can. Ryan eagerly points out the lit up buildings, and Frenchie patiently lists off each one’s purpose as it’s chosen.

Paranoid in a way he hasn’t been for a while, Butcher lets his eyes wander up and among the clouds. He stiffens, sighting first the cape and then the boots and then the man himself. Before he can react any other way, even to unfreeze himself, John disappears in the pulse of a lightning flash.

“Butcher,” Ryan says, pulling at his hand. “Butcher, did you hear?”

“What’s that, monkey?”

“Mr. Campbell’s making hot chocolate. Do you want some?”

“Sure, I’ll have a cup.”

“C’mon.”

He lets himself be dragged around the apartment so Ryan can look at everything. It’s a nice place, probably gets loads of light in the daytime. Butcher don’t even wanna think about how much it costs when you’re not in with the owners. Some friends, Hugh’s boys from the gym.

Ryan breaks away to check on Hugh’s progress with the hot chocolate, and Kimiko goes with him. That leaves Frenchie and Butcher arriving at a sleek, glossy console table without their betters. There’s a few pictures on the table, one of the few personal flourishes in the whole joint. Most of them are framed shots of Hugh Campbells One and Two, but there’s one among the bunch without a frame. Frenchie lifts that one for inspection, then flips it around.

“Kyle, Kenny, Hugh, and Ajax,” he reads. A look of bafflement crosses his features. “Zut alors. They do appear to be a great bunch of guys after all.”

He turns the photo around for Butcher to see, and Butcher snatches it from him, reading the caption on the back again like it’ll tell him something different, but no. There’s Hugh in the middle with three muscle heads, his arms around two of them and the remaining one with his arms around the lot of them.

“Fucking Ajax?” Butcher says, re-examining the picture for a better look.

“My guess, he is the big one,” Frenchie tells him, in an academic sorta tone. Nearly officious, or — Butcher looks at him sideways — maybe a lot officious. Berk. “Butcher, you know, this part of the city…”

“What about it?”

Walking up behind them, MM says, in an undertone, “It’s a gay neighborhood.” He peers at the picture in Butcher’s hand and raises his eyebrows. “That’s a whole lotta man meat.”

Butcher snorts, and Frenchie, upon hearing it, cracks a smile.

“Your paramour is in very high demand, it seems.”

“Yeah, all right.” Butcher sets the photo back on the console table. “Laugh it up.”

A door snicks shut down the hall, and out walks Hugh, flushed and mussed and all smiles when he catches Butcher watching him.

“Hughie’s down for the count. Annie’s staying with him.”

“We’ll get outta your hair,” MM says, still amped up from being on the road all day.

“I can make up the guest rooms,” Hugh entreats. “Clean sheets, I promise.”

“Surely you do not have space for us all,” Frenchie counters, accepting a small steaming mug of hot chocolate from Kimiko. 

She offers the other one to MM, but he waves her off. Probably he’s way over his calorie intake for the day after a few too many gas station sandwiches and drive-thru cheeseburgers. Kimiko shrugs and sips from the mug instead. Ryan gives one of the mugs he’s holding to Butcher and offers the other to Hugh, but like MM, he passes, and like Kimiko, Ryan keeps the extra mug for himself.

“Annie and Hughie are in the master, so there’s two leftover. I know you’ll say I don’t need to, but I’d like to do something nice for you, after…everything that’s happened. You’ve had a long day.” Hugh shrugs a little helplessly. “And I— well, to tell you the truth, I’d appreciate the company.”

MM sighs, his propriety warring with his empathy. They’re all watching him for instructions on how to proceed, and finally, he cracks.

“Lemme see the room.”

Frenchie and Kimiko make to follow, and Ryan looks up at Butcher expectantly, a silent question in his eyes.

“You go ahead. If you wanna stay, we’ll figure something out.”

“But you will, too?” Ryan asks. “You’ll stay?”

“Sure. Where you go, I go.”

While the rest of them shuffle off, Butcher hangs back to go look for Terror. He finds him in the kitchen, surprise, surprise, eating out of a stainless steel dish on the floor. At a glance, Butcher can tell it’s the special kibble he likes. 

It makes a lot of sense to him then. The kitchen, more than anywhere else in the glistening flat, feels most like a lived-in space. It looks the most like Hugh, from the lone mug and spoon stood up in drying rack to the corner of the room set aside for all Terror’s things — a snap-lock container for his food, a clean bowl of water, a basket of his toys. With his mug balanced in his palm, Butcher digs around for the sloth rope toy he knows he’ll find there.

Terror looks up from the nearly empty dish and trots over, panting and smiling, his stub of a tail wagging. Butcher gives it to him, aware of the warmth bleeding out from behind his sternum. It’s a kind of agony then, knowing what he knows. He downs his drink quickly, as if one way of being warm can mask the other, but no such luck.

Just to keep busy, he washes his mug out in the sink and snaps the nearest dish towel up to dry it off. The garish lobster print catches his eye, and for a second all he can think of is a bleary plan he hatched to Hugh from his sad little hospital bed.

Here’s what we do on the drive back, all right? Seafood restaurant.

Fuck, he thinks, tickled and fond, hopelessly fond.

Someone walks into the kitchen behind him, and out of everyone, he’s not expecting to see Annie. She looks between him, the clean mug in his hand, and Terror. The visible clarity on her face immediately has him suspicious. He sets about putting things away just to avoid meeting her eyes. When that doesn’t magically diminish her focus on him, Annie walks into the kitchen proper, running her knuckles down Terror’s back as he toddles past her to carry his rope toy into the living room. She leans her hip against the counter.

“You didn’t expect any of this to get real, did you?”

“Don’t know what you mean,” he says.

She nods, hearing in it the same thing he does. That it’s too much of a deflection. That he didn’t even wait for her to specify which ‘this’ she could possibly mean. 

He sighs, caught, and too tired to pretend otherwise. 

“No, I didn’t.”

Putting away the last of the dishes leaves his hands well and truly empty. He pivots and leans against the counter, the speckled granite edge digging into the small of his back, hands planted on either side of himself. After everything, he refuses to look defensive now.

“They’re easy people to love,” Annie says.

Remembering the last time they had this conversation but about Hughie, Butcher mumbles, “Yeah, and he’s still too good for me. Both of ‘em.”

Shrugging, she asks him, “Well, what’re you gonna do about it?”

“I’ll put some distance between us, and John — I’ll handle him.”

“John,” Annie repeats, impossible to read either by her tone or by her expression.

“’s all part of the plan, Annie.”

“Hughie told me. I think…” Her mouth forms a moue, halfway to disgust but middling somewhere in the realm of fascination. “It’s good. It might be the only play we have left.”

“My thinking exactly.”

“About the other thing.”

Butcher feels his eyebrows creep high up on his forehead, daring her to say her piece. Annie’s the same kinda bedrock Hughie has it in him to be, though, and she don’t back down.

“They’re easy people to love,” she says again, softer now than before. “That’s not a weakness, Butcher.”

Thinking of Becca in that moment, and of Ryan, too, he murmurs, “No, it ain’t.”

“Homelander’s taken so much from so many people. Don’t let him take this, too.”

“Just so I know I’m hearin’ you right, and cuz I know Hughie’s gonna complain later, you’re saying fuck his dad?”

“I can’t express enough that I am— patently— not saying that.”

“’s what I heard.”

Annie rolls her eyes. “Can you not make this a sex thing? He sat in a car for twelve hours just to see you. He didn’t do that because you ‘wanna fuck Hughie’s dad,’” she says, finishing in a parody of his accent. “And neither did you, Butcher.”

Butcher stares at her for a minute. He can’t not.

“Are you giving me a shovel talk? Is that what this is?”

“They’re easy people to love,” she repeats — he gets the feeling she don’t do it for her health. “And they’re too good for us. And yes, if you hurt him, Hughie will kill you, and I’ll help him hide the body.” With a picture perfect, middle America smile, sweet as cobbler, she adds, “Everyone’ll pitch in.”

When he doesn’t have a readymade answer in his pocket for that one, and when he hears how telling his stunned silence is, it creeps up on him that he’s still just staring at her. Still just wondering how she can be encouraging this.

“You got that he was attacked because of me, right?”

“I got that he was attacked. Saying it was because of you takes the onus off Homelander.” She raises one eyebrow at him, serving him a line he won’t be able to dismiss out of hand so easily. “Of all people, I’m surprised you’d be so eager to do that for him. Besides, Insight…that’s on us as much as it is on you. We should’ve known he’d cause problems just for the hell of it.”

“If that’s even why he did it. Fucking shit.”

“Hughie might actually kill him the next time he sees him.”

“He can get in line.”

Annie snorts. “Yeah, he said the same thing about you.”

Ryan comes bounding out of the hallway, checking the living area first and then the kitchen where he finds them. 

“I’ll take that off you,” Butcher tells him, holding out for his empty mug. “Didja find where you’re gonna kip down for the night?”

“Yeah, Frenchie and Kimiko said I could stay with them. Their room has bunkbeds and a daybed.”

Hearing him call it theirs brings a smile to Butcher’s face. He shoots a glance at Annie, and she’s smiling, too. Undeterred by their silent exchange, Ryan points his attention to Annie next.

“Hughie’s asleep?”

“Yeah, he knocked out pretty hard once he had a pillow under his head.”

“Always needs to be tucked in, that one.”

“Didn’t you tuck him in at the hospital after we left?” Ryan says.

Butcher ruffles Ryan’s hair. “How’d you think I know?”

Hugh walks into the kitchen next, two empty mugs in hand. Butcher holds out for them, too, a foamy sheaf of bubbles clinging to the side of his hand as he does. Washing up gives him the distraction he’s looking for, but it doesn’t help him escape the situation altogether. To drive that point home, Annie chooses then to make herself scarce.

“I’m gonna turn in. Thank you for putting us up for the night, Hugh.”

“Of course. Have a good night, Annie.”

“You, too,” she says, going in for a hug. “Goodnight.”

“Goodnight!” Ryan echoes, hugging Hugh and then budging up under Butcher’s arm to hug him, too. “Night, Butcher!”

He looks down at Ryan, dear heart, clocks the slight uptick at the corner of his mouth. On his mum, that look always meant someone, usually Butcher, was about to be in trouble. He’s halfway to asking what Ryan’s up to when he darts out of the kitchen hot on Annie’s heels.

“Thank you for doing that. You didn’t have to.”

“Didn’t wanna be a poor houseguest.” Feigning nonchalance, he adds, “Your lads Kenny, Kyle, and Ajax might take offense.”

Hugh smiles. “That’s very thoughtful of you. They’ll appreciate it.”

“Got everyone settled then?” Butcher asks, stalling and wondering if Hugh can tell.

Taking up the lobster towel to dry what he washes, Hugh hums in the affirmative. “Almost.”

Butcher shuts the tap off. Hugh shelves the last of the mugs while Butcher dries his hands. The pause climbing up in between them itches at his skin.

“Where’re you gonna be?”

“The couch pulls out, or I can take the floor.”

“I ain’t putting you on the floor, Hugh.”

Nodding patiently, Hugh leans down over the counter, forearms pressed flat to the granite. He’s since rolled his sleeves up, and though Butcher looks, he can’t find any bruises painting him up. Not there at least. A dimple appears in Hugh’s cheek where he rolls his lips together, thinking over something. “You know,” he muses, “Frenchie and Kimiko offered to share the couch. Ryan talked them into the bunkbeds.”

Butcher looks straight at him, startled at the cheek of it. “He what?”

“Yeah, he, uh.” He bites his lip, obviously trying to keep from laughing. It doesn’t work, and a second later, he’s chuckling, the tips of his ears gone red. “He was pretty adamant.”

“Little shit,” Butcher says, beginning to laugh himself.

“He reminds me of Hughie at that age.”

“Oh, don’t tell me that,” he moans. “I got eight more years of that to look forward to.”

“And his whole life after that,” Hugh reminds him, a smile on his face like he knows just how good Butcher’s gonna have it, no matter how terrifying the prospect might seem on its face. 

Butcher knocks on the counter top, still just stalling. The illusion he’s kept up for himself that Hugh don’t know fizzles out while they’re watching each other. It’s in the soft set of Hugh’s eyes, like he’s trying even now to do what he’s always done when faced with Butcher’s flighty, showy bullshit — let him down easy. And just like every other time, it’s not for a lack of interest that he does it, but for Butcher’s sake. To give him space.

“Whew, it’s late,” Hugh says, doing it again. He checks his watch. “I can make up the couch? We’ll flip for the floor.”

Crossing his arms, Butcher dodges the bait. “You don’t look tired.”

“Had a lot of coffee today.” Hugh pushes a hand through his hair, probably having not a single notion of how badly Butcher wants to do that for him. “Usually I wouldn’t. I know it keeps me up, but…I had to see you home safely. Hughie, Ryan, all of you.”

Butcher nods, and feeling himself beginning to break, reaches out to tap Hugh’s arm. “Why don’t you sit, Hugh?”

“Oh, sure. Thanks, I’m a bit sore.”

“I noticed.” In lieu of pointing out the limp, Butcher casts about for an alternative. “Got anything to drink?”

“There’s a Cabernet in the fridge. I don’t suppose you’re a wine guy?”

“Becca liked beer. Pale ales.” He gets the bottle and then goes hunting around for something to open it. “Me, I’m all about a good stout.”

But wine will do in a pinch. He dims the lights so it feels more like late evening than morning time and parks himself at the kitchen island next to Hugh. The Cab’s fragrant out the bottle, pleasant. He can picture a scenario where this woulda been his idea, to get Hugh alone and liquor him up. Shoulda guessed Hugh’d go for wine, though.

“So a beer girl, huh?”

Butcher looks up at him over his glass, struck by the question. So many little things made up the person Becca was to him, foibles and all. Her crap taste in music, her love of IPAs, her goddamn icicle toes budged up against his under the covers, even in summertime.

God, but he loved her. He loved that she was smart and fearless and undeniable, and he loved that she was silly and stubborn and fathomless.

And the answer to Hugh’s question? It’s so much more than just a yes or a no. Cuz Becca, was she a beer girl?

“When she was happy, she was.”

Cuz when she was working it was always dry martinis or fucking champagne, or if she weren’t drinking, it was diet coke with lime. But when she was happy, when she was just hers — nobody else’s, not even his — and her time didn’t belong to anyone else, yeah. Yeah, she was a beer girl, and some of the best times in their relationship, especially looking back, were those nights she let him see her just as she was. No borders, no compromises, no bullshit. Just Becca and her shitty IPA beer.

Butcher hears himself telling Hugh all about it, sharing things he ain’t talked about in years. Things he forgot he missed as much as he has. Hugh lets him talk, and he listens, and he pours more wine.

When he winds down and doesn’t say anything for a while, and when he realizes Hugh ain’t gonna leap up to fill the silence, he adds, “I dunno what she saw in me.”

Hugh smiles, cheek in hand. “I know that feeling well.”

Butcher snorts, thunking his glass against Hugh’s in a halfhearted toast. He drains the last of what’s in his glass and tops them off, draining the last of the bottle.

“Billy, do you ever think about his name? Ryan Butcher.” Hugh looks straight at him, fuzzy about the eyes but paying attention. “She gave him your name. Not her maiden name, not…I don’t know, Smith or Doe. Your name. Butcher.”

“What’re you getting at?”

“Just that maybe it’s okay if you want to be his dad. I know,” he adds, when Butcher coils up to argue. He keeps his voice gentle, soft as snow. “I know you’re not, but he’s a great kid and he’s crazy about you. It’s okay to want to give him the kind of life she wanted him to have.”

“I can’t.” 

“But you want to try,” Hugh persists. “Don’t you?”

“Yeah, I do, but so what? What’s that amount to? Against everything else, what does it matter that I wanna give that kid everything? Everything Becca wanted for him. Everything— everything his dad never had. Everything I never had. That don’t mean I know how to do it.”

“Billy, do you think I knew how to be a dad?” He laughs. “Do you think I knew the first thing?”

“Sayin’ you didn’t?”

“Of course not, and well, I know you know Hughie thought so, too, for a while. The main thing is you just keep trying. Just keep being there. Keep letting him know he isn’t alone. That you love him, no matter what.”

“And that’s enough?”

“You hope it is.” He shrugs, at the end of his wisdom, but that’s not a bad place to end, with hope. “He’s his own person, Billy. Most of the time, he’ll tell you what he needs.”

“Well, if he’s anything like Hughie…”

“Yeah,” Hugh laughs. “Oh, yeah.”

“Y’know,” Butcher purrs, making an effort to leer. “If Ryan got his way, they’d be brothers by now.”

Hugh slaps a hand over his mouth to muffle his snickering. It’s contagious, and Butcher has to laugh, too. He tries to be quiet, too. No doubt the walls in this place are thick enough to mask a lotta sins, but he don’t wanna deal with an MM robbed of beauty sleep first thing in the morning. He puts his mouth on his glass to curb his grinning and has another big swallow of the Cab. 

“This ain’t bad, Hugh.”

“Maybe you’re a wine guy after all,” he murmurs, still with his cheek in his hand. Might be the drink’s starting to get to him. His face has got a rosy cast from his cheeks to his hairline, and his eyes are soft, slow to blink and tending to linger. “Certainly agrees with you.”

Feeling safe inside this new, buoyant comfort, Butcher lets himself lean in close to dish out a smooth one-liner, cool and confident, but then there’s Hugh leaning in, too, swooning and hazy to catch himself with a soft hand at Butcher’s collar bone. He stalls there, close enough his nose bumps Butcher’s.

“Billy,” he whispers, the bright sweetness of his breath right there on Butcher’s lips.

He feels the same way he had on the anti-V, loose about the joints like he ain’t yet figured out gravity or the lay of his body in its thrall, and he’s warm, he’s really warm. Hugh’s warm, too.

Butcher shouldn’t. He’s been trying the whole drive here to want this less than he does. It’s already cost them too much, but then he’s touching Hugh’s face and drawing him in for that first dizzying press of lips, soft and sweet and stirring heat low in his belly. Hot skin and the barest scrape of teeth gives way to a hot flash of tongue, Hugh’s hum buzzing in Butcher’s mouth and lighting him up from the inside out. He chases Butcher’s mouth when he turns away, tentative, shy, but he wouldn’t be, if he knew.

“Hugh, I gotta tell you somethin’.”

“Tell me what?”

He slips a finger down the high collar on Hugh’s turtleneck. The bruising looks worse with the accumulation of days. The colors are deeper than they were, a sallow yellow at the edges and lurid purple closer to the middle. Not only that, they drop down lower than can be seen without stretching the neck. It’s obscene, how dark and plentiful and shiny they’ve gone. 

“Why he did it,” he says hoarsely.

Old tactic, this, and it should work. Nothing ever does the trick of scaring someone off quite like the truth. The uglier, the better, and this one’s currently the most despicable in Butcher’s toolbox. Says something dreadful about him, that, but he’s counting on it. If he don’t give Hugh a reason to walk away, Butcher’ll keep on kissing him, and Homelander already nearly killed both of them for less than that.

It’s agony waiting for Hugh to ask, waiting for his own reckoning to bite him at long last, so many years overdue by now, but Hugh don’t ask and he don’t pull away. Instead, he slips a hand into Butcher’s hair, holding him in place as much as he is standing his ground.

Realization a live wire inside him, Butcher mutters, “He fucking told you.”

“He thought it was something I already knew.” Hugh leans up to kiss Butcher’s temple, aided in the way Butcher’s taken to hanging his head. “In hindsight, I guess my reaction surprised both of us.” 

Catching Hugh’s hand in his, Butcher asks him, “What’s that mean?”

“I just…”

Butcher smooths Hugh’s collar back in place, careful not to press too hard. He’s been reckless for so much of his life, he doesn’t want this to be one more thing he fumbles.

“You what?” Butcher asks, pressing his thumb beneath Hugh’s lip, just like he had the first time he ever came onto Hugh. His throat clicks in a swallow, and he asks again. “You what, Hugh?”

“I wanted to know what it was like,” he whispers. 

“Want me to show you?”

“I—” Hugh huffs a strained, fretful laugh. “Just now, Billy, I really can’t.”

Comes of nearly having your dick twisted off, Butcher thinks grimly. Trust John to fucking ruin this for him, too. Butcher keeps that train of thought off his face, or tries to, and palms Hugh’s cheek.

“Are you hurting now?”

Turning into the hand at his cheek, Hugh tells him, softly, “No.”

“But then?”

“Oh, sure,” Hugh says, smiling, even as his eyes fill with tears. He cuts the track of one escaped tear with a fingertip. “He’s a scary guy.”

Butcher ain’t made of fucking stone. He never has been, no matter how he pretends, and he thinks Hugh knows that, as easily as he sinks into Butcher’s arms when he makes room for him there. It’s just familiar, holding onto him. Making a fortress out of his body how Hugh’s done for him, even knowing by now that they’re both far from invulnerable.

“Hughie thought you musta said summat to him, sent him running.”

“I told him the same thing I told Ryan after he saw you laid up in the hospital,” Hugh says, pressing his hand to his eye. “That it’s up to him to become a man his son can be proud of.”

“Did you say that to him before or after he put his hands on you?”

“It was in between sessions,” Hugh quips, and Butcher can’t help that he starts to smile.

Though his eyes are stinging, too. It must be audible in his voice.

“Hugh…”

“Please don’t say you’re sorry. Hey!” He sits up, setting his hand against Butcher’s cheek, cool where Butcher’s burning up. “I didn’t tell you the best part! After all that nastiness at Sage Grove, when Hughie got hurt, I upped our insurance, so after the apartment’s done being fixed up, I can finally get a new stove!”

Shouldn’t be that hearing it makes Butcher laugh, but it does. The practicality of it, the plain sweetness of it. It’s armor against devastation the likes of which people like him and John bring to the table, and Hugh— he’s still just cupping Butcher’s cheek in his hand like he ain’t a part of what makes the world so goddamn ugly to live in.

“I’m okay, Billy.”

Butcher nods, no choice but to take his word for it. “You need me to finish that for you?”

“Oh.” Hugh slides the glass over to him. “Please.”

Shooting both glasses in short order, Butcher gets to his feet easily. There’s no brain fog or vertigo, thanks to the V. He feels like he’s had a nip rather than half a bottle of wine. Hugh covers half his face with his hand and laughs.

“I’m a little jealous right now.”

“It ain’t all it’s cracked up to be,” Butcher tells him, leaving a kiss on Hugh’s forehead. He fills a glass of water from the tap and brings it back for Hugh to drink. “The linen closet?”

Hugh tells him where it is, and Butcher’s off. Terror gets up to trail him, trying to figure out where his bed for the night’s gonna be. Butcher gets the couch bed out and fitted with sheets, just flinging down pillows when Hugh turns off the light in the kitchen. Butcher watches him immediately stub his toe on the kitchen island.

“Jesus Christ,” Butcher says, going to get him, and trying not to burst a vein holding back his laughter.

Terror trundles over next to Butcher’s side of the couch bed and flops down with his toy again.

Butcher’s got no great ulterior motive in mind when he helps Hugh out of his shirt. It’s just that it ain’t fit for sleeping in, and he wants him comfortable. That he can finally make out the rest of Hugh’s bruises— though not all of them— is only an added bit of torture. Hugh must feel him looking in the half-dark while his eyes take their time adjusting because he feels around for Butcher’s hand and pulls him over to lay down beside him.

They could’ve stretched out back at Hugh’s, before all this went off the rails. They could’ve taken their time. It could’ve been good. Butcher runs a hand up and down Hugh’s arm, feeling gooseflesh.

“Billy, do you think you’d like to go out on a date with me?”

“What for?” He soothes his hand over Hugh’s belly, chaste but for the intention behind it. “I don’t need all that.”

“Maybe I do. I’d like to be good to you.”

“You are good to me,” Butcher says, kissing him.

“Mmm.” Hugh leans in close to him, winding his arm around Butcher’s back to kiss him deeper. It’s a sweet feeling, like being laid down and gently taken apart. “You can say no if it’s not what you want. I won’t pressure you.”

“I ain’t sayin’ no.”

Hugh’s answering laugh tickles at Butcher’s cheek. He pushes up, tipping Butcher over onto his back so he can lean over him. His smile’s bright against the dark.

“I worried, you know.”

“What’d you worry about?” Butcher murmurs, winding a hand around the back of Hugh’s neck.

“That you’d try to push me away because of what happened.”

“I did try to push you away.”

“No, you didn’t.” Hugh’s eyes in the quiet dark are vulnerable, giving away as much as they get. Another Campbell trick he passed onto Hughie. “I think you tried to try, but…we see how that went.”

“Not my fault you’re irresistible, is it?”

Snuffling a laugh, he presses his forehead to Butcher’s chest, warm and lovely and reminding Butcher what life used to be like, what it could be again, maybe. Butcher wraps his arms around him, holding tight. He’s lived the loss of this man, and knowing the pain of it, he doesn’t wanna let go of him again anytime soon. He just wants to earn him, now that he’s got him. Maybe he’s gone about it backwards, but if Hugh can talk plainly about courting Butcher and winning him that way, Butcher can think about one day deserving him.

“Tell me we’ve got time.”

“There’s time,” Hugh whispers, Hugh pressing his hand flat over Butcher’s heart. “You know how I know?”

“How?”

“Because you made up your mind,” Hugh tells him, back to dealing in absolutes. He’s calm, from the steady pumping of his heart to the easy frankness in his eyes. “I know what happens when you make up your mind.”

“I get real hard to be around?” Butcher quips, feeling around in the dark.

“You get your way. Maybe you catch a beating, maybe we all do, but it’ll work out. I know it will. One way or another, you’ll come out on top.”

“Actually,” Butcher says, giving Hugh his shirt to wear once he finds it. “Was hoping you’d do that.”

Distracted and muffled by the shirt he’s pulling on over his head, Hugh says, “What?”

“Top.”

Even in the dark, Hugh’s face is red, red. “Oh,” he breathes. “Well.”

“You know what that is, darlin’?”

Settling down next to him, demure-like, a half-step below innocent, Hugh says, “I may have done some research.”

Butcher kisses him. He can’t not, just like he can’t keep from laughing. Hugh shushes him, but he’s laughing, too. That’s fine, that’s all right. Fucking research, Jesus. That’s brilliant. He feels young then, curled up next to someone what makes his heart beat faster, giggling in the moonlight. Making plans for the future.

“When we go on that date, you’re gonna tell me all about it.”

Hugh tucks his chin in against Butcher’s shoulder. Shy again — as if he ain’t been pulling perfect marks all night, and like he don’t know he could have him right now if he weren’t hurting — he whispers, “I could even show you.”

Their eyes meet in the dark, shining with promise, with things wished for and unsaid. The moment lightens, and they’re all chuckles again. Maybe it ain’t just him that feels young tonight. That’s all right, that’s fine. It feels good to dream. In one sense of the word, anyway. When he does drift off, it’s to heavy, comfortable, dreamless sleep, only broken up by the coming morning, and only then because he smells coffee. 

He blinks his eyes open draped all around Hugh like an octopus, like he just couldn’t help himself. Sounds about right. He extricates himself from Hugh as carefully as he can so as not to wake him and tiptoes into the kitchen. 

MM sights him, shakes his head, and throws a neatly folded shirt from the counter directly at his face. “You know you’re the most predictable motherfucker on the face of the earth?”

In lieu of jumping up to defend himself or Hugh’s honor, in spite of the fact that nothing happened, Butcher pulls the shirt on over his head. Feeling the fit, he tugs at the bottom hem and raises an eyebrow at MM.

“I ain’t your boyfriend,” he says, as succinct an answer as any for why he dug up one of Hugh’s shirts to pelt him with rather than one of his own.

Shrugging grandly, Butcher pours himself a cup of coffee. Strong, steaming, and delicious. If he didn’t think he’d wake Hugh with it, he’d be whistling. MM must pick up on the subtext cuz he rolls his eyes.

“Don’t you think your dick’s gotten you in enough trouble by now?”

“Says the trouble!”

“It was one—” MM exhales forcefully, hand out like he’s physically stopping Butcher from dredging up old shit. “It was one fucking time, Butcher.”

“Your loss.”

“Hughie’s gonna kill you. You know that, right?”

“Think I got bigger problems just now. ‘Sides,” he adds, just as he’s thinking better of it.

But MM knows him better than most, and he knows hesitation’s smoke signaling vulnerability like a great fucking fire. And MM, because he’s still a good sort even when he is at the end of his patience, just sighs and leans back against the counter. With strange, close warmth approaching affection, he says, “You simple bitch.”

“He coulda died.”

“You realize—” MM’s cheek twitches to match the tension in his jaw. “And this is not me putting that on you, Butcher, it’s not, but you realize…?”

“He coulda died,” Butcher says again, slower, for emphasis. To show that he knows what’s at stake. Maybe he didn’t before, but he does now, and that’s not a mistake he’s ever made twice. “Over somethin’ never even happened. He paid the price for it. He shouldn’t’a had to, MM, but he did, and maybe that don’t make it right. Maybe I shoulda cut and run last night, keep him outta the blast radius, but the bitch of it is? Me, I thought I was doin’ that.”

MM tips his head, listening closely enough that the spaces in between words become their own kinda code to decipher. He’s had a lot of practice ferreting out Butcher’s bullshit over the years. He knows when he’s not being told the whole truth.

“You thought you were doin’ that, when?”

“Couple thousand feet in the air over backwater Kentucky,” Butcher tells him, taking a long drink of his coffee. “Reached into my pocket for what I needed to bring him down, to finally end it, and it weren’t there.”

“Butcher…”

“And then I nearly died, too,” he continues. “For what, MM?”

MM crosses his arms over his chest, head down, and doesn’t say anything right away. It’s gonna be a beautiful day, Butcher thinks, looking past him out the windows. None of the rain from last night, just sun streaking through the holdover storm clouds and lighting ‘em up from the inside out. Even the gray looks vibrant set against the steadily brightening sky.

“For the record, this thing you do? Where you self-destruct and call it a strategy? I don’t condone it. You’re not expendable, and this—” MM waves his hand in a circle to indicate Butcher’s relative state of undress. “—tells me you know you’re not, so why don’t you get over yourself and quit tryin’ to go out in a fiery blaze of glory? It’s getting old, man.”

His eyes stay locked onto Butcher’s, austere how he often can be, and tempered by compassion, like he always is. Big softy.

Butcher holds his arm out, and MM meets him in the middle.

“You were right. It’s me, and I’m gonna finish it.”

“You gonna need backup?”

“Nah. Made my bed, and so did he. Tonight I’m gonna lay him down in it.”

Butcher draws back, gesturing for MM’s phone. He taps out a message for MM to read and gives him the phone back. MM deletes the message, tucking the phone back in his pocket.

“All right?” Butcher asks him.

Nodding, MM tells him, “Yeah, I got you.”

The morning’s easy then. He gets a lotta looks over Hugh wearing his shirt, especially from Hughie, but no interference otherwise. Leastways not till he dips to take Terror out for a walk and a wee.

It’s gorgeous out, clear skies and warm sun bouncing off storefronts and apartment buildings. No cars, but smatterings now and then of people, some of them holding hands and all of them going about their own business, not seeing him unless they see Terror first. He’s all right with that, and Terror always did like the attention. They’re looping around the block vaguely heading back in the direction of Hugh’s temporary flat when someone steps out from beneath an awning to walk alongside them.

“Kimiko,” he says, tipping his head.

She presses all five of her fingertips to her chest and scoops away from herself. It don’t look like any greeting he’s ever seen, except maybe a few insults he’s seen in bars, so he asks.

“What’s that then?”

Her answer is to point at him. Maybe it’s what she calls him to Frenchie.

He tries the sign out for himself, saying, “Asshole?”

It’s such a near thing. She almost smiles at him. Instead, she points at him again and repeats the sign, then a different sign. A chopping motion with her hand.

“All right, ‘Butcher’. I got it.” When they clear a few streets, and she hasn’t made any further attempt to talk to him, he says, “Did you really track Hughie down usin’ his snapchat location?”

Kimiko nods, a despairing look on her face like, He’s an idiot, but he’s our idiot.

“Love that kid.”

She nods again, and this time the expression on her face is softer. It’s not escaped his notice, how Frenchie’s exact brand of tenderness dwells in her so easily. He hasn’t earned seeing this side of her, but Hughie’s a mitigating circumstance, just like Becca was — a reason to call Butcher a man and not a piece of shit. Cuz if someone so good could give a shit about him, that had to count for something, and sure, it does. But not because of anything it says about him.

Annie had the right of it before. Any heart she has, or ever did have, for Homelander and all that’s been done to him, all that’s made him what he is, it wasn’t any charm of John’s that made it so. It was just her, caring, even against her own best interest.

Just like Hughie, and— Butcher looks over.

“I was wrong about you.”

Kimiko’s always had expressive eyes, and it’s easy to see from the twinkle in her eyes that she’s savoring this moment.

“Yeah, yeah,” he mutters. “No need to gloat.”

She shrugs, sinks her hand in her pocket, and offers him a small round case. She raises her eyebrows, and he takes it, knowing without opening it what’s inside. He tucks it safely away in his pocket. Kimiko cuts her hand across her opposite forearm in a downward swipe and curtly shakes her head. He gets it. Don’t lose this one.

“That coulda happened to anyone,” he says, all innocence.

Distinctly unimpressed with him, shocker, she pulls out her phone. At first he thinks she means to type, but all she does is open snapchat so he can see the canary yellow loading screen.

He snorts. That’s a point pretty well made.

They come to a stop outside Hugh’s place. Kimiko’s studying him, like she knows even before he’s made it clear that he never had any intention of heading back inside once he left. Makes sense. She knows what he is and what he has to do.

He kneels to give Terror a rub about the ears, pressing his forehead to Terror’s.

“Be a good boy for Hugh, all right, darlin’?”

Terror licks his nose, and Butcher straightens out, all business to match the stern, sad eyes watching him. Kimiko holds her palm out and makes like to grab something off it, pulling it toward her. She points away, as if to say, Go on and go, but she repeats that first sign, and again. It’s in her eyes even more than it is in her hands, how much she means it.

“Come back?”

She nods, signing again emphatically. Go, but come back.

“I’ll see what I can do.” He starts to walk away, but he turns, calling over his shoulder, “Tell everyone…”

Kimiko waits, but the words don’t come. She presses her hand to his arm. It’s enough.

Butcher takes to the sky. For a while he just takes it in, the sights right there along with the bugs, and then he makes for the townhouse. He’s never been in person, but he knows from Ryan where it is and how to get there. He ain’t quick like John, but he still gets there faster than any human could. True to what he’s expecting, the door opens before he can even climb the steps. Clean shaven and orderly from his gauntlets to his boots stands fucking Homelander, helming the darkened doorway like a figurehead at the prow of a sinking ship. His grin is an airplane wreckage. Twisted fuselage made camera ready for the six o’clock news.

“Well, look what the cat dragged in.”

“You gonna let me up, John, or what?”

He opens the door, leaving just enough room for Butcher to squeeze past him, and it’s as he’s forced to brush against him that he catches the faintest whiff of something he recognizes but can’t quite place. It’s there and gone before he can name it.

“You might’ve called.”

“Why, so you could clean up?”

Wouldn’t’ve been such a bad idea. The place is a mess. All the curtains have been drawn tight to keep the light at bay, and the close, stuffy tang of stale air hits him at the same time that the faint, cloying smell of milk touches his nose. Several emptied bottles litter the coffee table in the living room, and more over in the kitchen.

“I didn’t think you’d come.” John— no, Homelander, that’s who he is in that stupid fucking cape with the suit holding all his fictitious muscles in place— puts his hands behind his back. Officious, matter-of-fact. “You didn’t answer my texts.”

“I didn’t read them.”

“I noticed.” He smiles, calm and fixed like a painted doll. “Yet here you are.”

Butcher stares at him, unease prickling at the back of his neck. The stillness jars him. Doesn’t match up with the fifty-some messages stacked up in his phone. That smell from before hits his nose again, and like a shot, even as it fizzles out of his head a second later, he remembers it. He rushes forward with his arm out, pinning Homelander to the wall by the throat.

“Gimme one good reason I don’t fucking end you right now.”

“Aww, Billy.”

“Forester.”

“Well, that didn’t take long, did it,” drawls John, his words sliding together with drink. He descends the stairs heavily, a silk robe sluggishly tied across his waist, the cut of his hip visible through the part in the robe. He’s got a rock glass in his palm, some amber spirit sloshing inside. “Was his impression of your homely sweetheart any better?”

“Not by much,” Butcher mutters, still just holding his arm to Insight’s throat. “How’d he do at bein’ me?”

John pauses at the bottom of the steps, eyes as bright as they are hazy. “I got what I needed.”

“And that’s all that ever fucking matters to you, isn’t it?”

“I tried to be what you needed,” John says, and then him and Butcher are talking over each other, voices overlapping.

“Oh, you tried, yeah, you did—”

“Do you think I wanted to stop in Kentucky—”

“When’s the last time you did anything wasn’t just what you wanted—”

“—or to see you brought down like that—”

“Because you put me there!”

“You wanted me to!”

And because he’s got as great a sense for timing as anyone, Insight says, “Technically true, Billy.”

“Open up your mouth again,” Butcher says, feeling his eyes shimmering in his skull, growing hotter by the second. “And see if I don’t finish what he fucking started.”

A hand smooths over his belly, heralding by mere moments the weight of a chin tucking in at his shoulder. “So do it then,” John tells him, lips brushing the pulse in Butcher’s neck at the same time that his hand wanders lower, seeking Butcher’s dick through the barrier of his jeans. “Save me the trouble.”

“This is certainly somebody’s fantasy,” Insight mumbles.

Butcher can hear John’s eyes flare up just over his shoulder. He waits for the devastation to follow, but there’s nothing.

“Maybe run his pocket before you jerk him off over my corpse.”

There’s movement at his side, almost too quick to feel. He looks up, and Kimiko’s round little case is in the center of John’s naked palm. Unopened, unobtrusive. He catches Butcher’s eyes, the blues of his like dead fish gaping at an unfeeling sun.

“What’s this?”

“Open it and see.”

John looks from him to Insight, cool, calculating, becoming sharper by the minute. He nods at whatever drivel ekes out of Insight’s head into his and disappears for the span of a breath.

Under the lamp shape in the bedroom, whispers a far back corner of his mind. Not his voice, but Insight’s. You dropped it at mine.

Reappearing as instantaneously as he’d gone, John says, “Out.” 

The sudden nearness of him, like setting foot onto green grass and hearing a click where there shouldn’t be, startles Butcher into freezing. At least he doesn’t flinch, but it’s enough of a reaction all the same that he can see it on John’s face that he’s noticed. As if spurned into action by the sight, John reaches past Butcher to yank Insight out from under his hold and spin him out in the direction of the door.

“Go. Before I change my mind.”

I’ll fucking get you, Butcher thinks acidly.

Without diverting away from his escape, Insight projects back at him, So long as you get him first.

And then Butcher’s alone with John, the anti-V gone from his pocket but another dose close at hand. Upstairs, in fact. In the bedroom. He just has to get to it.

However will he manage.

 

/

 

Homelander sweeps away from the foyer, trailing one hand along the small of Butcher’s back as he goes. He takes a sip from the drink in his glass, not sure what it is, only knowing that between him and Insight, they finished the bottle. 

“How did you like fucking him?” he asks, his voice all gone to gravel.

Blandly, Butcher says, “Insight?”

“Hugh Campbell,” he mutters. “What did his dick feel like?”

“Held it in your hand, didn’t ya? Ain’t like you don’t know what he’s working with.” Butcher steps around him, taking the glass out of Homelander’s hand to down the last of it. He smacks his lips, sets the emptied glass on the coffee table amongst drained bottles of curdled milk, and uncoils to full height, chin out as if to take a punch. “Explain that logic to me, John.”

“I wanted to see what all the fuss was about,” Homelander croons. “So? How was he?”

“He’s walking with a limp because you nearly twisted his dick off, you fucking lemon. That’s how he was.”

Simpering, Homelander tells him, “I’m so sorry I interfered.”

“Right, sure you are. And Insight? Was that meant to get under my skin? Said you got what you needed. What’d you want me here for?”

“Insight,” Homelander mutters, rolling his eyes. “He was a paltry substitute. Catty where you would’ve been coy, and coy where you would’ve been cruel. I didn’t want to fuck that miserable parasite. I wanted you to come home.”

“This ain’t my home.”

“You don’t have a home. Oh, neither do I. Look around, Billy.” He gestures at the pigsty of the apartment, sloppy these last several hours but largely un-lived in. “We’re living the same life.”

Billy glances pointedly from the milk bottles to Homelander, an arch look on his face.

Unmoved, Homelander says, “I would’ve waited for you anywhere.”

Butcher steps into his space, a certain set to his face that sharpens Homelander’s blurry edges into keen awareness. “You’d’a waited for me to do what, John?” He grasps Homelander’s chin in his hand, and on a breath, he asks, “Apologize?”

It’s his first instinct, and once upon a time, that would have been enough, but all along he’s been learning truths about himself from Billy. Learning the aspects of these hands and this body and that mouth he craves, whether he can articulate the shape of his desires or not, has given him a concept, heretofore unplumbed, of what he’s been missing since their spat. He wants to feel prized and needed, yes, and he’d conflated those things for so long with supplication. With being worshipped. He could have the world entire at his feet, and he would still be nothing more than a paper man, fragile and incendiary. 

But at Billy’s feet, perhaps, it would be all right to burn.

“Something else then.” Billy’s thumb slips over Homelander’s lower lip, sending his heart skipping. “Want me to punish you?” Billy asks, passing just the tip of his thumb over Homelander’s teeth, grazing the tip of his tongue. 

Homelander groans, eyes slamming shut at the weight dropping onto his tongue, filling up his mouth. Garbled, gagged, and thrilling with it, he says, “Billy.”

A strand of spit follows Billy’s thumb as it’s drawn out of his mouth.

“Y’know, the whole way here, I was turning it over in my mind, what’s to be done about your misbehaving. I think I worked it out.” Billy brushes his knuckles across Homelander’s bare stomach, catching on the open flap of the robe as he walks a loose, indolent circle around him. “’s a matter of payment, really, to say nothin’ of the interest. See, you almost killed my boys, John, and that’s gonna cost you.”

“Cost me what?”

Butcher leans in close behind him, close enough his breath’s warm at Homelander’s ear. “All the stars in the sky,” he says, methodical, patient, and scintillating like a great fire, “and the mountains from up outta the ground. The space stations and the consulates and the fucking cathedrals, too. It’s gonna cost you every last thing you got,” he murmurs, slipping a recently broken finger in the line of Homelander’s gaping lapel. “But it’s all right, John. You know why?”

Breathless, pupils filling out to match his dick beneath the silk robe, Homelander gasps, “Why?”

“Cuz you wanna give me everything,” Butcher whispers, grazing Homelander’s freshly shaven cheek with his beard. “Don’t you, sweetheart?”

With mounting desperation, Homelander meets him. “I do, Billy.”

“Yeah, and you will. You will.”

“Let me be what you need,” he babbles, an endless stream of such platitudes spilling out of him as he backs Billy over onto the couch. He sinks between his knees, wanting to taste him, to choke himself on Billy’s dick, but Billy stops him with his fingers in his mouth.

“Bring yourself off. Right here.”

Homelander fumbles his hand down the front of the robe, fighting with sash and silken folds to get his dick in his hand. Billy has three of his fingers halfway to Homelander’s throat when he finally palms himself, skin on skin. A hot tongue replaces the fingers in his mouth, slick fingers gripping him by the back of the neck. The orgasm that follows unstacks the tension behind his eyes and in his shoulders. 

He’s still floating, carried away on relief and pleasure so intertwined they may as well be the same sensation, when Billy smears his fingers across Homelander’s stomach. His intention’s impossible to follow until, without any preamble, he bends down to tease his fingers where Homelander’s been stretched open a few times today already. This being the case, Homelander takes him easily. He drops his forehead to Billy’s knee, letting himself be plundered, and when that sweet ache begins to rise in him again, frantic and hazy, he reaches for Billy’s fly. He isn’t sure what state he’ll find him in, but he’s half-mast, all decadence, and Homelander swallows him down eagerly. Moaning wantonly at the graze of fingers at his prostate, Homelander feels Billy firming up on his tongue. 

“You didn’t fuck him,” he slurs, moving his hand to compensate for his empty mouth.
