“Told you he’s fucking limping after what you did to him.”

A pleased flip seizes his stomach. It’s an easy thing to speed off in pursuit of a bottle of lube and speed back, slicked and stretched and half-delirious with it.

This time, as before, Billy’s ragged stillness gives him away. Homelander touches his face, nearly healed up but for the sallow bruises falling down the planes of his face. The sallowest of the lot cascades over his brow bone in a lopsided reflection of the deeper bruise dropped beneath his cheekbone. 

“You don’t have to be afraid of me, Billy.”

“Not till the next time I do.”

“You weren’t afraid then,” Homelander whispers, sliding down onto Billy’s dick. “You’re not really going to start now, are you?”

Sharp teeth at Homelander’s collar bone herald the low groan smeared into his skin. The robe slips off Homelander’s shoulder, catching in the bend of his elbow. He loses track of the argument then, swept away in how right it feels to be here again. Whether it’s Arcadia or the shores of Agriomandra or his suite at Vought, or yes, even if it’s here in his facsimile of a home, pressed close to Billy is just where things begin to make sense again. Where he feels the depth of his loneliness reaching toward an escape hatch, toward someone who might finally bring him out of it. Billy grips him tightly, like an escape is what he wants, too.

“Oh, I love you. Tell me you love me.”

“If I say I fucking hate you—”

“That’s close enough.” Homelander puts his hand through the top of the couch seeking traction. He fucks Billy harder, winning more of those gorgeous, desperate sounds out of him. “Hate me, wish I was dead. It doesn’t change what I said. You’re mine. You’re only mine.”

Billy’s body answers him and fills him right up.

“You’re insane,” he mumbles, suffering Homelander’s tenderest kisses at his temple, where the bruising fades to yellow.

“Like calls to like.”

“Yeah, I remember what else you said.”

I am a monster. I’m yours.

“Like,” Homelander murmurs, licking Billy’s mouth open. “—calls to like.”

The fingers in Homelander’s hair fist and pull, reminding him of his second orgasm, close at hand but not quite within reach just yet.

“Still want me to punish you?”

Smiling, overloaded with that elusive calm he’s been chasing since Kentucky, he says, “Yes, Daddy.”

“Take this off.”

Homelander shrugs, and there it goes, drooping in a heap at his hips. 

“Good. Now go and wait for me on the bed.”

He goes, and between his haste and Billy’s lack thereof, he waits for what feels like forever. The bedding’s in some disarray, but it’s the only sign of wear in the room. Homelander had been mostly downstairs before Insight came to him, so aside from the bedsheets and some neglected laundry on the floor, most of the mess hadn’t made it up here. There is a shirt tucked up underneath his pillow — Billy’s shirt, the one with the prowling tiger down the front. It had smelled like him when Homelander first brought it out, and it still does, faintly, beneath the stronger notes of himself overpowering it.

Billy catches him running a finger down the glimmering threads as he darkens the door. “Over on your belly,” he says, working the buttons on his shirt.

Homelander turns, pillowing his cheek on his arms so he can watch Billy disrobe.

He’s a beautiful man, Billy Butcher. Homelander doesn’t know how he ever failed to notice him, even stood next to his wife, lovely and neat and, in hindsight, a well-matched set. Maybe he could’ve bedded them both once upon a time, but then, no, he couldn’t have. To even consider this, as he was then, would have destroyed him. It was only after the loss of every other fiction in his life that he could look at Billy and want.

Climbing up onto the bed behind him, scooping his hips up to drag Homelander upright onto his knees, Butcher warms his hand on Homelander’s ass.

“Count with me, John,” he says, and then—

“One— two— three—”

“Good boy.”

It goes on like that, a sharp sting across his ass giving way to cool, brilliant relief. Billy gives no quarter. He hardly waits for Homelander to count the few times that his voice fails him. It’s only when he clears thirty, his dick hanging full and heavy between his legs, that Billy relents.

“What’s that, John? You want it harder?”

He makes a noise halfway between a moan and a whimper, fraught with need, and whining with it when Billy makes good on his tease.

“Count.”

“Thirty-one, ahh. Thirty-two. Thirty-three…”

“I’m gonna give you fifty like this.” Smack. “And if I like the sounds you make—” Smack. Smack. Smack. “—I’ll give you another fifty after that.”

“Thirty-eight. Oh! Thirty-nine!”

“And then, John—” Smack. “If you’re really good—” Smack. “I’ll put my tongue in you.”

Homelander loses track of the numbers tumbling out of his mouth. Automatic memory stops him from miscounting, but he can’t think how much longer this onslaught will continue before it ends. All rational thought leaves him, reason flies out of his head, along with all sensation outside of where Billy disciplines him with his hand.

“Eighty-four,” he gasps into the back of his wrist. “Eighty-five.” It’s fire at the base of his spine and weightlessness behind his eyes. His balls are tight against his body, in agony awaiting relief. “Eight-six.”

Billy passes ten more spankings before bothering to partake of any movement that isn’t just bringing his hand down on Homelander’s ass. He shifts behind him, drawing near enough that his dick, heavy and thick to match Homelander’s, brushes his skin, leaking where the head touches him. It’s enough to make Homelander’s mouth water.

“Oh, fuck me. Oh, please.”

“Count,” Billy rumbles, still with his dick drooling against Homelander’s ass.

“Ninety… ninety…”

“Ninety-seven.”

“Ninety-seven,” Homelander echoes, squirming. Smack. “Ninety-eight.”

He’s not ready for the two subsequent slaps that follow in rapid succession, faster and harder than the dozen or so that led up to them. Billy’s word is good. He bends down low behind Homelander and eases his tongue into him. Anticipating his orgasm, Billy reaches around Homelander’s front to grip the base of his dick tightly enough to almost be painful. The scratch of his beard against Homelander’s ass, tender and raw from Billy’s ministrations, does hurt, but it’s a good ache. A delicious one. Delirious.

“Were you bad, darlin’?” Billy asks, with crushed glass in place of an ordinary voice.

“I was, I was.”

“And are you sorry?”

“Yes—” He hisses, jerking away from Billy’s teeth at his ravaged backside. “No.”

“Would you do it again?”

Nodding, ruffling his hair against the rumpled pillow, Homelander groans, “And again and again.”

Hands seize him about the waist and flip him over. Billy’s inside him before he can catch his breath, and then there’s no reason to breathe when they’re moving together like this, when his body and Billy’s body merge to become one bottomless, rapacious, indefatigable leviathan. This is what he wants, he thinks, hot tears streaming to mirror his pounding heart.

“No one else can give you this,” he whispers, shaking all over, hands slipping down Billy’s arms and his back, gone slippery with sweat. “No one else wants you like this.”

“Yeah, you’re right,” Billy grunts, squeezing Homelander’s ass in his hands to raise him up, to alter the simple geometry of their flesh and blood. “You’re right.”

Homelander staves off the crest of his long-awaited second orgasm with his hand crafted into a vise around himself, how Billy’d held him, but harder. The creeping spread of pain brings him back from the edge far enough that he can pass some of his frantic urgency onto Billy so they’re both crashing into each other full force, so they’re both clawing at skin, sweat, and hair, so they’re both saying things they’re too wound up to notice. So Homelander can’t rightly say which one of them says what, only that they’re saying it.

You ruined me— You’re all I’ve got now— I need you— I’m close— I’m close — I’m—

His orgasm, sharp and intense, incinerates him.

It stretches on endlessly, squeezing out every last drop of vitality he has. It gnaws at the edges of his awareness, one stretching moment at a time, how exhausted he is, and how heavy Billy is where he’s laying on top of him.

He blinks his eyes open.

Glistening with sweat and panting hard from his efforts, Billy mutters, “Monsters, right?”

A blow, sharp and swift, catches Homelander perfectly in the temple. Black, leaden and complete, descends.

“Yeah,” he hears through the liquid abyss. “That’s us.”

Homelander wakes in sips and bursts after that. He sees a square of blue sky and then the slats of a cracked window. His semi-consciousness comes in fits, and it takes him a while to realize he’s in the back of a moving vehicle.

Ryan’s there, or he thinks he hears his voice anyway. His and Billy’s.

There’s only Billy with him when Homelander finally comes to, and even then, it’s only the vehicle jolting to a brisk stop that brings him back to full wakefulness. Billy’s wearing his tiger shirt, and the plain button-down he’d been wearing, Homelander’s wearing it now. One of the buttons near the the bottom got skipped over, and it draws Homelander’s attention. He tries to reach forward to touch the bunched up fabric or maybe try his luck with the buttons, but his hands are bound behind him.

“What did you do?”

Billy looks at him, and without answering, thumps his hand on the rear doors.

They creak open to a beautiful, hyper-bright sunset. With his eyes unused to the light, he has a queasy flash of over-stimulation, of his senses being cranked up to eleven. He’s frogmarched out into the makings of a military compound, Billy walking easily alongside him. Ryan disembarks the passenger’s seat of the transport vehicle, and then, from the front seat, Marvin Milk climbs out from behind the steering wheel.

Dread sinks in Homelander’s stomach. He looks around for the rest of Billy’s cohorts, for the Frenchman or the Female, or for young Hughie Campbell. For Starlight. He doesn’t see them.

Ryan jogs after them, coming up on Billy’s side to take his hand.

“Ryan,” Homelander tries, heart lurching up into his throat. “Ryan—”

“Don’t start, John.”

He stumbles, hobbling himself to pass through the underground passage he’s led through. At some point, Billy and his handler trade charges. Ryan stays behind with their anonymous aide, and Billy assumes the position with his hand sealed tightly over the zip tie at Homelander’s back. They walk farther down the sloped, dismal tunnel, traversing through a number of chambers with pneumatic locks. Each station light flickers green before allowing them passage.

Once they’ve gone far enough that Homelander thinks they must be in a bomb shelter, Billy liberates a multitool from his pocket to clip Homelander’s restraints. He rubs at his wrist with his thumb, keenly aware in that moment of how sensitive the skin there is.

It’s cold down there, gray and walled off everywhere in concrete but for the few vents here and there to allow for air circulation.

“A cage,” he says, unimpressed. “That was your big fucking plan?”

Billy folds his arms over his chest and doesn’t reply. Homelander’s gearing up to dress him down when he hears something.

Himself.

Absurd, surely, but no. It’s undoubtedly him grunting and gasping and moaning, all to the mainstay of Billy’s low, coaxing voice, tethering Homelander’s soaring cries back down to earth. Homelander follows the noise to its source and finds a meager television set equipped with an absolutely ancient VCR. For all that it whirs threateningly, it does work, and the video it plays shows Homelander at Arcadia, decadently taking Billy’s dick six ways to Sunday.

His ears ring, and his heart jumps up into his throat, fluttering, racing. Fit to explode.

“What the fuck?”

“Well,” whispers the darkness at his back, half an unused rasp and half a whiskey melody. “If it isn’t the happy couple.”

Chapter 20: only begotten

Chapter Notes

Chapter warning for he.

Butcher wanders down the narrow concrete tunnel into the wider, darker barracks. He hears the TV before he sees it, and he feels that he’s being observed before he gets eyes on the watcher.

Out of the darkness he emerges, like some mythic construct from the smoke of a thousand funerary pyres. A dimple screwed in at the corner of his cruelly smiling mouth speaks of those sacrifices and allotments made, speaks to the carrion scraps of humanity that fell away to bring him here to this hole. 

Soldier Boy.

Even for the damp and the dark, he looks the same. Butcher has the strangest sense of vertigo looking from him to John, both of them stripped down to civvies with their hair twisted up in the back. Both of them trapped down here, twin flames caught under a glass and choking on the lack of combustible oxygen. A crash of metal and fracturing plastic breaks out through the fleshy, infrequent sounds of sex. The bunker plunges into silence.

White teeth flashing in a grin, humming from far back in his chest, the man and the myth, together with the legend, muses, “Billy.”

“Ben,” Butcher says.

Whipping around, and stumbling when he trips over the smashed A/V set, John says, “You can see him?”

Eyebrows dropping low and hard over his eyes, Soldier Boy mutters, “What a waste they let you turn out like this.”

“You—” John exclaims, pointing from Butcher to his dear ol’ dad. “—can fucking see him?”

“Sure I can, John.”

“He can see you, too,” Soldier Boy croons, dark eyes twinkling like the last flaring light out of a star long dead. “Sport.”

“Oh, God.”

“Thought that was you,” Butcher quips.

Soldier Boy laughs sharply. John flinches, backtracking right into the same heap of busted electronics he already walked into once. He catches some of the detritus underfoot and overbalances trying to get off it. It’s hard to watch, but it’s a familiar enough scene. Butcher’d been heavy-handed and clumsy his first time on the anti-V, too. It’s easier for him on this bout, but he’s been human more than just the one time. Coming back to this skin is like correcting the balance on his scales. It’s like re-breaking a bone to make sure it sets right. For John, it must still just feel like falling.

“God, huh?” Soldier Boy glances from John to Butcher, then tilts his head in consideration that has Butcher’s skin crawling. “I don’t see it.”

“No, no, no, this isn’t happening. This isn’t real. This isn’t real! You’re dead!”

Rather than grace John’s hysterics with a response, Soldier Boy rolls his eyes and turns to Butcher. “Got some time to kill before my directives from the ol’ bitch kick in. What do you think?”

“‘Bout what?”

“C’mon,” he purrs, a laugh living deep in his chest. He’s wide and made of muscle just like Butcher remembers, even under the plain scrubs Mallory dressed him in before setting him loose down here. “Your little porn reel? Pretty inventive stuff, I don’t mind telling you. Phew, and I thought the eighties were wild.”

Butcher doesn’t react. He’s viscerally aware of John’s eyes on them, though he makes no sound.

“Admittedly, you’re gonna lose some of your curb appeal once I get my juice back and do what I gotta. This broad, am I right? You go through all this trouble ’n she locks you in here with him.”

“Nobody put me here.”

“Huh.” Frowning, Soldier Boy folds his arms over his chest, tightening the hems on his sleeves where they pull at his biceps. He leans back against the wall, the long, lean line of him so clearly an invitation that Butcher can’t rightly say if it’d be a good idea to meet the intention, even as a joke. “Okay. Wasn’t expecting that. But, like I said. After. I’ll be here.”

“Yeah,” Butcher says.

“And what I was thinking was,” he murmurs, steamrolling right on ahead. “I mean, if you take it the other way? Up the ass, I mean.”

Butcher waits, his only reply being to cross his arms similarly across his chest and prop himself up against the opposite wall. It’s as much distance as he can put between them without openly retreating, and he’s spent enough time around Soldier Boy to know how he’ll take it if Butcher gives him a single opening or weakness to exploit. Artificial, subterranean light catches in his eyes, glimmering with understanding. A cobra bundled up in plain, inoffensive linens, poised to strike and only waiting for a signal.

“Don’t tell me this one ever really did anything for you,” he adds, droll, like they’re old sweethearts and he’s trying to get Butcher to go all the way. “I always did have a feeling about you, back when. Your skinny friend with the Bambi eyes kept me from seeing it, but it was there. You’re a man’s man, like me. You can’t get what a man needs,” he drawls, with a sly, sideways glance at John, “from a little boy.”

John doesn’t say a word. Neither does Butcher. He barely breathes.

“Ehh, don’t say no yet.” Soldier Boy favors him with a wink. Oily, lecherous, and every bit as scummy as he is handsome. “Think about it.”

There’s a scuffling then as John picks himself up from the ruins of the smashed TV and VCR.

Soldier Boy doesn’t even look at him. He’s only got eyes for Butcher, and isn’t that sweet. Isn’t that a fucking compliment, to have pulled not just Homelander, but his dad. 

If there were anything to be gained by it, he might even laugh, but as things stand, he’s a little too fucking unnerved to find any of this funny. It doesn’t help that he don’t rightly know which one of them’s getting their powers back first or how much of a lag there’ll have been between one’s dosing and the other. If they’re staggered too far apart and Soldier Boy gets his strength back first, that could put Butcher in a whole world of hurt. 

Then again, if him and John get theirs back too soon, there’ll only be Butcher to stop John killing his dad, which he’ll have to, if he gets the chance. It’s the only way he won’t permanently lose his powers.

“How are you alive,” John mumbles, bracing himself with his palm flat to the wall.

“After your lesbian girlfriend chucked herself and me out of a window, my, uh, madame was good enough to scrape me up off the pavement. Apparently they made a couple more advances in the chemical compounds since the last time I was vertical.” He holds one hand out in front of himself, tenses all his fingers so the tendons stretch taut. “I had to guess, I’d say they used me to do it. That about right?”

“Got it one,” Butcher tells him.

“You already tried this once,” John mutters, walking away from them and leaning on the wall for support. His gait’s hobbled on account of him favoring his left side. “It didn’t work then, it’s got no chance of working now.”

“Who’s gonna tell him he’s in a cage of reinforced steel coated in a special polymer blend,” Soldier Boy says, the words sluicing out of him like a saucy riddle, to match the twinkling in his eyes. He thumps his fist on the cold gray wall. In a voice not unlike an infomercial straight outta the fifties, he announces, “Made to tolerate extreme temperatures and absorb the radiant heat of laser beams. Called Starlite.”

John spins around, expression pinched off and flushed red at the edges. “Called what?”

“You heard him,” Butcher says, pushing his shoulders off the wall. He hones in on Soldier Boy as soon as he’s moving, keen on the smoky gaze tracking him. “Been down here long?”

“Couple hours, give or take. Started out pretty boring till they sent the tapes down. At first…” He dawdles, angling his head one way then the other like he’s got water in his ears. “Hell, at first, I was pretty upset, I admit it. Always thought if I did have a family, no son of mine was gonna be a goddamn pansy. Prided myself on that. But it wasn’t enough for you to be fucking soft, was it,” he says, cutting a harsh, icy look back at John. A beat hangs in the air, tense and dreadful, and then he’s shrugging loosely, muscles shifting beneath the skin of his forearms. He smiles lazily, nothing sweet about it or the glint in his eyes. “Hey, I get it. Times have changed.”

“Oh, this is you being progressive,” John spits, maybe forgetting himself. Maybe not.

“He whines, he cries, he talks back.” Bare feet pivoting on the plaster floor, he saunters over to John, in no great rush. His stride never breaks once until he can perch his open hands on John’s shoulders. Even then, he’s calm. He’s only calm. “You know what you remind me of? Those stand-up string puppets.” He half-turns to Butcher, cueing him in on the joke even as he seems to anticipate John’s attempt to get away from him. Unfazed, budging not an inch, not a millimeter, despite John’s struggling, he says, laughing, “A little painted doll held together by someone else’s handiwork, collapsible at the press of a button.”

And there he goes, collapsing his only son to the floor with the barest show of force. Maybe without any force at all. But that had been the point.

On a sigh, and while gazing down upon the quivering, whimpering mess at his feet, Soldier Boy says, almost wistfully, “I don’t need a reason to get my dick wet, and I don’t need permission to like it.” Looking over his shoulder again at Butcher, inviting him again to join in on the fun, he adds, “Here I thought we had that in common.”

“This doin’ something for you, Ben, or what?”

“Me? No.” He flashes a smile that touches his eyes and steps away from the crumpled form of his son, America’s supe. He moseys over towards Butcher like he’s got all the time in the world to close that distance. Like what he wants’ll be there as soon as he decides it’s his, so he can stand to wait a little longer. No big deal. “Thought that was your beat.”

“You thought what was?”

“Punishment.”

Hackles raising, Butcher flickers his eyes from Soldier Boy to the overturned TV set and says, “How many of them tapes did you watch?”

“I watched everything they sent down.” Holding Butcher’s eyes, he says, “Probably there was more I didn’t see.” He shrugs. “Yeah, you know, for a while I was pretty hot about the whole thing. Sat up thinking on all the things I’d do the next time I got my hands on you.” He traces the lingering bruises in Butcher’s face with his eyes, worse than if he’d just reached out and touched him. “From the looks of things, somebody else got there first.”

There’s nowhere to go. Butcher can’t get away from him without looking like he’s turning tail, and he can’t be seen doing that. So he leans in, smirking, and says, “You wanna go, I got a space free on my dance card. We’ll cut a rug.”

Soldier Boy hums a laugh. “How ‘bout a wager then? As long as we’re gonna be stuck down here for the foreseeable future, I mean, we gotta make our own entertainment, right?”

“What’s the wager?”

“We bare-knuckle it. You and me, mano a mano. First to tap out loses.”

“What’s the winner get?”

Eyes twinkling, a dynamite wick burning itself down ever closer towards destruction, Soldier Boy tells him, “Whatever he wants.”

“That could be bad for me,” Butcher says airily.

“Or for me,” Soldier Boy answers, clearly not meaning it. Wheedling, like ain’t a walking atom bomb when the V in his veins is active, he urges, “C’mon. I haven’t had a clean, decent fight in probably sixty years.”

“And you wanna put your dick in me.”

Tipping his head, he says, “I like a fight and a fuck. Telling me you don’t? You?”

“I ain’t sayin’ I don’t, but if I win—” Butcher plows right through Soldier Boy’s derisive snort. “—and I wanna put it in your mouth, you might get buyer’s remorse.”

“I’d never,” Soldier Boy deadpans around a razor sharp grin. “You wanna put your pecker near my teeth, Billy, well, you can. No complaints outta me. And hey, same goes the other way, right? If I win and it won’t do for me but to see you two lovebirds tear each other apart…” He hums, the full curve of his mouth set in an exaggerated moue, a hand to his chest clutching his pearls. “Well, in that case, maybe you’d be the one having second thoughts, hmm? You just never know, and that’s the beauty of it. Carte fuckin’ blanche.”

“Uh huh. So lemme see if I got this right. If you win, you get your way; if you don’t, you still get your way.”

Soldier bares his teeth in a white, white grin. “Doesn’t much sound like losing, does it.”

“No, but it wouldn’t,” Butcher says, cracking his neck one way and then the other. “Man like you bein’ made to feel stupid, I know you gotta take it outta my hide. I ain’t dumb enough to think you’d do this cuz you actually want it.”

“No, no, hey. Me? I’ve come around to it. ‘Love is love,’ right? And I am just— well, Billy, I’m overcome, and all I want is to show you exactly how much.”

Facetious fucking prick.

Butcher shakes out his hands and twists from one side to the other, working out a few kinks in his lower back from the long drive up. “No-holds-barred?”

“Yeah, that’d be Jake.”

Without taking his eyes off Soldier Boy, Butcher asks, “What about him?”

What has Butcher nervous don’t hold Soldier Boy up none. He looks away to assess the still-crumpled shape of his son puddled on the floor. The careless line of his neck is long and open, an invitation as much as it is an insult. Butcher knows from talking to him that Soldier Boy ain’t unfeeling. He’s been the one to feel small, once, but never again, and certainly not here. Not when he’s got the current source of his trouble locked in a cage with him, at his disposal.

Turning back to Butcher at last, impassive, bored, even, Soldier Boy says, “What about him?”

“Are you really gonna sic him on me, after?”

“If I feel like it, we’ll see. Between you and me?” He leans in just the barest bit, dark eyes glittering. “I never thought about it with a guy before. Not even as a, uh, well, what you’re saying this is.”

“A bid to humiliate me.”

“Whatever.” He shrugs, loose about the shoulders as he has been. Nothing’s roused him since they came down here. Not John, and not Butcher. His lips peel back in a distasteful, exaggerated wince. “I never got that. Sex and power, power and sex. What kinda man needs one to get the other?”

“The kind what’s empty ‘less he can say he’s got both,” Butcher mutters, darting a glance at John, to see him looking back.

Soldier Boy takes a sedate step toward him, and Butcher returns his gaze where it belongs. Only his eyes move, no other part of him. He’s aware of the certainty that even as they’re standing here sizing each other up, he’s in a fight for his life.

“We gonna do this or not?”

Butcher shifts onto the balls of his feet, settling into his stance. “We’re doin’ it. Any old time you’re ready, Granddad.”

The corners of his eyes crinkle in a smile, and then he strikes, fast as a fucking snake.

Scrapes like this Butcher knows well. He’s been sidestepping black eyes or walking into ‘em smirking since he was a kid, and his right cross is as strong as it ever was, without the V backing it up. It’s the tiniest comfort seeing the companion acknowledgment on Soldier Boy’s face, that he asked for a fight and now he’s fucking got one, though it’s not likely to stay clean for long. That’s all right. Butcher knows those kinds of fights, too. He tags Soldier Boy right across the cheek with his fist and pays for it with a sock to the mouth that bloodies up his teeth.

Soldier Boy brushes his knuckles down his reddening cheek, already puffing up under his eye, and Butcher spits a bloody gob onto the concrete, staining it black. Looking down at it reminds him of pus and catarrhal rot sliding down the back of his throat, sitting on his chest, blocking up his ears. Reminds him of reading Becca’s book at the kitchen table, ready to die until his stay of execution came in through the window, leading with his dick as per usual.

 “Y’know what our problem is, Billy?” The smile and his carefree sway of his shoulders suggests evening strolls through the park, not this. He blasts through Butcher’s guard with a swift one-two punch, hardly flinching at all when Butcher rallies back with a right hook. “We were born in the wrong era.”

Butcher doesn’t get a chance to pop back before he’s slammed bodily into the wall. Even watered down to his core parts, Soldier Boy’s a fucking tank. In his efforts to twist free, he finds himself smeared into the plaster, heat and muscle pressed to every inch of his back. It’s not good. He braces himself and shoves off, hoping like hell Soldier Boy takes the bait. With Soldier Boy bear-hugging him from behind, Butcher kicks off the wall to create the space he needs, insinuates one hand beneath the chain of Soldier Boy’s arms to grip his own wrist, and breaks the hold.

Caught by the arm, he’s forced to stagger wherever Butcher leads, even when his own basic range of motion won’t allow for it. Butcher forces back the leg closest to him, wrenching Soldier Boy in place so he folds forward at the waist.

With his shoulder shoved up under Butcher’s armpit, the blood probably rushing to his head because of the angle, Soldier Boy snaps, waspishly, “Fuck kinda queer ballerina bullshit is this?”

“Physics lesson,” Butcher grits out. “This here’s called — a vector,” he says, yanking up on the arm he’s got control of.

Soldier Boy spins out and hits the ground hard, laid out flat on his back. Butcher springs away, wanting as much distance as he can get now that he can pass it off as a tactic. It’s all stillness then, until Soldier Boy gets back to his feet. His smirking mouth’s the same, but his eyes are different, harder. Angry, where they weren’t before.

“Tough guy,” he croons, tonguing at a jeweled spot of blood at his bottom lip. “I like it.”

“C’mon then.”

No sooner than Butcher’s said it, there’s Soldier Boy charging him full force. They trade blows back and forth, neither managing to land anything without getting drilled right back. A cut opens up over Butcher’s eyebrow that bleeds like hell. He smashes Soldier Boy in the ribs just to slow him down, just to get a free second to try wiping the blood out of his eye.

It’s time he doesn’t have. Soldier Boy catches him on the back foot, punching him square in the face while he’s distracted. Half-blinded by his own blood and reeling from the hit, Butcher fires back senselessly with a right cross. He’s still blinking stars from his eyes when he feels his fist connect.

Soldier Boy slumps against the wall, a line of red streaked across the bridge of his nose and bleeding sluggishly. His breath comes fast and hard, to match Butcher’s.

“Why’d we never do this before, eh?” Butcher asks, maybe curious, maybe stalling. He’s got one eye squeezed shut against the sticky warmth gluing his eyelashes together. “Temp V came with a time limit, sure, but…weren’t so bad we couldn’t’a thrown down if we felt like it.”

“I didn’t have much of a stake in wanting to see you bleed at the time.”

“Or in makin’ it happen,” Butcher adds, letting the cheek show in his eyebrow, the one that ain’t currently pumping blood straight into his eye.

“I’ll give you this, Billy,” Soldier Boy murmurs, lurching off the wall to stand more or less upright. He passes beneath one of the overheads, and the cold white light plunges the shadows of his face into their darkest parts for the span of a held breath. Then he’s taking another step, and he just looks like himself again. “Teaming up with you? Shit was better than the good ol’ days. Payback? They were smalltime compared to you, and you wanna know why?”

“They were afraid of you.”

Soldier Boy smiles patiently. “You’re afraid of me.”

“Your curb appeal ain’t so high with all that red on your mug.”

“Funny you mention that.” He runs his tongue over his teeth, a flash of red catching from his busted lip. “I can’t remember the last time I tasted blood. Lotsa times it’s just what I wanted, but…nobody ever could give it to me like this.”

“Ben, don’t take this the wrong way,” Butcher mutters, swiping at the blood on his face, “but that’s the gayest thing anyone’s ever said to me.” His eyelashes are all gummed up, but the bleeding’s lessened to the point he can actually keep both eyes open. “And your son calls me fucking daddy.”

“He does, doesn’t he,” he says, loping closer in a way that’s so like John it makes Butcher dizzier than his concussion. “Think you’ll call me daddy?”

Butcher’s halfway to answering when Soldier Boy rushes him. He’s got his breath back enough that it don’t bowl him over, but it’s a close thing. His feet skid across the ground while he’s propelled back. He’s expecting the wall again, but then there’s a foot hooking his ankle and sweeping his leg out from under him. All the breath squeezes out of him in one ragged gasp, flattened to nothing under the crushing weight of America’s first darling. Her first Homelander.

“Go ‘head,” Soldier Boy purrs, “give it a try.”

His fist in Butcher’s side gets something vital that feels like a crushed cyanide capsule spilling into his meat. His kidney, maybe, or his liver.

Teeth glinting in a bright grin to match his sparking eyes, Soldier Boy whispers, “Say daddy.”

Butcher rears back as far as he can what with the pavement at his back, sets his jaw, and smashes his forehead into Soldier Boy’s. It offsets him enough that Butcher can lever him off, but the sick feeling crawling from his side makes him slow. He only halfway manages to unseat Soldier Boy from on top of him, but that’s enough to get a clear shot at his temple, and Butcher takes it.

Soldier Boy slips sideways off him, his shoulder hitting the ground and shortly followed by his hand. He tries to get up, with shit results. Butcher drags himself into a sit, feeling like he might puke any minute, and looks up when movement draws his eye — John tromping over, pissed off and maladroit and about as unsubtle as a heart attack. Butcher scrambles to his feet to divert him, catching him about the waist like they’re partners in a dance. Unfazed, John tries it again, getting as far as lifting his foot in a deliberate setup for a curb stomp, before Butcher shoves him away.

“Don’t make me fucking hurt you.”

John opens his mouth, and then Butcher’s vision whites out and the world goes sideways before dropping out from under him. He feels the path Soldier Boy cuts stepping around Butcher, and he hears, as if underwater, the next words outta his mouth.

“Something to say, Son? What’s the matter, cat got your tongue? I can fix that for you.”

Butcher peels himself off the ground, or tries to. He can’t fucking see. Can’t make his limbs firm up from the soup they’ve turned into. The back of his skull’s on fire.

Next thing he knows Soldier Boy’s hand’s a claw in his hair, dragging him up onto his knees.

“This about what you had in mind, Billy?”

Dazed, Butcher gets his eyes open to see what he’s being shown. That’s what Soldier Boy wants from him, to bear witness, and he does. John’s laid out, not moving. Maybe he’s had his bell rung. Butcher can relate.

As before, he’s got no chance at putting an answer together before Soldier Boy’s moving in on him again. He’s pulled up onto his feet, clearly at some cost to both their bodies if the same stretched groan it draws outta them both is any indication. Butcher’s collapsed against the wall for the two seconds he’s left to stand on his own power, and then, he’s forced up onto his toes, a big hand wrapped tightly around his throat. If nothing else, it at least wakes him up.

“You wanna know why we never did this?” Soldier Boy croons, winded but coherent. “Because even without the V, I’d fucking kill you.”

Butcher chokes, spots dancing across his eyes, and wads his hand up into an uncooperative fist. He tries for a haymaker, as well as he can while he’s pinned like a bug to a board, and the blow catches Soldier Boy dead in the ear. Must be hell on his eardrum since it gets him to let go. Good thing, too. Another few seconds without air woulda put him under.

“So why don’t you do it then,” he grunts, staggering back blindly, still in survival mode. “Kill us both. No one’ll stop you.”

Caught again under one of the overhead lights, his whole face falling into shadow, Soldier Boy tips his head, like a dog hearing a whistle. For a second, Butcher’s hit with the certainty that he’s about to die, that Soldier Boy’s powers just came back and he’s about to pop Butcher like a grape, but he just walks out from under the light, back to looking like a man, same as any other. Butcher’s heart keeps on beating. The world still spins.

“Well, see, I’ve got selfish reasons for wanting you to walk outta here.”

“Them bein’ what?”

“When they gas the room and put me under again, I go back to being Gracie’s dirty little secret, and for all her hangups, there’s one thing she’s got up on you. That ice water in her veins.” He feigns a shiver, grinning widely as he does. “She ain’t much to look at now, but lemme tell ya, back then? Mmm. She was real trim. Buck-twenty, if that, natural blonde. Pretty. And boy, she looked right through me. Like I was just another creep at the bar.”

“Cuz you were.”

“My point is,” Soldier Boy says, drawing up within hitting range of Butcher but still with his hands down at his sides and his chin up. “Shit, what was I saying?”

“I think you were saying you’re a goddamn pest.”

“Maybe to her, but not to you. Not to Homelander. No, to you, I’m…well, I’m more of a nightmare.”

“And you wanna be remembered, is that it?”

“Tellin’ me you don’t?”

“I am tellin’ you that, yeah.”

It draws him up short, and Butcher can’t help but relieved for the break, however short. He stays on guard, can’t afford not to, but he does let himself take two full breaths to build back his strength. If this ain’t over in the next couple minutes he ain’t gonna have the steam to keep getting back up. Whatever organ in his side that took damage before’s still giving him grief. 

“Quiet life, that’s what you want?”

“Was once. Now, I dunno.”

“What’s her name?” Soldier Boy asks, that goddamn dimple making a reappearance.

“Her name was Becca.” Butcher presses a hand gingerly to his ribs, just above that sweet spot that feels like the muscle’s eating itself. “His name is Hugh.”

Eyebrows pulling down to match his frown, Soldier Boy grimaces and says, “Cum-guzzler Hugh? Hughie Hugh?”

“Nah, his dad.” He straightens out as best he can. It’s fire in his side, but at least he manages to hold his head up. “You wanna hear me call out for daddy so bad, that’s whose name I’ll be thinking, and you know what else? You, him,” Butcher says, nodding his head at John, still flat on his back. “You ain’t never gonna have from me what Hugh’s got. Or Hughie, for that matter.”

“What’s that?”

“My fucking respect.”

Soldier Boy rolls his eyes, but there’s something almost rueful settling in on his face. It ages him, some. Makes him look every bit as mortal as the anti-V’s rendered him. He says, more to himself than to Butcher, “My old man woulda loved you.”

“Funny. Your son said the same thing to me about you.”

“What’s not to love,” Soldier Boy murmurs, sardonic, beginning to smile for real again. He drops his eyes to where Butcher’s holding that tender spot under his ribs. Just like before when he gave Butcher his neck without a care in the world, he’s saying with his body what he don’t need to say in words. That he knows exactly how much space he takes up, how all the fucking air goes outta the room just for him, and he ain’t a bit worried about where his next breath’s coming from. “You ready to go again?”

Some cross between his low baritone and the question itself gets the blood rushing to Butcher’s face. It’s a slight and a threat, masquerading as courtesy.

“Gotta tell ya, I ain’t got much left in me.”

“Oh, I know. That’s all right.”

Butcher takes one last breath to fill his lungs, takes his hand away, and nods. Unlike the previous few times, Soldier Boy doesn’t run at him first thing. He paces, leonine, eyes on Butcher all the while, even when he passes under a a black shadow between overhead lights. It puts a long tunnel at his back, cavernous in the dark and seeming to go on forever.

“Come on back this way.”

The skin on the back of his neck prickling up, Butcher asks, “Why?”

“More room to stretch out.” He shrugs, shaking his head mildly in that aw-shucks kinda way. Back to treating Butcher like a bird he’s trying to fuck, and well, yeah, he’s said as much. “I noticed you don’t do so well in close corners.”

“That’s the brain damage,” Butcher deadpans, to a snort from his adversary.

“I shouldn’t’a hit you in the back of the head. That was dirty, even for me, but hell. I lost my temper. It won’t happen again,” he promises, holding out his hand.

Facetious fucking prick.

Butcher brushes him off, walking ahead into the dark, more boldly than he feels. Figures, if this is it, it’s it. He ain’t got long before the charade’s up. Hardly makes any difference whether it’s now or in ten minutes. Soldier Boy lets him pass, unseen and unheard following after Butcher in the dark. 

He puts one foot in front of the other, following the narrow corridor as his eyes adjust. Sure enough, it feeds into an open chamber. There’s nothing so mundane as furniture inside, but there is a bedroll budged up into one corner and some gallon jugs of water. In one corner, there’s a bucket standing in for a toilet. Grazing Butcher’s shoulder with his chest, Soldier Boy reaches up and gets the pull chain for the light.

“Not quite the lifestyle you’re used to,” Butcher notes.

“It’s a half-step up from cryo,” Soldier Boy murmurs, close enough to grab Butcher in another one of his bear hugs, except he doesn’t. He doesn’t move a bit. “Y’know, we could get outta here. There’s enough steel and concrete and fuckin’ Starlite to hold off a nuclear apocalypse down here. You stay on the right side of these barracks, and I could blast Junior without getting you. When he’s outta the way? We walk.”

“They ain’t ever gonna open them doors while you’re upright.” In the silence that follows, Butcher hears Soldier Boy’s proposal. “You want me to be your fucking mule.”

“Could always use him.” Soldier Boy palms Butcher’s side where the worst of his hurt’s at. “I’d rather not, but needs must if you’re gonna be fucking difficult about it. So what do you say? Can I count on you?” He applies pressure, white-hot, daring Butcher to jerk away from him, but he stays put, bearing it. “Or are you gonna disappoint me, too?”

Choking down the cry rising in his throat like bile, Butcher covers Soldier Boy’s hand with his, just so he can pry it off him. “That’s a no from me, Ben.”

Soldier Boy’s grip switches around to Butcher’s wrist, yanking hard so Butcher spins around into him, his one arm held tightly behind him. There’s a hand at his jaw, not to silence him, but keeping him from breaking away. They’re standing so close, all it takes for Butcher to test the veracity of the shit talking to this point is to move his leg. He finds what he’s looking for and doesn’t have it in him to be surprised.

“Yeah, I feel you, too,” Soldier Boy rumbles, unsmiling and implacable in his intensity. 

His hand moves from Butcher’s jaw to the side of his face, and then he’s kissing him, hard and deep and dropping Butcher down a long way from rational thought and common sense. He feels himself surge into that pressure, gripping onto the body that’s holding him upright. There’s another tongue in his mouth in place of air to breathe, but it does the same thing for him, and he chases it. He clings to the heat it fills him up with, and he’s not the only one. Once he catches himself, once he realizes how hard his heart’s pounding and why, he shoves Soldier Boy off him.

“Fuck you.”

Soldier Boy presses his tongue to a sharp canine in a silent laugh. Not all the blood in his smile is his. “Last chance, Billy.”

“I don’t want a fucking chance with you. I’d rather die down here.”

Tilting his head, Soldier Boy says, “Well, all right.”

He stalks forward, back to trading blows with Butcher. Every hit he lands is solid but, unbeknownst to him, inconsequential. But Butcher wants him to think he’s burning him down. Wants him to think he’s done, and he’s close, definitely, but he’s got the juice for one last hail Mary pass. 

His chance comes at the expense of a sharp jab that crunches his nose and makes his eyes water. Soldier Boy’s good and open for an uppercut to his chin, and Butcher puts all his strength into it. When the hit does just what Butcher hoped it would and Soldier Boy reels back, Butcher seizes on a rear naked choke, twining his arms about his neck from behind and holding tight.It works for all of three seconds before Soldier Boy slams him back into the wall, trying to stun him back. Butcher just needs a couple seconds more when his grip loosens. Soldier Boy breaks his hold and throws him over his shoulder.

Butcher hits the ground hard. He tries to get to his feet but Soldier Boy’s right there, falling onto him and pinning him down with his body. They’re about the same size, height-wise, but Butcher ain’t got the body mass Soldier Boy has, and he feels every bit of that difference squeezing the life of out of him now. He flings an elbow back, catching dead air on the first few tries but cracking a lucky shot off Soldier Boy’s temple on the last, toppling him off. Desperate to regain control of the fight, Butcher scrambles on top of him. He only gets a few decent hits in when Soldier Boy yanks him in close and hammer-fists Butcher over the back of the head, just about in the same spot he’d drilled him earlier.

Soldier Boy flips them again just to get blindsided by a right cross to his chin. He drops his forehead into Butcher’s shoulder, gasping for breath, chest heaving right there with Butcher’s. This time he moves his leg, and Butcher’s body, without input from his brain, falls open for him. Feeling it, or maybe hearing Butcher’s sharp intake of breath, Soldier Boy looks up at him, chin propped on Butcher’s collar bone, face peppered in new bruises and fresh cuts. He’s a handsome guy, and Butcher’s always known, but goddamn. Their bodies rise and rock together, hands and hips moving as one uncoordinated, frantic beast. They kiss like they’re trying to devour and they pull at each other like they’re trying to dismantle. Maybe they are, on both counts. 

It feels like ripping himself out of dreaming into wakefulness, but Butcher gets free. He slips out from under that body, out from under those seeking, grasping hands and that hot mouth he can still taste on his tongue. 

Get up, he thinks. Get away from him. Get out of here. It’s all over.

He just lays there, unable to follow the prompts beamed down from his instincts. From his higher reasoning. None of it makes it through to his skin. Soldier Boy fists his hand in Butcher’s shirt, aligning his knuckles over his thundering heart. He laughs, soft and rueful. 

“I’m not getting out of here, am I?”

Butcher expects to feel calm. He keeps on waiting to be at peace down here, confronted with his inevitable, terrible demise, so long as it means John’ll go with him one way or another, and here, now, it should be what he wants to hear. It should reassure him. Any of this should, but that feeling of standing on the ledge, about to be pushed off, lingers. It’s only now that he realizes the drop isn’t what he thought it was. He grasps after Soldier Boy’s wrist, trying to preserve this moment so he won’t have to face that truth, even as it crashes over him.

Soldier Boy turns to look at him, still just speaking with that same soft voice. “And then what?”

“There’s a world outside of this,” Butcher whispers, looking away.

“Yeah, but for us?” He laughs again, the blood in his teeth drawing the light. His fist tightens against Butcher’s heart. “You’ll walk outta here today— you and him— but you won’t, really. Even with the sun on your face, you’ll be down here with me. Part of you’s always gonna be down here with me.”

Talking through the tremulous, terrible feeling in his throat, Butcher tells him, flip as can be, “Sounds kinda romantic when you put it like that.”

“Oh, yeah, I’m Clark fucking Gable.”

“Well, you ain’t no gentleman,” Butcher jokes, starting to get unsteadily to his feet.

Laughing sharply, Soldier Boy retorts, “And you’re sure as shit no lady.”

He lumbers onto his feet after Butcher, lurching but managing to stay upright. Butcher does, too, though it’s a kinda miracle on its own. A sharp breeze would knock him down right about now, to say nothing of Soldier Boy’s fists. He waits patiently for his punishment, knowing this is the end, and Soldier Boy knows it, too. He’s calm with it, like he’s been for most of their time down here.

“Time?” he asks, back to that gentle, near-sweet tone he can switch on and off.

Butcher cracks his neck, fills his lungs to say, Yeah, it’s time, and falters, feeling everywhere in his body the oxygen’s moving. Feeling a second wind hit him fast and sure. Molten light crackles in his eyes.

He lunges forward, hands on Soldier Boy’s arms to heave him out of the way. It takes all his precision to move quickly without hurting him, but it still feels molasses-slow to Butcher. Anything would feel slow compared to the lightning strike that sweeps him off his feet and through the wall at his back. John’s leaned over him, literal fire in his eyes and his hands at Butcher’s throat. The black eye his dad gave him mirrors the one he gave Butcher back in Kentucky. Least his eye ain’t swollen shut.

“I gave you all of this,” he hisses, a fleck of spittle clinging to his bottom lip. “You think you can just take it away from me?”

“No,” Butcher rasps, robbed of air by the hands choking him, “but he can.”

Silent like a big cat prowling the jungle, like a full-maned lion descending on his own young, Soldier Boy hauls John off Butcher by the scruff of his neck. He holds him from behind, hands locked in an s-grip that has Butcher feeling, in turns, sympathy and a kind of dejection he’s never not gonna understand. He wonders how long it’ll take John to realize what Butcher knows for certain looking up at him tangled in his dad’s killer embrace. That he’s never getting out of here. None of them are.

Soldier Boy’s chest starts to glow, an oven coming up to temp. Ready to change them forever. Butcher tries to keep his eyes open, but the light’s too much. It feels like the end of the world. It feels like the beginning.

He shuts his eyes.

 

/

 

After the light, after the walls shake and burn and disintegrate, there’s a vapor left creeping amidst the smoke. Homelander sees Dad through the clouds for just a second. He’s like a mirage, a dream dissolved in the trap of his mind before it can fully take shape. He’s crouched over Billy, offering his hand, pulling him up in the same breath that his legs buckle beneath him.

Billy follows him down, laying him to rest easy even as his eyelids flutter. His mouth moves, saying something Homelander can’t hear. Something that’s for Butcher, and that Butcher has an answer for, though Homelander can’t hear that either. There’s so much he can’t hear now. He can’t hear Billy’s heart beating in his chest. Can’t hear Ryan playing cards with Marvin Milk. Can’t hear Grace Mallory’s hushed commands to her teams above and blow ground. These are things that exist outside of him now. Beyond his reach.

He doesn’t remember being hauled out of the subterranean facility. He doesn’t remember where they take Dad— doesn’t know what refuse compartment they shove him into when he’s not of use to anyone— and he’s struck by the realization that he doesn’t want to know what discard pile for defunct supes looks like.

Wherever Billy is, Homelander doesn’t see him again. He keeps expecting him every time he turns around, but he’s never there. He sees Ryan for a minute outside, but his demeanor’s confusing, strange. It isn’t until some hours later when Homelander’s being transported to a different facility that he understands why Ryan wouldn’t meet his eyes.

“What charges?”

Ashley sits opposite a wall of shatterproof glass, wig and fuchsia skirt suit immaculate to match her expression. “You wanna hear them all? Mallory’s hit squad of attorneys sent a dated and itemized list giving names and complaints. They sent it to us, to the D.O.J., and even the New York Fucking Times. Here’s the highlights: the jumper, Chelsea Horowitz, Amy Mitchel, the little girl from the airplane video, and Becca fucking Butcher, Homelander. Becca fucking Butcher.”

Homelander feels the bottom dropping out, and he lashes out wildly looking for a life raft. So often, that’s been Ashley. “Okay,” he mumbles, lost, “what do we do?”

“We? No, no, no. That’s what I came here to tell you. You’re no longer Vought’s problem.”

“I— you’re fucking dropping me?”

“We deal in supes.” She presses her hand to the glass. “Which you aren’t. Not anymore.”

He stares at her, open-mouthed, stupid with shock. He slams his hand into the glass to overlay hers, and though she doesn’t flinch, he has to grit his teeth against the pain, sudden and bright in his stinging palm.

“This is fucking temporary. Get me the V, and I’ll be back to myself in a fucking second.”

“No, you won’t.” Ashley takes her hand away to root around through the pocket of her blazer. She unsheathes a series of printed screenshots and fans them out on the table before looking up at him, calm, cool, and collected. “You won’t. See?”

He cranes his neck to look, bumping his forehead on the glass in his haste. A dizzy, slip-sliding feeling shudders through him at the unfocused blurriness to his vision. The articles themselves are too small for him to read, but he can see the headlines. Even at a glance they’re damning. Career suicide, to say nothing of the leaked videos. Or the fucking charges.

“They can’t prove I did any of this—”

“Even barring the fact that you called him your son in front of a live crowd, Ryan Butcher agreed to a DNA test.”

“The statute of limitations—”

“Does not apply in instances of rape. Not in the state of New York.”

“I didn’t—”

Ashley’s eyes flash, no-nonsense and shutting him up in a moment. She stares at him, her anger fast deteriorating to disgust the longer he has nothing substantial to say in his defense that isn’t just shirking responsibility. Nostrils flaring, she points at him, saying, “Do you know what Blindspot’s doing right now? Do you even remember him?”

Homelander has to think, but Ashley lets him. After a few seconds of dread silence, his memory conjures up a costume. The stupid helmet, blood pouring out the earholes and puddling around a writhing body on the mats.

“I remember him.”

“We were able to secure a role for him in a special program upstate. Equine Therapy, teaching kids to ride horses, but he doesn’t handle the horses. He mucks out the stables. Sometimes they let him work in the garden, planting vegetables. The staff tells me half the time they don’t root, and when they do, mites and whiteflies eat away at what grows. That’s the life you gave him. He was a sweet kid and he could’ve been your teammate, but you picked a fucking Nazi.”

She shuffles the printouts together and tucks them back into her pocket. With a furious tremor in her voice, she says, “No one’s coming for you, Homelander. Not now, not ever.”

“Ashley—”

“You deserve to be here, and someday? When the dust clears, and all you have to your name is weeds and food for weevils? It still won’t be enough of a punishment for all the terrible things you’ve done.”

The phone slams into its cradle and Ashley stands, brandishing a middle finger at him before straightening her jacket and walking off. Walking out of a life that was his six hours ago, before the world tilted into this funhouse approximation of what it ought to be. He crushes the phone in his hand, and the cheerful yellow plastic doesn’t even creak.

In the media storm that follows, in the tizzy of being shuffled from one facility to another and trying to obtain legal representation without backing or guidance from Vought, Homelander only ever receives one visitor, and it’s not Ashley or Ryan or Billy. It’s Victoria Neumann, polished and queenlike in her royal blue blazer. Her dark eyes glitter watching him be walked into the room, flicking from his leg irons to his face.

“What do you want?” he says, slumping at the table as soon as the guards leave.

“Just to look at you,” she muses. “I wanted to let you know the world’s still spinning. Oh, and your boyfriend bonded out of county today.”

Homelander looks up at her. “Bonded out?”

“Nonconsensual pornography is actually punishable by law.” She props her cheek in her hand. “Even if it was a brilliant fucking coup on his part, nobody wants to be seen endorsing revenge porn. It’s not a good look. So, he’ll be tried just like you will. The difference is, in your case, the courts won’t be setting bail. You’ve been deemed a flight risk.” A smirk cuts across her red mouth. “Gosh, that’s kind of funny. You, flying out of here.”

They pass a brief silence watching each other. He leans forward, some of the conspiratorial perhaps glinting in his eyes because she leans forward, too, a tranquility emanating out of her that lights into a smile as he hesitates. Her dulcet smile becomes sharper at the edges the longer she holds his gaze.

“If you’re about to ask for my help, please do. Get on your knees, and say, please, Vick, please come to my rescue.”

He grits his teeth and doesn’t answer.

Leering, she says, “You threatened my daughter, you son of a bitch. Did you think I forgot?”

“If you get me out of here—” Homelander falls over himself to plead, as close to begging for his life as he’s ever been. 

“Not gonna happen.”

“I could protect her, I could protect both of you—”

Neumann rises to her feet, abhorrence plain to see in her eyes and in the set of her frown. “In all your life, John,” she drawls, staring him down, “who have you ever protected?”

“I can change! I can…”

Pressure rises against the inside of his skull, strange vertigo stretching the room like taffy all around him. A trickle of warm liquid heat runs down one nostril, and then, like coming up for air after too long underwater, his ears pop and his vision clears, and Neumann’s watching him. The marbled storm clouds clear from her eyes.

“You’re not going to hurt anyone else. No more families are going to be destroyed because of you. You’re done. I wanted to be the one to tell you. I wanted to look you in the eye and see the moment it hit you that this is it. This is your life now.”

Homelander presses his hand to the blood at his lip and doesn’t say a word.

Neumann waves at the guard through the window. The door opens, and just like Ashley before her, she walks out of his life for good. He wonders absently how many of those he has left, and how many more have passed him by already without his knowing it. He wonders if he’ll see Ryan again, or Billy. Or Dad. He wonders if he’ll see himself as he was, but maybe that version of him walked out, too. Maybe he really is alone, after everything. Maybe this is how it was always going to be.

Returned to his cell, curled up on the sparse little mattress therein, he contemplates the bloodstain on his hand. He tries to fathom the scope of Neumann’s words, tries to fathom a life confined to a cage, and has to laugh hysterically through a flood of tears at the realization that— 

It’s the only life he’s ever known.

Chapter 21: said Isaac to Abraham

Chapter Notes

If you read the first version of this chapter that was posted.......no, you didn't. 🥹

Anyway, Butchdaddy! <3

See the end of the chapter for more notes

The sun’s shining down on Butcher the day he strolls on out of County. He sees the lads waiting for him on the other side of a chainlink fence at the end of a winding concrete footpath. MM holds up his hand in a wave without pushing off from where he’s leaned up against the driver’s side door to the VW bus. Frenchie looks up next, and then Hughie, then the girls. Butcher offers up a wave, squinting against the high noon sun. 

At the end of the concrete slope, he has to wait for the pneumatic fence to slide open before he can saunter out to meet his freedom. The first taste he gets comes bookended by dear Hughie’s freshly healed fist in his face.

“Oy! The fuck was that for?”

Hughie shakes his hand out, his face all screwed up in discomfort. He uses his other healed hand to dig a folded up envelope out of his pocket. 

Dehydrated sunflower petals spill out of the card inside when Butcher opens it.

 

Your dad fucks like a pornstar. <3
—Your Local Mindreader ;)

 

“Ah.”

“Asshole,” Hughie snipes without heat, only a little at odds with the hug he follows up with.

Butcher pats Hughie on the back. Even for the smarting in his cheek, this welcome feels miles above what he really deserves. He’s not sure what he thinks he’s owed in lieu of what he’s getting, not anymore, but this ain’t a bad place to start, with Hughie’s bollocksed strawberry shampoo in his nose. Right around when he’s started to accept that he’s allowed to have this, the door to the VW bus creaks open, and who else gets down to look up at him but Ryan.

Hughie twists around to look at him, no danger for him now in popping open a hole in his neck. He lets Butcher go and steps aside.

“Hey, monkey.”

“Hi, Butcher.” Ryan walks right up to him, his mum’s eyes soft in his face. Calm and knowing, now that he’s learned where he comes from and how. “Are you okay?”

“I’m all right.” He covers Ryan’s shoulder with his hand, denying his impulse to ruffle Ryan’s hair, lest he feel babied by it. “Are you?”

Ryan bounces on his heels, restless, fretful, looking every bit a child. He nods but doesn’t answer right away, and Butcher, having fucked up royally the first time he found himself confronted with this worried, fraught silence from Becca, has learned how to meet it the right way. He slips his hand around to the back of Ryan’s neck, holding there until he relents to look Butcher in the eye again, and waits. 

Mouth squashed into a wobbly line, Ryan’s eyes well up with tears, and he lurches forward, forgetting his strength a little when he wraps Butcher in a tight hug, but that’s fine, that is.

“I’m sorry I weren’t here for you,” Butcher murmurs, taking great care just then to cast a wide net on his saying of it. He looks around, clocking MM and Frenchie and Hughie; even Annie and Kimiko. He’s been a right bastard and he knows he has, but here they are, ready to bring him back to some kinda life he’d long given up on wanting. “I’m gonna be better.”

“Really?” Ryan asks, at the same time that Hughie says—

“Give us your word.”

“What difference is that gonna make, Hughie,” MM muses.

“I tend to agree,” Frenchie chimes in.

“It’ll make him feel shittier the next time he decides to act like an asshole prima donna bitch baby,” Hughie informs them, folding his arms testily in front of his chest. “I want your word, Butcher.”

“Asshole prima donna bitch baby,” Butcher repeats under his breath. “That’s not bad, Hughie. Where’d you come up with that one?”

“He was writing you a letter when we heard you posted bail,” Annie informs him.

“You heard I did?” he asks, looking at her. “Thought it was you lot what paid it.”

“Oh, hell nah,” MM tells him.

“What is this, why are you looking at me?” Frenchie says, cross-like, hands on his hips like a schoolmarm. “Because I am a collector of emergency evacuation funds?”

Butcher puts his own hands on his hips, arches an eyebrow.

“No longer,” he snaps, distracted briefly from pointing at Butcher’s face by Kimiko catching his hand to hold. “Our money is set aside for more innocent forms of escape now.”

“Christ, fine, don’t get your panties in a twist.”

Ryan snorts, and Butcher, encouraged by his tentative smile, drops his hand in his hair.

“Don’t suppose you know who busted me out?”

“Nope. I would’ve, but I don’t have any money.”

“It’s the thought that counts,” Butcher tells him, generously, and then, altogether bitchier, “boys.”

Laughing, MM says, “C’mon, prima donna. Let’s take you home.”

“Prima donna bitch baby,” Frenchie corrects, dainty in how he does it.

Talking over the slamming of the bus doors, Annie says, “Asshole prima donna bitch baby.”

“All right, I got it!”

The small indignity of the gang’s ribbings are worth the tiny laugh they get out of Ryan. Butcher leans into it the whole ride home, grateful they can talk about that and not Insight’s cheeky Hallmark card. Or the rest of it, Jesus Christ. He’s healed up since his ass kicking, courtesy of Soldier Boy, and that’s the extent of what he wants to say about it. Nobody but him needs to know the things that transpired down there. They happened somewhere else, and that place, that pocket of time, don’t exist anymore. That’s all that needs to be said about it, and it’s all he wants to believe about it, besides. It’s not only him that needs to believe that.

“Have you talked to my dad since he went away?” Ryan asks him in a quiet voice, hard to hear over the low rumble of the radio and the rush of traffic through the open windows. 

“Nah.” Butcher drapes an arm around Ryan’s hunched shoulders, hoping maybe it’ll help to steady him. “Have you?” When Ryan’s only answer is to slump in his seat, Butcher nudges him and asks, “Did you want to?”

On that front, Ryan doesn’t have an answer prepared, and Butcher don’t press him for one. He’s heard from Hugh — Junior and Senior — that John did the bare minimum, if that, to curb his opinions of Becca and Mallory around Ryan, even at the expense of hurting Ryan’s feelings. Butcher ain’t got it in him to make the same mistake if it’ll cause Ryan any kinda pain. He set out to leave him worse than when he found him once already, and forcing himself to walk away that time nearly killed him. It would’ve, if the brain death hadn’t been gunning for him so much harder at the time.

Not that there’s really any point in pussyfooting around the truth of Homelander anymore. John told on himself back in Kentucky, way back before the news coverage kicked in, and Ryan listened. He hasn’t stopped listening. Butcher’s pretty sure he got that from his mum cuz there’s no way he got it from John.

“I miss him,” Ryan says, low, in the tone of a confession. “Do you ever? I know it was all…twisted up and tangled for you, because of Mom. But, was it ever not?”

Setting a hand in Ryan’s hair, just to hold onto him, Butcher thinks about his reply for a few long seconds before he says it out loud, and he doesn’t change a word of it from his initial knee-jerk response: “It was always complicated between me ’n your dad.”

Ryan watches him, applying every bit as much concentration to his hearing of the answer as Butcher did for the crafting of it. It settles something on his face, whatever he gleans from it, and the resolve there, how it gives Ryan peace he’s so clearly been grasping after, feels right when stacked up against Butcher’s idea of the truth. 

Because, sure, he wanted John so he could have a better shot at destroying him, but there was a single insane moment in time there that he wanted him just to want him, and for all that he can say it, now, the thing between them was never anything but exactly what it had to be. It stayed twisty-turny for them, whether Butcher was fucking John to fuck him over, or just to fuck him.

“Do you think it’s bad if I want to see him?”

“He’s your dad, Ryan. You love him.”

“Yeah, but.” Ryan sits up, dislodging Butcher’s hand from the top of his head, though he doesn’t shrug him off in any other capacity. His eyes are serious, grownup, and at stark odds with the baby fat clinging to his splotchy cheeks. “I love Mom, too, and I— um.”

Butcher settles his arm across Ryan’s shoulders, and he eases back into it. Maybe he can tell the difference in Butcher’s diminished strength, in his lessened durability, but he doesn’t comment on it. He just leans back into it, grateful, and still, all the while, that spark in his eyes stays put, fixed on Butcher like a star anchored in the map of the sky.

“Me, too,” Butcher tells him, soft. And then, softer, “But he’s your dad.”

And I’m not, he thinks but can’t make himself say, though he can see it catch in Ryan’s ears.

“Mom…didn’t pick him,” Ryan persists, obstinate in that way he gets sometimes. Childish, if it weren’t for the simple fact that he’s right and he knows it. “She picked you, and— and she picked me, a-and now we can choose to pick each other, so I’m picking you.”

He thought he’d have time before Ryan came at him again with this. He thought he would’ve changed his mind, what with everything floating around in the media about the tapes and the fucked up chain of buggery linking Homelander not just to a fractured marriage ultimately dissolved in rape and death, but to eventually fucking his victim’s surviving spouse. Maybe if Ryan had had options, or if John weren’t the standard to beat, he’d be less forgiving, Butcher doesn’t know.

“I ain’t earned that from you,” Butcher tells him, in a final frantic bid to sway Ryan some other way. To save them both from himself, from that life they maybe might’ve had once if Becca really had chosen him like Ryan thinks she did.

“Well, start,” Ryan says, implacable. On the brink of eleven years old, and a leader of men. Wouldn’t his mum be proud of him now if she could see. “You said you’d be better, so do it. What are you waiting for?”

He’s got a point, and as much as Butcher don’t have it in him to tell Ryan for fear that he’d hear it the wrong way, it’s his dad talking through him. From the sentiment right down to the words, nearly verbatim: You want to come with me. What’s stopping you? What could ever stop you? He thinks he will tell Ryan about it someday, if only so he can know that the unstoppable and unquenchable in John found their home in Ryan as softer virtues— as honor and care for others. That reminds him of John, too. Reminds him of his idea about mirrors, dark and bright. Ryan was John’s, or he could’ve been. Now Butcher has the distinct feeling that privilege has shifted over to him.

“Guess I was waiting for you,” he admits.

Ryan stares at him for a second, obviously waiting for something further that Butcher’s blanking on, at least until Hughie twists halfway in his seat.

In a stage whisper, out the side of his mouth, he says, “He wants your word, man.”

It hits Butcher then that the whole bus is sat waiting on his answer. He flicks Hughie’s ear to get him to face front and mind his own business. Same for the rest of them.

“Well, seein’ as I ain’t in the business of lettin’ you down no more,” Butcher says, unwinding his arm from around Ryan’s shoulders to offer his hand instead. “And since my word still means summat to you even after all we’ve been through, you can have it.” He knows the rest of the bus is still listening, so he adds, “On my honor as a scoundrel.”

That gets the snickers out of everyone he was counting on. Even Ryan laughs.

“You can count on me,” Butcher adds, just for Ryan’s ears. “All right?”

Ryan shakes his hand. “Then you can count on me, too.”

Butcher likes the sound of that.

“That’s legal and binding, just so you know,” Hughie says.

“Keep running your mouth, Hughie. I’ll come up there and put you in a headlock.”

Kimiko signs something to Frenchie, the hand sign matching up with the too-soft look on her face. Butcher points at her and then at Frenchie.

“Quit saying sweet things about me.”

The smallest tinge of pink crosses the bridge of Kimiko’s nose. She signs the same exact thing, but more forcefully, bearing down. Her eyebrows are doing the equivalent of parroting his words back to him in a mocking tone. Frenchie signs to her without stopping to tell Butcher he jumped to the wrong conclusion. Hughie does that.

“She said, Oh, no, not a headlock.” He copies the one sign integral to parsing the meaning. “A sexy headlock?”

“A sensuous headlock,” Frenchie corrects, but too late to stop Ryan’s laughing, or MM’s.

It’s easy, then, to let the boys take over. To sit back while Hughie gets dressed down half in sign and half in spoken English, to watch Ryan and Annie join in on the fun, and to catch MM’s eyes in the rearview mirror. Just for a second. Just long enough for a beat to hold and pass between them, and that’s all the time Butcher needs to ask in their own unspoken language: All right?

Yeah, he reads in MM’s nod. We got you.

Butcher’s lucky to have made it this far with one crew, even beyond the point that his purpose has been met. Maybe that’s optimistic of him, to think it ends here and now, with Homelander locked up in a cage fit to hold him— his cell, yeah, sure. But his body, too. The one he’s got since Soldier Boy took the other off him and left it on the cutting room floor, right there next to Butcher’s. And that’s a fitting end to the story, it is. That an integral part of who they were died there, swallowed first by a hole in the earth and then by a blackhole of a man, every bit John’s father, even if he never knew it.

He’d’ve been all right with that ending, if his powers had been all he left down there, but he can’t think about that right now. He can’t think about the end of the world now that his life’s trying to start.

The lads drive him to his flat, where all he wants to do is grab Terror and take him for one of those walks that ends with both of them dragging their feet. Ryan de-boards with him, and even in spite of knowing he signed on for that, a spike of white-hot panic unspools behind his chest. Is it dinnertime soon? He can’t remember the last time he had food in the fridge, even before he landed in the pen. He can’t remember the last meal he cooked what didn’t come right outta the freezer and end in the microwave.

“Butcher, are you coming?” Ryan asks, tugging on his hand but without any real force.

“Yeah, monkey. You go on up, I’ll be right behind you.”

Ryan shrugs, waves goodbye to the lads, and heads for the main entrance to the building. It warms something over in him that he doesn’t go for the window like his dad. Maybe that kinda thing still strikes him as impolite.

Hughie cranks the window open and pokes his head out, cheek pillowed on one arm to look at Butcher while he quietly freaks out.

“Uh, dude?”

“I’m in over my fuckin’ head here, Hughie.”

Whatever petty satisfaction a lesser man might gain hearing that, nothing close shows on Hughie’s face. He just looks steady, in that way of his. Like he’s casting his memory back and filling in the gaping silence between what Butcher can own in words and what he’s sure is rising up like a tidal wave to knock him down for good. And because it’s Hughie, because he can read these kinda silences better than just about anyone Butcher’s ever met— even including the coupla telepaths he’s known— he figures it out in a second. Less than.

“She’d be proud of you, Butcher, and if you can’t believe that yet, then take Ryan’s advice. Start.”

“I’ll fuck it up. I will, and he’ll end up like me.”

“Butcher, today? That’s not a bad thing. Today you’re kinda my hero.” Hughie rolls his eyes. “Even though you had weird role-play sex about my dad and it almost got you and him killed.”

The window next to Hughie’s slots down, and Kimiko holds her phone out the window.

 

Every day we can be better because people love us and believe in us. He loves you and believes in you. Just give him the same in return. It’ll be like you said. You’ll be better. You’ll earn it.

Legal and binding, remember?

 

He can’t find it in himself to blow her off. He made a promise today to Ryan, and years before he was born, he made a promise to Becca, too. It’s time he followed through, but still.

“I might be sick.”

“Not on my bus you will not!” MM yells from behind the wheel.

“Butcher, you’re gonna be fine,” Annie says, rolling her window down to peer out at Butcher.“Mallory’s coming by later tonight to pick Ryan up. They made plans to go camping this weekend. You’ve just gotta get through this afternoon.”

“Well, fuck’s sake, Hughie, why didn’t you lead with that!”

“Because the rest of it’s true,” he says, easy as anything. “And you needed to hear it. Now will you go upstairs? My dad’s gonna be pacing the length of the apartment if you stay down here any longer.”

Butcher drops his hands to his sides. He doesn’t know what his face is doing, but Hughie has the audacity to smile at him.

“Asshole,” he says, and reaches outside the bus to thump his hand on the metal.

MM pulls the big beast of an automobile away from the curb and off they go cruising down the street. Butcher watches them go, statue still and internally spinning his wheels. He hadn’t let himself consider it. Hadn’t wanted to ask, not even as a joke, what Hugh’s take on his extracurriculars has been. There hadn’t been time in between the news breaking and the cuffs hitting his wrists to make any crack at directing the fallout. Everything had happened too fast, and what would he have said anyway? It was their only chance. Even knowing he had a dream in mind for what his life could be after the dust cleared, he wouldn’t go back and undo what he did. The end result, this end result, was too important. It always was too important to allow for anything else.

All that means now is that he’s gotta face the music. That’s how it is. You do the wrong thing, you get punished, and maybe he can’t see what he did as being wrong just now, but Hugh ain’t like him. He’d be right for looking at Butcher and seeing an animal. He’d be normal. He’d be soft but uncompromising and easy to love, and Butcher would feel it like a stone in his heart how badly he didn’t want to disappoint him. He does feel it like a stone in his heart.

Nothing for it now but to take his medicine. Hugh’s been kind to him in the past. He’ll be kind about it now if he can’t stand the sight of Butcher, knowing what he did to someone that trusted him. Even if that someone was annihilation packaged up tightly into the shadow of a man.

Butcher takes the elevator to his floor. No sooner than the lift doors have opened, he hears a jangling collar, and there’s Terror trotting over to him. Butcher takes a knee to catch him in the middle of jumping and roughhouses with him just outside the path of the closing doors. He looks back the way Terror had come, sees the door sitting open. Ryan leans outside to get eyes on him. There’s a skinny box of something in his one hand that looks suspiciously like pasta.

“C’mon!” He calls, already disappearing back into the flat. “We’re doing the pantry!”

Catching Terror’s face in his hands, Butcher asks him, very seriously, “Doin’ the what?”

Terror licks him, obviously conveying that he don’t have the slightest idea what’s happening here neither. The two of them make their way to the open door, and once Terror gathers from Butcher’s stride that that’s where they’re headed, he trots out ahead confidently, stubby tail wagging. Butcher steps inside the flat and shuts the door behind him, looking twice at the state of affairs he walks in on.

It looks—

Well, the same. A little bit shit, a little bit devoid of any kind of fucking personal flourishes, and yet there’s something warm about it. The windows are open, maybe that’s it, or it could be the groceries littering the floor and the countertops. There’s even a few smaller bags on the table, and Butcher can’t guess at what they are or if they’re even from the same store. Everything’s in its own markedly different but equally loud reusable shopping bag, and every one of them is packed to the brim with stuff. It looks—

Oddly, like a place that could be somebody’s home.

“Oh, there you are!” Hugh says, with a box of tea in his hands. Because of course that’s what he’s holding when he gets eyes on Butcher for the first time since his arrest. “We’ve got a lot left to unpack, but then we can get started on dinner. What are you in the mood for?”
