Butcher stares, and Terror, helpfully, goes and sits on Hugh’s foot, looking back at Butcher with the same calm, expectant expression on his face that Hugh’s got.

“Butcher, are you okay?”

“Huh? No, yeah, I’m good.”

Seeing something in Butcher’s face that Ryan’s not equipped to understand, Hugh roots around for one of the bags on the floor and offers it to Ryan. “Kiddo, would you mind taking these to the linen closet?”

“Oh, sure!” Ryan carries the heavy laden bag out of the kitchen carefully with both hands, like he knows, even now, that all the strength in the world ain’t a guarantee of safety, or of handling a thing with the care it deserves.

“Thank you, Ryan.”

“No problem, Mr. Campbell!”

Hugh sets the box of tea on the counter and then he sets his hands there, too, leaning back against the formica. “I can go,” he says, being cautious with his voice in the same way Ryan was being with his hands. “I don’t want to crowd you. You’ve been in lockup all the past week, so—”

“Yeah.” When he sees the way hearing that locks Hugh up by degrees, he realizes how it sounded coming out and fumbles. “No, I meant—”

“It’s okay.”

“No, I ain’t saying go. I’m saying…” He shakes his head, at a loss. “I dunno why you wouldn’t.”

“Well, I don’t want to,” he says, plainly and matter-of-factly, like he’s reading off the box of tea he left on Butcher’s counter and not reading him an easy truth right out of his heart. Nothing guarded or deceptive about it. “I will if you ask me to. Of course I will, but I hope you won’t do that just on my account.”

“But you know what I did.”

Hesitating, Hugh eventually has to nod, ceding the point. He doesn’t offer anything conciliatory in response. In his brief silence, Butcher tries to suss out the condemnations he’s been waiting to find. He’s surprised when he can’t.

Taking his time with the words so they seem to be walking out of him in single file, one foot after the other, Hugh says, “I don’t like that you did it, even if it was to him. If you need me to have an opinion about it, I’ll say that. I’m sorry it happened. I’m sorry that was the only way, but…Billy, I’m sorrier that it had to be you, and that it had to be Becca. None of the people he hurt—” Here he falters, but just for a second. “None of them deserved the pain he caused, but out of all of them, you were the one to do something about it. You were the one to make it right, even if it had to be in a way that not everyone will understand.”

“Do you understand it?”

“I do.” He nods grimly, swallowing hard and planting himself just where he is. Going nowhere. Quieter, he asks, “Are you okay?”

He is. He is, but—

But the question has his eyes stinging, for some reason. Or maybe for one reason he can think of. Because he didn’t expect this. He didn’t expect open arms or fucking groceries or his wife’s son carefully and discreetly stocking the linen closet as if it’s just one of his daily chores.

Hugh crosses over to him, a tried and true move of his when Butcher’s playing the idiot and doesn’t anticipate his kindness. He crosses over, and he folds Butcher up into a hug.

Like he deserves it. Like it gives Hugh something— anything at all— to be gentle with him.

Butcher fists his hand in the back of his shirt, no danger to it now. Not with his hands back to being his and only his, excepting the times, maybe, when Hugh might want to requisition them for a lark. Or a handjob, whatever.

“I told you, didn’t I?” Hugh tells him, all warmth to match the light he brought into Butcher’s squat, barren space. “That it would all work out?”

“Guess you did,” Butcher allows, and fuck, but he wants to kiss him. Wants to put his mouth elsewhere, everywhere, just as soon as Hugh will let him. Just as soon as they have a second to themselves. He splits the difference and kisses the edge of Hugh’s mouth, dizzy and amazed at the smile that’s waiting for him. “You gotta tell me how you do that.”

“Do what?”

He bites his lip and risks saying, “Make me want you so fucking bad.”

Hugh looks away to laugh, the sound bright and full of promise in the sunlit kitchen, even in spite of the faintest hint of yellowed bruises falling down his throat. “It’s been a long time since anybody let me take care of them. You seem to like it, though.”

It’s Butcher’s turn to laugh, hearing that. He glances around at the many grocery bags surrounding them, catches sight of Terror with his sloth rope toy, and holds his hand out for the first part of Hugh he can grab. Turns out to be his side, unhurt now if his lack of flinch is any indicator. Hugh leans in close, at first Butcher thinks to kiss him, but no, he presses their foreheads together. For one strained moment, Butcher can’t breathe, and then Hugh’s hand slips around his neck, soft but standing his ground.

“I think sometimes you still don’t really understand what you’ve done for me,” Hugh says, honey and chamomile on his breath, like before. “And I know you don’t expect anything for it, but you helped my boy find a life he loves. You kept him safe when I couldn’t. I know you don’t want repayment—”

“Goddamn right I don’t,” Butcher mumbles, bunching his fingers up in Hugh’s shirt. He’s just about had his fill of fucking tit-for-tat quid pro quo double meanings and ulterior motive bullshit. He’s distracted from spiraling off on that tangent by Hugh’s hand layering over top of his.

“But it would make me very happy to try and give you a little peace of mind if I can.”

Butcher gives him a look that’s equal parts skeptical and droll. “Peace of mind, that’s what you wanna give me?”

“Mmhmm,” Hugh tells him, pretty fucking articulately.

Butcher’s more than three-quarters of the way towards hitting on him when Hugh cups his cheek in his hand. Maybe he can feel the reorientation of blood moving away from his heart and up to his face. Can probably see it, if nothing else.

“I know you’re not free and clear yet. You still have your trial coming up, and maybe prison time after that, but I want to be here with you. I want that, if you want it, too.”

“I do.”

Hugh flashes a smile, that rare, full one that reminds Butcher how lucky he is to have this chance again. To maybe be loved by someone better than him, who looks at him and sees a good man. Or at the very least, the makings of one.

“Which brings me back to my question — what do you feel like having for dinner?”

“Can we make lasagna?” Ryan asks, swanning back into the kitchen with the empty bag looped over his shoulder. He sets right about putting groceries away again, seeing that Hugh and Butcher ain’t made any progress. Business as usual. He don’t look like he’s been eavesdropping, but he wouldn’t have needed to, as close as Hugh and Butcher are standing. “There’s all the stuff to make it.”

“What do you think, Billy? Lasagna?”

“Suits me.”

Butcher sets about putting the rest of the groceries away. Half of it’s stuff he hasn’t kept in the flat for years: orange juice, a head of iceberg lettuce, flour, bandaids. They’ve got ingredients to make Becca’s chocolate chip cookies, and Ryan realizes as much at the same exact time that Butcher does, holding a carton of eggs while Butcher’s got a plump paper bag of sugar balanced in his hand.

“Can we make cookies after? Mom used to make ‘em with—”

“Brown sugar,” Butcher finishes, to another smile he hopes he can be worthy of one day.

So that’s the plan, that’s the afternoon. Puttering around the kitchen with Ryan and Hugh, mostly getting in their way if he’s honest, but they put up with his two left feet and get Ryan’s lasagna in the oven. Then they get started on Becca’s cookies.

“Ah-ah,” he says, intervening when Hugh starts to add a second egg into the bowl.

“What?”

“You have to mix them in one at a time,” Ryan informs him.

“Make way for the pros, Hugh,” Butcher teases, like he ain’t been getting underfoot himself.

Playing along, Hugh smiles and gives them room to work. It’s fun, actually, this one part he can do. This one part he don’t have to worry about falling short of what Becca would be able to do if she were here, if she could parent Ryan like she’d done herself all those years, alone. Ryan makes it easy on him, and of the two of them, it should be laughable that it’s Ryan who’s gotta have kid gloves on to give this any shot at working, but it makes sense. He’s had practice doing that with John after all, and Butcher don’t have it in him to aim disparaging thoughts in his direction. Ain’t like he’s doing much better, all told.

But they do get through dinner, and then they dig into the cookies, and Ryan tells them both about his day. Butcher catches Hugh’s eyes a few times over the course of Ryan’s summary of the dog park with Terror that morning, and the near-sly look there clearly telegraphs, Look at you, brought in from out of the cold. Having dinner with your kid. It occurs to Butcher, as he’s licking melted chocolate off his fingers, that he’s gonna suck Hugh’s dick tonight. Minimum.

Mallory swings by in the next hour as they’re winding down and cleaning up. Apparently they’re going up to the Catskills. Ryan had a bag packed up and ready in Butcher’s room and everything.

“So you didn’t set this up just to get him out of my capable hands for the weekend?” Butcher asks Grace over Ryan’s head while he’s having his goodbye ’n goodnight hug.

“You think too much of yourself,” she says, as dryly as ever.

“I’m good for some things. Tell her, Ryan.”

“He didn’t burn the chocolate chip cookies,” Ryan answers solemnly. “I saved some for the trip.”

“How can I argue with that?” she muses, smiling with her eyes and her voice. Cuz it’s Ryan, and she’s under the same spell anyone who knows him longer than a minute falls victim to.

“Butcher, you won’t be in jail again before I get back, will you?”

“Nah, love. Legal system don’t work that fast.”

Ryan looks at him steadily for one second, two, then past him at Hugh, who says gamely, “I’ll make sure he doesn’t knock over a bank.”

“You’ll make sure he eats and leaves the apartment sometimes?”

“Oy, I’m standin’ right here.”

“To the best of my ability,” Hugh promises.

Butcher loops his arm around Hugh’s neck, effectively shushing him without outright doing it. “Have a nice time with Aunt Grace, monkey, all right?”

“I will. You guys have fun, too.”

Mallory opens the door to her shiny SUV for him to get in, gives the pair of them a cursory once-over, her expression perfectly unreadable, until she cracks a smile. “It’s good to see your choice in partners is a little more like what it used to be, Billy. Gentlemen.”

“Grace,” Butcher replies.

“Have a safe trip, ma’am.”

They watch the SUV drive off down the street and disappear around the corner, and that warm anticipation from before stirs low in Butcher’s belly. Nothing anxious or uncertain about it, just the wanting that comes of not knowing yet how far a thing will be allowed to go. The kind what comes of thinking all of this could still go either way, that Hugh could still walk out on him, and Butcher would have to let him.

“Stay the night, Hugh,” he says. 

“You don’t think we’re moving too fast?” Hugh asks, nakedly, genuinely worried about Butcher’s answer.

He hooks a finger in Hugh’s collar, walks backward so his shoulders hit the wall of his building and Hugh’s got nowhere to go but up close against him. Plastered to his front, wide-eyed and warm and wondering. Maybe thinking the same thing Butcher is, that this isn’t guaranteed. That Butcher could change his mind, and Hugh would have to let him.

“You wined me, you dined me. You gave me a head-start putting my life back together.” Butcher leans down to suck a kiss into Hugh’s throat and then another one beneath his jaw. “We don’t gotta do anything you don’t want, but Hugh, I’ve been thinkin’ about it all night.”

“So have I,” he murmurs, a hand closing softly against Butcher’s scalp, tangling in his hair. “Oh, but I don’t want to rush you.”

“You won’t,” Butcher tells him, nearly kissing him already.

It’s Hugh that closes the distance. Hugh that crowds Butcher against the brick and sinks into him, a comfortable, unassuming weight. He tastes molasses sweet like Becca’s cookies, and he feels like coming home.

“But maybe you got the right idea,” Butcher murmurs. “Maybe we shouldn’t.”

Hugh’s hands gripping him loosen up almost immediately, though he don’t go nowhere. Not like he can, what with Butcher latched to his neck lapping him up like he’s ice cream. “Okay,” Hugh says, hazy just like Butcher’s hazy, even if Butcher can tell just by his voice that he’s smiling.

“Tonight’s been nice, y’know,” Butcher adds, sucking a mark beneath Hugh’s collar.

“It has,” Hugh says on a sigh, laughing at little. He palms at Butcher’s ribs, neither to push him away or to reel him in.

“And, uh.”

“What?” Leaning away now, because for some reason it genuinely matters to him the things Butcher says, or starts to say. His expression goes from searching to softly comprehending, brightening at the same moment that his tone becomes so soft and so fond that Butcher’s chest aches at the implications. “Billy Butcher,” he muses, the corners of his eyes creasing, “doesn’t fuck on the first date.”

Butcher narrows his eyes, half playing. But only half. “Say that again.”

“Billy Butcher,” Hugh repeats gamely, beaming and sighing a laugh around Butcher’s name like it means something or someone else. “Doesn’t fmph—”

Shutting him up with a belligerent kiss, Butcher says, “How dare you!”

Giggly bastards, the two of them, draped against the doorway to Butcher’s building and necking like there’s no tomorrow. Except there is a tomorrow, and contrary to a great majority of his life up to now, he finds he’s looking forward to it. He’s looking forward to when Ryan gets back from his weekend with Grace; he’s looking forward to whatever fresh hell they’re up against next from Vought and Neumann and Soldier Boy; hell, he’s even looking forward to the impending circus of his trial, and John’s. But, most immediately, he’s looking forward to tonight.

And Hugh, really, he’s being a good sport about Butcher ravishing him within an inch of his life outside his building. It’s just not the whole of what he wants outta their first night properly alone together.

“Hugh,” he says. “Come upstairs with me?”

He feels Hugh’s nod before he hears his answer. A soft, barely vocal, “Yeah. Okay.”

Butcher has a frantic, kaleidoscopic picture in his head of what he wants to happen next: myriad touches and kisses he’s been dreaming of for weeks now. What he gets instead of flinging Hugh onto the bed and ripping his clothes off the first chance he gets is Terror pacing near the door, his standard tell for when he’s gotta go out.

“Ah, son, now?”

Hugh snorts. “Do you want any company taking him?”

“No, I got him. Do me a favor and be wearin’ less of these when I get back?”

Blushing, Hugh sinks his hands into his pockets and hunches his shoulders a bit. Nervous-like, and that won’t do. Butcher gets Terror’s leash on, detours to give Hugh a kiss, a peck, giving some comfort, he hopes, and ducks out into the hall.

“We gotta work on your timing, lad.”

Boof, says Terror.

Schemer.

Terror makes him walk a few blocks before he’ll do the damned thing. Butcher brings him back up, less urgent for fucking than he’d been five minutes ago, but maybe that ain’t such a bad thing.

Back in the flat, he gets Terror situated with his sloth toy in his little dog bed and follows the music to his bedroom.

“Not bad,” he says, leaning in against the doorjamb.

Hugh looks up from the study he’d been making of Butcher’s limited library of books and the odd CD or cassette tape. Setting down the book he’d been inspecting, Hugh presses a button on the stereo to change the song. “Don’t laugh,” he says, though he’s got the makings of a smile on his own face.

“Why would I?”

“Well, because I was wondering,” Hugh murmurs, holding out his hand. “If you’d let me have this dance.”

 

I only want to love you
Please don’t push me away

 

Butcher feels himself starting to smile in response, and he does want to laugh, but not for the reason he thinks Hugh’s probably worried about. He lets Hugh reel him in close, doesn’t think too hard about the last time he danced with Becca like this. Doesn’t let himself think about that goddamned party at all. It’s got no business here in this room now, except to help him keep off Hugh’s toes, and that’s easy enough. They’re just swaying, slow and warm and easy.

“I don’t count this as a date, by the way.”

Now Butcher does laugh. He gives Hugh a little twirl in retaliation, and when he’s got Hugh good and close, he keeps him there. Just to look at him, just to find that brown fleck in his eye.

“What?” Hugh asks softly, even as the song changes into something a bit faster.

Butcher figures that’s his cue from Mr. Withers to go in for the kiss, so he does, and Hugh, beauty that he is, goes soft and sweet in his arms. The kaleidoscope of all the things Butcher wants to happen tonight narrows down to one pretty immediate thing, and there go his hands at Hugh’s belt.

“You’re so beautiful, Billy.”

“Yeah, you, too,” he says, voice gone thready with need. With vulnerability.

Without Butcher inadvertently holding him down, Hugh pushes up on his hands to sit up, to hold Butcher close to him, to lick into his mouth. The salt of his skin and the sweetness of his mouth and the scrabbling of his hands at Butcher’s skin get him so goddamn hot. Hugh’s voice goes high at the end like a song, and Butcher can’t fucking get enough.

Around a delicate cry, he says, “What, Billy?” into his mouth.

“Feeling like I want you to hold me down.”

“I’ll be lifting weights a little while longer before I can manage that,” he says with a laugh, grunting when Butcher pulls off him to go down to his belly.

“I’ll make it easy on you.” And then, with a coy look over his shoulder, he adds, “Just this once.”

Hugh comes in close behind him to lean down and kiss Butcher first on the temple, and then his cheek, and then the back of his neck. He gathers Butcher’s hands behind his back, held by his wrists in a big hot hand and sparking a need down Butcher’s spine. With his other hand, he sinks his dick back inside Butcher, a deeper plunge than they’d been able to manage before.

“Like that?”

“Yeah, Hugh, like that. Like that, darlin’.”

“You’re so lovely, Billy,” he says, sounding drunk. “You’re so wonderful.”

Butcher tries to touch himself, not remembering in his rising desperation that his hands are pinioned behind his back, and groans, frantic, and helplessly turned on, when Hugh doesn’t release him.

“Billy,” Hugh whimpers, hips jerking, hands tightening around Butcher’s wrists. “Oh! Oh, Billy.”

He slows, grip slackening on the manacle of Butcher’s wrists, and that’s all he needs to pull one hand free and bring himself off. He’s so close, it’s not gonna take much. Hugh pulls out of him and pulls him gently onto his side, arms wrapped around him from behind and lips beneath his ear. His hand drifts down to take over for Butcher, tugging at his dick. Leisurely, forcing Butcher to fuck into his hand.

“You make me feel so good,” Hugh murmurs, sealing his lips around a spot on Butcher’s neck that always drives him crazy. “I want you to feel good, too.”

“I do, I do, I—” Butcher breaks off in a sharp cry at Hugh’s hand moving faster, his grip tightening. “Oh, my God, Hugh, I’m right there, I’m—”

He spills over Hugh’s knuckles, bright and intense and knocking him down.

Lucky him he’s got Hugh wrapped tight around him.

With a low chuckle, Hugh says, “Told you I did my research.”

Butcher presses his hand to his face and laughs with him. He can’t believe this is his life. He can’t believe he gets to have this and that he doesn’t have to worry it’ll be snatched away from him. Not tonight, anyway. Not by Soldier Boy or Homelander or anyone else.

Bill Withers croons around the mutual noise of their breathing and the rustling of linens. To get out of the wet spot, Butcher kicks the covers off until it’s just him and Hugh and the sheets, miles of bare, cooling skin and swollen red lips Butcher can’t not be kissing just now. He smooths his palm over the racing pulse in Hugh’s neck, amazed that he gets to do this. Amazed that sex could still be about this, about fucking someone not just to get off but to feel close to them. To feel held.

“You were right,” Butcher tells him.

“About what?” he asks softly, that brown fleck in his eye drawing Butcher’s gaze, like always.

“This,” he murmurs, sneaking a leg between Hugh’s, just to add another layer of touch. “That it shouldn’t be complicated.”

Hugh smiles, and even laying here in the dark with Butcher, he’s made of light. Butcher’s struck by the hope that he gets to learn more of where it comes from and how deep it runs. It thrills him to think they’ve got time for it now.

“I was thinking,” Hugh says, nothing in his tone to suggest that he has any idea Butcher’s entertaining such tender thoughts about him. “It’s a three hour drive to Maryland. Maybe we could get up there for the weekend. Excellent seafood, salty ocean air. Change of scenery, without anyone after us. It might be nice.”

“And you get to take me on that date,” Butcher croons.

Laughing, Hugh kisses him, and he doesn’t deny it. And Butcher, well.

“Yeah, Hugh, I’d like that.”

Cuz he really, really would, is the thing. It’s a hell of a beautiful feeling.

 

/

 

Homelander’s transfer to a special facility upstate comes with court-ordered therapy twice a week and three square meals a day. Patients in the neighboring rooms on his ward finger paint and watch Doctor Oz in onesie smocks with faded slippers, regardless of where the hands fall on the clock face. If they’re simple and not much for conversation, well, Homelander isn’t in much of a position to judge at present. The only difference between him and them is the relative newness of his slippers, and that, he knows, will change.

He raged in the beginning. Raged at the audacity of men to question him, to judge him, to inflict their idea of justice on him as if he were anyone else. 

That fire in him didn’t last beyond the first jailhouse fight, and when he really thinks about it, all the great failures of his life could be chalked up to fights he picked and lost, and he can’t think why. He was Earth’s strongest, her shining, golden son, and the world, likewise, should have been his to take back. His birthright. He couldn’t even win his case in court, much less secure a mistrial, but they did agree with him on one front. They let him claim insanity. And though they deemed him criminally responsible in the eyes of the law, they’ve had him sectioned here instead of general population, and while that grates some, he has to admit he wouldn’t have lasted long in a regular prison.

Oh, the Aryan Nation would have snatched him up, certainly. He could’ve survived with them. Perhaps they would’ve even found a way to smuggle in a vial of V to break him out someday, or perhaps they would’ve just passed him around in a reenactment of the sex tapes people on the other side of the planet will have seen by now. Perhaps these are fairytales he tells himself.

They’re all that live in his head now. Mirrorlander and Stormfront and even Becca Butcher have left him, leaving him one final companion. Dad.

When he speaks, Homelander can’t find it in himself to respond. Luckily, on the rare occasion he sees Dad out of the corner of his eye, he doesn’t have much to say. Or if he does, it never feels quite like he’s talking to Homelander, or like he gives a shit if he’s listening. Nothing new there.

An hour after their appointed invalid dinner, Homelander’s escorted by staff to his mandatory therapy appointment. It’ll be the first of many, though he has met the man in question before. There was a brief moment at his trial. Both sides had presented expert witnesses to review his psych evaluation. The doctor brought by his attorneys was an imbecile, but the doctor called by the prosecution to testify had been another story altogether; that was Dr. Muhammed Sayeed Kamara. That is the man waiting to meet Homelander for therapy, backlit and golden with the late afternoon sunlight streaming in through the big south-facing windows.

“Hello, John,” he says, holding out his hand. “We almost met at the trial, I’m—”

“I know who you are,” he mutters waspishly, bypassing the offered handshake to drop heavily into the open seat across from the desk.

Kamara pockets his hands and nods at the attending staff to give them the room.

“I’m sorry, I should’ve asked. Do you prefer to be called Homelander?”

Kamara has the physique of a professional athlete. Big hands, narrow waist, and a pugilistic lilt to his shoulders that says he knows how to take a punch as well as give one. He’d been impressive in court, ruthless and concise, poised, stoic. A blank space. And his shoes. Ferragamo? He’s no overworked civil servant.

Sitting across from him now, Homelander refuses to feel cowed. It’s an uphill battle. One more confrontation that’s sure to end in failure for him. So, he ignores him.

“I hope you’ll forgive the assumption on my part, but it is your name.”

Homelander grits his teeth together, eyes carefully averted. Kamara, while feigning ignorance, presses down upon the blister.

“It was your official request to have it legally changed,” Kamara continues, leaning back against his desk rather than claiming the seat behind it. It puts him over Homelander, at enough of a distance so as to be polite. Even cordial. “Now, obviously, the motivations behind your choice in nomenclature, as regards your son, are fairly easy to deduce. In the event that you don’t intend to answer to the name John Butcher, however, let’s get that out of the way now. What do you say?”

Several acerbic— and admittedly juvenile— rejoinders fly in and out of his head. There’s nothing he can say to leverage this man. Nothing he can offer that will even up the imbalance between them. To choose Homelander would reveal his weakness as readily as fire, but to choose John would be its own kind of surrender.

Ultimately, it’s what decides him. Because it isn’t just himself he gives away if he shirks John now. It’s Ryan.

“John is fine,” he mumbles.

A beat passes, and then Kamara is holding his hand out to him again. Slower, more deliberate in the gesture this time around. His steady gaze and serene smile come with the same caliber of focus. Homelander waits to feel condescended to. Waits for the other shoe to drop, and keeps waiting. Kamara shakes his hand, a straightforward and undemanding, yet uncompromising, exchange.

“Muhammed Kamara,” he says, releasing Homelander’s hand. “I won’t be offended if you call me by my first name. Dr. Kamara is also acceptable.”

Homelander drops his gaze to his lap together with his hands.

“How are you adjusting to your stay here, John?”

He feels his lip curl and his eyes wanting to roll. A muscle in his neck twitches.

“That bad?”

“I don’t belong here,” Homelander whispers.

“You belong in a maximum security facility, which is where you are,” Kamara returns, neatly and gently, like he’s speaking to a child and not a man who was a god. “Your preliminary psych eval says you present with features of schizoaffective disorder. That’s the only reason you’re in an institution rather than a penitentiary. For your own safety, as well as the safety of others.”

When the silence between them stretches, Kamara places the heels of his hands on either side of himself, cupping the edges of the desk. He asks, “Do you want to talk about the decisions you made that put you here?”

“No, I don’t.” Homelander folds his arms across his chest, not liking how small the question makes him feel. Why that is, when this should be a topic of confidence for him, he can’t place.

“Are you proud of the things you’ve done?”

Historically, he has been. Or, hasn’t he? Isn’t that predominantly what he’s felt and why he’s sought power in any capacity that he could get it? Certainly not for any other reason. “What else is there?” he drawls.

“Remorse, guilt.” Kamara tips his head, the delicate glasses perched on the bridge of his nose catching twin drops of sunlight that sting Homelander’s eyes. “Shame.”

Homelander looks away.

“Did you know,” Kamara murmurs, “that there is an evolutionary imperative behind every human emotion? Each one of them serves some utilitarian purpose. Any feeling you’ve ever felt, no matter how big or small or useless, has been for the sake of ensuring your survival.”

“What could be useful about shame?”

“Well, societies used to be founded on cooperation. Shame was a guideline. It still is. That sinking feeling just here,” Kamara says, holding his hand over his sternum. “It’s an instinct given to us by our first ancestors, warning us of behavior that threatens our place among people. Shame is a great healer of damaged relationships, or it can be.”

Frowning, suspicious, Homelander glances up at Kamara. He doesn’t ask what the hell that’s supposed to mean, but Kamara notes his expression and chuckles.

“Look at it this way. If something is broken, you might just have the opportunity to fix it. It’s not a guarantee by any means, but it’s a chance. And it’s the same with feelings of shame. If you can understand through your own emotions that you’ve done wrong, you can address it. You can begin to make amends, whether that’s with another person or with yourself or even with someone no longer living. The presence of the feeling isn’t the end, it’s only the start. How you choose to act on it is what gives it the power over your life that you perceive it to have.”

Homelander rolls his lips together, pondering this.

“Do you struggle with shame, John?”

Does he struggle. Oh, he’d laugh if the question itself didn’t make him want to claw his own eyes out and scream himself hoarse. “No,” he lies weakly.

Of course Kamara doesn’t buy it. “What are you ashamed of?”

Myself, he thinks, shrinking. My sham of an existence. My pathetic whims. My failures.

“I’m not ashamed of anything,” he says. “I never have been ashamed of anything.”

“Okay.”

Homelander looks up sharply, but he meets no opposition in Kamara’s neutral stare. It’s plain enough to see he doesn’t believe the answer he’s been given, and of all things, that tilting, slip-sliding feeling closes in around his heart. I will not be cowed by this man, he thinks, as if shouting inside his head. I will not be ashamed. Not now.

Oh, really? Dad croons. Now’s when you draw the line?

Flinching, Homelander drags his gaze away from Kamara to the windows, annoyed until he sees both figures suspended on sunbeams, waiting to demean him.

Ain’t like you to hide once your bluff’s been called, says Billy.

God, even like this, captured in the shape and form of the last time Homelander felt his hands and could touch him back, even with dark blood smeared at his lip— some of it Billy’s, and some of it’s Dad’s— he’s beautiful, and Dad…he’s every bit as resplendent. In the prime of his life, despite his chains and his hatred and his primitive captivity.

Not for the first time, Homelander has to concede how right they look together. As if one is the extension of the other, like the Dioscuri of old. One unending spark of immortal flame shared between a single combined flesh. The same strength and indomitability, shared by two.

“Tell me what you’re seeing, John,” Kamara says, quiet.

Turning his head toward Kamara without tearing his eyes away, he can only stammer. He’s unused to this side of himself being known.

Lit all over by the sun, not unlike the blast of his ability, Dad says, Tell him, Sport.

Right, Butcher purrs, flashing bloody teeth in a grin that puts a crinkle in the bridge of his nose. Tell Daddy.

“It’s— it’s him.”

“Your father?”

“And Billy.”

“I see. Do you normally see them together?”

“No, I…sometimes I see— Soldier Boy, but I’ve…I’ve never…”

It’s not as if he thinks they’re actually here. It had been easier to think he had been seeing ghosts with Becca Butcher and with Stormfront, but these shades are living. Just like Mirrorlander came from within him, so, too, have they come from within him. 

“Why do you think you’re seeing them now?”

With Billy, there had been the chance for that impossible and uncharted terrain: freedom. A life, his own life, and a future far away from this churning regret. This constant humiliation.

“I feel…I’m…”

 Fucking empty’s what you are, Billy murmurs, and against the fall of sunlight, the specks of red standing out on his face look like shimmering gemstones. That’s why you hold on so tight, and why you can’t stand to be left alone.

Always was the difference between you, Soldier Boy rumbles, putting his hand on Billy’s shoulder, and us.

“John.”

He snaps out of it, tears on his face and a tremor rattling the deepest part of his skeleton. “Dr. Kamara?” he asks, voice breaking in one final display of weakness.

“I’m right here, John. Look at me. I’m here.”

He is. And they’re not. Just like everyone else, they’re not. “Everybody leaves me,” he whispers, barely audible over the squeaking of his plastic chair as he rocks himself in place. 

“I imagine it hurts you a great deal to believe that,” Kamara says, shuffling through a ream of papers on his desk. He’s sitting behind it now, though Homelander didn’t hear him move. “Can I show you something, John?”

“What? What does it matter? What does any of this matter?”

Without another word, Kamara extracts a single page and slides it over to Homelander. It’s a release form of some kind. He has to squint and hold it up to his face to be able to read the fine print, and for the first time that he’s noticed, Kamara scratches out a note on his legal pad without offering an explanation. Homelander doesn’t ask.

“This is…what is this? A visitation form?”

“For your son,” Kamara tells him, setting his pen down.

The faint ringing in Homelander’s ears dies away, like a missile defying terminal velocity. He meets Kamara’s eyes, feels one more fat, piteous tear escape, and quickly dashes it away. “He wants to see me?”

“Yes, he does.”

“And…you’d let him see me?”

“Your right to monitored visitation with him is contingent upon our progress in therapy. You keep our appointment and you work with me, I’ll authorize it. Ask me what I mean when I say you’re gonna work with me.”

“What do you mean?”

“I am going to show up for you every day that we’re in this office,” Kamara tells him, and while his tone has remained consistent throughout the duration of their meeting, there’s a solemnity to him now. A solidity with refined edges and hard limits. A fortress safeguarding him from the rest of the world, with the exception of this drawbridge he’s extending to Homelander. “The catch is this: you’ve gotta show up for me, too. Can you do that?”

“Show up,” Homelander echoes, wondering at the simplicity of it. “You don’t just mean physically.”

“No, John. I mean that all of you’s gonna be tuned into what we talk about here. I don’t want the canned answers you’ve been trained on since you were in diapers. I want the real stuff, and once we really get going on this and you’ve done just a small portion of the work we’ve got to look forward to, none of the fake stuff’s gonna do it for you either.” 

Kamara stands fluidly and rounds his desk. John wonders idly— Homelander— Homelander wonders— if Kamara has powers. He doesn’t know how to ask. Doesn’t know what he hopes the answer might be.

Pulling up to a smooth stop against his desk, Kamara leans back to sit in the same spot as before.

“It’s a long climb up from here. The highs are never going to be as high as they were, but your best can still be better than it was.”

Eyes stinging, Homelander looks away from him to where Dad and Billy were. They aren’t there. He can’t feel the presence of them anywhere, not even at the back of his mind. Broken futures. Dreams unrealized. A stratosphere he’ll never again feel at his fingertips.

“What do you mean my best can be better?” he rasps.

“Think about the last time you had everything you thought you wanted,” Kamara says. “Were you happy, or was there something missing?”

A chime sounds over Homelander’s shoulder, and a few moments later, the door opens.

“That’s our time for today, John.”

Homelander stands unsteadily to his feet. He eyes the hand extended out to him warily, and after a beat, he takes it.

“I’ll see you back in here on Friday?”

Nodding vaguely, Homelander finds it himself to say, “Fine.”

“Good. That’s good, John. We’re already off to a great start.”

Homelander picks apart the entire hour minute by minute back in his room. He tries to find a flaw with the plan that’s been presented to him and is beyond annoyed when he can’t. The whole experience is enough to rattle him on its own, but he can’t stop himself from replaying key moments of Kamara’s testimony at trial. He hadn’t been cruel, but he had been precise, exacting. Patient, but intransigent. Impossible to challenge, much less undermine. Sitting down with him for a brief hour had been more of the same.

His days progress. The only thing that changes while he’s waiting to see Kamara again is that he’s given an eye exam and a pair of reading glasses to hold him over until his prescription can be filled. He sits in his room with the cheap plastic in his hands, overhearing the distant fuzz of daytime television. With the V, he would be able to hear every word, but now, he’s hard-pressed to identify the program. 

He can hardly hear anything beyond the limits of this room, and clearly, he thinks, bending the flimsy frames experimentally in his hands, he can’t see anything beyond it either. 

By the time Friday rolls around, Homelander’s spoiling for a fight.

“Hello, John,” Kamara says, meeting him at the door.

Homelander breezes past him and slumps into his same seat from before. He folds his arms in front of himself and sulks. Kamara doesn’t take the bait. He lingers by the door to speak to the staff who’ve walked Homelander down here for his appointment.

“Thank you, fellas.”

“No problem, Doc.”

The door clicks shut, and unhurried, Kamara strolls over to the window to look out at the day. It’s overcast, has been since yesterday, but somehow the view doesn’t suffer any for it. As far as institutions go, he landed in one of the best in the country. Even if it leaves much to be desired in terms of patients, and even if it is a hole for abandoned lunatics, Pembroke is, objectively, a beautiful place. On nothing but the view from this window, he’d have to give it that.

Abruptly, he wishes he could fly. Not even to leave, necessarily— although he would, if it were possible— but just to see all of it. Just to have a glimpse of everything there is outside these walls: the red brick and crawling ivy, the storm clouds billowing up from the west, the sprawling bucolic grounds, the greenhouse he’d seen for the barest fraction of a moment on the drive up. He’s turning away when his eyes catch on a distant glare through the window, and then he’s taking another look, a closer look, that draws him up onto his feet.

“Isn’t that a drowning hazard,” he says, craning his head to better see the pond emerging through the canopy of trees and green, green grass.

“Oh, yes. Many years ago, someone did drown.”

Homelander tears his eyes away from the patch of sun shimmering on the smallest jewel of water he can see from here. Without looking away from the pond and with his hands buried in his pockets, consciously or unconsciously, Kamara opens himself up for study, and Homelander seizes the opportunity. He’s a sturdy man and impressively built. About Homelander’s age, or perhaps older. Self-possessed and disarming. Good-looking. He wonders what his dick looks like.

When he brings his eyes back to Kamara’s face, Kamara’s watching him, dark eyes amused and nothing else.

Clearing his throat and averting his eyes, Homelander asks him, “Who was it, that drowned?”

“Laura Pembroke.”

“Any relation?”

“Yes, actually. Her father was the founder of this institution. Terrible thing, to lose a child.”

“Not to everyone,” Homelander mutters, staring hard at the pond so he won’t have to look at Kamara. He tries to picture the scene so long ago, a little girl in sodden clothes, strands of hair floating along the clear blue water’s surface. He tries to imagine her face and just sees himself, water filling up his ears and his nose, clouding his eyes. “Some of us…”

Should never have been born, he thinks, except he wasn’t born. He was a science experiment and then a profit margin, and now he’s something approaching a man. With astigmatism in both eyes and a permanent ache in his badly healed fingers. That old panic he’s lived with for so long coils in him. Spirals.

“The circumstances of your birth aren’t your responsibility, John,” Kamara murmurs.

“I wasn’t.”

“What, born? You’re standing next to me, aren’t you?”

“You’ve read my file. You know what I mean.”

“Do you often attach so much weight to technical terminology?”

“The only difference between me staying in this room and leaving this room is a fucking technicality.”

“When you say it’s a matter of a technicality, are you referring to the eighty-seven indictments against you, or are you referring to the agreement we made?”

“No,” Homelander snaps, “I’m referring to the Compound V.”

“Do you believe your life, or your quality of life, to be predicated on a technicality?”

Homelander opens his mouth, but Kamara’s straight stare stops him. He gnashes his teeth and turns away from the window.

“I was— made. Manufactured, to sell fucking merchandise. I was merchandise. And you know what I am now? I’m fucking spoiled goods. Bad product. And without the V? Without being trapped in this fucking body? Yeah, I do, I do think my quality of life is predicated on a technicality. This—” He presses sweating hands to his chest. “—is an aberration. I’m not human. This should never have happened.”

“But it did.” Kamara turns from the window to give him his full attention. “You were born with a franchise in lieu of a family, and now you’re in this office, one titan of industry poorer, and less a titan yourself.”

Clenching his fists at his sides, his useless, milk-white fists, touched neither by sun nor by V, Homelander grinds his teeth together. There’s nothing he can say to that. It’s only the truth.

“Are you thinking about hitting me, John?”

“No,” he says, throwing himself back into the armchair across from the desk. Angry tears spring to his eyes, and shame curdles low in his belly. “Even if I did, I couldn’t hurt you. I’d probably…” —break my hand on your face.

The words dry up on his tongue. He wonders what it says to him that he is such a hostage to his body, but then, he supposes he always has been. It’s merely that it’s never been pain driving him. What does that say about Becca Butcher or the Campbells? Or Serge the Frenchman and Marvin Milk. Or about Billy, back before the V.

He presses a shaking hand to his face. Finds it wet with streams and streams of futile, inexhaustible tears. Enough to drown in and somehow, still, never quite enough to touch or name or describe the absolute bottom of what he feels. A box of tissues appears at his elbow, and he takes the whole box, though he doesn’t pull any of the tissues free. Kamara steps up to the side of his desk and leans a hip against it, not quite hovering, but too close to be ignored.

“You were merchandise,” Kamara tells him, still just speaking in that low, assured voice. “You were a marketing ploy. You were someone’s profit margin. You were, or you could’ve been, a man’s son. When you mourn those losses, John, what is the true object of your grief?”

“I w—” He blinks a few times rapidly, eyelashes sticking together. A sensation swells in him like an atom blast come to bring annihilation, and then— “I w-wanted to m-matter…to someone.”

And he thought he had been of tears before. He thought, at one time, that the crying boy inside him could be terminated like that long ago blastocyst Vought crafted him from. But no, oh, no. These tears are beyond the limits of his measly, incomplete flesh. They crest high above his head in a great wave to strike him down.

“I wanted to be needed. I wanted to be loved.”

He hardly knows what he says, only that the saying of it carves a deep trench out of him, burns him like a cauterizing wound, and leaves him aching.

“Nobody’s ever wanted me,” he whimpers. Fucking baby.

It’s his own voice telling him so. All along, whichever ghost he was seeing, it was only ever really his voice. His words, his opinions, his foolhardy, stupid dreams.

“John,” Kamara says softly. “Hear me on this, okay? Hear me. You were a child, and you deserved love. Not because you earned it, not because you were more special than other children, but because every child, regardless of where they come from or how, deserves care and attention and love.”

“Please, please…”

“What is it?”

“Please—” Hold me down, rip me open, take it out, take it out—

“Take what out of you?”

“This emptiness, this void, this fucking chasm, please…”

He rocks in place, an obstreperous and unsightly Gordion knot seeking a blade. Anyone else would give it to him. Anyone else would be right. But Kamara merely offers his hand.

“John, I want to give you my hand if you—”

Homelander catches it between both of his.

“Breathe with me. In, two, three, four. Out, two, three, four. In…”

He thinks probably this is the most anyone will ever hold him again, now that he’s in here for the rest of his life. It’s difficult to find fault with it, when it tells where the ground is, even if that’s not a detail he’s like to forget these days. Physically, in any case. And what does Kamara want out of this touch? The answer can only be nothing.

It puts a drop of cool relief between Homelander’s shoulders. Because there’s not a thing anyone could gain anymore from holding a hand out to him. Not fame, not accolades, not strength. Not sex. It’s—

Oh, it’s strange.

“I don’t know how to be here. I don’t know how to be alone.”

“You’re not alone,” Kamara tells him, again extending a drawbridge to John. Offering him safety. Offering shelter. “I’m right here with you, and I’m not going anywhere.”

“Why? Why, when I can’t give you anything?”

“Because it means something to me, to leave people better than I found them.”

John’s never—

Homelander’s never—

He takes a few more deep breaths in and out, feels the tears and his heartbeat begin to slow, and at length, makes himself sit up. Embarrassed, he peels his hands off Kamara’s and piles them in his lap. There are still rivers of tears standing out on his blazing cheeks, caught in his eyelashes. It should be such a source of humiliation, but there’s no judgment on Kamara’s face. Nothing in his calm expression to suggest he’s put off by having another man’s tears all over him.

“I can’t say I’ve ever…thought about it that way,” he admits.

“It’s not too late for you to start.”

He palms at his burning face, wishes his color would go down already, and before he can say another word, the door chimes. The faintest flash of irritation lights through him. He doesn’t want to go back to his room. He doesn’t want the conversation to be over. He doesn’t want to be left to his own devices again.

“We’ll pick this up again next week, John.”

And John—

John gets to his feet and turns for the door, that same wicked lick of suspicion pulling at the back of his mind. He looks over his shoulder at Kamara, unassuming, unimposing, and perfectly formidable all at the same time.

He searches Kamara’s lax, unguarded expression. The unbroken ease of him. Wonders how anyone can be so good at pulling the inside of a person out with little more than words.

“Next week,” he croaks, and then the staff come to take him away.

“And John?” Kamara rolls his sleeves up as John looks on, speechless. “Sleep well tonight, all right? You know what Saturday is.”

“No, what…?”

“Visitor’s Day,” he says brightly, nodding past John to the opening door and the staff trickling in to collect him. “You’ll want to be well rested when Ryan gets here.”

Ryan.

Ryan coming here to see him for the first time since the trial, when he’d still been avoiding John’s desperate looks. Ryan coming to speak to him. Ryan coming to see John in his dilapidated state, in his beige inmate onesie. All night, John doesn’t sleep.

Or he must, because he cracks his eyes open well after sunrise. There’s a new pair of glasses waiting for him in his mail tray, as well a Get Well Soon card. He opens the card, sighs under his breath, and puts the glasses on his face. For a moment, the sharpening of his vision is so viscerally familiar that he feels dizzy. Even with the help of bifocals, the handwritten words blur and bleed. He tosses the card back in the mail tray and walks out of his room to hunt down breakfast and instructions. An orderly advises him of when visiting hours begin, and then it’s a matter of keeping himself busy.

He still can’t stand daytime TV, but he likes the views from the windows well enough. It gives him the smallest comfort to map out the grounds in his mind’s eye. Eventually he locates the greenhouse on the south lawn, even bigger than he remembered and catching the sun in its glass like fire. Imagining the noises he knows he’s missing helps him to feel less like he’s pacing the limits of a gilded cage.

Ryan comes at ten sharp. John’s eyes fill with tears at the sight of him in his neatly pressed clothes with a crisp haircut and brand new shoes. Eleven, with twelve right around the corner.

“Hi, Dad.”

“Hi, buddy.”

“Am I allowed to hug you?”

“Uh—” He locks eyes with the security guard who’s two feet away supervising the room as a whole, and gets a firm nod in response. “Yeah. Yeah, I could go for a hug.”

He kneels, and stays kneeling, swept away in a relief so staggering he doesn’t think he’d be able to stand right now even with the V. Ryan’s arms around his neck are all that he’s been wanting since this whirlwind began— one of the things he’s wanted. He’s not much banking on getting the other thing.

“Oh, how have you been, huh? Where are you staying these days? I missed your birthday, I’m sorry.”

“It’s okay, Dad.”

“I, um.” He runs a finger beneath his glasses to get at his eye, smearing the tears rather than catching them. “Well, I’m really happy to see you, bud.”

Ryan nods and keeps hugging him, but he doesn’t say it back.

“Do you want to tell me about what you’ve been up to lately?”

“I guess.”

Visitor’s Day consists of time-blocked mornings and afternoons. They only get an hour until next week, if Ryan even chooses to come back. Maybe he won’t, but John can’t think like that. Not right now when the hope is this bright and painful in his chest.

Except then the hour whittles down faster than he can comprehend, and they’re left with ten minutes, and all he can think is that he can’t take this time for granted.

It isn’t owed to him. Ryan isn’t owed to him.

“Ryan, I need to apologize to you.”

“And to Mom,” he says without a moment’s hesitation.

“I’m—” John sighs. “Yes, I…”

“Can you even do it?” he asks, a certain defeated air lingering about him, like he’d known all along it would come to this. That he would make this sacrifice for John and John wouldn’t meet him in the middle.

How often had he been sitting where Ryan sits now, wishing anyone would see him? Would listen? And Becca Butcher had given Ryan that his whole life, until John came along. He thinks on Kamara’s aphorism on emotions. The utility of them in navigating relationships, specifically the function of shame.

“You’re right,” he admits, tentatively at first, until the words begin to run out of him like water from a damaged vessel. “I ruined everything. I almost— ruined you, the very first chance I had, and then I kept almost ruining you. I kept— making the same mistakes, and all that did was hurt you the way I was hurt. All I’ve ever done is hurt people, but I was never sorry about it until I hurt you, Ryan. And I am, buddy, I’m sorry. I really do love you, you know?”

There’s no crying boy in Ryan today. There’s only stout attention and firm resolve. John’s proud of him. He thinks he would be either way. Ryan had been stronger than him on that rooftop in Kentucky, and he’d been crying then.

“I love you, too, Dad,” he says quietly.

A frail smile touches John’s lips. He doesn’t have Ryan’s forgiveness, not yet, but maybe he can earn it. Maybe there’s still a chance.

“I want…” John presses his hand to his mouth, breathes, and forces himself to get through it, even for his renewed tears. “I want so badly for you to have a better life than I did.”

“I know.” Ryan seems to consider this, and then, deliberately, he reaches out to hold John’s hand. “I want us both to have a better life.”

“That might be a tall order for me, bud,” John says with an exaggerated glance around them.

“No. It’ll be easier.”

“How do you figure?” he asks with a strained laugh.

“Because here you don’t have to be him. You can just be you.”

The faintest ringing begins in John’s ears. As if he hears it, Ryan sandwiches John’s hand with both of his. He uses none of his strength. His touch is light. Automatically careful. Gentle.

“You didn’t stop being him when Grandpa took away your powers or when they put you in here for killing people and— a-and doing what you did to Mom. You stopped being him just now when you said you were sorry. And if that’s real, if you’re not him anymore and you don’t want to be, then I want to know the person you’re going to be instead. It doesn’t take away the things you did. It doesn’t make them okay. But if you mean it, if you’re really trying to be good now and you tell me that, then I’ll believe you. I’ll trust you. And even if you don’t, Dad, I’ll love you anyway. I’ll always love you, but—” Here, Ryan pauses to swallow. “But if you don’t promise that you’re going to try to be different, part of me will always still be afraid of you.”

“You don’t have to be afraid of me, Ryan,” he says, hoarsely, for his half-hearted attempts to stifle further tears. “I…I don’t want that to be who I am anymore. I’m— I’m talking to a doctor twice a week. I’m trying. And I— I understand if you can’t always make it out here to see me, or if you don’t want to, but—”

“I want to.”

John’s laugh this time is more one of elation. Of gratitude. He licks his lips and tastes tears. It reminds him. “Do you think you could ever be proud of me, Ryan?” he asks in a whisper.

Ryan’s lip wobbles for the briefest second, and he looks away.

“Do you think,” John continues, voice breaking and further tears spilling over. “Do you think that could ever change?”

“It might take a long time, Dad,” Ryan answers quietly, eyes averted.

“That’s okay.”

Ryan looks at him, eyes frank and very blue. His features are already beginning to sharpen with the onset of adolescence. Someday soon he’ll lose all the roundness in his face. He’ll shoot up like a weed, and his voice will be different. He’ll grow up. He’ll have a whole life, and John’s going to miss it but for the glimpses he’ll steal on the occasional Saturday when Ryan can be imposed on to make the journey.

“I read the transcripts from court; the testimonies, the victim impact statements, the judge’s verdict, and I read the report from Vought, the things Mr. Vogelbaum said about when you were little. They treated you like an animal— worse— but you were just a baby.”

John’s heart races. He’s sure Ryan can hear it.

“I’m sorry that happened to you, Dad.”

“You don’t—” He covers his mouth with his hand. His face is on fire and running with tears. “You don’t owe me an apology for that.”

“Somebody does.” Ryan frowns at him. “You’re my dad, and— and I’m your son, and I am sorry that people hurt you, just like I’m sorry you hurt people. If there’s ever a day where I’m not just sorry, for you or because of you, then maybe I’ll be proud then, I don’t know. You said you’re trying, so I’ll try, too. Okay?”

“Okay, Ryan.”

That familiar chime John’s become so familiar with during Kamara’s sessions rings out. John shuts his eyes, wishing he could stop himself.

Ryan stands up and hugs him, still seated and hunched at the table where he is.

“It’s gonna be okay, Dad.”

I’m sorry you haven’t learned yet how special he is, he hears in Hugh Campbell’s voice. There’s no apparition to accompany the words, there’s no need. His memory’s enough to bring the words back, and oh, John wishes he’d learned this lesson a year ago, or twelve. So much of his life to now has been for nothing. For someone else’s idea of glory. Inane and useless, devoid of meaning, not like Ryan. Ryan is everything. The best part of him. The only part of him, now, that can amount to any worthwhile legacy outside of this institution.

“I love you.”

“I love you, Son. I love you.”

“I’ll come see you next week.”

John hugs him and hugs him and hugs him. All day after he’s gone, he keeps that embrace at the front of his mind, savors it like the precious thing it is.

And then he goes back to his room for lights out, and he sees the Get Well Soon card.

He takes it up as he’s crossing to his bed, opens it, reading idly as he kicks off his slippers. Beneath the flourishing text factory-printed on the card, It’s a bad day, not a bad life, the sloppily handwritten message reads: 

 

This is only a hiccup in the greater plan, and someday, when you’re ready to rise again, we, the one true state, will be here to welcome you back into the sky.

Warm regards,
Team Homelander

 

His heart skips a beat. The card shakes in his hand.

He plucks the glasses off his nose, swallows hard, and looks around at his warm, simple room. It’s smaller than any other he’s had, with the obvious exception of the few weeks he spent in County, but it’s his. He doesn’t have to share it or look over his shoulder in the night. He thinks about his bargain with Kamara and his promise to Ryan, clutching the card in his hand so hard that the paper creases around his fingers.

Skin feeling too tight for his body, unease creeping up the back of his throat, he casts around for a place to put the damning card. He walks to the trash in the corner of the room and stands before it, mind racing, heart pounding, and tears it into several fragments over the bin.

Team Homelander, he thinks, nausea rising together with bile.

Team Homelander.

He runs to the bathroom to be sick, and after he’s rinsed the spittle and flecks of vomit off his chin, he paces back to the trash to stare down at the ripped segments of cardstock.

Sinking to his knees, hands trembling, he retrieves the pieces from the bin.

There’s a desk in his room beneath the one window of shatterproof glass. He opens the drawer and drops the torn pieces of the card inside, and he shuts the drawer without a second glance or another thought. He tells himself it’s because he doesn’t know why he keeps it. Because there’s no reason. Nothing to be gained by it.

And no reason to be ashamed. Evolutionary, or otherwise.

Chapter End Notes

Big shoutout to Beguile for having the best answer to the question I put to her of what power Kamara would have if he was a supe. If you stick around for the epilogue you'll get to see what she came up with. She really is my better half and soulmate. 🦞

Also, in case anyone wonders about my OCs, Kamara is modeled after Idris Elba, little shit Insight is modeled after Jake Gyllenhaal, and the speakeasy queens Nona and Kitty are modeled after Pam Grier and Storm Reid. <3

Chapter 22: fullness of joy

Chapter Notes

See the end of the chapter for notes

What’s happened with Homelander has been a blow to the credibility of our heroes, but at the same time, it has shown a fundamental and unequivocal flaw in the superhero paradigm: that while we always strive to hold ourselves to a higher standard, the simple truth of the matter is that we are human, just like you.
— Silver Kincaid, newly of the Seven
 NPR

Homelander was the best of us, but even the brightest fall without guidance and support. If you find yourself in a toxic or abusive relationship, or if you are currently struggling with sex addiction, there are resources available to you. Just call the number on your screen, and remember: if it feels wrong, don’t do it.
— Ashley Barrett, Vought International, C.E.O.
 The Wall Street Journal

I’d be flat-out lying if I tried to sit here and tell you I hadn’t been waiting a long time for this day to come. The thing is, though…sorry, what was the question? Oh, right. I feel good. I’m ready to be captain, and I know, uh…you know what, actually? I can’t say that. No, you don’t have to fix the teleprompter, it’s saying the words, I’m telling you, I can’t say them. They’re not true, and so much of what y’all loved about him in the end was that he just said what was true, right? So I’m gonna say this, now, because it’s the truth. As bad as you could possibly imagine him to be, you’re not even halfway— not even a third — of the way to who he really is. Was. I don’t care what he’d have to say about the team changing hands. He isn’t Vought, he isn’t the Seven, and neither am I. So you can print that however you like, but I’m going home.
— A-Train, Co-Captain of the Seven
 Good Morning America

Gosh, the scandal? I don’t know. I didn’t see a thing.
— Insight, newly of the Seven
 V-Tv

 

/

 

John’s been having a recurring dream the last few weeks. He dreams he’s standing on the roof of Starlight and Hughie Campbell’s apartment and he’s asking Billy to leave with him; he dreams that this time, this time, Billy says yes, and that’s when he wakes up. Every night that’s how he wakes up, until tonight. Tonight he dreams Billy flies away with him, higher and higher into the sun, into eternity, and then he reaches into his pocket. As if through a funhouse mirror, John watches his own hand dart out for Billy’s, stopping him in the act.

Nonplussed, mischief personified, Billy in John’s dream tips his head back and says, What’s a matter, darlin’? Don’t wanna see the ring Daddy picked out?

You didn’t, John mumbles, half-despondent and half-petulant.

Nah, Butcher agrees coquettishly. Just be another collar on you, though, wouldn’t it?

No, John says, and then, inanely, Not if it’s yours.

Cuz you wanna be mine, John? That why?

John misses Billy. He can’t hide himself from the truth of it inside the unforgiving echo chamber of his dreams. He misses the way they’d move like this, hands wandering, mouths seeking and sealing, comportments of flesh aligning and re-aligning, aligning and re-aligning. He liked the way it felt to hold onto someone with everything he had, and to feel them holding on right back. Billy’s hands had always been so rough and wonderful, and to feel them again, even through the ether of dreaming, makes him feel alive in a way he’s sorely missed these many long months.

Reducing down to cinders against the crushing heat of Billy’s mouth at his neck, John hears himself saying, whimpering, I am yours, Billy. I’m only yours.

Daddy’s little monster, Billy croons, easing away. His soft mouth is slick, but not with spit, and his eyes are dark, but not with desire. He’s battered and bruised with blood in his teeth, eyes gleaming, as he slips his hand out of his pocket. Grinning wide, Billy holds the cell phone in his hand at level with John’s face. He says, Smile for the camera, gorgeous.

The flash pierces John’s eyes and jolts him into waking.

It’s warm in his bedroom. The sun hasn’t filled the space with light yet, and nothing in the darkness around him assembles into concrete images. It leaves him clearer impressions behind his eyelids, Billy in the flesh, Billy clasping John to him, Billy kissing him.

Billy looking the way he did after John savaged him in Kentucky. The way he looked after his fight with Dad. Two generations of violence and destruction written clearly on his face. Kamara would have a field day with that observation during therapy. He’d probably also have a lot to say about John slipping his hand underneath his clothes to finish what his dream started. It’s still dark out when he finishes, the first rays just beginning to crest over the horizon and beginning to streak sunshine through the damask curtains framing his room.

The first thing he sees once his eyes adjust is the desk. Even without the x-ray vision, he can see the letters stashed in the drawer, a handful of them now where he’d started with just one.

Kamara would have much to say about those, too, if he knew.

It informs the remainder of his day, that he dreamed about Billy’s hands on him and that he’s back to keeping secrets. He tries to ignore his hyperawareness of it; tries to play the part of the fool and pretend he doesn’t understand why he does it. Why he lies, why he makes exceptions for the parts of himself he knows he’s being asked to leave behind.

So he distracts himself. He liberates a newspaper he stole off the nightwatchman a few nights ago from beneath his pillow, and he pores over its contents. He’s still razor focused on the paper when his appointment with Kamara rolls around.

Holding the trampled newspaper aloft, he takes great care to enunciate as he reads from the article: “‘Newlyweds Chimera and Mauna Kea spoke with us before jetsetting off on their honeymoon, set to take place in the latter’s native Honolulu. They neglected to give comments regarding the restructuring of the Seven, except to say that they are ‘excited beyond words’ to start their new lives together among the nation’s most trusted heroes.’ Can you believe that?”

Kamara stays firmly planted on the edge of his desk, impassive and saying not a word as John paces with the paper. It’s not explicitly contraband, but Kamara will be patrolling the halls of Pembroke later trying to ascertain who let his patient have it.

“Are you asking for my opinion on interracial marriage?” Kamara asks slowly.

“I’m not— look, I don’t care about that. I don’t claim to be color blind, even if my eyes are for shit these days, but skin color, race, religion, it’s all just noise to me. Always has been.”

Nodding idly, Kamara says, “So you didn’t veto Annie January’s pick during your last run of American Hero when she wanted to admit Silver Kincaid? You didn’t say, what was it, that you wouldn’t ‘let a fucking Muslim’ on the Seven?”

“You’re taking that out of context. It was an ‘us versus them’ thing. America, hoo-rah. That.”

“What you’re describing is called xenophobia, John. Ethnocentrism.”

John rolls his eyes. “But I didn’t mean it like that, and in any case, no, I wasn’t asking for your opinion on interracial marriage. They’re a good-looking couple. See for yourself.”

He struts back in Kamara’s direction and holds the paper up, picture side out, for Kamara’s viewing. When he doesn’t take his eyes away from John’s face, John returns his own gaze to the paper for further inspection. The young couple in question is decidedly handsome, and well-matched, too. He’s reminded of Billy and Becca Butcher that night at the party, how he hadn’t even noticed Billy for wanting Becca. What a mistake that had been.

Looking at the picture of Chimera and Mauna Kea now, he thinks he probably would still gravitate toward slender features and pink lips rather than serious eyes and big hands. Not that he’s in in any position now to tempt anyone. Between this spectacles and his carpal tunnel and the significant loss of definition to what little musculature he had with the V, he’s no one’s first choice. But even with his powers, Becca hadn’t chosen him either, had she?

“You said, ‘Can you believe that?’” Kamara redirects. “To what were you referring?”

“Hmm? It’s not important.”

“I disagree. The things you say to me in this room are of the highest importance. We wouldn’t be here otherwise.”

“I just,” John sighs gustily. “I happen to think it’s irresponsible that they rushed off to a scenic honeymoon mere hours after their matriculation into the Seven.”

“I see. Should they have gone to Greece instead?”

“I don’t care where they go; they just shouldn’t be going now. It’s reckless. The situation at Vought is precarious at best.”

“They’re up nine points since the wedding announcement.”

John tosses the paper on Kamara’s desk and sits unceremoniously in his chair. It puts Kamara at his right, unobtrusive, but inescapable.

“Maybe what you’re upset about isn’t the timing or the demographics of the team.”

“Oh, I’m plenty upset about that.”

“‘Upset’ is a wide net. Try to narrow it down for me.”

“I feel…” John reaches vaguely for the paper, pushing it back and forth with his fingertips. “Disappointed.”

“Why?”

“Because I’m stuck here and this is what they’re talking about,” he says, like an eruption. “Because they’re moving on. Because they’re happy, and I’m never going to be happy. Not like that.”

Not anymore, he thinks. 

“You’re never going to be happy ‘like that,’” Kamara repeats, setting his hand near the paper’s edge without touching it outright. “Do you mean publicly? In such a way as to be consumed by the masses?”

John lifts his fingertips away from the article to look up at Kamara, at first startled and then remembering. “Oh, of course. Your distinction between real and fake.”

“It’s not mine, John. Real’s got nothing to do with what we want it to be. It just is.”

Folding his arms over his chest, John shrugs, and says, “Well, as you know, I haven’t had much practice with the concept.”

“I don’t think you believe that’s true.”

John doesn’t answer for a long beat. He redirects his gaze out the window, looking for the dead girl’s pond. He can’t see it from here, and he can’t get up from his seat without brushing past Kamara to do it, so he tables that impulse for later. Unfortunately, that sets his gaze to middling in the vicinity of Kamara’s hand perched on the table just adjacent to his hip. If he had his x-ray vision, he could see through to the rhythm of his pulse, the heat of his blood, anything to tell him the man isn’t actually made of stone.

“What are you thinking, John?”

“You don’t know?” he challenges, testily. “You always know.”

“I can guess. With you there’s always a couple things it could be.”

“What would those be?”

With a curt sigh, Kamara takes up the newspaper and folds it up neatly before tossing it across the desk over his day planner. “The actions you’d take if you were suddenly reacquainted with your powers, fixation with regard to my anatomy—” Without pausing to dignify John’s panicked utterance of denial, Kamara continues: “And recollections concerning any number of the Butcher family, living or dead.”

Face burning, John mutters, “For the record, your second point is connected to the first.”

“So, I believe, is the third connected to the first,” Kamara tells him calmly.

“I’m— I don’t—” But try as he might, he can’t deny Kamara’s observation. Even if he wasn’t currently in a cage, even if he could meet with Billy on even ground after everything, he wouldn’t want anything to do with John. He knows that, and so what’s left?

“Is that one of your fantasies, John? That if you had the V, you could reinitiate that relationship?”

“I disagree with your characterization of the question,” John mumbles, shoving his chair back to retreat to the window, only he doesn’t get that far. He gives Kamara a dark look. “I never forced him. I never had to.”

“What does that mean, that you never ‘had’ to?” Kamara asks, so tonelessly as to be devoid even of a rising inflection.

“We— had an arrangement,” John answers, haltingly.

“At Arcadia?”

“No, before that. In his kitchen, when he said yes to me the first time.”

“And when you said yes to him,” Kamara fills in, to a thunderous and horridly telling silence. “Given your history with Becca Butcher, I can’t see what it does for you to maintain that your relationship with Billy Butcher was entirely consensual a hundred percent of the time, especially considering the transactional nature of what you’ve just termed ‘an arrangement.’ Would you like to explain it to me?”

He was my equal, is his first knee-jerk response, but that doesn’t make it true, when he really takes time to let it sit with him. Because Becca Butcher only ever wasn’t John’s equal for being leagues above him, and he’d been too shortsighted and too simple-minded to see it until it was far too late. 

Because what did John think, that a man like Billy would pledge his life to a woman who was anything less than his match? More than that, hadn’t he said? She was someone he needed in order to matter at all, to render him human. With her, he could be a man, and not a monster. John hadn’t understood it then, but he does now. Oh, he does.

Except he’d thought he could be a god with Billy, rather than what he’s been left with. Rather than what they’ve both been left with. Weak, breakable bodies— or at least, relatively weak, and relatively breakable. Billy had looked far from fragile down in the pit with Dad; they had both been frightful to look upon. They had both played the part of the beast, and that was with nothing but their fists to rely on. It was a different kind of weakness John had felt watching them down there. The weakness of a son who could only ever be found wanting. Who could only ever look up at his destiny, and never once be at eye level with it.

“He was supposed to…complete me,” he whispers. “And I was supposed to complete him.”

“Is that what you believe a partner does?” Kamara asks lightly.

“Well…” He thinks about Maeve, and about Madelynn, and about Stormfront. He thinks about the incinerating flash of Billy he sometimes sees in his dreams. “Isn’t it?” He thinks about what Becca Butcher was to the only man John has ever found it in himself to love. “Isn’t that what everyone wants?”

“Isn’t what, what everyone wants?”

“To feel whole!” John looks away, ashamed, trying to pinpoint a reason for why he feels it. He has no success. “To find someone who makes all of this worth enduring!”

Kamara barely blinks. He just watches John to make sure he’s done and says, “In this scenario you’ve postulated, what is it you’d bring to the table?”

The downward spiral John’s thoughts had been veering into scrapes to an unwilling standstill.

“What do you mean?”

“Say you find this person that makes you feel whole, and being with them gives your life meaning, gives you purpose and joy. For this relationship to be equal, for it to be a true partnership, you would expect to put in that effort on your side to enrich this person’s life, to surround them with comfort and encouragement. Wouldn’t you?”

“You mean what could I give them?” John asks, a strangely discordant note of recognition entering into his voice that doesn’t make sense against the cold dread trickling down his neck.

“No, John,” Kamara soothes. “What did I say to you the first time you walked into this office?”

“That I’d…” John clears his throat. “That I’d have to show up for you.”

“Yes, and I explained to you then what that meant to me. I want you to explain to me now what it means to you.”

“But I did.”

“You saved Billy Butcher’s life so that you could be challenged by him,” Kamara fills in. “And that made you feel whole?”

“For a while it did.”

“And what did it do for him?”

“He got a fight and a fuck,” John says crudely, to match his feeling. “Oh, and footage, apparently.”

“What about Greece?”

John folds his arms over his chest. “Well, Greece was different.”

“Why was it different? What changed?”

“He was— only ever a moment, before Greece.” Quietly, he adds, “Just like she was a moment.” Even hearing that it’s not true, he can’t stop himself. “She wasn’t for me. I didn’t want her to be.”

“Did you want him to be?”

Relieved for a question with an easy answer, it’s easier to sink into an honest reply. “In the end, yes,” he confesses.

“Becca Butcher wasn’t a moment, John. You understand that, don’t you?”

“She was—” He bites his tongue, says what he’s thinking anyway, too piqued not to bite back. “She was, when I made her that way.”

And now Billy’s reduced him to a moment. To that moment in the bunker with dad, when he should’ve tried to break free and had gone for the kill instead— for Dad, or for Billy, or for both of them, he’s never been able to figure out which. It would be ironic if it wasn’t for the fact that it’s exactly what he knows Billy’s always wanted. He never lost sight of it, not even when John did, and it won him his victory. It put John here, in obsoletion.

Part of him knows that’s why he’s saying these things, these ugliest, darkest things, out of anger. Because people are forgetting about him. Him. And he can’t stand it. Kamara allows the quiet to linger. Leaves John to marinate in it. He can’t much stand that either, so he doubles down.

“I wanted him to be mine. I wanted him. I could’ve— I could’ve had anyone, and I picked him!”

“But he didn’t pick you.”

“No, he picked a soft, domesticated, frumpy man with a kitchen cabinet full of multivitamins.”

Still in that same patient, meditative voice, Kamara says, “Is it safe to say you were so attached to the idea of a relationship with Billy Butcher because you could see a future in him?”

John bites back a scowl. “Yes—” and the amendment, I suppose, stalls on the back of his tongue.

“And it stands to reason that if he chose someone else, he would have made that decision based on a similar judgement.” Kamara pauses for breath, for effect, not long enough for John to do anything but flap his lips. Unfazed, Kamara continues: “Have you thought about why a gentle man taking care of himself might appeal to someone you yourself deemed the ideal partner?”

As a matter of fact, John’s thought a great deal about little else. Between his weekly visits with Ryan and the infrequent letters he receives, thinking about Billy is the only reprieve he has from facing the shit-show that is Vought in the aftermath of Homelander. All he’s been able to come up with, in his more spiteful moods, is that Billy has a proclivity for people who’ve survived him. It’s been a bitter pill to swallow, and knowing how it would taste coming up prevents him from saying it out loud to Kamara, regardless of his suspicion that Kamara already knows.

“Billy Butcher, whatever it is you apotheosize about him or your relationship with him, did not choose you.” Kamara clasps his hands, demonstrating with them. “He chose someone you’ve not only degraded vocally but physically, in the same manner you vocally and physically degraded his spouse.” His hands come apart, gently, just as everything about him is gentle. “Comparison is the thief of happiness, John. I’m not saying this to hurt you,” Kamara tells him, when it becomes clear that John has nothing to say for himself. “But you need to understand, people have value. Not because of what they can give or because of how powerful they are, but because life is short and the time runs out. You didn’t ask to be on this earth with the rest of us, you didn’t ask to be made different. But you know what you got in common with the rest of us who didn’t ask to be here, who didn’t ask to be made the way we were made? The time, John. It runs out. For you, for me. Someday, it is gonna run out.”

Hoarsely, finding his voice at last, John says, “So what am I supposed to do?”

The bell over the door chimes. Kamara holds John’s gaze. “Why don’t we save that for next time? I want you to tell me what you think the answer is.”

John stands shakily to his feet. He always feels out of sorts after his hour with Kamara, like his skin doesn’t fit, but contrary to how he’s always felt too small for his body, he almost feels too big for the skin he’s in.
