Like suddenly there’s a lot more to him than he thought there was, or like maybe there always had been, far from the surface, wan and starved for light. For fresh air. He takes a shuffling step toward the door as it opens, antsy and impatient over things left unsaid. All those things he hasn’t been able to make himself say.

“John?” Kamara asks, still seated on the edge of the desk.

“Do we have to meet here?” explodes out of him, a deflection, and he’s sure Kamara can tell, but it doesn’t stop him from staying on this thread. “I can’t fucking think in this office.”

“Okay.”

“I’m suffoca—” He grinds to a halt. “Okay?”

“Sure. Not the pond. I’ve seen you making eyes at it.”

“N-no. I thought— the greenhouse?”

“That’s fine.” Kamara nods over John’s shoulders at the attending staff waiting to take John back to his room. “You catch that?”

“Yes, Doctor.”

“I’ll see you on Friday, John.” He casts an unhurried glance over his shoulder at the discarded newspaper. “Leave out the unassigned reading next time, all right?”

The admonishment doesn’t deter John from stealing the nightwatchman’s daily paper, but it does reinforce his decision to keep his predilections to himself. He keeps squirreling away the newspaper clippings in the same drawer he’s been hiding the fan mail, catching himself up on the workings of the world from what he can read in columns of black and white. He reads about the million dollar wedding for Chimera and Mauna Kea, sees the glossy photos of them done up in perfectly tailored Alexander McQueen dresses, exchanging vows on top of an active volcano; he reads about Silver Kincaid’s burgeoning ambassadorship between Vought and the Peace Corps that he keeps trying— and failing— to find fault or prevarication with; and he reads about fucking Insight’s matriculation into the fucking Seven before the ink had even dried on John’s sentencing documents.

There are other things he can’t read about via news publications. Other things he’d like to be updated on but that aren’t accessible to him anymore. He’d like to know what Billy’s up to, for starters. Newman had mentioned bail the last time John saw her, but he doesn’t know where logistically that puts Billy in proximity to the long arm of the law. He could still be awaiting trial. None of the local news outlets have seen fit to report on it, and if he’s done anything noteworthy in the meantime, Billy’s kept his head down so as to avoid public scrutiny. Maybe to avoid John.

He doesn’t know how he feels about that. Come to it, he’s not sure what he feels about Billy anymore. 

Kamara’s put him through the fucking wringer on the topic of Billy Butcher lately. He’d thought it would be first on the docket when they started this arrangement, but it’d taken them a few months to get around to it, and as it happens, that’s right about when he started having the same dream night after night after night.

Since he can’t taken the newspaper with him to the greenhouse on Friday, he comes armed with his dreams instead, ready for Kamara to come after him, but he arrives on time for their appointment, and a confrontation isn’t what he gets. He’s dropped off at the front entrance with the attending staff lingering just outside the closed doors, his therapist nowhere in sight. He glances around, straining his ears to listen, and very quietly on the other end of the greenhouse, he can make out the faintest rustling noise. He shuffles toward it, his hospital-issue slippers shuffling against gritty sandstone. His view of the greenhouse from afar didn’t do the place justice. 

It’s more green than he can remember seeing in a good little while, foliage and rose bushes and tiny trees covering every available surface. There are hanging pots overhead dripping beads of water that look like diamonds against the refracted sunset catching in the domed glass walls. He holds his hand up to catch a cold water droplet on the edge of his hand. It runs down the length of his wrist and disappears beneath his sleeve.

“Kamara?” he calls out.

“Back here, John.”

He walks a little more confidently, following his voice. Around a tight corner at the very back of the greenhouse, far enough from the one access door that they won’t be heard, stands Kamara, shoulders curved to match the downward cast to his gaze. John looks where Kamara’s looking, to  the gnarled mess of thick roots emerging out of crumbling soil at the base of a sizable, vibrantly green plant. Kamara kneads at the roots, shaking more heavy dirt free and glances up at John to meets his eyes.

“Wanna get in here?”

“W-what?”

“Yeah.” Kamara tips his head, motioning for John to step in and take over. “Sleeves.”

John blinks, notes Kamara’s sleeves neatly folded halfway up his forearms, and shoves his sleeves up haphazardly past his elbows. He grabs the plant by the trunk, awkward, fumbling, nearly dropping the plant if not for Kamara’s support. A panicked syllable ekes out of him, but Kamara doesn’t bat an eye.

“The bulk of the weight’s here,” Kamara instructs, pointing without moving John’s hand to where he needs to hold. “Firm, but gentle. Same down here. You want the roots free and clear, but you don’t wanna break ‘em.”

“Okay.”

John combs down the length of the roots with a light-fingered touch, loosening the substrate a little bit at a time. He feels a distinctive pull-pop sensation and freezes.

“That’s all right,” Kamara assures him. “See how many more there are?”

Nodding, John resumes his work, even more careful than he’d been initially. “I thought plants were supposed to be in soil.”

“Sometimes the soil it’s in runs outta nutrients, or the roots die off. Could be from disease or too much water. Could be from pests.”

John looks up at him sharply. Feels another root snap under his fingers, a larger one this time.

“Shit,” he grits out, shifting his hand around to adjust his hold on the plant. “What’s the issue with this one?”

“Well.” Kamara claps his hands a few times, dusting the soil off, and holds up a terra cotta pot with calcified hard water stains across the lip and a crack all down the side of it. “This one needed more room to grow.”

Eyeing the fault line in the clay, John says, “A plant did that?”

“Take another look at those roots,” Kamara tells him, setting the broken pot on the ground. “See for yourself.”

The roots in question are robust, certainly, and a stark white against the dark, rich soil, not unlike his knuckles disappearing into the dirt. He hums, mind working. “This is a very clever metaphor, even for you, Kamara.”

“I’m sure I don’t know what you mean,” Kamara replies, as close to glib as John’s ever seen him.

John clears out as much of the dirt from the roots as he can, then brandishes it carefully to Kamara. “Now what?”

“Bring it over here.”

He hoists the plant up off the table, one of his sleeves sliding down to his wrist and dirt particles flying everywhere. Kamara leads him over to a basin with a dripping hose fed into it.

“You can set it down and spray the rest. Just like that.”

It’s meditative, actually, the spray of the water hitting the soil and clearing it out, leaving behind clean, thick roots at the base of a green, green stem. John returns the hose to the basin and brushes his fingers over the knobs separating the trunk of the plant from the exposed rootball. There’s a new growth unfurling from one segment, a brighter, younger green than the rest of the matured fenestrated leaves.

“Now what?” John asks in a whisper.

“Now we repot. Back to the table, c’mon.”

John goes with him to pick out a pot, a great, heavy thing Kamara hefts under one arm. Making a concerted effort not to stare, John backtracks to the table. Kamara shows him a tub of soil and other mixed media and hands him a cheerfully yellow scoop with which to transfer the soil into the pot. Not wanting to move the pot up onto the table, John takes the hulking plant down with him to the ground, sinking to his knees on the sandstone. Rather than take the opportunity to loom over him, Kamara plucks at his slacks and crouches opposite John on the other side of the pot. Somehow he looks right at home here, even still in his shirtsleeves with a gleaming tie clip pinned some ways below his Windsor knot. There’s dirt under his nails, just like there’s dirt under John’s.

“You know a lot about all this,” John says, mainly to fill the silence, although it feels different here than it had in the office.

“Opens up your world,” Kamara tells him, “to be curious.”

John’s throat tightens as if around fumes. It brokers disbelief, to be gifted something so simple, so huge, with such aplomb. Of all the things he’s ever been given, for the greatest of those to be something abstract, yet endless, he doesn’t know how to respond to it. He doesn’t know how to respond to the idea of something that could begin and endure inside of him, because of him, for no one else but him.

“You asked me before if I was happy, when I had everything I wanted,” John says slowly, methodically back-filling the pot with dirt so it covers the roots one scoop at a time. “Or if there was something missing.” Swallowing, he says, “I only ever…wanted things, before. I only wanted to have. I thought it was the only way…not to be empty or weak.” He brushes his thumb over a green stalk topped with a leaf bigger than his head. “I didn’t know.”

“What didn’t you know, John?” Kamara asks, soft.

“That it was the thing making me empty,” he mutters bitterly. “Making me weak.”

“Well, are you gonna keep letting it?”

John meets the frank, austere gaze honed in on him and nothing else. He quails, but he doesn’t look away. “I don’t know how to be any other way.”

Kamara cracks a smile, nearly wry. “John, I ain’t here for my health right now.”

Blinking, John tears his eyes away to focus once more on filling the pot. His own lips are pulling into an unwitting smile, and the feeling accompanying it is so alien to him, he doesn’t know the first thing about keeping it at bay. He isn’t performing; he isn’t trying to protect his image or win anything. He’s just covering a plant, and himself, in dirt so it can thrive, smiling at someone because they smiled at him.

“I’m sure you want a lot of things right now, but there’s more than one way to have something,” Kamara tells him, back to his familiar stoic veneer. “You can have dignity and self-respect. You can have empathy for others. You can have interests, hobbies,” he adds, gesturing toward the greenhouse with a generous wave of his hand. “You can have a set of skills that improve more and more with each day that you work on them. Confidence can be a skill; kindness and patience can be skills.”

“What if all I ever want is the things I’ve lost?” John asks.

“Then you’re gonna be disappointed.”

John sighs, throwing down some more dirt. Kamara sets his hands against the sides of the pot and gives it a shake so the soil settles. He nods for John to add a little more dirt to cover the last of the rootball.

“Does it strike you as ironic, John?”

“Does what strike me as ironic?”

“This yearning that you feel for Billy Butcher,” he clarifies, “even knowing it’s impossible to go back to the arrangement you had with him. Do you understand that you condemned him to the same thing when you assaulted his wife?” After a long pause wherein John doesn’t answer, not even to look up from the plastic scoop in his hand, Kamara continues: “The difference is stark and plain. For you it was an optimistic farce, a distraction from yourself; for him it was his life. It was her life. Theirs. And now it’s gone, every bit as impossible as your fantasies. Can you appreciate the gravity of that?”

John— well, he always knew that’s what he’d done. He knew. Yet, somehow, he’d never felt the implications of it. He’d made exceptions for why he didn’t need to feel remorse. For why it was beneath him, when all things were his right to take. Only, now? Now he has nothing to his name but his slippers and a stack of letters from a hateful assemblage of people that admired him for no other reason than that he made them feel less pathetic. Because they saw themselves in his emptiness and in his bloated sense of entitlement.

And how many more will feel the way he feels now, the way he made Billy feel time and time again, just because he created the type of vacuum in the world that Vought created inside of him?

“Irony I can laugh at,” John murmurs, scooping up another mound of dirt.

Kamara nods and presses his palm to the side of the pot. He gives it a few thumps, disturbing the soil again so it falls down into whatever air pockets remain. “That’s enough,” he says, pushing to stand upright. He helps John get to his feet as well, palm warm from the humidity of the greenhouse and gritty from handling the plant. “Not bad, right?”

John shakes his head, mind still turning in on itself, turning on him. Like the roots of this plant that had broken through fired clay but which fit in his hands like a liberated set of intestines. If his mind is a vessel and his thoughts are breaking out of it, then does that spell destruction for him, or can he expand himself in that same abstract way Kamara’s trying to teach him about? Can he change his mind? Can he learn how? What would someone else— a man— in his situation do if he wanted to grow in the only way that was left to him? The only way that would provide him with real happiness?

“I’ve been receiving mail,” he babbles in a rush, heart racing, unsure of his stride in this direction even as he knows he can’t take it back. “From— my fans,” he finishes lamely.

Kamara tips his head, expression changing not a whit.

Deflating, but more from relief than from dread, John says, “You knew.”

“John, you’re in a high security institution. We read your mail. We listen to your phone calls. Your visitations are supervised. Everything you do within the limits of Pembroke is strictly monitored.” He raises one eyebrow the tiniest bit. “You didn’t really think I’d leave you to fend ‘em off on your own, did you?”

A sensation like breaking apart and spilling fills up the space behind John’s breastbone. He blinks, amazed and perplexed. “You aren’t upset with me?”

“No, John. I figured you’d tell me, when you were ready. Are you?”

John’s nodding before he understands the depth of his answer. The truth of it. “Yes,” he says.

“Good.” Kamara angles his chin back in the direction of the hose. “Wanna give our friend here a drink and tell me about it?

“Y-yeah. Yeah,” John murmurs, sidestepping the plant. There’s a spider on the lip of the basin and he yanks his hand back, startled. “Jesus.”

“Hardly,” Kamara says, holding a finger out to tempt the spider to hitch a ride. Because he’s a little bit insane himself, apparently. “When you get bugs in here, this little guy’s gonna catch and eat ‘em.” 

“Bugs?” John asks, feeling nauseous and uneasy, remembering Blindspot.

“Sure.” Kamara raises his hand up near a ceiling beam for the spider to climb off and away. “Comes with the territory.” He looks away from the fleeing spider and back at John, expression serene but alert. “Or would you rather we take our appointments back to the office?”

“No! No, I like it here. Besides…” John busies himself with watering the plant so he won’t have to meet Kamara’s eyes when he says, haltingly, “We’ll be here, together. Won’t we?”

“That’s right.”

“And you’ll know what to do, if I don’t,” he says, a note he recognizes and hates beginning to creep into his voice, a plaintiveness, that he forces back. Clearing his throat and shaking himself, he says, firmer, “I can— count on you.”

“Yeah, you can.”

When John’s finished with the watering, and when he can make himself look up again, Kamara’s smiling at him. Calm, assured. Bedrock.

“So why keep the letters, John? What were you holding onto?”

John thinks about it for a moment, really thinks about what it was he found contained in those poorly written pieces of correspondence. The answer is obvious when he arrives to it. He presses his lips together and says, low, “Homelander.”

“Do you think there’s room for him in your life going forward?”

“No.” Eyes stinging, he shakes his head. “I know there’s not, I know…they only celebrate who I was because they didn’t know who I really am. I know that they’re empty, the way I’m empty.”

“You’re not always gonna feel the way you do today, John,” Kamara tells him. “It hurts now but someday it won’t, and that time is gonna come, okay? I promise you that.”

There’s knocking at the main door, and Kamara holds his hand out to shake.

“Meet here next week, John?” he asks.

“Yes.” John shakes his hand, imagining a presence inside of himself bearing the same qualities as the roots drinking up the water in the pot he just put them in. Imagining a mind with the capacity to adapt. Imagining a freedom that transcends confinement. “I’ll be here.”

“I hope you have a good visit with Ryan tomorrow, and John? Good work today.”

Possessed again of that spreading, unfurling sensation behind his sternum and the choking feeling in the back of his throat, he says, croaks, “Thank you.”

He turns on his heel to meet the attending staff, and his eye wanders as he’s walking out, catching on a mobile speck on a leaf. His skin crawls, but he thinks back on the spider, on Kamara, and aims a look over his shoulder, catching that straight stare one final time before he leaves. Kamara tips his chin and waves a hand caked in dirt and terra cotta dust.

It’ll be all right, he thinks.

“It’ll be all right,” he says, back in his room, standing over the trash bin with the stack of letters in his hands. He lets them go, from his hands, from that deeper place inside of him that made him keep them in the first place, and he scoops up the lining from the receptacle, carrying it out into the hallway. It’s lights out soon, so he won’t be able to take it back. Not if he does it now.

Not if he walks out to the medical wing and asks the employee to toss his effects in the incinerator. The man on night shift looks at him funny, but he does it, and his only reaction when John bursts into tears is to pat him brusquely on the back.

“It’ll be all right,” the man says, nervously.

It will be, John thinks. It will be all right, and he will be, too.

He will.

 

/

 

“—and then the boat just flipped over!” Ryan says, waving his hands to show what it was like.

Hughie barks a laugh, and Annie has to laugh, too.

Hugh, even though his face is bright red, has a big smile on his face when he says, “He had to flip the boat back around and then fish me out of the water.”

“Oh, man,” Hughie sighs, wiping a tear from the corner of his eye. “Did you even catch the fish?”

“No!” Ryan sighs, put out. “It got away.” And then, brighter, “But that’s okay. They stink anyway.”

Hughie and his dad snort one after the other. Annie has a follow-up question about the fishing rod, but a knock at the door interrupts her train of thought.

“I’ll get it,” she offers, taking a quick drink of her iced tea before heading in the direction of the door. The foyer looks the same as it did before the remodel, only it looks so much more lived in with all their shoes kicked up against the wall. Annie opens the door and freezes in place, looking from their surprise guest to the camera crew behind him. “Hi?”

“Hey, Starlight,” A-Train says, in a bright but soft-spoken voice. Upbeat, more so than he’s looked in a lot of media footage lately. “Or, uh, do you mainly go by Annie now?”

“Yeah,” she tells him, stepping outside the apartment and pulling the door carefully shut behind her. “What are you doing here?”

A-Train looks over his shoulder at the camera crew. “Guys, take five. What do you say?”

Annie folds her arms over her chest and watches them filter down the hallway toward the stairs. She leans back against the doorframe, no intention of inviting him in, even when he looks back at her. He takes off his sunglasses and sighs.

“Guess you haven’t heard about our little project we’re workin’ on.”

“I don’t keep up with Vought news anymore.”

“Fair. Actually, that’s what I came here to talk to you about.”

She stares at him, eyebrows pulling down. “I’m not coming back, A-Train.”

“Annie—”

“No, Reggie,” she persists, holding her ground. “I don’t want that life anymore.”

“Fine then, but I still gotta ask you something else,” he says, managing to look contrite. “You saw the guys.”

“I didn’t know you had a personal entourage these days,” she quips.

“Yeah. See, uh, this independent film studio reached out to me. They wanna do this grassroots thing, like an exposé.” He rolls his lips together, choosing his next words with what looks like a lot of care. “Like did you know the chemical compound for V came from a Nazi? Like, literally a Nazi, from World War Two.”

Annie frowns, taking in A-Train’s demeanor, trying to discern his intent behind being here. “You’re making a documentary?” At his answering nod, she asks, “Won’t Vought have a problem with that?”

“Fuck Vought. I wanted to be right where I am for as long as I can remember, but I didn’t know…” He shakes his head, licking his lips. “I didn’t know it was gonna cost me the girl I loved. I didn’t know it was gonna cost me my brother. I didn’t— I didn’t know I could kill someone for no reason and everybody’d just be okay with that, including me,” he adds, voice wavering right at the end. “I’m tired, Annie. I’m fed the fuck up with it. With all of it. And yeah, you know, you’re right. If I do this, if I put my name on it, they probably will get rid of me. They’ll make Chimera and Mauna Kea co-captains, and they’ll be the face of Vought going forward, and I’ll have to be all right with that. I can be all right with that,” he amends. “If I do this.”

She’s never seen him like this, with his heart on his sleeve. Even when he was in cardiac arrest and she was doing everything she could to save his life, he didn’t look broken open like this.

“What do you need from me, Reggie?”

He shrugs. “You can just tell them. Tell them about— about Homelander and Deep and— about me, too. Blow the whole fuckin’ thing open.”

“Where is this coming from? Why do you want to do this? Why now?”

Looking incredibly young with a wet gleam to his eyes, and especially when his voice breaks, he says, “I just wanna go home, Annie. Y’know?”

She starts to say she does know when the door swings open behind her.

“Annie, why—?” Hughie stops, gawking. “What the fuck is this?”

“Hughie—”

“I come in peace, man,” A-Train says wearily.

“Like fuck you do.”

“What’s going on out here?” Hugh asks, poking his head out of the door and hesitating when he sees their guest. “Oh.”

Ryan peeks around him, like he isn’t in the middle of a growth spurt that means he won’t be able to do that for much longer.

“Hi, Mr. Campbell,” A-Train says, even to Annie’s thinking, with more penitence than she thought he was really capable of. “Actually, it’s good that I ran into you cuz, uh, I owe you an apology, sir. For the last time we saw each other. I didn’t act right; I haven’t been acting right, and I’m sorry, really.”

Hugh’s expression opens up. Annie watches it, shifting in a single heartbeat from worry to relief.

“Well, that’s okay, son.”

Hughie whips his head around to glare at his dad. “No! It is not okay! Dad!”

“He said he was sorry, Hughie. Have you eaten yet, Mr. A-Train?”

“Dad!”

“Oh, uh.” A-Train flounders, turning around when the camera crew comes back. It saves him from having to have an answer straight away. “You know what, we got a pretty tight schedule to stick to, and—”

“I’ll make it quick then,” Annie says, rescuing him, whether he knows it or not, though his slackening expression says he probably does know. “Do you guys wanna talk out here or in the apartment?” she calls out to the advancing crew members.

“Annie!” Hughie protests, quieting the instant she reaches out to hold his hand.

In a lower voice, just for him, she says, “Will you trust me on this?”

He watches her then, his sweet eyes searching hers for a handhold. She sees him relax, and find it.

“Okay,” he sighs. “Okay, I trust you.” He narrows his eyes at A-Train, probably thinking the residual softness in his eyes has already dried up, but it definitely hasn’t. “You are not staying for lunch.”

A-Train raises his hands. Hugh stands outside the front step and raises his hands, too. He hovers one behind A-Train’s shoulder and extends the other in the direction of his home, his home that he almost died in twice now. Once because of A-Train, and once because of Homelander. Even for all that, he smiles.

He smiles, and he says, “Come on in, son.”

 

/

 

It’s a quarter to nine in the morning when Butcher’s called down for a visitor. He’s confused; his day with Ryan ain’t till tomorrow, and the lads, when they come to call, they let him know beforehand. It’s as he’s being walked down to the one-way phones that he wonders idly if Hugh’s swinging by to tease him with the promise of something sweet later, but then they pass the phones in favor of a more isolated room with lots of windows to see out of. There’s a man sat at the table when Butcher’s brought in. He stands at attention, and straight away, Butcher knows where he’s seen him before.

“You’re that doctor.”

Holding his hand out to shake with Butcher, the guy tells him, “That’s right, Mr. Butcher. I’m Muhammed Kamara, I was the behavioral health expert for the Prosecution in the criminal case against John Butcher, formerly known as Homelander.”

Nodding, and having exactly nothing to say about the name change, Butcher says, “Right, and what’re you doin’ here?”

Carefully, like he’s defusing a bomb with the power of his voice and nothing else, Kamara says, “Officially, nothing.”

“And unofficially?”

“I came to ask you a favor.”

Butcher considers this and has a sit, asking, “What’s the favor?”

“Vought is going to approach you for a retrospective on your case,” Kamara says, placing his hands on the table in front of him. “Yours and John’s. They want to repackage your dealings with him and launch it into their cinematic canon to combat the narrative of the documentary currently in production. I’m here today hoping I can convince you to turn them down.”

“I’m fucking convinced,” Butcher replies flatly. “What else you got?”

Kamara blinks, and Butcher’d bet money that’s about as flustered as the guy gets. He clears his throat, says, “I brought forms I’d like you to review.”

“What, NDA-type shit?”

“NDA-type shit is weak shit,” Kamara murmurs, popping open a briefcase with a flourish. “What I’ve got is military-grade kevlar.”

Butcher skims the sheaf of papers as they’re handed to him. He glances over the top of the page at Kamara and taps the pages against the table a few times to even them out. “Thought you couldn’t disclose the names of your patients.”

“I haven’t, nor will I. My proposal to you is this: you agree to work with me for the duration of these machinations by Vought to keep dragging this chapter of your life through the mud for profit, and your contract with me will preclude any offers they make to you, financial or otherwise.”

“Did you have him sign these papers, too?” Butcher asks, holding out his hand for the pen.

“I don’t know who you mean,” Kamara answers smoothly.

With a shrug, Butcher signs where the arrow stickers point. He doesn’t need to be asked twice to keep his name outta Vought’s mouth, at least until he gets out of here.

“That’s great, Mr. Butcher. Thank you.”

“Billy’s fine.”

“Billy,” Kamara says, shuffling the paper back into his briefcase. “I’m surprised you haven’t asked for anything in return for your cooperation.”

“Already got everything I wanted.”

“You mean because you put him away?”

Butcher hesitates, feeling the makings of an impulse unfurling behind his ribs. He frowns, rolling the answer around in his mouth. It wasn’t the vitriolic brushoff he’d had at the ready like a gun concealed underneath his shirt; this thing wanting to come out of him now has a deeper origin. Deeper than he can track without looking right at it, and clawing, the more that he digs his heels in against it. 

“It’s what’s best for him,” he answers, haltingly.

Kamara tips his head. “How do you mean?” he asks.

With a sigh that comes from the same place his words burrowed out of, Butcher tells him, “The whole time we were doin’ our thing, whatever it was ‘n whatever we wanted it to be, it wouldn’t do for him but to give me things. The stars, the mountains, skyscrapers, my fucking friends dead at my feet… see where I’m goin’ with this?”

“Yes, I understand.”

“Do you?” Butcher leans his elbows on the table in between them, loosening up the more that he lets himself give into that urge to keep talking. “He was a kid. Like Ryan’s a kid.”

A shudder stops him from continuing down that line of thinking, but Kamara, impassive, managing to look alert without seeming too invested either way, doesn’t interrupt him. He just waits, and Butcher finds it’s easier to speak into the silence.

“He thought I’d fix what those cunts, Vogelbaum ’n the rest, did to him, but I couldn’t. Could I?”

“No,” Kamara agrees softly. “You couldn’t.”

Butcher swallows and looks away. “No one’s asking what he can give them anymore. Does that change anything? I don’t know.” He rolls his eyes. “I fucking hope so, but I don’t know.”

Kamara nods slightly, eyes as serious as they have been. “I hope so, too.”

Cracking his neck, Butcher tosses a sideways glance at Kamara. Really lets himself feel the loosening up of tension between his shoulders. He narrows his eyes. “I usually fucking hate mind readers,” he says, slowly.

“Oh?” Kamara asks, not a thing in his face or his eyes changing the slightest bit.

“Yeah. But I don’t think that’s your deal, is it?”

“You’re very astute, Billy.”

“And that’s all you got to say about it?”

“Well, you’ve been cooperative, which is more than I was given to expect, and I don’t much want to keep you in suspense. What you’re experiencing right now is a chemical reaction to pheromones—”

“Fuckin’ A right it is,” Butcher says blandly.

Kamara huffs a laugh, a small thing. An allowance. He shakes his head. “Have you heard of truth serum?”

“I’ve heard of psychotropic drugs eliciting crap confessions.”

“Weak shit,” Kamara says primly and pointedly.

Butcher leans back in his seat. “Then you’re the kevlar.”

“If you like. I’ll certainly take it.”

“Pretty handy in your line of work.”

“It can be,” he admits with the smallest shrug. “In my experience, though, most people don’t need any help telling the truth.”

“I know a couple of ‘em,” Butcher mumbles, then shuts his mouth. “Are you still doin’ it?”

“No, this is all residuals. I’ll refrain from asking you any further questions if you’d like to wait it out.” Kamara opens his briefcase again and goes rummaging through it. “On the topic of your friends, I’ve been to speak with them as well.”

He extracts a polaroid and slides it over to Butcher. It’s a group shot of Butcher and the lads back at the office before his trial, back when they all knew he’d be doing time but it was anybody’s guess how much.

Butcher’d been braced for a heavy sentence, but they gave him a year stretch, and that time’s been easy. He’s done six months of it already and slept great every night, but he does miss home. He misses the silently bitchy knowing looks and kindness from MM and Hughie, and he misses the hard-line no-bullshit policy shared by Annie, Frenchie, and Kimiko, and he misses the soft spaces he’d found for resting in Hugh’s arms and in Ryan’s confidence. 

“Thought you said you’d quit it,” Butcher mumbles, dropping his cheek into his hand.

“I did, Billy,” Kamara says, smiling.

“Uh huh. What’d you talk to them about anyway?”

“You. John, Soldier Boy.”

Butcher puts the photo down and turns his face away, eyes shut against the implied question.

“We can talk about it next time,” Kamara promises, a calm, easy smile on his handsome face. At Butcher’s curious look, he pats the briefcase with his hand, closed again, though Butcher didn’t catch him doing that. “Per our agreement.”

“Ah.”

“You know, they all had complimentary things to say about you. I was impressed. Especially Hugh, Sr. He’s very taken with you.”

“Yeah? It’s fuckin’ mutual,” Butcher snaps, and promptly winces at himself.

Kamara cracks a smile. “Billy, I wonder if I could ask another favor of you. There’s an inmate incarcerated here. If you could get a message to him for me, I would greatly appreciate it.”

And well, goddamn. Goddamn.

Butcher’s released into the yard after his business with Kamara’s deemed concluded. He’s itching to look at the polaroid in his pocket again, but it’ll be there for him later when he’s laid up in bed waiting on sleep to bite him. A nice dream, and it’s never felt quite so near as this, even with the provisions of jail time and runnings errands for his brick shithouse of a therapist. Cuz that’s a thing now.

He finds Edgar where he can usually be found. By the fence looking out at the green.

“Stan,” Butcher greets, sidling up next to him.

“Billy,” he replies, cordial-like. He turns a wry look on Butcher, one eyebrow raised. “We wondered if you’d been whisked away.”

“Nah, you ain’t seen the last of me.” Tipping his head, he adds, “Not yet.”

Edgar hums, a sound of consideration very nearly approaching a laugh. “Don’t tell me, Vought finally came sniffing around to get their hooks into you on the Homelander scandal.”

“Better,” he says.

“Better?” Edgar muses, the barest upward tick in his voice.

“I met your friend. The really honest one?”

Butcher gets a full show then watching Edgar’s mind work. The light that enters his eyes when he puts it together precedes a full laugh the likes of which Butcher ain’t heard once since his booking.

“I’m sure that was quite the treat for you,” Edgar prompts, settling down again.

“Right. Who don’t love a good interrogation after breakfast.”

“He always did conduct his best work in the small hours. Granted,” Edgar adds, with a glance that cuts from the shadow cast by the fence up at the position of the sun in the sky. “Polite society hasn’t caught up to the time we kept back in those days.”

“What, you mean with Vought?”

Edgar looks at Butcher, distracted. With a curt, dry chuckle, he says, “No, Billy. In a CIA black site where Vought, to the best of my knowledge, you understand, never had security clearance.”

“Then what were the two of you doin’ there?”

“I was attempting to obtain said security clearance,” he answers. “As for our friend, Kamara, he cut his teeth in intelligence. Simply put, he was not ‘out’ as a supe. Difficult to say if he is now, but it is interesting, don’t you think? To see the outcome of a life with so much potential, not given over to silliness and popularity contests.”

Butcher sinks his hands in his pockets. He thinks about Ryan, and even, at length, about Head-Popper. It’s not something he can ever lose sight of, when it’s Edgar he’s talking with.

“‘Attempting?’” he asks.

“It didn’t work out,” Edgar croons dispassionately. “Oh, it never had any chance, with the failure of Payback so fresh in the public’s perception. It does bear mentioning that, in the natural course of our dealings with one another, I became aware of a defect in our business model.”

“Which was what?”

“That it was predicated on frippery and vanity. That it would never yield champions or leaders in the way that we promised. That an angry young man who started with nothing had done more to carve a path for himself in this world than any of our gilded, flightless birds. Or yes, even those that soared. Maybe especially them.”

“So you rewrote the model,” Butcher says.

“As your late wife saw fit to do,” Edgar replies smoothly, unruffled. Unbothered. Calm as the ten pounds of pressure poised to pull a sniper’s trigger. “We can all only do what we can do, Billy, and clarity comes from the strangest places, if one merely listens.”

Nothing for that but to nod. He squints up at the sky, saying, “Funny you say that. He had a message for you.”

Edgar turns his head to look right at Butcher, but he doesn’t say a word. He just listens.

“Said John fessed up, on record. Guess since he’s dead to rights anyway, it weren’t nothin’ to him now to own up to your charges. Long as you don’t get buried in paperwork and red tape, you’ll be outta here and home by Christmas. Earlier, if Kamara gets his way.”

Blinking, Edgar casts a slow, deliberate hand to catch his fingers in the chainlink.

“Well, he does have a tendency,” Edgar says faintly.

Butcher huffs a laugh through his nose. “This hurts my feelings, y’know. Who else’m I supposed to talk to in here?”

“Oh, please,” he counters, sounding more like he’s got his feet back under him. “Your parole date is fast approaching, and you’ve been obscene since you got here. There isn’t a better behaved inmate in our block. Frankly, it’s annoying.”

Rolling his shoulders, Butcher smirks and opines, “’s easy time when you got someone waiting on the other side.”

Edgar breathes in deeply, his free hand joining the other threaded through the fence. He breathes out, nothing tense about him that Butcher can see, but it’s in his hands, coiled up into fists around the chainlink, quietly keeping the world at bay. “Yes,” he agrees, crisply, tightly controlled, peering out at the world through the gaps in the fence. “It certainly changes your outlook.”

“Maybe you’d put in a good word for the lads ’n me when you’re out.”

“Hmm?” Edgar searches Butcher’s face for the context he’s leaving out of his words. His expression slackens, finding it. “With Vicky? Why?”

“Cuz I like my head where it is, Stan.”

Laughing, Edgar flashes a camera-ready grin and purrs, “Billy, who do you suppose set and met the parameters of your bail requirements?”

Butcher stares at him, frown deepening on his face. “No.”

“Yes, as a matter of fact.”

“How the fuck did you get her to agree to it?”

“I didn’t have to, seeing as it was her idea.” Edgar withdraws his hands from the fence, one at a time. “She felt a sense of loyalty to your friend, Hughie. A sense of loyalty, I might add, that he neglected to extend to her.” He holds a hand up at Butcher’s mouth flapping open, and adds, “You threw her to the wolves, and still, she helped you.”

“After blowing up a lotta people’s heads.”

“Were there no casualties to your name after the V,” Edgar murmurs. “Or before, for that matter.”

“I ain’t sayin’ there were or weren’t, but even you gotta admit that’s a shit way to go.”

“You can say you’re afraid of her, Billy. Plenty of people would be, if they knew, but so, too, would they fear you, if they knew the entirety of your personal history. In the same vein, many of them would have heart for you. As they would for Vicky, if they knew.”

“It ain’t the whole story.”

“No, it’s only a part of it. The rest of it is you,” he says, another one of those laser-focused looks of his pointed at Butcher. “And what you make of it.”

Butcher shakes his head, and reluctantly, he has to cede the point. He’s got a little one with killing power in his eyes now, too. It ain’t enough anymore for him to write off supes altogether. The only thing he can do is live up to his promise to do better. Cuz it’s Ryan what’s his future now, just like Neumann was Edgar’s, and like Edgar before him, Butcher’s the one entrusted with getting Ryan from Point A to Point B. From lost kid to, hopefully, a decent fucking human being.

“Vicky is plenty happy with you just now, and if what you’ve said is true,” he adds, thoughtfully, “then we will, both of us, have yet another reason to count you as a friend.”

Butcher folds his arms in front of himself. “Didn’t do nothin’, me.”

“Well, I’m indebted to you all the same.”

Mulling over the wealth of possibilities at his fingertips, Butcher finds he don’t need to think very long about what he wants, especially if Edgar’s telling him he don’t need to worry about Neumann, at least for now. Butcher clears his throat and asks, “What can you tell me about a twat supe called Insight?”

With the slightest moue shaping his mouth, Edgar says, “The underwear model with his eyes burnt out of his head?”

“Right. Any weaknesses he’s got?”

Edgar thinks this over, mind visibly working. Slowly, he says again, “The underwear model, with his eyes burnt out of his head?”

Butcher hears the implication. “He’s got eyes, just not his own.”

Still turning the problem over in his mind, something occurs to Edgar that opens up his expression. He says, “Would it surprise you to know that he was once the scourge of the Vegas strip?” With a sideways glance at Butcher to gauge his reaction, he continues, “He had quite the gambling addiction, and as these things are wont to do, it got away from him. His contract changed hands several times before Vought snatched him up. They paid his debts in full, on the condition that he not return to his former bad habits. Apart from that one caveat, he’s been given free rein by the brass to do most anything he likes.”

“So if he walked into a casino tonight…”

“Tomorrow he would be out of a job,” Edgar reports matter-of-factly, “and legally responsible for paying back the full amount of money paid by Vought on his behalf.”

Butcher cracks his knuckles idly, already thinking ahead to the letter he’s gonna write Hughie later. “Now, see, that? That is very interesting, Stan.”

“An ordinary solution will often suffice, even when faced with extraordinary circumstances.”

“I’ll drink to that, when we’re outta here.”

“Billy, when we’re out,” Stan muses, smiling, “drinks and cigars are on me.”

“Bet,” Butcher says, holding his hand out to shake on it. 

They stand for a little while in the quiet, in the sun, watching the birds fly, maybe thinking of the things waiting for them on the other side of all this concrete. Butcher’s thinking about Ryan’s birthday coming up; maybe Edgar’s thinking about his granddaughter, and about Neumann. He’s lost in his thinking and doesn’t expect it when Edgar speaks again.

“I never would have thought. You, his best handler.”

“Guess I never looked at it that way.”

Edgar looks askance at him. “How did you look at it?”

“Like a really drawn out bullet to the head,” Butcher tells him. “Then like a fever dream, and then right back to the bullet.”

“Fever dreams aren’t inherently good or bad,” Edgar says cautiously.

“No, they’re really fucking confusing. Bein’ with him was like that. Not for long, just for a moment, but…”

“But that moment is all it takes.”

“I kept thinking that, right at the end. When it was me ’n him, ’n his dad— or, the fuckin’— how’d it go? What did the official reports call it.”

“Homelander’s spontaneous permanent reaction to Grace Mallory’s chemical antidote,” Stan rattles off.

“Fuckin’ right. The chemical antidote.”

Edgar watches him curiously, eagle-eyed as ever. With unprecedented gentleness, he asks, “You kept thinking what, right at the end?”

“That there was a moment, just one. But not with John, with—” Butcher stops, aware of a certain clawing sensation he’s learned to identify just today. He scratches at his cheek. “D’you know what, Stan? I’m still roofied.”

“Oh? Oh.” Edgar covers his mouth with his hand, poorly concealing a grin. “Maybe you had better walk it off then.”

Butcher considers that. Really thinks through the ramifications of staying and of going. In his heightened state, whatever it is in him that’s heightened right now, there’s something in him that’s always known he’s the type to leave active mines for himself to step on later. Like the Temp V, like Soldier Boy.

“Billy?”

“I thought about stayin’ down there with him,” he confesses. “With Ben.”

With a blink and nothing else to convey how fucking strange this utterance is, Edgar says, “He could be charismatic, when it served him. And he could be monstrous.”

“Yeah, he could.”

Edgar tips his head, and without another word said, turns his eyes on Butcher. Maybe he’s wondering why Butcher’d tell on himself like this, truth serum pheromones or no. Butcher don’t know how to tell him he learned it from Hughie; putting the kill-switch in someone else’s hands so it becomes a precision instrument rather than a bludgeoning fist. Bit ironic, that. Since he met Hughie at all on account of him putting his kill-switch in Butcher’s hands.

“Y’ever hear it said,” Butcher murmurs, “that you hate most in others, what’s in you?”

“Of course. Classic projection. Though I fail to see how that’s applicable here.” At Butcher’s querulous look, he says, “Homelander is alive. Soldier Boy is, unfortunately, alive. You had the perfect opportunity to be the worst version of yourself. If you hesitated for considering it, for that one moment when judgement left you, you are still left with one incontrovertible truth. That despite any doubts or reservations you might have had about following through on your course of action, you afforded them a dignity they themselves scorned. You empathized with them. You saw them as human. If you’ll recall, Billy, that is not the defect to the model. That is the improvement upon the original formula.”

Butcher crosses his arms in front of his chest, ears burning. Contradictions crowd the back of his throat, raring for a fight.

“Oh, yes, pout because you’ve lost the argument,” Edgar sighs. “Vicky was the same way.”

Irked, Butcher uncrosses his arms. “You weren’t never worried she’d pop your head like a pimple?”

One eyebrow arched, Edgar says, enunciating, “Do I strike you as the type to worry?”

Snorting, Butcher shrugs.

“She was not some despoiling tyrant, Billy. She was a child.”

He was a kid, Butcher thinks, recalling his own words to Kamara less than an hour ago. Like Ryan’s a kid.

“I don’t think I need to tell you that you can’t really love someone if you’re afraid of them, and I do love Vicky.” With a sense of both finality and protection, he adds, “She is my daughter.”

“Hey, I get it.” He didn’t, once. But he’s Ryan’s dad now. Now, he has to get it.

“I suppose you do. That’s good. Childhood is a vital deciding factor in the rest of an individual’s development, as you well know. You’d hate to set Ryan on the wrong path now, after the sacrifices his mother made to get him this far. You’ve seen the outcome of a faulty upbringing, after all.”

“Don’t remind me.”

Again, there’s a pocket of quiet, and again, Butcher could leave. Again, he doesn’t. He and Edgar get on, and now that there’s an expiration date on how many more conversations they’ll have by this fence looking out at regular life, he’s not in any rush for the time to run out on him. Call it sentimental, but there’s a practical angle in it for him, too, now. Butcher never had a dad to model himself after. He never saw, until really goddamn recently, what a real father looked like.

“Stan,” he starts.

“Just love him, Billy.”

He drags his eyes away from the farthest point on the lawn he can see, caught. Edgar tips his chin, raises his eyebrows, like Butcher’s a puzzle he solved in two moves.

“There’s an element here you aren’t considering,” Edgar tells him, plainly. No ornamentation to how he says it. “The tide is, and has been, turning. For all our contrivances and contingencies— for all our meddling— Vought is an unsustainable science experiment. Homelander has proven that; the first time when he gave away the chemical compound, and the second time when he gave away himself. He has forced the world to think, and think very critically, about the caliber of their heroes. About what makes a hero. Your son will very likely age into a world that cares less for superpowers than it does for the contents of a person’s character.”

“Fuckin’ way it should be,” Butcher mutters.

“Oh, yes,” Edgar drawls, smiling a little. “The meek will inherit the earth.”

With a wry smile of his own, Butcher pops back: “What d’you look so happy about? That ain’t us.”

“The tide is turning, and the meek will inherit the earth. But not yet, Billy. Not yet.”

“Hell, I’ll drink to that, too.”

“We’ll add it to the list,” Edgar says, good-naturedly. Laid-back, easy, on the cusp of humorous. The exact same temperament he’d have even if Butcher hadn’t just given him the best news he’s probably had since getting clapped with charges in the first place. “Was there something else you wanted to ask me?”

Butcher shrugs, hands in his pockets, eyes on the horizon one more time. He says, “As far as his teens go…?”

Edgar tips his head back and laughs.

 

/

 

The whole thing at the Caesars Atlantic in NJ was just boys being boys tbh. It’s unfortunate that it turned out that way, but man. Who knew you could be banned from ever playing poker in public!!! Also that wasn’t me in the fish tank. Pls stop tweeting the hashtag #ballsdeep.
- Deep, former Co-Captain of the Seven
 r/AMA

I was invited to return to the Seven, but it didn’t align with my values or with my long-term goals. I’m at a place in my life now where I know that’s true and that it always was. A-Train deserves to headline the new team, and I hope this work he’s doing to bring superheroes back down to earth is seen and appreciated. People deserve to know the truth, and now they have someone who wants to tell it, if they’re only willing to listen.
- Annie January, A.K.A. Starlight, formerly of the Seven
 Barbie Doll Soldiers: The Vought Story, “A Documentary for the People, by the People”

With the clearing of Stan Edgar’s name, we are seeing a new leaf turning in our modern times. There is no expiration date on the truth, and there is no glory to be had in building up false idols meant to carry our strength. If you need support, look to your community; if you need guidance, look to your friends; if you need hope, look to your children and remember that they are the future. People are our strength, and we are stronger together.
- Victoria Neumann
 The Washington Post

My dad did a lot of really bad things, and I know in his own way that he’s sorry about it. Nothing could ever make it okay that so many people were hurt because of him. I’m here at all because he hurt my mom— my mom who gave me everything, even before I was born. She sacrificed so much. She gave up the person she loved the most, she gave up her whole life, just so I could be safe, so we could have a life together, and it makes me sad that my dad wasn’t lucky like I was. Her loving me made all the difference, for me and for her husband, Billy Butcher, my other dad. I wish we could’ve all been a family together, but this is the family we have now, and it’s a family I’m going to protect. Because that’s what you should do when you have the strength of an army. You should use it to keep people safe, to stand with them. I learned that from my mom, but I think I learned it from my dads, too.
- Ryan Butcher, surviving son to Becca Butcher
 The New York Times
